heading: Single-Manager vs. Multi-Manager
body:
Structure
Description
Single-Manager Fund
One PM team investing in one strategy or style. Purer factor exposure,
cleaner attribution.
Multi-Strategy Fund
Teams of PMs running multiple different strategies under the same fund
structure. Faster reallocation, netting risk absorbed by GP.
Fund-of-Funds (FoF)
FoF manager allocates to separate, underlying hedge funds. Double fee
layer; netting risk borne by investor.

body:
Strategies are classified by: (1) instruments traded, (2) trading philosophy (systematic vs discretionary), (3) types
of risk assumed (directional, event-driven, relative value).

heading: Long/Short (L/S) Equity
body:
Long/Short Equity — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Primarily stock selection (long: undervalued; short: overvalued); market timing
secondary and mostly poor
Net Exposure
Typically 40%-60% net long; gross long 70%-90%, gross short 20%-50%
Leverage
Variable; quant managers more levered than fundamental managers
Liquidity
Liquid — can be LP or mutual fund structure; exchange-listed positions
Benchmarks
HFRX/HFRI Equity Hedge Index; Credit Suisse L/S Equity Index

AUM Share
~30% of all hedge funds — most prevalent strategy
Key Risk
Embedded net long beta — beware paying hedge fund fees just for beta

Investment Characteristics
Managers specialise by geography (US, Europe, Asia), sector (tech, healthcare, biotech, financials), or style
(value/growth, large/small cap). Sector-specialist managers have deeper informational edge but narrower
mandate. Generalist managers scan wider universe but may lack industry depth.
Most managers maintain net long exposure because equity markets secularly rise. Short positions used as: (1)
pure hedges against market drawdown or (2) alpha generators from specific short theses (accounting fraud,
failing business models, overvalued companies).

Exam Traps — L/S Equity

!
L/S equity is NOT market-neutral. Average net exposure is 40%-60% LONG, not zero. True
market-neutral strategies are EMN (separate category).

!
Sector specialists have HIGHER information edge but NARROWER mandate — may suffer if
sector falls out of favour. Generalists are more flexible but may lack depth. Both have tradeoffs.

heading: Dedicated Short Selling & Short-Biased
body:
Dedicated Short / Short-Biased — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Short-side stock picking: overvalued companies with deteriorating fundamentals
Net Exposure
Dedicated short: 60%-120% short; Short-biased: 30%-60% net short
Leverage
Low — sufficient natural volatility without additional leverage
Liquidity
Liquid but best in LP structure (operational complexity of short selling)
Correlation
Negatively correlated with equity markets — key portfolio diversification benefit
Key Risk
Short squeeze; secular equity uptrend; borrow unavailability; rising collateral
costs

Short-Selling Mechanics
Borrow securities → sell at current price → repurchase (cover) at lower price → return to lender → profit = sell
price minus buy price minus borrow cost. Must post collateral; must pay stock loan fee (higher for 'special' hard-
to-borrow names).

Risk
Description
Short Squeeze
If many shorts, rising prices force covering → accelerates price rise.
High short-interest + hard-to-borrow = danger signal.

Position Growth
Unlike longs (positions shrink as price falls), short positions GROW as
price rises — harder risk management.
Borrow Recall
Lender may demand shares back before thesis plays out.
Alt Uptick Rule (US)
If stock drops >10%, short sales can only execute at prices above best
bid — limits ability to short falling stocks.
Negative Carry
Borrow cost + dividends paid to lender reduce profit.

Tools for Identifying Short Candidates
•
Altman Z-score: measures bankruptcy probability
•
Beneish M-score: identifies potentially fraudulent financial statements
•
Single-name CDS spread widening; bond yield spread expansion
•
Implied volatility of exchange-traded put options
•
Technical analysis / pattern recognition for timing

Exam Trap — Short Bias

!
High short-interest ratio + high borrow cost ('on special') = SHORT SQUEEZE risk. Even a
fundamentally strong short thesis should be AVOIDED if these conditions apply. Consider using
put options instead (Example 2 logic).

heading: Equity Market Neutral (EMN)
body:
Equity Market Neutral — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Paired stock selection (long undervalued / short overvalued within same factor
exposure)
Net Exposure
~Zero market beta; often also sector-neutral, size-neutral, style-neutral
Leverage
HIGH — up to 300% long / 300% short (prime broker portfolio margin); beta
hedged away so leverage justified
Liquidity
Must be LP structure (too levered for mutual fund regulatory limits)
Benchmarks
HFRX/HFRI Equity Market Neutral Index; Credit Suisse EMN Index
Key Risk
Prime broker forced deleveraging at excess drawdown; quant crowding
Best Period
Non-trending or declining markets; when fixed income yields unattractive

EMN Sub-Types
Type
Description
Pairs Trading
Long undervalued / short overvalued pair of co-integrated securities;
profit from mean reversion of spread

Stub Trading
Buy/sell parent company and subsidiaries weighted by ownership
stake; profit from historical mispricing
Multi-Class Trading
Buy/sell different share classes (voting vs non-voting) of same
company when relative price dislocates
Statistical Arbitrage
Very short time horizon; algorithmic; exploits microstructure mean-
reversion and momentum anomalies
Capital Structure Arb
Long/short equity vs. bonds of same issuer when relative pricing
dislocates (event-driven overlap)

EMN Portfolio Construction — Four Steps
Step 1: Screen investment universe — only tradable, liquid, shortable securities.
Step 2: Rank securities on fundamental or statistical models for long/short candidates.
Step 3: Construct portfolio with market value-weighted beta ≈ 0; often also sector-neutral, dollar-neutral.
Step 4: Evaluate leverage availability and cost vs. desired return; set rebalancing hurdle rates to contain
transaction costs.

EMN Exam Calculation — Beta-Weighted Pairs Trade

Short Position $
Long $ × (β_Long / β_Short) = Beta-Weighted Short

Example (from curriculum): Long KO $1,000,000 (β=0.55); Short PEP to match beta. PEP β=0.65. Short PEP =
$1,000,000 × (0.55/0.65) = $846,154. Combined portfolio beta = $1,000,000 × 0.55 - $846,154 × 0.65 ≈ 0.

!
EMN does NOT mean zero leverage. It means zero net market BETA. Most EMN managers
apply very HIGH leverage (up to 6:1) precisely because beta risk is hedged away. The high
leverage is what makes the strategy dangerous during market stress.

heading: Merger Arbitrage
body:
Merger Arbitrage — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Spread between current target price and acquisition price; captures insurance
premium for deal risk

Deal Types
Cash-for-stock (long target only) vs. stock-for-stock (long target + short acquirer
in offer ratio)
Typical Spread
3%-7% per deal; 7%-12% annualised net after recycling capital and leverage
Leverage
Moderate to high — typically 3-5x to generate meaningful returns
Liquidity
Relatively liquid (public equities); preferred LP structure for leverage
Historical Success
70%-90% of announced mergers close successfully in US
Time to Close
Typically 3-4 months; capital recycled into new deals

Payoff Profile
Merger arbitrage resembles: LONG RISKLESS BOND + SHORT PUT OPTION. If deal succeeds → collect
spread (like bond coupon). If deal fails → suffer large loss on long/short positions (like put being exercised).
Additional free 'White Knight call option' if a higher competing bid emerges.

Merger Arbitrage Worked Example (Exam-Style)

Stock-for-Stock
Payoff
Profit = [Post-announcement prices relative to offer
ratio] × shares

Setup: A ($45/share) offers 1:2 ratio for T. T was $15; jumps to $19. A falls to $42. Manager buys 20,000 T
($380,000) and shorts 10,000 A ($420,000). Net spread = $40,000.
Scenario
P&L
Deal Succeeds
+$40,000 net spread (long T converges to offer value; short A covered
at offer-adjusted price)
Deal Fails
-$110,000 [A: $420K received - $450K to cover = -$30K; T: -$380K
cost + $300K value at $15 = -$80K. Total: -$110K]
White Knight
Additional upside if higher bid emerges — free call option for the
arbitrageur

Sources of Deal Failure Risk
•
Regulatory / anti-trust rejection (especially cross-border or vertical integration deals)
•
Financing failure — acquirer cannot secure funding
•
Failed financial due diligence
•
Hostile deals — lower completion probability than friendly deals
•
Market stress — deals most likely to fail when broader markets are falling (left-tail equity correlation)

!
Merger arb looks like a short put option — steady income with OCCASIONAL large losses. The
strategy has LEFT-TAIL RISK: deal failures in stress periods can cause 20%-40% losses on
positions. This is why leverage must be managed carefully.

heading: Distressed Securities
body:
Distressed Securities — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Illiquidity premium + institutional seller constraints create mispricing of
financially distressed firms
Return Profile
Higher end of event-driven spectrum but lumpy and cyclical; most attractive in
early economic recovery
Leverage
Low to moderate: 1.2x-1.7x NAV — inherent volatility limits use of leverage
Liquidity
LOW — illiquid securities; Level 3 mark-to-model; extended lock-ups required
(often 2+ years)
Approach Types
Passive (rely on others to negotiate) vs. Active (build control position, join
creditor committees)
Key Skill
Legal/bankruptcy analysis; creditor committee dynamics; capital structure
recovery modelling

Bankruptcy Outcomes
Outcome
Manager Focus
Liquidation
Estimate recovery values for each class of claimant (senior secured →
junior secured → unsecured → convertible → preferred → common).
Buy if recovery estimate > current market price.
Reorganisation
Evaluate enterprise value and future claim values post-restructuring.
Identify FULCRUM SECURITIES — partially in-the-money claims
whose holders end up owning the reorganised entity.

Fulcrum Securities — Key Concept
Fulcrum securities are the claims that are PARTIALLY in-the-money in a restructuring — not fully repaid, so their
holders receive new equity in the reorganised firm. The hedge fund manager who identifies and buys the fulcrum
security at a discount can profit substantially if the reorganised company recovers.

Capital Structure Arbitrage
In the same distressed entity: go long securities where recovery prospects are good (e.g., senior unsecured debt
priced below recovery value) AND short other securities (including equity) where recovery is dim. Example: E&P
companies in 2015-2016 energy crisis — junior debt priced for bankruptcy while retail equity investors were
complacent → buy debt + short equity or buy put options on equity.

!
Distressed investing is typically LONG-BIASED. It is not like merger arb (which can be short).
The hedge comes from short equity or buying puts as a hedge, NOT from a primary short
position in the distressed securities themselves.

heading: Fixed-Income Arbitrage
body:
Fixed-Income Arbitrage — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Relative mispricing of debt securities from differences in duration, credit quality,
liquidity, or optionality
Leverage
HIGH — 4x-5x typical; up to 12x-15x in market-neutral multi-strategy funds
Liquidity
Variable — on-the-run govts very liquid; corporates/off-the-run/structured
products much less so
Duration
Typically duration-neutral (immunised against small parallel rate shifts);
derivatives used for larger/non-parallel shifts
Payoff Profile
Like a SHORT PUT — steady positive carry in normal markets; large losses
when spreads unexpectedly widen
Key Risk
Leverage magnification; illiquidity at worst times; structured product complexity;
margin calls force selling

Common Fixed-Income Arb Strategies
Strategy
Description
Yield Curve / Calendar Spread
Long and short at different points on yield curve where relative
mispricing is best (curve steepening or flattening plays)
Carry Trade
Long higher-yielding security + short lower-yielding duration-matched
security. Earn positive carry as spread reverts to normal.
On-the-Run / Off-the-Run
Classic trade: Buy off-the-run (cheaper, less liquid) + short on-the-run
(more expensive, more liquid). Same duration/credit. Earn the liquidity
premium as spreads normalise.
L/S Credit
More volatile version; accepts relative credit risks across different
issuers, not just sovereign.
Structured Products Arb
Exploit mispricing in tranched MBS/ABS products. Complex; negative
convexity risk; high model dependence.

TIPS vs. Treasury Arbitrage Example (Curriculum)
In extreme distress: TIPS (inflation-adjusted) yielding significantly more than Treasuries + inflation swap at
deflation-implied pricing creates near risk-free arbitrage: Buy TIPS + Receive fixed on inflation swap + Short
Treasury = locked-in positive carry of ~254 bps.
Key execution risks: Credit line contraction during stress, inability to borrow Treasuries for short, brief window
before arbitrage closes.


!
On-the-run/off-the-run arb interest rate risk IS hedged (same duration). But LIQUIDITY RISK
remains — the off-the-run bond is cheaper because it is less liquid. The spread often
temporarily widens before converging → can trigger margin calls before profit is realised. This
is LTCM's exact failure mode.

heading: Convertible Bond Arbitrage
body:
Convertible Bond Arbitrage — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Embedded equity call option in convertible bonds typically trades at LOWER
implied vol than standalone options on same stock → buy cheap vol
Classic Trade
Buy convertible bond + Short delta-adjusted amount of underlying stock = long
gamma/vega position
Leverage
HIGH — typically 300% long / 200% short (lower short because delta < 1)
Liquidity
LOW — convertibles are thin, non-rated, complex securities; borrow for short
can be difficult
Best When
High convertible issuance (cheap pricing), moderate vol, good market liquidity
Worst When
Credit weakness + illiquidity + redemption pressure — all converge in crises

Convertible Bond Mechanics
Convertible = Straight Bond + Long Equity Call Option. Delta = sensitivity of convertible to stock price. If stock >>
conversion price (ITM call): delta ≈ 1, behaves like stock. If stock << conversion price (OTM call): delta ≈ 0,
behaves like bond.

Term
Definition
Conversion Ratio
Number of shares received per bond on conversion
Conversion Value
Current stock price × conversion ratio (intrinsic equity value)
Conversion Price
Convertible bond price / conversion ratio (threshold stock price for
ATM)
Delta Hedge
Short (conversion ratio × delta) shares per bond to remain delta-neutral
Gamma Trading
As stock rises, delta rises → sell more shares (at higher price). As
stock falls, delta falls → buy back shares (at lower price). Locks in
profit from volatility.

Convertible Arb Worked Example
QXR Conv Bond: Price 120% of par = €1,200; Conversion ratio = 50; Conversion price = €1,200/50 = €24. Stock
price = €30 (stock overvalued: 50% higher P/E than peers). Trade: Buy conv bond at €1,200 + Short 50 shares ×
delta. Profit locked at €6/share under any terminal price.
With carry adjustments: +€50 coupon (€1/share equiv) - €2 borrow cost - €1 dividend = net adjustment of -
€2/share. Total profit = €6 - €2 = €4/share.


!
Convertible arb earns from REALIZED vol > IMPLIED vol in the convertible's embedded option.
If realized volatility is low and implied vol declines (vol compression), the strategy loses money
even without any credit event. Also: convertibles are marked-to-model in illiquid markets →
return smoothing.

heading: Global Macro
body:
Global Macro — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Top-down directional or relative value views on interest rates, currencies,
equities, commodities across global markets
Approach
Discretionary (most) or systematic; fundamental and/or technical analysis
Instruments
Futures, forwards, swaps, options on sovereign debt, equity indices, currencies,
commodities; also single stocks/bonds
Leverage
HIGH — margin-to-equity of 15%-25% allows controlling 6-7x face value of
assets
Liquidity
Generally liquid instruments; some crowding effects
Best Environment
Trending, volatile markets; regime changes; rate/currency dislocations
Worst Environment
Low volatility, range-bound, central bank-suppressed markets (e.g., 2011-2018)
Nature
Heterogeneous — managers vary widely in style and risk exposure

Key Global Macro Approaches
Approach
Description
Directional
Buy undervalued / sell overvalued asset based on macro fundamental
model (e.g., buy EM exporter stocks on currency depreciation)
Relative Value
Long one asset / short another based on macro mispricing (e.g., long
Italy equity / short Germany equity on eurozone divergence)
Carry Trade
Long high-yielding / short low-yielding currency or rate instrument
Thematic
Position around a specific macro theme (e.g., subprime crisis CDS
positions in 2006-2007; Brexit winners/losers)

Exam Trap — Global Macro vs. Managed Futures

!
Global macro is MORE ANTICIPATORY than managed futures. Macro managers often position
BEFORE trends develop — they can be EARLY. Managed futures is REACTIVE — follows

trends after they emerge. Macro is more heterogeneous; managed futures more systematic and
consistent.

heading: Managed Futures (CTA)
body:
Managed Futures — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Pattern recognition: momentum/trend signals across futures markets globally;
time-series and cross-sectional approaches
Instruments
Futures and options on futures (equity indices, fixed income, currencies,
commodities, metals); capital-efficient leverage
Leverage
HIGH — futures margin 0.1%-10% of notional vs 50% for equity; 85%-90% of
capital in T-bills as collateral
Liquidity
VERY HIGH — futures among most liquid instruments globally; 24-hour trading
Correlation Benefit
Positive right-tail skewness in market stress — profits from short equity/long
bond futures during crises
Best Environment
Strongly trending markets across multiple asset classes (2007-2009, 2022)
Worst Environment
Range-bound, low-volatility, mean-reverting markets (2011-2018)

Time-Series vs. Cross-Sectional Momentum
Strategy
Logic / Net Exposure
Time-Series Momentum (TSM)
Go long assets with POSITIVE trailing returns; short assets with
NEGATIVE trailing returns. Based on absolute performance. Can be
net long ALL assets or net short ALL assets simultaneously. Higher
volatility but better crisis diversification.
Cross-Sectional Momentum
(CSM)
Go long BEST relative performers within an asset class; short WORST
relative performers. Always quasi-market-neutral within the asset class.
Lower volatility but less diversifying in crises.

Managed Futures Capital Structure
Futures require only 0.1%-10% initial margin (vs 50% equity margin). Therefore: 85%-90% of fund capital sits in
short-term government bonds earning T-bill return. Only 10%-15% posted as margin for long/short futures
contracts. Result: total return = T-bill return + futures alpha ± commodity carry.

Managed Futures vs. Global Macro Comparison

Dimension
Managed Futures
Global Macro
Implementation
Systematic (rules-based, algorithm-
driven)
Discretionary (most); some
systematic
Timing
Reactive — follows trends after
they develop
Anticipatory — positions before
trends emerge

Consistency
More consistent; same system
across periods
Heterogeneous — managers differ
widely
Crowding
Herding risk as AUM grew; slippage
on reversals
Less crowding due to
heterogeneous approaches
Crisis Skewness
Strong positive skewness in crises
(trend follows equity sell-off)
Similar but more variable by
manager
Benchmark
HFRX/HFRI Macro Systematic;
CISDM CTA Equal-Weighted
HFRX/HFRI Macro Discretionary;
Credit Suisse Global Macro

heading: Volatility Trading
body:
Volatility Trading — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Buying cheap implied volatility / selling expensive implied volatility; gamma
trading profits from realized vol > implied vol
Instruments
Exchange options, OTC options, VIX futures, variance swaps, volatility swaps
Correlation
Long vol ~80% negatively correlated with equity returns → natural equity hedge
Leverage
Natural convexity of long options = asymmetric payoff with limited downside
premium
Liquidity
VIX futures/exchange options = liquid; OTC = illiquid, counterparty risk; ISDA
required
Benchmark
CBOE Eurekahedge Long Vol Index, Short Vol Index, Relative Value Vol Index,
Tail Risk Index

Volatility Instruments
Instrument
Characteristics
Exchange-traded options
Most liquid; up to ~2 years tenor; requires active delta hedging to
isolate vol exposure; options have theta decay and gamma
OTC options
Customisable tenor and strikes; less liquid; counterparty risk; ISDA
documentation required; min AUM ~$100M for access
VIX futures
Pure vol expression without delta hedging need; subject to mean
reversion; steep term structure creates roll-down drag for long vol
Variance swap
Forward on REALIZED variance; OTC; 'pure' vol exposure; no delta
hedging needed; squared return so large moves disproportionately
impact
Volatility swap
Forward on REALIZED volatility; OTC; also 'pure' vol; strike = fair
volatility at inception; payoff at expiry based on realized vol vs strike


Key Volatility Concepts
Vol Term Structure: normally upward sloping (higher implied vol for longer dated) but INVERTS in crises (near-
term fear spikes).
Vol Smile: OTM options trade at higher implied vol than ATM options.
Vol Skew: OTM puts often trade at higher implied vol than OTM calls (downside protection demand).
Gamma Trading: Long option buyer rebalances delta-hedge by selling stock on rallies and buying on dips →
profits if realized vol > implied vol of option.

!
Long volatility strategies suffer from THETA DECAY (time value erodes daily) and VOL TERM
STRUCTURE ROLL-DOWN (as time passes, longer-dated options become shorter-dated at
lower implied vol). Must actively gamma trade or realize gains during vol spikes before expiry.

heading: Reinsurance / Life Settlements
body:
Reinsurance / Life Settlements — Key Profile
Alpha Source
Actuarial skill in estimating mortality/catastrophe risk better than market
consensus → earn insurance premium
Correlation
UNCORRELATED with financial markets — pure biometric/geophysical risk
Instruments
Life settlement pools; catastrophe bonds; catastrophe risk futures; reinsurance
contracts
Key Risk
Mortality estimation error; long tail on payouts; catastrophe model error;
geographic concentration
Life Settlements
Buy life policies from policyholders (via brokers) at > surrender value; pay
ongoing premiums; collect death benefits. Profit if PV(benefit) > PV(premiums +
purchase price).
Cat Reinsurance
Provide reinsurance capacity above insurer threshold; earn premiums; seasonal
(North American hurricane season)

heading: Fund-of-Funds (FoF)
body:
Fund-of-Funds — Key Profile
Structure
FoF manager allocates to 15-20+ separate, underlying hedge funds across
different strategies
Advantage
Diversification; access to closed funds; due diligence expertise; economies of
scale; lower min investment (~$100K vs $1M direct)
Disadvantage
DOUBLE fee layer; netting risk borne by investor; slower tactical reallocation;
less transparency into underlying PMs

Fees (Historical)
Underlying: 1%-2% + 10%-20%; FoF adds: 1% + 10%. Total fee drag ~39%-
42% of gross returns
Fees (Current Trend)
FoF fees compressing: 50 bps + 5% incentive OR flat 1% management
common now
Netting Risk
Investor pays incentive fees on winning funds while suffering drag from losing
funds — even if overall FoF return is flat
Liquidity
1-year initial lockup; monthly/quarterly thereafter with 30-60 day notice; may
face liquidity squeeze if underlying funds have longer lockups

FoF Fee Calculation (Exam-Style)

FoF Net Return
= Gross Return - Mgmt Fee - Incentive Fee (on net of
mgmt fee) at each layer

Both funds earn 20% gross; each charges 2% + 20% incentive. Net per underlying fund = 20% - 2% - (20% ×
[20%-2%]) = 20% - 2% - 3.6% = 14.4%. FoF charges 1% + 10%. FoF net = 14.4% - 1% - 10% × (14.4%-1%) =

heading: % - 1% - 1.34% = 12.06%. Fee drag = 39.7% of gross 20% return.
body:
Asymmetric case: Fund A earns 20% gross (net = 14.4%); Fund B loses 5% (net = -7%). FoF gross = 0.5×14.4%
+ 0.5×(-7%) = 3.7%. FoF net = 3.7% - 1% - 0.27% = 2.43%. Fee drag = 67.6% of gross 7.5% blended return —
fee netting risk in action.

heading: Multi-Strategy Funds
body:
Multi-Strategy — Key Profile
Structure
Multiple PM teams running different strategies under one fund umbrella; shared
ops and risk systems
Key Advantage over
FoF
Faster tactical reallocation (in-house); full position transparency; GP absorbs
netting risk (investor-friendly fees)
Fees
GP absorbs netting risk — investors pay incentive fee only on TOTAL fund
performance after netting all teams
Drawback
Higher leverage; more concentrated operational risk; team talent constraints;
occasional left-tail blow-ups (e.g., Ritchie Capital 2005)
Empirical
Multi-strategy outperforms FoF historically on mean returns and Sharpe/Sortino;
BUT higher Rho (serial autocorrelation) → illiquidity signal; investor/fund-level
redemption gates common
Leverage
Significantly more than FoF → more vulnerable to left-tail blow-up in stress

FoF vs. Multi-Strategy Comparison Table

Dimension
Fund-of-Funds
Multi-Strategy
Fee layers
Double (underlying + FoF)
Single effective layer; GP absorbs
netting risk

Netting risk
Borne by investor
Borne by GP (investor-friendly)
Strategy diversity
High (separate underlying funds)
Limited to in-house talent
Transparency
Low — underlying managers
opaque
High — all internal
Tactical speed
Slow — must redeem/invest
through fund cycles
Fast — direct internal reallocation
Leverage
Modest
High — prone to blow-up risk in
stress
Operational risk
Diversified across many funds
Concentrated — all under one
structure
Performance
Lower historical returns and Sharpe
Higher historical returns and
Sharpe
Redemption gates
Less common at FoF level
Common investor/fund-level gates

heading: Why a Conditional Factor Model?
body:
A conditional (two-state) model distinguishes NORMAL market periods from CRISIS periods. This is critical
because: (1) hedge funds often appear to have low/no exposure to certain risks during normal markets — but
these exposures emerge dramatically during crises; and (2) many funds failed during 2007-2009 GFC because
their actual crisis-period risk exposures were unknown. Post-crisis, industry AUM fell by 17% CAGR from $2.6T
peak.

The Model Formula

Model
R(HFi)t = α + β1(F1) + β2(F2) + β3(F3) + β4(F4)
[normal] + Dt×[δ1(F1) + δ2(F2) + δ3(F3) + δ4(F4)]
[crisis incremental] + ε

Dt = 1 during financial crisis (June 2007 – Feb 2009); Dt = 0 otherwise. Crisis period betas are INCREMENTAL —
total crisis exposure = normal beta + incremental crisis beta.

heading: The Four Risk Factors
body:
Factor
Definition
SNP500 (Equity Risk)
Monthly total return of S&P 500 Index (incl. dividends). Positive beta =
long equity exposure.

CREDIT (Credit Risk)
Change in Moody's Baa-Aaa corporate bond yield spread. POSITIVE
CREDIT = widening spreads = bad for credit. Negative beta = short
credit (benefits from narrowing spreads or deteriorating credit).
USD (Currency Risk)
Monthly return of US Dollar Index. USD appreciates in crises (flight to
safety). Positive DUSD in crisis = long USD = defensive.
VIX (Volatility Risk)
First difference of end-of-month CBOE VIX. VIX spikes in crises.
Positive DVIX = long vol = defensive/protective.

Note: BOND (corporate bond return) and CMDTY (Goldman Sachs Commodity Index) were dropped from the
final model due to multi-collinearity with CREDIT and SNP500 respectively.

heading: Key Findings by Strategy
body:
Strategy Category
Risk Factor Findings
L/S Equity
Significant positive SNP500 normal-period beta (0.24-0.58 by region).
Total crisis beta remains positive but managers partially deleveraged.
Little significant incremental exposure.
Equity Market Neutral
Low but significant positive SNP500 (0.09-0.11). Neutral to CREDIT
and VIX in both periods. As expected — truly market neutral.
Dedicated Short Bias
Significant negative SNP500 (-0.28 to -0.46). Also significant negative
VIX — implies some short volatility component (selling puts against
shorts).

Fund-of-Funds
Mostly positive SNP500 (0.14-0.33 except macro/systematic FoF). In
crises: significant negative USD and VIX exposure emerging —
seemingly undesirable (unintended).
Multi-Strategy
Higher serial autocorrelation (Rho >20%) → illiquidity. Strong equity
exposure but heterogeneous sign across databases. Crisis period:
unexpected negative VIX for majority.

!
In crisis periods, many multi-manager funds showed NEGATIVE VIX exposure (short vol) —
exactly the wrong position when vol spikes. This emerged even though these funds had no
significant VIX exposure in normal markets. This is the core lesson of the conditional factor
model: normal-period betas are insufficient for risk assessment.

heading: REIT Qualification Requirements
body:
To qualify as a REIT in most countries, a company must meet minimum thresholds. These restrictions
prevent individuals or small groups from using REIT structures for single assets:
• Distribute 90%–100% of otherwise taxable earnings to shareholders as dividends
• Invest at least 75% of total assets in real estate
• Derive at least 75% of gross income from real estate rental income or mortgage interest
• US-specific: At least 100 shareholders; no five or fewer shareholders may own more than 50% of shares
(the '5/50 rule')
• Countries may also specify: maximum asset concentration, maximum non-rental income, maximum
development activity, leverage limits

Self-managed (internal)
Senior executives are company employees reporting to board elected by
shareholders. Most US REITs. Fewer conflicts of interest — management
incentives aligned with shareholders.
Externally
advised/managed
Third-party adviser paid asset management fees, often based on AUM
(incentive to grow size, not profitability). External managers may also
charge for affiliated services (property management, acquisitions, debt
placement). Higher conflict of interest.

2. Valuation: Net Asset Value (NAV) Approach

heading: Why NAVPS, Not Book Value?
body:
Historical cost accounting (US GAAP) distorts real estate valuations in two specific ways:
• Understates carrying values: Long-held properties often appreciate in value (due to inflation or property-
specific factors) but remain on the balance sheet at depreciated cost.
• Overstates depreciation: Companies often use accelerated depreciation for tax purposes, further
depressing accounting book value below economic value.


IFRS offers companies a choice — the cost model (identical to PP&E) or the fair value model (all
changes in fair value flow through net income). Under the fair value model, accounting values approach
NAV. Under US GAAP historical cost, adjustment to NAV is always needed.

NAVPS Definition: (Market Value of Assets − Market Value of Liabilities) / Shares
Outstanding
NAV = book value of assets REPLACED with market values, adjusted for:
Included in NAV: Value of operating real estate (capitalised NOI) + cash + accounts
receivable + land for development + prepaid/other tangible assets + non-asset-based
income streams (fee/management income) + value added by management
Excluded from NAV: Goodwill, deferred financing costs, deferred tax assets ('soft' assets),
deferred tax liabilities ('soft' liabilities). These are accounting provisions, not economic items.

heading: Step-by-Step NAVPS Calculation
body:
The standard analyst process (illustrated in Exhibit 2 of the curriculum — Office REIT example):
Step
What to Do
Step 1:
Calculate
Pro Forma
Cash NOI
Last 12-month NOI − Non-cash (straight-line) rent + Adjustment for full-year impact of
mid-year acquisitions → Pro Forma Cash NOI
Step 2:
Estimate
Next-12-
Month Cash
NOI
Pro Forma Cash NOI × (1 + growth rate). Growth estimated at ~1–3% based on
analyst view.
Step 3:
Capitalise to
get RE
Value
Estimated next-12-month cash NOI / Cap Rate = Estimated Value of Operating Real
Estate. Cap rate derived from recent comparable property transactions.
Step 4: Add
other
tangible
assets
+ Cash & equivalents + Accounts receivable + Land held for future development (often
at book if market value uncertain) + Prepaid/other tangible assets
Step 5:
Subtract
liabilities
− Total debt (at market value if materially different from face) − Other liabilities. Exclude
deferred tax liabilities.
Step 6:
Divide by
shares
NAV / Shares Outstanding = NAVPS

Worked Example — Exhibit 2 (Office REIT, USD thousands)

Last-12-month NOI
$270,432
Less: Non-cash (straight-line) rent
($7,667) — removes accounting fiction of averaged rent
Plus: Full-year acquisition adjustment
$4,534 — adds incremental 50% of annual return on mid-
year acquisitions
= Pro Forma Cash NOI (last 12 months)
$267,299
Plus: Next-12-month growth at 1.5%
$4,009
= Estimated Next-12-Month Cash NOI
$271,308
÷ Cap rate (7.00%)
$271,308 / 0.07 = $3,875,829
+ Cash and equivalents
$65,554

NAVPS = $52.33

Straight-line rent: Under IFRS/GAAP, companies recognise the AVERAGE contractual rent
over the entire lease term, not actual cash received. In early years of a step-up lease, cash
rent < accounting revenue; in later years, cash rent > accounting revenue. The straight-line
adjustment removes this non-cash recognition so NOI reflects actual cash received — which
is what matters for capitalisation.

heading: NAVPS Application and Premium/Discount Analysis
body:
NAV is primarily used in Europe and Asia. US analysts more commonly use FFO/AFFO multiples.

How to Interpret Premium/Discount to NAV
Scenario
Interpretation
Trading AT NAV (P/NAV
= 1×)
Market values the REIT at its underlying property value. Neutral signal.
Trading at PREMIUM to
NAV
Higher NAV may be warranted if: (1) public market liquidity commands a
lower required return; (2) management is creating above-average value
(track record of superior acquisitions/developments); (3) access to capital
at lower cost. Can range >25% premium at peak cycles.

Scenario
Interpretation
Trading at DISCOUNT to
NAV
May signal: undervaluation opportunity, governance concerns, high
leverage, unfavourable asset mix, or anticipated NOI decline. Can range
>25% discount in downturns.
Practical use as relative
tool
If all REITs trade above NAV, compare P/NAV ratios — buy the smallest
premium. If all trade at discount, sell the smallest discount. Also use
implied cap rates: work backward from current stock price to solve for what
cap rate the market is implying.

Key limitations of NAV: (1) NAV is static/point-in-time — ignores management's future
value creation potential. (2) Becomes subjective when markets are illiquid and few
transactions are observable. (3) Large portfolios (hundreds of properties) are difficult to value
individually. (4) Assets like undeveloped land, special-use properties, JVs, and service
businesses complicate NAV accuracy.

3. FFO, AFFO, and Relative Valuation (Price Multiples)

heading: Why Not Use Net Income or EPS for REITs?
body:
Net income for REITs is distorted by two major accounting adjustments that do not reflect economic
reality:
• Depreciation: GAAP/IFRS depreciate buildings over their useful life. But real estate often APPRECIATES
in value. Depreciation deductions are economically misleading for income-producing property — they
overstate the expense and understate true earnings.
• Gains/losses on property sales: These are non-recurring. Including them in 'income' creates volatility that
doesn't reflect sustainable operating performance.

heading: Funds from Operations (FFO)
body:
FFO formula:  FFO = Net Income + Depreciation & Amortisation − Net Gains on Sale of
Real Property
More complete Nareit definition: Net income (GAAP) + Losses (−Gains) from property sales
+ D&A on real estate + real estate impairments/write-downs unrelated to depreciation

• Standardised by Nareit. SEC-accepted non-GAAP measure (like EBITDA) — must be reconciled to
GAAP net income.
• Widely used in US. Increasingly used in Asia Pacific. Europe/Asia still prefer NAV as primary.
• FFO is NOT a cash flow measure — excludes investment and financing activities. Also includes FFO
from unconsolidated businesses.
• P/FFO is the REIT equivalent of P/E for regular equities.

• FFO still includes straight-line rent (non-cash) and does not adjust for maintenance capex — it is the
starting point, not the endpoint for REIT income analysis.

heading: Adjusted Funds from Operations (AFFO)
body:
AFFO formula:  AFFO = FFO − Non-cash (Straight-Line) Rent − Recurring Maintenance
Capex & Leasing Commissions
Also called: Funds Available for Distribution (FAD) or Cash Available for Distribution.

• Removes straight-line rent: The non-cash portion of rental revenue (accounting fiction). Cash rent ≠
reported rent when leases have step-ups. This difference is the straight-line adjustment.
• Removes recurring maintenance capex: Capital expenditures needed to MAINTAIN (not improve)
properties — leasing commissions, roof/parking repairs, basic tenant buildouts. These costs must be
incurred to preserve income-generating ability.
• AFFO is superior to FFO as a measure of current economic income and dividend-paying capacity.
• AFFO is more variable and harder to estimate precisely (timing of capex and lease expirations is lumpy
year to year).
• AFFO computation methodologies vary by company — no universally accepted standard.
Bloomberg/Refinitiv do NOT compile AFFO estimates because of this inconsistency. FFO estimates are
readily available; AFFO less so.
• P/AFFO is a 'cash flow' multiple — a quick approximation of what the market is paying per dollar of true
cash earnings.

body:
Net income

+ Depreciation & amortisation

+ Losses on sale of depreciable RE
$25,000 (adding back losses = gains would be
subtracted)
= FFO

($21,103)

commissions

Reversing AFFO to FFO (exam-critical): FFO = AFFO + Non-cash rent + Recurring capex
+ Leasing commissions. The exam often gives AFFO and asks for FFO per share. Work
backward: add back the straight-line rent and maintenance capex that were subtracted. Then
divide by shares outstanding.

heading: P/FFO and P/AFFO: Drivers and Limitations
body:
What Drives Multiples Higher/Lower
Driver
Effect on Multiple
Expected FFO/AFFO
growth
Higher expected growth → higher multiple. Driven by: business model
(development-focused REITs), geography (supply-constrained prime markets
like NYC/London give more pricing power), management skill, lease structure.
Property risk
Cash flow volatility by asset type, quality, age, lease structure. Apartments =
lower variability than hotels → apartment REITs trade at higher multiples than
hotel REITs. Young, well-maintained portfolios trade at premium to older,
deferred-maintenance properties.
Capital structure risk
Higher financial leverage → lower FFO/AFFO multiples (equity riskier). High
leverage also constrains incremental borrowing and may create stock
overhang if equity offerings anticipated.

P/FFO & P/AFFO Advantages
P/FFO & P/AFFO Disadvantages
1. Widely accepted across global equity markets
— allows cross-industry context
2. FFO estimates readily available from
Bloomberg/Refinitiv → easy P/FFO calculation
3. Can be combined with leverage and growth
data to deepen relative analysis
1. May miss non-income assets: Land for
development, vacant buildings, properties under
development, assets with below-market rents
2. P/FFO ignores recurring capex impact
(P/AFFO addresses this but assumptions vary
widely)
3. One-time gains, accounting charges, new
revenue rules complicate comparisons between
companies

heading: EV/EBITDA
body:
Also used for REIT/REOC valuation — less common than P/FFO or P/AFFO but has one key
advantage: it measures pre-leverage income, making it a genuine like-for-like comparison across
companies with different capital structures.
The inverse (EBITDA/EV) closely approximates the real estate capitalisation rate formula (NOI/market
value) — so EV/EBITDA is conceptually the most aligned with how property investors actually think
about value.

4. Mini Case Study: Capitol Shopping Center REIT (CSC)

CSC is a fictitious retail shopping center REIT in the Washington DC area. This case covers all four
valuation methods. Key background:
• Defensive tenant mix: Grocery stores, drug stores (necessity retailers) — more resilient than
discretionary retail.
• Favourable market: DC's government employment base provides stability; dense zoning limits competing
new supply.
• Historical NOI growth: 2%–3% annually over past decade.
• Capital strategy: Target <50% debt/market cap (preference ~40%). Interest-only debt at 5.7% average.
• Dividend policy: ~80% AFFO payout — satisfies REIT requirements (which are based on taxable income,
lower than AFFO due to depreciation deductions), provides cushion for downturns.

heading: Valuation Method 1 — NAV Approach (for CSC)
body:
CSC's NAV is computed by capitalising cash NOI and netting against liabilities. The curriculum does
not compute a full CSC NAV in the exhibit, but the method is the same as Exhibit 2 above. Key point for
CSC:
• CSC has Land held for future development ($133,785K) — this represents internal growth potential not
captured in FFO/AFFO multiples. Land has no current income contribution, so it does not appear in NOI.
Analysts must add it at market value (adjusted if land values have appreciated from book).
• Shopping center REITs sector estimated trading at 7.6% premium to NAV; all REITs at 14.8% premium.
• NAV becomes LESS useful in negative economic environments when transaction volumes decline and
comparable data is scarce.

heading: Valuation Method 2 — P/FFO and P/AFFO Multiples
body:
Inputs: Stock price $69.85. Analyst consensus estimates: FFO/share = $4.23 (Yr3E), $4.59 (Yr4E).
AFFO/share = $3.76 (Yr3E), $4.09 (Yr4E).

P/FFO (Yr3E)
$69.85 / $4.23 = 16.5×
P/FFO (Yr4E)
$69.85 / $4.59 = 15.2×
P/AFFO (Yr3E)
$69.85 / $3.76 = 18.6×
P/AFFO (Yr4E)
$69.85 / $4.09 = 17.1×
vs Shopping centre peers
(Yr3E)
P/FFO: 16.5× vs 14.5× → CSC at 13.8% premium; P/AFFO: 18.6×
vs 16.1× → CSC at 15.5% premium
vs All REITs (Yr3E)
P/FFO: 16.5× vs 14.2× → CSC at 16.2% premium; P/AFFO: 18.6×
vs 16.5× → CSC at 12.7% premium

Why might CSC trade at a premium? (1) Defensive tenant mix (grocery-anchored) — less
FFO volatility than peers. (2) Advantaged market (DC government employment + zoning
constraints on new supply). (3) Lower leverage (40.5% vs 47.1% peers). (4) CSC maintained
positive FFO/AFFO growth while peers saw declines during the difficult Year 1–2 period. The
caveat: because CSC did not suffer the downturn, it may have less upside as conditions
normalise.

heading: Valuation Method 3 — DDM / DCF Approach
body:
REITs pay out most income as dividends → DDM is applicable. The curriculum uses a two-step DDM
for CSC. The same framework is used across all REIT practice problems.
Two-step DDM template:
Step 1 (explicit period): Discount each year's projected dividend. D₁/(1+r) + D₂/(1+r)² + ... +
Dₙ/(1+r)ⁿ
Step 2 (terminal value): At end of explicit period, Gordon Growth Model: TV = Dₙ₊₁ / (r − g).
This is the terminal VALUE at year n, then discount back: TV/(1+r)ⁿ
Discount rate: Often given as CAPM rate (r = rf + β × MRP) or stated explicitly. For CSC: rf
= 4.0%, MRP = 5.0%, β = 0.80 → r = 4.0 + 0.80 × 5.0 = 8.0%

Baldwin REIT DDM Example (from Practice Problems)
Baldwin: β = 0.90, rf = 4.0%, MRP = 5.0% → r = 4.0 + 0.90 × 5.0 = 8.5%. Dividends: Yr1 = $1.00, Yr2 =
$1.06. Long-term growth from Yr3 = 5.0%.
Terminal value at end of Yr2
D₃ / (r − g) = ($1.06 × 1.05) / (0.085 − 0.05) = $1.113 / 0.035 =
$31.71
PV calculation
PV = $1.00/(1.085) + ($1.06 + $31.71)/(1.085)² = $0.922 +
$32.77/1.177 = $0.922 + $27.84 = $28.76
Intrinsic value
$28.76 per share

REIT C DDM Example (from Practice Problems) — 3-Year Explicit Period

REIT C: r = 8%, D₁ = $4.00, g₂₋₃ = 4.5%, gₜₑᵣₘᵢₙₐₗ = 4.0%.
Dividends
D₁ = $4.00; D₂ = $4.00 × 1.045 = $4.18; D₃ = $4.18 × 1.045 = $4.37; D₄ =
$4.37 × 1.04 = $4.54
Terminal Value at end
of Yr3
TV = D₄ / (r − g∞) = $4.54 / (0.08 − 0.04) = $113.57
PV calculation
D₁/(1.08) + D₂/(1.08)² + (D₃ + TV)/(1.08)³ = $3.70 + $3.58 + ($4.37 +
$113.57)/1.2597 = $100.91

heading: Selecting Among Valuation Methods
body:
Method
When Most Appropriate
NAV approach
Best when: Active private market transactions exist for comparable properties.
Primary in Europe/Asia. Becomes unreliable when markets are illiquid and
few transactions are observable.
P/FFO multiple
Best for: Quick cross-sectional comparison among similar REITs. Forward
estimates widely available. Does not capture all asset value (non-income-
producing assets missed).
P/AFFO multiple
Better than P/FFO for economic income — accounts for maintenance capex.
Harder to standardise due to methodology differences. Reflects closer
approximation to sustainable dividend capacity.
DDM/DCF
Best when: Stable, predictable dividend streams. Can incorporate growth
assumptions and discount rate explicitly. Less useful when NOI is volatile or
dividend history is short.
In practice
Analysts often use all methods and triangulate. May weight by reliability.
Divergence between methods signals need to re-examine assumptions. Most
US REIT analysts lead with P/FFO.

heading: The Arbitrage Between Public and Private Valuations
body:
Sustained differences in public vs. private valuations create strategic opportunities:
• Public REITs trade well below NAV → REITs may go private or sell assets to private buyers (who value
properties at higher private market prices).
• Private RE values high → Private fund managers may exit via IPO into the public market at premium
pricing.
• Listed real estate can express short-term tactical views: When public markets are overly pessimistic on
retail and drive prices below NAV, listed RE represents a cheaper entry than buying properties directly.
