heading: Critical Exam Traps
body:
• FFO adds back DEPRECIATION but SUBTRACTS gains (adds back losses) on property sales. The sign
convention matters: gains are subtracted FROM net income, losses are added back. FFO = NI + D&A −
GAINS (not +gains).
• AFFO subtracts non-cash rent AND maintenance capex from FFO. When reversing (given AFFO, find
FFO), ADD both back. Do not also add depreciation — that was already done in the FFO step.
• Straight-line rent: non-cash rent REDUCES AFFO below FFO. It is the difference between accounting
revenue (average over lease) and actual cash rent. Positive in early years of step-up leases (cash <
accounting); negative in later years.
• NAVPS excludes deferred tax liabilities (soft liabilities — not real economic obligations). NAVPS also
excludes goodwill, deferred financing costs, and deferred tax assets.
• NAV cap rate comes from MARKET TRANSACTIONS for comparable properties — not from the required
rate of return (r − g). The cap rate used in NAV = direct market cap rate; the cap rate in direct
capitalisation valuation = r − g. These are conceptually related but come from different sources.
• Higher cap rate = LOWER NAV (NOI/cap rate: as denominator rises, value falls). Lower growth = higher
cap rate used = lower NAV.
• Trading BELOW NAV ≠ automatically buy. May reflect justified concerns: high leverage, management
quality, unfavourable asset type, poor governance.
• Trading ABOVE NAV may be justified by: liquidity premium (public markets demand lower return),
superior management track record.
• P/FFO and P/AFFO are based on LEVERED income (equity earnings after interest). EV/EBITDA is pre-
leverage. When comparing REITs with very different capital structures, EV/EBITDA gives cleaner
comparison.
• A REIT with lower cap rate (same P/NAV) has lower NOI relative to value → lower FFO → HIGHER
P/FFO. High cap rate REIT has more NOI per dollar of value → lower P/FFO (Q18 in question set: REIT
A at 6% cap → more expensive → higher P/FFO than REIT B at 8% cap).
• For DDM terminal value: the terminal dividend (Dₙ₊₁) = Dₙ × (1 + long-term growth rate). Then TV = Dₙ₊₁ /
(r − g). The total PV at year n = Dₙ + TVₙ (for discounting back).
• AFFO is NOT compiled by Bloomberg/Refinitiv — no universally accepted methodology. FFO and NAV
are widely available. Don't confuse which metric is most available vs. which is most accurate.
• Taxable REIT subsidiaries (TRS): REITs form these to engage in non-qualifying activities (development,
brokerage, property management). TRS earnings ARE taxed at corporate rates — eliminates the REIT
tax benefit for those activities.
• REIT vs REOC: The key tradeoff is tax vs. flexibility. REIT = no corporate tax, must distribute 90%+,
restricted to RE income. REOC = double-taxed, retains earnings freely, can do any RE activity. For
GROWTH-focused investors, REOC may be better despite tax disadvantage.
• Healthcare REITs (Baldwin) are defensively oriented — resistant to recessions, but exposed to
demographic changes (aging population = tailwind) and government funding changes
(Medicare/Medicaid cuts = risk).
• Self-storage REITs: Principal risk is COMPETITIVE NEW SUPPLY — ease of entry into self-storage
leads to periodic overbuilding. Month-to-month leases give pricing flexibility but increase demand
sensitivity.
• CSC land held for future development is non-income-producing → not captured in FFO/AFFO. Should be
valued separately in NAV and justifies a higher P/FFO multiple than peers with no development land.

heading: Practice Problem Calculation Templates
body:
Finding FFO per share when given AFFO
Shares outstanding = AFFO / AFFO per share
FFO = AFFO + Non-cash rent + Recurring capex + Leasing commissions
FFO per share = FFO / Shares outstanding
Example: AFFO = $4M, AFFO/share = $5.00 → Shares = 800,000. FFO = $4M + $215K +
$700K = $4.915M. FFO/share = $4.915M / 800,000 = $6.14. P/FFO = $80 / $6.14 = 13.0×

NAVPS Calculation
Step 1: NOI / Cap rate = Value of operating real estate
Step 2: + Cash + Accounts receivable + Other tangible assets
Step 3: − Debt (at market) − Other liabilities (exclude deferred taxes)
Step 4: ÷ Shares = NAVPS
Example: NOI = $115M, cap = 7% → RE value = $1,642.86M. + (Cash + AR) as given. −
Debt $505M. → NAV = $1,137.86M / 100M shares = $11.38

P/FFO or P/AFFO Valuation
FFO per share = FFO / Shares outstanding → Value = FFO/share × sector P/FFO multiple
AFFO per share = (FFO − Non-cash rent − Recurring capex) / Shares → Value =
AFFO/share × sector P/AFFO multiple
Example REIT B: FFO = $290,612, shares = 67,900 → FFO/share = $4.28. × 13.5× =
$57.78
Example REIT A (AFFO): FFO = $316,965 − $25,991 (non-cash rent) − $63,769 (capex) =
AFFO $227,205. /56,100 shares = $4.05/share. × 18.3× = $74.12

Two-Step DDM
1. Project explicit period dividends: D₁, D₂, ... Dₙ using given growth rates
2. Terminal value at end of year n: TVₙ = Dₙ₊₁ / (r − g∞) where Dₙ₊₁ = Dₙ × (1 + g∞)
3. Intrinsic value = Σ[Dₜ/(1+r)ᵗ] + TVₙ/(1+r)ⁿ
Common exam format: Year 1 and Year 2 dividends given explicitly, long-term growth from
Year 3. TV = D₃/(r − g∞). Total value = D₁/(1.08) + (D₂ + TV)/(1.08)². Note: TV is discounted
at end of Year 2, same period as D₂.

CFA Level II | Alternatives | Investments in Real Estate through Publicly Traded Securities | Study Notes

heading: General Characteristics
body:
Real estate involves heterogeneous assets in fragmented local markets that are bought and sold
infrequently with high transaction costs. Properties range from relatively stable, income-producing
core real estate to speculative opportunistic investments.

Risk–Return Spectrum (Exhibit 1 from text)
Category
Characteristics
Senior Debt
First mortgages, Investment Grade CMBS — lowest risk, debt-like return,
mostly public
Core
Stable income-producing properties, Diversified Public REITs, Credit
Sale/Leaseback — bond-like return
Core-Plus
Minimal refurbishment, tenant repositioning, cash flow stabilization
Value-Add
Vacant space, property upgrade/repositioning, Sub-Investment Grade CMBS
Opportunistic
New development, mezzanine debt, distressed situations — highest risk,
equity-like return, mostly private

Key characteristic driving return: The economic USE of the property — residential or commercial —
is the primary driver of expected income and price appreciation. Location, size, age, and available
amenities all affect individual property valuation.

heading: Net Operating Income (NOI) — Core Return Metric
body:
NOI is the primary income measure for income-producing property — analogous to EBITDA for
companies, but specifically for real estate. It is calculated before financing costs or income taxes.

Equation 1 — NOI:  NOI = Effective Gross Income − Operating Expenses − Property
Maintenance Allowance
Effective Gross Income = Gross Rent + Other Income + Pass-through Recoveries −
Vacancies − Concessions
Gross Rent = Base rent (per m²) × Rentable area
Operating Expenses: Fixed (taxes, insurance, service/repairs) + Variable (utilities). May be
borne by owner or passed through to tenants.
Property Maintenance Allowance: Allows for capital expenditures to MAINTAIN current
economic use only — NOT for upgrades or major improvements. Common in
European/North American markets; less so in Japan.

Worked Example — Wallonia Transit NOI (Example 1)
Single-tenant warehouse, 10,000 m², EUR 52.50/m², 5-year fully rented lease. Tenant covers
taxes/insurance. Owner recovers service/repairs up to EUR 102,500.
Gross Rent
EUR 52.50 × 10,000 = EUR 525,000
Operating Expense Recovery
EUR 10.25 × 10,000 = EUR 102,500
Pass-Through: Property Tax
EUR 12,500
Pass-Through: Insurance
EUR 40,000
Effective Gross Income
EUR 680,000
Less: Services & Repairs
(EUR 120,750)
Less: Property Tax
(EUR 12,500)
Less: Insurance
(EUR 40,000)
Total Operating Expenses
(EUR 173,250)
Less: Property Maintenance Allowance
(EUR 100,000)
Net Operating Income
EUR 406,750

Key trap: If service/repair costs rise 20% (to EUR 144,900), the OWNER bears the
incremental EUR 24,150 above the EUR 102,500 cap. NOI falls to EUR 382,600. Property
tax and insurance are fully passed through — cost increases there do NOT affect NOI.

heading: Leverage Measures — LTV and DSC
body:
Real estate is heavily leveraged — long-term secured debt using property as collateral. Two key credit
metrics:

Equation 2 — Loan-to-Value (LTV):  LTV = Mortgage Debt Outstanding / Current Property
Value
Lower LTV = higher equity position for borrower. LTV fluctuates as property values change
and principal is amortized.
Equation 3 — Debt Service Coverage (DSC):  DSC = NOI / Debt Service
Debt service = full installment payment (interest + principal). DSC > 1.0 means NOI covers
debt payments. Lenders impose minimum DSC covenants.

Wallonia Transit Leverage Example (Example 2)
Purchase: EUR 3,750,000. Mortgage: EUR 3,000,000, 20-year fully amortizing, 4% annual rate. Annual
payment: EUR 220,745 (Year 1 interest: EUR 120,000).
LTV at end of Year 1 (constant value)
Amortization = EUR 220,745 − EUR 120,000 = EUR 100,745.
Outstanding = EUR 3,000,000 − EUR 100,745 = EUR
2,899,255. LTV = 2,899,255 / 3,750,000 = 0.773
LTV with 10% property appreciation
New value = EUR 4,125,000. LTV = 2,899,255 / 4,125,000 =

heading: (leverage declines)
body:
DSC Year 1
NOI / Debt Service = 406,750 / 220,745 = 1.84×
DSC with higher costs (NOI = EUR
382,600)
382,600 / 220,745 = 1.73× — higher operating costs reduce
credit quality

heading: Equity Returns — Equity Dividend Rate & After-Tax Cash Flow
body:
Equation 4 — Equity Dividend Rate:  = Pre-tax Cash Flow / (Purchase Price − Mortgage
Loan)
Equation 5 — Pre-tax Cash Flow:  = NOI − Debt Service
Equation 6 — After-tax Cash Flow:  = Pre-tax Cash Flow − Taxes
Equation 7 — Taxes:  = t × (NOI − Interest Expense − Depreciation Expense)

Depreciation: Real estate is depreciated over its estimated useful life. The depreciable base = original
acquisition cost + improvements, EXCLUDING land cost (land has infinite life). Annual depreciation =
depreciable base / useful life (straight-line).
Wallonia After-Tax Example (Example 4)
Depreciable base
EUR 3,750,000 − EUR 750,000 (land) = EUR 3,000,000
Annual depreciation
EUR 3,000,000 / 30 years = EUR 100,000/year
Taxable income
NOI (406,750) − Interest (120,000) − Depreciation (100,000) =
EUR 186,750

Taxes at 25%

heading: × EUR 186,750 = EUR 46,688
body:
Pre-tax cash flow
EUR 406,750 − EUR 220,745 = EUR 186,005
After-tax cash flow
EUR 186,005 − EUR 46,688 = EUR 139,317
Equity dividend rate
EUR 186,005 / EUR 750,000 (initial equity) = 24.8%

Why after-tax favours lower land value: Lower land value → higher depreciable base →
higher depreciation expense → lower taxable income → lower taxes → higher after-tax cash
flow. With the same purchase price and NOI, the property with AUD 600,000 land (vs AUD
800,000) has AUD 1,400,000 depreciable base vs AUD 1,200,000 — the first property has
higher after-tax cash flow.

heading: Funds from Operations (FFO) — REIT Performance Metric
body:
Equation 8 — FFO:  FFO = Net Income + Depreciation + Amortization − Net Gains from
Property Sales
FFO adds back non-cash depreciation (a distortion for real estate, since properties often
appreciate) and strips out gains from property sales to show recurring operating
performance. Used primarily for REITs.

heading: Classifications
body:
GICS Classification
MSCI's Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) splits real estate into two groups:
• REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts): Directly own and operate properties. Must distribute nearly all
earnings to avoid corporate income tax. Restricted to owning/operating rental properties or purchasing
mortgages. Available in 30+ countries. Organized as corporations (US, Europe) or trusts (Australia).
• Real Estate Management & Development: Firms engaged in operating, developing, or servicing real
estate (REOCs — Real Estate Operating Companies). Taxable corporations with fewer restrictions,
more flexibility.

Cyclicality by property type: Healthcare REITs = more defensive (healthcare demand is stable).
Industrial/office REITs = more cyclical (underlying tenants face volatile demand). Sensitivity depends
on the TENANT's industry, not just the property type.

Property Classes (Multi-family as Example)
Class
Description
Class A
Built within past 10 years or substantially renovated; top exterior/interior amenities;
highest quality construction. Highest rents, LOWEST capital appreciation potential
(already priced in).

Class
Description
Class B
Built within past 20 years or renovated; dated amenities; good construction with limited
deferred maintenance.
Class C
Built within past 30 years; limited/dated amenities; aging construction with deferred
maintenance.
Class D
Over 30 years, less desirable location; no amenities; poor condition. Lowest prices,
HIGHEST capital appreciation potential if conditions improve.

Sustainable Building Certification
• BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Methodology): UK-origin,
prevalent in UK and Europe.
• LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design): US-origin, most widely recognised global
certification. China, Canada, India have most LEED projects outside US.
• Both: Third-party, rating-based evaluation on energy/water efficiency, sustainable materials, transport
access, carbon footprint.
• Green premium: Higher build/renovation costs, but potentially lower operating costs, higher rents, lower
vacancies, ESG appeal, and tax incentives in many jurisdictions.

heading: Basic Forms of Real Estate Investment
body:
Form
Vehicles
Private Equity
Direct ownership (sole, JV, LP) + Indirect (real estate funds, private REITs)
Private Debt
Mortgage debt, construction loans, mezzanine debt
Public Equity
Publicly traded shares (construction, operating, development companies),
Public REITs, UCITs/Mutual Funds/ETFs
Public Debt
MBS/CMBS/CMOs, Covered bonds, Mortgage REITs, Mortgage ETFs

Topic
Detail
REOCs vs REITs
REOCs: Taxable corporations, fewer restrictions, can reinvest
earnings. REITs: Tax-efficient pass-through structure, must distribute
nearly all earnings, restricted to owning/operating rental properties.
Covered bonds vs MBS
Covered bonds: Issuer retains loan pool on balance sheet; dual
recourse (both bank AND collateral); investor substitution rights.
Lower risk/return. MBS: Securitized off balance sheet to SPV;
tranched; prepayment risk; higher risk/return.
Prepayment risk in MBS
Contraction risk (early repayment) when rates fall. Extension risk
(slower repayment) when rates rise. More prevalent in US due to
lack of prepayment penalties common elsewhere.

heading: Principal Risks
body:
• Macroeconomic/market factors: Changes in economic activity, demographics, supply/demand balance,
capital costs — affect market rents, occupancy, prices.
• Management risk: Failure to maintain properties, manage tenant relationships, minimise vacancy.
• Obsolescence risk: Renovation costs may exceed estimates; may not be economically viable to update
older buildings.
• Environmental/climate risk: Energy efficiency requirements, exposure to floods, earthquakes, hurricanes.
• Technological/structural risk: Industry shifts (e-commerce vs retail, remote work vs office) create
structural demand changes.
• Algorithm/model risk (Zillow case study): Overreliance on statistical models ignoring qualitative local
factors. Zillow Offers lost USD 1 billion before shutting down — could not scale home-
buying/renovation/selling activities despite algorithmic valuations.

2. Economic Value Drivers and Portfolio Characteristics

heading: Macroeconomic Drivers
body:
Macroeconomic factors affect all real estate via demand for space and credit availability:
• GDP growth: Increases demand for industrial, office, retail space (real estate as factor of production).
Given long development lags, initial effect is higher rents and lower vacancies BEFORE supply catches
up.
• Job creation + wage growth: Drive household formation → demand for both rental housing and owner-
occupied housing.
• Credit conditions / interest rates: Critical because both residential and commercial are heavily debt-
financed. Lower mortgage rates stimulate construction and purchase activity. Higher rates compress
activity.
• Housing prices vs income: When housing prices are low relative to household income + mortgage rates
are low, homeownership becomes more attractive, reducing multi-family rental demand.

heading: The Real Estate Cycle — Four Phases
body:
The real estate cycle combines short-term rent/occupancy adjustments with long-term supply decisions.
New supply lags demand signals — often completing AFTER market conditions have already changed.

Phase
Dynamics
Recovery
Business cycle
trough. Little/no new
construction
(uncertain outlook,
tight credit). Low
occupancy. Owners
cut lease rates to
retain tenants.
Downward pressure
on rents and prices.
Interest rates reach
bottom; NOI
reaches bottom;
DSC reaches
bottom; LTV peaks

Phase
Dynamics
Expansion
Economic growth +
easing credit. Rising
occupancy; owners
can raise lease
rates. New
construction begins.
Consumer
confidence rises;
demand for housing
up.
Interest rates rising;
NOI rising; DSC
increasing; LTV
decreasing
Oversupply
Growth softens but
construction pipeline
completes. Property
glut → occupancy
declines, rents fall.
Prices plateau then
fall.
Interest rates peak;
NOI peaks then
falls; DSC peaks
then falls; LTV
reaches bottom
then rises
Recession
Economic slowdown
+ tightening credit.
Accelerating
occupancy decline.
Landlords offer
concessions.
Construction starts at
lows. Prices decline.
Interest rates low;
NOI falling; DSC
decreasing; LTV
increasing

Exam trap — NOI vs DSC vs LTV timing: NOI PEAKS in oversupply (rents are still high as
supply hits); DSC also peaks in oversupply. LTV reaches a LOW in oversupply (values near
their peak). The relationship reverses in recession/recovery.

Local economic factors determine how the macro cycle affects individual properties: business climate
quality, presence of major employers, infrastructure, tax policy, zoning, transportation links, school
quality, public safety. Properties in areas with declining industries or shrinking populations face
structural headwinds regardless of the national cycle.

heading: Portfolio Characteristics of Real Estate
body:
Current Income
Despite similarity to bond coupons, real estate lease income can be structured in several ways:
Type
Description
Fixed rent
Standard periodic payment — most similar to bond coupon.

Type
Description
Step-up clause
Pre-specified future rent increases (NOT contingent — unlike bond
step-ups). Locks in rent increases on agreed schedule.
Indexed rent
Rent adjusts based on observed market variable (e.g., CPI). Links
income to inflation.
Overage / Percentage rent
Base rent PLUS percentage of tenant sales above a breakeven level.
Common in retail. Example: Chandra Shops — base EUR 135,000 +
8% of sales > INR 1,000,000.
Rollover risk
When holding period > lease term, owner faces risk of losing tenant
and income gap during re-leasing.

Capital Appreciation
Property value increases contribute to total holding period return. Because real estate is illiquid and
heterogeneous, price can only be precisely measured at the point of sale. Price estimation methods
(income, cost, comparables, indexes) all involve approximation.

Inflation Hedge
Rents and property prices tend to rise with inflation — whether through indexed lease clauses explicitly
or because physical assets preserve real value. Unlike fixed-income instruments with fixed nominal
cash flows, real estate has natural pricing power in inflationary environments.

Diversification
Private real estate has historically shown LOW correlation with public equities and bonds, providing
genuine portfolio diversification. However, PUBLIC real estate (REITs, MBS) exhibits HIGHER
correlation with public markets in the short to medium term — the diversification benefit is primarily in
private real estate.

Tax Benefits
• Depreciation: In some jurisdictions (US), private real estate can be depreciated faster than actual useful
life, reducing taxable income.
• REIT pass-through: REIT structures distribute income before tax to shareholders. Avoids double taxation
(corporate tax + investor tax) common for regular corporations.

3. Commercial Property Investment Characteristics

heading: Residential Properties
body:
Multi-family properties form the bulk of residential properties in commercial real estate categories.
Categorised by location (urban/suburban), structure height (high-rise, garden apartments, townhouses),
and amenities.


Key residential-specific income metric:
Equation 9 — GPRI:  Gross Potential Rental Income = Market Rent × Rentable Space
GPRI is at FULL occupancy at current market rates. Actual income differs due to:
• Loss to Lease: Difference between market rents and current contracted rents — negative
when current leases are below market, positive when above market.
• Vacancy & Collection Costs: Empty units + uncollected rent.
• Concessions & Adjustments: Tenant incentives, free rent periods.

Pinebranch Estates NOI (Example 6 — Multi-family)
240 units, avg 1,200 ft², AUD 2.00/ft²/month. NOI calculation:
GPRI
240 × 1,200 × AUD 2.00 × 12 = AUD 6,912,000
Add: Other income + Expense recovery
AUD 820,211
Less: Rental deductions (vacancy, loss
to lease, concessions)
(AUD 1,404,560)
Less: Operating + Maintenance
expenses
(AUD 3,408,000)
NOI
AUD 2,919,651

Key contrast with Wallonia (single-tenant warehouse): Wallonia fully passes through
property tax + insurance — cost increases don't reduce owner's NOI for those items.
Pinebranch absorbs those costs (multi-tenant = can't pass through as easily). Multi-tenant
properties also face much larger vacancy/concession deductions due to constant tenant
turnover and short lease terms.

Demand drivers: Local economic conditions, availability and affordability of owner-occupied
alternatives. Tenants may have rent increase/eviction protections. Leases are much shorter than non-
residential (month-to-month to 1 year vs. 5–10 years commercial).

heading: Commercial Use Properties
body:
Office Properties
• Range from major multi-tenant CBD buildings to single-tenant suburban facilities.
• Often built for key tenant (anchor tenant reduces development risk).
• Historically correlated with service industry employment (financial services, healthcare).
• COVID-19 structural shift: Remote/hybrid work permanently reduced demand in many markets. JLL data:
global leasing fell from 11m m² to 5m m² per quarter in Q2 2020, remained below pre-pandemic through
2022.


Industrial and Warehouse Properties
• Includes wholesale/retail distribution centres, combined warehouse/showroom/office, manufacturing +
warehouse.
• E-commerce STRUCTURAL increase in demand — online retailers need more warehouse space to fulfil
orders.
• Rents in logistics/warehouse rose at 2–3× pre-pandemic pace as e-commerce share of retail sales rose
from 15% (2019) to 21% (2021).
• Often designed for specific industrial use — DIFFICULT to convert to alternative uses. This is a key exam
point.

Retail Properties
• Includes regional malls with anchor department stores, local shopping centres with smaller anchors,
standalone grocery/restaurants.
• Structural shift to web-based sales has reduced mall and shopping centre demand — many properties
converting to alternative uses.
• Retail on lower floors of CBD buildings often combined with office or residential.
• Proximity to local workers and residents is critical for rents and occupancy.

Hospitality Properties
• Business travel hotels: Range from extended-stay units to convention centres. More cyclical — firms cut
travel expenses in downturns.
• Leisure/tourist hotels: Depend on consumer confidence, disposable income. Less correlated with
business cycle but still economically sensitive.
• Destination resorts: Most luxurious, management-intensive, highest fixed costs, all-inclusive models.
Most exposed to consumer sentiment.

Specialty and Mixed-Use Properties
• Special-use: Student housing, senior housing, hospitals, self-storage, cell towers, data centres, parking.
• Self-storage: Low capital requirements, low operating costs, month-to-month leases = pricing flexibility
BUT higher demand volatility.
• Senior housing: Ranges from independent living to continuing care. Aging populations support demand;
rising wealth has also increased supply/competition.
• Mixed-use development: Combines more than one tenant type (e.g., office + retail + residential). Income
streams diversified but remain closely correlated because retail tenants in the building ARE the
customers of adjacent office workers/residents.

Eastmain Plaza Mixed-Use (Example 7)
350,000 ft² Kuala Lumpur building: 90% office at MYR 100/ft², 10% retail at MYR 250/ft² + cell tower,
parking.
Gross Rent
(0.9 × 100 × 350,000) + (0.1 × 250 × 350,000) = MYR 31,500,000 +
MYR 8,750,000 = MYR 40,250,000

Retail downside scenario
If retail rent halves + overage rent lost: NOI falls by MYR 4,825,800
(MYR 4,375,000 retail + MYR 450,800 overage) = 37% decline from
MYR 13,085,217 to MYR 8,259,417

Insight: Mixed-use properties diversify income streams but CORRELATE them — office
workers using retail in the same building means a recession hits both simultaneously. The
diversification is less real than it appears.

4. Real Estate Due Diligence and Valuation Approaches

heading: Real Estate Due Diligence
body:
Real estate requires more detailed due diligence than public securities due to: lack of transparency, no
standardised financials, importance of physical attributes and exact location. Six key areas:
Area
What It Covers
Market Review &
Outlook
Current sale/lease prices from web aggregators and brokers. Phase of real
estate cycle affects whether transactions occur above/below offer.
Environmental vulnerabilities (wildfire, hurricane, flood risk) affect insurance
costs and future values. Use ACTUAL transaction prices over estimates
when available.
Current Lease Review
Current tenant rents vs. forecasted market rents. Vacancies as potential
future income. Lease expiration schedule to forecast when income may
change. Tenant creditworthiness, payment history, late payments, defaults.
Future Lease Outlook
Costs/incentives for renewing existing tenants vs. acquiring new ones:
broker commissions, downtime between leases, free rent periods, tenant
improvement allowances (capitalised and amortised, NOT in annual NOI).
Impact of new local supply (like Eastmain retail competition). Legal
changes: rent caps, stabilisation laws.
Financial Review
Several years of audited financials. Checks for inflated NOI via deferred
maintenance, overstated occupancy, or unreported tenant incentives.
Documentation Review
Legal/tax review of ownership history. Ensures free of liens, tax obligations,
encumbrances. Confirms compliance with zoning laws and environmental
regulations. Key for mortgage lenders to secure collateral claim. This
component does NOT feed into NOI projections.
Property Inspection &
Service Agreements
Comprehensive physical, engineering, and environmental inspection
(foundation, building systems, utilities). Assessment of property manager.
Review of existing service/maintenance agreements. Discrepancies →
price adjustment or walk away.

Berlin Rent Cap case study (Mietendeckel, 2020): Froze rents at June 2019 levels for pre-
2014 properties for 5 years. Immediate result: 11% rent decline AND 59% drop in supply of
rental apartments (owners withdrew from market or converted to condos). Supply of post-

2014 units (exempt) rose 6.7%. Struck down as unconstitutional 2021. Key lesson: price
controls suppress supply more than they control prices long-term.

heading: Income Approach — Direct Capitalization Method
body:
Estimates property value as the present value of a perpetuity of NOI. Analogous to P/E valuation for
equities but using NOI.

Equation 10 — Direct Capitalization:  Property Value = Expected NOI / Capitalization Rate
Equation 11 — Cap Rate:  Cap Rate = r − g
where r = required rate of return (risk-free + property-specific risk premium) and g = constant
expected NOI growth rate.
Rearranging Equation 10: Cap Rate = Expected NOI / Property Value. This makes it
interpretable as a YIELD on the property — comparable to a bond's current yield or inverse
of EV/EBITDA.

Wallonia Direct Capitalization (Example 8)
NOI = EUR 406,750. r = 12.5%. g = 2%.
Property value (base case)
EUR 406,750 / (12.5% − 2%) = EUR 406,750 / 10.5% = EUR
3,873,810
Property value (higher costs, NOI =
EUR 382,600)
EUR 382,600 / 10.5% = EUR 3,643,810 — 6% decline for a
modest NOI drop

When to use direct cap: Best for properties with consistent, stable NOI where single-year
income is representative of future periods with steady growth. If NOI is volatile or changing,
use a NORMALISED/STABILISED NOI, or switch to DCF.
Going-in cap rate vs terminal cap rate: Going-in cap rate = Year 1 NOI / purchase price.
Terminal cap rate = used to determine terminal value at end of holding period. If Year 1 NOI
is temporarily depressed (like Eastmain's downside retail scenario), the going-in cap rate will
be LOWER than the terminal cap rate — not because of less risk, but because of temporarily
low NOI.

heading: Income Approach — Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method
body:
Equation 12 — DCF Value:  Property Value = Σ [NOIₜ / (1+r)^i] + Terminal Value / (1+r)^n
Equation 13 — Terminal Value:  = NOIₙ × (1+g) / (r−g)
Terminal value = the perpetuity value of NOI beyond the holding period n, calculated by
capitalising the next year's NOI beyond the forecast.


Eastmain Plaza DCF (Example 9)
5-year holding period. r = 14%. g = 3% after Year 5.
NOI: Y1 / Y2 / Y3 / Y4 / Y5
MYR 8.26M / 6.09M / 13.54M / 14.02M / 14.52M (Y1–2
depressed by retail tenant loss)
PV of 5-year NOI
MYR 36,911,526
Terminal value (NOIy5 × 1.03 /
(0.14−0.03))
14,515,622 × 1.03 / 0.11 = MYR 135,919,006
PV of terminal value
MYR 135,919,006 / (1.14)^5 = MYR 70,592,073
Total property value
MYR 36,911,526 + MYR 70,592,073 = MYR 107,503,599
Going-in cap rate
Y1 NOI / Value = 8,259,417 / 107,503,599 = 7.68% —
LOWER than terminal cap rate (11%) because Y1 NOI is
depressed

Key exam points on cap rates: (1) Going-in cap rate = Year 1 NOI / purchase price. (2)
Terminal cap rate = r − g = 14% − 3% = 11% for Eastmain. (3) Going-in < terminal when NOI
is temporarily below stabilised level — NOT because risk differs across years, since the
same r = 14% is used throughout. (4) Use DCF over direct cap when NOI dynamics are
complex (rising, falling, recovering).

heading: Cost Approach
body:
Values a property at what it would cost to REPLACE it — buy equivalent land and construct a new
building with the same economic use. Adjustments for depreciation and obsolescence.

Cost approach value:  = Land cost + Construction cost + Developer profit + Soft costs
(architect, legal, permits, financing) − Accumulated depreciation

Wallonia Cost Approach (Example 10)
Construction subtotal
EUR 2,675,000 (excavation, steel, walls, electrical, HVAC, etc.)
Soft costs
EUR 925,000 (architect/legal/permits + development period
interest + contractor profit)
Comparable land
EUR 750,000
Total cost estimate
EUR 4,350,000
Depreciable base
EUR 4,350,000 − EUR 750,000 (land) = EUR 3,600,000
Annual depreciation (30 yr)
EUR 3,600,000 / 30 = EUR 120,000/year
After 4 years of depreciation
EUR 4,350,000 − (4 × EUR 120,000) = EUR 4,350,000 − EUR
480,000 = EUR 3,870,000


Key insights on cost approach: (1) Investor should not pay MORE than replacement cost
(including developer profit for time, complexity, financing risk). (2) Significant divergence
between replacement cost and market price can reflect supply/demand imbalances. (3)
During OVERSUPPLY phase: replacement cost EXCEEDS market price (prices weakening
while construction costs remain high). (4) During EXPANSION: market price rises faster than
costs (new supply not yet available).

heading: Sales Comparison (Market) Approach
body:
Uses recent transactions of comparable properties as basis for valuation. Adjustments for differences in
characteristics and timing:
• Price per square foot/metre of leasable area — most common unit of comparison.
• Other metrics: price per gross/net rent, price-to-revenue.
• Adjustments for: sale timing (earlier sales adjusted up if prices have risen), size, location (distance from
CBD), age/condition.
• More recent transactions weighted more heavily than older ones.
• Best for: properties with many similar transactions — especially single-family residential.
• Less applicable for: large commercial properties (too few comparable transactions) — income approach
preferred there.

Pinebranch Sales Comparison (Example 11)
Pinebranch (288,000 ft², 5 years old, prime location, AUD price/ft² to be estimated from three
comparables:
Comp 1 adjusted
AUD 86.67 × (1 + 3%) = AUD 89.27/ft² [adjustments: 0% sale date,
−4% size, +10% location, −3% age = net +3%]
Comp 2 adjusted
AUD 103.20 × (1 + 1%) = AUD 104.23/ft² [net +1%]
Comp 3 adjusted
AUD 96.82 × (1 + 7%) = AUD 103.60/ft² [net +7%]
Average adjusted price/ft²
(89.27 + 104.23 + 103.60) / 3 = AUD 99.03/ft²
Estimated value
AUD 99.03 × 288,000 ft² = AUD 28,520,640

Adjustment logic: A positive adjustment INCREASES the estimated value of the subject
(e.g., subject is farther from city than comparable → adjust comparable UP). A negative
adjustment DECREASES the estimated value (e.g., subject is larger than comparable →
discount for size premium paid in comp). Think of it as: what would the comparable have
sold for if it were MORE like the subject?

When to Use Each Valuation Method

Method
When to Use
Income approach
Large commercial properties (office, retail, industrial) where fewer
comparables exist and income generation dominates investment
thesis. Also appropriate when NOI is complex and dynamic (use DCF).
Direct capitalization
Stable, consistent NOI; single-year representative of long-term
performance. Quick screen.
DCF / multi-period
NOI is changing: recovering tenants, lease roll-overs, capital
improvements, changing market rents.
Cost approach
New construction or special-use properties with no market
comparables. Also as a sanity check — buyer shouldn't pay more than
replacement cost.
Sales comparison
Single-family residential (many similar transactions). Less reliable for
large commercial.
Independent appraisers
Often weight sales comparisons heavily alongside income approach.

5. Real Estate Indexes
Real estate indexes track market risk and return, enabling performance evaluation, manager
benchmarking, and index-based investments. Key distinction:
Type
Characteristics
Private market indexes
Use appraisals or actual transactions. NOT directly investable.
Public market indexes
Made up of listed equity or debt securities. Directly investable via
mutual funds, ETFs, UCITS (European).

heading: Appraisal-Based Indexes
body:
Rely on professional appraisals to estimate property values. Used because frequent transactions for
the SAME property are not available — appraisals provide continuity.

Example — GREFI (Global Real Estate Fund Index): Capitalisation-weighted index, published
quarterly, launched 2014. Combines NCREIF (US), INREV (Europe), ANREV (Asia). Investment
managers contribute appraised values, NOI, capex, occupancy. Returns disaggregated by property
type and region.

Equation 15 — Holding Period Return:
HPR = [NOI − Capital Expenditures + (Ending Market Value − Beginning Market
Value)] / Beginning Market Value
Equivalent to a single-period IRR as if purchased at beginning and sold at end of period.
Market values are APPRAISED (not actual transaction prices).


Wallonia Holding Period Return (Example 12)
INREV Belgian warehouse
returns
Year 1: +5.6%, Year 2: +3.2%
Beginning market value
(Year 2)
EUR 3,750,000 × 1.056 = EUR 3,960,000
Ending market value (Year
2)
EUR 3,960,000 × 1.032 = EUR 4,086,720
HPR
(406,750 − 100,000 + (4,086,720 − 3,960,000)) / 3,960,000 = (306,750
+ 126,720) / 3,960,000 = 10.95%

heading: Appraisal Lag — Biases and Corrections
body:
Appraisal-based indexes are criticised for systematic lags that distort measured performance:
Effect
Consequence
Appraisal lag in rising markets
Transactions rise first; appraisals follow later. Index UNDERSTATES
returns in rising markets.
Appraisal lag in falling markets
Transactions fall first; appraisals follow. Index OVERSTATES returns
in falling markets.
Infrequent appraisals
Not all properties appraised every quarter. Manager may assume
constant value until next formal appraisal, creating multi-quarter stale
prices.
Smoothing of returns
Lag creates artificially smooth return series — UNDERESTIMATES
true volatility.
Understated correlation
Smoothed returns show LOWER correlation with public markets than
actual. This creates false diversification appeal.
Overstated Sharpe ratio
Lower vol + same return = higher Sharpe. If used in asset allocation,
will OVERALLOCATE to real estate.

Correcting for Appraisal Lag — Unsmoothing
Equation 16 — Smoothing model:  Rt* = a·Rt + (1−a)·Rt−1*
Appraised index return (Rt*) = weighted average of actual return (Rt) and prior period
appraised return (Rt−1*). Coefficient a = speed of adjustment (higher a = faster incorporation
of actual returns).
Equation 17 — Unsmoothed actual return:  Rt = Rt*/a + [(1−a)/a] × Rt−1*
Reversing the smoothing model gives the estimated TRUE return. With a = 0.5, true returns
= double the recent reported change. Unsmoothed returns show higher volatility and higher
correlation with other asset classes — more accurate for asset allocation.

Unsmoothing Example (Example 13)
With a = 0.6: Rt = Rt*/0.6 + (0.4/0.6) × Rt−1*

Period 1: Rt* = 0.50%, Rt−1* = 2.70% → Rt = 0.50/0.6 + (0.4/0.6) × 2.70 = 0.833 + 1.80 = 2.63%
Period 6: Rt* = −8.70%, Rt−1* = 1.00% → Rt = −8.70/0.6 + (0.4/0.6) × 1.00 = −14.50 + 0.667 =
−13.83% (dramatically larger than the smoothed −8.70%)

Key implication: Unsmoothed returns are MORE volatile and show the true magnitude of
market movements. The appraisal-based index made Period 6 look like a modest decline;
the true underlying return was nearly double the loss. Portfolio allocations based on
smoothed data will underweight risk.

heading: Transaction-Based Indexes
body:
Based on actual transaction prices rather than appraisals. Solve the lag problem but introduce different
challenges:
Topic
Detail
Challenge
The same property rarely sells multiple times per quarter. Must use
econometric techniques to control for differences across properties.
Repeat sales index
Uses only properties that have sold more than once. Allocates the
price change between sales to each quarter using regression. More
reliable with more transactions. Example: RCA Commercial Property
Price Index (US). One-time transactions excluded.
Hedonic index
Does NOT require repeat sales. Regression controls for property
characteristics (size, age, quality, location) to isolate true market
price changes. Data-intensive; most reliable at national level for
major property types.
Transaction-based vs
appraisal
Transaction indexes LEAD appraisal indexes (capture market moves
first). But can include statistical noise quarter-to-quarter due to
regression techniques. Long-term trend is accurate; short-term can
be noisy.

heading: Public Real Estate Equity Indexes
body:
REIT indexes are the most common public equity real estate indexes. Based on industry classification
or region.
Example — S&P Asia-Pacific REIT Index: Developed Asia-Pacific markets (Australia, Hong Kong,
Japan, NZ, Singapore, South Korea). Float-adjusted market-cap weighted — skewed toward largest
markets (e.g., Singapore) rather than transaction volume. Annual rebalance (September) with quarterly
updates.

heading: Real Estate Fixed-Income Indexes
body:
MBS and covered bond indexes have unique fixed-income characteristics:

Topic
Detail
MBS indexes — prepayment
risk
Contraction risk when rates fall (early repayment shortens duration).
Extension risk when rates rise (slower repayment lengthens duration).
Prepayment risk is highest in US (no prepayment penalties common
elsewhere).
MBS index duration
When rates fall: actual prepayments > forecasted → shorter effective
duration. When rates rise: prepayments below forecasted → effective
duration INCREASES. This negative convexity makes MBS indexes
sensitive to interest rate direction.
Covered bond indexes
Simplest securitisation. Issuer (European bank) retains loan pool on
balance sheet. Pool serves as collateral for non-amortizing fixed-
maturity bonds. Dual recourse: investor has claim on BOTH issuing
bank AND collateral pool. Borrower prepayment penalties. Lower
risk/return than MBS.
Denmark covered bond case
study
Homeowner is effectively the bond issuer. Lender is intermediary.
~25% owned by foreign investors by 2021. AAA rated. Considerable
market liquidity: borrowers shifted from long-term fixed → adjustable-
rate as rates rose in 2022.

6. Formula Reference & Exam Traps

heading: All Key Formulas
body:
Formula
Expression
NOI
Effective Gross Income − Operating Expenses − Property Maintenance
Allowance
Effective Gross Income
Gross Rent + Pass-Through Recoveries + Other Income − Vacancies −
Concessions
Gross Rent
Base Rent/m² × Rentable Area
GPRI (residential)
Market Rent × Rentable Space (at full occupancy)
LTV
Mortgage Debt Outstanding / Current Property Value
DSC
NOI / Debt Service
Pre-tax cash flow
NOI − Debt Service
Equity dividend rate

Taxes
t × (NOI − Interest Expense − Depreciation Expense)
After-tax cash flow
Pre-tax Cash Flow − Taxes
FFO
Net Income + Depreciation + Amortization − Net Gains on Property
Sales
Direct cap value
Expected NOI / Cap Rate   where Cap Rate = r − g
Cap rate (from market)
Expected NOI / Property Value

Formula
Expression
DCF value
Σ NOIₜ/(1+r)^i + Terminal Value/(1+r)^n
Terminal value
NOIₙ × (1+g) / (r−g)
HPR (appraisal-based)
(NOI − CapEx + ΔMarket Value) / Beginning Market Value
Smoothing model
Rt* = a·Rt + (1−a)·Rt−1*
Unsmoothed return
Rt = Rt*/a + [(1−a)/a] × Rt−1*

heading: Critical Exam Traps
body:
• NOI EXCLUDES: debt service, income taxes, depreciation, extraordinary items. It is a pre-financing, pre-
tax measure.
• Property maintenance allowance vs. capital expenditures: Maintenance allowance = MAINTAIN current
economic use (included in NOI calc). Upgrades/improvements that change economic use = NOT in
maintenance allowance.
• Land is NOT depreciated (infinite life). Only the building/improvements are depreciated. Lower land value
→ higher depreciable base → higher depreciation → lower taxes → higher after-tax cash flow.
• Pass-through vs. non-pass-through: Property tax and insurance pass-through to tenant → owner's NOI
NOT affected by cost changes. Service/repairs with a cap → excess above cap reduces owner's NOI.
• Cycle metrics: NOI peaks in OVERSUPPLY (not expansion). DSC peaks in OVERSUPPLY. LTV reaches
its LOWEST in oversupply (values near peak). LTV peaks in recovery (values at trough).
• Direct cap method does NOT require a terminal value — that's the DCF method. Use direct cap for stable
NOI; use DCF when NOI is dynamic.
• Going-in cap rate < terminal cap rate when Year 1 NOI is temporarily depressed — NOT because of
lower risk in Year 1.
• Terminal cap rate = r − g (e.g., Eastmain: 14% − 3% = 11%). The terminal value NOI uses Year n+1
(Year 6, not Year 5).
• Sales comparison adjustments: Adjust the COMPARABLE upward/downward to match the subject.
Positive adjustment = subject is better than comparable on that dimension. Negative = subject is worse.
• Documentation review does NOT provide input to NOI forecasting — it ensures clean legal title. Current
lease review and future lease outlook DO feed into NOI forecasts.
• Appraisal-based indexes UNDERSTATE volatility and UNDERSTATE correlation with other asset
classes (due to smoothing). This OVERSTATES Sharpe ratios and causes overallocation to real estate
in mean-variance models.
• Appraisal lag direction: lag causes understatement in rising markets (index trails actual prices) and
overstatement in falling markets (prices have fallen but appraisals haven't caught up).
• Repeat sales index excludes one-time transactions. Hedonic index uses ALL transactions but controls for
property characteristics.
• MBS effective duration INCREASES when rates RISE (slower prepayments = longer maturity). Negative
convexity.
• FFO adds back DEPRECIATION because real estate often appreciates — depreciation distorts true
economic income for REITs.

• Loss to lease: POSITIVE loss to lease = current rents are BELOW market (potential upside). NEGATIVE
loss to lease = current rents are ABOVE market (potential decline on rollover). A negative loss to lease
may indicate excess supply.
• Residential vs commercial lease terms: Residential leases are much SHORTER (month-to-month to 1
year). Commercial leases are 5–10+ years. Multi-family properties therefore face more frequent rollover
risk and higher sensitivity to vacancy.

heading: Practice Problem Quick Reference
body:
Max loan given both LTV and DSC constraints: Calculate max loan based on each constraint
separately; take the LOWER of the two. Example from curriculum: LTV = 65% × USD 283M = USD

heading: M; DSC constraint: Max debt service = NOI/1.65 = 15.6M/1.65 = 9.454M → loan =
M/0.0525 = USD 180.1M. LOWER = USD 180.1M.
body:
Sales comparison value using per-ft² averages: Apply adjustment percentages to each
comparable's price/ft², average the adjusted prices, multiply by subject property's square footage.

Cost approach value: Land + Building construction + Soft costs − Accumulated depreciation.
Developer profit IS included in the building cost estimate. Value = USD 57M (construction) + USD 7M
(land) − USD 5M (accumulated depreciation) = USD 59M.

CFA Level II | Alternatives | Overview of Types of Real Estate Investment | Study Notes

heading: What Is a Commodity?
body:
A commodity is a physical good attributable to a natural resource that is tradable and supplied without
substantial differentiation by the general public. Commodities trade in two market forms:
•
Spot (physical) markets – immediate delivery; prices reflect current supply & demand
•
Futures & forward markets – price agreed today for delivery at a future date

Unlike equities and bonds (which are claims on productive capital and generate cash flows),
commodities are physical assets whose value derives from their use as consumables or as production
inputs. They do NOT generate future cash flows — they generate costs (storage, transport, insurance,
spoilage).
KEY: The fundamental driver of commodity valuation is supply & demand analysis, not discounted
cash flow.

heading: Fundamental Analysis Framework
body:
Because raw data is often proprietary and commodity markets are subject to non-normal distributions
and non-stationary relationships, commodity analysis is inherently imperfect. Four analytical tools:
•
Direct announcements: Government/industry data (USDA, OPEC, IEA, NBS China) can be
used to infer supply and demand even with reporting lags.
•
Component analysis (stock & flow): Stock = total productive capacity (arable land, mine
acreage, well capacity). Flow = actual utilization of that stock (tanker traffic, crop yields, piglet
counts per sow). Policy changes affect both simultaneously.

•
Timing considerations: Seasonality (crop growing cycles, winter heating demand) and
logistics shocks (pipeline damage stops flow but not stock). Seasonality directly feeds the
futures curve shape.
•
Money flow: Macro sentiment, risk tolerance, interest rates (low rates fund new mines, affecting
future supply), government subsidies to substitutes (electric vehicles indirectly cap gasoline
prices).

heading: The Six Commodity Sectors
body:
Using the Bloomberg Commodity Index segmentation:

Sector
Primary Commodities
Stock Influences
Flow Influences
Energy
Crude oil, nat. gas, coal,
gasoline, heating oil
Field discovery/depletion,
refinery tech, power plant
capacity, GDP size
Pipeline/tanker reliability,
seasonality, hurricanes,
auto sales, geopolitics,
environmental regs, GDP
growth
Grains
Corn, soy, wheat, rice
(also ethanol feedstock)

storage/port infrastructure,

KEY: Exam tip: Copper demand is driven by industrial activity. Natural gas demand is driven by
weather (not primarily industry). Precious metals are LEAST affected by short-term weather risk
(ease of storage).

2. Life Cycles of Commodity Sectors

heading: Core Concept
body:
Life cycle = the time from production/extraction through to final consumption. A short life cycle allows
rapid adjustment to supply/demand shocks; a long life cycle limits flexibility. Timing/seasonality is an
overlay on underlying supply-demand fundamentals.

heading: Energy Life Cycle
body:
Key distinction: natural gas can be consumed almost immediately after extraction. Crude oil must be
refined before use.

Step
Stage
Key Details
1
Extraction
Well drilled after surveys; pressure or fracking required to extract
hydrocarbons from shale.
2
Storage
Crude oil stored commercially (US, Singapore, N. Europe) or
strategically; also stored on tankers. Summer nat. gas injected into
storage for winter.
3
Consumption (gas)
Natural gas can be consumed at this stage — no refining required.
4
Refining (oil)
Crude 'cracked' by heat — components boiled off successively
(gasoline, kerosene, etc.); residue = asphalt. Refineries cost billions.
Keystone XL expansion: $5bn → $10bn.
5
Consumption
(refined)
Distilled products shipped by ship/pipe/train/truck to end consumers.

Key benchmarks: WTI (West Texas Intermediate) — US crude, delivery at Cushing, Oklahoma. Brent
crude — North Sea, UK delivery. Both represent high-quality refinery inputs used for hedging.
Pipelines are vulnerable to disruption; refineries are rarely destroyed even in conflict zones due to their
value to all parties.

heading: Industrial & Precious Metals Life Cycle
body:
Most flexible life cycle — ore and finished metal can be stored months or years without spoilage (unlike
food commodities). Parallel to energy:

Step
Detail
Extracting &
Preparing
Ore (~2% metal content) removed via mine/open pit; ground to powder;
concentrated to ~25% purity.
Smelting
Heated; slag removed → 60% purity; further processes → 99.99% pure metal.
Storage/Logistics
Stored in bonded warehouse; shipped to end user on demand.

Critical economic point: Smelting/processing facilities have enormous fixed costs and declining
marginal costs at scale. When demand falls, producers CANNOT easily cut production — financial
penalties of idling facilities are severe. Ironically, new capacity often arrives just as demand declines,
exacerbating price drops. Most time variability in metals comes from the demand side (construction,
economic growth).

heading: Livestock Life Cycle
body:
•
Poultry: matures in weeks

•
Hogs: ~6 months to slaughter weight; fed soymeal/cornmeal in mass production
•
Cattle: 1–2 years as 'feeder cattle' on grass, then 6–12 months in feedlot on corn-based diet

Historically the least export-oriented commodity due to spoilage risk after slaughter. Advances in
cryogenics (freezing) have enabled growing international trade, especially to emerging market middle-
class consumers. Futures contracts available for both feeder cattle (young, still in pasture) and live
cattle (being fattened for slaughter).

heading: Grains Life Cycle
body:
Four stages: Planting → Growth → Pod/ear/head formation → Harvest. Northern and Southern
Hemisphere cycles are opposite (Brazil/Argentina harvest when North America plants).

Stage
Corn
Soybeans
Wheat (hard winter)
Planting
Apr–May
May–Jun
Sep–Oct
Growth
Jun–Aug
Jul–Aug
Nov–Mar
Pod/Head Formation
Aug–Sep
Sep
Apr–May
Harvest
Sep–Nov
Sep–Oct
Jun–Jul

Grains stored year-round (silos, warehouses globally). Poor storage hygiene causes mold/insect
infestation. Government tender purchasing patterns useful signal for demand analysis. Futures delivery
months reflect growing cycle stages.

heading: Softs (Coffee Example)
body:
Harvested year-round across equatorial countries. Two main methods:
•
Dry method: cherries laid in sun 2–3 weeks (>50% of production)
•
Wet method: water soak → pulp removed → fermentation 12–48 hrs → drying

Two futures contracts: Robusta (London) and Arabica (New York). Contracts are for unroasted 'green'
beans. Physical delivery to bonded warehouse permitted. Farmers hedge sale price (short futures);
coffee roasters hedge purchase cost (long futures).

3. Valuation of Commodities vs. Equities & Bonds

heading: The Fundamental Distinction
body:
Equities & Bonds
Commodities
Asset type
Financial — claims on productive
capital
Physical (mostly) — some newer
classes like electricity/weather are
non-physical
Cash flows
Generate periodic cash flows
(dividends, coupons)
Incur costs: storage, transport,
insurance, spoilage — no cash flows
generated


Equities & Bonds
Commodities
Valuation basis
PV of expected future cash flows
(DCF)
Discounted forecast of future
supply/demand-based prices;
expected price volatility
Investment vehicle
Directly through equity/bond markets
Primarily through derivative contracts
(futures, swaps) — finite lifetime
Price discovery
Forward-looking profit and cash flow
estimates
Futures markets + spot markets +
arbitrage across delivery locations

Technical analysis is also sometimes used in commodity valuation — the CFAI explicitly notes this.
Valuation can range from detailed fundamental models (oil companies with full cost/extraction data) to
experienced floor trader judgment.

heading: Storage Costs & the Price Curve
body:
Owning physical commodities incurs ongoing storage and transportation costs. These costs shape the
forward curve:
•
If storage/transport are substantial → futures prices incrementally higher further into the future
(contango)
•
If current demand is high relative to supply → spot can exceed futures (backwardation)
•
Arbitrage enforces price relationships only within bounds — not an exact equality — because
some participants cannot make/take physical delivery

KEY: Airlines hedge jet fuel costs by going LONG energy futures — they are locking in their cost of
a future input.

4. Commodity Futures Markets: Participants

heading: Futures vs. Forward Contracts
body:
Feature
Futures
Forwards
Trading venue
Centralized exchanges (CME, ICE,
SHFE)
OTC — bilateral between known
parties
Standardization
Highly standardized — counterparty
unknown
Customized to specific hedging needs
Settlement
Daily mark-to-market; margin calls
Settlement at expiration (or custom
frequency)
Regulation
Exchange-regulated + NFA/CFTC
(US)
Less regulatory oversight
Counterparty
Exchange acts as guarantor
Financial institution typically provides
liquidity/customization

Historical note: first modern organized futures exchange was the Dojima Rice Exchange in Osaka,
Japan (1710), although futures traded in England in the 16th century. Early exchanges were forward

markets — participants went bankrupt when unrealized losses became realized. Futures with daily
mark-to-market solved this.
Important distinction: commodity futures markets are net zero in aggregate (every long = one short).
Equity markets have net long aggregate positions (all issued shares represent net long exposure).
Shorting futures is simpler than shorting equities — no need to locate shares, no dividend
reimbursement, same margin for longs and shorts.

heading: Five Categories of Participants
body:
1. Hedgers
Trade to manage price risk related to their physical commodity exposure. Both long AND short
positions can be hedging positions — the key is whether the futures position offsets physical exposure:


Long Futures (Hedging)
Short Futures (Hedging)
Example
Food manufacturer hedging corn
needed for snack chips
Gold mining company hedging against
future gold price declines
Motivation
Locks in purchase price; risk =
opportunity cost if prices fall
Locks in sale price; risk = opportunity
cost if prices rise

Risk for hedgers is more opportunity cost than actual financial loss (they can make/take delivery).
Hedgers also often speculate — corporate treasuries as profit centers, individual farmers with
inconsistent hedging. Metallgesellschaft AG (Germany, 1993) collapsed from a poorly constructed
gasoline/fuel oil hedge — defining example of flawed commercial hedging.
2. Traders & Investors (Speculators)
Three types:
•
Informed investors/hedgers: trade on superior information about supply and demand
fundamentals (weather models, proprietary flow data)
•
Liquidity providers: buy when producers want to sell, sell when consumers want to buy; profit
by providing insurance in return for expected profit; producers and consumers may not be in the
market simultaneously
•
Arbitrageurs: hold physical inventory; capitalize on mispricing between spot + storage +
financing vs. futures price; own bonded warehouses, grain silos, feedlots
3. Exchanges/Clearing Houses
Set trading rules, transmit prices and payments, post margin requirements, provide self-regulation. Key
global exchanges:
•
US: CME (Chicago Mercantile Exchange) and ICE (Intercontinental Exchange) — consolidated
most specialist exchanges
•
Americas: B3 Brazil (softs, grains, livestock)
•
Europe: London Metal Exchange (owned by HKEX) — main global industrial metals venue
•
Asia: Dalian Commodity Exchange, Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE), Tokyo Commodity
Exchange
•
Other: Indonesia (palm oil), Singapore (rubber), Australia (energy, grains, wool)
4. Analysts & Non-Trading Participants
Research (commercial, academic, policy), commodity price benchmarking for inflation indexes,
ETF/swap product creation, broker-dealers providing liquidity. Governments controlling natural resource
extraction or taxing it use futures information for revenue/investment policy.

5. Regulators
US: CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission) — separate from the SEC; delegates monitoring
to NFA (National Futures Association, a self-regulatory body). Outside US: China uses unified China
Securities Regulatory Commission. EU: ESMA (European Securities and Markets Authority);
MiFID/MiFID II (2007/2018) focused on deregulation; post-2009 legislation strengthened oversight.
Global harmonizer: IOSCO (International Organization of Securities Commissions).

5. Commodity Spot & Futures Pricing

heading: Definitions
body:
•
Spot price: current price to deliver/receive a physical commodity at a specific location (grain silo,
nat. gas pipeline, oil storage tank, sugar refinery)
•
Futures price: exchange-based price agreed today for delivery/receipt of a defined quantity and
quality at a future date
•
Basis: difference between spot and futures prices


Backwardation
Contango
Definition
Spot price > futures price; near-term
futures > longer-term futures
Spot price < futures price; near-term
futures < longer-term futures
Calendar spread
Positive (near minus far > 0)
Negative (near minus far < 0)
Roll return
Positive for long holder
Negative for long holder
Economic
interpretation
Current demand dominates supply;
convenience yield high
Storage costs dominate; supply
adequate; convenience yield low
Common in...
Energy (esp. crude oil, pre-shale era)
Agriculture, precious metals, softs —
consistently stored

Origin of 'contango': theory is it derives from 'continuation' — a fee paid on the London Stock Exchange
in mid-1800s by a buyer to defer settlement. Backwardation = the 'backward' reversed arrangement.

heading: Physical vs. Cash Settlement
body:
•
Cash settlement: contract has no value after maturity; settling in cash on the futures vs. spot
price difference. Enabled wider participation by speculators and arbitrageurs who cannot
physically deliver. Example: feeder cattle (CME).
•
Physical settlement: title of actual commodity transferred. WTI crude must be delivered to
tanks at Cushing, Oklahoma. Brent crude delivered to North Sea. Ensures futures-spot
convergence. Supply/demand differences at delivery locations cause price divergences even for
similar specifications.

Quality/variety complications: Robusta coffee (London) cannot be delivered for Arabica (New York).
Raw US sugar ≠ white UK sugar. Some contracts allow premiums/discounts for specification
differences — e.g., arabica coffee prices adjusted by country of origin and warehouse location.

KEY: Spot prices are highly localized and tied to physical delivery. Futures prices are
regional/global, standardized, and serve as reference prices for forward contracts and policy
decisions.


6. Theories of Commodity Futures Returns
Three primary theories explain the shape of the futures price curve and its implications for returns:

heading: Insurance Theory (Keynes, 1930) — 'Normal Backwardation'
body:
Keynes, in A Treatise on Money (1930), proposed that commodity producers (who are long physical)
sell futures contracts to lock in their revenue and thus create predictable cash flows. To induce
speculators to absorb this price risk, futures must trade below the expected future spot price — a risk
premium payment by producers to speculators. This persistent producer-driven selling pushes the
futures curve into backwardation.

Mechanics: A farmer sells next harvest's soybeans forward today at the futures price. As time passes
and the October contract approaches expiration, if spot prices are stable, the October contract
converges up to the spot — speculator profits 2.25 cents/pound × 50,000 lbs = $1,125 per contract.

Empirical verdict: FAILS. Kolb (1992) examined 29 futures contracts and concluded 'normal
backwardation is not normal' — backwardation does NOT generate statistically significant positive
excess returns for investors. Fama & French (1987) confirmed. This led to the hedging pressure
hypothesis.
KEY: Insurance theory predicts futures are always in backwardation and long investors always earn
a positive risk premium. Evidence rejects both claims.

heading: Hedging Pressure Hypothesis (De Roon, Nijman & Veld, 2000; Cootner,
body:
1960)
More general than Keynes — recognizes BOTH producers and consumers seek price protection. The
shape of the curve depends on the relative hedging pressure of each group:

Scenario
Curve Shape
Mechanism
Producer hedging > Consumer
hedging
Backwardation (consistent with
Keynes)
Excess producer selling drives
futures below expected spot;
speculators need discount to
absorb risk
Consumer hedging > Producer
hedging
Contango
Excess consumer buying bids
futures above expected spot;
speculators paid to supply price
insurance to buyers
Balanced (equal pressure)
Flat curve
Hedging needs of buyers and
sellers offset each other; no
speculator discount required

Real-world complication: producers are generally more concentrated/exposed than consumers (BP,
ExxonMobil, BHP Billiton vs. diffuse individual consumers). Fast food companies (McDonald's, Burger
King) DO hedge commodity costs; apparel companies (Gap) generally do not (cotton = small % of
costs). Theory is intuitive but measuring asymmetric hedging pressure is extremely difficult in practice.

heading: Theory of Storage (Kaldor, 1939)
body:
Focuses on inventory levels as the driver of curve shape. Physical commodities must be stored — this
is the key insight.
Theory of Storage: Futures Price = Spot Price + Direct Storage Costs − Convenience Yield

Convenience yield: the benefit of holding physical inventory as a buffer against supply disruptions. A
manufacturing plant, refinery, or bread company values having stock on hand — if supply is disrupted,
operations continue. Convenience yield is inversely related to inventory size:
•
High inventory → low convenience yield → futures price likely ABOVE spot (contango) →
storage costs dominate
•
Low inventory / supply concern → high convenience yield → futures price likely BELOW spot
(backwardation) → demand dominates

Libya civil war (2011) example: Loss of high-quality Libyan crude → spot prices rose sharply; near-
term convenience yield spiked (European refiners scrambled for substitutes); farther-out contracts less
affected as traders expected replacement supply over time. Result: crude oil in backwardation
throughout 2011.

Practical limitation: storage costs are proprietary (companies don't disclose them). Events (war,
weather, technology) can radically and unpredictably shift convenience yield. Corn for feed ≠ corn for
human consumption, making 'inventory' difficult to define cleanly. The theory provides a framework but
requires significant judgment.
KEY: When inventory is scarce → high convenience yield → backwardation. When inventory is
abundant → low convenience yield → contango. This theory is most grounded in physical
economics.

7. Components of Total Return on Commodity Futures
Investors use futures (not physical commodities) for portfolio exposure — avoiding the storage, feeding,
and treatment costs of physical ownership. Total return of a fully collateralized commodity futures
position:

Total Return: Price Return + Roll Return + Collateral Return  (+ Rebalance Return for indexes)

heading: Price Return (Spot Yield)
body:
Price Return: (Current Futures Price − Previous Futures Price) ÷ Previous Futures Price
Measured using the front-month (nearest to expiration) contract. Differs from physical commodity price
change due to standardization.

heading: Roll Return (Roll Yield)
body:
When a futures contract approaches expiration, the long investor must 'roll' — sell the expiring contract
and buy the next contract — to maintain exposure. The price difference between near and far contracts
creates the roll return:
Roll Return: [(Near-Term Closing Price − Farther-Term Closing Price) ÷ Near-Term Closing
Price] × % Position Rolled


Example: WTI crude, 7 February 2019, rolling March → April (S&P GSCI rolls 20%/day over 5 days):
March = $52.64/barrel; April = $53.00/barrel
Gross roll return = ($52.64 − $53.00)/$52.64 = −0.68% × 20% rollover = −0.13% net (contango →
negative roll return)

Market State
Near vs. Far Price
Roll Return
Contracts at Roll
Backwardation
Near > Far
Positive
Must buy MORE far
contracts to maintain
same $ exposure
Contango
Near < Far
Negative
Can buy FEWER far
contracts to maintain
same $ exposure

Important caveat (Ilmanen, 2011): Roll return ≈ risk premium (challenged view). While Keynes argued
positive roll return compensates long speculators in backwardation, Ilmanen found 40% of commodities
with negative roll returns still had positive total returns. Cannot directly equate backwardation with
positive total return.
Roll return is an accounting calculation — cannot be isolated and independently captured in a
standalone portfolio strategy.

heading: Collateral Return (Collateral Yield)
body:
Interest earned on cash/bonds posted as margin to maintain futures positions. For indexed
investments, cash = 100% of notional (no leverage). Use risk-free government bonds matching the
intended investment term (T-bills for short-term, 10-year constant maturity for long-term projections).

heading: Historical Context: S&P GSCI (Jan 1970 – Mar 2019)
body:

Key takeaways from the table: (1) ~75% of total return came from collateral (interest rates). (2) Spot
return drove ~45% of total return. (3) Roll return was a DRAG of −130 bps per year. (4) Nearly all
volatility (97% correlation) comes from spot price changes — roll and collateral barely move the needle
monthly.

heading: Roll Return by Sector
body:
From S&P GSCI sector data (periods vary by sector):
•
Industrial metals, agriculture, livestock, precious metals, softs all show statistically NEGATIVE
mean roll returns — these are stored commodities, so contango is structurally typical

•
Precious metals: perpetually negative roll return because gold is stored as an alternative
currency; convenience yield is essentially zero
•
Energy: historically had potential for positive roll return (minimal storage buffer = higher
convenience yield). Post-2010 US shale revolution dramatically increased oil supply, reducing
scarcity risk; roll return has diminished toward negative. OPEC + Russia (OPEC+) have partially
reclaimed pricing power through supply restriction.

KEY: Indexes overweight to agriculture, livestock, precious metals, and softs will structurally suffer
negative roll return drag. Sector diversification significantly determines overall roll return
experience.

8. Commodity Swaps
A commodity swap is a legal contract involving the exchange of payments over multiple dates as
determined by specified reference prices or indexes relating to commodities. A series of futures
contracts typically underlies the reference prices.
Why swaps over futures? Swaps provide risk management and risk transfer while eliminating the need
to manage multiple individual futures contracts. They also offer customization impossible with
standardized futures — specific crude grades, shifting quantities, maintenance shutdown adjustments.

heading: Swap Market Structure
body:
Typical structure: Commercial end-user (e.g., refiner) ↔ Swap Dealer ↔ (Futures market / Offsetting
swap / Physical purchase contract). The dealer bears the price risk and hedges through one of these
channels, or keeps the exposure to profit from market information.

heading: Types of Commodity Swaps
body:
Excess Return Swap
Payments driven by changes in price of the underlying futures contracts ('excess' = net change in
underlying futures prices). Example: oil refiner pays a premium for protection against oil prices
exceeding $70/barrel monthly. If prices exceed $70, refiner receives the excess; if not, receives nothing
(paid only the upfront premium). Acts as a series of call options on monthly oil prices.
Total Return Swap
Payments driven by change in the index level (futures price changes + collateral/interest return). Used
by large institutional investors (pension plans) seeking commodity return exposure for: (a)
diversification (low correlation with stocks/bonds), (b) inflation hedging for ALM purposes.
Example: Pension fund wants 5% commodity exposure (£100m of £2bn AUM) in China Futures
Commodity Index for 5 years. Contracts Swiss bank. If CFCI +1% on Day 1 → dealer pays £1m. If
CFCI −5% on Day 2 → fund pays dealer £5m. Dealer typically hedges short index exposure via futures
or physical commodity investments.
Basis Swap
Periodic payments exchanged based on values of two related but not perfectly correlated commodity
reference prices. Used to hedge spread between a liquid futures benchmark and an illiquid but related
physical commodity. Example: Gulf Coast refinery processes cheap heavy crude from
Mexico/Venezuela; enters basis swap paying Brent crude, receiving heavy crude price — hedges the
Brent/heavy crude spread.

Note: 'basis' has commodity-specific meanings; in grains, basis may be the soybean contract vs.
physical soybeans at the Mississippi River.
Variance Swaps
Two parties exchange payments based on the difference between observed/actual variance of
commodity prices over consecutive periods and a fixed variance agreed at inception. If observed
variance > fixed variance, variance buyer receives payment (and vice versa). Often capped to limit
extreme outcomes.
Volatility Swaps
Similar to variance swaps but based on price volatility (standard deviation) rather than variance.
Volatility seller profits if realized volatility < expected; volatility buyer profits from higher-than-expected
volatility. Both sides are not speculating on price direction or level — purely on the magnitude of price
fluctuations.

KEY: Exam tip on swap types: Excess return = futures price changes only. Total return = price
changes + collateral return. Basis swap = spread between two related commodities.
Variance/volatility swap = betting on how much prices move, not direction.

9. Commodity Indexes

heading: Role of Commodity Indexes
body:
Three primary functions:
•
Performance benchmark for evaluating broad commodity price movements
•
Macroeconomic indicator — statistically significant relationships with inflation, GDP, etc.
•
Basis for investment vehicles (ETFs, swaps, structured notes) — index as reference price for
contracts
For an index to be viable, it must be investable — investors/agents must be able to replicate the
methodology. This requires tradable contracts on accessible exchanges with sufficient liquidity.

heading: Five Key Differentiating Characteristics
body:
•
Breadth of coverage: Number of commodities/sectors; some commodities have multiple
reference contracts (e.g., WTI and Brent for crude oil)
•
Weighting methodology: Production-weighted, liquidity-weighted, or fixed-weight; critically
determines concentration
•
Rolling methodology: How expiring contracts are rolled; directly impacts roll return — most
important driver of index return differences
•
Rebalancing frequency and methodology: More frequent rebalancing benefits from mean-
reverting markets; less frequent benefits from trending markets. Fixed-weight indexes rebalance
more aggressively than floating-weight. Rebalance return is the fourth component of index total
return.
•
Governance: Rules-based (quantitative methodology) vs. selection-based (committee
judgment). Independence of index provider from asset pricing and product provision is a best
practice per Index Industry Association.
