S&P GSCI — Key Features
•
Production-value weighted → highly concentrated; energy historically up to 80% of index (crude
oil single largest weight)
•
Most similar to market-cap weighting in equities — can be as concentrated as cap-weighted
emerging market indexes
•
Rolling focuses on front (near-term) contracts for maximum liquidity and supply/demand signal
sensitivity
•
Lowest rebalancing costs among the five due to production-value float; advantages in trending
markets
Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) — Key Features
•
Selection-based (committee), but rules impose diversification constraints (33% sector cap, 2%
single commodity floor)
•
Energy sector ~30% vs. S&P GSCI's ~80% — much more diversified
•
Natural gas ~9% weight (vs. ~3% in GSCI) — nat. gas has historically had ~19% annualized roll
cost; this is a structural headwind that requires offsetting from price and rebalance return
•
Rolling: front month to next or second month
Deutsche Bank Liquid Commodity Index (DBLCI) — Key Features
•
Most UNIQUE rolling methodology: rolls to the contract within the next 12 months with the
maximum backwardation / minimum contango ratio (not just front month)
•
Example: if July copper is 1% backwardation but July contract implies 1%/month while August
copper implies 3%/2 months = 1.5%/month → DBLCI rolls to August
•
Actively optimizes roll return — only index doing this
•
No livestock exposure
Thomson Reuters/CoreCommodity CRB Index (TR/CC CRB) — Key Features

•
Oldest investable commodity index (CRB started 1957; current version 2005)
•
Fixed weight; tiered by committee; rolls mechanically to front or 2nd front month
•
Monthly rebalancing — benefits when commodity prices mean-revert; less effective in trending
markets
Rogers International Commodity Index (RICI) — Key Features
•
38 commodities — most diverse; designed by investor Jim Rogers, late 1990s
•
Includes exotic/illiquid commodities (lumber, oats, rubber; historically adzuki beans and palm oil)
•
Some constituents in non-USD currencies (rubber in JPY, cocoa in GBP) → adds FX exposure
to index returns
•
Monthly rebalancing; tiered fixed weights

heading: Rebalancing Frequency: When Does It Matter?
body:
•
Frequent rebalancing (monthly — TR/CC CRB, RICI): superior in mean-reverting markets
(buy low after underperformance, sell high after outperformance — 'locking in' gains)
•
Annual rebalancing (GSCI, BCOM, DBLCI): superior in trending markets (don't sell
outperformers too early; let them run)
•
Production-value / floating-weight (GSCI, BCOM): inherently lower rebalancing magnitude
because weights drift naturally with prices; typically lower transaction costs

heading: Index Construction Impact on Performance
body:
Rolling methodology and weighting directly determine roll return, which can dramatically differ across
indexes. Indexes inadvertently biased toward backwardation (like DBLCI) may appear to have
structurally superior performance. All five major indexes have been highly correlated (>70%) with each
other and have had near-zero correlations with traditional asset classes (US large-cap stocks, US
bonds, international stocks). No dominant index methodology exists — relative performance depends
entirely on market environment and time period.

10. Key Formulas & Calculations
Price Return
Price Return: (Current Price − Previous Price) ÷ Previous Price

Roll Return
Roll Return: [(Near-Term Futures Price − Farther-Term Futures Price) ÷ Near-Term Futures
Price] × % of Position Rolled
Sign check: backwardation → near > far → numerator positive → positive roll return. Contango → near
< far → negative roll return.

Collateral Return
Collateral Return: Risk-Free Rate × Period Length as Fraction of Year

Total Return (Fully Collateralized)

Total Return: Price Return + Roll Return + Collateral Return

Theory of Storage Pricing
Futures Price: Spot Price + Direct Storage Costs − Convenience Yield

Calendar Spread
Calendar Spread: Near-Term Futures Price − Longer-Term Futures Price
Positive spread = backwardation; Negative spread = contango

Worked Example: Soybean Trade (Practice Problem 7)
Long soybeans at 865.0 cents/bushel. 3 months later: near-term = 877.0; farther-term = 883.0.
Collateral rate: 0.60% p.a. Entire position rolled.
Price return = (877.0 − 865.0)/865.0 = +1.387%
Roll return = [(877.0 − 883.0)/877.0] × 100% = −0.684%  [contango, negative]
Collateral return = (3/12) × 0.60% = +0.150%
Total Return: 1.387% − 0.684% + 0.150% = 0.853%  ≈ 0.85%

Worked Example: Total Return Swap Settlement (Practice Problem 8)
S&P GSCI total return swap, notional $25m, monthly resets. May index = 2,582.23; June index =
2,525.21.
June return = (2,525.21 − 2,582.23)/2,582.23 = −2.2082%
Long position (swap buyer) pays because index declined:
June Payment (Long owes): $25,000,000 × −2.2082% = −$552,042.23

11. High-Yield Exam Tips

heading: Backwardation vs. Contango — Core Logic
body:
•
Backwardation: spot > near futures > far futures. Calendar spread = POSITIVE. Long investor
benefits — contracts bought cheaply converge UP to spot.
•
Contango: spot < near futures < far futures. Calendar spread = NEGATIVE. Long investor loses
— contracts bought expensively converge DOWN to spot.
•
Neither state persists permanently — they can shift rapidly during supply disruptions or
structural market changes.
•
Roll return ≈ 3% correlation with price return (near zero) — knowing the roll return tells you
almost nothing about price direction.

heading: Theory Identification
body:
•
Insurance theory (Keynes): futures in backwardation because producers sell forward; speculator
receives risk premium. Implies ALWAYS backwardation — empirically rejected.

•
Hedging pressure: if consumer pressure > producer pressure → contango. If balanced → flat.
Key signals: 'consumers more concerned than producers' = contango.
•
Theory of storage: convenience yield high (scarce inventory) → backwardation. 'Consumed in
real-time, minimal storage' (energy) → lower convenience yield → more likely backwardation
context from storage perspective.
•
If exam question says 'convenience yield > direct storage costs' → futures < spot →
backwardation.

heading: Participant Type Identification
body:
•
Arbitrageur: has storage facilities, capitalizes on mispricing between spot + storage + financing
vs. futures
•
Liquidity provider: provides insurance to hedgers; earns expected profit from absorbing timing
mismatch between producer and consumer hedging needs
•
Hedger: mitigates cash flow risk; opportunity cost only, not actual loss (can make/take delivery)
•
Hedging vs. speculating is NOT the same as long vs. short — both positions can serve either
purpose

heading: Index Construction
body:
•
DBLCI is the ONLY index that actively optimizes roll return (rolls to best backwardation/least
contango month within 12 months)
•
GSCI = most concentrated (energy 80%); RICI = most diversified (38 commodities); BCOM =
most constrained diversification (sector cap 33%)
•
DBLCI = only index without livestock exposure
•
Backwardation-biased indexes improve performance, contango-biased indexes hurt
performance — this is a key exam statement
•
All five major indexes >70% correlated with each other; all ~0% correlated with equities and
bonds

heading: The Three Categories of Structural Change
body:
Category
Definition
Examples
Investment
Increases the size or scope of operations —
external/inorganic growth
Equity investment, joint venture,
acquisition
Divestment
Reduces size or scope — sheds slower-
growing or lower-profitability operations
Sale (divestiture), spin off
Restructuring
Does NOT change size/scope — improves
cost structure, financing, or capital allocation
Cost restructuring, balance sheet
restructuring, reorganization

heading: The Nine Specific Restructuring Types
body:
Note: Leveraged buyouts (LBOs) are a SPECIAL CASE combining elements of all three categories — investment,
divestment, and restructuring — in sequence.

Type
Category
Key Features
Equity
Investment
Investment
Acquires <50% stake in another company. Companies remain
independent. Used for: strategic partnership, initial step toward
acquisition, undervalued target exposure.
Joint Venture
Investment
Two or more companies form and jointly control a NEW separate
company. Each contributes assets/employees/know-how. Used for:
new market entry, technology sharing, sharing risks of expansion.
Acquisition
Investment
Acquirer purchases >50% (usually 100%) of target. Target ceases to
exist as independent entity. Full financial statement consolidation.
Acquirer gains full operational control.
Sale
(Divestiture)
Divestment
Seller sells a company, segment, or asset group to an acquirer for
cash/stock. Seller no longer exposed to the divested business.
Raises liquidity and/or sharpens focus.
Spin Off
Divestment
Parent separates a business into a new independent company. Stock
dividend to parent shareholders — they now own two separate
publicly traded entities. Parent raises no cash. Improves focus,
removes incompatibilities.
Cost
Restructuring
Restructuring
Reduces costs to improve operational efficiency/profitability — often
targeting peer-median margins. Methods: outsourcing, offshoring.
Typically involves headcount reductions.
Balance Sheet
Restructuring
Restructuring
Alters the composition of assets or capital structure. Examples: sale
leaseback (asset → cash + lease liability), dividend recapitalization
(equity → debt).
Reorganization
Restructuring
Court-supervised restructuring for companies facing insolvency.
Business continues operating. Negotiate with creditors for debt-to-
equity conversion, asset sales, refinancing. Exit with lighter debt
burden.

LBO
Special Case
Leveraged acquisition by PE fund using significant debt. FCF during
ownership used to pay down debt. Exit via sale or IPO. Returns
driven by: purchase price, leverage, FCF, and exit price.

heading: Top-Down Drivers (Apply to All Three Categories)
body:
Macro Drivers of Restructuring Activity
PROCYCLICALITY: All restructuring types correlate strongly with equity prices and economic
expansions (BCG: 0.80 correlation vs. MSCI World, 2000–2019). Activity falls in recessions.
LOWER FINANCING COSTS: High equity prices = lower ERP = less dilution from equity-financed
deals. Low rates = cheaper debt financing. Both make transactions more attractive.
CEO CONFIDENCE: Rising markets increase CEO willingness to execute large, transformational
transactions.
MANAGEMENT OVERVALUATION THEORY: If mgmt believes stock is overvalued, equity-financed
acquisitions allow them to exchange overvalued shares for real assets.

INDUSTRY SHOCKS: Regulatory changes, technological disruption, or changes in industry growth
rates drive waves of sector-specific restructuring activity.
COUNTER-INTUITIVE: BCG found 'weak economy' deals generate ~10% higher 3-year TSR vs.
'strong economy' deals — risk-taking during stress creates value.

heading: Sale vs. Spin Off — Key Decision Factors
body:
Factor
Sale
Spin Off
Liquidity
raised
High — seller receives cash (or stock)
Low — parent receives no cash;
shareholders get shares in new company
Regulatory
scrutiny
Higher — may face antitrust review
(increases market power)
Lower — reduces market concentration,
rarely blocked
Valuation
certainty
Definitive — deal price is agreed upfront
Uncertain — depends on market reception
post-separation
Best for
Liquidity needs, many interested acquirers,
need for capital
Focus, conglomerate discount, removing
incompatible businesses, no cash urgency
Tax
treatment
Taxable event for seller on gain
Often structured as tax-free stock dividend
to shareholders
Timeline
Faster execution
Slower — must create standalone
operations, new management, separate
legal/finance functions

heading: The Four Initial Evaluation Questions
body:
Initial Evaluation: Four Questions Every Analyst Must Answer
WHAT is happening? — Read the press release, securities filings, conference call transcripts, third-
party research. Apply professional skepticism — management always frames restructurings positively.
WHY is it happening? — Identify the issuer-specific motivation (growth, synergies, focus, liquidity,
distress) and any top-down drivers (high asset prices, industry shock).
IS IT MATERIAL? — Assessed along two dimensions: SIZE and FIT.

WHEN is it happening? — Note the announcement-to-close gap (often 6–12+ months for large
transactions). The market prices in a risk-adjusted expected impact upon announcement.

heading: Materiality: Size and Fit
body:
Dimension
Definition
Rule of Thumb
Size
How large is the restructuring relative to the
issuer?
For acquisitions: transaction value (cash
paid + stock issued + debt assumed) > 10%
of acquirer's pre-announcement enterprise
value = LARGE/MATERIAL. For cost
restructurings: announced cost reduction as
% of revenue or opex.
Fit
How does this action align with strategy and
analyst expectations?
A SMALL acquisition that changes strategy
may be more material than a LARGE one
that is consistent with it. Example:
Farfetch's acquisition of New Guards (8% of
EV) — small financially but massive
strategic signal → stock fell 45%.

!
Materiality has TWO dimensions — size AND fit. A large transaction (>10% of EV) is material on
size alone. But a small transaction that signals a strategy shift may be equally material on fit.
The junior analyst error at Stratton: claiming immateriality solely because the acquisition fit
strategy — the correct answer was that it WAS material because consideration exceeded 10%
of EV (USD40bn / USD170bn = 23.5%).

heading: Timing Considerations
body:
Timing in Restructuring Evaluation
Announcement-to-close gap: Small cost restructuring = months. Large acquisition or spin off = 12+
months from announcement to close.
Financial statement impact: NONE until deal closes. No consolidation of revenues/expenses/assets
until the closing date.
Market pricing: Capital market participants discount the EXPECTED impact (probability-weighted) at
announcement. Security prices move on day 1, not on close.
Key approvals required: Shareholder approval (large transactions), regulatory/antitrust clearance in
each operating jurisdiction. Sector-specific scrutiny: tech, telecom, defence, financial services.
Announcement-day returns ≠ long-term outcomes: Research (Rehm & West 2016; Ben-David et al.
2020) shows NO correlation between day-1 price reaction and 2+ year TSR.

heading: Comparable Company Analysis
body:
Uses valuation multiples of listed companies similar to the target to estimate the target's standalone trading value.
Best for: spin off valuations. Acquisition multiples will typically EXCEED trading multiples because of control
premiums.

Step
Action
1. Define
peer set
Use data aggregators (Bloomberg, FactSet, Capital IQ). Match on: industry, financial
characteristics (size, revenue growth, margin, ROIC). Peer set from management's annual
disclosures is a useful starting point.
2.
Calculate
multiples
EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales (most common — capital structure neutral). P/E, EV/FCF less
common. Sector-specific: EV/Subscribers (tech), EV/Reserves (oil & gas), EV/FFO (REITs).
3.
Descriptive
statistics
Calculate mean, median, and range for each multiple.
4. Apply to
target
Multiply target's financial metric by peer median/mean multiple → estimated enterprise value.
5. Interpret
Compare estimated EV to current market price or acquisition price. A sum-of-the-parts (SOTP)
analysis uses segment-specific peer multiples for conglomerate valuation.

Advantages
Disadvantages
Reasonable approximation of value relative to 'like'
assets in the market
Comparable set may be difficult to find — especially
for unique large-cap companies (e.g., YouTube)
Data are readily available via financial data
providers
Sensitive to market mispricing — if all peers are
overvalued, the analysis gives an inflated estimate
Value derived directly from market prices — no need
for long-run DCF assumptions
Yields a TRADING price — must add a
control/takeover premium to estimate a fair
acquisition price

heading: Comparable Transaction Analysis
body:
Uses multiples paid in historical acquisitions of SIMILAR targets. Unlike company analysis, transaction multiples
INCLUDE the takeover premium because they reflect actual acquisition prices, not trading prices.

Advantages
Disadvantages
Values reflect actual deal prices — no need to
separately estimate a takeover premium
Market for corporate control is illiquid — comparable
transactions may be scarce or non-existent
Value derived from real transactions, not DCF
assumptions
Historical multiples reflect historical macro
conditions (business cycle, interest rates, tax rates)
that may not apply today

Useful for acquisitions — natural control premium
included
Risk that past acquirers over- or underpaid
(especially in auctions with multiple bidders)

Comparable Transaction Analysis — Worked Example (Medical Services)
Comparable acquistions: mean P/E = 18.3×; mean P/CF = 10.2×; mean P/BV = 3.7×; mean P/Sales =
2.4×
Analyst weighting: P/CF weighted 40% (most relevant); others 20% each — professional judgment
applied
Target metrics: EPS = $2.62; CF/share = $4.33; BV/share = $12.65; Sales/share = $22.98
Estimated values: P/E → $47.95; P/CF → $44.17; P/BV → $46.81; P/Sales → $55.15
Weighted average estimated price: ($47.95×20%) + ($44.17×40%) + ($46.81×20%) + ($55.15×20%) =
$47.65
Actual acquisition price: $55.00 → implies General Health OVERPAID by ~$7.35/share (13% above
estimated fair value)

heading: Premium Paid Analysis
body:
Takeover Premium
PRM = (DP − SP) / SP
DP = deal price per share; SP = unaffected target share price (typically from 1 week
prior to announcement)

Historical median acquisition premium (1990–2018): ~30%, range 20%–40%. Use the 'unaffected' price —
exclude any pre-announcement run-up from leaks or rumors. Use VWAP over 1–4 weeks if persistent rumors
preceded the announcement.

Which Valuation Method to Use?
SPIN OFF valuation → Comparable Company Analysis (no control premium needed; market-price-
based)
ACQUISITION/SALE valuation → Comparable Transaction Analysis (control premium embedded) +
Premium Paid Analysis
Cross-check: Use all three methods together; triangulate to a valuation range
Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP): Use segment-specific comparable company multiples to identify
conglomerate discount

heading: Acquisition Pro Forma Income Statement — Line-by-Line
body:
Income
Statement
Line
Pro Forma Adjustment
Revenue
Acquirer + Target revenues. Add revenue synergies (often zero in Year 1). Subtract any
dis-synergies (revenue lost due to incompatibilities).
Operating
Expenses
Acquirer + Target opex. Subtract cost synergies (phased in over 2–4 years). Add one-
time restructuring/integration costs (lump sum or phased).
D&A
Acquirer + Target D&A. ADD incremental amortisation of acquired intangible assets (fair
value step-up). Note: D&A increases reduce EPS but are non-cash.
Interest
Expense
Acquirer's existing interest + NEW interest on acquisition debt (new debt × new interest
rate). Deduct interest on cash used.
Income Taxes
Use EBT-weighted average of acquirer and target effective tax rates, or as guided by
issuer.
Shares
Outstanding
Acquirer existing shares + any NEW shares issued as acquisition consideration. This is a
KEY EPS driver.

EPS Impact
ΔEpro forma = (NI_combined) / (Shares_acquirer + Shares_issued)
Dilutive if: incremental interest + amortisation + share dilution > incremental earnings
from target + synergies

Net Debt/EBITDA
Net Debt/EBITDA = (Total Debt − Cash) / EBITDA
For acquisitions: Total Debt increases (new borrowings + assumed target debt);
EBITDA increases (target + synergies)

heading: Effect on WACC
body:
A restructuring changes WACC by changing both (a) the WEIGHTS of each capital type (wd, we) and (b) the
COSTS of each type (rd, re). These often move in the same direction.

Restructuring
Action
Effect on Capital Structure
Effect on WACC
Acquisition financed
entirely with debt
wd↑, we↓ (assuming stable equity
price)
Cost of debt may rise (higher leverage).
Moving from investment-grade to
speculative-grade = empirically hundreds
of bps increase in WACC.
Sale of division —
proceeds used to retire
debt
wd↓, we↑
Lower leverage → lower cost of debt →
lower WACC (if maintains investment-
grade status)

Sale-leaseback
Debt↓, operating lease liability
added (IFRS 16/ASC 842)
Reduces gross debt; may improve credit
rating → lower rd → lower WACC
Dividend
recapitalisation
Equity→Debt (replace expensive
equity with cheaper debt)
Aims to LOWER WACC by substituting
cheap after-tax debt for expensive equity.
Risk: excessive leverage can trigger rating
downgrade, raising rd.
Acquisition of higher-
risk business
Increases revenue/earnings
volatility of combined entity
Higher business risk → higher cost of
equity (β rises) → higher WACC

!
Moving from investment-grade to speculative-grade (junk) rating empirically results in a JUMP of
several hundred basis points in WACC. Investment-grade issuers will often deliberately structure
transactions (e.g., less cash, more stock) to preserve their rating.

heading: EPS Accretion/Dilution — Key Drivers
body:
What Makes an Acquisition EPS Accretive vs. Dilutive?
ACCRETIVE: If the target's earnings contribution (after synergies) > incremental interest expense +
amortisation of intangibles + dilution from new shares issued
DILUTIVE: If incremental interest + amortisation + share count dilution > target earnings contribution.
Note: a transaction can be EPS-dilutive but still NPV-positive if the synergy value exceeds the price
paid.
A HIGH-MARGIN target (even with one-time acquisition costs) can flip the deal from dilutive to
accretive quickly (e.g., AstraZeneca/Alexion: high-margin Alexion lifted AstraZeneca's operating
margin by 400–500 bps)
DEBT-FINANCED deals preserve share count but add interest expense — net effect depends on
target yield vs. interest rate
EQUITY-FINANCED deals avoid interest but dilute share count — net effect depends on relative P/E
multiples (accretive if target P/E < acquirer P/E)

heading: Equity Investment — Dilmun Inc. / Spina Ltd.
body:
Dilmun (mature tobacco company with declining volumes, ESG pressure) invests USD1.2bn for a 30% stake in
Spina (fast-growing e-vapor market leader). Financed with credit facility at 600 bps. Spina remains independent.

Equity Investment — Key Financial Statement Impacts
Accounting: equity method — Dilmun recognises 30% of Spina's net income/(loss) as 'income from
associates' on the income statement

Impact on EBITDA: income from associates = −(0.30 × Spina loss) − amortisation of FV adjustments =
−(0.30 × 450) − 10 = −$145m
Impact on interest expense: +$72m (USD1.2bn × 6% interest rate)
Impact on EPS: NI falls from $7,256m to $7,084m → EPS falls from $3.94 to $3.85 (dilutive — target
is loss-making)
Impact on Debt/EBITDA: rises from 1.37× to 1.51× (debt +$1.2bn; EBITDA −$145m)
Comparable company check: Dilmun paid 6.7× EV/Sales for Spina vs. peer median 5.1× — implies
control/synergy premium + superior growth thesis

vs. Equity
Investment
Acquisition
Advantage
Acquisition
Disadvantage
JV Advantage
JV Disadvantage
vs. Equity
Investment
Full control —
Spina cannot act
against Dilmun's
interests
Substantially
greater capital
required — riskier if
target is early-
stage
Shared
governance —
reduces Dilmun's
risk
Larger investment
required; Spina's
independence may
be part of its value
proposition

heading: Joint Venture — Opone-Hapalla Automotive Alliance (OHAA)
body:
Opone SA (Brazil, vehicle manufacturer) and Hapalla AG (Europe, vehicle brands) form OHAA 50/50 JV to sell
Hapalla vehicles in Latin America. OHAA grows from <10k to 1.5m vehicles/year over 7 years. In 20X8, Hapalla
offers to buy a further 25% stake for BRL45bn.

JV to Consolidation — Financial Statement Transition
BEFORE partial buyout: Opone records 50% of OHAA profit as 'joint venture income' (equity method).
JV is NOT consolidated.
KEY ANOMALY: In 20X7, OHAA paid BRL40bn in dividends vs. BRL15.3bn profit — dividends far
exceed profit. This explains Opone's P/E (21×) vs. P/FCF (7×): earnings understate cash flows
received.
AFTER Hapalla buys 25%: Hapalla changes from equity method → FULL CONSOLIDATION. OHAA's
revenues/expenses now appear in Hapalla's income statement. Opone reduces stake to 25% and
recognises gain on sale.
Opone gain on sale: Proceeds (BRL45bn) − Carrying value of 25% sold (BRL13bn) = BRL32bn pre-
tax gain
Opone going forward: JV income falls from 50% to 25% of OHAA profit → income falls by
~BRL4.7bn/year
OHAA implied P/E from Hapalla's offer: BRL180bn EV − BRL32bn cash = BRL148bn equity /
BRL15.3bn PAT ≈ 10× vs. peer median 12× — slight discount

heading: Acquisition — Tulor / Caracol Petroleum Retail Segment
body:
Tulor (Australian convenience stores, 6× Caracol's store count) acquires Caracol's Retail segment for AUD2bn
cash + 80m Tulor shares (total AUD3bn). Synergies: AUD125m EBITDA by Year 3. Caracol uses cash proceeds
to retire debt.

Acquisition — Pro Forma Analysis (Tulor / Caracol Retail)
Transaction multiple: AUD3bn / AUD183m EBITDA = 16× EV/EBITDA vs. peer median 8× → appears
expensive
Synergy-adjusted multiple: AUD3bn / (183 + 125)m = 10× — within peer range. Control premium +
synergies explain the premium.
EPS impact (20X3): Before = AUD0.96; After = AUD0.70 → dilutive by AUD0.26/share
Key dilution driver: +80m shares issued (648m → 728m) alone costs AUD0.26/share; incremental
amortisation (AUD200m) also depresses EPS
EBITDA impact: +AUD232m (Retail EBITDA AUD190m + AUD42m synergies in Year 3) → combined
EBITDA = AUD2,035m
Debt/EBITDA: Rises from 3.4× to 3.5× — modest increase despite AUD1bn new debt, offset by Retail
EBITDA contribution
Caracol: Uses AUD1.6bn after-tax proceeds to retire debt → balance sheet strengthened; retains
strategic equity stake in Tulor (no disposal for 5 years)

!
When an ACQUISITION target is a SEGMENT (not a whole company), the accounting is not
categorically different for the acquirer. But there is a SELLER that continues to operate after the
transaction — you must model both sides. Caracol's balance sheet improves; Tulor's EPS takes
a hit from share issuance.

heading: Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP) Valuation & Conglomerate Discount
body:
A conglomerate discount exists when the market values the company BELOW the sum of the values of its
individual business segments. SOTP analysis quantifies this using segment-specific comparable company
multiples.

SOTP Procedure — Benefit Ltd. (Consulting + BenefitsExchange)
Step 1 — Identify segments and their financial metrics (Revenue, EBITDA, or relevant driver)
Step 2 — Find peer median multiple for each segment independently (peers may differ significantly
between segments)
Step 3 — Calculate segment enterprise values: Segment EV = Metric × Peer Median Multiple

Step 4 — Subtract allocated corporate/unallocated costs from relevant segment EBITDA
Step 5 — Sum segment EVs → Total estimated EV
Step 6 — Compare to current trading EV → Conglomerate Discount = Estimated EV − Actual EV
Example: Consulting (EBITDA 80 × 9× = 721) + BenefitsExchange (Sales 75 × 12× = 900) = 1,621 vs.
trading EV 1,437 → Discount = ZAR184m
Sensitivity: Using growth-matched peer multiple (15× for BenefitsExchange) → Discount widens to
ZAR409m

!
ALWAYS allocate corporate/unallocated costs to the relevant segments in SOTP analysis. If
corporate costs are allocated proportionally by revenue, each segment's adjusted EBITDA will
be lower. Failing to do this overstates the sum-of-the-parts and the implied conglomerate
discount.

heading: Sale vs. Spin Off — Benefit Ltd. Decision
body:
Benefit Ltd. — Sale vs. Spin Off of Consulting Segment
Sale offer: ZAR800m for Consulting → EV/EBITDA ≈ 10× (bid) vs. comparable transactions median
11× → moderately undervalued on comp transactions
Sale vs. implied market value: If BenefitsExchange = 15× EV/Sales = 1,125m, then Consulting implied
by market = 1,437 − 1,125 = 312m → bid of 800m is 488m ABOVE implied market value — highly
attractive vs. current market pricing
Spin off alternative: If Consulting spun off at 13× EV/EBITDA = 13 × 80 = ZAR1,040m → much higher
than sale price of ZAR800m
But: Spin off valuation is uncertain; sale is definitive. Capital market correction could erode spin off
value.
Pro forma EPS after sale + ZAR800m ASR (200m shares repurchased): Net loss of ZAR25m →
diluted EPS of −1.99c (vs. +2.41c before) — selling a profitable segment while BenefitsExchange is
still loss-making makes consolidated results worse
Conclusion: Spin off at 13× is superior if market cooperates; sale at 10× is safer but forgoes
~ZAR240m in value vs. spin off

heading: Valuation in Spin Offs — Wang / Media-Connectivity Example
body:
Spin Off Valuation — SOTP (Wang, Choice Fund)
Company EV: EUR96,380m at 10× EV/EBITDA (consolidated 9,638m EBITDA)
Connectivity EBITDA: EUR7,638m × peer median 13× = EUR99,294m
Media EBITDA: EUR2,000m × peer median 6× = EUR12,000m

Total estimated SOTP EV: EUR111,294m vs. current EUR96,380m → ~15% upside from spin off
Important caveat: Media may not trade at peer median 6× due to under-investment by current
management → lower near-term FCF expected. Use range of multiples.
Corporate cost allocation: If EUR250m HQ costs are 28.5% Media / 71.5% Connectivity (by revenue
split), Media's adjusted EBITDA = 2,000 − 71 = EUR1,929m
EV multiples are capital-structure neutral → colleague's concern about debt allocation is valid only if
leverage significantly exceeds peers (raises WACC), not from the EV multiple calculation itself

heading: Cost Restructuring — Cyrene SARL
body:
Cyrene received and rejected an unsolicited acquisition offer (20% premium). Large shareholders warned that the
low-profitability business was a takeover target. Cyrene responded by launching a cost restructuring review.

Cost Restructuring — Cyrene SARL Analysis
Cyrene EBIT margin: 14% vs. peer median 20% → gap = 6 percentage points to close
Operating expenses to cut: EUR16,331m opex × 6% = EUR1,139m (7% of opex) to reach peer
median margin
Pro forma (Year 1): Revenue growth 3% → revenues EUR19,560m; EBIT margin improves by

heading: %/year (6%/4 years) → EBIT ex. costs = EUR3,032m (15.5% margin)
body:
One-time restructuring costs: EUR1,250m in Year 1 → Pro forma EBIT = EUR1,782m (9.1% margin
— appears worse temporarily)
Key risks: (1) Faster revenue growth (4% CAGR vs. peers' −2% to +2.5%) may be driven by spending
being cut → cutting costs may kill the growth advantage; (2) Political risk — layoffs and facility
closures create government/public pressure
Alternative (Colleague's proposal): Sell/spin off Household Goods segment → removes lowest-margin
business → pro forma EBIT margin improves from 14% to 16%, but total EBIT FALLS by 19%
(EUR2,659m → EUR2,163m)
Analytical gap: Must also estimate Household Goods valuation, synergy/dis-synergy with remaining
segments, and whether corporate costs can be reduced if segment is separated

heading: Balance Sheet Restructuring — Kosala Corp. Sale-Leaseback
body:
Kosala (global omnichannel retailer) owns distribution center real estate. E-commerce growth has driven cap
rates on distribution real estate to attractive levels. Kosala sells 100% of owned distribution centers via sale-
leaseback to REITs at a 4.5% cap rate, raises CHF425m, retires CHF215m debt, repurchases 10m shares.

Sale-Leaseback — Kosala Corp. Pro Forma Analysis
Transaction valuation: 4.5% cap rate = 1/4.5% = 22.2× net operating income multiple → compares to
peer 50th percentile cap rate of 5.5% → Kosala's rate is 100 bps BELOW median → FAVOURABLE
pricing for Kosala (lower cap rate = higher valuation for seller)
Annual income statement effects: +CHF19m lease expense; −CHF30m depreciation; −CHF15m
interest (debt retired) → net annual D&A change = −17m (10m depreciation savings less 13m gain
amortisation over 15 yrs)
Pro forma Debt/EBITDA: 3.2× → 2.3× (gross debt CHF615m → CHF400m; EBITDA improves from
D&A reduction)
Pro forma EBIT/Interest (IC ratio): 2.9× → 4.3× (interest falls CHF43m → CHF28m)
Credit rating implication: Debt/EBITDA 2.3× + IC 4.3× → falls in BBB range (from speculative). BBB
spread = 450 bps + 125 bps Treasury = 575 bps → interest on CHF400m = CHF23m → additional
CHF5m savings vs. pre-upgrade interest
EPS impact: NI rises CHF61m → CHF70m; shares fall from 97m to 87m (repurchase) → EPS rises
from CHF0.63 to CHF0.81
Key takeaway: Sale-leaseback unlocks non-core real estate value, improves credit rating, reduces
cost of debt — but replaces depreciation with lease expense (usually higher total cost) and adds a
lease liability on the balance sheet

Sale-
Leaseback
Benefits
Costs/Risks
For the
seller/lessee
Receive cash up front immediately.
Reduce costs of ownership (obsolescence,
maintenance, disposal). Can use proceeds
for debt retirement or share repurchases.
May achieve better credit rating → lower
WACC.
Annual lease expense typically EXCEEDS
prior depreciation expense (lessor earns
interest return). Adds operating lease
liability to balance sheet (IFRS 16 / ASC
842). Lose upside from asset appreciation.
Operational constraints from lease terms.

heading: Reorganization vs. Liquidation — Six Flags
body:
Process
Reorganization (Chapter 11)
Liquidation
Trigger
Company cannot service debt but
business operations remain viable
Reorganization failed; company cannot
pay debts even after restructuring
Who controls
Court-supervised; existing management
typically remains in place
Bankruptcy court takes full control and
disposes of assets
Outcome
Reach agreement with creditors: debt-to-
equity conversion, asset sales,
refinancing. Company SURVIVES with
lighter debt burden.
All assets sold; proceeds distributed to
creditors in strict priority order. Company
CEASES to exist.

Equity outcome
Prior equity may be wiped out (as with Six
Flags) or receive residual stake if value
supports it
Equity shareholders receive nothing
unless all creditors fully repaid (extremely
rare)
Speed
Can be pre-negotiated ('pre-pack') —
sometimes less than 24 hours. Typical =
months to years.
Faster finality, but often lower recovery
values due to fire-sale liquidation pricing

Six Flags — Reorganization Case Study
2006–2009: Revenue stagnation, EBIT fell 50% (2005→2006). High operating leverage + high
financial leverage = fatal combination. Net Debt/EBITDA rose to 12.7× by 2007.
Failed credit refinancing in recession (2008–2009) + mandatory preferred dividend payments →
missed interest payments → default.
June 2009: Chapter 11 filing. Theme parks continued operating throughout. Cost restructuring
improved profitability but couldn't overcome debt burden.
Outcome (May 2010): Bondholders invested USD725m new equity + converted >USD1bn existing
debt to equity → net debt fell to USD784m (<3× expected 2010 EBITDA). Prior equity holders lost
100% of investment.
June 2010: Re-listed on NYSE as Six Flags Entertainment Corp. — same business, drastically
different balance sheet.
Lesson: Reorganisation preserves BUSINESS value while eliminating FINANCIAL structure that was
unsustainable. Old equity bears the cost.

heading: Key Formulas
body:
Takeover Premium
PRM = (Deal Price − Unaffected Price) / Unaffected Price
Historical median ~30%; range 20%–40% (1990–2018). Use 1-week prior price, or
VWAP to avoid rumour contamination.

EPS (post-
acquisition)
New EPS = (NI_acquirer + NI_target + Synergies − Incremental interest −
Incremental amortisation × (1−t)) / Total shares
Accretive if new EPS > pre-acquisition EPS; dilutive if lower

Net Debt/EBITDA
(Total Debt − Cash) / EBITDA
Key credit metric. Increases in acquisitions (debt rises, EBITDA rises less).
Decreases in divestitures/sale-leasebacks.


Cap Rate (Real
Estate)
Cap Rate = Net Operating Income / Property Value
Reciprocal of a valuation multiple. Lower cap rate = higher valuation. Seller prefers
lower cap rate; buyer prefers higher.

SOTP EV
Total EV = Σ(Segment EBITDA_i × Peer Multiple_i)
Allocate corporate/unallocated costs to segments first. Conglomerate Discount =
SOTP EV − Actual Trading EV.

Conglomerate
Discount
Discount = SOTP EV − Actual Trading EV
Positive discount = sum of parts > whole → potential value unlock from separation

heading: Exam Traps — Master Reference
body:
!
Materiality has TWO dimensions: SIZE (>10% of acquirer EV rule of thumb) and FIT (does this
action signal a strategy change?). A transaction can be IMMATERIAL on size but HIGHLY
MATERIAL on fit. Both dimensions must be evaluated independently.

!
Announcement-day share price reaction does NOT predict long-term deal outcomes. Research
shows zero correlation between day-1 price reaction and 2+ year excess TSR. Do not use short-
term price reaction as the primary evaluation metric.

!
Pro forma financial statements DO NOT appear until the CLOSING DATE — not the
announcement date. The announcement creates a market price adjustment, but no P&L or
balance sheet changes occur until close. This can be 6–18 months later for large transactions.

!
Comparable Company Analysis yields a TRADING value — to estimate a
TAKEOVER/ACQUISITION price, you must ADD a control premium. Comparable Transaction
Analysis already embeds the control premium (these are actual acquisition prices). Know which
method is appropriate for which question.

!
EQUITY-financed acquisitions: accretive if target P/E < acquirer P/E (acquirer issues fewer
shares relative to earnings acquired). DEBT-financed acquisitions: accretive if target earnings
yield > after-tax cost of debt. Both can be EPS-dilutive yet still create NPV-positive value if
synergy NPV > price premium paid.

!
In sale-leasebacks: annual LEASE EXPENSE is typically HIGHER than prior depreciation
expense (the lessor earns an interest return on the transaction). This is a NET COST on the
income statement, even though gross debt falls. Do not assume the balance sheet improvement
is 'free'.

!
SPIN OFF vs. SALE: In a spin off, the PARENT RECEIVES NO CASH — existing shareholders
receive shares in the new independent company. In a SALE, the parent receives CASH (or
stock of the acquirer). If the company needs liquidity, only a sale achieves it. This is frequently
tested.


!
REORGANISATION: existing management typically REMAINS IN PLACE throughout the
Chapter 11 process (unlike liquidation where court takes control). Business OPERATIONS
CONTINUE NORMALLY. Prior EQUITY HOLDERS often lose 100% — debt holders receive the
reorganised company's equity.

!
EQUITY INVESTMENT accounting: investor recognises its PROPORTIONAL SHARE of the
investee's NET INCOME/(LOSS) — not dividends received — under the equity method. If the
investee is loss-making (like Spina), the investor records a proportional LOSS. This reduces the
investor's reported EBITDA.

!
JV to CONSOLIDATION trigger: when a company acquires a controlling interest (typically
>50%), it shifts from EQUITY METHOD to FULL CONSOLIDATION. The JV investment is de-
recognised; ALL of the JV's revenues, expenses, assets, and liabilities are brought on to the
acquirer's balance sheet. A non-controlling interest (NCI) is recognised for the remaining
partner's stake.

!
DIVIDEND RECAPITALISATION lowers WACC only if the company is not already over-
leveraged. Excessive leverage can trigger a credit rating downgrade, which raises the cost of
BOTH debt AND equity, potentially INCREASING WACC and destroying value. The strategy
works best with stable, predictable cash flows.

heading: Type Classification Quick-Reference
body:
Restructuring Type
Category
Cash to
Issuer?
Financial Statement Impact
Equity Investment
(<50%)

investee NI; D/EBITDA rises
Joint Venture
Investment

shift to consolidation on control acquisition

Investment
Outflow
Full consolidation —
revenues/expenses/assets/liabilities
combined; goodwill recognised; EPS often
diluted initially
Sale
Divestment

Gain/loss on disposal; discontinued operations
treatment; D/EBITDA falls (if proceeds used to
retire debt)
Spin Off
Divestment
None to
issuer
Shareholders receive shares of new company
as stock dividend; segment no longer
consolidated
Cost Restructuring
Restructuring
None
(outflow for
one-time
costs)
One-time charges lower near-term EPS;
ongoing opex savings improve EBIT margin
over 2–4 years
Sale-Leaseback
Restructuring
Inflow
Gain on sale (amortised or recognised);
D&A↓, lease expense↑; D/EBITDA falls; IC
ratio rises

Dividend
Recapitalisation
Restructuring
None (to
shareholders)
D/EBITDA rises (more debt); EPS may rise
(fewer shares); WACC may fall if not over-
leveraged
Reorganisation
Restructuring
Negotiated
Debt-to-equity conversion; prior equity
typically wiped out; balance sheet emerges
with lower leverage
LBO
