Outflow (then
inflow on exit)
Take private → massive leverage increase;
FCF used to pay down debt; exit via IPO or
sale

section_title: WACC FRAMEWORK AND COST OF CAPITAL
heading: Top-Down (External/Systematic) Factors
body:
Top-Down Factor
Effect on Cost of Capital
Capital Availability
Greater capital availability → more favourable terms → LOWER cost of capital.
Developed markets have deeper, more liquid capital markets. Less developed
markets may rely on shadow banking, raising costs.
Market Conditions —
Interest Rates & Credit
Spreads
Lower rates + tighter spreads → lower cost of debt & equity. Higher inflation
(embedded in rf) → higher cost of capital. Credit spreads widen in recessions,
tighten in expansions. Business cycle timing matters.
Legal & Regulatory
Environment
Common law jurisdictions → stronger investor protections → lower ERP and
credit spreads → lower cost of capital. Civil law jurisdictions → weaker
protections → higher costs. Country risk is embedded here.
Tax Jurisdiction
Higher marginal corporate tax rate → greater tax shield benefit of debt →
LOWER after-tax cost of debt. If interest is NOT tax-deductible (or company has
no taxable income), no WACC reduction from leverage.

section_title: WACC FRAMEWORK AND COST OF CAPITAL
heading: Bottom-Up (Company-Specific) Factors
body:
Bottom-Up Factor
Effect on Cost of Capital
Revenue, Earnings &
Cash Flow Volatility
Subscription/recurring revenue → stable cash flows → LOWER cost of capital.
Cyclical industries, pay-per-use models, customer concentration risk →
HIGHER volatility → HIGHER cost. High operating leverage (fixed costs) or
high financial leverage amplifies earnings volatility → HIGHER cost.
ESG Risks
High ESG risk (significant carbon footprint, poor employee safety, weak
governance) → higher perceived financial risk (litigation, fines, boycotts, anti-
takeover provisions) → HIGHER cost of capital. Analysts can reflect ESG risk in
either the discount rate OR adjusted cash flow forecasts.
Asset Nature & Liquidity
Tangible assets → can pledge as collateral → LOWER cost of debt. Intangible-
heavy companies cannot pledge goodwill/patents → HIGHER cost.
Fungible/liquid assets (cash, marketable securities) → LOWER cost.
Specialised, illiquid PP&E → HIGHER cost. Note: collateralised assets lower
cost of DEBT but may raise cost of EQUITY (creditors have prior claim on those
assets).
Financial Strength &
Leverage
Higher profitability, stronger cash flow, higher IC ratio, lower leverage →
LOWER cost. Higher net debt/EBITDA, lower IC → higher probability of default
→ HIGHER credit spread → HIGHER cost of capital.
Security Features —
Debt
Callable: issuer benefits → investors demand HIGHER yield at issuance.
Putable: investor benefits → LOWER yield at issuance (but future refinancing
cost may rise). Convertible: investor benefits → LOWER yield at issuance (but
equity dilution risk later).
Security Features —
Equity
Cumulative preferred: investor benefits → LOWER required return vs. non-
cumulative. Dual-class shares (inferior cash flow/voting rights) → HIGHER cost
of equity for inferior share class.


Security Feature Cost of Capital Summary
CALLABLE debt → HIGHER initial cost of debt (call is issuer benefit)
PUTABLE debt → LOWER initial cost of debt (put is investor benefit)
CONVERTIBLE debt → LOWER initial cost of debt (conversion is investor benefit)
CUMULATIVE preferred → LOWER cost vs. non-cumulative (greater investor protection)
Memory aid: If the feature benefits the INVESTOR → lower initial yield; if it benefits the ISSUER →
higher initial yield

heading: Methods by Debt Type
body:
Debt Type
Estimation Method
Traded Straight Debt
Use YTM on the company's existing debt (longest maturity, or most liquid if
short-term bonds trade more actively). YTM reflects the current market cost of
issuing similar debt. Most direct and reliable method.
Non-Traded / Illiquid
Debt — with rating
Matrix pricing: use YTMs of comparable bonds (same credit rating, similar
maturity, similar features) to estimate a YTM for the subject company. Average
YTMs across maturities, then interpolate for the target maturity.
Non-Traded / Illiquid
Debt — no rating
Synthetic credit rating: use fundamental ratios (IC ratio, D/E) to infer a credit
rating class. Then use the YTM or credit spread for that rating class + risk-free
rate to estimate cost of debt. Requires proprietary or published rating-to-ratio
mapping.
Bank Debt
Use the interest rate on the company's most recent new bank borrowing as a
proxy — if conditions have not materially changed since issuance. Amortising
loans typically have lower cost than bullet (non-amortising) loans due to lower
default risk.
Finance Lease (IFRS 16
/ ASC 842)
Use the rate implicit in the lease (RIIL) — the discount rate equating PV(lease
payments) + PV(residual value) to FV(leased asset) + lessor direct costs. If RIIL
is unknown, use the incremental borrowing rate (IBR). Leases are typically
cheaper than unsecured borrowing (secured by the asset).
International / Emerging
Market Debt
Add a country risk premium (CRP) to the domestic cost of debt estimate. CRP
derived from country risk ratings or sovereign yield spreads relative to
benchmark developed market.

heading: Matrix Pricing — Step-by-Step
body:
Matrix Pricing Procedure (BBB-rated, 7-year bond — Gretna Engines)
Step 1: Find liquid comparable bonds (same credit rating, bracket target maturity). Calculate YTM for
each.
Step 2: Average YTMs at each known maturity bracket (e.g., 4-year avg = 5.166%; 8-year avg =

Step 3: Linear interpolation — spread incremental yield evenly across years between known
maturities
Calculation: 8-yr YTM − 4-yr YTM = 6.011% − 5.166% = 0.845% over 4 years → 0.211% per year
increment
7-year interpolated YTM: 5.166% + 3 × 0.211% = 5.799% ≈ 5.8%
Note: Add a LIQUIDITY PREMIUM if the subject company's bonds are less liquid than the
comparables used

heading: Synthetic Credit Rating
body:
Build a mapping table from IC ratio and D/E ratio to rating classes. When ratios give conflicting signals across
rating categories, examine TRENDS in those ratios. Use judgment — trending ratios may warrant an upgrade or
downgrade from the current implied class.

Lease RIIL
PV(Lease Payments) + PV(Residual Value) = FV(Leased Asset) + Lessor
Direct Costs
Solve for the discount rate. Calculator: N=term, PV=−(FV_asset + costs),
PMT=payment, FV=residual → CPT I/Y

!
After-tax cost of debt = rd × (1 − t) ONLY if interest expense is tax-deductible AND the company
has sufficient taxable income to benefit. If the company is loss-making or interest deductibility is
capped, use the pre-tax rate. This is an easy trap to fall into in complex scenarios.

!
Putable bonds have a LOWER initial yield than callable bonds — the put benefits the investor,
who accepts lower compensation for holding it. Callable bonds have a HIGHER initial yield —
investors demand a premium for the issuer's right to call. Don't confuse which party holds the
option.

heading: Historical (Ex-Post) Approach
body:
Historical ERP
ERP = Mean(Equity Index Return − Risk-Free Rate)
Calculated over a historical sample period. Assumes returns are stationary.

Four key decisions an analyst must make:

Decision
Options & Trade-offs
1. Equity Index
Use a broad-based, market-value-weighted index representative of equity
investor experience (e.g., S&P 500, Russell 3000, MSCI EAFE, Australia All
Ordinaries). Narrow indexes introduce survivorship bias.
2. Time Period
Longer series: reduces noise / standard error; assumes ERP has not
permanently shifted. Risk: distant past not representative. Shorter series: more
current but noisier; may exclude or over-weight unusual periods (GFC, COVID).
Industry tends to favour longer periods.
3. Mean Type —
Arithmetic
Best for single-period returns. Easy to calculate. Consistent with CAPM (single-
period model). OVERESTIMATES expected terminal wealth; sensitive to
extreme values.
3. Mean Type —
Geometric
Compound rate from beginning to end value. Less sensitive to outliers.
Consistent with expected TERMINAL WEALTH estimates. Generally preferred;
both are used in practice.
4. Risk-Free Rate Proxy
— Short-term T-bill
Zero-coupon structure → truly known return (no reinvestment risk).
Disadvantage: does not match the duration of an infinite-life equity security.
4. Risk-Free Rate Proxy
— Long-term gov't bond
YTM
Matches duration of equity better. Disadvantage: not truly risk-free due to
coupon reinvestment rate uncertainty. Industry practice favours this option.

Historical ERP Biases
SURVIVORSHIP BIAS: Poorly performing or defunct companies are removed from index history →
only relative winners remain → UPWARD bias in historical returns and ERP
NON-STATIONARITY: If the ERP has permanently shifted (e.g., lower risk aversion, better
diversification), long historical averages are unrepresentative of future expectations
SAMPLE PERIOD BIAS: A sample dominated by a major disruption (war, GFC) → DOWNWARD
bias; a bull market-heavy sample → UPWARD bias

heading: Forward-Looking (Ex-Ante) Approaches
body:
Forward-looking ERP estimates are based on current market prices and expectations. Less subject to non-
stationarity and survivorship biases vs. historical approach. Three main methods:

Method 1: Survey-Based Estimates


Ask experts or market participants for their ERP expectations. Develops: simple, intuitive. Developing markets →
higher survey ERP than developed. Main limitation: results are sensitive to recent market performance (recency
bias), sampling bias, and confirmation bias.

Method 2: Constant Growth DDM (Gordon Growth Model)

DDM-Based ERP
ERP = E(D₁/V₀) + E(g) − rf
E(D₁/V₀) = forward dividend yield of broad market index; E(g) = expected long-term
earnings growth rate

Underlying assumption: constant P/E (earnings, dividends, and prices grow at the same rate). If the analyst
expects P/E expansion or contraction, an adjustment is required. Some analysts include buyback yield in D₁/V₀ to
capture total payout.

DDM ERP Example — FTSE 100
Forward dividend yield E(D₁/V₀): 1.94%
Expected long-term earnings growth E(g): 5.00%
Long-term Gilt yield (rf): 1.63%
ERP = 1.94% + 5.00% − 1.63% = 5.31%
Sensitivity range (analyst forecasts): 3.0% to 8.0% — wide range highlights estimation uncertainty

Method 3: Macroeconomic Modeling — Grinold-Kroner Model

Grinold-Kroner ERP
ERP = [DY + Δ(P/E) + i + g − ΔS] − rf
DY = dividend yield; Δ(P/E) = expected P/E change; i = expected inflation; g = real
earnings growth; ΔS = dilution (change in shares outstanding)

Implied Inflation
i ≈ YTM_Treasury − YTM_TIPS
Exact: i = (1 + YTM_nominal)/(1 + YTM_real) − 1

G-K Factor
Proxy / Note
DY — Dividend yield
Broad market index dividend yield (income component)
Δ(P/E) — P/E change
Analyst's view on market over/undervaluation; commonly set to 0 as base case
i — Expected inflation
Nominal gov't bond yield minus TIPS/inflation-linked bond yield
g — Real earnings
growth
Real GDP growth rate
ΔS — Dilution effect
Net change in shares outstanding; varies by country; assume 0 unless specified


Grinold-Kroner Key Insight
If BOTH expected inflation (i) AND the risk-free rate (rf) increase by the same amount → ERP is
UNCHANGED (both cancel out)
Only a change in DY, Δ(P/E), g, or ΔS affects the ERP in isolation
Model is inappropriate for countries where public equities represent a small fraction of the economy
(most developing markets)

ERP Approach
Key Limitations
Historical
Non-stationarity; survivorship bias; sample period may not be representative;
arithmetic mean overestimates terminal wealth
Survey
Recency bias; confirmation bias; sampling/response biases; anchored to recent
returns
DDM
Assumes constant P/E ratio; must adjust for P/E expansion/contraction;
sensitive to growth assumption
Grinold-Kroner
Modeling error in macro forecasts; behavioral biases in variable estimation;
inappropriate for small/developing equity markets

heading: Method 1: Dividend Discount Model (DDM)
body:
DDM — Constant
Growth
re = D₁/P₀ + g
D₁ = next expected dividend; P₀ = current share price; g = constant perpetual growth
rate

DDM — Multi-Stage
(IRR Form)
P₀ = Σ[Dₜ/(1+re)ᵗ] + Pₙ/(1+re)ⁿ
Solve for re as IRR. Excel: =IRR({-P₀, D₁, D₂, ..., Dₙ+Pₙ}). Python: np.irr([...])

Requirements: Publicly traded shares; company pays dividends that are stable and predictable. Not suitable for
non-dividend-paying companies or those with irregular dividends.

heading: Method 2: Bond Yield Plus Risk Premium (BYPRP)
body:
BYPRP
re = rd + RP
rd = YTM on company's long-term debt; RP = historical equity-over-corporate-bond
risk premium

BYPRP
Details

Advantage
Starting from company's own cost of debt provides a logical anchor; intuitive build-up
logic
Disadvantage — RP
estimation
RP is determined by the historical mean difference between equity index and
corporate bond index returns; somewhat arbitrary; historically has been ~3–6% in
developed markets
Disadvantage —
debt selection
If company has multiple outstanding bonds with different features/maturities, which
YTM to use? Convention: use long-term bond YTM
Disadvantage — no
public debt
Not applicable if company has no publicly traded debt

heading: Method 3: Risk-Based Models
body:
CAPM (Single-Factor)

CAPM
re = rf + β̂ × ERP
β̂ estimated by regressing excess stock returns on excess market returns: (ri−rf) = b₀
+ b₁(rm−rf) + ε

Key analyst considerations: (1) Equity index used as market proxy — should be broad-based. (2) Estimation
period for beta — balance data sufficiency vs. representativeness. (3) Risk-free rate proxy — short-term bill gives
higher re in normal upward-sloping curve environment.

Fama-French Three-Factor Model

FF3
re = rf + β₁(ERP) + β₂(SMB) + β₃(HML)
SMB = size premium (small minus big cap returns); HML = value premium (high
minus low book-to-market returns)

FF Factor
Description
SMB — Size
Premium
Average return difference between SMALL-cap and LARGE-cap stocks. Small-cap
stocks have historically earned higher returns (higher risk).
HML — Value
Premium
Average return difference between HIGH book-to-market (value stocks) and LOW
book-to-market (growth stocks). Value stocks have historically outperformed growth
stocks.

Fama-French Five-Factor Model

FF5
re = rf + β₁(ERP) + β₂(SMB) + β₃(HML) + β₄(RMW) + β₅(CMA)
RMW = profitability premium (robust minus weak); CMA = investment premium
(conservative minus aggressive)


FF5 Additional Factor
Description
RMW — Profitability
Premium
Average return difference between companies with ROBUST vs. WEAK
profitability. Higher profitability → higher returns.
CMA — Investment
Premium
Average return difference between companies with CONSERVATIVE vs.
AGGRESSIVE investment spending. Conservative capex firms historically
outperform.

!
The market factor beta (β on ERP) will differ between the CAPM and Fama-French models
because additional factors absorb some of the explained variance. Do NOT compare a CAPM
beta directly with a Fama-French market beta — they are not the same parameter.

heading: Private Company Cost of Equity
body:
Private companies lack observable market prices and returns, preventing direct application of market models.
Additional risk premiums are required to capture company-specific risks not captured by public market betas.
Private companies are typically smaller, less liquid, may have concentrated ownership, and disclose less
information.

Private Company
Premium
Description
Size Premium (SP)
Inversely related to company size. Reflects greater difficulty accessing capital,
more uncertain growth, and higher operational risk for smaller firms. Often
based on historical return data for lowest market-cap decile of public companies
(micro-caps). Caution: may include distressed companies → may need
downward adjustment for healthy small firms.
Industry Risk Premium
(IP)
Added for companies in particularly risky industries. Reflects sector-specific
risks not captured in the base ERP.
Specific-Company Risk
Premium (SCRP)
Reflects qualitative/quantitative idiosyncratic factors: key-person risk, customer
concentration, geographic concentration, governance model, asset type,
earnings predictability, pricing power. Larger = higher SCRP.
Discount for Lack of
Marketability (DLOM)
Illiquidity of private company equity is NOT captured as a higher required return
but as a REDUCTION in estimated value (valuation discount). Applied to the
estimated equity value, not added to the discount rate.

Method A: Expanded CAPM (for Private Companies)

Expanded CAPM
re = rf + βpeer(ERP) + SP + SCRP
βpeer = beta estimated from publicly traded peers in the same industry; unlever then
re-lever for subject company's capital structure

Beta Unlevering & Re-levering
Step 1 — UNLEVER peer beta: βAsset = βC × [1 / (1 + (1 − tc)(D/E)peer)]
Step 2 — RE-LEVER for subject company: βSubject = βAsset × [1 + (1 − ts)(D/E)subject]

Note: Use each company's respective marginal tax rate (tc and ts may differ)
Purpose: Remove the financial leverage component of the peer's beta, then add back the subject
company's financial leverage

Method B: Build-Up Approach (for Private Companies)

Build-Up
re = rf + ERP + SP + IP + SCRP
ERP is NOT beta-adjusted (implicitly β = 1.0 → assumed average systematic risk)

Best used when comparable public companies are unavailable or of low comparability. Starts with required return
for an average-risk large-cap public company (rf + ERP), then adds incremental size and company-specific
premia. IP is not included in Expanded CAPM (captured in peer beta selection).

heading: International Cost of Equity — Country Risk Adjustments
body:
Model
Description
Country Spread Model
ERP_local = ERP_developed + (λ × CRP). CRP = sovereign yield spread (local
bond in developed currency minus developed sovereign bond yield). λ =
company's exposure to the local country. Issue: bond yield spread may not
adequately proxy equity risk premium.
Damodaran CRP
Refinement
CRP = Sovereign yield spread × (σEquity / σBond). Adjusts the bond spread
upward to reflect that equity is more volatile than bonds. Requires historical equity
and bond return data for the local market.
Global CAPM
(GCAPM)
Uses a global market index as the single risk factor. Assumes no significant risk
differences across countries. Issue: emerging market correlations with developed
markets are low → may produce low or negative betas.
International CAPM
(ICAPM)
E(re) = rf + βG(E(rgm) − rf) + βC(E(rc) − rf). Two factors: global market premium
(βG) + currency premium (βC, sensitivity to wealth-weighted currency index).
More flexible for companies with significant cross-border cash flows.

Damodaran CRP
CRP = Sovereign Yield Spread × (σEquity / σBond)
Then adjust for company's exposure λ: Adjusted CRP = λ × CRP

ICAPM
E(re) = rf + βG[E(rgm) − rf] + βC[E(rc) − rf]
βG: sensitivity to global market; βC: sensitivity to foreign currency index (wealth-
weighted, not cap-weighted)

heading: Gretna Engines — Public Company (CAPM, FF5, BYPRP, Matrix Pricing)
body:
Small-cap public industrial equipment manufacturer. Cyclical industry, high operating leverage, tangible asset
base (PP&E + inventory). Thinly traded BBB-rated debt (7%, 7-year remaining). Preferred equity outstanding.

Gretna: Cost of Debt via Matrix Pricing
Step 1 — Calculate YTMs for 4 comparable BBB bonds: Bond 1 (4yr): 5.140%; Bond 2 (4yr): 5.191%;
Bond 3 (8yr): 6.000%; Bond 4 (8yr): 6.021%
Step 2 — Average by maturity: 4-year avg = 5.166%; 8-year avg = 6.011%
Step 3 — Interpolate: (6.011% − 5.166%) ÷ 4 years = 0.211%/year increment
7-year estimate: 5.166% + 3 × 0.211% = 5.799% ≈ 5.8%
Adjustment: Gretna's bonds are LESS LIQUID than comparables → actual YTM likely slightly above

heading: % (liquidity premium)
body:
Gretna: Cost of Preferred Equity
Preferred: 7% dividend rate, par value 1,000, current price 980
Annual dividend = 7% × 1,000 = 70
Cost of preferred = DDM with g=0: rp = D/P = 70/980 = 7.14%

Gretna: Cost of Common Equity — Three Models
Risk-free rate: 20-year government benchmark = 2.1%
CAPM: re = 2.1% + 0.91 × 5.5% = 7.11%
FF5:  re = 2.1% + 0.95(5.5%) + 0.45(1.8%) + 0.14(3.9%) − 0.19(3.1%) + 0.30(3.7%) = 9.20%
BYPRP: re = 5.8% + 6.2% = 12.0%
CAPM (7.11%) < preferred equity cost (7.14%) → CAPM estimate is unrealistic; common equity must
cost MORE than preferred equity
FF5 (9.20%) is likely the most defensible single-factor adjusted estimate; BYPRP (12%) reflects a
historical premium that may be high

heading: Precision Irrigation — Private Company (Expanded CAPM, Build-Up,
body:
Synthetic Rating)

Private software company in an emerging market. Small (total assets $105m vs. $1.28bn peer avg), intangible-
heavy (47% vs. 32% peers), high customer/geographic concentration (70% from 5 local customers), key-person
risk (founder-CEO, no succession plan). Non-traded debt.

Precision: After-Tax Cost of Debt

IC ratio = EBITDA / Interest = 12.2 / 1.6 = 7.63× → synthetic rating: A
D/E ratio = 18.4 / (105.2 − 18.4) = 18.4 / 86.8 = 21.2% → also consistent with A
Credit spread for A rating: 1.46%
Benchmark 10-year government bond: 5.41%
CRP for sovereign risk: 2.00%
Pre-tax cost of debt: 5.41% + 1.46% + 2.00% = 8.87%
After-tax cost of debt: 8.87% × (1 − 0.20) = 7.10%

Precision: Cost of Equity — Expanded CAPM
Step 1 — Unlever peer beta (industry avg, tc=25%, D/E=30.25%): βAsset = 1.25 / [1 + (0.75)(0.3025)]
= 1.019
Step 2 — Re-lever for Precision (ts=20%, D/E=21.2%): βPrecision = 1.019 × [1 + (0.80)(0.212)] = 1.19
Expanded CAPM: re = 5.41% + 1.19(6%) + 5% (SP) + 1% (IP) + 6% (SCRP) + 2% (CRP) = 26.55%

Precision: Cost of Equity — Build-Up Approach
re = rf + ERP + SP + SCRP + CRP (no IP, no beta adjustment)
re = 5.41% + 6% + 5% + 6% + 2% = 24.41%
WACC: wd = 18.4/105.2 = 17.5%; we = 82.5%
WACC = (0.175)(8.87%)(1 − 0.20) + (0.825)(24.41%) = 1.24% + 20.14% = 21.38%

section_title: DIVIDEND FORMS AND EFFECTS ON WEALTH &
heading: Types of Dividends
body:
Dividend Type
Description
Effect on Shareholder Wealth
Regular Cash
Paid on a fixed schedule (quarterly
US/Canada; semi-annual Europe/Japan;
annual Asia)
No change in total wealth at ex-date
(price drops by dividend amount);
represents real cash distribution
Extra / Special
Paid outside normal schedule; used in
good earnings years; signals non-
recurring nature
Same as regular cash — price drop
offsets cash received; signals current
strength but not ongoing commitment
Liquidating
Return of capital: company winds up,
sells division, or pays > accumulated
retained earnings
Same nominal wealth but is RETURN
OF CAPITAL, not earnings distribution
— tax treatment differs
Stock Dividend
Additional shares issued (~2–10%); no
cash leaves company; retained earnings
→ contributed capital
NO change in total wealth; price falls
proportionately; shareholders own
same % of same underlying value
Stock Split
Increase shares outstanding; decrease
per-share price proportionally (e.g. 2-for-
1 halves price)
NO change in total wealth or financial
ratios (P/E, debt ratios unchanged);
brings price to 'optimal' range
Reverse Stock Split
Reduce shares outstanding; increase
per-share price (e.g. 1-for-10: price × 10)
NO change in total wealth; typically
used to regain minimum listing price
requirements

section_title: DIVIDEND FORMS AND EFFECTS ON WEALTH &
heading: Financial Ratio Impacts — Summary Table
body:
Ratio / Measure
Cash Dividend Effect
Stock Dividend / Split Effect
Shareholders' equity
DECREASES (cash and retained
earnings both fall)
UNCHANGED (retained earnings →
contributed capital; no cash flows)
Current ratio / Liquidity
DECREASES (cash outflow reduces
current assets)
UNCHANGED
Debt-to-equity ratio
INCREASES (equity shrinks while
debt stays the same)
UNCHANGED
EPS
UNCHANGED (same net income,
same shares)
DECREASES proportionally (same
net income, more shares)
P/E ratio
UNCHANGED at ex-date (both price
and EPS unchanged in same period)
UNCHANGED (price and EPS both
decline by same factor)

Dividend yield
N/A (declares new dividend amount)
UNCHANGED (DPS and price both
adjust by same split ratio)
Book value per share
DECREASES (equity falls, shares
unchanged)
UNCHANGED (equity unchanged,
shares increase proportionally)

!
Stock dividends and stock splits are purely cosmetic — they divide the 'pie' into smaller slices
but do NOT create wealth. Both leave total market cap, P/E, and debt ratios unchanged. A cash
dividend DOES affect the current ratio (reduces cash) and the debt-to-equity ratio (reduces
equity). Common exam mistake: treating stock dividends as equivalent to cash dividends in ratio
effects.

!
REVERSE STOCK SPLITS typically signal that the stock price has fallen to a problematic level
(e.g., below exchange minimum). While they have no direct effect on wealth in theory, they are a
NEGATIVE signal in practice — they do not reflect confidence or growth.

heading: Theory 1 — Dividend Irrelevance (Miller & Modigliani, 1961)
body:
In a perfect capital market (no taxes, no transaction costs, symmetric information), dividend policy has no impact
on share price or cost of capital. Investors can create 'homemade dividends' by selling shares — making
corporate dividend policy redundant.

MM Dividend Irrelevance — Key Assumptions
No taxes (on dividends or capital gains)
No transaction costs (no flotation costs on new shares; no brokerage to sell shares)
Symmetric information (insiders and outsiders know equally about the firm's prospects)
Investment policy is fixed and independent of dividend policy
Implication: dividend policy is a FINANCIAL decision, not a VALUE-CREATING decision

Real-world violations of MM assumptions: Flotation costs (4%–10% on new equity) make external funding
expensive; transaction costs and capital gains taxes on 'homemade dividends'; volatile share prices make selling
shares to create income unreliable.

heading: Theory 2 — Bird in the Hand (Dividends ARE Preferred)
body:
Investors prefer a certain dollar of dividends today over an uncertain dollar of capital gains tomorrow. Higher
dividend-paying companies should have lower cost of equity (higher valuation) because dividends are less risky
than uncertain future capital gains.

MM's rebuttal: Under their assumptions, paying dividends today simply lowers the ex-dividend share price by
exactly the same amount. The risk of the firm's total cash flows — the source of all returns — is unchanged by the
dividend decision. 'Bird in hand' confuses dividend yield with return risk.

heading: Theory 3 — Tax Preference (Capital Gains Preferred)
body:
In jurisdictions where dividends are taxed at higher rates than capital gains, taxable investors should prefer
companies that retain and reinvest earnings (growing share price) rather than distributing taxable dividends.
Taken to the extreme: zero payout ratio is optimal for high-tax-rate investors.
Nuance: Even when dividend and capital gains tax rates are equal, capital gains are only taxed when realised —
the deferral option has value. Tax-exempt investors (pension funds, endowments) are indifferent.

Theory
Dividend Policy Stance
Core Logic
Miller & Modigliani
(1961)
IRRELEVANT — no impact on value
Perfect markets: homemade dividends
replicate any policy; investment drives
value
Bird in the Hand
MATTERS — higher dividends = higher
value
Certain income today preferred over
uncertain capital gains; lowers required
return
Tax Preference
MATTERS — lower dividends = higher
value (if dividends taxed more)
Tax disadvantage of dividends causes
tax-sensitive investors to prefer capital
gains

!
MM's irrelevance argument does NOT say dividends are unimportant to investors — it says
dividend POLICY (i.e., the pattern of distributions) doesn't affect VALUE in perfect markets. The
key driver of value remains the firm's INVESTMENT decisions and cash flows from operations.

heading: Dividend Signaling
body:
MM assumed symmetric information. In practice, managers have MORE information than outside investors
(asymmetric information). Dividend changes can credibly communicate management's private beliefs about future
earnings prospects.
For a signal to be effective, it must be COSTLY to mimic by firms without the same attributes. Dividend increases
are costly because a firm that cannot sustain higher dividends will eventually have to cut them — or borrow,
destroying value.

Dividend Action
Signal / Interpretation
Initiation of a regular dividend
Positive — management confident in sustained profitability; company
maturing; reduces info asymmetry
Increase in regular dividend
Positive — management expects permanent increase in earnings;
undervaluation signal

Extra / special dividend
Positive but transitory — management signals current strength, NOT
ongoing commitment
Maintenance of dividend despite
earnings decline
Moderately positive — management views decline as temporary;
dividend safe
Reduction in dividend
Generally negative — management expects earnings weakness to
persist; credibility loss
Omission of dividend
Strongly negative — severe financial stress; possible default risk
Dividend cut accompanied by
strategic reinvestment rationale
Can be positive — e.g. Telstra 2017: 30% cut presented as strategic
reinvestment; share price eventually recovered

!
Dividend reductions are not ALWAYS bad signals — context matters. If accompanied by a
credible strategic rationale (major reinvestment, restructuring), the market can interpret a cut
positively. The BT Group 2018 example: dividend cut accompanied by a 6.9% share price RISE
as market recognised the prudence of the cut given collapsing cash flows.

heading: Agency Costs and Dividend Policy
body:
Agency problems arise from separation of ownership (shareholders) and control (managers). Managers may
pursue private benefits at the expense of shareholder wealth — particularly by investing in negative-NPV projects
that grow their span of control.

Agency Conflict
How Dividends Help / Hurt
Manager vs. Shareholder
(overinvestment)
HELPS — paying out free cash flow constrains managers from investing in
negative-NPV projects. Jensen's Free Cash Flow Hypothesis: forcing cash
out prevents empire-building.
Shareholder vs. Bondholder
(wealth transfer)
HURTS — large dividends reduce the cash cushion protecting
bondholders; increases default risk; can transfer wealth from bondholders
to shareholders, violating bond covenants.
Utility regulator vs.
Shareholder
Paying high dividends + re-issuing equity forces market scrutiny of whether
rates are set fairly — using capital markets to discipline regulators.

!
Jensen's Free Cash Flow Hypothesis: companies with HIGH free cash flow and POOR
governance are most at risk of overinvestment. Paying dividends forces discipline. But for high-
growth companies with abundant profitable projects, hoarding cash and NOT paying dividends is
optimal — retaining funds for value-creating investments.

heading: Double Taxation System
body:
Corporate earnings taxed at corporate level THEN again at personal level when distributed as dividends. Used in:
United States (historically), mainland China (partially).

Effective Tax Rate
(Double)
Eff. Rate = Corp Rate + [(1 − Corp Rate) × Personal
Div. Rate]

Double Taxation — Worked Example
Pretax corporate earnings = $100
Corporate tax rate = 35% → Tax = $35 → After-tax net income = $65
Dividend (100% payout) = $65 → Personal dividend tax = 15% × $65 = $9.75
Net dividend received = $55.25
Effective tax rate = ($35 + $9.75) / $100 = 44.75% ≈ 44.8%

Formula check: 35% + (1 − 35%) × 15% = 35% + 9.75% = 44.75% ✓

heading: Dividend Imputation System
body:
Shareholders receive a TAX CREDIT (franking credit) for corporate taxes already paid, so profits are taxed
effectively at the SHAREHOLDER'S rate — not double taxed. Used in: Australia, New Zealand.

Effective Tax Rate
(Imputation)
Eff. Rate = Shareholder Marginal Rate (on pretax
income)

Imputation System — Worked Example
Corporate pretax income = A$100; Corporate tax rate = 30% → Net income after tax = A$70
Dividend (100% payout) = A$70
Low-bracket shareholder (15% rate): Tax on pretax = 15%; Less franking credit = 30%; Tax credit to
shareholder = −15% → Effective tax rate = 15/100 = 15%
High-bracket shareholder (47% rate): Tax on pretax = 47%; Less franking credit = 30%; Additional tax
owed = 17% → Effective tax rate = 47/100 = 47%
KEY: The shareholder's effective rate = their marginal rate, regardless of corporate rate paid

heading: Split-Rate Tax System
body:
Corporate earnings distributed as dividends are taxed at a LOWER corporate rate than retained earnings.
Shareholders then also pay personal income tax on the dividends. Of greater historical than current importance.

Effective Tax Rate
(Split-Rate)
Eff. Rate = Div. Corp Rate + [(1 − Div. Corp Rate) ×
Personal Rate]

Split-Rate System — Worked Example
Pretax earnings = €200; €100 retained (taxed at 35%) + €100 allocated to dividends (taxed at 20%)
Tax on retained: 35% × €100 = €35; Tax on distributed: 20% × €100 = €20
Dividends distributed: €100 − €20 = €80
Shareholder personal tax (35% rate): €80 × 35% = €28
Net after-tax dividend received: €80 − €28 = €52
Effective tax rate on dividend income: (€20 + €28) / €100 = 48%

Tax System
How Many Times Taxed?
Effective Rate Logic
Double Taxation
TWICE (corporate + personal)
Corp rate + (1−corp rate) × personal
rate; Always ≥ max of either rate alone

Imputation
ONCE (effectively at shareholder rate)
Franking credits ensure corporate taxes
'pass through' to shareholder; rate =
shareholder marginal rate
Split-Rate
TWICE but mitigated (low corp rate on
distributions)
Lower corporate tax on dividends
reduces the double-tax penalty vs.
standard double taxation

!
For the imputation exam question: the effective tax rate = the SHAREHOLDER's marginal rate,
not the corporate rate, not a blend. A 15% shareholder gets a TAX REFUND if corporate rate
(30%) exceeds their personal rate. A 47% shareholder pays the difference. Always express the
effective rate as personal-tax-rate / pretax-income.

heading: Stable Dividend Policy
body:
Most common policy. Dividends based on long-run SUSTAINABLE earnings — they grow gradually and do not
track short-term earnings volatility. Payout ratio may fluctuate widely year to year. Companies strongly resist
cutting dividends.
Operationalised by Lintner's Target Payout Adjustment Model (1956):

Lintner Model —
Expected Dividend
Div_t = Div_{t-1} + [(EPS_t × Target Payout − Div_{t-
1}) × (1/Years)]

Where: Div_t = expected dividend this period; Div_{t-1} = last period's dividend; EPS_t = expected earnings;
Target Payout = long-run target ratio; Adjustment Factor = 1/n (n = years over which adjustment takes place).

Lintner Model — Worked Example (Exam-Style)
Luna Inc.: Last year EPS = $2.00, Dividend = $0.40. Target payout = 30%, 4-year adjustment period.
Current year expected EPS = $2.80
Step 1: Target dividend = $2.80 × 30% = $0.84
Step 2: Underpayment vs. target = $0.84 − $0.40 = $0.44
Step 3: Adjustment this year = $0.44 × (1/4) = $0.11
Step 4: Expected dividend = $0.40 + $0.11 = $0.51
Note: EPS rose 40% but dividend rises only 27.5% — this is the smoothing effect
Payout ratio FALLS from 20% to 18.2% — takes time to reach target

heading: Constant Dividend Payout Ratio Policy
body:
A fixed percentage of net income is paid as dividends each period. Dividends are highly volatile because they
track earnings directly. Infrequently adopted in practice — managers dislike communicating volatile dividends to
shareholders.

Constant Payout
Dividend = EPS × Target Payout Ratio

Dimension
Stable Dividend Policy
Constant Payout Ratio Policy
Volatility
LOW — dividends smooth over
time
HIGH — dividends track earnings
directly
Payout ratio
Variable year-to-year
FIXED per period
Typical use
Most common globally
Cyclical industries (e.g. fertilisers,
mining)
Dividend coverage ratio
Variable — low in bad years, high in
good years
Fixed by definition
Investor certainty
HIGH — predictable cash flows
LOW — uncertain amounts
Signaling
Strong commitment signal
Less credible long-term signal

heading: Global Trends in Payout Policy
body:
•
Fraction of dividend-paying companies has been in LONG-TERM DECLINE in most developed markets
(US, Canada, EU, UK, Japan)
•
Share repurchases have trended upward since early 1980s (US) and 1990s (Europe, UK); recent
acceleration in Asia (China, Japan)
•
Aggregate AMOUNTS of dividends have risen, but concentrated in fewer, larger companies
•
Post-GFC: partial reversal of declining payer fraction; dividend yield sought as fixed-income substitute in
low-rate era
•
Countries with stronger governance, IFRS adoption, and better investor protection show LESS signaling
power from dividends (reduced information asymmetry lowers dividend's role as signal)

!
The trend is: FEWER payers but LARGER aggregate dividends. Do not confuse 'fraction of
companies paying dividends is falling' with 'total dividends are falling' — both can be true
simultaneously as large, mature firms dominate the payout landscape.

heading: Share Repurchase Methods
body:
Method
Description
1. Open Market (most
common)
Company buys shares in open market at discretion. Most flexible — no legal
obligation to follow through. No shareholder approval required in US (required
in Europe). Best for exploiting perceived undervaluation. Most cost-effective if
well-timed.
2. Fixed Price Tender
Offer
Offer to buy fixed number of shares at a fixed PREMIUM to market (e.g. A$40
when market = A$37). Pro-rata if oversubscribed. Fast (typically 30 days).
Premium signals management confidence but higher cost.
3. Dutch Auction
Tender offer specifying a RANGE of prices (e.g. A$38–A$40). Shareholders bid
minimum acceptable price. Company qualifies bids from lowest upward until
target shares reached. All qualifying shareholders receive the CLEARING
PRICE. Faster than open market; cheaper than fixed-price tender (no fixed
premium required).
4. Direct Negotiation
(least common)
Negotiate with a major shareholder to buy their block at a price (usually
premium). Used to remove overhanging block or prevent activist/takeover. If at
PREMIUM: transfers wealth from remaining shareholders to selling shareholder
— known as GREENMAIL. Can also occur at discount if seller has liquidity
urgency.

!
Greenmail: company pays a PREMIUM to buy back shares from a hostile activist/acquirer to
keep them from gaining influence. This is WEALTH-DESTRUCTIVE for remaining shareholders
— their share price falls as the total equity value is reduced by more than proportional, enriching
the seller at their expense.

heading: EPS Effects of Repurchases — Three Key Cases
body:
EPS After Repurchase
(Surplus Cash)
EPS_new = Net Income / Shares After Repurchase

EPS After Repurchase
(Debt-Financed)
EPS_new = (Net Income − After-Tax Interest Cost) /
Shares After Repurchase

EPS Impact Rule
(Debt-Financed)
EPS increases if Earnings Yield > After-Tax Cost of
Debt; decreases if EY < After-Tax Debt Cost

Earnings Yield = EPS / Share Price = 1 / P/E

EPS Calculation — Surplus Cash (Takemiya Industries)
Shares outstanding = 10M; Net income = ¥100M; EPS = ¥10.00; Share price = ¥120
Surplus cash = ¥240M invested in near-zero T-bills (no income foregone)
Repurchase at ¥140/share: shares bought = ¥240M / ¥140 = 1.714M shares
Remaining shares = 10M − 1.714M = 8.286M
New EPS = ¥100M / 8.286M = ¥12.07 — EPS INCREASES by ~20%

Reason: net income unchanged; fewer shares in denominator → higher EPS

EPS Calculation — Debt-Financed (Selamat Plantations)
Shares: 2.2M → repurchase 200K at MYR60 → borrow MYR12M; remaining = 2.0M shares
Net income = MYR6.6M; Earnings yield = MYR3/MYR60 = 5%
Case 1 (after-tax debt cost = 5% = earnings yield): EPS = (6.6 − 12 × 0.05)/2.0 = (6.6 − 0.6)/2.0 =
MYR3.00 — UNCHANGED
Case 2 (after-tax debt cost = 6% > earnings yield): EPS = (6.6 − 12 × 0.06)/2.0 = (6.6 − 0.72)/2.0 =
MYR2.94 — DECREASES
Case 3 (after-tax debt cost = 4% < earnings yield): EPS = (6.6 − 12 × 0.04)/2.0 = (6.6 − 0.48)/2.0 =
MYR3.06 — INCREASES

!
EPS increase from repurchase does NOT automatically mean shareholder wealth increased. (1)
Leverage increases → required return rises → P/E could compress. (2) Finance theory: if FCF is
fixed, changing share count does not change total firm value. EPS boosting is an accounting
effect, not a value creation mechanism.

heading: Book Value Per Share (BVPS) Effect
body:
BVPS After
Repurchase
BVPS_new = (Book Equity − Repurchase Cost) / Shares
Remaining

BVPS Rule After Repurchase
If Market Price > BVPS before repurchase → BVPS DECREASES after repurchase
If Market Price < BVPS before repurchase → BVPS INCREASES after repurchase
If Market Price = BVPS before repurchase → BVPS UNCHANGED after repurchase
Intuition: overpaying (market > book) means each dollar of cash removed 'costs more' than the book
equity it retires

BVPS Worked Example
Company A: Book equity = $100M, 10M shares → BVPS = $10. Market price = $20 (> BVPS)
Repurchase $5M at $20 → buy 250K shares → 9.75M remain → equity = $95M
New BVPS = $95M / 9.75M = $9.74 → BVPS DECREASED (market > book ✓)

Company B: Book equity = $300M, 10M shares → BVPS = $30. Market price = $20 (< BVPS)
Repurchase $5M at $20 → buy 250K shares → 9.75M remain → equity = $295M
New BVPS = $295M / 9.75M = $30.26 → BVPS INCREASED (market < book ✓)

heading: Valuation Equivalence
body:
Under MM-like assumptions (same tax treatment, same information content, no transaction costs), paying a cash
dividend of amount X and repurchasing X worth of shares are EQUIVALENT in their effect on total shareholder
wealth.

Equivalence Proof (Rohit Chemical Industries)
10M shares × Rs200/share = Rs2,000M equity; FCF = Rs500M
Option 1 — Special Dividend: Rs50/share → ex-div price = Rs150; Total wealth per share = Rs150 +
Rs50 = Rs200
Option 2 — Repurchase: Rs500M / Rs200 = 2.5M shares bought; 7.5M remain; Post-buyback price =
Rs1,500M/7.5M = Rs200; Wealth per share = Rs200
Shareholder who sells: receives Rs200 cash; Shareholder who doesn't sell: holds share worth Rs200
RESULT: Both methods = Rs200 total wealth per share. Perfect equivalence.

!
The equivalence result BREAKS DOWN if: (1) Tax treatment differs (dividends vs. capital gains
at different rates); (2) Information content differs (a buyback may signal undervaluation; a
dividend initiation signals maturity/earnings confidence); (3) A repurchase occurs at ABOVE-
MARKET price (greenmail) — then remaining shareholders LOSE wealth.

heading: Why Companies Choose Repurchases Over Dividends
body:
Reason
Explanation
Tax advantage
If capital gains taxed < dividends, shareholders defer or reduce tax by not
selling into buyback. Deferral option always valuable even if rates are
equal.
Flexibility
Repurchase programs do NOT create same expectation of continuance as
regular dividends. Can be stopped without triggering negative signal.
Dividends are sticky — cutting is costly reputationally.
Undervaluation signal
Management buying own shares signals confidence in intrinsic value.
Positive market reaction to buyback announcement (similar to better-than-
expected earnings surprise).
Offset option dilution
Repurchasing shares offsets EPS dilution from employee stock option
exercises. Companies repurchase at least as many shares as options
exercised.
Increase leverage
Debt-financed repurchases increase financial leverage, potentially toward
optimal capital structure.

No future commitment
Announcing a buyback does NOT commit management to execute —
provides price support without binding obligation.

!
Repurchases are procyclical — they INCREASE during economic booms (excess cash) and
FALL sharply in recessions. S&P 500 data: repurchases fell from $680B (2007) to $223B (2009).
Cash dividends are much stickier — fell only from $286B to $262B in the same period. This
cyclicality matters for forecasting corporate distributions.

heading: Dividend Coverage Ratios
body:
Dividend Payout Ratio
DPS / EPS  (or total dividends / net income)

Dividend Coverage
Ratio (EPS)
EPS / DPS  (or net income / dividends)  — inverse of
payout

FCFE Formula
FCFE = CFO − Fixed Capital Investment + Net Borrowing

FCFE Coverage Ratio
FCFE / (Dividends + Share Repurchases)

The FCFE coverage ratio is the more analytically rigorous measure because it captures capital expenditure needs
and financing that net income ignores. A ratio consistently below 1.0 is unsustainable — the company is depleting
cash or increasing debt to fund payouts.

Lygon Resources — FCFE Coverage (Exam-Style Calculation)
2015: FCFE = $837 − $554 + (−$120) = $163M; Div = $121M; Repurchases = $0
       Earnings coverage = $540/$121 = 4.46x | FCFE coverage = $163/($121+$0) = 1.35x
2016: FCFE = $824 − $417 + (−$39) = $368M; Div = $256M; Repurchases = $105M
       Earnings coverage = $458/$256 = 1.79x | FCFE coverage = $368/($361) = 1.02x
2017: FCFE = $679 − $296 + $79 = $462M; Div = $277M; Repurchases = $277M
       Earnings coverage = $399/$277 = 1.44x | FCFE coverage = $462/($554) = 0.83x ← BELOW 1
2018: FCFE = $628 − $327 + (−$7) = $294M; Div = $323M; Repurchases = $0
       Earnings coverage = $341/$323 = 1.06x | FCFE coverage = $294/$323 = 0.91x ← BELOW 1
→ Dividend at serious risk; management should cut

heading: Early Warning Signs of Dividend Unsustainability
body:
Red Flags — Dividend Safety at Risk
Dividend payout ratio > 60-70% AND declining earnings trend
Earnings/dividend coverage ratio approaching or below 1.0x
FCFE coverage ratio consistently < 1.0x (paying out more than cash generated)
Company borrowing to fund dividends (unsustainable by definition long-term)
Abnormally HIGH dividend yield relative to peers or historical range — market pricing in a cut
Decline in capital expenditures to maintain dividends (underinvesting in the business)
Deteriorating credit rating or covenant pressure from bondholders
History of prior dividend cuts (negative indicator of management commitment)

Rule of thumb: Large, mature companies typically target 40%–60% payout ratios → coverage 1.7x–2.5x.
Coverage below ~1.5x warrants scrutiny. Coverage below 1.0x is generally an immediate danger signal unless
caused by a clearly non-recurring item.

!
An extremely high DIVIDEND YIELD is usually a WARNING SIGN, not an opportunity. High
yields often mean the market is pricing in a forthcoming dividend cut — the share price has
fallen faster than the dividend. Research: top 5% of dividend yielders in S&P 500 (2005-2015)
were over-represented in the bottom decile of total returns. The market is usually smarter than
the screen.

heading: Dispersed vs. Concentrated Ownership
body:
Structure
Definition / Key Characteristics
Dispersed Ownership
Many shareholders; none individually can exercise control over the corporation.
Associated with WEAK SHAREHOLDERS and STRONG MANAGERS. Primary
conflict: principal–agent problem (managers may act in self-interest at
shareholders' expense).

Concentrated Ownership
Individual or group (family, company, sovereign) holds a controlling position.
Associated with STRONG SHAREHOLDERS and WEAK MANAGERS. Primary
conflict: principal–principal problem (controlling owners may exploit minority
shareholders).
Hybrid
Mix of dispersed and concentrated elements. Control mechanisms (dual-class
shares, pyramids) may give some shareholders disproportionate voting power
relative to economic ownership.

OECD Global Classification (47 Jurisdictions)
Concentrated ownership (38/47): Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Chile, China, France, India, Italy, Mexico,
Norway, Russia, Singapore, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Sweden, Turkey, UAE, and others
Dispersed ownership (4/47): Australia, Ireland, United Kingdom, United States
Hybrid (5/47): Canada, Germany, Japan, Netherlands, Switzerland
Key insight: Concentrated ownership is the global norm — dispersed ownership (UK/US model) is the
exception

heading: Control Mechanisms That Disconnect Ownership from Voting Power
body:
Mechanism
Description
Horizontal Ownership
Companies with mutual business interests hold cross-shareholdings in
each other. Facilitates strategic alliances; can entrench insider control and
block hostile takeovers.
Vertical (Pyramid) Ownership
A parent entity controls holding companies, which in turn control operating
companies. A small equity stake at the top can control large downstream
operations — disproportionate control.
Dual-Class / Multiple-Class
Shares
One share class gets superior or sole voting rights; another gets inferior or
no voting rights. Founders/management maintain voting control while
selling economic interest to public investors. Used widely in tech IPOs.
Voting Caps
Legal restrictions on the voting rights attached to large share positions.
Used by sovereign governments to prevent foreign investors from
obtaining controlling stakes in strategic industries.

!
Ownership percentage ≠ control. A shareholder owning 20% of shares can still exert majority
control through dual-class structures or pyramid ownership arrangements. Always assess
VOTING POWER separately from ECONOMIC OWNERSHIP when analysing governance risk.

heading: Types of Conflict by Ownership Structure
body:
Ownership Type
Voting Power
Conflict Type & Description
Dispersed
Dispersed
PRINCIPAL–AGENT: Weak shareholders cannot control
strong managers; managers may pursue self-interest
(empire-building, excess perks, negative-NPV projects).

Concentrated
Concentrated
PRINCIPAL–PRINCIPAL: Strong controlling shareholders
can exploit minority shareholders via related-party
transactions, wealth transfers, or non-value-maximising
decisions.
Dispersed
Concentrated
PRINCIPAL–PRINCIPAL: Controlling shareholders (via
dual-class or pyramids) with <50% economic ownership
can still harm minority shareholders with outsized voting
power.
Concentrated
Dispersed
Voting caps imposed by law; concentrated economic
owners cannot translate ownership into control. Less
common, typically regulatory in origin.

heading: Types of Influential Shareholders
body:
Shareholder Type
Key Governance Characteristics
Banks
Common in Europe and Asia. Dual role as creditor AND equity holder creates
conflict of interest — may push company toward larger loans on terms
benefiting the bank as lender, to detriment of equity holders.
Families
Dominant in Latin America, Asia, Europe. Benefit: lower principal–agent risk
(family has aligned ownership + management responsibility). Drawback: poor
transparency, weak minority rights, interlocking directorates (same family on
boards of multiple companies), difficulty attracting outside management talent.
State-Owned Enterprises
(SOEs)
Mixed-ownership model: government owns a controlling stake; shares also
trade publicly. Lower market scrutiny due to implicit state backstop. May pursue
social or public policy goals at the expense of shareholder value maximisation.
Institutional Investors
Pension funds, mutual funds, insurance companies, hedge funds. Large
resources + expertise. In dispersed markets, rarely large enough to be a
controlling shareholder but can promote good governance by holding boards
accountable and exercising voting rights actively.
Group Companies
Horizontal/vertical cross-holdings (e.g., Samsung, Sanwa Group, Grupo Carso).
Long-term relationships between group companies restrict share transfers. Risk
of related-party transactions at the expense of minority shareholders without
strong governance protections.
Private Equity (VC/LBO)
Strategic majority owners in private/taken-private companies. Often bring
positive governance: professional management codes, performance-based
compensation, board restructuring. Important governance upgrade mechanism
in portfolio companies.
Foreign Investors
In emerging markets, may own more than domestic investors. From higher-
standard jurisdictions, can demand transparency and accountability —
benefiting local minority shareholders. Cross-listing in stricter markets (e.g.,
NYSE) also raises standards.
Insiders
(Managers/Directors)
As ownership increases, interests align with external shareholders (reducing
agency costs). But large positions also grant power and incentive to protect
insider interests at minority shareholders' expense — entrenched management
risk.

heading: Structural Effects on Corporate Governance Practices
body:
Governance
Dimension
Key Points
Director Independence
Higher % of independent directors in dispersed-ownership jurisdictions (where
principal–agent risk is greater). US requires independent majority on audit,
nomination, compensation committees. Concentrated-ownership jurisdictions:
nomination/remuneration committees not mandatory; when present, should be
independent.
Board Structure — One-
Tier
Single board of executive (internal) and non-executive (external) directors. Most
common globally. US, UK, most of Asia use this structure.
Board Structure — Two-
Tier
Supervisory board oversees management board. Mandatory in: Argentina,
Germany, Russia. Optional in: Brazil, France. Supervisory board inspects
books, reviews annual report, oversees auditors, sets management
compensation. In Germany: supervisory board includes bank and labour
representatives.
Corporate Governance
Codes
'Comply or explain' model: companies disclose adoption of recommended
practices or explain deviations. Japan requires companies with no outside
directors to justify why. Chile and India use company law or stock exchange
listing requirements instead of national codes.
Stewardship Codes
Voluntary (sometimes semi-mandatory) codes encouraging institutional
investors to actively exercise ownership rights. UK Stewardship Code: asset
managers investing in UK equities must publish 'comply or explain' statement
on commitment to the Code.
Special Voting
Arrangements
Brazil, India, UK, Italy and others have arrangements facilitating minority
shareholder participation in board nomination. UK premium listing: independent
directors must be separately approved by both the full shareholder base AND
non-controlling shareholders when a controlling shareholder exists.

heading: Board of Directors — Evaluation Framework
body:
Board Dimension
What Analysts Evaluate
Board Structure
One-tier vs. two-tier: does the structure provide sufficient oversight,
representation, and accountability? Is the structure appropriate for the
ownership type and jurisdiction?
CEO Duality
Is the CEO also chair of the board? CEO duality raises concern that the
monitoring role of the board is compromised. Best practice: separate,

independent chairperson. If not independent, a Lead Independent Director
should be appointed to protect investor interests.
Board Independence
% of independent directors (no material employment, ownership, or
remuneration relationship with the company). Absence of a meaningful
independent minority is a NEGATIVE governance signal. Most OECD countries
recommend/require 20%–50%+ independence.
Board Committees
Are there sufficiently independent committees for audit,
remuneration/compensation, nomination, and risk/compliance functions? Non-
independent or executive committee members may signal conflicts of interest in
compensation decisions, management selection, or financial reporting integrity.
Board Skills &
Experience
Does the board have skills directly relevant to the company's core operations?
Industry expertise is critical. Sector-specific: for resource-intensive or high-
ESG-risk companies, environmental/climate/social expertise expected on the
board.
Board Tenure
Tenure >10 years considered long by most governance codes. Positive: deep
institutional knowledge of the business. Negative: possible independence
compromise, overly aligned with management, resistant to change.
Board Composition
Number of directors, gender/cultural/geographical diversity, age range, tenure
diversity. Large, homogeneous, long-tenured boards tend to underperform. Too
few directors can also impair oversight quality.
Board Evaluation
Self-evaluation vs. external review. Covers: duties performed, leadership
quality, committee structure, board–management interaction and culture.
Targeted at: shareholders, regulators, external stakeholders.

Board Evaluation — Worked Example (Style Fashion — Exam-Style)
Style: 11-member board; Chairperson = Leila Donato (not the CEO → POSITIVE: no CEO duality)
Board independence: 2 independent out of 11 → only 18% independent → NEGATIVE (well below
20%–50% OECD guidance)
Family representation: 4 Donato family members, all with 20+ year tenure → NEGATIVE: far exceeds
10-year threshold for independent assessment
Gender diversity: 5 women including chairperson → POSITIVE
Age diversity: 35–75 → POSITIVE
Key takeaway: Strong demographic diversity but severely inadequate board independence —
governance risk is HIGH

heading: Executive Remuneration
body:
Element
Key Analytical Considerations
Transparency
Are compensation details fully disclosed? Opaque remuneration is a
governance red flag.
Performance Criteria
Are KPIs in incentive plans disclosed and aligned with the company's long-term
strategy? Short-term vs. long-term incentive balance?
CEO Pay Ratio
CEO pay vs. average worker pay ratio. Increasingly scrutinised as a
governance and social concern.

Say-on-Pay
Shareholders can vote/provide feedback on remuneration. Absence of say-on-
pay provision = reduced accountability.
Clawback Policy
Allows the company to recover previously paid remuneration following financial
restatements, misconduct, breach of law, or risk management failures.
Presence is a positive governance signal.

heading: Shareholder Voting Rights
body:
Structure
Description
Straight Voting
One vote per share. All shareholders treated equally. Most investor-friendly
structure.
Dual-Class Shares
Founders/management hold shares with superior voting power; public investors
hold inferior or non-voting shares. Creates potential conflict between minority
shareholders and management/founders. Common in tech sector. Key risk:
limits accountability of controlling class to other shareholders.

!
Absence of independent directors + CEO duality + dual-class shares is the 'triple threat'
governance structure. Any one alone is a yellow flag; all three together represents a HIGH
governance risk concentration that should significantly raise the risk premium in equity or credit
analysis.

heading: Materiality and Investment Horizon
body:
In an ESG context, materiality refers to ESG-related issues expected to affect a company's operations, financial
performance, or security valuation. Materiality assessments must be company- and industry-specific — what is
material for an energy company may be irrelevant for a bank.

Dimension
Key Points
Materiality Definition
ESG issue is material if its omission or misstatement would influence users'
investment decisions. Companies may over-emphasise positive ESG factors
while downplaying or omitting material negative ones.
Investment Horizon
Short-term investors: long-term ESG risks (e.g., future regulation, resource
depletion) may have little near-term price impact. Long-term investors: these
same factors could fundamentally reshape earnings and valuations over the
holding period. Credit analysts: bond maturity determines horizon — a 1-year

note vs. a 10-year note from the same issuer can have dramatically different
ESG risk exposures.
Dynamic Materiality
ESG issues can shift from long-term to short-term rapidly — e.g., an oil spill, a
new carbon regulation, or a product safety scandal can make a long-term ESG
risk immediately material to valuation.

heading: Three Approaches to Identifying ESG Factors
body:
Approach
Sources / Method
Limitations
1. Proprietary Research
Analyst's own judgment or firm's tools.
Sources: annual reports, sustainability
reports, proxy filings, 10-K, news,
industry associations, NGOs,
government filings, labour
organisations. Publicly available from
company websites.
Inconsistent reporting between
companies; voluntary disclosure
means gaps and biases in reported
data; comparability difficult without
standardisation.
2. ESG Data Providers /
Vendors
MSCI, Sustainalytics, and others.
Translate public ESG disclosures into
scores, rankings, and industry
analyses. Include subjective analyst
assessments beyond raw data.
Subjectivity in ESG score construction
and weighting. Different providers can
assign different ratings to the same
company. Scores may lag actual ESG
performance.
3. Not-For-Profit
Initiatives & Frameworks
IIRC (integrated reporting framework),
GRI (Global Reporting Initiative —
sector-specific sustainability
standards), SASB (materiality maps
by sector), TCFD (climate-related
disclosures), 2DII (2 Degree Investing
Initiative). Provide standardised
frameworks and materiality guidance.
Currently largely voluntary; adoption
uneven across jurisdictions;
frameworks not fully harmonised with
each other.

Key ESG Frameworks to Know
GRI (Global Reporting Initiative): Sector-specific sustainability topics; one of the most widely adopted
voluntary frameworks globally
SASB (Sustainable Accounting Standards Board): Materiality maps by industry sector — defines
which ESG factors are financially material for each sector
TCFD (Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures): Climate-specific: transition risks (policy,
technology, market, reputational) and physical risks (acute and chronic)
IIRC (International Integrated Reporting Council): Promotes integrated reporting of financial and ESG
information in corporate reports
Green Bond Principles: Voluntary standards for green bond labelling (use of proceeds, reporting,
verification)

heading: Equity vs. Fixed Income ESG Integration — Key Differences
body:
Dimension
Equity Analysis
Fixed-Income (Credit) Analysis
Primary Objective
Identify BOTH opportunities AND
downside risk
Focus primarily on MITIGATING
DOWNSIDE RISK (bond redeems at par
— asymmetric return profile)
Valuation Impact
Adjust discount rate, valuation multiples,
DCF terminal value, growth rate
Adjust credit spread, CDS spread,
internal credit rating; duration and
relative value analysis
ESG Adjustment
Mechanism
Forecast revenues, operating margins,
capex, earnings; adjust WACC/discount
rate for ESG risk
Forecast credit ratios, cash flow,
liquidity; adjust credit spread; assess
stranded asset risk by bond maturity
Time Horizon
Sensitivity
Long-term ESG risks more directly
reflected in equity value (perpetuity
claims)
Critically dependent on bond maturity —
1-year note vs. 10-year note face very
different ESG risk exposures from same
issuer

heading: Green Bonds
body:
Green bonds are debt instruments where proceeds are earmarked for specific environmental or climate projects.
First issued by the European Investment Bank in 2007 (Climate Awareness Bond). Green Bond Principles (2014):
voluntary standards for labelling, developed by investment bank consortium; ongoing governance by ICMA.

Feature
Detail
Structure
Same credit rating, bondholder recourse, and seniority as issuer's conventional
bonds — except use of proceeds is restricted to qualifying green projects.
Types include: corporate bonds, project bonds, mortgage-backed securities,
municipal bonds.
Analysis
Valued essentially the same as conventional bonds. May command a pricing
premium (tighter spread) vs. comparable conventional bonds due to strong
demand from ESG-mandated investors.
Greenwashing Risk
Key unique risk: proceeds not actually used for genuine environmental benefit.
Can cause overpayment (if investor paid green premium) or mandate breach.
Independent third-party review used to mitigate but not eliminate this risk.
Liquidity Risk
Often purchased by buy-and-hold ESG investors → secondary market may be
less liquid than comparable conventional bonds.
Issuer Benefits
More diversified investor base; potential new-issue premium. Offset by:
additional monitoring, reporting, and third-party review costs.

!
Green bonds have the SAME CREDIT RISK as the issuer's conventional bonds (same recourse,
same seniority). A green label does NOT reduce default risk. The only analytical difference is
use-of-proceeds restrictions and greenwashing risk. Exam trap: treating green bonds as
inherently lower credit risk because of their 'green' designation.

heading: General ESG Integration Process
body:
ESG Integration —
Equity
Identify material factors → Adjust revenues / opex / capex → Adjust
discount rate / multiples → Revised equity valuation

ESG Integration —
Fixed Income
Identify material factors → Adjust credit ratios / cash flow / liquidity
→ Adjust credit spread / CDS → Revised bond valuation

Adjustment Type
Where It Appears
Revenue adjustment
Income statement: ESG issues affecting market share, pricing power, product
demand (e.g., regulatory pressure on product, product recalls reducing sales)
Operating cost
adjustment
Income statement (COGS, SG&A): Higher/lower input costs from ESG
efficiency or penalties (e.g., water efficiency reducing COGS; regulatory fines
increasing non-operating charges)
Capital expenditure
adjustment
Cash flow statement: Mandatory upgrades required by regulation; stranded
asset write-offs
Balance sheet
impairment
Write-down of assets that become economically unviable due to ESG-related
regulatory or market shifts (stranded assets)
Discount rate adjustment
Equity valuation: Higher ESG risk → higher risk premium → lower DCF value;
Lower ESG risk → lower discount rate → higher value
Credit spread adjustment
Fixed income: Higher ESG risk → wider spread; Lower ESG risk → tighter
spread. Effect on spread may vary by bond maturity

heading: Case Study 1 — Environmental Factors: Frizzle Drinks (Beverage)
body:
Frizzle is a large US non-alcoholic beverage company with significant water usage — operating in water-scarce
emerging markets. Water intensity (litres of water per litre of beverage) is the key ESG metric for this industry.

Environmental Integration — Frizzle Drinks
Key ESG metric: Water Intensity = Total litres of water used / Litres of beverage produced
Historical trend: Water intensity ratio declined 27% from 2009–2021 (positive trajectory)
Target: Additional 13% reduction by 2025 — proactive resource management signal
Peer comparison: Frizzle's water intensity improvement significantly better than peer group in recent
years (2019–2021)

Financial adjustment — Income statement: COGS/Revenue reduced by 1% in forecast year 2022 due
to water efficiency gains
Result: Frizzle gross margin improves vs. peers (e.g., 61% vs. 58% peer group after ESG adjustment)
Equity implication: Higher forecast earnings → higher fair value for Frizzle stock (all else equal)
Bond/CDS implication: Improved operating cash flow → assess whether lower relative ESG risk
already reflected in current credit spreads

Environmental ESG — Analytical Toolkit
Quantify ESG metric (e.g., water intensity, carbon intensity, energy use per unit of output)
Track trend over time AND relative to peer group (normalise by revenue for comparability)
Translate ESG metric into P&L impact: revenue effect, COGS effect, operating margin effect
Adjust equity valuation: higher earnings → higher stock value; OR raise/lower discount rate for ESG
risk
Adjust bond valuation: improved cash flow → tighter spread justified; OR if risk remains,
maintain/widen spread

heading: Case Study 2 — Social Factors: Well Pharma (Pharmaceuticals)
body:
Well Pharma is a European pharmaceutical company with the worst product quality and safety record in its peer
group over five years — four major drug scandals, multiple regulatory warning letters, product recalls, and
significant fines.

Social Factor
Financial Statement Impact
Product Quality Controversies
Damage to brand value → decrease in sales revenue
Regulatory Warning Letters
Increased compliance costs → higher operating expenses
Product Recalls
Lost sales revenue + direct recall implementation costs
Regulatory Fines
Provisions for litigation and sales returns → non-operating charges
Low Product Quality
Certification %
Higher risk of future recalls and related costs → ongoing risk premium

Social Integration — Well Pharma Adjustments
Revenue adjustment: −2% revenue forecast due to existing product quality controversies (damage to
brand/demand)
Operating cost adjustment: COGS/Revenue +1.3% to reflect product quality improvements required
and additional manufacturing investment
Non-operating adjustment: +4.5% of operating income for restructuring, legal charges, and other non-
recurring costs
Result: Well Pharma COGS ratio and non-operating expense ratio both move materially above peer
group average after ESG adjustment

Equity implication: Forecast earnings and cash flow reduced → lower fair value; potential brand
impairment further compresses multiples
Bond implication: Reduced cash flow and earnings increase default risk → credit spread should widen
vs. peers
Key distinction: Analyst must assess whether product quality issues are RECURRING or NON-
RECURRING — affects whether adjustments are applied to base case or treated as one-off items

heading: Case Study 3 — Governance Factors: Sumiyoshi Banking Group (Bank)
body:
Sumiyoshi is a Japanese bank holding company (80% Japan revenues). Governance analysis benchmarks board
composition and practices against domestic peer group. Credit risk (non-performing loans ratio) used as a proxy
for corporate governance quality.

Governance Metric
Domestic Peer Group
Sumiyoshi Bank
Board type
Two-tier
Two-tier
Board size
13 directors
14 directors
CEO duality
Yes
Yes
Independent chairperson
Yes
NO ← Negative
Board independence %
47%
36% ← Below peers
Board gender diversity
17% female
7% female ← Well below peers
Directors with long tenure (>10
yrs)
0%
14% ← Negative
Number of board committees
5
4 ← No governance committee
Say-on-pay provision
Yes
NO ← Negative
Non-executive directors with
industry experience
67%
20% ← Significantly below peers

Governance Integration — Sumiyoshi Bank
Governance lags: No independent chairperson; lower board independence (36% vs 47%); less gender
diversity (7% vs 17%); more long-tenured directors (14% vs 0%); no governance committee; no say-
on-pay
Credit risk proxy: Non-performing loan (NPL) ratio = NPLs / Total loans outstanding
Sumiyoshi NPL/Total Loans ratio: 50 bps HIGHER than peer group average → reflects weaker
credit/governance risk management
Equity implication: Analyst increases risk premium embedded in equity valuation to reflect governance
discount
Bond implication: Analyst widens credit spread on Sumiyoshi's bonds relative to domestic peers to
reflect 50 bps NPL disadvantage
Key insight: For banks specifically, NPL ratio can serve as a quantitative governance proxy — poor
governance often manifests as weaker risk management and higher credit impairments


!
For FIXED INCOME, ESG integration adjusts the CREDIT SPREAD (or CDS) — NOT the
discount rate. The discount rate is an equity valuation tool. Adjusting the spread is appropriate
for bonds because it reflects the probability of default and recovery, which are the primary value
drivers for bondholders.

!
Social risk in pharma (product quality) is both a revenue risk AND a cost risk. Do not limit your
adjustment to just one financial statement line. Product controversies affect revenue (market
share, pricing), COGS (quality improvement investment), AND non-operating expenses (fines,
litigation provisions) — all three lines require adjustment.

heading: Green Bond vs. Conventional Bond
body:
Dimension
Green Bond vs. Conventional Bond
Credit rating
SAME as issuer's conventional bonds (same recourse and seniority)
Credit risk
SAME — green label does not reduce default risk
Use of proceeds
RESTRICTED to qualifying green/environmental projects (key distinguishing
feature)
Pricing
May trade at a PREMIUM (tighter spread) vs. comparable conventional bonds if
demand strong
Unique risks
Greenwashing (proceeds not used as intended); potential illiquidity (buy-and-
hold investor base)
Analysis approach
Same as conventional bond analysis — adjust for greenwashing risk and any
liquidity premium/discount
