heading: No-Arbitrage Assumptions for Option Valuation
body:
• Replicating instruments are identifiable and investable
• No market frictions (no bid-ask spreads, taxes, or transaction costs)
• Short selling is allowed with full use of proceeds
• Underlying follows a known statistical distribution
• Borrow or lend at the risk-free rate (rf)

heading: The Two Valuation Models
body:
Binomial Model
BSM / Black Model
Binomial Model
Discrete-time. Underlying moves to one of two
values each period. Works backward from
expiration. Can value American options (early
exercise check). More flexible but computationally
intensive for many periods.
Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM)
Continuous-time. Closed-form formula. Assumes
lognormal distribution. Only European options.
Fast and elegant. Basis for the Black Model
extensions.

2. Binomial Option Valuation Model — One Period

heading: Setting Up the Lattice
body:
The binomial model assumes the underlying can move to only two values over each period:
• u = up factor = S+ / S  (u > 1)
• d = down factor = S− / S  (d < 1, typically d = 1/u)
• S+ = uS  (up node),   S− = dS  (down node)
• c+ = Max(0, S+ − X)  for a call;   p+ = Max(0, X − S+)  for a put
• c− = Max(0, S− − X)  for a call;   p− = Max(0, X − S−)  for a put

heading: The Hedge Ratio (Delta, Δ or h)
body:
Step 1: Find the hedge ratio that creates a risk-free replicating portfolio (long Δ shares, short 1
call):


Hedge Ratio:  Δ = h = (c+ − c−) / (S+ − S−)
For puts: Δ = (p+ − p−) / (S+ − S−)  — this will be negative (short position in underlying)

Why does Δ create a risk-free portfolio?
The hedge ratio is chosen so that the portfolio value (Δ shares − 1 call) is IDENTICAL in
both the up and down states. A portfolio with the same value in all future states earns
exactly the risk-free rate — no arbitrage is possible.
• Up state:  Δ(S+) − c+  =  Δ(S−) − c−  :Down state
• Solving:  Δ(S+ − S−) = c+ − c−  →  Δ = (c+ − c−)/(S+ − S−)

heading: Risk-Neutral Probability (π)
body:
Step 2: Instead of the hedge ratio approach, we can use risk-neutral probabilities — the backbone of all
no-arbitrage pricing:

Risk-Neutral Probability (Up):  π = [(1 + r) − d] / [u − d]
Down probability = (1 − π).  These are NOT real-world probabilities — they are the weights that make
the expected return on the underlying equal to the risk-free rate.

Key insight: π + (1 − π) = 1 (they sum to 100%). The option price is the present value of
the expected payoff under risk-neutral probabilities. This eliminates the need to estimate
real expected returns.

heading: Step 3 — Computing c₀ (or p₀)
body:
One-Period Option Price:  c₀ = [π·c+ + (1−π)·c−] / (1+r)
p₀ = [π·p+ + (1−π)·p−] / (1+r)

heading: Put-Call Parity (Verification)
body:
Put-Call Parity:  S₀ + p = X/(1+r)ᵀ + c

Use this as a sanity check: if you get c, solve for p using put-call parity and compare to the binomial
result.

heading: Recombining Lattice Structure
body:
In a two-period recombining lattice, u then d gives the same price as d then u (i.e., S+− = S−+):

Node Prices
Call Payoffs (use Max(0, X−S) for puts)
Nodes at T₂:
• S++ = u²S (up-up)
• S+− = udS (up-down = down-up)
• S−− = d²S (down-down)
Terminal Payoffs:
• c++ = Max(0, u²S − X)
• c+− = Max(0, udS − X)
• c−− = Max(0, d²S − X)

heading: Two-Period Valuation — Work Backwards
body:
Always work from right to left (backward induction):

Period 1 Call Prices (from Period 2 payoffs):  c+ = [π·c++ + (1−π)·c+−] / (1+r)   c− =
[π·c+− + (1−π)·c−−] / (1+r)
Then: c₀ = [π·c+ + (1−π)·c−] / (1+r)

Shortcut — Direct Two-Period Formula:  c₀ = [π²c++ + 2π(1−π)c+− + (1−π)²c−−] /
(1+r)²
This is a binomial distribution weighting of the three terminal payoffs, discounted at (1+r)².

heading: Hedge Ratios in a Two-Period Model
body:
Delta changes at each node — you must rebalance the hedge each period:


Period 1 Deltas:  Δ+ = (c++ − c+−) / (S++ − S+−)       [upper node at T₁] Δ− =
(c+− − c−−) / (S+− − S−−)       [lower node at T₁]
Δ at T₀: Δ₀ = (c+ − c−) / (S+ − S−)

4. American Style Options
American Calls
American Puts
American CALLS
No early exercise for non-dividend stocks:
Early exercise forfeits the remaining time
value. You ALWAYS do better selling the call
than exercising it early (for non-dividend-
paying underlyings).
→ American call = European call
Exception: Deep-in-the-money calls on dividend-
paying stocks — may be optimal to exercise just
before the ex-dividend date to capture the
dividend.
American PUTS
Early exercise CAN be optimal:
For deep-in-the-money puts, the intrinsic
value (X − S) may exceed the option's fair
value. Exercising early captures this premium
immediately, which can be reinvested at rf.
American put > European put
The difference is the early exercise premium.
At each node, check:  Value =
Max(European value, Intrinsic value)

heading: Valuation Rule at Each Node
body:
For American puts — at every intermediate node, compare:
(1) Continuation value = [π·p+ + (1−π)·p−] / (1+r)   [hold the option]
(2) Intrinsic value = Max(0, X − S)   [exercise immediately]
American put value at node = Max(continuation, intrinsic)

heading: Example — Early Exercise Premium
body:
Given: S = 72, X = 75, u = 1.356, d = 0.541, r = 3%, 2-period
At down-down node:  S−− = 72(0.541)² = 21.07  →  p−− = Max(0, 75−21.07) = 53.93
Intrinsic at down node:  X − S− = 75 − 38.95 = 36.05
European p− = [π(22.18) + (1−π)(53.93)] / 1.03 = 33.86

→ Since Intrinsic (36.05) > European (33.86): EXERCISE EARLY
Early Exercise Premium = 36.05 − 33.86 = 2.18

5. Identifying Arbitrage Opportunities with Options

heading: Put-Call Parity Arbitrage
body:
Put-Call Parity:  S₀ + p = X·e⁻ʳᵀ + c   (continuous)   or   S₀ + p = X/(1+r)ᵀ + c
(discrete)
If the left side ≠ right side, an arbitrage exists. Buy the underpriced portfolio, sell the overpriced.

Overpriced Call Arbitrage
Underpriced Call Arbitrage
If c is too HIGH (LHS < RHS):
• Sell (write) the call
• Buy the put
• Buy the stock
• Borrow X·e⁻ʳᵀ
If c is too LOW (LHS > RHS):
• Buy the call
• Sell (write) the put
• Sell (short) the stock
• Lend X·e⁻ʳᵀ

heading: Replicating Positions via Put-Call Parity
body:
Put-call parity also tells us how to synthetically replicate any position:

Position
Synthetic Equivalent
Buy a Call
= Buy stock + Buy put + Borrow PV(X)
Buy a Put
= Buy call + Lend PV(X) + Short stock
Long stock
= Long call + Short put + Lend PV(X)
Long bond (lend PV(X))
= Short call + Long put + Long stock

6. Interest Rate Options — Two-Period Binomial Model

heading: Setup
body:
• Underlying: an interest rate (e.g., LIBOR), not a stock price

• Interest rate call (caplet): pays Max(0, rate − XR) × (days/360) × NA at the END of the accrual
period
• Interest rate put (floorlet): pays Max(0, XR − rate) × (days/360) × NA
• Each node discount factor (DF) = 1 / (1 + rate × days/360)
• Risk-neutral probability π = 0.50 is often assumed unless otherwise stated

heading: Valuation Steps
body:
Step 1: Build the interest rate binomial tree (rates at each node)
Step 2: Calculate payoffs at terminal nodes: Max(0, rate − XR) × AP × NA for calls
Step 3: Discount each payoff at the rate AT THAT NODE (not the risk-free rate): payoff / (1
+ node rate × days/360)
Step 4: Apply risk-neutral probabilities backward through the tree

heading: Worked Example
body:
Given: NA = $1M, X = 3.25%, π = 0.50, 2-period tree (rates already given)
Rates at T₂:
r++ = 3.9706%,  r+− = 3.2542%,  r−− = 2.2593%
Call payoffs at T₂ (Max(0, rate − 3.25%) × 0.5):
c++ = (3.9706% − 3.25%) × 0.5 = 0.007206 (per $1 notional)
c+− = (3.2542% − 3.25%) × 0.5 = 0.000042
c−− = 0  (below the exercise rate)
Discount at T₁ node rates:
c+ = [0.5(0.007206) + 0.5(0.000042)] / 1.039084 = 0.003488
c− = [0.5(0.000042) + 0.5(0)] / 1.026034 = 0.000020
Final value at T₀:
c₀ = [0.5(0.003488) + 0.5(0.000020)] / 1.030454 = 0.001702 per $1 notional
Total call value = 0.001702 × $1,000,000 = $1,702


7. Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) — Assumptions
The BSM model moves from discrete-time (binomial) to continuous-time. It requires the following 10
assumptions:

Assumption
What It Means
1. Stock prices are lognormally
distributed
Stock prices are bounded below by zero and skewed right —
consistent with prices that can't go negative but can rise dramatically.
2. No transaction costs
Markets are frictionless: no commissions, bid-ask spreads, or taxes.
3. No arbitrage opportunities
Mispricing is instantly exploited and eliminated.
4. Trading is continuous
Positions can be rebalanced at any instant.
5. Borrow/lend at rf, rf is
constant
A single, constant risk-free rate applies to all maturities.
6. European options only
No early exercise; BSM does not directly apply to American options.
7. Constant & known volatility
(σ)
The biggest and most-criticized assumption. Volatility is assumed
constant over the option's life.
8. Liquid underlying, divisible
The underlying can be traded in any quantity, including fractional
shares.
9. Short selling with full use of
proceeds
Short-sellers receive and can invest the full proceeds.
10. GBM describes price
movements
Geometric Brownian Motion: price changes are continuous and
smooth — no jumps.

The Lognormal Distribution — Critical Implication:
Percentage changes in stock prices in a short period are approximately normally distributed.
Therefore, the stock price itself (ST) has a lognormal distribution — it's bounded below by
zero and right-skewed.
ln(ST) ~ N(mean, variance)
Mean of ln(ST) = ln(S₀) + (μ − σ²/2)T

Std dev of ln(ST) = σ√T

heading: Confidence Interval for ST
body:
95% CI for Future Stock Price:  e^[ln(S₀) + (μ−σ²/2)T − 1.96σ√T]  <  ST  <
e^[ln(S₀) + (μ−σ²/2)T + 1.96σ√T]
Example: S₀ = $40, μ = 16%, σ = 20%, T = 0.5 → 95% CI: $32.55 < ST < $56.56

8. The Black-Scholes-Merton Model

heading: Core BSM Formulas
body:
BSM Call Formula:  c = S·N(d₁) − X·e⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
S·N(d₁) = stock component (levered equity position) X·e⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂) = bond component (amount
borrowed)

BSM Put Formula:  p = X·e⁻ʳᵀ·N(−d₂) − S·N(−d₁)
X·e⁻ʳᵀ·N(−d₂) = bond component (lending) S·N(−d₁) = stock component (short shares)

d₁ and d₂:  d₁ = [ln(S/X) + (r + σ²/2)T] / [σ√T] d₂ = d₁ − σ√T
r = continuously compounded risk-free rate; σ = annual volatility; T = time to expiration in years

N(·) = standard normal CDF:
N(d₂) = risk-neutral probability that the CALL expires in-the-money = P(ST > X)
N(−d₂) = risk-neutral probability that the PUT expires in-the-money = P(ST < X)
N(d₁) = call option delta (fraction of shares to buy to replicate the call)

heading: BSM Two-Component Interpretation
body:
Call Structure
Put Structure
CALL = S − B  (Buy stock, sell bonds)
• Stock component: Buy N(d₁) shares of
stock at price S
• Bond component: Borrow (sell) N(d₂)% of
a zero-coupon bond with face value X
• The call replicates the payoff of owning a
leveraged equity position
PUT = B − S  (Buy bonds, short stock)
• Bond component: Buy N(−d₂)% of a zero-
coupon bond
• Stock component: Short (sell) N(−d₁)
shares
• The put replicates the payoff of a long
bond, short stock position

heading: Replication Summary — Buy/Sell Positions
body:
Position
Stock Leg
Bond Leg
Buy a Call
Buy N(d₁) shares (+Sᵀ)
Sell N(d₂)% of a zero (−X)
Sell a Call
Sell N(d₁) shares (−Sᵀ)
Buy N(d₂)% of a zero (+X)
Buy a Put
Buy N(−d₂)% of a bond (+X)
Sell N(−d₁) shares (−Sᵀ)
Sell a Put
Sell N(−d₂)% of a bond (−X)
Buy N(−d₁) shares (+Sᵀ)

heading: Worked BSM Example
body:
Given: S = 100, X = 100, r = 5%, T = 1.0, σ = 30%
Calculate d₁, d₂:
d₁ = [ln(100/100) + (0.05 + 0.3²/2)(1)] / [0.3√1]
   = [0 + 0.095] / 0.3 = 0.3167
d₂ = 0.3167 − 0.3(1) = 0.0167
Normal CDF values:
N(d₁) = N(0.3167) = 0.624     N(d₂) = N(0.0167) = 0.507
N(−d₁) = 1 − 0.624 = 0.376   N(−d₂) = 1 − 0.507 = 0.493
Call and Put values:
c = 100(0.624) − 100e⁻⁰·⁰⁵(0.507) = 62.40 − 48.23 = 14.17

p = 100e⁻⁰·⁰⁵(0.493) − 100(0.376) = 46.88 − 37.60 = 9.29
Replication check — Call:
Buy 0.624 shares (cost = 62.40), borrow 48.23 → net cost = 14.17  ✓

9. BSM Modifications for Carry Benefits (γ)
γ (gamma, also written δ or δ) represents a continuous yield that reduces the cost of carrying the
underlying. Think of it as continuously paying out a benefit:

Modified d₁ (with carry benefit γ):  d₁ = [ln(S/X) + (r − γ + σ²/2)T] / [σ√T]   d₂ =
d₁ − σ√T
Carry benefit LOWERS d₁ and d₂ → reduces call value, increases put value

Equities
Currencies (Garman-Kohlhagen)
Equities (γ = δ = continuous dividend
yield):
c = Se⁻δᵀ·N(d₁) − Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
p = Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(−d₂) − Se⁻δᵀ·N(−d₁)
The dividend yield reduces the effective cost of
holding the stock. Higher δ → lower forward price
→ lower calls, higher puts.
Currencies (γ = rf = foreign risk-free rate):
c = Se⁻ʳ𝒻ᵀ·N(d₁) − Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
p = Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(−d₂) − Se⁻ʳ𝒻ᵀ·N(−d₁)
The foreign interest rate is the carry benefit:
holding foreign currency earns interest. Higher
foreign rate → lower call, higher put on the
currency.

Put-Call Parity — With Carry Benefits:  Se⁻γᵀ + p = Xe⁻ʳᵀ + c
The stock term is discounted at γ (carry benefit), while the strike is discounted at r (cost of money).

10. The Black Model
The Black Model is a BSM adaptation for underlying assets that are costless to carry (futures,
forwards). Since you don't need to pay to hold a futures contract (no upfront investment), the carry
benefit equals the risk-free rate — making the futures price replace the stock price × growth factor:

heading: Black Model — Options on Futures
body:
Black Model (Futures Options):  c = [F₀(T)·N(d₁) − X·N(d₂)]·e⁻ʳᵀ   p = [X·N(−d₂) −
F₀(T)·N(−d₁)]·e⁻ʳᵀ
F₀(T) = current futures price; X = exercise price

Black Model d₁, d₂ (Futures):  d₁ = [ln(F₀(T)/X) + (σ²/2)T] / [σ√T] d₂ = d₁ − σ√T
Note: no r in the numerator (futures are costless to carry). The futures price itself reflects cost-of-
carry.

Key: The futures contract is the underlying.
F₀(T) = N(d₁) is the risk-neutral probability-weighted futures price conditional on exercise
N(d₂) = probability the call expires ITM under risk-neutral measure
The e⁻ʳᵀ discount factor converts to present value (since the futures payoff occurs at T)

heading: Black Model — Interest Rate Options (Caplets & Floorlets)
body:
For interest rate options, the underlying is an FRA rate. The Black model prices each caplet and
floorlet:

Interest Rate Call (Caplet) — Black Model:  c = AP·[FRA·N(d₁) − XR·N(d₂)]·e⁻ʳᵀ
AP = accrual period (days/360 or days/365); FRA = forward rate; XR = exercise rate (cap rate)

Interest Rate Put (Floorlet) — Black Model:  p = AP·[XR·N(−d₂) − FRA·N(−d₁)]·e⁻ʳᵀ
The discount factor e⁻ʳᵀ discounts to the payment date T₂ (end of accrual period)

d₁ for Interest Rate Options:  d₁ = [ln(FRA(T₁,T₂)/XR) + (σ²/2)T₁] / [σ√T₁] d₂ = d₁ −
σ√T₁
T₁ = option expiration date (when the rate is set); note the 'T₁' subscripts — discount over the option
life

heading: Caps, Floors, and Collars
body:
Cap
Floor
CAP (series of caplets — interest rate call
options)
A borrower buys a cap (at strike XR = cap
rate). If the floating rate rises above XR, each
caplet pays the excess × AP × NA.
Cap Value = Σ (individual caplet values)
Long cap = protection against rising rates
(borrower's hedge)
FLOOR (series of floorlets — interest rate
put options)
A lender buys a floor (at strike XR = floor
rate). If the floating rate falls below XR, each
floorlet pays the shortfall × AP × NA.
Floor Value = Σ (individual floorlet values)
Long floor = protection against falling
rates (lender's hedge)

Cap-Floor Parity:
Long floor + Short cap = Pay fixed, receive floating (like a plain swap)
If XR = swap rate → Vcap = Vfloor → cost of collar = $0

heading: Swaptions (Options on Swaps)
body:
A swaption gives the holder the right to enter into a swap at a pre-agreed fixed rate (XR) on a specified
expiration date:

Payer Swaption
Receiver Swaption
Payer Swaption
Right to ENTER a swap as the fixed-rate
PAYER (get floating).
Value rises when swap rates RISE above
XR
Receiver Swaption
Right to ENTER a swap as the fixed-rate
RECEIVER (pay floating).
Value rises when swap rates FALL below
XR

Payer Swaption — Black Model:  Pay_swn = [Rfix·N(d₁) − XR·N(d₂)] × PVA
PVA = present value annuity factor = Σ DFj for all settlement dates j = 1..n (embeds the discount
factor)

Receiver Swaption — Black Model:  Rec_swn = [XR·N(−d₂) − Rfix·N(−d₁)] × PVA
Rfix = current market swap rate; XR = exercise (strike) swap rate


d₁ for Swaptions:  d₁ = [ln(Rfix/XR) + (σ²/2)T] / [σ√T] d₂ = d₁ − σ√T
T = time until the swaption EXPIRES (when the holder can choose to enter the swap)

11. Option Greeks
The Greeks measure sensitivity of option prices to changes in input variables.
Each Greek quantifies how much the option price (or delta) changes when ONE variable
changes by a small amount, holding all else constant.

heading: Delta (Δ)  —  Sensitivity to Underlying Price
body:
Delta (BSM):  Δc = e⁻δᵀ·N(d₁)   (call delta, always ∈ [0,1]) Δp = e⁻δᵀ·N(−d₁)
(put delta, always ∈ [−1,0])   Δc + |Δp| = 1.0  for the same X
δ = continuous dividend yield (or foreign rf). Without dividends, Δc = N(d₁).

Scenario
Delta Behavior
Deep OTM call
Δ → 0  (unlikely to expire ITM; small sensitivity)
ATM call
Δ ≈ 0.50  (equal chance of expiring ITM or OTM)
Deep ITM call
Δ → 1  (moves almost 1-for-1 with the stock)
At expiration (ITM)
Δ = 1  (certain to exercise)
At expiration (OTM)
Δ = 0  (won't be exercised)

heading: Delta Hedging
body:
A delta-neutral portfolio has zero net delta — it is hedged against small price movements:

Number of Hedging Instruments (NH):  NH = −(Portfolio delta) / (Delta of hedge
instrument)
Example: Short puts on 10,000 shares with Δp = −0.419 → Portfolio delta = −10,000 × −0.419 =
+4,190 Hedge: Short 4,190 shares (Δ = 1 each) → Net = 4,190 − 4,190 = 0


Delta hedging is DYNAMIC — you must continuously rebalance:
As the stock price changes, delta changes (due to gamma). A delta-neutral portfolio today
will NOT be delta-neutral tomorrow. The delta hedger must constantly buy and sell shares to
maintain neutrality.
Gamma is the reason you can't set-and-forget a delta hedge.

heading: Gamma (Γ)  —  Sensitivity of Delta to Underlying Price
body:
Gamma (BSM):  Γc = Γp = e⁻δᵀ·n(d₁) / [S·σ√T]
n(d₁) = standard normal PDF (not CDF). Gamma is always non-negative for long options.

Scenario
Gamma Behavior
ATM options
Gamma is LARGEST (delta most sensitive to price changes)
Deep ITM or OTM
Gamma is SMALLEST (delta barely changes)
Long options (call or put)
Positive gamma (you benefit from large moves)
Short options
Negative gamma (you suffer from large moves)
Underlying asset itself
Gamma = 0 (delta is always exactly 1)

Option Price Approximation using Gamma:  c_new − c_old ≈ (S_new − S_old)·Δc +
(Γc/2)·(S_new − S_old)²
This is a second-order (Taylor series) approximation. Delta gives the first-order term; gamma
provides the correction for curvature.

Gamma Risk — The Unhedgeable Residual:
Gamma measures the risk that REMAINS even after a portfolio is delta-neutral. A delta-
neutral portfolio is still exposed to gamma risk (exposure to large moves). Gamma risk can
be managed by adding options to the hedge, but it can NEVER be fully eliminated.

heading: Theta (Θ)  —  Time Decay
body:
Long Options
Short Options
Long options: NEGATIVE Theta
Time is your enemy. As time passes, option
value decays. Each day that passes reduces
the time value of a long option position.
"Time is your enemy when you are long options."
Short options: POSITIVE Theta
Time is your friend. As time passes, options
you have written lose value, benefiting the
writer's position. Time decay is the core
advantage of selling options.
"Time is your friend when you are short options."

Key Theta Facts:
• Theta is the RATE OF OPTION DECAY — measured in $ per day
• Theta is largest (fastest decay) for ATM options close to expiration
• Theta CANNOT be managed by taking positions in the underlying stock
• To adjust theta, you must trade OPTIONS (not stock)

heading: Vega (ν)  —  Sensitivity to Volatility
body:
Long Options
Short Options
Long options: POSITIVE Vega
Long options benefit from increased volatility.
Higher vol = wider distribution of future prices
= more chance of a big payoff. You are 'long
volatility'.
Short options: NEGATIVE Vega
Short options suffer from increased volatility.
More vol = more chance of large adverse
moves against the writer. You are 'short
volatility'.

Critical: Vega is based on FUTURE volatility — unobservable!
• Option value is VERY sensitive to vega — small changes in vol assumptions create large
price differences
• BSM assumes constant vol (σ) — but in reality, vol changes over time
• This is the foundation of implied volatility trading

heading: Rho (ρ)  —  Sensitivity to Risk-Free Rate
body:
Option Type
Rho Direction & Explanation
Call options
POSITIVE Rho
Calls benefit from higher rf. Higher rf raises the growth factor on S (S·eʳᵀ)
and lowers the PV of the strike (X·e⁻ʳᵀ). Both effects make calls more
valuable.
Put options
NEGATIVE Rho
Puts suffer from higher rf. Higher rf lowers the PV of the strike (X·e⁻ʳᵀ),
which is the key value driver for puts.

heading: Greeks Summary Table
body:
Greek
Definition
Sign
Delta (Δ)
Change in option price per $1 change in
underlying
Call: [0,+1]  |  Put: [−1,0]
Gamma (Γ)
Change in delta per $1 change in underlying
Always ≥ 0 for long options;
largest ATM
Theta (Θ)
Change in option price per 1-day passage of
time
Long = negative (enemy); Short
= positive (friend)
Vega (ν)
Change in option price per 1% change in
volatility
Long = positive; Short =
negative
Rho (ρ)
Change in option price per 1% change in rf
Call = positive; Put = negative

12. Implied Volatility
Volatility is the only unobservable input in the BSM model. We can reverse-engineer it from market
option prices:

Implied Volatility Concept:  Market price = BSM(S, X, r, T, σ_implied)   → Solve
for σ_implied
There is no closed-form solution — must be found iteratively (trial and error or numerical methods).

heading: The Volatility Smile and Skew
body:
BSM Prediction
Market Reality
If BSM were perfect:
All options on the same underlying with the
same expiration should have the same
implied volatility — regardless of the strike
price.
What we actually observe:
Implied volatility differs across strikes. OTM
puts typically have HIGHER implied vol than
ATM options (the skew). This reflects the
market's fear of large downside moves —
demand for OTM put protection is high.

Pattern
Description & Implication
Volatility Smile
Implied vol is HIGHER for both deep OTM and deep ITM options vs
ATM. Symmetric U-shape. More common in currency options.
Volatility Skew / Smirk
Implied vol is HIGHER for OTM puts than ATM or OTM calls.
Downward sloping. Most common in equity markets — reflects crash
risk (left-tail demand for puts).
Term Structure of Volatility
Implied vol varies across different expiration dates for the same strike.
Often upward-sloping (uncertainty increases over time); can invert
before major events.

heading: Implied Volatility Trading
body:
Comparing implied vol to your forecasted vol creates trading opportunities:
• If implied vol > your forecast → Option is OVERPRICED → SELL (short vol) → e.g., sell
calls, sell puts, write straddles
• If implied vol < your forecast → Option is UNDERPRICED → BUY (long vol) → e.g., buy
calls, buy puts, buy straddles
Example from notes:
3-month ATM call at 19% IV; 1-month ITM put at 24% IV; Your belief: 25% vol
Action: Buy the index call (long vol at 19% < 25%) + Sell the component put (short vol at 24% < 25%... but
wait — 24% < 25% so the put is also underpriced vs belief; need to long both here or adjust for position)

13. Master Formula & Concept Reference Sheet

heading: Binomial Model — Key Formulas
body:
Formula
Expression
Risk-neutral probability (up)
π = [(1+r) − d] / [u − d]
One-period call price
c₀ = [π·c+ + (1−π)·c−] / (1+r)
One-period put price
p₀ = [π·p+ + (1−π)·p−] / (1+r)
Two-period shortcut (call)
c₀ = [π²c++ + 2π(1−π)c+− + (1−π)²c−−] / (1+r)²
Hedge ratio (delta)
Δ = (c+ − c−) / (S+ − S−)
American put at node
Value = Max(Intrinsic, Continuation)
Put-call parity
S + p = X/(1+r)ᵀ + c   or   Se⁻γᵀ + p = Xe⁻ʳᵀ + c

heading: BSM / Black Model — Key Formulas
body:
Formula
Expression
BSM call (no dividends)
c = S·N(d₁) − Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
BSM put (no dividends)
p = Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(−d₂) − S·N(−d₁)
BSM call (equities, δ)
c = Se⁻δᵀ·N(d₁) − Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
BSM call (currencies, rf)
c = Se⁻ʳ𝒻ᵀ·N(d₁) − Xe⁻ʳᵀ·N(d₂)
Black Model (futures)
c = [F₀·N(d₁) − X·N(d₂)]e⁻ʳᵀ
Black d₁ (futures)
d₁ = [ln(F₀/X) + (σ²/2)T] / (σ√T)
Caplet (Black)
c = AP·[FRA·N(d₁) − XR·N(d₂)]e⁻ʳᵀ
Payer swaption (Black)
Pay_swn = [Rfix·N(d₁) − XR·N(d₂)]·PVA
Receiver swaption (Black)
Rec_swn = [XR·N(−d₂) − Rfix·N(−d₁)]·PVA
d₁ (BSM, no carry)
d₁ = [ln(S/X) + (r + σ²/2)T] / (σ√T)
d₁ (with carry γ)
d₁ = [ln(S/X) + (r − γ + σ²/2)T] / (σ√T)
d₂
d₂ = d₁ − σ√T   (applies to all variants)

heading: Option Greeks — Signs at a Glance
body:
Greek
Long Options
Short Options

LONG Options
SHORT Options
Delta (Δ)
Call: +, Put: −
Call: −, Put: +
Gamma (Γ)
+ (always positive)
− (always negative)
Theta (Θ)
− (time hurts you)
+ (time helps you)
Vega (ν)
+ (vol helps you)
− (vol hurts you)
Rho (ρ)
Call: +, Put: −
Call: −, Put: +

heading: Exam Trap Reminders
body:
• N(d₂) = prob call expires ITM;  N(d₁) = call DELTA — these are DIFFERENT things
• BSM uses continuously compounded r — convert from periodic if given (rc = ln(1+r))
• The Black model has NO r in d₁ numerator (futures are costless to carry)
• American call ≠ European call ONLY when dividends exist (non-div: they're equal)
• American put > European put always — early exercise premium is always ≥ 0
• Gamma = 0 for the underlying itself (delta is always 1)
• Theta cannot be neutralized by trading the underlying — must trade options
• Implied vol varies across strikes (smile/skew) — this VIOLATES BSM's constant vol
assumption
• Cap = series of caplets (calls on rates); Floor = series of floorlets (puts on rates)
• Payer swaption profits when swap rates RISE; Receiver swaption profits when rates FALL


CFA Level II | Derivatives: Valuation of Contingent Claims | Complete Reading

heading: Two Key Concepts: Price vs. Value
body:
Forward Price
Forward Value
Forward PRICE (F₀)
The fixed rate/price agreed upon at initiation
that makes the contract's VALUE equal to
zero at initiation. It does not change over the
life of the contract — it is locked in at T=0.
F₀ is determined at initiation and never
changes.
Forward VALUE (V)
The current worth of the forward contract to
the long (or short) side. At initiation V=0. As
the underlying price changes over the life of
the contract, V fluctuates. At expiration, V =
ST − F₀.
V changes continuously as market
conditions evolve.

heading: No-Arbitrage Assumptions
body:
• Replicating instruments are available and investable at known prices
• No transaction costs, taxes, or market frictions
• Short selling is permitted with full use of proceeds
• Borrowing and lending at the same risk-free rate (r)
• Risk-free rate is constant over the contract period

heading: Carry Arbitrage — The Core Logic
body:
Strategy
Action
Carry arbitrage (forward overpriced)
Short (sell) the forward + Long (buy) the underlying using
borrowed funds. Earn the difference risk-free.
Reverse carry arbitrage (forward
underpriced)
Long (buy) the forward + Short the underlying + Lend the
proceeds at rf. Earn the difference risk-free.
No-arbitrage forward price
The unique price at which neither strategy generates
profit. Cost of carry = rf × S₀ × T.

2. LOS a — Carry Arbitrage Model

heading: Forward Price: No Cash Flows on Underlying
body:
When the underlying pays no dividends, coupons, or storage costs — the forward price is simply the
future value of the spot price:

Forward Price (Periodic Compounding):  F₀(T) = S₀ × (1 + r)^T

S₀ = spot price today; r = risk-free rate (periodic); T = time to delivery in years

Forward Price (Continuous Compounding):  F₀(T) = S₀ × e^(rT)
Use when rates are given as continuously compounded. Both forms are equivalent depending on
convention.

heading: Forward Price: With Carry Costs and Benefits
body:
Real-world underlyings generate cash flows and involve storage costs. The general formula adjusts for
these:

General Carry Arbitrage Formula:  F₀(T) = [S₀ + PV(θ) − PV(γ)] × (1 + r)^T
θ = carry costs (storage, insurance, convenience costs) — these ADD to F₀ γ = carry benefits
(dividends, coupons, convenience yield) — these SUBTRACT from F₀

Intuition: The forward price = what it costs you to 'carry' the position to delivery. You must
pay to borrow the money (cost = r × S₀), pay any storage/insurance costs (θ), but you
receive any income from holding the asset (γ).

heading: Forward Value During Life of Contract
body:
After initiation, the forward's value changes as the spot price moves. To the LONG:

Value of Long Forward (Periodic):  Vt(T) = St − F₀(T) / (1 + r)^(T−t)
St = current spot price; F₀(T) = original forward price; (T−t) = remaining time to delivery

Value of Long Forward (Continuous):  Vt(T) = St × e^−γ(T−t) − F₀(T) × e^−r(T−t)
With continuous carry benefit γ (dividend yield). Short forward value = negative of long value.

Time
Value to Long
At initiation (t=0)
V₀ = 0. The forward price is set so both parties are indifferent. No
cash changes hands.

Time
Value to Long
During the contract (0 < t < T)
V = St − PV(F₀). Long benefits when S rises above PV(F₀); short
benefits when S falls.
At expiration (t=T)
VT = ST − F₀. The long profits if ST > F₀; the short profits if ST <
F₀.

heading: Worked Example — Equity Forward (No Dividends)
body:
Given: S₀ = 1,000, r = 2.5%, T = 0.75 years (9 months). No dividends.
Forward price at initiation:
F₀ = 1,000 × (1.025)^0.75 = 1,000 × 1.01869 = 1,018.69
Value 3 months later (t=0.25) when S = 1,050:
Vt = 1,050 − 1,018.69 / (1.025)^0.50 = 1,050 − 1,006.24 = 43.76
Interpretation: The long has a mark-to-market gain of $43.76 per unit. The short has a loss
of $43.76.

3. LOS b/c — Equity Forwards & Forwards with Cash
Flows

heading: Equity Forwards — Discrete Dividends
body:
For a single stock paying known discrete dividends, remove the PV of dividends from S₀:

Equity Forward Price — Discrete Dividends:  F₀ = [S₀ − PV(Dividends)] × (1 + r)^T
PV(Div) = Σ [Dj / (1 + r)^tj] for each dividend Dj paid at time tj during [0, T]

Worked Example:
S₀ = CHF 70, T = 1 year, r = 1.0%
Dividend CHF 2.20 paid at t = 0.5 years

PV(Div) = 2.20 / (1.01)^0.5 = 2.20 / 1.00499 = 2.1891
F₀ = (70 − 2.1891) × (1.01)^1.0 = 67.8109 × 1.01 = 68.489

heading: Equity Index Forwards — Continuous Dividend Yield
body:
For equity indices paying a continuous dividend yield δ:

Equity Index Forward (Continuous Yield):  F₀ = S₀ × e^(r−δ)T
δ = continuous dividend yield of the index. Higher δ → lower F₀ (dividends reduce the cost of carry).

Value of Equity Index Forward:  Vt = St × e^−δ(T−t) − F₀ × e^−r(T−t)
St = current index level; discounts both spot (at δ) and F₀ (at r) to present value.

Example — EURO STOXX 50:
S₀ = 3,000, δ = 3.00%, r = 0.15%, T = 0.25 (3 months)
F₀ = 3,000 × e^(0.0015 − 0.03) × 0.25 = 3,000 × e^(−0.007125)
   = 3,000 × 0.99290 = 2,978.71
Observation: F₀ < S₀ because the dividend yield (3%) exceeds the risk-free rate (0.15%).
The index forward trades at a discount to spot.

heading: Fixed-Income Forwards
body:
For bond forwards, the underlying generates coupon cash flows (γ = PV of coupons). Accrued interest
must be handled carefully:

Fixed-Income Forward — Full Price:  F₀(Full) = [S₀(Full) + AI₀ − PV(Coupons)] × (1 +
r)^T − AIT
AI₀ = accrued interest at initiation; AIT = accrued interest at delivery; PV(Coupons) = PV of coupons
paid during [0,T]


Quoted (Clean) Forward Price:  QF₀ = F₀(Full) − AIT
Market convention quotes the CLEAN (quoted) price. AIT is subtracted to remove embedded accrued
interest at delivery.

heading: Futures on Fixed Income — Conversion Factor & CTD
body:
Bond futures are standardized and quote a 'quoted futures price'. The invoice price paid at delivery
involves a conversion factor (CF):

Concept
Formula / Explanation
Quoted futures price (QF₀)
QF₀ = F₀ / CF   where F₀ is the full forward price
Invoice price
Invoice = QF₀ × CF + AIT   (what the short receives at
delivery)
Conversion factor (CF)
Price of the deliverable bond per $1 face value if yield = 6%.
Standardizes bonds to common pricing.
Cheapest-to-Deliver (CTD)
The bond the short chooses to deliver. Short picks
whichever bond minimizes: (Quoted price − QF₀ × CF).
Always verify by comparing CTD candidates.

CTD Intuition: The short in a bond futures contract wants to deliver the cheapest bond.
Bond A is CTD if: Quoted_A − QF₀ × CF_A < Quoted_B − QF₀ × CF_B. The short delivers
CTD, so pricing should use the CTD bond's characteristics.

4. LOS d — Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs)
Definition: An FRA is an OTC forward contract on an interest rate. The buyer agrees to pay
a fixed rate and receive the floating rate (LIBOR) on a notional principal for a specified future
period. Used to hedge or speculate on future interest rate moves.
Notation: FRA(m×n) means: the FRA starts in m months and the underlying rate covers a
period from m to n months. E.g., 3×9 FRA: starts in 3 months, underlying rate is a 6-month
rate covering months 3→9.

heading: FRA Fixed Rate (No-Arbitrage)
body:
The FRA fixed rate is the implied forward rate derived from the yield curve:

FRA Fixed Rate — Money Market Rates:  FRA(m×n) = { [1 + L(n) × n/360] / [1 + L(m)
× m/360] − 1 } × 360/(n−m)
L(m) = LIBOR for m-day period; L(n) = LIBOR for n-day period; n−m = days in the forward period

heading: FRA Settlement — Advanced Set, Settled in Arrears
body:
Settlement is advanced (paid at the start of the forward period, not the end). The payoff is discounted:

FRA Payoff (Long = Fixed Rate Payer):  Payoff = NA × (LIBOR − FRA fixed) ×
(days/360) / [1 + LIBOR × (days/360)]
Long profits when LIBOR > fixed rate (receives net). Short profits when LIBOR < fixed rate (pays net).

Sign Convention:
• Long FRA (fixed rate PAYER): Profits when rates RISE above the fixed rate → use as
borrowing hedge
• Short FRA (fixed rate RECEIVER): Profits when rates FALL below the fixed rate → use as
lending hedge

heading: FRA Valuation During Life of Contract
body:
FRA Value (to Long, at time t):  Vt = [new FRA rate − original FRA rate] × (days/360)
× NA / [1 + current LIBOR × (remaining days/360)]
New FRA rate = recalculated forward rate using current market rates at time t

heading: Key Structural Differences
body:
Feature
Forward
Futures
Feature
Forward
Futures
Venue
OTC — bilateral
Exchange — standardized
Credit risk
Counterparty risk exists
Clearinghouse eliminates credit
risk
Settlement
Single settlement at expiry
Daily mark-to-market (variation
margin)
Flexibility
Customizable (size, date, price)
Standardized lots and delivery
dates
Margin
Not required (credit lines)
Initial + variation margin required
Pricing
F₀ determined at initiation
Marked-to-market daily

heading: Why Futures Price ≠ Forward Price
body:
In theory with constant interest rates, futures price = forward price. In practice, they differ due to:


Driver
Effect
Daily mark-to-market (MTM)
Futures gains/losses are settled daily in cash. These
flows can be reinvested (or must be funded) at
prevailing rates.
Correlation effect
If the underlying and interest rates are POSITIVELY
correlated: daily gains on long futures → received
when rates are high (reinvested at high rates) →
futures price HIGHER than forward price.
Negative correlation
If underlying and rates are NEGATIVELY correlated:
daily gains arrive when rates are low → futures price
LOWER than forward price.
Zero correlation
If no correlation between underlying and rates →
futures price ≈ forward price.

Example — Interest Rate Futures: Eurodollar futures are positively correlated with interest
rates (bond prices inversely). Short Eurodollar futures profits when rates RISE (rates up →
prices down). These profits arrive when rates are HIGH → reinvested at high rates →
futures price < forward price for bonds.

heading: Futures Pricing — Cost of Carry
body:
Futures Price Formula:  f₀(T) = S₀ × (1 + r)^T   [same form as forward, but marks
to market daily]
For equity index futures: f₀ = S₀ × e^(r−δ)T  where δ = continuous dividend yield

6. LOS f/g — Interest Rate Swaps
A swap = series of forward contracts. Each settlement date in a swap is equivalent to
settling a separate FRA (for interest rate swaps) or forward (for currency/equity swaps). The
key difference: all legs use the SAME fixed rate, whereas a series of FRAs would use
different forward rates for each date.
Plain Vanilla Interest Rate Swap: Fixed-rate payer pays fixed, receives LIBOR. Fixed-rate
receiver pays LIBOR, receives fixed. Net payments are exchanged; notional is never
transferred.

heading: Swap Fixed Rate (No-Arbitrage)
body:
The fixed rate is set so the swap has zero value at initiation (present value of fixed leg = PV of floating
leg):

Discount Factors:  DFt = 1 / (1 + Lt × t)   [money market]   or   DFt = 1 / (1 +
spot_t)^t
Lt = LIBOR for period t; ΣDF = sum of all periodic discount factors

Swap Fixed Rate:  r_fix = (1 − DFn) / ΣDF
DFn = discount factor for the final period; ΣDF = sum of all discount factors over all settlement dates

Intuition: The numerator (1 − DFn) = the implied floating leg payment (difference between
$1 today and PV of $1 at final settlement). Dividing by ΣDF converts this into a level annuity
— the fixed rate that makes both legs equal in PV.

heading: Swap Fixed Rate — Worked Example
body:
Given: 5-year annual swap, rates: L(1)=0.5%, L(2)=0.7%, L(3)=0.9%, L(4)=1.0%,
L(5)=1.25%
Step 1 — Compute Discount Factors:
DF₁ = 1 / (1.005)^1 = 0.99502
DF₂ = 1 / (1.007)^2 = 0.98609
DF₃ = 1 / (1.009)^3 = 0.97352
DF₄ = 1 / (1.010)^4 = 0.96099
DF₅ = 1 / (1.0125)^5 = 0.93949
ΣDF = 0.99502 + 0.98609 + 0.97352 + 0.96099 + 0.93949 = 4.85511
Step 2 — Fixed Rate:
r_fix = (1 − 0.93949) / 4.85511 = 0.06051 / 4.85511 = 1.2464%

Interpretation: Both parties agree to the 1.2464% fixed rate. At initiation, the swap has zero
value to both parties.

heading: Swap Valuation During Life of Contract
body:
After initiation, rates change and the swap takes on positive or negative value. Two equivalent
methods:

Rate Differential Method
Bond Equivalent Method
Method 1 — Fixed Rate Differential
V = (FS_old − FS_new) × ΣDF_new × NA
FS_old = original fixed rate; FS_new =
current market swap rate; ΣDF_new = sum of
discount factors at current rates
Method 2 — Bond Equivalent
V_long = PV(fixed leg) − PV(floating leg)
Model the swap as long a fixed-rate bond +
short a floating-rate bond (or vice versa).
Floating bond = par at each reset date.

Swap Value — Rate Differential Method:  V(fixed payer) = [r_fix_new − r_fix_old] ×
ΣDFnew × NA
Positive = gain for fixed-rate payer (rates rose); Negative = loss (rates fell). For fixed-rate RECEIVER:
V = [r_fix_old − r_fix_new] × ΣDFnew × NA

heading: Swap Valuation — Worked Example
body:
Setup: 5-year annual swap, r_fix = 1.2464% (from above), NA = $1M. After 1 year, rates
shift: L(1)=0.7%, L(2)=1.0%, L(3)=1.4%, L(4)=1.7%
Remaining 4 years — New DFs:
DF₁ = 1/(1.007)^1 = 0.99304,  DF₂ = 1/(1.010)^2 = 0.98030
DF₃ = 1/(1.014)^3 = 0.95942,  DF₄ = 1/(1.017)^4 = 0.93560
ΣDF_new = 0.99304 + 0.98030 + 0.95942 + 0.93560 = 3.86836
New Swap Rate:
r_fix_new = (1 − 0.93560) / 3.86836 = 0.06440 / 3.86836 = 1.6644%
Value to Fixed-Rate PAYER:

V = (1.6644% − 1.2464%) × 3.86836 × $1M = 0.4180% × 3.86836 × $1M
  = $16,170
Interpretation: Rates rose, so the fixed payer gained $16,170. The fixed receiver lost
$16,170.

7. Currency Swaps
Structure: In a fixed-for-fixed currency swap, both parties pay fixed rates — but in
DIFFERENT currencies. Notional principals are exchanged at initiation AND at expiration
(the only swap type with this feature). Intermediate payments are coupon-like fixed
payments in each currency.
Three risk exposures: (1) Domestic interest rate risk, (2) Foreign interest rate risk, (3)
Exchange rate risk. This makes currency swaps more complex than plain vanilla rate swaps.

heading: Currency Swap Fixed Rates
body:
Each leg's fixed rate is calculated using that currency's yield curve:

Domestic Fixed Rate:  r_dom = (1 − DFn,dom) / ΣDF_dom
Calculated using domestic currency discount factors

Foreign Fixed Rate:  r_for = (1 − DFn,for) / ΣDF_for
Calculated using foreign currency discount factors

Notional Equivalence:  NA_domestic = S₀(d/f) × NA_foreign
S₀(d/f) = spot exchange rate (domestic per foreign); notionals are exchanged at S₀ at inception.

heading: Currency Swap Valuation
body:
Value of Currency Swap (Pay Foreign Fixed, Receive Domestic Fixed):  V = NA_d × (r_dom ×
ΣDF_d + DFn,d) − St × NA_f × (r_for × ΣDF_f + DFn,f)

St = current spot exchange rate; first term = PV of domestic leg; second term = PV of foreign leg at
current spot.

Feature
Explanation
Principal exchanges
At initiation: swap equivalent notionals at S₀. At
expiration: re-exchange at the ORIGINAL S₀ (not
current spot). Exchange rate risk arises because
the final exchange uses a spot rate fixed years
ago.
Value changes
Swap gains value to the domestic payer when
domestic rates fall (domestic bond rises in value)
OR when foreign currency appreciates (makes
foreign leg cheaper in domestic terms to offset).
Opposite for foreign payer.

8. Equity Swaps
Definition: One party pays the return on an equity index (or single stock); the other pays
fixed or floating rate. Equity return includes both price appreciation and dividends. If the
index falls, the equity payer receives a payment from the other side.

heading: Types of Equity Swaps
body:
Type
Description
Receive Equity, Pay Fixed
Receive total equity return + pay fixed rate. Gain if equity
outperforms. Long equity + short fixed bond.
Receive Equity, Pay Floating
Receive total equity return + pay LIBOR. Gain if equity
outperforms LIBOR. Long equity + short floating bond.
Receive Index 1, Pay Index 2
Exchange returns of two different equity indices. Used for
asset allocation tilts without transaction costs.

heading: Equity Swap Fixed Rate
body:
Same calculation as interest rate swap — fixed rate set so PV of fixed leg = PV of equity leg at
initiation:


Equity Swap Fixed Rate:  r_fix = (1 − DFn) / ΣDF
Identical formula to interest rate swap. The equity leg is implicitly worth par at initiation.

heading: Equity Swap Valuation
body:
Value to Fixed-Rate RECEIVER (Equity Payer):  V = NA × (r_fix × ΣDF + DFn) − NA × (St
/ S₀)
St/S₀ = total return index level ratio; first term = PV of fixed payments + principal; second term =
current equity value.

Value to Equity RECEIVER (Fixed Payer):  V = NA × (St / S₀) − NA × (r_fix × ΣDF +
DFn)
Positive when equity has risen (St > S₀ adjusted for time value). Negative when equity has fallen.

heading: Worked Example — Equity Swap
body:
Setup: 5-year annual swap, r_fix = 1.2464%, NA = $1M, S₀ = 100 (index). Receive fixed,
pay equity.
After 1 year: Index = 105 (rose 5%), same new discount factors as Section 6 (ΣDF_new =

heading: , DFn = 0.93560)
body:
Value to Fixed-Rate RECEIVER (pays equity):
V = NA × (r_fix × ΣDF + DFn) − NA × (St / S₀)
  = $1M × (0.012464 × 3.86836 + 0.93560) − $1M × (105/100)
  = $1M × (0.04820 + 0.93560) − $1M × 1.05
  = $1M × 0.98380 − $1M × 1.05
  = $983,800 − $1,050,000 = −$66,200
Interpretation: Fixed receiver lost $66,200 because the equity (which they pay) rose 5%,
outperforming the fixed leg.


9. Exam Traps & Key Distinctions
• Price ≠ Value: Price is fixed at initiation; Value fluctuates daily. Never confuse them.
• At initiation: Value = 0. After initiation: V changes with spot prices.
• Carry costs (θ) ADD to forward price; Carry benefits (γ) SUBTRACT from forward price.
• FRA long = fixed-rate PAYER = benefits when rates RISE (used to hedge future
borrowing costs).
• FRA settlement is ADVANCED: payoff discounted at current LIBOR for the forward
period.
• Swap fixed rate formula: r_fix = (1 − DFn) / ΣDF — memorize this cold.
• Currency swaps exchange NOTIONAL at both initiation AND expiration. Other swaps do
NOT.
• Equity swap: if index FALLS, the equity payer RECEIVES net payment. Read carefully.
• Swap valuation: V = (r_fix_old − r_fix_new) × ΣDF_new × NA for FIXED-RATE
RECEIVER.
• Futures vs. forward: positive correlation between underlying and rates → futures price >
forward price.
• CTD bond: short in bond futures delivers the cheapest bond. Always compute Quoted −
QF × CF for each candidate.
• For the equity index forward, use continuous compounding: F₀ = S₀ × e^(r−δ)T.


10. Master Formula Reference Sheet

heading: Deriving the Forward Price: The Arbitrage Logic ................................................... 7
Example: Valuing a Forward Position .................................................................. 11
body: 5. Carry Arbitrage Model: WITH Underlying Cash Flows .............................................. 12

heading: Example: Stock Index Forward with Dividends .................................................... 13
body:
6. TO BE CONTINUED... .............................................................................................. 14




Page 3
1. Foundational Principles: The Arbitrage-Free Framework
Forward commitments (forwards, futures, swaps) are priced using a NO-ARBITRAGE
approach. This entire module is built on thinking like an arbitrageur.

heading: The Arbitrageur's Rules
body:
RULE #1: Do not use your own money. RULE #2: Do not take any price risk.

Arbitrageurs make risk-free profits by exploiting mispricings. To follow these rules, they:
•
Borrow or lend money (don't use own capital)
•
Buy or sell assets simultaneously (eliminate price risk)
•
Lock in profits with offsetting positions

heading: The Law of One Price
body:
If two investments have IDENTICAL future cash flows (regardless of what happens),
they MUST have the same current price.

If not → Arbitrage opportunity:
•
Buy the cheaper asset
•
Sell the more expensive asset
•
Lock in risk-free profit

heading: Value Additivity
body:
The value of a portfolio = Sum of values of each instrument in the portfolio.

This allows us to:
•
Replicate derivative cash flows with portfolios
•
Value derivatives by valuing the replicating portfolio

Page 4

heading: Pricing vs. Valuation (Critical Distinction)
body:
Concept
Definition
When
Result
Pricing
Determine
appropriate forward
price/rate at
initiation
Contract initiation
(Time 0)
F₀ set so V₀ = 0
Valuation
Determine value of
existing contract
After initiation (Time
t)
Vₜ (can be +/−)

At initiation: Contract structured so both parties have zero value (V₀ = 0)
After initiation: Market moves → Contract has positive value to one party, negative to
the other



Page 5
2. Notation and The Convergence Property

heading: Standard Notation
body:
Symbol
Meaning
Sₜ
Spot price at time t (immediate delivery)
Fₜ
Forward price at time t
fₜ
Futures price at time t
S₀, F₀, f₀
Spot, forward, futures price at initiation
(Time 0)
Sᴛ, Fᴛ, fᴛ
Spot, forward, futures price at expiration
(Time T)
Vₜ
Value of forward contract at time t
vₜ
Value of futures contract at time t

heading: The Convergence Property (CRITICAL)
body:
AT EXPIRATION: Fᴛ = fᴛ = Sᴛ

At expiration, forward price = futures price = spot price.


Page 6
WHY? Because buying via forward/futures at expiration IS equivalent to spot
transaction.

Arbitrage Enforcement of Convergence
If Fᴛ > Sᴛ at expiration (forward price above spot):
1. Sell forward contract
2. Borrow money, buy asset in spot market
3. Deliver asset via forward, repay loan
4. Profit = Fᴛ − Sᴛ

If Fᴛ < Sᴛ at expiration (forward price below spot):
5. Buy forward contract
6. Short sell asset in spot market
7. Take delivery via forward, cover short
8. Profit = Sᴛ − Fᴛ

Arbitrageurs' actions force convergence!

heading: Prior to Expiration: Fₜ ≈ fₜ (Usually)
body:
Before expiration, forward and futures prices are usually very close but may differ
slightly due to:
•
Counterparty default risk (forwards have more)
•
Daily settlement in futures (mark-to-market)
•
Correlation between underlying asset price and interest rates

For most purposes: ASSUME Fₜ = fₜ before expiration.



Page 7
3. Carry Arbitrage Model: No Underlying Cash Flows
This is THE fundamental model. We'll build it step-by-step using arbitrage logic.

heading: The Setup
body:
Assume:
•
Underlying asset pays NO dividends, interest, or other cash flows
•
Can borrow/lend at risk-free rate r
•
No storage costs (or they're negligible)
•
No transaction costs

Example: Non-dividend paying stock, gold (ignoring storage)

heading: Deriving the Forward Price: The Arbitrage Logic
body:
We want to find F₀ (forward price at initiation) using no-arbitrage.

Create two portfolios with IDENTICAL cash flows at Time T:

Portfolio A: Buy forward contract
•
Time 0 cash flow: 0 (no payment at initiation)
•
Time T cash flow: Receive asset, pay F₀
•
Net Time T: Own asset, paid F₀

Portfolio B: Buy asset today with borrowed money
•
Time 0: Borrow S₀ at rate r, buy asset for S₀
•
Time 0 net cash flow: 0 (borrowed = spent)
•
Time T: Own asset, owe S₀(1+r)ᵀ on loan
•
Net Time T: Own asset, paid S₀(1+r)ᵀ

Both portfolios result in owning the asset at Time T!

Page 8

Portfolio A pays: F₀
Portfolio B pays: S₀(1+r)ᵀ

By Law of One Price (identical outcomes must have identical costs):

F₀ = S₀(1 + r)ᵀ    (discrete compounding)

Or with continuous compounding:
F₀ = S₀ × e^(rT)

heading: Interpretation: What Does This Mean?
body:
The forward price = Spot price × Future Value Factor

Intuition: Forward price compensates for:
•
Interest foregone by NOT buying asset today (opportunity cost)
•
Seller's cost to finance holding the asset until delivery

Forward price > Spot price when r > 0 (normal case)
→ Called "contango" (forward price above spot)

heading: Example: Pricing a Gold Forward
body:
Data:
•
Current spot price of gold (S₀): $1,800/oz
•
Risk-free rate (r): 4% per year
•
Contract maturity (T): 1 year

Page 9
•
Assume no storage costs, no cash flows

Calculate forward price:
F₀ = S₀(1 + r)ᵀ
F₀ = $1,800 × (1.04)¹
F₀ = $1,872

The 1-year forward price is $1,872/oz.

Interpretation: If you agree today to buy gold in 1 year, you pay $1,872. This is $72
more than spot because the seller must finance holding the gold (4% × $1,800).



Page 10
4. Valuing Forward Contracts (After Initiation)
Once a forward is established, its value changes as market prices move.

heading: Value at Expiration (Simple)
body:
Vᴛ (long) = Sᴛ − F₀
Vᴛ (short) = F₀ − Sᴛ

At expiration, value = Spot price − Forward price locked in

heading: Value Before Expiration
body:
Before expiration, we need to discount to present value.

The value of a LONG forward at time t (before expiration):

Vₜ (long) = [Fₜ − F₀] / (1 + r)^(T−t)

Or equivalently:
Vₜ (long) = Sₜ − F₀ / (1 + r)^(T−t)

Where:
•
Fₜ = Forward price of NEW contract at time t (with same expiration T)
•
F₀ = Forward price locked in at initiation
•
T−t = Time remaining to expiration

heading: The Logic Behind Valuation
body:
Page 11
Think of it this way:
•
You're LONG a forward to buy at F₀
•
Current forward price for same transaction is Fₜ
•
If Fₜ > F₀ → Your contract is valuable (locked in lower price)
•
Profit at expiration: Fₜ − F₀
•
PV of profit today: [Fₜ − F₀] / (1+r)^(T−t)

heading: Example: Valuing a Forward Position
body:
You entered a LONG forward on gold 3 months ago:
•
Agreed price (F₀): $1,872/oz
•
Maturity: 1 year from initiation
•
Now: 3 months have passed (t = 0.25 years)
•
Time remaining: T−t = 0.75 years
•
Current spot price: $1,950
•
Risk-free rate: 4%

Step 1: Calculate current forward price (9-month contract):
Fₜ = Sₜ × (1 + r)^(T−t)
Fₜ = $1,950 × (1.04)^0.75 = $2,007.15

Step 2: Calculate value of your existing position:
Vₜ = [Fₜ − F₀] / (1 + r)^(T−t)
Vₜ = [$2,007.15 − $1,872] / (1.04)^0.75
Vₜ = $135.15 / 1.0294 = $131.26

Your long forward has gained $131.26 in value! You locked in $1,872 when current
forward price is $2,007.



Page 12
5. Carry Arbitrage Model: WITH Underlying Cash Flows
Now we add DIVIDENDS, INTEREST, or other benefits from holding the underlying.

heading: The Modified Model
body:
When underlying pays cash flows (dividends, coupons), we need to adjust for this
CARRY BENEFIT.

General principle: Cash flows reduce the cost of carrying the asset.

F₀ = (S₀ − PV(Cash Flows)) × (1 + r)ᵀ

Or rearranged:
F₀ = S₀(1 + r)ᵀ − FV(Cash Flows)

Intuition:
•
Start with no-cash-flow formula: S₀(1+r)ᵀ
•
Subtract benefit received: FV(dividends/coupons)
•
Forward price is LOWER because holder receives cash flows

heading: Continuous Dividends (Dividend Yield)
body:
For assets paying continuous dividend yield δ:

F₀ = S₀ × e^((r − δ)T)

Or discrete:

Page 13
F₀ = S₀ × [(1+r)/(1+δ)]ᵀ

Note: Net cost of carry = r − δ
•
If r > δ → Forward > Spot (contango)
•
If r < δ → Forward < Spot (backwardation)
•
If r = δ → Forward = Spot

heading: Example: Stock Index Forward with Dividends
body:
Data:
•
S&P 500 index level (S₀): 4,500
•
Dividend yield (δ): 2% per year
•
Risk-free rate (r): 4% per year
•
Contract maturity (T): 6 months = 0.5 years

Calculate forward price:
F₀ = S₀ × e^((r−δ)T)
F₀ = 4,500 × e^((0.04−0.02)×0.5)
F₀ = 4,500 × e^0.01
F₀ = 4,500 × 1.01005 = 4,545.23

Forward is 45.23 points above spot (1% over 6 months at net rate of 2%).



Page 14
6. TO BE CONTINUED...

Full coverage will include: Interest rate forwards, FRAs, bond forwards, and all swap

heading: Bid–Offer Definitions and the Dealer Model
body:
Term
Definition & Key Relationships
Bid price
The price (in price currency) at which the DEALER will BUY one unit of the BASE
currency. This is the lower of the two quoted prices. The client 'hits the bid' when
selling the base currency to the dealer.
Offer (Ask) price
The price (in price currency) at which the DEALER will SELL one unit of the BASE
currency. This is the higher of the two quoted prices. The client 'pays the offer'
when buying the base currency from the dealer.
Bid–offer spread
Offer − Bid = dealer's gross compensation. Always positive (Offer > Bid always,
without exception). Expressed in pips.
Pip
Fourth decimal place (0.0001) for most currencies. JPY exception: quoted to 2
decimal places, so pip = 0.01.
Interbank market
The wholesale FX network among professional participants. Standard deal size ≥
1 million units of base currency. Non-bank institutions (hedge funds, asset
managers) can also access. Client spreads are slightly wider than interbank
spreads.
Dealer P&L from
spread
If a client hits the dealer's bid and the dealer offsets in the interbank at the
interbank bid, the dealer earns the difference. E.g.: Client hits dealer bid at

heading: ; dealer sells EUR in interbank at 1.1649 → USD 0.0001 (1 pip) profit per
body: EUR. At EUR 1m, that is USD 100.

heading: Five Factors Affecting the Bid–Offer Spread
body:
Factor
Detail
Direction
1. Currency pair
liquidity
Major pairs (USD/EUR, JPY/USD, USD/GBP): very deep
markets, multiple global bids and offers. Obscure crosses
(MXN/CHF): thin markets, few participants.
More liquid →
tighter spread
2. Time of day
London–New York overlap (~8am–11am New York / 1pm–4pm
London): highest liquidity period. Asian session: less liquid for
most pairs. Between New York close and Asian open (only
Sydney active): very thin.
Overlap hours →
tightest
3. Market volatility
Uncertainty → dealers widen spreads to compensate for risk of
adverse moves before they can offset. Geopolitical events,
market crashes, major data releases (US non-farm payrolls) all
widen spreads.
Higher vol →
wider spread

4. Transaction
size
Larger sizes: harder to offset in interbank → wider spread. Retail
sizes (<1m units): also wider (proportionally). Mid-range (~1–
10m): tightest.
Very large or very
small → wider
5. Client
relationship
Dealer may tighten spread to win multi-product business (FX +
bonds + equities). Repeat business, overall revenue relationship.
Poor credit → slightly wider (though credit risk is low for spot
given T+2 settlement).
Better relationship
→ tighter

Forward contracts add a SIXTH factor: term. Longer maturities → wider bid–offer on forward points because: (1)
less market liquidity in long-dated forwards; (2) greater counterparty credit risk exposure over longer period; (3)
greater interest rate risk sensitivity (forward points depend on rate differentials).

!
Exam question: 'rank these three transactions by bid–offer spread, tightest to widest.' Check
ALL five factors simultaneously. A trade at the NY–London overlap in a major currency pair at a
standard size for a top-tier client will be tightest. The trap: a large USD/EUR trade may still be
wider than a small USD/EUR trade even though USD/EUR is the most liquid pair, because size
matters too.

heading: The Core Rule for Bid/Offer in Cross Rate Construction
body:
Cross-Rate Construction Rules
RULE 1 — Buying the base: When you need to BUY the currency in the denominator (base), use the
OFFER rate of that component pair.
RULE 2 — Selling the base: When you need to SELL the currency in the denominator (base), use the
BID rate of that component pair.
RESULT: Cross-rate BID = product of component BIDs. Cross-rate OFFER = product of component
OFFERs (when no inversion required).
INVERSION RULE: If the shared (third) currency appears in the NUMERATOR of both given quotes,
you must INVERT one of the quotes. When inverting: new BID = 1/original OFFER; new OFFER =
1/original BID (bid and offer swap, then take reciprocals).
VERIFICATION: Implied cross-rate BID must always be less than implied cross-rate OFFER. If your
computed bid > offer, you have made an error.

heading: Case 1 — No Inversion Required
body:
Worked Example: JPY/EUR from JPY/USD and USD/EUR
Given: JPY/USD = 105.39/105.41   and   USD/EUR = 1.1649/1.1651
Set up: JPY/EUR = JPY/USD × USD/EUR  (USD in denominator of first, numerator of second →
cancels correctly)
Cross-Rate BID (sell JPY, buy EUR → buy the base EUR → offer rates on both):
  JPY/EUR BID = 105.39 × 1.1649 = 122.77
Cross-Rate OFFER (buy JPY, sell EUR → sell the base EUR → bid rates on both):
  JPY/EUR OFFER = 105.41 × 1.1651 = 122.81
Result: JPY/EUR = 122.77/122.81. Check: 122.77 < 122.81 ✓
Intuition: To buy EUR you always pay more (higher JPY per EUR) → that is the offer side (122.81).

heading: Case 2 — Inversion Required
body:
Worked Example: GBP/EUR from USD/GBP and USD/EUR
Given: USD/GBP = 1.2302/1.2304   and   USD/EUR = 1.1649/1.1651. Want: GBP/EUR.
Need: GBP/EUR = GBP/USD × USD/EUR. But we have USD/GBP, not GBP/USD → must invert
USD/GBP.
Inverting USD/GBP = 1.2302/1.2304:
  New BID (GBP/USD) = 1 / OFFER(USD/GBP) = 1/1.2304 = 0.81274
  New OFFER (GBP/USD) = 1 / BID(USD/GBP) = 1/1.2302 = 0.81288
  → GBP/USD = 0.81274/0.81288 (extend to 5 dp to avoid truncation errors)
GBP/EUR BID = GBP/USD BID × USD/EUR BID = 0.81274 × 1.1649 = 0.9468
GBP/EUR OFFER = GBP/USD OFFER × USD/EUR OFFER = 0.81288 × 1.1651 = 0.9471
Result: GBP/EUR = 0.9468/0.9471. Check: 0.9468 < 0.9471 ✓

heading: Triangular Arbitrage — Identifying and Executing
body:
Triangular arbitrage is possible when a dealer's posted cross-rate bid or offer violates the implied interbank cross-
rate. Specifically: (a) Dealer's OFFER < Interbank BID → buy from dealer (cheap), sell in interbank (higher). (b)
Dealer's BID > Interbank OFFER → buy from interbank (cheap), sell to dealer (higher).

Worked Example: JPY/CAD Triangular Arbitrage

Interbank: JPY/USD = 105.39/105.41   CAD/USD = 1.3199/1.3201
Step 1 — Compute implied JPY/CAD interbank cross rate:
  JPY/CAD = JPY/USD × USD/CAD. Must invert CAD/USD:
  USD/CAD BID = 1/1.3201 = 0.75752;  USD/CAD OFFER = 1/1.3199 = 0.75763
  Implied BID: 105.39 × 0.75752 = 79.84
  Implied OFFER: 105.41 × 0.75763 = 79.86
  → Interbank implied JPY/CAD = 79.84/79.86
Step 2 — Compare with dealer quote of 79.81/79.83:
  Dealer's OFFER (79.83) < Interbank BID (79.84) → ARBITRAGE EXISTS
Step 3 — Execute: BUY CAD from dealer at 79.83 JPY/CAD (pay dealer's offer).
  SELL CAD in interbank at 79.84 JPY/CAD (hit interbank bid).
  PROFIT = (79.84 − 79.83) × CAD quantity = JPY 0.01 per CAD transacted.
Step 4 — No arbitrage check: dealer quote 79.82/79.87 → bid (79.82) < interbank offer (79.86) AND
offer (79.87) > interbank bid (79.84) → BOTH within range → no arbitrage.

!
The profit is calculated as the DIFFERENCE between the dealer's mispriced side and the
interbank's opposing side. Direction: if dealer's offer < interbank bid → buy at dealer offer, sell at
interbank bid. If dealer's bid > interbank offer → buy at interbank offer, sell at dealer bid. Never
try to trade both legs against the SAME counterparty.

heading: Covered Interest Rate Parity — Derivation
body:
Consider a domestic investor with 1 unit of domestic currency (base currency in the f/d notation). Two equivalent
investment strategies:

Strategy
Steps
Return at Maturity

A —
Domestic
Invest domestically at risk-free rate id for t days
1 × (1 + id × t/360)
B — Hedged
Foreign
(1) Convert to foreign at spot Sf/d; (2) invest at if for t
days; (3) sell foreign proceeds forward at Ff/d to lock
in domestic currency return
Sf/d × (1 + if × t/360) × (1/Ff/d)

Arbitrage requires A = B. Setting equal and solving for Ff/d:

CIP — Forward Rate
Ff/d = Sf/d × [(1 + if × t/360) / (1 + id × t/360)]
Exact formula. t = days. Domestic = base currency. Risk-free rates typically quoted as
Market Reference Rates (MRR). Also: Fp/b = Sp/b × [(1+ip×t/360)/(1+ib×t/360)]

Forward
Premium/Discount
Ff/d − Sf/d = Sf/d × [t/360 / (1 + id×t/360)] × (if − id)
Positive value = domestic currency at forward PREMIUM (foreign currency at
discount). Proportional to spot rate, interest differential, and approximately
proportional to term.

Approximate
Forward %
(Ff/d − Sf/d) / Sf/d ≅ if − id
Useful approximation for short tenors. Exact form has (1+id) denominator but result is
close.

heading: Forward Premium/Discount — Intuition
body:
if vs id
Direction of Ff/d
vs Sf/d
Forward
Points Sign
Economic Intuition
if > id
Ff/d > Sf/d
POSITIVE
Higher foreign yields → invest abroad is
attractive → arbitrageurs sell domestic currency
forward → domestic currency appreciated in
forward market. The premium offsets the foreign
yield advantage.
if < id
Ff/d < Sf/d
NEGATIVE
Lower foreign yields → invest at home is
attractive → arbitrageurs buy domestic currency
forward → domestic currency discounted in
forward market. The discount offsets the
domestic yield advantage.
if = id
Ff/d = Sf/d
ZERO
No yield differential → no arbitrage pressure →
forward equals spot.

!
A forward PREMIUM on the DOMESTIC currency means Ff/d > Sf/d (more foreign currency per
domestic unit in the forward market). This means the FOREIGN currency is at a DISCOUNT.
High foreign interest rates → domestic at a PREMIUM (foreign at discount). Many candidates
mix up which currency is at a premium vs. discount.

heading: Reading Forward Points — Practical Application
body:
In professional FX markets, forwards are quoted as POINTS to be ADDED to the spot rate. Points are scaled to
the same decimal place as the spot quote (i.e., already divided by 10,000 for most currency pairs, or 100 for
JPY/USD). Points are also already scaled to the specific maturity — do NOT annualise them.

Reading a Forward Quote Table (USD/EUR)
Spot USD/EUR = 1.1649/1.1651
1-month points = −5.6/−5.1   |   3-month points = −15.9/−15.3   |   6-month = −37.0/−36.3   |   12-month
= −94.3/−91.8
Negative points → EUR at forward DISCOUNT (costs fewer USD in forward vs. spot) → USD interest
rates lower than EUR → wait, re-read: negative F−S means Ff/d < Sf/d → if < id → USD rates
HIGHER than EUR rates.
3-month all-in BID: 1.1649 + (−15.9/10,000) = 1.1649 − 0.00159 = 1.16331
3-month all-in OFFER: 1.1651 + (−15.3/10,000) = 1.1651 − 0.00153 = 1.16357
SELLING EUR forward 3 months (hit bid): receive USD 1.16331 per EUR
BUYING EUR forward 3 months (pay offer): pay USD 1.16357 per EUR
Note: −5.6 bid < −5.1 offer numerically (−5.6 is more negative → smaller → still Offer > Bid). This trips
up many candidates.

!
With NEGATIVE forward points: −5.6 (bid) < −5.1 (offer) because −5.6 is more negative. The all-
in forward bid (spot bid + point bid) will still be less than the all-in forward offer (spot offer + point
offer). The offer is always the higher number. Never forget to ADD (not subtract) the (already-
negative) points — the negative sign is embedded.

heading: Mark-to-Market Value of a Forward Contract
body:
The mark-to-market (MTM) value of a forward contract reflects the profit or loss that would be realized by closing
out the position at current market prices. At inception, MTM = 0 (no cash changes hands). Over time, MTM
fluctuates as spot rates and interest rates move.

Step
Procedure
1. Identify
offsetting
forward
Create an equal and opposite forward to the SAME settlement date. If original = long base
currency, offset = short base currency in same amount.
2.
Determine
all-in
forward
rate for
offset
Use the bid or offer as appropriate. If selling the base in the offset transaction → use BID. If
buying the base in the offset → use OFFER.
3.
Compute
settlement
CF = (Original forward rate − Offset all-in rate) × Contract size. If original was long and
currency appreciated → inflow (+). If original was long and currency depreciated → outflow (−).

date cash
flow
4. Present
value
PV = CF / [1 + r × (days remaining/360)]. Use MRR of the SETTLEMENT CASH FLOW
CURRENCY. Days = time remaining to the settlement date (not original contract length).

MTM Worked Example 1 — Long GBP Forward (Profitable)
Original position: bought GBP 10m forward at AUD/GBP 1.8100 (6-month contract). Now closing out 3
months before settlement.
Current spot AUD/GBP: 1.8210/1.8215. 3-month forward points: 130/140.
Offset = SELL GBP 10m 3m forward (to settlement date). Selling GBP (base) → use BID side.
Offset all-in 3m forward (BID): 1.8210 + 130/10,000 = 1.8210 + 0.0130 = 1.8340
Settlement CF: (1.8340 − 1.8100) × 10,000,000 = +AUD 240,000 (inflow — GBP appreciated)
3-month AUD MRR = 2.40% (annualised). Remaining time = 90 days.
MTM Value = AUD 240,000 / [1 + 0.024 × (90/360)] = AUD 240,000 / 1.006 = AUD 238,569

MTM Worked Example 2 — Short USD (Losing) Position
Original position: sold USD 10m forward at USD/NZD 0.7900 (long NZD). Now 3 months to
settlement.
Current spot USD/NZD: 0.7825/0.7830. 3-month points: −12.1/−10.0. NZD MRR: 3.31%.
Offset = BUY USD 10m forward 3m (to settlement). Buying USD = selling NZD (base). Selling base →
BID side.
Offset all-in 3m BID: 0.7825 + (−12.1/10,000) = 0.7825 − 0.00121 = 0.78129
At settlement: receive NZD 12,658,228 (10m/0.7900) from original; pay NZD 12,799,345
(10m/0.78129) on offset.
Settlement CF: NZD 12,658,228 − 12,799,345 = −NZD 141,117 (outflow — USD appreciated, short
USD position lost)
MTM Value = −NZD 141,117 / [1 + 0.0331 × (90/360)] = −NZD 141,117 / 1.008275 = −NZD 139,959

!
TWO common MTM errors: (1) Discounting in the WRONG CURRENCY — always use the
MRR of the currency in which the settlement cash flow is denominated. (2) Using the ORIGINAL
contract term rather than REMAINING days to settlement for the discount period. The remaining
term is what matters for PV.

heading: Covered Interest Rate Parity (CIP)
body:
CIP
Ff/d = Sf/d × [(1 + if × t/360) / (1 + id × t/360)]
Only parity condition enforced by ARBITRAGE. Holds in all normal market conditions
with free capital mobility.

CIP says: the return on a fully currency-hedged foreign investment must equal the return on an equivalent
domestic investment. The forward rate is not a forecast — it is the arbitrage-free price that equates the two
investment returns. If CIP is violated, traders borrow in the lower-yielding currency, invest in the higher, and
hedge the FX risk — instantaneous riskless profit arises and is immediately competed away.

WHEN CIP CAN FAIL: During extreme financial stress (e.g., 2008 GFC, March 2020 COVID shock), CIP
violations can persist temporarily because of: (1) counterparty credit risk in the forward market, (2) balance sheet
constraints limiting arbitrage by financial intermediaries, (3) regulatory capital requirements that make the
arbitrage trade costly. These are known as 'CIP deviations' and are an active research area.

heading: Uncovered Interest Rate Parity (UIP)
body:
UIP (Approximate)
% ΔS(f/d)^e = if − id
Expected change in spot rate = interest rate differential. High-yield currency expected
to DEPRECIATE by the yield gap. Uses f/d notation: Sf/d = foreign per domestic.


INTUITION: If Japanese rates are 1% and Australian rates are 3%, Australian dollars are expected (under UIP) to
depreciate by 2% against the yen. Why? Because risk-neutral investors would otherwise shift capital to Australia
for the higher yield, driving up the AUD until expected depreciation exactly offsets the yield advantage.

UIP is NOT enforced by arbitrage — there is no riskless trade that locks in a profit if UIP is violated. It would only
hold if enough risk-neutral speculators enter the market and bet on the expected spot rate path. In reality, most
investors are risk-averse, and currency risk is not diversifiable. This means a risk premium is typically embedded
in exchange rates, causing systematic UIP violations.

UIP Empirical Evidence — The Forward Premium Puzzle
SHORT-TERM (1 month to 1 year): Studies consistently find that HIGH-yield currencies do NOT
depreciate as much as UIP predicts. In many periods, they actually APPRECIATE — the exact
opposite of UIP.
MEDIUM-TERM (1–5 years): UIP violations persist but are less severe.
LONG-TERM (5–10+ years): Some evidence that UIP holds better — exchange rates do gravitate
toward interest-rate-implied paths over very long horizons.
POLICY IMPLICATION: The failure of UIP is the empirical foundation of the FX carry trade. If UIP
held, carry trades would earn zero excess return. Since it fails, systematic profits have been
documented.
The 'forward premium puzzle' (Fama 1984): forward rates not only fail to predict future spot rates, they
sometimes predict in the WRONG direction.

heading: Forward Rate Parity (FRP)
body:
Forward Rate Parity
Ff/d = Se(f/d)
Forward rate = expected future spot rate. If this holds, the forward rate is an unbiased
predictor of the future spot rate.

DERIVATION: CIP gives us: (Ff/d − Sf/d)/Sf/d ≅ if − id. UIP gives us: %ΔSe(f/d) = if − id. Combining: the forward
premium ≅ expected % change in spot. Therefore: Ff/d ≅ Se(f/d). Because CIP always holds, FRP holds IF AND
ONLY IF UIP holds. Since UIP often fails, forward rates are typically POOR predictors of future spot rates in the
short run.

Forecasting
Method
Theoretical Basis
Empirical Evidence
When
Appropriate
Forward rate
Assumes CIP + UIP (risk
neutrality)
Poor short-run predictor.
Forward premium puzzle
means it often predicts in
wrong direction.

risk premiums are
small

(random walk)
Assumes exchange rates
follow a random walk — no
information predicts direction
Surprisingly hard to beat,
especially short term. Meese
& Rogoff (1983) classic result.
Short-run
forecasting (less-
bad option)

inflation differential

when inflation differentials are

heading: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
body:
PPP is grounded in the LAW OF ONE PRICE: identical goods should cost the same across countries when
converted to a common currency. Pf = Sf/d × Pd. Three versions of PPP build on this foundation:

Version
Formula
Key Assumptions
Practical Assessment
Law of One Price
P^x_f = Sf/d × P^x_d
Zero transaction costs;
goods fully tradable;
identical quality.
Holds approximately for
highly tradable
commodities. Fails for non-
tradable goods and
services (haircuts, rent).
Absolute PPP
Sf/d = Pf/Pd
Same consumption bundle
across countries (same
weights, same goods).
Rarely holds. Different
consumption baskets and
non-tradable sectors create
persistent deviations.
Relative PPP
%ΔSf/d ≅ πf − πd
Transaction costs/trade
barriers CONSTANT over
time (even if non-zero).
Holds better over long
horizons. Works well when
inflation differentials are
large.
Ex Ante PPP
%ΔSe(f/d) = πe_f − πe_d
Expectations version of
relative PPP. Driven by
expected, not actual,
inflation.
Theoretical anchor for
long-run FX models.
Empirically validated over
10–25 year periods.

EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE (from Exhibit 2 in reading): At 1-year intervals, no meaningful relationship between
inflation differentials and FX changes (appears random). At 6-year intervals, a positive relationship begins to
emerge. At 12 and 24-year intervals, a clear upward-sloping pattern confirms PPP's long-run validity. Exception:
when inflation differentials are EXTREME (e.g., Brazil 1980s hyperinflation — REAL/USD exchange rate tracked
inflation differential closely even over short horizons).

!
Absolute PPP fails because: (1) not all goods are tradable (the 'Balassa-Samuelson effect' —
rich countries with high productivity in tradable sectors also have high wages and prices for non-
tradables); (2) different consumption bundles across countries; (3) significant transportation
costs and trade barriers. Relative PPP is more empirically supportable because it only requires
these factors to be CONSTANT over time.

heading: The Fisher Effect and International Fisher Effect
body:
Domestic Fisher
Effect
i = r + πe
Nominal interest rate = real interest rate + expected inflation. Separates the two
drivers of nominal rates.

Applying the Fisher effect to both a domestic and foreign country:


Country
Fisher Equation
Domestic
id = rd + πe_d
Foreign
if = rf + πe_f
Difference (Foreign −
Domestic)
if − id = (rf − rd) + (πe_f − πe_d)

If both UIP and ex ante PPP hold simultaneously, then %ΔSe = if − id = πe_f − πe_d, which implies if − id = πe_f
− πe_d. Substituting into the real rate differential formula: (rf − rd) = (if − id) − (πe_f − πe_d) = 0. This is REAL
INTEREST RATE PARITY — real rates equalize across countries.

International Fisher
Effect (IFE)
if − id = πe_f − πe_d
Nominal yield spread = expected inflation differential. Follows if both UIP and PPP
hold AND real rates are equal worldwide.

Real Interest Rate
Parity
rf = rd → 0 = (if − id) − (πe_f − πe_d)
Holds if both UIP and ex ante PPP hold. Violated when currencies carry different risk
premiums (e.g., EM currencies with default/depreciation risk).

WHY IFE BREAKS DOWN: Not all currencies carry the same risk. An emerging market with high sovereign debt
may have a high nominal interest rate that is NOT fully explained by inflation expectations — part of it is a RISK
PREMIUM for the probability of currency depreciation or default. In this case, the 'real' interest rate calculated
naively (i − πe) is artificially high because it conflates the genuine real rate with a currency risk premium.

If ALL Parity Conditions Held Simultaneously...
The expected % change in the spot rate would equal ALL OF: (1) forward premium/discount, (2)
nominal yield spread, (3) expected inflation differential.

Result: NO incentive to shift capital between currencies — all risk-adjusted expected returns would be
equal everywhere.
FX carry trades would be unprofitable (zero excess return). Forward speculation would be pointless
(forward = expected future spot).
The real yield spread between any two countries would be zero.
Practical implication: NONE of this holds short term. The gaps between actual spot rates and parity-
implied levels create the opportunities that FX traders exploit.

body:
