Carry Trade Net
Return
Return = (1 + if) × (S1/S0) − (1 + id)
S1, S0 in f/d terms. Simplified approximation: Return ≅ (if − id) + %ΔS. Positive when
high-yield currency does not depreciate by the full yield spread.

heading: Worked Example — JPY Funding / AUD Investment
body:
Carry Trade Calculation (Tokyo-based manager, borrow JPY, invest AUD)
Today: JPY MRR = 0.10%;  AUD MRR = 1.70%.  JPY/USD spot = 105.40;  USD/AUD spot = 0.6810
→ JPY/AUD spot today = 105.40 × 0.6810 = 71.78
One year later: JPY/USD = 104.60;  USD/AUD = 0.6850
→ JPY/AUD spot in 1 year = 104.60 × 0.6850 = 71.65
AUD DEPRECIATED from 71.78 to 71.65 JPY per AUD (only −0.18% depreciation vs. UIP-predicted
−1.60%)
Gross AUD investment return in JPY: (1/71.78) × (1 + 0.017) × 71.65 = 1.0152 → +1.52%
Less JPY borrowing cost: 0.10%. NET CARRY RETURN = 1.52% − 0.10% = +1.42%
CHECK: Under full UIP, AUD would depreciate by 1.60% → JPY/AUD would fall to ~70.63 → gross
return ≈ −0.34% → net return: −0.34% − 0.10% = −0.44% (loss). UIP failure is what makes carry
profitable.

heading: Risk Profile and Return Distribution
body:
Feature
Detail
Expected return in
calm markets
Steady accrual of the yield differential. Gradual income unrelated to FX volatility.
Historically positive over extended periods.
Crash risk
When volatility spikes, the carry trade collapses suddenly. High-yield currencies
depreciate sharply; low-yield funding currencies (JPY, CHF) appreciate sharply.
Losses can exceed months of carry income in a single day.
Why crashes occur
Carry trades are LEVERAGED. When losses reach stop-loss levels, traders unwind
simultaneously. Selling high-yield and buying low-yield currencies generates
feedback: further price moves trigger more stop-losses → cascade.
Leverage
amplification
Borrow in funding currency, invest in high-yield — this is inherently leveraged. A
small adverse FX move translates to a large equity loss given the leverage.
'Flight to quality'
effect
During crises, investors rush to safe-haven assets (USD, JPY, CHF) — exactly the
funding currencies of carry trades. Safe haven appreciation compounds carry trade
losses.
Distribution shape
NON-NORMAL. More peaked (leptokurtic), FAT TAILS, NEGATIVE SKEW. The
negative skew means losses are larger and more frequent than a normal distribution
would imply. 'Picking up nickels in front of a steamroller.'

!
The carry trade is based on the FAILURE of UIP — if UIP held, carry trades would generate
zero excess return (depreciation of high-yield currency perfectly offsets yield advantage). The
relevant parity condition for carry trade is always UIP, not CIP. CIP is about hedged returns;
carry trades are deliberately UNHEDGED. Do not confuse.

heading: Current Account Influences on Exchange Rates
body:
Current account imbalances affect exchange rates through three distinct channels. Each operates over different
time horizons and with different mechanisms:

Channel
Mechanism
Time
Horizon
Self-Correcting?
Flow
Supply/Demand
Exporters receive foreign currency →
sell it for domestic currency (demand
for domestic). Importers need foreign
currency → sell domestic (supply of
domestic). Surplus → persistent
demand → appreciation. Deficit →
persistent supply → depreciation.
Medium to
long run
YES — appreciation of
surplus country's currency
erodes its export
competitiveness; depreciation
of deficit country's currency
improves its competitiveness.
Gradual rebalancing.
Portfolio Balance
Current account surpluses shift
financial wealth to surplus nations.
Surplus nations accumulate foreign
assets (especially USD for countries
running surpluses with the US). At
some point, holdings may exceed
desired portfolio allocation → they sell
foreign assets → downward pressure
on foreign currency.
Medium
run
PARTIAL — investors may
gradually rebalance portfolio,
exerting steady pressure.
Debt
Sustainability
Persistent deficits → rising external
DEBT as % of GDP. If investors believe
debt is on an unsustainable path, they
pre-emptively revise DOWN their
estimate of the currency's long-run
equilibrium value. This revision
happens NOW, not when the crisis
arrives.
Long run
YES — but correction can be
abrupt (currency crisis) or
gradual.

KEY INSIGHT from empirical evidence: In the SHORT RUN, capital flows DOMINATE current account flows in
determining exchange rates. This is because: (1) asset prices adjust instantly; goods prices are sticky; (2)
portfolio rebalancing shifts vast stocks of wealth, dwarfing current transaction flows; (3) expectational dynamics
mean FX can move based on anticipated future CA developments, not just current ones.

heading: Historical Case Studies — Current Account & Currency
body:
Case Study 1: The US Dollar and the US Current Account
1980–1985: US current account DETERIORATED sharply (growing deficit). Despite this, the USD
SOARED. Why? High US real interest rates from Volcker-era tight monetary policy attracted massive
capital inflows, overwhelming the trade effect. Classic example of capital flows dominating over
current account.
1985–1987: USD reversed sharply downward (Plaza Accord). Concerns about sustainability of ever-
widening US current account deficit finally triggered adjustment.
1995–2000: US current account widened again while USD soared — driven by strong FDI and
portfolio inflows (US equity market boom).
2002–2008: US current account deficit + declining interest rates → capital attraction weakened →
USD began multi-year decline. Three major dollar downward cycles (1977–78, 1985–87, 2002–2008)
each coincided with outsized CA deficits + relatively low real rates.
LESSON: CA deficits do cause long-run depreciation pressure, but can coexist with short/medium-run
appreciation if capital flows are favourable. Lags can be very long.

Case Study 2: Japan and China — Surplus Nation Currencies
JAPAN: Rising current account surplus → persistent upward pressure on yen. Two channels: (1)
demand for JPY to pay for Japanese exports; (2) political pressure from the US (bilateral trade deficit
→ protectionist rhetoric → Japan tolerated yen appreciation). JPY value broadly tracked Japan's CA
surplus trend.
CHINA: For years ran massive CA surplus. Chinese authorities intervened heavily in FX market
(selling RMB/yuan, buying USD) to prevent currency appreciation that would have harmed exporters.
Result: massive accumulation of USD-denominated reserves (reaching >USD3 trillion at peak).
Eventually managed to appreciate RMB gradually.
LESSON: Surplus countries face steady appreciation pressure via both the flow and portfolio
channels. Government intervention can delay but rarely permanently prevent adjustment.

heading: The Mundell–Fleming Model
body:

AGGREGATE DEMAND, which influences interest rates, capital flows, and ultimately the exchange rate. Key: the

economics.
Rational expectations
needed. Empirical
evidence mixed on
magnitude of
overshooting.

DORNBUSCH OVERSHOOTING IN PRACTICE: The model predicts that after an expansionary monetary shock,
the currency will immediately DEPRECIATE MORE than its new long-run equilibrium level, then gradually
APPRECIATE back. This creates the 'overshooting' pattern. Historical examples: Japan's 2013 Abenomics (QE
→ rapid JPY depreciation from ~90/USD to ~120/USD by 2015). ECB QE in 2015 → euro rapid decline. US QE
end (2014) + rate rise anticipation → dollar surge.

heading: Portfolio Balance Approach — Long-Run Fiscal Effects
body:
The portfolio balance model addresses the LONG-RUN consequences of persistent fiscal deficits that the
Mundell–Fleming model ignores. Global investors hold diversified portfolios of domestic and foreign bonds. When
a government runs persistent deficits, the supply of domestic government bonds grows continuously. For
investors to willingly absorb this growing supply, they must be compensated through:

Compensation
Mechanism
How it Works
FX Effect
(1) Higher interest rates /
risk premium
Bond yields rise to attract buyers; risk
premium on domestic assets rises as debt
metrics deteriorate.
Initially: higher rates attract
capital → appreciation. But:
rising risk premium signals

deteriorating credit quality →
eventually depresses currency.
(2) Immediate
depreciation
Currency depreciates to a level where future
APPRECIATION is expected →
compensates investors with capital gain
component.
Depreciation occurs now to
create room for future
appreciation.
(3) Debt monetisation
Central bank eventually pressured to 'print
money' to fund government debt
(monetisation). Massive inflationary
implications.
Severe depreciation, potentially
hyperinflationary.

Integrated Short + Long Run Fiscal Policy Impact (High Capital Mobility)
SHORT RUN (Mundell–Fleming): Expansionary fiscal → higher interest rates → capital inflows →
currency APPRECIATES.
MEDIUM RUN (Portfolio Balance): Accumulated deficits → growing bond supply → rising risk
premium → investors require higher returns → puts upward pressure on yields but downward on
currency.
LONG RUN: Either (a) debt monetisation → severe depreciation, or (b) fiscal consolidation (austerity)
to prevent default → lower spending → lower rates → depreciation.
NET RESULT: Expansionary fiscal policy is a SHORT-TERM BULLISH but LONG-TERM BEARISH
signal for the currency.
German Reunification Example: 1990–1992. Highly expansionary fiscal (to finance East Germany
reconstruction) COMBINED with restrictive Bundesbank monetary policy (to fight unification-driven
inflation) → classic bullish mix for DEM. DEM appreciated strongly, putting severe strain on ERM and
eventually contributing to the 1992 ERM crisis for weaker currencies.

heading: Pull vs. Push Factors for Capital Flows
body:
Type
Definition
Examples
PULL factors
(country-
specific)
Positive domestic developments
that ATTRACT foreign capital.
Driven by improved economic
management or structural
reforms.
Reduced inflation and inflation volatility; more flexible
FX regime; improved fiscal position; privatisation of
state enterprises; liberalisation of financial markets;
lifting FX restrictions. → Attract FDI + portfolio flows.
Healthy export sector builds CA surplus + FX reserves
as buffer.

PUSH factors
(external)
External conditions in capital-
exporting countries that DRIVE
capital outward, regardless of
domestic fundamentals.
Low interest rate policies in industrial countries (post-
2008 QE era) → global investors seek yield → surge to
EM. Rising EM weight in global equity indices (EM
share of world GDP now >40%) → structural allocation
shift to EM by global investors.

heading: Capital Flow Surges and Crisis Episodes
body:
Capital flow surges plant the seeds of crises by contributing to: (1) unwarranted currency appreciation, (2) buildup
in external indebtedness, (3) asset price bubbles, (4) consumption binges and CA deficit growth, (5)
overinvestment in risky projects. Three major 1990s crisis episodes were all preceded by capital flow surges:

Three 1990s Currency Crisis Episodes Triggered by Capital Flow Reversals
1992–93 ERM CRISIS: Investors took leveraged long positions in high-yield European currencies
(funded by low-yield DEM/CHF) betting on yield convergence as EMU approached. When macro
conditions diverged, speculative positions unwound → Italy and UK forced to exit ERM. Classic carry
trade unwind at scale.
1994–95 MEXICAN PESO CRISIS: Investors and banks highly leveraged with extensive derivative
positions in long MXN/short USD. Sudden reversal of capital flows amid political shocks → peso
devalued December 1994 → 'Tequila Effect' contagion to other EM.
1997–98 ASIAN FINANCIAL CRISIS: Asian companies and banks had borrowed massively in short-
term USD and JPY (cheap foreign funding) to invest in local assets. Currency mismatches + maturity
mismatches. When investor confidence broke → capital outflows → currency devaluations → debt
servicing costs exploded → banking crises. Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia worst
affected.

heading: Forms of Capital Controls
body:
Country/Year
Control Mechanism
Brazil (2016)
Tax (IOF) on foreign exchange transactions to limit inflows, reduce currency
appreciation, and raise government revenue.
Thailand (2006)
30% one-year non-interest-bearing deposit requirement on all new foreign
investment inflows. Aim: limit baht appreciation from carry trade inflows.
Malaysia (1998)
Post-Asian crisis: prohibited banks from offering transactions where ringgit was sold
offshore. Required a 12-month holding period for repatriation of equity investments.
Ukraine (2015)
Prevented foreign investors from repatriating proceeds from stock sales.
Consequence: removed from MSCI Frontier Markets Index — a severe self-inflicted
cost.
Venezuela (2008+)
Four-tier official exchange rate system based on purpose of USD purchase.
Resulted in massive black market, economic distortion, hyperinflation. Controls
loosened 2018–2019.
China (ongoing)
Gradual, partial liberalisation. Maintains capital account controls while widening daily
trading bands for RMB. Allows capital flows for trade purposes; restricts 'hot money'
speculative flows.

heading: Effectiveness of FX Intervention
body:
Developed Markets (DM)
Emerging Markets (EM)
Effectiveness
Generally LIMITED. Most studies conclude
little lasting impact on exchange rate
LEVELS. Some reduction in volatility.
MORE EFFECTIVE. Some studies find
that intervention can influence exchange
rate levels. Volatility-dampening effects
more robust.
Why the
difference?
DM central bank FX reserves are TINY
relative to average daily FX turnover in
their currencies. E.g., EUR daily turnover is
USD 500bn+; ECB reserves could not
meaningfully shift supply/demand for more
than a day.
EM central bank reserves are LARGE
relative to their domestic FX market
turnover. E.g., a central bank with USD
200bn in reserves for a currency with USD
5bn daily turnover has real firepower to
move the market.
Key metric
Official reserves / Average daily FX
turnover → negligible for most DM. Market
dwarfs the central bank.
Official reserves / Average daily FX
turnover → can be significant. EM can
credibly threaten sustained intervention.
Direction
asymmetry
Easier to WEAKEN own currency (can
print unlimited domestic currency to sell).
Harder to STRENGTHEN it (limited FX
reserve stockpile; 'running out of bullets').
Same asymmetry, but larger reserve buffer
for strengthening means EM can defend
more credibly.
Side effects
Persistent intervention can undermine
domestic monetary policy (selling domestic
currency to weaken it adds money supply;
must be 'sterilised').
Same side effects apply. Capital flow
deflection to neighbouring countries also a
concern.

!
The exam question on why EM central banks are more effective at FX intervention: the answer
is always that EM FX RESERVES ARE LARGE RELATIVE TO AVERAGE DAILY FX MARKET
TURNOVER IN THEIR CURRENCIES. Not their absolute reserve level — the RATIO to
turnover. For DM, this ratio is negligible; for EM, it is substantial.

heading: The Nine Warning Signs
body:
#
Warning Sign
Direction Before
Crisis
Mechanism
1
Capital market liberalisation
PRESENT (prior
condition)
Free capital flows enable large inflows that can
also reverse instantly. More liberalised → more
vulnerable to sudden stops.
2
Foreign capital inflows
(relative to GDP)
LARGE /
ELEVATED
Short-term foreign-currency borrowing especially
dangerous: creates currency mismatch (assets in
local currency, liabilities in foreign) + maturity
mismatch.
3
Banking sector condition
BANKING CRISIS
coincides or
PRECEDES
Banks borrowing short-term foreign to fund long-
term local assets = double mismatch. Bank stress
→ deposit outflows → need to sell local currency
assets → currency pressure.
4
Exchange rate regime
FIXED or
PARTIALLY
FIXED
Pegged rates invite speculative attacks because
the government must defend at a fixed level,
burning reserves. Floating rates absorb shocks
continuously, reducing the one-way bet.
5
Foreign exchange reserves
DECLINING
PRECIPITOUSLY
Central bank burning reserves to defend the peg.
Once reserves approach exhaustion, market
participants know the peg is indefensible →
accelerate the attack.
6
Exchange rate level
RISEN
SUBSTANTIALLY
vs. historical
mean
Real overvaluation erodes export competitiveness.
The more overvalued, the larger the eventual
correction.
7
Terms of trade
(exports/imports)
DETERIORATING
Fewer export revenues → less FX inflow → harder
to service foreign debt. Worsens the CA deficit.
8
Broad money growth / M2
to bank reserves
RISING
Credit boom → domestic demand + inflation
pressure → real currency overvaluation →
competitiveness loss. Also fuels asset bubbles.
9
Inflation
SIGNIFICANTLY
HIGHER than
tranquil periods
Domestic inflation erodes purchasing power parity
→ real currency overvaluation even if nominal rate
stable → pressure to depreciate.

heading: The Iceland 2008 Crisis — All Nine Signs Present
body:
Iceland 2008 Currency Crisis — Complete Case Study

BACKGROUND: Population 320,000. Banking sector liberalised 2001. Three banks (Glitnir,
Kaupthing, Landsbanki) grew explosively by offering internet-based foreign-currency deposits to UK
and European savers. Guaranteed by government.
SIGNAL 1 (Liberalisation): Banking liberalised 2001. Internet deposits attracted massive foreign
capital.
SIGNAL 2 (Capital inflows): Banks' assets grew 30× between 2000 and 2008. Foreign debt >5× GDP.
External debt >7× GDP and 14× export revenue.
SIGNAL 3 (Banking crisis): Banks had severe maturity mismatch (short-term foreign deposits + long-
term local loans). Banks constituted >70% of national stock market cap.
SIGNAL 4 (Fixed rate): Government tried to fix the krona to the euro in October 2008. Abandoned
ONE DAY later.
SIGNAL 5 (Reserves): Central bank had EUR 2bn in assets vs. EUR 50bn in foreign debt →
technically insolvent. Reserves were wholly inadequate to defend the currency.
SIGNAL 6 (Overvaluation): Krona had risen 40% vs. USD from 2001 to 2007. Chronically overvalued.
SIGNAL 7 (Terms of trade): Trade surplus in 2002 turned to deficit 2003–2007 as consumption boom
drove imports.
SIGNAL 8 (Money growth): Broad monetary aggregates grew 14–35% annually 2002–2007.
SIGNAL 9 (Inflation): Reached 14% by autumn 2008.
OUTCOME: Krona fell >60% in trading. Banks collapsed (Oct 2008). Stock market fell 77%. GDP
contracted 9.2% in 2009. Unemployment rose from <1% to 9%. Required IMF bailout of USD 4.6bn.
Per capita GDP fell from USD 70,000 to a fraction of that.

heading: Ideal Characteristics of an Early Warning System
body:
GOOD Feature
POOR Feature
Strong track record of predicting actual crises (high
true positive rate)
Generates many false alarms (low precision →
alarm fatigue)
Indicators available with short data lags (timely)
Data available only with long lags (crisis may be
under way before EWS signals)
Flashes warning well IN ADVANCE of crisis (early)
Signals only after crisis is already in progress (too
late to reposition)
Broad-based: incorporates multiple economic and
financial indicators
Relies on a single indicator (most crises are multi-
causal)
Can be updated in near-real time
Requires annual or quarterly data that may not be
revised promptly

!
The three most commonly misremembered crisis warning sign DIRECTIONS: (1) FX
RESERVES DECLINE (not rise) — central bank is burning them defending the peg; (2)
exchange rate is ABOVE (overvalued vs.) historical mean — not below; (3) broad money
GROWTH and inflation are ELEVATED — not declining. All three require knowing the direction,

not just the variable. Prediction 1 in Example 11 ('reserves will increase') is specifically tested —
it is WRONG.
1-year EUR/USD projected: CAD/USD = 1.3151 and EUR/CAD = 0.6567 → EUR/USD = 1.3151 ×

Gross return in USD from EUR deposit: 0.8588 × (1 + 0.022) × (1/0.8636) − 1 = 1.01632 − 1 = 1.632%
Less USD borrowing cost: 0.80%. Net carry return = 1.632% − 0.80% = 0.83% (≈ Answer A: 0.83%)

Q4: Narrowest Bid–Offer Spread for Industrial Company's JPY/EUR Trade (Answer: B —
credit rating)
The trade: JPY/EUR at 8:05am New York (= London ~1pm, well within NY–London overlap). Large
AA-rated firm. JPY and EUR are both major currencies.
Factor A (timing): 8:05am NY ≈ 1pm London = WITHIN NY-London overlap → FAVOURABLE for tight
spread.
Factor B (credit rating): Spot FX settles T+2 → credit risk is minimal; AA vs A rating barely matters for
spot settlement. CREDIT RATING IS THE LEAST IMPORTANT FACTOR here.
Factor C (currency pair): JPY/EUR is a major cross rate. While not as liquid as USD/EUR or
JPY/USD, it is well-traded. FAVOURABLE.
Answer: B — credit rating is the factor LEAST LIKELY to lead to a narrow spread for a spot FX
transaction.

Q5: If Both CIP and UIP Hold, Future Spot Rates Best Forecast By? (Answer: B — forward
exchange rates)
If CIP holds: Ff/d = Sf/d × [(1+if)/(1+id)]  (forward rate set by arbitrage).
If UIP holds: %ΔSe = if − id  (expected spot change = yield spread).
Combining: Ff/d = Se(f/d)  → forward rate = expected future spot → FORWARD RATE is the unbiased
predictor.
If only random walk: current spot = best predictor (UIP not assumed).
If only PPP: inflation differential = best predictor.
Both CIP AND UIP → forward rate is the predictor.

Problems 6–13 (Anna Goldsworthy / Scott Underwood)

Q6: JPY 225m → GBP Conversion (Answer: A — GBP 1,734,906)
Goldsworthy is BUYING GBP (the base in JPY/GBP). Buying base = PAY THE OFFER.
JPY/GBP OFFER = 129.69 (from Exhibit 1).
GBP received = JPY 225,000,000 / 129.69 = GBP 1,734,906.

Q7: Why Did Dealer Revise USD/GBP to 1.2299/1.2307 (Wider)? (Answer: A — larger
transaction)

Original quote: 1.2301/1.2305 (4 pip spread). Revised: 1.2299/1.2307 (8 pip spread — wider on both
sides).
Wider spread after Goldsworthy provided transaction details → she asked for a LARGER AMOUNT
than the standard 1 million units.
Larger transaction harder for dealer to offset in interbank → demands wider spread.
B (drop in volatility) is wrong — lower vol → tighter spreads. C (NY-London overlap) is wrong — that
would also tighten the spread.

Q9: Mark-to-Market Gain on Forward Position (Answer: A — GBP 19,971)
Original: sold EUR 5m 9-month forward at GBP/EUR 0.9526 (i.e., received GBP per EUR at 0.9526).
Now 3 months to settlement.
Offset: BUY EUR 5m 3-month forward (to same settlement date). Buying EUR (base in GBP/EUR) →
OFFER side.
3-month forward GBP/EUR OFFER: spot offer 0.9471 + points offer (15.0/10,000) = 0.9471 + 0.0015
= 0.9486
Settlement CF: (0.9526 − 0.9486) × 5,000,000 = GBP 20,000 (inflow — sold EUR at higher GBP rate
than current)
PV at 3-month GBP MRR (0.58%): GBP 20,000 / [1 + 0.0058 × (90/360)] = GBP 20,000 / 1.00145 =
GBP 19,971

Q10: 3-Month EUR MRR vs. GBP MRR Based on Forward Points? (Answer: A — EUR MRR
below GBP MRR)
GBP/EUR forward points are POSITIVE (14.0/15.0 for 3 months). Positive points mean Ff/d > Sf/d.
GBP/EUR: if EUR is the 'foreign' currency (numerator = price = GBP), then positive points mean GBP
premium → GBP rate > EUR rate.
From CIP: Ff/d > Sf/d iff if > id. Here f = GBP (price), d = EUR (base). So GBP MRR > EUR MRR.
Therefore: 3-month EUR MRR is BELOW 3-month GBP MRR.

section_title: GROWTH IN THE GLOBAL ECONOMY — CONTEXT
heading: Economic Growth and Equity Returns — The Grinold-Kroner Decomposition
body:
Grinold-Kroner
Equity Return
E(Re) = dy + Δ(P/E) + i + g − ΔS
dy = dividend yield; Δ(P/E) = P/E repricing; i = inflation; g = real GDP growth; ΔS =
dilution from share issuance

The dilution term (ΔS) captures why economic growth does not automatically translate to equal equity returns. It
can be expanded as: ΔS = nbb + rd, where nbb = net buybacks (positive = buybacks → EPS accretive) and rd =
relative dynamism (growth from private/unlisted firms that doesn't accrue to listed equity investors).

Component
Long-Run Behaviour
Investment Implication
Dividend yield
(dy)
Fairly stable over time; a
consistent contributor to total
return.
2–3% for most developed markets historically.
P/E repricing
Δ(P/E)
CANNOT grow or shrink at a finite
rate indefinitely. Must average ~0
over very long horizons. Evidence:
Not a reliable long-run driver. Short-term
volatility only. Do not extrapolate P/E expansion.

with high GDP growth, P/E ratios
trend somewhat higher, but this is
the secondary effect.
Inflation (i)
Varies. High inflation → nominal
earnings grow faster but real
growth unchanged.

section_title: GROWTH IN THE GLOBAL ECONOMY — CONTEXT
heading: –3% in well-managed economies.
body:
Real economic
growth (g)
Bounded by potential GDP growth
rate in the LONG RUN. Corporate
profit share of GDP CANNOT rise
indefinitely (workers would stop
working; demand would be
undermined).
Primary long-run driver. Earnings growth upper
limit = potential GDP growth.
Dilution (−ΔS)
Varies significantly by country. Can
be large positive (net buybacks in
US) or large negative (privatisation
waves in EM like China).
IMPORTANT: High GDP growth ≠ high equity
returns. China had 8.2% real GDP growth but
only 0.7% real equity return 1997–2017 due to
massive state-enterprise privatisation dilution.

!
High economic growth does NOT guarantee high equity returns. The China example (8.2% real
GDP growth, 0.7% real equity return) is the canonical exam trap. The dilution effect from
privatisation of SOEs flooded the market with new shares, severely diluting existing
shareholders. Always check the ΔS component when evaluating equity return prospects in high-
growth EM countries.

section_title: GROWTH IN THE GLOBAL ECONOMY — CONTEXT
heading: Potential GDP and Fixed Income Investors
body:
Channel
How Potential GDP Matters to Fixed Income
Inflation pressure signal
Actual GDP > Potential GDP → output gap positive → inflationary pressure →
central bank likely to tighten → bond prices fall. Actual GDP < Potential GDP →
slack → disinflationary → easing likely → bond prices rise. This is the most
direct fixed-income use of potential GDP estimates.
Real interest rate level
Faster potential GDP growth → higher required real interest rates to induce
savings and fund capital accumulation → higher equilibrium real yield on bonds.
Sovereign credit quality
Credit rating agencies use potential GDP growth in sovereign credit
assessment. Higher potential growth → better ability to service debt → lower
credit risk premium. Slower potential growth → higher perceived sovereign
default risk → wider spreads.
Output gap & fiscal
position
Actual fiscal positions are judged relative to 'structural' or 'cyclically adjusted'
deficits — what the deficit would be if the economy were at potential. This
requires knowing potential GDP.

heading: The Seven Preconditions for Growth
body:
Factor
How It Affects Growth
Developed vs.
Developing
1. Saving & Investment
Countries accumulate physical capital through
investment. Low savings → low investment → low
growth. Low per capita income creates a vicious
cycle: low income → low savings → low investment
→ slow growth → low income. FDI can break this
cycle by providing external funding.
DM: generally adequate
savings/investment. DM:
20–35%
investment/GDP. EM:
ranges from low (Brazil
~15%) to high (China
~44%).
2. Developed Financial
Markets
Financial intermediaries channel savings to highest
risk-adjusted-return projects (screening &
monitoring). They aggregate small savings into
large investable pools. They create instruments to
facilitate risk-sharing and diversification. Evidence
(Levine 2005): better-functioning financial markets
→ faster growth. NOTE: Financial sector
expansion that increases leverage without
productive allocation does NOT promote long-run
growth.
DM: well-developed
capital markets, banking
systems, bond markets.
EM: financial markets
often thin, illiquid, subject
to political interference.
3. Political Stability, Rule
of Law
Property rights create incentives to invest and
save. Substantive law (rights/responsibilities) +
procedural law (enforcement) form the legal
bedrock. Political instability (wars, coups,
corruption) raises investment risk, deters FDI,
weakens growth.
DM: well-established
legal protections. EM:
often weak or corrupt
legal systems, especially
in sub-Saharan Africa
where legal reform is
often the first priority.
4. Education & Health
Systems
Education raises skill levels (human capital),
making workers more productive and adaptable.
Physical capital and human capital are
COMPLEMENTARY — education also increases
productivity of existing physical capital. Health
investment is especially critical in developing
countries (e.g., AIDS epidemic in Botswana). DM
should emphasise post-secondary (innovation); EM
should emphasise primary/secondary (adoption of
technology). Brain drain from EM is a constraint.
DM: post-secondary
spending → innovation
→ growth. EM:
primary/secondary
spending → technology
absorption → growth.
Key: optimal education
mix differs by
development stage.
5. Tax & Regulatory
Policies
Limited regulation → encourages entrepreneurship
and firm entry. Entry of new firms is strongly
correlated with average productivity levels (OECD:
low administrative startup cost = key driver).
Regulatory burden discourages risk-taking and
capital allocation to productive uses.
DM: generally supportive
regulatory environments,
though some have
become burdensome.
EM: often high startup
costs, excessive
bureaucracy.
6. Free Trade & Capital
Flows
Open economy: world savings finance domestic
investment (breaks low-savings trap). FDI provides
capital AND technology transfer. Free trade:
specialisation per comparative advantage → higher
productivity. Competition disciplines domestic
DM: generally open. EM:
historically more
protectionist; outward-
oriented EM grew much
faster (East Asia) than

firms. Access to larger markets enables economies
of scale.
inward-oriented EM
(Latin America/Africa in
1950s–80s).

heading: Summary — Seven Factors Limiting Growth in Developing Economies
body:
Seven Factors Limiting Growth in Developing Countries
1. Low rates of saving and investment (vicious cycle: low income → low savings → low investment →
slow growth)
2. Poorly developed financial markets (cannot efficiently channel savings to productive investment)
3. Weak or corrupt legal systems / failure to enforce laws
4. Lack of property rights and political instability (deters domestic and foreign investment)
5. Poor public education and health services (low human capital; 'brain drain')
6. Tax and regulatory policies discouraging entrepreneurship and firm formation
7. Restrictions on international trade and capital flows (forfeits gains from specialisation, FDI, and
technology transfer)

heading: The Aggregate Production Function
body:
Two-Factor
Production Function
Y = AF(K, L)
Y = aggregate output; K = capital services; L = labour; A = Total Factor Productivity
(TFP)

Cobb-Douglas
Production Function
Y = AK^α × L^(1-α)
α = capital's share of GDP (≈ 0.3 for US); (1-α) = labour's share (≈ 0.7 for US). α is
between 0 and 1.

KEY PROPERTIES OF COBB-DOUGLAS: (1) CONSTANT RETURNS TO SCALE — double all inputs → double
output. (2) DIMINISHING MARGINAL PRODUCTIVITY — adding more of ONE input (holding others fixed) yields
decreasing incremental output. The significance of diminishing returns depends on α: low α → strong diminishing
returns; high α → weak diminishing returns.

Per-Worker (Per
Capita) Form
y = Ak^α

y = Y/L = output per worker (labour productivity); k = K/L = capital-to-labour ratio.
Derived by assuming constant returns to scale.

INTERPRETATION: Output per worker depends on: (1) technology/TFP level A, (2) capital available per worker k,
and (3) capital's income share α. Labour productivity (y = Y/L) and TFP (A) are DIFFERENT measures — TFP is
a scale factor for the whole production function; labour productivity is output divided by labour input only.

heading: Capital Deepening vs. Technological Progress
body:
Source of Growth
Definition
Graphical
Representation
Long-Run
Sustainability
Capital Deepening
Increase in the capital-to-
labour ratio (K/L). More capital
per worker via higher net
investment.
MOVEMENT ALONG the
production function curve
(e.g., point A → point B in
per-capita diagram). Curve
shape unchanged.
LIMITED:
Diminishing
marginal returns to
capital mean each
additional unit of
capital adds less
output per worker.
Eventually the
marginal product of
capital falls to its
marginal cost and
further deepening
stops.
TFP / Technological
Progress
Increase in Total Factor
Productivity A. Economy
produces more output from
the SAME inputs.
UPWARD SHIFT of the
entire production function
curve (e.g., point B → point
C). ALL capital-labour
ratios now produce more
output.
UNLIMITED: No
diminishing returns
to TFP itself.
Continued TFP
growth enables
sustained per capita
income growth even
in the steady state.

!
Capital deepening is a MOVEMENT ALONG the production function. TFP improvement is a
SHIFT OF the production function. These are tested visually. Exhibit 3 in the reading shows:
A→B = capital deepening (same curve); B→C = TFP shift (higher curve). Moving from B to D
(further along the same curve) has diminishing impact — capital deepening alone cannot sustain
long-run per capita growth.

heading: Growth Accounting — Solow Growth Equation
body:
Solow Growth
Accounting
ΔY/Y = ΔA/A + α(ΔK/K) + (1−α)(ΔL/L)
ΔA/A = growth in TFP (measured as the residual). α ≈ 0.3 (US capital share). Labour
elasticity = (1−α) ≈ 0.7.

USES OF THE GROWTH ACCOUNTING EQUATION: (1) Estimate contribution of TFP to growth (Solow
residual). (2) Decompose growth into contributions of labour quantity, labour quality, non-ICT capital, ICT capital,
and TFP (as in Conference Board Exhibit 11). (3) Estimate potential GDP using trend estimates of factor inputs.


Growth Accounting Calculation — Spain 2006–2015 (from Exhibit 19 in reading)
Capital growth (ΔK/K): (2,898/2,036)^(1/9) − 1 = 4.0% per year
Labour growth (ΔL/L): (31,527/35,358)^(1/9) − 1 = −1.27% per year (hours worked fell)
TFP growth (ΔA/A): geometric average of annual TFP growth rates = −0.85% per year
Capital share (α): average labour cost as % of total factor cost = 58.7% → α = 1 − 0.587 = 41.3%
Potential GDP growth = (0.413)(4.0%) + (0.587)(−1.27%) + (−0.85%)
= 1.65% − 0.75% − 0.85% = 0.06% per year → approximately zero
KEY: All potential GDP growth came from capital deepening; TFP and labour SUBTRACTED from
growth

heading: Labour Productivity Method
body:
Labour Productivity
Potential GDP
Potential GDP growth = Labour force growth + Labour productivity
growth
Simpler than growth accounting. Avoids need to estimate capital stock and TFP
separately. Labour productivity = GDP per hour worked.

ADVANTAGE: Simple, avoids capital stock measurement. DISADVANTAGE: Cannot separately identify capital
deepening vs. TFP as drivers of productivity growth — both are bundled together in the productivity term. Makes it
harder to assess sustainability (capital deepening is not sustainable long-run; TFP is).

Labour Productivity Method — Japan 2005–2018
From Exhibit 10: Hours worked growth (labour force growth) ≈ 0.0% per year
Labour productivity growth (2005–2018): 1.0% per year
Potential GDP growth = 0.0% + 1.0% = ~1.0% per year
This is consistent with Japan's actual average GDP growth of ~1% since 1990
WHY Japan is slow: zero labor force growth (population flat); sluggish TFP (0.4% then −0.1%); capital
deepening dominant but yields diminishing returns at Japan's high K/L ratio

Capital Deepening as % of Productivity Growth — Key Diagnostic
Capital deepening contribution = Labour productivity growth − TFP growth
South Korea 2005–2018: Labour productivity 3.3%; TFP 0.8% → Capital deepening = 2.5% (76% of
productivity growth)
United States 2005–2018: Labour productivity 1.2%; TFP 0.0% → Capital deepening = 1.2% (100% of
productivity growth — unsustainable signal)

China 2005–2018: Labour productivity 9.2%; TFP 4.3% → Capital deepening = 5.0% (54%); TFP
component large and growing
Brazil 2005–2018: Labour productivity 1.3%; TFP −0.7% → Capital deepening = 1.9% (negative TFP
— productivity growth despite TFP drag)
DIAGNOSTIC: The larger capital deepening's share of productivity growth, the less sustainable it is.
TFP-driven growth is more durable.

heading: Natural Resources (LOS 6)
body:
Natural resources are an input to production but NOT a prerequisite for high growth. The relationship between
resource endowment and growth is weak or even negative in some cases.

Concept
Detail
Resource types
RENEWABLE: forests, fisheries, solar — can be replenished. NON-RENEWABLE:
oil, coal, minerals — finite stock depleted once consumed.
Resources ≠ growth
Countries without significant resources (South Korea, Singapore, Japan) have
achieved high growth. Countries with abundant oil (Venezuela, Saudi Arabia,
Nigeria) have had disappointing per capita growth. Zero correlation between oil
reserves and subsequent GDP growth in the empirical data.
Why resources are
NOT necessary
Access through TRADE is what matters, not ownership. Countries lacking oil import
energy; countries lacking land import food. Resource-poor countries can grow by
emphasising less resource-intensive sectors (especially services) and adopting
efficient production methods.
Resource curse
Countries rich in resources may actually suffer SLOWER growth for two reasons: (1)
Failure to develop broader economic institutions — oil revenues reduce pressure to
reform governance, legal systems, financial markets. (2) Dutch Disease: resource
exports → strong FX demand → domestic currency appreciates → manufacturing
sector becomes globally uncompetitive → country misses out on manufacturing-
sector TFP progress.
Dutch Disease
mechanism
Netherlands: natural gas discovery → guilder appreciated → Dutch manufacturing
contracted. Venezuela, Nigeria: oil exports → currency overvalued → manufacturing
deindustrialised. Country loses manufacturing TFP gains that resource-poor
countries exploit.
Resource depletion
argument
Long-run concern that non-renewable resources will limit growth is probably
OVERSTATED: (1) Technological progress reduces resource input per unit of
output. (2) Price signals as resources become scarce encourage substitution to
more plentiful alternatives. (3) Share of national income going to land and resources
has been declining globally.

heading: Labour Supply — Demographics, Immigration, Participation (LOS 7)
body:
The total labour input = Labour force × Average hours worked per worker. The labour force = Working age
population (ages 16–64) that is employed OR unemployed but actively seeking work. Four key determinants of
labour input growth:

Labour Factor
Driver
Long-Run Implication
Population growth
Determined by fertility
rates and mortality rates.
DM (Europe, Japan):
declining birthrates →
near-zero or negative
population growth. EM
(India, Mexico, Africa):
higher fertility → growing
working-age population.
Population growth raises TOTAL GDP growth rate but
NOT necessarily per capita GDP growth rate (more
workers produce more, but more mouths to feed). Only
labour PRODUCTIVITY increases per capita income.
Labor force
participation
% of working-age
population that is
employed or actively
seeking work.
Participation has risen in
most countries due to
rising female
participation. Low female
participation in southern
Europe (Greece, Italy)
and Turkey represents
upside potential.
A rise in participation rate DOES raise per capita GDP
(more workers per capita). But this is typically a LEVEL
shift, not a permanent rate change — participation has
limits and cannot rise indefinitely.
Net migration
Immigration can
significantly boost labour
force growth in countries
with aging native
populations. Ireland and
Spain 2000–2010:
immigration raised
population growth to

heading: % and 1.35%
body:
respectively — well
above European peers.
Without immigration,
Spain's underlying
population grew at only

heading: %/year.
body:
Immigration is a powerful demographic lever for DM
countries facing labour shortages. BUT: immigration
rates can change rapidly and reverse (Spain post-2009
financial crisis saw emigration exceed immigration).
Average hours
worked
Trending DOWNWARD
in advanced economies
over decades: legislation,
collective bargaining,
growth of part-time work,
wealth effect (higher
income → more leisure
valued), high marginal
tax rates on labour.
South Korea 1,993
hours/year vs. Germany
1,363 hours/year in 2018
— a 46% gap.
Declining average hours worked is a DRAG on labour
input growth in DM. Partially offset by rising female
participation rates (more workers, but more part-time).


DEMOGRAPHIC DIVIDEND vs. DEMOGRAPHIC BURDEN: Countries with large youth cohort (high % population
under 15) will see a rising labour force over the next decade. Countries with high % over 65 face growing
pension/healthcare costs and shrinking workforce. Mexico (26.5% under 15, 7.2% over 65) → demographic
tailwind. Germany (13.5% under 15, 21.5% over 65) → significant headwind. China has surprisingly adverse
demographics (similar to DM in its ageing profile) despite still being an EM.

heading: Physical Capital — ICT, Non-ICT, and Public Infrastructure (LOS 8)
body:
The physical capital stock grows when net investment (gross investment minus depreciation) is positive. Net
investment = I − δK. Countries investing a higher share of GDP in capital tend to grow faster: China (44% of
GDP), South Korea (30%), Singapore (27%) are among the fastest growers.

Capital Category
Role in Growth
Key Points
Non-ICT Capital
Traditional physical capital:
machinery, transport equipment, non-
residential buildings, manufacturing
plant. High levels → capital
deepening, which eventually hits
diminishing returns.
Positive but eventually limited
contribution to growth. High investment
share in GDP helps EM countries with
low K/L ratios. Less impact in DM where
K/L is already high. China's dominance
here: 44% investment/GDP, mostly non-
ICT, has powered growth but at
diminishing returns as K/L rises.
ICT Capital
(Information,
Computer, Telecom)
Computers, software,
telecommunications equipment,
semiconductors. Has demonstrated
NETWORK EXTERNALITIES — the
more nodes in the network, the
higher the productivity gain for all
participants (internet, email).
Steep decline in price of high-tech capital
goods (semiconductors) encourages
substitution toward ICT investment.
Network externalities mean ICT
investment may PERMANENTLY raise
the growth rate (as in endogenous growth
model). ICT/GDP share: typically 3–5% in
DM. Externality effects captured largely in
TFP.
Public Infrastructure
(Public Capital)
Roads, bridges, water systems,
electricity grids, ports. Government-
provided, largely non-excludable and
complementary to private production.
Aschauer (1990): infrastructure
investment is an important source of
productivity growth.
Infrastructure COMPLEMENTS private
investment — boosts returns to private
capital. Better roads → lower transport
costs → higher private sector
productivity. Evidence: improving
infrastructure in poor countries has large
growth dividends.

heading: Human Capital and Technology (LOS 8)
body:
Input
Definition & Role
Developed vs. Developing
Economy Strategy
Human Capital
Knowledge and skills workers acquire from
education, training, and life experience.
Better-educated workers are more
productive, more adaptable, and more
capable of innovating. Education has
SPILLOVER EFFECTS: educating one
person raises output of surrounding workers
too (externality). Education spending has
documented high returns.
DM: emphasise POST-
SECONDARY education → drive
innovation and frontier technological
progress. EM: emphasise
PRIMARY/SECONDARY education
→ improve capacity to ABSORB and
implement technology developed
elsewhere. Both: on-the-job training,
health investment.

Technology / TFP
State of technology = cumulative effects of
scientific advances, R&D, management
innovation, and production organisation
improvements. Measured as RESIDUAL
(the portion of output growth not explained
by capital or labour inputs). Main driver of
long-run per capita income growth,
especially in DM.
DM: must spend on R&D to push the
technological frontier; United States
and Japan spend ~2.7–3.1% of GDP
on R&D. EM: can grow by
IMITATING technology developed in
DM (imitation is cheaper than
innovation); R&D spending is lower
(India 0.8%, Mexico 0.5% of GDP).
R&D and Innovation
Direct investment in new knowledge.
Schumpeter's 'creative destruction':
innovation creates new industries/products
while rendering existing ones obsolete,
causing temporary disruption but permanent
long-run productivity gains. Patents signal
innovative output but imperfectly measure
actual innovation.
R&D spending is not directly
productive — it generates IDEAS.
Ideas have externalities (can be
copied). This is the foundation of
endogenous growth theory's
rationale for government support.

heading: Classical Growth Theory (Malthus, 1798)
body:
The original and most pessimistic theory. Also called Malthusian theory. Production function: labour + fixed land.
KEY ASSUMPTION: population accelerates when per capita income rises above the subsistence level.

Feature
Classical Model
Core mechanism
Higher income → population growth accelerates → more workers → diminishing
returns on fixed land → per capita income falls back to subsistence level.
Long-run outcome
Living standards remain at SUBSISTENCE LEVEL permanently. Technology
progress → larger population, NOT richer population.
Why it failed
(1) The income-population link broke down — as incomes rose in developed
countries, population growth actually SLOWED (demographic transition). (2)
Technological progress was rapid enough to more than offset diminishing
marginal returns. (3) Capital accumulation was not considered.
Legacy
Sometimes called the 'dismal science.' Concerns about resource limits (similar to
Malthusian thinking) resurface periodically but have consistently proven
overstated due to technological adaptation.

heading: Neoclassical Growth Model (Solow, 1957)
body:
The mainstream model. Based on the Cobb-Douglas production function with diminishing marginal returns to
capital. Key objective: find the steady-state growth rate of output per capita.


Neoclassical
Steady-State
Growth (per capita)
Δy/y = Δk/k = θ/(1−α)
θ = growth rate of TFP; α = capital share. The ONLY driver of long-run per capita
growth is TFP.

Total Output Growth
Rate (steady state)
ΔY/Y = θ/(1−α) + n
n = labour force growth rate. Long-run output growth = TFP-scaled growth + labour
force growth.

Steady-State
Output/Capital Ratio
Y/K = (1/s)[θ/(1−α) + δ + n] ≡ Ψ
s = saving rate; δ = depreciation rate. Ψ is the equilibrium output-to-capital ratio — a
constant in steady state.

STEADY-STATE EQUILIBRIUM: The economy converges to a path where capital per worker (k) and output per
worker (y) grow at the same rate θ/(1−α). At this point, GROSS SAVINGS exactly finances: (1) capital for new
workers entering at rate n, (2) replacement of depreciating capital at rate δ, and (3) deepening the capital stock at
rate θ/(1−α) needed to maintain the marginal product of capital equal to its rental cost.

Effect of Key Parameters on the Steady State

Parameter Change
Effect on Steady-State
k* and y*
Effect on Steady-State
GROWTH RATE
Graphical Effect
↑ Saving rate (s)
Both k* and y* increase
(move to a higher steady-
state level).
NO change in growth
rate — higher savings
raises level, not
permanent growth.
Growth temporarily
above steady state
during transition.
sf(k) curve shifts up;
new intersection with
required investment
line at higher k* and
y*. (Exhibit 13)
↑ Labour force growth (n)
Both k* and y* decrease
— more investment
needed to equip new
workers; less available
for deepening.
Growth rate of TOTAL
output rises (more
workers). But per capita
growth rate
UNCHANGED.
Required investment
line [δ+n+θ/(1−α)]k
rotates upward;
intersection shifts to
lower k* (Exhibit 14).
↑ Depreciation rate (δ)
Both k* and y* decrease
— more gross
investment wasted
replacing worn-out
capital.
NO change in per capita
growth rate.
Same graphical effect
as ↑n — steeper
required investment
line.
↑ TFP growth (θ)
k* and y* at any given
point in time DECREASE
— higher TFP growth
requires more investment
to keep MPK = rental
cost. But TRAJECTORY
of y* is higher.
Per capita growth rate
INCREASES
permanently: θ↑ →
θ/(1−α)↑.
Required investment
line steepens (like ↑n);
intersection at lower
k*, but y* grows faster
along a steeper path.

!
An increase in TFP GROWTH RATE raises the steady-state growth rate but LOWERS the
steady-state capital-to-labour ratio at any given point in time (compared to lower TFP growth

path). This is counterintuitive. The economy is on a STEEPER TRAJECTORY off a lower base.
Higher TFP means capital must work harder (faster depreciation of its relative advantage) — not
that more capital accumulates.

Transitional Dynamics — Growth Above and Below Steady State

Transitional Growth
Equation
Δy/y = θ/(1−α) + αs(Y/K − Ψ)
When Y/K > Ψ (capital scarce): actual investment > required → above-steady-state
growth. When Y/K < Ψ: below-steady-state growth. Economy converges to Ψ over
time.

INTUITION: Developing countries with LOW capital-to-labour ratios have HIGH output-to-capital ratios (above Ψ)
→ very high marginal product of capital → strong incentive to invest → rapid capital deepening → above-trend
growth. As k rises, Y/K falls toward Ψ, and growth slows to the steady-state rate. This is the mechanism behind
convergence.

Four Major Conclusions of the Neoclassical Model

Neoclassical Model — Four Key Results
1. CAPITAL ACCUMULATION: Capital affects the LEVEL of output but NOT the long-run GROWTH
RATE. Every growing economy converges to its steady-state growth path regardless of initial capital-
to-labour ratio.
2. CAPITAL DEEPENING vs. TFP: Sustainable long-run per capita growth requires TFP growth.
Capital deepening alone produces only TEMPORARY acceleration above steady state, then slows as
diminishing returns set in. More-rapid capital accumulation cannot permanently raise growth.
3. CONVERGENCE: Developing countries (low K/L → high MPK → high returns to investment) should
grow faster than developed countries. This implies convergence of per capita incomes over time.
4. SAVINGS: Higher savings TEMPORARILY raises growth during transition, moving the economy to
a HIGHER LEVEL of per capita output. But once the new steady state is reached, the growth rate
returns to θ/(1−α) + n — independent of the saving rate.

Neoclassical Saving Rate Increase — Worked Example (from Example 11)
Initial: s=20%, θ=0.02, α=0.35, δ=0.10, n=0.01
Steady-state growth rate (before AND after saving rate change): θ/(1−α) = 0.02/0.65 = 3.08% ←
UNCHANGED
Old steady-state Y/K = (1/0.20)(0.0308 + 0.10 + 0.01) = 0.7040
New steady-state Y/K (s=30%) = (1/0.30)(0.0308 + 0.10 + 0.01) = 0.4693
Immediately after s increases: Y/K still 0.7040 (unchanged immediately); Ψ drops to 0.4693
Transitional growth = 3.08% + (0.35)(0.30)(0.7040 − 0.4693) = 5.54% (immediate jump)

After 10 years: growth has slowed to 3.84%; after 25 years: 3.25%; after 100 years: 3.08% (back to
steady state)
Long-run impact: k* rises 86.68%; y* rises 24.42% — permanently higher LEVEL, same growth RATE

heading: Endogenous Growth Model (Romer, 1986)
body:
The endogenous growth model attempts to EXPLAIN technological progress within the model rather than treating
it as exogenous. Key feature: NO diminishing marginal returns to capital in the AGGREGATE economy (though
individual firms face constant returns to private inputs). Why? Because knowledge and R&D have POSITIVE
EXTERNALITIES — spending by one company benefits the whole economy.

Endogenous
Production Function
ye = c × ke
Output per worker is PROPORTIONAL to capital per worker. c = constant marginal
product of capital. No diminishing returns → linear production function.

Endogenous
Growth Rate
Δye/ye = sc − δ − n
Unlike neoclassical, a PERMANENT increase in saving rate (s) permanently raises
the growth rate. Growth rate is now directly linked to saving/investment decisions.

Feature
Neoclassical (Solow)
Endogenous (Romer)
Production function
shape
Curved — diminishing marginal returns
to capital eventually flattens the curve
Linear — constant marginal returns to
aggregate capital (c is constant)
TFP / technology
Exogenous — unexplained residual;
grows at rate θ outside the model
Endogenous — R&D spending and
knowledge accumulation WITHIN the
model generate TFP growth
Effect of ↑ saving rate
(s)
Temporary above-steady-state growth;
returns to old growth rate θ/(1−α) + n
PERMANENT higher growth rate:
Δye/ye = sc − δ − n (saving rate directly
in growth equation)
Diminishing returns
Yes — applies to capital; neoclassical
result is convergence to steady state
No — constant returns to aggregate
capital because of knowledge
externalities; no tendency to converge
Convergence
prediction
Yes — poorer countries should grow
faster and converge to rich countries
No — rich countries can grow as fast
as or faster than poor countries; no
automatic convergence
Scale effect
Economies of scale limited at firm level
(constant returns to private inputs)
Increasing returns to scale at economy
level (social return > private return from
knowledge spillovers)

R&D EXTERNALITIES AND MARKET FAILURE: Private firms invest in R&D until the private rate of return equals
the cost of capital. But because knowledge can be copied (it is non-rivalrous and partially non-excludable), the
SOCIAL return to R&D exceeds the PRIVATE return. This means private firms systematically UNDER-INVEST in
R&D from a societal perspective — a classic market failure. This provides the rationale for government subsidies
and tax incentives for R&D and knowledge capital investment.

Government Rationale for R&D Incentives (LOS 11)

R&D generates POSITIVE EXTERNALITIES — benefits spill over to other firms and the broader
economy beyond what the investing firm captures.
Social return to R&D > Private return → Private firms under-invest in R&D relative to the socially
optimal level.
Government corrects this market failure via: (1) Direct government funding of basic R&D (universities,
national labs); (2) Tax incentives (R&D tax credits, patent system protecting innovators); (3) Subsidies
for early-stage technology companies.
In endogenous growth models, higher R&D spending → higher permanent growth rate (not just a level
effect). This makes government support of innovation particularly valuable.
Distinction: Subsidising ALL private investment mostly promotes pure capital deepening (temporary
neoclassical effect). Subsidising R&D/knowledge capital specifically can permanently raise growth
(endogenous effect).

heading: Three Types of Convergence
body:
Type
Definition
Implied By
Empirical Support
Absolute
Convergence
ALL countries, regardless
of their characteristics,
will eventually converge
to the SAME per capita
income level. The
poorest grow fastest.
Not directly implied by
the neoclassical model,
which only predicts
convergence of
GROWTH RATES, not
income levels — unless
all countries have
identical fundamentals.
NOT well-supported
empirically. Many poor
countries have fallen further
behind, not converged.
Conditional
Convergence
Countries converge to
the same per capita
income level only if they
have SIMILAR saving
rates, population growth
rates, and production
functions (i.e., same
steady state).
Neoclassical model — if
all parameters except
initial k are the same,
countries with lower k
grow faster and
converge. Different
saving or growth rates →
different steady-state
income levels.
Better-supported empirically
among countries with similar
institutions. Countries with
same fundamentals do show
convergence.
Club
Convergence
Only countries that are
'members of the club'
converge — those that
have made appropriate
institutional changes (rule
of law, property rights,
Combination of
neoclassical and
institutional factors. Poor
countries can JOIN the
club by reforming
institutions. Countries
Best supported empirically.
Explains why OECD
countries converge among
themselves while sub-
Saharan Africa diverges. Also
explains why East Asian

open markets,
education). Countries
outside the club continue
to fall further behind.
stuck in bad institutional
equilibria continue to
diverge.
tigers converged while Latin
America stagnated.

!
The THREE convergence types are heavily tested. ABSOLUTE = all countries converge, no
conditions → NOT supported empirically. CONDITIONAL = converge if same fundamentals →
neoclassical model implication. CLUB = only institutional reformers converge → best empirical
fit. Klymchuk's Statement 5 ('poor countries will only converge if they make appropriate
institutional changes') → this is CLUB convergence, Answer A. The associate economist's view
('all developing countries will converge regardless of characteristics') → absolute convergence.

heading: Two Mechanisms of Convergence
body:
Mechanism
How It Works
Speed
Capital Accumulation /
Capital Deepening
Developing countries have LOW K/L → HIGH marginal
product of capital → HIGH returns to investment →
more rapid capital accumulation → per capita income
rises faster (neoclassical transitional dynamics). Exhibit
16 in reading: developing at point A (steep part of
curve), developed at point B (flat part) — same
investment has much larger productivity impact at A.
Gradual — capital
stock builds up slowly.
Speed depends on
domestic saving rate +
FDI inflows.
Technology Transfer /
Imitation
Developing countries can IMPORT and IMITATE
technology already proven in advanced countries,
rather than inventing it themselves. Knowledge and
management practices spread through globalisation,
FDI, trade, educated diaspora returnees. Technology
transfer speeds up convergence relative to waiting for
indigenous R&D. Countries MUST invest resources to
master and deploy imported technology (like R&D but
less risky).
Can be rapid when
executed well (Korea,
Singapore absorbed
foreign technology
very fast via FDI and
open trade).

EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE (Exhibit 17 in reading, 1950–2018): China and South Korea grew at 5.6% and 5.2% per
year respectively, far above the US (2.0%), supporting convergence. 17 of 27 countries grew faster than the US
over this period. BUT: Venezuela (0.2%), South Africa, Argentina fell further behind. The results are MIXED —
consistent with club convergence. Since 2000, convergence has been relatively strong (16 of 27 growing faster
than US), but not among the most advanced economies (France, Japan, Italy all lagged).

heading: Why Some Countries Fail to Converge
body:
Barriers to Club Membership and Convergence
Low rates of investment and savings (unable to accumulate capital; no FDI due to poor investment
climate)
Lack of property rights and political instability (deters both domestic and foreign investment)
Poor public education and health (cannot absorb technology; low human capital stock)

Restrictions on trade and capital flows (forfeits technology transfer, FDI, scale economies)
Tax and regulatory policies discouraging work and investment
Import substitution / inward-oriented policies: Latin America and Africa pursued these 1950s–1980s →
stagnation. East Asia pursued outward-oriented export-led growth → convergence.
Labour market rigidities: non-convergence traps from institutional arrangements that initially helped
but later hindered (e.g., European labour market protections that increase unemployment and reduce
labour productivity).

section_title: GROWTH IN AN OPEN ECONOMY — TRADE
heading: Five Channels Through Which Open Economies Grow Faster
body:
Channel
Mechanism
Growth Effect
1. Global
Savings
Pool
Domestic investment no longer limited
by domestic savings. Capital-poor
countries can borrow from global
savings to fund investment above what
domestic income would allow.
Breaks the low-savings trap. Especially
important for poor countries with high potential
return on capital.
2.
Comparative
Advantage
Resources shift to industries where the
country is relatively most efficient
(comparative advantage). Productivity of
the whole economy rises as high-cost
industries contract and high-productivity
industries expand.
Permanent productivity improvement from better
resource allocation. Neoclassical: level effect.
Endogenous: possibly permanent growth rate
effect.
3. Scale
Economies
Companies can access a larger
GLOBAL market for their products.
Allows better exploitation of economies
of scale that would be impossible
serving only the domestic market.
Higher output per unit input. Particularly
important for small countries whose domestic
markets are too small to reach minimum efficient
scale.
4.
Technology
Import
Countries can import technology
embedded in capital goods, FDI, and
knowledge from advanced countries.
Speeds up convergence.
Can dramatically accelerate productivity growth
in developing countries — imitation costs less
than innovation.
5.
Competition
Effect
Exposure to global competition forces
domestic companies to become more
efficient, innovate, or exit. 'Selection
effect' — less efficient firms exit; more
efficient firms survive and grow.
Raises average productivity across the
economy. Endogenous effect: surviving firms
also invest more in R&D to gain competitive
advantages.

section_title: GROWTH IN AN OPEN ECONOMY — TRADE
heading: Neoclassical vs. Endogenous Open Economy Predictions
body:
Aspect
Neoclassical Prediction
Endogenous Growth Prediction
Effect of removing
trade barriers
Capital flows from capital-rich DM to
capital-poor EM; convergence of K/L
ratios. TEMPORARY above-steady-
state growth in EM; EM runs trade deficit
while importing capital. No
PERMANENT change in steady-state
growth rate.
Selection effect + scale effect +
backwardness effect → PERMANENT
higher growth rate. Open trade
encourages more R&D (larger
addressable market for innovation). EM
countries benefit most from
'backwardness effect' (learning from
more advanced countries/sectors).
Trade policy
implication
Free trade accelerates convergence but
does not permanently change the
growth rate once the new steady state is
reached.
Free trade can permanently raise growth
by encouraging innovation and
technology spillovers. Some exceptions:
small countries that lack competitive
industries may be hurt by trade
openness (they may stop innovating if
they know they'll lose to more efficient
foreign competition anyway).
FDI
Capital flows to EM to exploit higher
returns. As K/L in EM rises, returns fall;
FDI slows; EM eventually shifts to trade
surplus and capital exporter.
FDI brings technology, management
practices, knowledge spillovers that can
permanently raise the growth rate in the
receiving country.

section_title: GROWTH IN AN OPEN ECONOMY — TRADE
heading: Inward vs. Outward-Oriented Policies
body:
Policy
Orientation
Definition
Historical Experience
Outcome
Inward-Oriented
(Import
Substitution)
Restrict imports; develop
domestic substitutes even if
more expensive. High
tariffs, quotas, protected
'infant industries.'
Latin America (Argentina,
Brazil, Mexico), sub-Saharan
Africa, 1950s–1980s.
Generally POOR:
inefficient domestic
industries, low-quality
goods, slow growth,
protection entrenched
politically → hard to
remove.
Outward-Oriented
(Export-Led)
Integrate with global
economy; make exports a
key growth driver. Low
trade barriers, FDI-friendly,
focus on competitive
exported manufacturing.
East Asia: Singapore, South
Korea, Taiwan, China from
1960s–present.
Generally STRONG:
rapid productivity
growth, technology
transfer, scale
economies,
convergence with DM
income levels.

Argentina/Venezuela vs. Japan/Singapore/South Korea — Trade Openness Explains the
Divergence
1950: Argentina ($6,164) and Venezuela ($8,104) were RICHER than Japan ($3,048), Singapore
($4,299), and South Korea ($1,185).
The three Asian countries pursued OUTWARD-ORIENTED policies: welcomed FDI, built export-
oriented manufacturing, invested heavily in education.

Argentina and Venezuela remained relatively CLOSED economies: high tariffs, import substitution,
restrictions on foreign investment, political instability.
By 2018: Singapore ($89,196), South Korea ($36,756), Japan ($39,313) far exceeded Argentina
($18,255) and Venezuela ($9,487 — barely above 1950).
KEY LESSON: Trade openness combined with institutional reforms enables convergence club
membership. Closed economies forfeited technology transfer, scale economies, and FDI.

section_title: GROWTH IN AN OPEN ECONOMY — TRADE
heading: Effect of Removing Trade Barriers — Distributional Effects
body:
Group / Variable
Effect of Trade Liberalisation in EM
Effect in DM (Capital Exporting)
Capital investment
in EM
Increases — FDI flows in; domestic
investment also rises as returns are high
and market access improves.
Capital EXPORTED to EM; domestic
investment may slow temporarily as
returns are higher abroad.
Profits in EM
Increases — improved access to
markets, technology, lower input costs.
MIXED — increased competition from
EM can compress profits in labour-
intensive DM industries.
Employment in EM
Generally RISES — more capital →
more production → more jobs.
Manufacturing employment tends to
grow in outward-oriented EM.
May FALL in labour-intensive sectors if
EM has lower wages. Overall DM
employment effect is ambiguous.
Wages in EM
RISE as labour demand increases with
capital and production growth.
May face DOWNWARD PRESSURE in
low-skill sectors competing directly with
EM labour. Skilled wages in DM often
rise (skilled labour is complementary to
capital).
Overall GDP growth
in EM
Strong POSITIVE effect — scale
economies, technology transfer, capital
accumulation, and selection effect all
reinforce each other.
Modest POSITIVE effect through
comparative advantage — DM focuses
on high-value-added sectors. Long-term
gains from cheaper EM imports improve
DM consumer welfare.

heading: Key Formulas
body:
Annual Growth Rate
g = (Ending/Beginning)^(1/t) − 1
t = number of years

Grinold-Kroner
Equity Return
E(Re) = dy + Δ(P/E) + i + g − ΔS
dy = dividend yield; i = inflation; g = real GDP growth; ΔS = nbb + rd (dilution)

Cobb-Douglas
Y = AK^α × L^(1-α)   and   y = Ak^α
y = Y/L (output per worker); k = K/L (capital per worker); α = capital income share

Growth Accounting
ΔY/Y = ΔA/A + α(ΔK/K) + (1−α)(ΔL/L)
ΔA/A = TFP growth (residual). US: α ≈ 0.3; (1−α) ≈ 0.7

Labour Productivity
Method
Potential GDP growth = Labour force growth + Labour productivity
growth
Labour productivity growth = output per worker growth rate (= labour prod. growth =
TFP + capital deepening)

Capital Deepening
Contribution
Capital deepening = Labour productivity growth − TFP growth
Larger difference → more growth from capital deepening → less sustainable in long
run

Neoclassical
Steady-State
Growth
Δy/y = θ/(1−α)   and   ΔY/Y = θ/(1−α) + n
θ = TFP growth rate; α = capital share; n = labour force growth. ONLY TFP drives per
capita growth.

Steady-State Y/K
Ratio
Ψ = (1/s)[θ/(1−α) + δ + n]
s = saving rate; δ = depreciation. Ψ is constant in steady state.

Transitional Growth
Δy/y = θ/(1−α) + αs(Y/K − Ψ)
Above steady state when Y/K > Ψ (capital scarce, high MPK). Economy converges to
Ψ over time.

Endogenous
Growth Rate
Δye/ye = sc − δ − n
c = constant MPK. ↑s → permanently ↑ growth rate (no diminishing returns to capital)


Capital Stock
Accumulation
K_t = K_{t-1} + I − D
I = gross capital spending (= GDP × investment share); D = depreciation (= GDP ×
depreciation rate)

heading: Exam Traps — Master Reference
body:
!
POPULATION GROWTH raises TOTAL GDP growth but NOT per capita GDP growth. More
workers produce more total output, but per capita GDP (which divides total output by population)
is unchanged. Per capita income growth requires LABOUR PRODUCTIVITY growth, not just
more workers. Frequently tested: 'population growth → higher per capita growth' is WRONG.

!
SAVINGS RATE: Higher saving rate raises the LEVEL of per capita output and capital per
worker permanently but does NOT permanently raise the GROWTH RATE in the neoclassical
model. Growth temporarily exceeds steady state during the transition, then returns to θ/(1−α) +
n. In the ENDOGENOUS model, higher saving rate DOES permanently raise the growth rate
(Δye/ye = sc − δ − n). Know which model you are in.

!
CAPITAL DEEPENING is a MOVEMENT ALONG the production function (same curve). TFP
improvement SHIFTS the entire production function UPWARD. Capital deepening alone cannot
sustain long-run per capita growth because of diminishing marginal returns. TFP is the ONLY
sustainable source of per capita growth in the neoclassical model.

!
HIGH GDP GROWTH ≠ HIGH EQUITY RETURNS. The China example: 8.2% real GDP growth
but only 0.7% real equity return 1997–2017. Reason: massive dilution from SOE privatisations
(negative nbb/ΔS). Always decompose equity returns using Grinold-Kroner: earnings growth ≈
nominal GDP growth MINUS dilution effects. High-growth EM often have significant dilution.

!
PROFITS SHARE OF GDP CANNOT RISE INDEFINITELY. In the long run, corporate profits-to-
GDP must revert toward historical mean. At some point, stagnant labour income makes workers
unwilling to work and undermines demand. Therefore: long-run earnings growth ≤ potential
nominal GDP growth. A vendor extrapolating high recent equity returns is embedding an implicit
assumption that P/E or profit share can rise indefinitely — which it cannot.

!
DUTCH DISEASE: Resource-rich countries may suffer SLOWER growth because resource
export demand appreciates the currency, making the manufacturing sector globally
uncompetitive. The country misses out on manufacturing-sector TFP gains. Venezuela and
Nigeria are canonical examples. The cure: diversify economic base BEFORE resource revenues
peak, sterilise resource revenues into sovereign wealth funds to prevent excessive currency
appreciation.

!
CONVERGENCE TYPE IDENTIFICATION: Absolute = all countries converge regardless of
characteristics (NOT empirically supported). Conditional = converge if same saving rate,
population growth, production function (neoclassical implication). Club = only institutional
reformers converge (best empirical fit). Statement 'poor countries will converge if they make
institutional changes' = CLUB convergence (Answer A in practice problems).


!
NEOCLASSICAL OPEN ECONOMY: Free trade accelerates convergence of K/L ratios but
leads to NO PERMANENT increase in steady-state growth rate (capital flows stop once K/L
equalises). ENDOGENOUS OPEN ECONOMY: Free trade can permanently raise growth
through selection, scale, backwardness effects, and R&D incentives. Know which model's
prediction is being described in the question.

!
ICT CAPITAL vs. NON-ICT CAPITAL: ICT investment may permanently raise the growth rate
through network externalities (more network nodes → higher productivity for all). Non-ICT
investment (buildings, machinery) is largely pure capital deepening — beneficial but subject to
diminishing returns and cannot sustain per capita growth indefinitely. The distinction matters for
policy questions.

!
DEVELOPED vs. DEVELOPING EDUCATION STRATEGY: DM should emphasise POST-
SECONDARY education (drives innovation on the technology frontier). EM should emphasise
PRIMARY/SECONDARY education (improves capacity to absorb technology from DM). The
optimal level of education spending differs by development stage. A developing country pouring
money into universities while neglecting primary education is misallocating resources.

heading: Using DuPont to Estimate Sustainable Growth ................................................... 20
Conceptual Understanding ................................................................................ 22
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction to Dividend Discount Models
Common stock represents ownership in a business that generates cash flows. Dividend
Discount Models (DDMs) value equity as the present value of expected future
dividends.

FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE: The intrinsic value of common stock equals the
present value of all expected future dividends, discounted at the required
rate of return.

heading: Four Steps in DCF Analysis
body:
1. Choose the class of DCF model (dividends, free cash flow, residual income)
2. Forecast the cash flows
3. Choose a discount rate methodology
4. Estimate the discount rate

In DDMs, we use dividends as the cash flow definition because:
•
Dividends represent actual cash payments to shareholders
•
Boards of directors authorize dividends based on company's ability to pay
•
Dividends are observable and verifiable

heading: When to Use DDM
body:
Best For
Less Suitable For
Companies with established dividend
policies
Companies that don't pay dividends
Mature, stable companies
High-growth companies with no dividends

Page 5
Companies where dividends reflect earning
power
Companies with erratic dividend policies
REITs and utilities (required dividends)
Early-stage companies
Dividend-focused investment strategies
Companies with excessive leverage

Alternative models (Free Cash Flow, Residual Income) may be more appropriate when
dividends don't represent value well.



Page 6
2. The General Dividend Discount Model
The general form of the DDM values a stock as the present value of all future dividends:

V₀ = D₁/(1+r)¹ + D₂/(1+r)² + D₃/(1+r)³ + ... + D∞/(1+r)∞
Or in summation notation:
V₀ = Σ(t=1 to ∞) Dₜ/(1+r)ᵗ

Where:
•
V₀ = Value of stock today
•
Dₜ = Expected dividend at time t
•
r = Required rate of return on equity
•
t = Time period

KEY INSIGHT: Dividends are assumed to continue forever (perpetuity). This
is appropriate because corporations have indefinite lives.

heading: The Required Rate of Return (r)
body:
The required return reflects the risk of the stock. Common approaches:

CAPM Approach
r = Rғ + β(Rᴍ − Rғ)
•
Rғ = Risk-free rate
•
β = Stock's beta (systematic risk)
•
(Rᴍ − Rғ) = Market risk premium

Build-Up Approach

Page 7
r = Rғ + Equity Risk Premium + Size Premium + Company-Specific Premium

EXAM TIP: You'll typically be given the required return. Focus on applying DDM
formulas correctly.



Page 8
3. The Gordon Growth Model (Constant Growth DDM)
The Gordon Growth Model assumes dividends grow at a constant rate forever. This
simplifying assumption makes the model tractable.

heading: The Formula
body:
GORDON GROWTH MODEL: V₀ = D₁ / (r − g)    where D₁ = D₀(1 + g)

Where:
•
V₀ = Value of stock today
•
D₀ = Most recent dividend (already paid)
•
D₁ = Next expected dividend = D₀(1 + g)
•
r = Required rate of return
•
g = Constant growth rate of dividends

heading: Critical Assumptions
body:
5. Dividends grow at constant rate g forever
6. g < r (growth rate must be less than required return)
7. g must be sustainable in perpetuity

CRITICAL CONDITION: r > g. If g ≥ r, the model produces undefined or
negative values. This would imply infinite value!

heading: Example Calculation
body:
Given:
•
Most recent dividend (D₀) = $2.00

Page 9
•
Expected growth rate (g) = 5% per year
•
Required return (r) = 11%

Solution:
D₁ = D₀(1 + g) = $2.00(1.05) = $2.10
V₀ = D₁ / (r − g) = $2.10 / (0.11 − 0.05) = $2.10 / 0.06 = $35.00

heading: Alternative Forms
body:
The Gordon Growth Model can be rearranged to solve for different variables:

Solving for Required Return
r = (D₁ / P₀) + g
This shows required return has two components:
•
Dividend yield: D₁ / P₀
•
Growth rate: g

Solving for Growth Rate (Implied Growth)
g = r − (D₁ / P₀)
Useful for inferring market expectations of growth given current price.

heading: When to Use Gordon Growth Model
body:
Suitable For
Not Suitable For
Mature, stable companies
High-growth companies
Stable dividend growth history
No dividend payment

Page 10
Low, stable growth (GDP-like)
Cyclical companies
Dividend aristocrats
Erratic dividend policies
Utilities, consumer staples
Technology startups



Page 11
4. Share Repurchases and Total Payout
Many companies return cash to shareholders through share repurchases in addition to
(or instead of) dividends.

heading: Why Repurchases Matter
body:
•
Tax efficiency: Capital gains taxed at sale vs. dividends taxed immediately
•
Flexibility: Repurchases can vary without signaling like dividend cuts
•
EPS accretion: Fewer shares outstanding increases EPS
•
Total payout = Dividends + Net Repurchases

heading: Adjusted DDM for Repurchases
body:
Method 1: Add value of repurchases to dividend
Total Cash Distribution per share = Dividend + (Repurchases / Shares
Outstanding)

Method 2: Use total payout ratio instead of dividend payout ratio when calculating
sustainable growth.

EXAM TIP: If a company has significant repurchases, consider whether
traditional DDM is still appropriate or if FCF model is better.



Page 12
5. Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model
The two-stage DDM assumes two distinct phases of growth:
8. High growth phase (usually 3-10 years)
9. Stable growth phase (perpetuity)

heading: The Formula
body:
TWO-STAGE DDM: V₀ = PV(Dividends in Stage 1) + PV(Terminal Value)

V₀ = Σ(t=1 to n) Dₜ/(1+r)ᵗ + [Dₙ₊₁/(r−gₗ)] / (1+r)ⁿ

Where:
•
n = Number of years in high-growth stage
•
Dₜ = Dividend in year t growing at rate gₛ (short-term/high growth)
•
gₗ = Long-term sustainable growth rate (mature phase)
•
Terminal value = Value at end of high-growth phase = Dₙ₊₁/(r − gₗ)

heading: Step-by-Step Calculation
body:
10. Calculate dividends for each year of high-growth phase
11. Discount each dividend to present value
12. Calculate terminal value at end of high-growth phase using Gordon Growth
13. Discount terminal value to present
14. Sum all present values

heading: When to Use Two-Stage DDM
body:
•
Company currently in high-growth phase expected to mature
•
Product lifecycle considerations (growth → maturity)
•
Market penetration (new markets → saturated markets)
•
Industries with predictable maturation patterns



Page 14
6. The H-Model
The H-Model is a variant of the two-stage model that assumes a linear decline in growth
rate from high (gₛ) to low (gₗ) over time period 2H.

heading: The Formula
body:
H-MODEL: V₀ = [D₀(1+gₗ) / (r−gₗ)] + [D₀ × H × (gₛ−gₗ) / (r−gₗ)]

Where:
•
D₀ = Most recent dividend
•
gₛ = Initial short-term growth rate
•
gₗ = Long-term growth rate
•
H = Half-life of high-growth period (years)
•
2H = Total length of transition period

heading: Intuition Behind H-Model
body:
The H-Model has two components:
15. Base value assuming immediate stable growth: D₀(1+gₗ) / (r−gₗ)
16. Premium for gradual decline from high growth: D₀ × H × (gₛ−gₗ) / (r−gₗ)

Advantages over two-stage:
•
Smoother transition (more realistic)
•
Closed-form solution (easier calculation)
•
One formula instead of summing multiple periods

heading: When to Use H-Model
body:
•
Company transitioning from high to stable growth
•
More realistic than abrupt change in two-stage
•
When you want simpler calculation than full two-stage
•
Analyst believes growth will decline linearly



Page 16
7. Three-Stage Dividend Discount Model
The three-stage DDM assumes three distinct phases:
17. High growth (Stage 1)
18. Declining/transitional growth (Stage 2)
19. Stable mature growth (Stage 3)

heading: The Formula
body:
V₀ = PV(Stage 1) + PV(Stage 2) + PV(Terminal Value)

Stage
Description
Calculation
Stage 1 (years 1 to n₁)
High growth at gₐ
Σ Dₜ/(1+r)ᵗ where Dₜ grows
at gₐ
Stage 2 (years n₁+1 to n₂)
Transitional growth at gᵦ
Σ Dₜ/(1+r)ᵗ where Dₜ grows
at gᵦ
Stage 3 (year n₂+1 onward)
Stable growth at gᶜ
Terminal Value =
Dₙ₂₊₁/(r−gᶜ)

heading: When to Use Three-Stage DDM
body:
•
Young companies with three distinct growth phases
•
Industries with extended transition periods
•
When two-stage is too simplistic but full detail needed
•
Analyst has conviction about multiple growth phases


Page 17
EXAM TIP: Three-stage calculations can be lengthy. Focus on
understanding the logic: discount each stage's dividends separately, then
sum.



Page 18
8. Sustainable Growth Rate
The sustainable growth rate (g) is the rate at which a company can grow dividends
without changing its capital structure or raising additional external equity.

heading: The Formula
body:
