SUSTAINABLE GROWTH RATE: g = ROE × Retention Ratio = ROE × (1 −
Payout Ratio)

Where:
•
ROE = Return on Equity = Net Income / Average Shareholders' Equity
•
Retention Ratio (b) = 1 − Payout Ratio
•
Payout Ratio = Dividends / Net Income

heading: Intuition
body:
Growth requires reinvestment. The sustainable growth rate depends on:
20. How much a company earns on equity (ROE)
21. How much it retains to reinvest (retention ratio)

Higher ROE → Higher growth potential
Higher retention → More reinvestment → Higher growth
But higher retention → Lower dividends

heading: Example Calculation
body:
Given:
•
ROE = 15%
•
Dividend payout ratio = 40%


Page 19
Solution:
Retention ratio = 1 − 0.40 = 0.60 or 60%
g = ROE × Retention Ratio = 0.15 × 0.60 = 0.09 or 9%

heading: DuPont Analysis for ROE
body:
ROE can be decomposed using DuPont analysis to understand growth drivers:

5-FACTOR DUPONT: ROE = Tax Burden × Interest Burden × EBIT Margin ×
Asset Turnover × Leverage

ROE = (NI/EBT) × (EBT/EBIT) × (EBIT/Revenue) × (Revenue/Assets) ×
(Assets/Equity)

Component
Formula
Measures
Tax Burden
Net Income / EBT
Effect of taxes
Interest Burden
EBT / EBIT
Effect of interest expense
EBIT Margin
EBIT / Revenue
Operating profitability
Asset Turnover
Revenue / Assets
Efficiency of asset use
Financial Leverage
Assets / Equity
Use of debt

3-Factor DuPont (Simplified)

Page 20
ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier
ROE = (NI/Revenue) × (Revenue/Assets) × (Assets/Equity)

heading: Using DuPont to Estimate Sustainable Growth
body:
Steps:
22. Decompose historical ROE using DuPont
23. Project each component into future
24. Multiply components to get forecasted ROE
25. Multiply ROE by retention ratio to get g

EXAM TIP: Be able to calculate sustainable growth rate and decompose
ROE using DuPont. These are heavily tested computational skills.



Page 21
9. Choosing the Right DDM
Model
When to Use
Advantages
Disadvantages
Gordon Growth
Mature, stable
dividend growth
Simple, easy to
calculate
Unrealistic for most
firms
Two-Stage
Clear transition
from growth to
maturity
Captures two
phases
Abrupt transition
unrealistic
H-Model
Linear decline in
growth
Smooth transition,
closed form
Linear decline
assumption
Three-Stage
Multiple distinct
growth phases
Most flexible
Complex, many
assumptions
Spreadsheet
Detailed forecasting
needed
Can model any
pattern
Time-consuming,
subjective

heading: General Guidelines
body:
•
Use simplest model that captures the company's dividend pattern
•
More stages = more assumptions = more potential for error
•
Gordon Growth only for true mature, stable companies
•
Growth rates must be sustainable and logical
•
Always check: Is g < r? If not, model won't work



Page 22
10. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Core Formulas to Memorize
body:
26. Gordon Growth: V₀ = D₁/(r−g) where D₁ = D₀(1+g)
27. Required return: r = (D₁/P₀) + g
28. Sustainable growth: g = ROE × (1 − Payout Ratio)
29. H-Model: V₀ = [D₀(1+gₗ)/(r−gₗ)] + [D₀ × H × (gₛ−gₗ)/(r−gₗ)]
30. Two-Stage: Sum PV of dividends + PV of terminal value

heading: Common Mistakes to Avoid
body:
•
Using D₀ instead of D₁ in Gordon Growth formula
•
Forgetting to discount terminal value to present
•
Using g ≥ r (must have r > g)
•
Confusing payout ratio with retention ratio
•
Incorrect terminal value calculation

heading: High-Yield Calculation Topics
body:
•
Gordon Growth Model calculation (heavily tested)
•
Two-stage DDM with terminal value
•
Sustainable growth rate calculation
•
DuPont decomposition of ROE
•
Implied growth rate from current price

heading: Conceptual Understanding
body:
•
Why r must be greater than g
•
When each DDM variant is most appropriate
•
Relationship between retention ratio and growth
•
Impact of share repurchases on DDM
•
Limitations of DDM (no dividends, unstable growth)

Page 23

EXAM STRATEGY: Practice calculations until automatic. Know when to use
each model. Watch for g ≥ r traps!

END OF STUDY NOTES
Next: Learning Module 3 - Free Cash Flow Valuation

heading: Categories of Valuation Models ........................................................................... 17
body: Page 3

heading: Exam Strategy ................................................................................................... 25
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction to Equity Valuation
Every day, investment professionals face a fundamental question: What is the value of
a particular asset? For equity analysts, answering this question is at the heart of their
professional activities.

VALUATION: The estimation of an asset's value based on variables
perceived to be related to future investment returns, comparisons with
similar assets, or estimates of immediate liquidation proceeds.

This reading addresses fundamental questions:
•
What is value and how is it defined?
•
Who uses equity valuations and for what purposes?
•
How important is industry knowledge?
•
How should analysts communicate their analysis?



Page 5
2. Value Definitions and Concepts
The context of a valuation, including its objective, determines the appropriate definition
of value and affects the analyst's selection of a valuation approach.

heading: Intrinsic Value
body:
Intrinsic value is the foundation of equity valuation. A critical assumption is that market
price can differ from intrinsic value.

INTRINSIC VALUE (V): The value of an asset given a hypothetically complete
understanding of the asset's investment characteristics. It represents the
'true' or 'real' value of an asset.

Key Concepts:
•
Market price (P) may differ from intrinsic value (V)
•
This creates opportunities for active investment managers to generate alpha
(excess risk-adjusted returns)
•
Grossman-Stiglitz paradox: If prices perfectly reflected value, no one would pay
for analysis—but then how would prices reflect value?

Sources of Perceived Mispricing
VE − P = (V − P) + (VE − V)
Where:
•
VE = Estimated intrinsic value
•
P = Market price
•
V = True intrinsic value (unobservable)

This expression shows that perceived mispricing (VE − P) has two components:


Page 6
1. True mispricing: (V − P) — the actual difference between intrinsic value and
market price
2. Estimation error: (VE − V) — the error in estimating intrinsic value

KEY INSIGHT: Active managers must have expectations that (1) differ from
consensus AND (2) are correct on average to generate alpha consistently.

Challenges in Realizing Value
•
Temporal market conditions may prevent capturing perceived mispricing
•
Convergence of price to value may not occur within investment horizon
•
Many investors look for a catalyst—a market or corporate event that will cause
re-evaluation

heading: Going-Concern Value vs. Liquidation Value
body:
Type of Value
Definition
When Appropriate
Going-Concern Value
Value assuming company
continues operations into
foreseeable future, using
assets optimally
Financially healthy
companies with sustainable
business models
Liquidation Value
Value if company dissolved
and assets sold individually
Companies in financial
distress or worth more
'dead than alive'


Page 7
GOING-CONCERN ASSUMPTION: The company will continue to produce
and sell goods/services, use assets in a value-maximizing way, and access
optimal financing sources.

For most companies, going-concern value > liquidation value due to:
•
Value added by assets working together (synergies)
•
Value added by human capital and management skill
•
Intangible assets (brands, customer relationships)

heading: Fair Value
body:
Fair value has different meanings in different contexts:

Context
Definition
IFRS 13 / US GAAP ASC 820
Price received to sell an asset or paid to
transfer a liability in orderly transaction
between market participants
Investment Management
Often used synonymously with intrinsic
value or fundamental value
Legal/Regulatory
May have specific statutory definitions (e.g.,
in merger law)

EXAM TIP: Pay attention to context when encountering 'fair value.' In valuation
discussions, it typically means intrinsic value. In financial reporting, it has a specific
accounting definition.



Page 8
3. Applications of Equity Valuation
Equity valuation is used in various contexts by different market participants:

heading: Stock Selection and Portfolio Management
body:
•
Buy-side analysts value stocks to make investment recommendations
•
Compare estimated value (V) to market price (P):
•
  • If V > P: Stock is undervalued → BUY recommendation
•
  • If V < P: Stock is overvalued → SELL recommendation
•
  • If V ≈ P: Stock is fairly valued → HOLD recommendation
•
Portfolio managers use valuations for:
•
  • Security selection decisions
•
  • Portfolio weighting decisions
•
  • Performance attribution analysis

heading: Inferring Market Expectations
body:
Analysts can 'reverse engineer' market prices to infer what expectations are embedded
in current valuations.

MARKET EXPECTATIONS ANALYSIS: Using current market price and a
valuation model to solve for implied assumptions (growth rates, margins,
etc.) that justify current price.

Applications:
•
Identify what growth rate is implied by current P/E ratio
•
Determine if implied assumptions are reasonable
•
Spot potential mispricings when implied assumptions seem unrealistic


Page 9

heading: Evaluating Corporate Events
body:
•
Mergers and Acquisitions: Value target companies, assess fairness of offer price
•
Divestitures: Determine appropriate sale price for business units
•
Corporate Restructurings: Evaluate value creation/destruction from
reorganizations
•
Going Private Transactions: Assess fairness to minority shareholders
•
Appraisal/Valuation of Private Business Interests

heading: Fairness Opinions
body:
•
Investment banks provide fairness opinions in M&A transactions
•
Opine on whether transaction price is fair from financial perspective
•
Required for board to fulfill fiduciary duties to shareholders

heading: Planning and Performance Measurement
body:
•
Value-Based Management: Manage company to maximize shareholder value
•
Stock-Based Compensation: Value options and restricted stock
•
Performance Attribution: Decompose portfolio returns into value-added
components



Page 10
4. The Valuation Process: Five Essential Steps
Valuation involves a systematic process with five key steps:

Step
Description
Key Activities
1. Understanding the
Business
Industry and competitive
analysis + financial
statement analysis
Porter's Five Forces,
competitive strategy,
historical performance
2. Forecasting Performance
Pro forma analysis
Sales, earnings, dividends,
financial position projections
3. Selecting Valuation
Model
Choose appropriate
model(s)
Absolute vs. relative,
consider company
characteristics
4. Converting Forecasts to
Value
Apply model with judgment
Calculate value, perform
sensitivity analysis
5. Applying Conclusions
Make
recommendation/decision
Buy/sell/hold, fairness
opinion, strategic evaluation

KEY PRINCIPLE: The valuation process is iterative. Analysts frequently
revisit earlier steps as new information emerges.



Page 11
5. Understanding the Business

heading: Industry and Competitive Analysis
body:
Industry knowledge is essential for understanding:
•
Basic characteristics of markets served
•
Economics and business model of companies
•
Key cost drivers and revenue drivers
•
Competitive dynamics

Critical Questions for Industry Analysis
3. How attractive are the industries in terms of sustained profitability prospects?
4. What is the company's relative competitive position?
5. What is the company's competitive strategy?
6. How well has the company executed its strategy?
7. What are prospects for future execution?

heading: Business Model Analysis
body:
The term 'business model' refers to how a company makes money:
•
Which customers does it target?
•
What products/services does it sell?
•
How does it deliver those products/services?
•
How does it finance its activities?

Example: Low-cost airline business model:
•
Single class of service
•
Single aircraft type (reduces training and maintenance)
•
Point-to-point routes (no hubs)
•
Secondary airports (lower fees)
•
No frills (charge for extras)

•

body:

legal disputes, contingent liabilities
9. Avoid Extrapolation: Don't simply project past results into future. Consider:

heading: Accounting Considerations
body:
Analysts must consider several accounting issues that affect valuation inputs:

Key Accounting Quality Issues
•
Revenue Recognition: Timing and sustainability of revenues
•
Expense Recognition: Capitalization vs. expensing (especially R&D)
•
Off-Balance Sheet Items: Operating leases, joint ventures, special purpose
entities
•
Goodwill and Intangibles: Impairment testing, amortization
•
Pension and Post-Retirement Benefits: Funded status, assumptions
•
Income Taxes: Deferred tax assets/liabilities, effective tax rates
•
One-Time Items: Restructuring charges, asset impairments, gains/losses on
disposals

Adjustments for Comparability
Common adjustments analysts make:
•
Normalize for one-time/non-recurring items
•
Adjust for different accounting policies (LIFO vs. FIFO)
•
Capitalize operating leases for comparability
•
Adjust for different depreciation methods
•
Remove acquisition-related amortization

EARNINGS QUALITY: High-quality earnings are sustainable, backed by cash
flow, and derived from core operations rather than accounting choices.

heading: Franchise Value vs. Tangible Value
body:
Intrinsic Value = Tangible Value + Franchise Value

•
Tangible Value: Value from assets in place under no-growth assumption
•
Franchise Value: PV of growth opportunities (PVGO)
•
Growth only adds value if ROE > cost of equity

heading: Accounting Methods Impact
body:

•
Inventory methods (FIFO vs. LIFO) affect COGS and net income
•
Depreciation methods affect earnings but not cash flow
•
Capitalization vs. expensing affects both balance sheet and income statement
•
Consolidation vs. equity method affects revenues and assets

heading: FCFE Formula Summary ..................................................................................... 23

heading: Quick Decision Tree ............................................................................................ 28
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction: Conceptual Foundation
Free Cash Flow (FCF) models are the most widely used DCF approach by professional
analysts (86.9% use FCF vs. 35.1% for DDM). Unlike dividends, free cash flows
represent the ACTUAL CASH AVAILABLE for distribution, not just what's paid out.

heading: The Two Perspectives
body:
Perspective
Cash Flow
Discount Rate
Result
Firm (Enterprise)
FCFF
WACC
Firm Value →
Subtract Debt →
Equity Value
Equity
(Shareholder)
FCFE
Required Return on
Equity (rₑ)
Equity Value
Directly

FUNDAMENTAL DEFINITIONS: FCFF = Cash available to ALL capital
providers (debt + equity). FCFE = Cash available to COMMON EQUITY
holders only.

heading: When to Use FCF Models
body:
•
Company doesn't pay dividends (or pays erratic dividends)
•
Dividends significantly differ from capacity to pay
•
FCF aligns with profitability within forecast period
•
Control perspective (M&A, LBO, activist investor)

heading: FCFF vs. FCFE: Which to Use?
body:
Page 5
Use FCFE when:
•
Capital structure is stable
•
Direct equity valuation is simpler

Use FCFF when:
•
Company has NEGATIVE FCFE (highly leveraged)
•
Capital structure is CHANGING (leverage fluctuating)
•
Historical FCFE is distorted by net borrowing

KEY INSIGHT: Both methods should give same equity value if assumptions
are consistent. Choose based on which is cleaner given company's capital
structure.



Page 6
2. Understanding Free Cash Flow: The Foundation
Before formulas, understand what free cash flow represents by connecting to the three
financial statements.

heading: Cash Flow Statement Structure (Refresher)
body:
Section
What It Shows
Key Components
Operating Activities (CFO)
Cash from core business
operations
NI + D&A ± WC changes
Investing Activities (CFI)
Cash used for/from
investments
CapEx, asset sales,
acquisitions
Financing Activities (CFF)
Cash from/to capital
providers
Debt, equity, dividends

heading: The Logic: What Makes Cash 'Free'?
body:
Start with a simple question: After a company generates cash from operations and
maintains/grows its asset base, what's LEFT?

1. Cash from Operations (CFO): Cash generated from running the business
2. MINUS Capital Expenditures (CapEx): Cash spent maintaining/growing fixed
assets
3. EQUALS Free Cash Flow: Cash 'free' to be distributed

CONCEPTUAL FOUNDATION: FCF = CFO − CapEx (plus adjustments for
who gets the cash)

Page 7

heading: The Key Difference: FCFF vs. FCFE
body:
The difference comes down to ONE question: Who are we calculating cash flow FOR?

Metric
For Whom?
Before/After
Interest?
Before/After Debt
Changes?
FCFF
All capital providers
BEFORE interest
(add it back)
IGNORE debt
transactions
FCFE
Equity holders only
AFTER interest
(already deducted)
AFTER net
borrowing

This is THE critical distinction. Every formula variation stems from this fundamental
difference.



Page 8
3. FCFF: Four Derivation Approaches (Mechanics)
There are FOUR common starting points to calculate FCFF. Each starts from a different
line on financial statements. Understanding WHY each adjustment is made is crucial.

MASTER PRINCIPLE: FCFF = Operating cash flow available to ALL
providers of capital, AFTER reinvestment but BEFORE any distributions.

heading: FCFF from Net Income (Most Common)
body:
The Formula
FCFF = NI + NCC + Int(1−T) − FCInv − WCInv

Where:
•
NI = Net Income (bottom line of income statement)
•
NCC = Non-Cash Charges (mainly depreciation & amortization)
•
Int(1−T) = After-tax interest expense
•
FCInv = Fixed Capital Investment (net CapEx)
•
WCInv = Working Capital Investment (increase in net WC)

The MECHANICS: Why Each Adjustment?
Let's think through this step by step:

START: Net Income
•
This is what's available to equity holders AFTER paying interest and taxes
•
But we want cash available to ALL capital providers
•
So we need to 'undo' the deductions made for debt holders

ADJUSTMENT 1: + Non-Cash Charges (NCC)

Page 9
Why? Depreciation and amortization reduce NI but are NOT cash outflows.
•
D&A is an accounting expense to match asset usage to revenue periods
•
No cash actually left the company
•
So ADD IT BACK to get to cash
•
Other NCC: Impairments, restructuring charges (non-cash portion)

ADJUSTMENT 2: + Interest Expense × (1 − Tax Rate)
Why? This is the critical adjustment! Here's the logic:
•
NI is AFTER interest expense was deducted
•
Interest went to DEBT holders, not equity
•
But FCFF is for ALL capital providers (debt + equity)
•
So we need to ADD BACK the cash that went to debt holders
•
BUT interest is tax-deductible, so we only add back the AFTER-TAX amount
•
After-tax interest = Int × (1 − T)

KEY INSIGHT: Interest(1−T) adds back the after-tax cash that was paid to
debt holders. We use after-tax because interest expense reduces taxes.

ADJUSTMENT 3: − Fixed Capital Investment (FCInv)
Why? These are CASH outflows for maintaining/growing productive capacity.
•
CapEx = Capital Expenditures = Purchases of PP&E
•
This cash is NOT free - it's needed to maintain/grow the business
•
SUBTRACT it
•
FCInv = CapEx − Proceeds from asset sales (net investment)

ADJUSTMENT 4: − Working Capital Investment (WCInv)
Why? Increases in working capital tie up cash.
•
Working Capital = Current Operating Assets − Current Operating Liabilities
•
Typical: (A/R + Inventory) − Accounts Payable
•
If WC increases, cash is TIED UP in operations

Page 10
•
If WC decreases, cash is RELEASED from operations
•
SUBTRACT the increase (or add the decrease)

Example Calculation: FCFF from Net Income
Given financial data:
•
Net Income = $100 million
•
Depreciation & Amortization = $30 million
•
Interest Expense = $20 million
•
Tax Rate = 30%
•
Capital Expenditures = $40 million
•
Increase in Working Capital = $10 million

Calculate FCFF:
FCFF = NI + D&A + Int(1−T) − CapEx − ΔWC
FCFF = $100 + $30 + [$20 × (1−0.30)] − $40 − $10
FCFF = $100 + $30 + $14 − $40 − $10
FCFF = $94 million

Interpretation: After generating $100M in earnings, adding back non-cash charges,
recovering after-tax interest paid to debt holders, and subtracting necessary
reinvestment, $94M is available to all capital providers.



Page 11

heading: FCFF from Cash Flow from Operations (CFO)
body:
The Formula
FCFF = CFO + Int(1−T) − FCInv

Where CFO already includes:
•
Net Income
•
+ Non-cash charges (like D&A)
•
± Working capital changes

The MECHANICS: Why This Is Simpler
CFO (from Cash Flow Statement) has ALREADY done most of the work:

4. CFO starts with Net Income
5. CFO adds back D&A and other non-cash charges
6. CFO adjusts for working capital changes

So we only need TWO adjustments:

ADJUSTMENT 1: + Interest(1−T)
•
Same logic as before: add back after-tax interest for debt holders
•
CFO treats interest as an operating outflow
•
We need to add it back for ALL capital providers

ADJUSTMENT 2: − Fixed Capital Investment
•
CFO doesn't deduct CapEx (that's in investing section)
•
We need to subtract it to get 'free' cash flow


Page 12
WHY USE THIS METHOD? When you have the cash flow statement readily
available. CFO is reported, so fewer manual adjustments needed.

Example Calculation: FCFF from CFO
Given:
•
CFO = $124 million (from cash flow statement)
•
Interest Expense = $20 million
•
Tax Rate = 30%
•
Capital Expenditures = $40 million

Calculate FCFF:
FCFF = CFO + Int(1−T) − CapEx
FCFF = $124 + [$20 × (1−0.30)] − $40
FCFF = $124 + $14 − $40
FCFF = $98 million

(Note: This differs from previous example because CFO includes different WC changes
than we specified.)



Page 13

heading: FCFF from EBIT
body:
The Formula
FCFF = EBIT(1−T) + Dep − FCInv − WCInv

The MECHANICS: Starting Higher Up the Income Statement
EBIT = Earnings Before Interest and Taxes
This is operating profit BEFORE any capital structure effects.

Why this is clean:
•
EBIT is already BEFORE interest (doesn't include debt financing effects)
•
So we don't need to add interest back!
•
But we DO need to account for taxes

ADJUSTMENT 1: × (1 − Tax Rate)
•
EBIT is before taxes
•
We need AFTER-tax cash flow
•
So multiply by (1−T) to get after-tax EBIT
•
This gives us NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax)

ADJUSTMENT 2: + Depreciation
•
EBIT includes depreciation expense
•
Depreciation is non-cash
•
Add it back

ADJUSTMENT 3: − FCInv
•
Same as before: deduct capital expenditures

ADJUSTMENT 4: − WCInv
•
Same as before: deduct working capital investment


Page 14
WHY USE THIS METHOD? When you want to focus on operating
performance independent of capital structure. Useful for comparing
companies with different leverage.

Example Calculation: FCFF from EBIT
Given:
•
EBIT = $150 million
•
Tax Rate = 30%
•
Depreciation = $30 million
•
CapEx = $40 million
•
Increase in WC = $10 million

Calculate FCFF:
FCFF = EBIT(1−T) + Dep − CapEx − ΔWC
FCFF = $150(1−0.30) + $30 − $40 − $10
FCFF = $105 + $30 − $40 − $10
FCFF = $85 million



Page 15

heading: FCFF from EBITDA
body:
The Formula
FCFF = EBITDA(1−T) + Dep × T − FCInv − WCInv

The MECHANICS: The Trickiest Derivation
EBITDA = Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization
This is the HIGHEST starting point on the income statement.

The logic requires careful thinking:

STEP 1: EBITDA(1−T)
•
We need after-tax cash flow
•
So multiply EBITDA by (1−T)
•
BUT WAIT - there's a problem!

STEP 2: The Depreciation Tax Shield Problem
•
When we multiply EBITDA by (1−T), we're applying taxes to a BEFORE-
depreciation number
•
But in reality, depreciation IS tax-deductible
•
So we've OVER-STATED the tax burden
•
Solution: ADD BACK the tax benefit of depreciation = Dep × T

CRITICAL CONCEPT: Dep × T is the 'depreciation tax shield' - the tax
savings from being able to deduct depreciation. We add this back because
EBITDA(1−T) doesn't capture it.

STEP 3: − FCInv and − WCInv
•
Same as all other methods


Page 16
Why the Formula Works: Mathematical Proof
Let's prove this reconciles to EBIT formula:

EBITDA(1−T) + Dep × T
= [EBIT + Dep](1−T) + Dep × T      [since EBITDA = EBIT + Dep]
= EBIT(1−T) + Dep(1−T) + Dep × T
= EBIT(1−T) + Dep − Dep × T + Dep × T
= EBIT(1−T) + Dep

Which is exactly the EBIT formula! ✓

WHY USE THIS METHOD? EBITDA is commonly used in practice and
comparable across companies. But be careful with the tax shield
adjustment!

Example Calculation: FCFF from EBITDA
Given:
•
EBITDA = $180 million
•
Depreciation = $30 million
•
Tax Rate = 30%
•
CapEx = $40 million
•
Increase in WC = $10 million

Calculate FCFF:
FCFF = EBITDA(1−T) + Dep × T − CapEx − ΔWC
FCFF = $180(1−0.30) + ($30 × 0.30) − $40 − $10
FCFF = $126 + $9 − $40 − $10
FCFF = $85 million



Page 17

heading: FCFF Formula Summary and Reconciliation
body:
Starting Point
Formula
When to Use
Net Income
NI + NCC + Int(1−T) −
FCInv − WCInv
Most common; when NI
readily available
CFO
CFO + Int(1−T) − FCInv
When you have cash flow
statement
EBIT
EBIT(1−T) + Dep − FCInv −
WCInv
Compare firms with different
leverage
EBITDA
EBITDA(1−T) + Dep×T −
FCInv − WCInv
Industry uses EBITDA; be
careful with tax shield!

EXAM TIP: Know ALL four formulas. Questions may provide different
starting points. The mechanics behind each formula matter more than
memorization.



Page 18
4. FCFE: Three Derivation Approaches (Mechanics)
FCFE is cash available to EQUITY HOLDERS ONLY. Key difference from FCFF: FCFE
accounts for debt transactions.

MASTER PRINCIPLE: FCFE = Cash available to common equity holders
AFTER all obligations and reinvestment, INCLUDING effects of debt.

heading: FCFE from FCFF (The Bridge)
body:
The Formula
FCFE = FCFF − Int(1−T) + Net Borrowing

Where Net Borrowing = New Debt Issued − Debt Repayments

The MECHANICS: Converting Firm to Equity Perspective
This is the CONCEPTUAL BRIDGE between the two approaches.

START: FCFF (cash to all capital providers)

ADJUSTMENT 1: − Int(1−T)
•
FCFF includes cash that went to debt holders as interest
•
From EQUITY perspective, this cash is GONE
•
SUBTRACT after-tax interest paid to debt holders

ADJUSTMENT 2: + Net Borrowing
•
If company borrows NEW debt, equity holders get that cash
•
If company REPAYS debt, cash leaves equity holders
•
Net Borrowing = Borrowing − Repayments

Page 19
•
ADD net new borrowing

KEY INSIGHT: Moving from FCFF to FCFE reverses the interest add-back
and incorporates debt cash flows. This is why FCFE can be negative even
when FCFF is positive!

Example: FCFE from FCFF
Given:
•
FCFF = $94 million (calculated earlier)
•
Interest Expense = $20 million
•
Tax Rate = 30%
•
New Debt Issued = $15 million
•
Debt Repaid = $10 million

Calculate FCFE:
FCFE = FCFF − Int(1−T) + Net Borrowing
FCFE = $94 − [$20(1−0.30)] + ($15 − $10)
FCFE = $94 − $14 + $5
FCFE = $85 million



Page 20

heading: FCFE from Net Income (Direct Approach)
body:
The Formula
FCFE = NI + NCC − FCInv − WCInv + Net Borrowing

The MECHANICS: Why This Differs from FCFF
Compare to FCFF from NI:
FCFF = NI + NCC + Int(1−T) − FCInv − WCInv
FCFE = NI + NCC − FCInv − WCInv + Net Borrowing

The differences:
7. NO interest add-back (equity perspective doesn't care about cash to debt
holders)
8. YES net borrowing (equity perspective DOES care about debt cash flows)

The Logic:
•
Net Income is ALREADY from equity perspective (after interest)
•
Add non-cash charges (D&A)
•
Subtract reinvestment (CapEx and WC)
•
Add cash from financing (net borrowing)

Example: FCFE from Net Income
Given:
•
Net Income = $100 million
•
Depreciation = $30 million
•
CapEx = $40 million
•
Increase in WC = $10 million
•
Net Borrowing = $5 million

Calculate FCFE:
FCFE = NI + Dep − CapEx − ΔWC + Net Borrowing

Page 21
FCFE = $100 + $30 − $40 − $10 + $5
FCFE = $85 million



Page 22

heading: FCFE from CFO
body:
The Formula
FCFE = CFO − FCInv + Net Borrowing

The MECHANICS: Simplest FCFE Calculation
CFO already includes:
•
Net Income (after interest)
•
Non-cash charge adjustments
•
Working capital changes

So we only need:
9. Subtract CapEx (not in CFO)
10. Add Net Borrowing (financing, not operating)

Example: FCFE from CFO
Given:
•
CFO = $120 million
•
CapEx = $40 million
•
Net Borrowing = $5 million

Calculate FCFE:
FCFE = CFO − CapEx + Net Borrowing
FCFE = $120 − $40 + $5
FCFE = $85 million



Page 23

EXAM TIP: FCFE formulas do NOT include interest add-back (already at
equity level) but DO include net borrowing (debt affects equity holders).

6. Valuation Models

heading: Single-Stage (Stable Growth) Models
body:
Firm Value = FCFF₁ / (WACC − g)
Equity Value = FCFE₁ / (r − g)

Where:
•
FCFF₁ = Next year's FCFF = FCFF₀(1+g)
•
FCFE₁ = Next year's FCFE = FCFE₀(1+g)
•
WACC = Weighted average cost of capital
•
r = Required return on equity
•
g = Constant growth rate (must be < discount rate)

heading: Two-Stage Model
body:
Value = PV(High-Growth FCF) + PV(Terminal Value)

Terminal Value calculation:
TV = FCFₙ₊₁ / (Discount Rate − gₙ)

Then discount TV back to present using: TV / (1+r)ⁿ

7. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Formula Mastery Checklist
body:
•
✓ Know all 4 FCFF formulas and when to use each
•
✓ Know all 3 FCFE formulas
•
✓ Understand WHY each adjustment is made (not just memorize)
•
✓ Grasp the FCFF → FCFE bridge formula
•
✓ Remember depreciation tax shield in EBITDA method

heading: Common Exam Traps
body:
•
  Forgetting to use AFTER-TAX interest: Int(1−T), not just Int
•
  Adding interest to FCFE (NO! Only FCFF)
•
  Forgetting depreciation tax shield in EBITDA method
•
  Confusing net borrowing with total debt
•
  Using wrong discount rate (WACC for FCFF, r for FCFE)

heading: Quick Decision Tree
body:
Given a problem, ask:
11. What's provided? (NI, CFO, EBIT, EBITDA) → Choose starting point
12. Need FCFF or FCFE? → Check if capital structure info given
13. Stable or changing leverage? → FCFE better if stable, FCFF if changing

EXAM STRATEGY: Focus on understanding the MECHANICS. If you know
WHY each adjustment is made, you can derive formulas on the fly. This is
far superior to rote memorization!


Page 29
END OF STUDY NOTES
Master the mechanics, not just the formulas!

body:
Page 4
1. Introduction: Why Multiples Dominate Practice
Market-based valuation using multiples is the MOST WIDELY USED approach in
practice:
•

heading: % of analysts use multiples (vs. 78.8% use DCF)
body:
•
Simple, intuitive, easy to communicate
•
Quick comparisons across companies
•
Market-based (reflects current market sentiment)

FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPT: Price multiples scale a stock's price by some
measure of value per share, making cross-company comparisons
meaningful.

heading: Two Foundational Approaches
body:
Approach
Logic
Economic
Rationale
Application
Method of

companies

Similar assets
should trade at

industry average
Method Based on

company's own
characteristics
Multiple reflects
expected growth,
risk, profitability
Calculate justified
P/E from DDM


Page 5
KEY DISTINCTION: Comparables = Relative valuation (vs. peers).
Fundamentals = Absolute valuation (vs. intrinsic value drivers).

heading: Price vs. Enterprise Value Multiples
body:
Type

(equity value)

out debt

Where: Enterprise Value (EV) = Market Value of Equity + Market Value of Debt − Cash
and Investments



Page 6
2. Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio
The P/E ratio is THE most widely used and recognized valuation multiple.

heading: Definitions
body:
P/E = Price per Share / Earnings per Share

Interpretation: How much investors pay for $1 of earnings. Higher P/E = pay more for
each dollar of earnings.

Trailing P/E vs. Forward P/E
Type
Formula
When to Use
Advantages
Disadvantages
Trailing P/E
P₀ / E₀ (or EPS
TTM)
Historical
comparison
Uses actual
earnings
Backward-
looking, may
not reflect
future
Forward P/E
P₀ / E₁ (or
forecasted
EPS)
Future-
oriented
valuation
Forward-
looking,
reflects
expectations
Relies on
estimates, can
be manipulated

Where:
•
P₀ = Current stock price
•
E₀ = Most recent 12-month earnings (trailing)
•
E₁ = Expected next 12-month earnings (forward)
•
TTM = Trailing Twelve Months


Page 7

heading: Fundamental Drivers (Justified P/E)
body:
The P/E multiple can be derived from the Gordon Growth Model:

Starting with: P₀ = D₁ / (r − g)
Where D₁ = E₁ × Payout Ratio

Substitute and divide both sides by E₁:

JUSTIFIED FORWARD P/E = (Payout Ratio × (1 + g)) / (r − g)    OR simply:
Payout / (r − g)

This reveals the FUNDAMENTAL DRIVERS:

Driver
Effect on P/E
Logic
↑ Payout Ratio
↑ Higher P/E
More earnings paid as
dividends → higher value
↑ Growth Rate (g)
↑ Higher P/E
Faster earnings growth →
higher value
↑ Required Return (r)
↓ Lower P/E
Higher risk → lower value
(higher discount rate)

KEY INSIGHT: P/E is positively related to growth and payout ratio,
negatively related to risk (required return).


Page 8

heading: When to Use P/E
body:
Best for:
•
Profitable companies with stable, positive earnings
•
Mature companies in established industries
•
Companies where earnings represent value creation

NOT suitable for:
•
Companies with negative or zero earnings (undefined P/E)
•
Cyclical companies at earnings peak/trough
•
Companies with volatile, unpredictable earnings
•
Companies with significant one-time items distorting EPS

heading: Normalizing Earnings
body:
When earnings are distorted by cyclicality or one-time items, analysts normalize
earnings:

Method 1: Historical Average EPS
•
Average EPS over a business cycle (typically 5-10 years)
•
Smooths cyclical fluctuations

Method 2: Average ROE Method
Normalized EPS = Average ROE × Current Book Value per Share
•
Uses average ROE from mid-cycle years
•
Applies to current capital base

heading: P/E-to-Growth (PEG) Ratio
body:
PEG = P/E / (g × 100)


Page 9
Where g is expressed as a percentage (e.g., 10% growth rate)

Interpretation:
•
PEG < 1: Stock may be undervalued relative to growth
•
PEG = 1: Fair value (paying 1× for each % of growth)
•
PEG > 1: Stock may be overvalued relative to growth

Limitations:
•
Ignores risk differences (required return)
•
Ignores duration of growth
•
No theoretical foundation
•
Rule of thumb only - use with caution

heading: Earnings Yield (E/P)
body:
Earnings Yield = E/P = 1 / (P/E)

Uses:
•
Compare to bond yields (earnings yield vs. bond yield)
•
Screen for value stocks (high E/P = low P/E)
•
Calculate when P/E is negative or undefined



Page 10
3. Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio

heading: Definition
body:
P/B = Market Price per Share / Book Value per Share
Where: Book Value per Share = (Total Shareholders' Equity − Preferred
Stock) / Shares Outstanding

Interpretation: How much investors pay for $1 of net asset value (accounting book
value).

heading: Fundamental Drivers (Justified P/B)
body:
Derived from Residual Income Model:

JUSTIFIED P/B = 1 + [(ROE − r) / (r − g)]

Or equivalently from DDM: P/B = (ROE − g) / (r − g) assuming Payout = 1 − g/ROE

Driver
Effect on P/B
Logic
↑ ROE
↑ Higher P/B
Higher returns on equity →
more valuable
↑ Growth (g)
↑ Higher P/B
Faster growth → higher
value
↑ Required Return (r)
↓ Lower P/B
Higher risk → lower value


Page 11
KEY INSIGHT: P/B above 1.0 means ROE > r (earning more than cost of
capital). P/B below 1.0 means ROE < r (destroying value).

heading: When to Use P/B
body:
Best for:
•
Financial institutions (banks, insurance) where book value approximates market
value of assets
•
Companies with significant tangible assets
•
Companies with negative earnings (P/E undefined, but P/B still works)
•
Distressed companies or potential liquidation scenarios

Less suitable for:
•
Service companies with few tangible assets
•
Technology/intangible-heavy companies (book value understates true value)
•
Companies with significant off-balance-sheet assets

heading: Adjustments to Book Value
body:
•
Adjust for off-balance-sheet items (operating leases, pensions)
•
Fair value adjustments (PPE, inventory at fair value)
•
Remove intangibles if not economically valuable
•
Consider different accounting standards (IFRS vs. US GAAP)



Page 12
4. Price-to-Sales (P/S) Ratio

heading: Definition
body:
P/S = Market Price per Share / Sales (Revenue) per Share
Or: P/S = Market Cap / Total Revenue

Interpretation: How much investors pay for $1 of sales/revenue.

heading: Fundamental Drivers (Justified P/S)
body:
Derived from DDM by expressing earnings as profit margin × sales:

JUSTIFIED P/S = [(Profit Margin × Payout × (1+g)] / (r − g)

Driver
Effect on P/S
Logic
↑ Profit Margin
↑ Higher P/S
More profitable sales →
higher value
↑ Growth (g)
↑ Higher P/S
Faster sales growth →
higher value
↑ Payout Ratio
↑ Higher P/S
More cash to shareholders
→ higher value
↑ Required Return (r)
↓ Lower P/S
Higher risk → lower value


Page 13

heading: When to Use P/S
body:
Best for:
•
Start-ups and early-stage companies with no earnings
•
Cyclical companies when earnings are temporarily depressed
•
Companies with negative earnings
•
Comparing companies in same industry (relatively comparable revenue
recognition)

Advantages:
•
Sales are less subject to manipulation than earnings
•
Sales are always positive (unlike earnings)
•
More stable than earnings

Disadvantages:
•
Doesn't account for profitability (high sales ≠ high profits)
•
Different cost structures across companies
•
Revenue recognition differences



Page 14
5. Price-to-Cash Flow (P/CF) Ratio

heading: Definitions of Cash Flow
body:
Multiple cash flow definitions exist - know which is being used:

Cash Flow Definition
Formula
When Used
CFO (Operating CF)
From statement of cash
flows
Most comprehensive
Free Cash Flow
CFO − CapEx
True discretionary cash
EBITDA
EBIT + D&A
Proxy, not true cash flow
EPS + D&A per share
Net Income + D&A
Simple earnings adjustment

CRITICAL: Cash flow definitions vary. EBITDA is NOT true cash flow
(ignores CapEx, WC, taxes, interest).

heading: When to Use P/CF
body:
•
When earnings quality is questionable
•
Capital-intensive industries (D&A significant)
•
Companies with negative earnings but positive cash flow
•
Comparing companies with different depreciation methods

Advantages:
•
Less subject to manipulation than earnings

Page 15
•
Cash flow more relevant for valuation

Disadvantages:
•
Definition inconsistency across analysts
•
CFO can be manipulated through working capital



Page 16
6. Dividend Yield
Dividend Yield = Annual Dividends per Share / Price per Share

Interpretation: Annual dividend return as a percentage of current stock price.

Uses:
•
Income-focused investors (retirees, income funds)
•
Screen for dividend-paying stocks
•
Compare to bond yields

Limitations:
•
Doesn't account for capital appreciation
•
High yield may signal dividend cut risk
•
Low growth companies typically have higher yields



Page 17
7. Enterprise Value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA)

heading: Definitions
body:
EV/EBITDA = Enterprise Value / EBITDA

Where:
EV = Market Value of Equity + Market Value of Debt − Cash & Investments
EBITDA = Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization

KEY CONCEPT: EV represents total firm value (all capital providers).
EBITDA is pre-interest (available to all providers). The numerator and
denominator MATCH perspectives!

heading: Why EV/EBITDA Works
body:
The logic of matching:
•
EV = Value to ALL capital providers (debt + equity)
•
EBITDA = Cash flow BEFORE interest (to all providers)
•
Both are pre-financing, making comparisons clean across different capital
structures

heading: When to Use EV/EBITDA
body:
Best for:
•
Comparing companies with different capital structures (leverage)
•
Capital-intensive businesses (telecom, utilities, manufacturing)
•
M&A valuation (buyer gets entire firm)
•
LBO analysis (evaluates total acquisition cost)


Page 18
Advantages:
•
Capital structure neutral (debt doesn't affect it)
•
Less affected by depreciation differences
•
Useful when earnings are negative but EBITDA positive

Disadvantages:
•
EBITDA ignores CapEx (can be misleading for capital-intensive businesses)
•
EBITDA ignores working capital needs
•
Not true cash flow (doesn't account for reinvestment)
•
Can hide real cash generation issues

heading: EV/EBITDA Calculation Example
body:
Company Data:
•
Market cap (equity value) = $10,000 million
•
Market value of debt = $3,000 million
•
Cash and investments = $500 million
•
EBITDA = $2,000 million

Calculate EV:
EV = $10,000 + $3,000 − $500 = $12,500 million

Calculate EV/EBITDA:
EV/EBITDA = $12,500 / $2,000 = 6.25×

Interpretation: Investors pay 6.25× EBITDA for the entire enterprise.



Page 19
8. Method of Comparables: Selecting Peers
The quality of relative valuation depends entirely on selecting appropriate comparables.

heading: Criteria for Good Comparables
body:
1. Similar Industry: Same or closely related industry
2. Similar Business Model: Revenue sources, cost structure, operations
3. Similar Size: Market cap, revenues in comparable range
4. Similar Growth Profile: Similar historical and expected growth rates
5. Similar Profitability: ROE, profit margins, ROIC
6. Similar Risk Profile: Business risk, financial leverage, cyclicality

Page 20
EXAM TIP: Harmonic mean is preferred for ratios (like P/E) because it's less
affected by outliers than arithmetic mean.

heading: Screening and Adjustments
body:
•
Remove outliers with extreme multiples
•
Adjust for non-recurring items
•
Adjust for differences in accounting policies
•
Consider relative position in industry

10. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Critical Formulas
body:
•
✓ Justified P/E = Payout / (r − g)
•
✓ Justified P/B = 1 + [(ROE − r) / (r − g)]
•
✓ Justified P/S = (Margin × Payout × (1+g)) / (r − g)
•
✓ EV = Market Cap + Debt − Cash
•
✓ PEG = P/E / (g × 100)

heading: Method Selection
body:
•
Use comparables when: Similar peers exist, market efficiency assumed
•
Use fundamentals when: Company unique, justified multiple needed for absolute
valuation
•
Best practice: Use BOTH and reconcile differences

heading: Common Exam Traps
body:
•
  Confusing trailing vs. forward P/E (check which E is used)
•
  Forgetting to subtract cash from EV calculation
•
  Using EBITDA as true cash flow (it's not!)
•
  Missing that P/B < 1 means ROE < r (destroying value)
•
  Comparing multiples across industries without adjustment

heading: High-Yield Concepts
body:
•
Know fundamental drivers of EACH multiple
•
Understand when to use each multiple (and when NOT to)
•
EV/EBITDA is capital-structure neutral
•
Harmonic mean preferred for averaging P/E ratios
•
Can use multiples for terminal value in DCF models

Page 24

EXAM STRATEGY: Focus on understanding WHY each multiple exists and
WHEN to apply it. Know the fundamental drivers (growth, risk, profitability).
Practice comparable selection.

END OF STUDY NOTES
Multiples are everywhere in practice - own this material!

body:
Page 4
1. Introduction: The Private Company Challenge
Private companies range from sole proprietorships to massive firms like Cargill, IKEA,
and Tata Group. Valuation principles are the same (DCF, multiples, asset-based), but
the LACK of market prices and unique ownership structures require significant
adjustments.

FUNDAMENTAL CHALLENGE: No observable market price. Must estimate
value from scratch with less transparent data and unique control/liquidity
considerations.

heading: Uses of Private Company Valuation
body:
Purpose
Examples
Key Focus
Transaction-Related
M&A, sale, IPO, fairness
opinion

value), tax reporting
(estate/gift tax)
Regulatory standards

Shareholder disputes,
divorce, damage claims
Expert testimony,
defensible methods
Corporate Planning
Stock-based compensation,
restructuring, strategic
decisions
Internal decision support


Page 5

heading: Standard of Value
body:
•
Fair Market Value: Price between willing buyer and seller, both informed, neither
compelled
•
Fair Value: Context-specific (financial reporting, litigation), often excludes
minority discounts
•
Investment Value: Value to specific buyer (may include synergies)



Page 6
2. Public vs. Private Company Differences

KEY INSIGHT: Smaller size and concentrated ownership are defining
characteristics. Both significantly impact valuation through discount rates
and control premiums/discounts.

These stock-specific factors lead to the key adjustments in private company valuation:
DLOC and DLOM.



Page 8
3. Normalizing Earnings and Cash Flows
Private company financials often need adjustment to reflect 'normalized' or sustainable
earning power.

heading: Common Adjustments to Increase Earnings
body:
•
Non-recurring items: One-time gains/losses, restructuring charges
•
Non-operating items: Income from discontinued operations
•
Owner compensation: Adjust excessive/below-market salaries, perks, related-
party transactions
•
Discretionary expenses: Excessive travel, entertainment, charitable contributions
•
Non-market transactions: Below/above-market rents, transactions with related
parties

Page 9

—

Normalized EBITDA of $1,350,000 is 35% higher than reported $1,000,000 - significant
impact on valuation!

heading: Strategic vs. Financial Buyer Perspective
body:
•
Financial buyer: Assumes business continues as-is (fewer adjustments)
•
Strategic buyer: Assumes synergies and restructuring (more adjustments,
potentially higher value)



Page 10
4. Estimating the Discount Rate (CRITICAL!)
CAPM often doesn't work well for private companies (no observable beta, not publicly
traded). Two alternative approaches:

heading: Expanded CAPM
body:
r = Rғ + β(Rᴍ − Rғ) + Size Premium + Company-Specific Risk Premium

Additions to standard CAPM:
•
Size Premium: Small companies have higher returns historically (Ibbotson data)
•
Company-Specific Risk Premium: Idiosyncratic risk that can't be diversified

To estimate beta:
•
Use beta of comparable public companies
•
Unlever public company betas, then relever to private company's capital structure

heading: Build-Up Approach
body:
r = Rғ + Equity Risk Premium + Size Premium + Company-Specific Risk
Premium

No beta needed! Build up return from components:
•
Rғ: Risk-free rate (government bond)
•
Equity Risk Premium: Historical equity return over risk-free (e.g., 5-7%)
•
Size Premium: From Ibbotson or Duff & Phelps data (e.g., 2-5% for small-cap)
•
Company-Specific: Analyst judgment (0-5% typical)

EXAM TIP: Build-up approach is MORE COMMON for private companies
because it doesn't require beta estimation.

Page 11

heading: Example: Discount Rate Calculation
body:
Using build-up approach:
•
Risk-free rate: 4%
•
Equity risk premium: 6%
•
Size premium: 3% (small-cap)
•
Company-specific: 2% (moderate additional risk)

Required return = 4% + 6% + 3% + 2% = 15%

Compare to typical public company cost of equity: 10-12%. Private companies typically
have higher discount rates due to size and company-specific risk.



Page 12
5. Valuation Discounts and Premiums (THE KEY
ADJUSTMENTS)
This is where private company valuation gets unique. After calculating base value, apply
adjustments for control and marketability.

heading: The Valuation Levels Framework
body:
Level
Perspective
Adjustments

Example Value
Strategic Control
Value

synergies

FRAMEWORK: Start with appropriate base value, then adjust UP for control
OR adjust DOWN for lack of control and lack of marketability.

heading: Control Premium
body:
Control Premium: The additional value from owning controlling interest.

Page 13

Controlling shareholders can:
•
Select/fire management
•
Set strategic direction
•
Approve M&A transactions
•
Determine dividend policy
•
Liquidate or sell the company

Typical control premiums: 20-40% over marketable minority value
Estimated from: M&A transaction premiums over pre-announcement stock price

heading: Discount for Lack of Control (DLOC)
body:
DLOC = 1 − [1 / (1 + Control Premium)]

DLOC is the inverse of control premium.

Example calculation:
If control premium = 25%
DLOC = 1 − [1 / 1.25] = 1 − 0.80 = 0.20 or 20%

CRITICAL RELATIONSHIP: DLOC ≠ Control Premium. They're
mathematically related but NOT the same percentage!

Proof: If controlling interest worth $125, minority worth $100:
Control premium = ($125 − $100) / $100 = 25%
DLOC = ($125 − $100) / $125 = 20%

heading: Discount for Lack of Marketability (DLOM)
body:
Page 14
DLOM: Reduction in value due to inability to quickly sell shares at fair value.

Causes of illiquidity:
•
No public market
•
Restricted shares (lock-up periods)
•
Small pool of potential buyers
•
High transaction costs

Typical DLOM ranges: 20-35% (but can vary widely)

DLOM Estimation Methods
Method 1: Restricted Stock Studies
•
Analyze publicly traded companies' restricted shares vs. freely traded
•
Historical studies show 25-35% discounts

Method 2: Pre-IPO Studies
•
Compare private transaction prices to post-IPO prices
•
Historical studies show 40-50% discounts (but dated)

Method 3: Option-Based Approach
•
Value using put option to quantify cost of illiquidity
•
DLOM = Put option value / Stock price

body:

EXAM KEY: Know which discounts apply depending on valuation method
and level of value being estimated!

heading: Full Example: Applying Discounts
body:
Base company value (controlling interest): $10,000,000
Valuing a 10% non-controlling, non-marketable stake
Control premium: 30%
DLOM: 25%

Step 1: Calculate DLOC from control premium
DLOC = 1 − [1 / (1 + 0.30)] = 1 − 0.769 = 0.231 or 23.1%

Step 2: Adjust for lack of control
Marketable minority value = $10,000,000 × (1 − 0.231) = $7,690,000

Step 3: Adjust for lack of marketability
Non-marketable minority value = $7,690,000 × (1 − 0.25) = $5,767,500

Page 16

Step 4: Calculate value of 10% stake
10% stake value = $5,767,500 × 0.10 = $576,750

Note: Pro rata (no discounts) would be $1,000,000. After discounts: $576,750 - a 42%
total discount!



Page 17
6. Three Valuation Approaches

heading: Income Approach
body:
Uses DCF models: FCF, dividends, or residual income.

Most common for private companies: FCF to Equity or FCF to Firm

Steps:
1. Normalize historical earnings
2. Forecast future cash flows
3. Estimate discount rate (expanded CAPM or build-up)
4. Calculate terminal value
5. Discount to present
6. Apply DLOC and/or DLOM if appropriate

Advantages:
•
Forward-looking
•
Considers company-specific factors
•
Theoretically sound

Disadvantages:
•
Highly sensitive to assumptions
•
Requires detailed forecasts
•
Terminal value dominance

heading: Market Approach
body:
Uses multiples from comparable companies or transactions.

Guideline Public Company Method (GPCM)
•
Use multiples from publicly traded comparable companies

Page 18
•
Common multiples: EV/EBITDA, P/E, P/B, EV/Sales
•
Must apply DLOM (comparables are liquid, subject company is not)
•
Typically don't need DLOC (public comps already minority)

Steps:
7. Identify comparable public companies
8. Calculate relevant multiples
9. Adjust multiples if needed (size, growth, profitability differences)
10. Apply multiple to subject company's metric
11. Apply DLOM

Guideline Transaction Method (GTM)
•
Use multiples from M&A transactions of similar private companies
•
Reflects control (transactions typically acquire control)
•
May need to apply DLOC for minority valuation
•
May need to apply DLOM

Advantages of market approach:
•
Market-based (reflects actual transactions)
•
Simpler than DCF
•
Easy to understand

Disadvantages:
•
Finding true comparables is difficult
•
Doesn't capture company-specific factors well
•
Backward-looking

heading: Asset-Based Approach
body:
Values individual assets minus liabilities.


Page 19
Two methods:
Adjusted Net Asset Method
•
Adjust book values to fair market values
•
Value intangible assets not on balance sheet
•
Best for: Holding companies, liquidations

Excess Earnings Method
Value = Adjusted Net Tangible Assets + PV(Excess Earnings from
Intangibles)
•
Separate tangible from intangible asset value
•
Value intangibles based on excess returns

When to use asset-based:
•
Holding companies (real estate, investments)
•
Companies with significant tangible assets
•
Liquidation scenarios
•
NOT suitable for service companies or those with intangible value



Page 20

Page 21
8. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Must-Know Formulas
body:
•
✓ DLOC = 1 − [1 / (1 + Control Premium)]
•
✓ Build-up: r = Rғ + ERP + Size Premium + Company-Specific
•
✓ Expanded CAPM: r = Rғ + β(Rᴍ−Rғ) + Size + Company-Specific

heading: Critical Concepts
body:
•
DLOC ≠ Control Premium (they're inverse but not the same %)
•
DLOC and DLOM are SEPARATE and typically BOTH applied to minority, illiquid
stakes
•
Build-up approach is MORE common than CAPM for private companies
•
Normalizing earnings is ESSENTIAL (remove non-recurring, adjust owner comp)
•
Know which discounts apply with which valuation method

heading: Common Exam Traps
body:
•
  Thinking DLOC = Control Premium (NO! Use the formula)
•
  Forgetting to normalize earnings before valuation
•
  Applying DLOM to control valuations (usually not appropriate)
•
  Not adjusting discount rate for size/company-specific risk
•
  Using wrong base for percentage (DLOC is % of control value, not minority
value)

heading: High-Yield Topics
body:
•
Calculating DLOC from control premium
•
Normalizing earnings (removing discretionary/non-recurring items)
•
Build-up approach to cost of equity
•
When to apply DLOC vs. DLOM
•
Selecting appropriate valuation approach

Page 22

heading: Calculation Checklist
body:
12. Normalize earnings/cash flows
13. Estimate discount rate (build-up or expanded CAPM)
14. Calculate base value (DCF, multiples, or asset-based)
15. Determine appropriate valuation level (control vs. minority, marketable vs. not)
16. Apply DLOC if minority interest
17. Apply DLOM if non-marketable
18. Calculate final value

EXAM STRATEGY: Private company valuation is about ADJUSTMENTS.
Master normalizing earnings, estimating discount rates for illiquid
companies, and applying DLOC/DLOM correctly.

readings!

body:
Page 4
1. Understanding Residual Income: The Foundation
This is where many candidates struggle, so let's build understanding from first
principles.

heading: The Problem with Accounting Income
body:
Traditional income statements have a fundamental limitation:

•
✓ They DEDUCT interest expense (cost of debt)
•
✗ They DON'T deduct cost of equity capital

Result: A company can show POSITIVE net income but still DESTROY shareholder
value if it's not earning enough to cover its cost of equity.

CRITICAL INSIGHT: Accounting profit ≠ Economic profit. Residual Income
measures ECONOMIC profit by deducting ALL capital costs, including
equity.

heading: What IS Residual Income?
body:
Residual Income = Net Income − Equity Charge
Where: Equity Charge = Equity Capital × Cost of Equity (r)

Or per share:
RI = EPS − (r × Beginning Book Value per Share)
RI = EPS − (r × B(t−1))


Page 5

heading: Example: The Mechanics
body:
Company ABC:
•
Total assets: $2,000,000
•
Financed: 50% debt ($1M), 50% equity ($1M)
•
Cost of debt: 7% (4.9% after-tax at 30% tax rate)
•
Cost of equity: 12%
•
EBIT: $200,000
•
Tax rate: 30%

Calculate Net Income:
Line Item
Amount
EBIT
$200,000
− Interest Expense (7% × $1M)
($70,000)
= Pretax Income
$130,000
− Taxes (30%)
($39,000)
= Net Income
$91,000

Accounting says: Company is profitable! ($91,000 net income)

But let's calculate Residual Income:

Calculation
Amount

Page 6
Net Income
$91,000
− Equity Charge ($1M × 12%)
($120,000)
= Residual Income
($29,000)

NEGATIVE RESIDUAL INCOME = Destroying shareholder value! Company
earned $91K but needed to earn $120K to cover cost of equity.

heading: The Value Implication
body:
Positive RI → Creating value → Stock trades at PREMIUM to book value (P/B > 1)
Negative RI → Destroying value → Stock trades at DISCOUNT to book value (P/B < 1)
Zero RI → Earning exactly cost of equity → Stock trades AT book value (P/B = 1)

Condition

> 1.0
Creating value

= 1.0

capital

Page 7
2. The Clean Surplus Relation (CRITICAL!)
This is THE foundational accounting relationship for RI models. Many exam questions
test whether you understand this.

heading: The Formula
body:
CLEAN SURPLUS RELATION: B(t) = B(t−1) + E(t) − D(t)

Where:
•
B(t) = Ending book value of equity
•
B(t−1) = Beginning book value of equity
•
E(t) = Earnings (net income) for period t
•
D(t) = Dividends paid in period t

heading: What Does It Mean?
body:
The clean surplus relation says:

Ending Book Value = Beginning Book Value + Earnings − Dividends

Or rearranged:
Change in Book Value = Earnings − Dividends

KEY CONCEPT: ALL earnings changes flow through to book value, EXCEPT
for dividends paid out and ownership transactions (issuing/repurchasing
shares).


Page 8

heading: Why 'Clean Surplus'?
body:
'Clean surplus accounting' means:
•
Income (earnings) reflects ALL changes in book value
•
EXCEPT ownership transactions (share issues/buybacks)
•
No bypassing the income statement (no 'dirty surplus')

heading: Violations of Clean Surplus
body:
Items that violate clean surplus (bypass income statement):
•
Foreign currency translation adjustments
•
Certain pension liability adjustments
•
Changes in revaluation surplus (IFRS)
•
Some fair value changes through OCI

These items go directly to equity (comprehensive income) without hitting net income,
creating 'dirty surplus.'

heading: Example: Clean Surplus in Action
body:
Company XYZ:
•
Beginning book value: $100
•
Earnings: $15
•
Dividends: $5

Ending book value = $100 + $15 − $5 = $110

Retained earnings increased by $10 ($15 earned − $5 paid out).



Page 9
3. The Residual Income Valuation Model

heading: The General Formula
body:
RESIDUAL INCOME MODEL: V₀ = B₀ + Σ [RI(t) / (1+r)ᵗ]    for t=1 to ∞

Or explicitly:
V₀ = B₀ + Σ [(E(t) − r × B(t−1)) / (1+r)ᵗ]

Where:
•
V₀ = Intrinsic value today
•
B₀ = Current book value per share
•
E(t) = Expected EPS in period t
•
r = Required return on equity (cost of equity)
•
B(t−1) = Book value per share at beginning of period t
•
RI(t) = Residual income in period t = E(t) − r × B(t−1)

heading: Interpreting the Model
body:
The RI model says stock value has TWO components:

1. CURRENT book value (what's already on the balance sheet)
2. Present value of FUTURE residual income (value created above/below cost of
equity)

CONCEPTUAL POWER: If a company earns exactly its cost of equity forever
(RI = 0), it trades at book value. Any value above/below book comes from
expected future RI.


Page 10

heading: Derivation from DDM (Understanding the Connection)
body:
The RI model can be derived from the Dividend Discount Model using the clean surplus
relation.

Start with DDM:
V₀ = Σ [D(t) / (1+r)ᵗ]

From clean surplus: D(t) = E(t) − [B(t) − B(t−1)]
(Dividends = Earnings − Change in Book Value)

Substitute into DDM and rearrange... you get the RI model!

This proves: DDM and RI model are EQUIVALENT under clean surplus accounting.

heading: Worked Example: Full Valuation
body:
Company Bugg Properties:
•
Current book value (B₀): $6.00 per share
•
Expected EPS: $2.00, $2.50, $4.00 (Years 1-3)
•
Expected dividends: $1.00, $1.25, $12.25 (Years 1-3)
•
Required return (r): 10%
•
Year 3 is liquidating dividend (company ceases operations)

Step 1: Calculate book values using clean surplus:

Step 3: Discount residual income to present:

PV(RI₁) = $1.40 / 1.10 = $1.273
PV(RI₂) = $1.80 / 1.10² = $1.488

Sum of PV(RI) = $5.146

Step 4: Add current book value:

Page 12
V₀ = $6.00 + $5.146 = $11.146 ≈ $11.15

Verify with DDM:
V₀ = $1.00/1.10 + $1.25/1.10² + $12.25/1.10³ = $11.15 ✓



Page 13
4. Single-Stage RI Model (Constant Growth)

heading: The Formula
body:
If residual income grows at constant rate g forever:

SINGLE-STAGE RI MODEL: V₀ = B₀ + [RI₁ / (r − g)]

Where:
•
RI₁ = Expected residual income next period = E₁ − r × B₀
•
g = Constant growth rate of residual income

heading: Alternative Expression Using ROE
body:
Can also express in terms of ROE and growth:

V₀ = B₀ + [(ROE − r) × B₀] / (r − g)

Where ROE = E₁ / B₀

This clearly shows:
•
If ROE > r → V₀ > B₀ (premium to book)
•
If ROE = r → V₀ = B₀ (at book value)
•
If ROE < r → V₀ < B₀ (discount to book)

heading: Example: Single-Stage Valuation
body:
Company data:
•
Current book value (B₀): $20 per share
•
Expected ROE: 15%

Page 14
•
Required return (r): 12%
•
Growth rate (g): 6%

Step 1: Calculate E₁:
E₁ = ROE × B₀ = 15% × $20 = $3.00

Step 2: Calculate RI₁:
RI₁ = E₁ − r × B₀ = $3.00 − (12% × $20) = $3.00 − $2.40 = $0.60

Step 3: Apply single-stage formula:
V₀ = B₀ + [RI₁ / (r − g)]
V₀ = $20 + [$0.60 / (0.12 − 0.06)]
V₀ = $20 + [$0.60 / 0.06]
V₀ = $20 + $10 = $30

The stock is worth $30, a 50% premium to book value of $20.
P/B = $30 / $20 = 1.5



Page 15
5. The RI Model and Justified P/B Ratio (KEY RELATIONSHIP)

heading: Deriving Justified P/B
body:
From the single-stage RI model, we can derive the justified P/B ratio.

Start with:
V₀ = B₀ + [(ROE − r) × B₀] / (r − g)

Divide both sides by B₀:
V₀ / B₀ = 1 + [(ROE − r) / (r − g)]

JUSTIFIED P/B = 1 + [(ROE − r) / (r − g)]

heading: What Drives P/B?
body:
Driver
Effect on P/B
Logic
↑ ROE
↑ Higher P/B
Higher returns → more
value creation
↑ r (risk)
↓ Lower P/B
Higher discount rate →
lower value
↑ g (growth)
↑ Higher P/B
Faster growth → higher
value


Page 16
CRITICAL: P/B ratio fundamentally depends on (ROE − r). If ROE > r, P/B > 1.
If ROE < r, P/B < 1.

heading: Example: Justified P/B Calculation
body:
Company ABC:
•
ROE = 15%
•
Required return (r) = 11%
•
Growth rate (g) = 7%

Justified P/B = 1 + [(ROE − r) / (r − g)]
Justified P/B = 1 + [(0.15 − 0.11) / (0.11 − 0.07)]
Justified P/B = 1 + [0.04 / 0.04]
Justified P/B = 1 + 1.0 = 2.0

If book value is $25 per share, justified price = 2.0 × $25 = $50



Page 17
6. Multistage Residual Income Models

heading: The General Approach
body:
For companies where RI changes over time (e.g., high growth → stable):

V₀ = B₀ + PV(RI in explicit forecast) + PV(Terminal Value)

heading: Terminal Value Approaches
body:
Three common methods:

Method 1: Persistence Factor
Continuing RI = RI(T) × ω
Terminal Value = [RI(T) × ω] / (1 + r − ω)

Where:
•
ω (omega) = persistence factor (0 to 1)
•
ω = 1: RI persists forever
•
ω = 0: RI drops to zero after forecast period
•
Typical: ω = 0.4 to 0.6 (RI fades over time)

Method 2: Constant Growth
Terminal Value = RI(T+1) / (r − g)

Assumes residual income grows at constant rate g after forecast period.

Method 3: Zero RI (P/B = 1)

Page 18
Terminal Value = 0
V(T) = B(T)

Conservative assumption: Company earns exactly cost of capital in perpetuity, so
trades at book value.

heading: Example: Two-Stage Model
body:
Company XYZ:
•
Current book value: $30
•
High-growth phase: 3 years
•
Expected RI: Year 1 = $4, Year 2 = $5, Year 3 = $6
•
After Year 3: RI grows at 4% forever
•
Required return: 11%

Step 1: PV of explicit RI forecasts:
PV(RI₁) = $4 / 1.11 = $3.604
PV(RI₂) = $5 / 1.11² = $4.058
PV(RI₃) = $6 / 1.11³ = $4.383
Sum = $12.045

Step 2: Terminal value at Year 3:
RI₄ = $6 × 1.04 = $6.24
TV₃ = $6.24 / (0.11 − 0.04) = $89.14
PV(TV₃) = $89.14 / 1.11³ = $65.17

Step 3: Total value:
V₀ = B₀ + PV(explicit RI) + PV(TV)
V₀ = $30 + $12.045 + $65.17 = $107.22



Page 19

Situation

Why

✓ Works well

without needing dividends

quality

heading: Advantages of RI Models
body:
•
Uses readily available accounting data (book value, earnings)
•
Terminal value less significant (present value effect)
•
Focuses on value creation (economic profit concept)
•
Works for firms without dividends or negative FCF
•
Early recognition of value (vs. DDM which loads value into terminal)

heading: Disadvantages of RI Models
body:
•
Requires clean surplus accounting (not always holds)
•
Sensitive to accounting policies and manipulation

Page 20
•
Book value can be distorted (intangibles, off-balance-sheet items)
•
Requires quality financial reporting



Page 21
8. Accounting Adjustments (CRITICAL FOR ACCURACY)
RI models are only as good as the accounting inputs. Adjustments often necessary.

heading: Violations of Clean Surplus
body:
Adjust for items bypassing income statement:
•
Foreign currency translation gains/losses
•
Pension-related adjustments to equity
•
Fair value changes through OCI
•
Revaluation surplus (IFRS)

Treatment: Add back to earnings or adjust beginning book value.

heading: Intangible Assets
body:
•
Goodwill: May need to be removed if not economically valuable
•
R&D: Expensed under GAAP but capitalize under IFRS (adjust for consistency)
•
Internally developed intangibles: Not on balance sheet (adjust book value up)

heading: Off-Balance-Sheet Items
body:
•
Operating leases: Capitalize (IFRS 16/ASC 842)
•
Pension liabilities: Adjust book value for unfunded obligations
•
Special purpose entities: Consolidate if material

(expense more)

(capitalize more)
Higher BV

Goal: Adjust to economic book value (true investment base).
