10. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Must-Memorize Formulas
body:
•
✓ RI = EPS − (r × B(t−1))
•
✓ Clean Surplus: B(t) = B(t−1) + E(t) − D(t)
•
✓ RI Model: V₀ = B₀ + Σ[RI(t) / (1+r)ᵗ]
•
✓ Single-Stage: V₀ = B₀ + [RI₁ / (r−g)]
•
✓ Justified P/B = 1 + [(ROE−r) / (r−g)]

heading: Conceptual Must-Knows
body:
•
RI = economic profit (Net Income minus ALL capital costs)
•
Positive RI → P/B > 1 (value creation)
•
Negative RI → P/B < 1 (value destruction)
•
Clean surplus: ALL earnings flow to book value (except ownership transactions)
•
RI and DDM are equivalent under clean surplus

heading: Common Exam Traps
body:
•
  Forgetting to use BEGINNING book value for equity charge
•
  Using wrong period's book value in RI calculation
•
  Not understanding clean surplus violations
•
  Confusing accounting vs. economic profit
•
  Missing the P/B connection to (ROE − r)

heading: Calculation Checklist
body:
3. Calculate book values for each period (clean surplus)
4. Calculate equity charge for each period (r × Beginning BV)
5. Calculate RI = EPS − Equity Charge
6. Discount RI to present value

Page 25
7. Add current book value

EXAM STRATEGY: Master the CONCEPT first (economic profit, value
creation). Then formulas become logical, not memorized. Understand clean
surplus cold!

END OF STUDY NOTES
Economic profit is the key. Everything else follows.

heading: Ethics: Learning Module 1
body:
CFA LEVEL II 
Ethics: Learning Module 1 
Code of Ethics & Standards of Professional Conduct 
2 Learning Outcomes 
Code + Standards 
3 Updates in 2023 
I(E) · V(B) · VI(A) 
 
LEARNING OUTCOMES & PURPOSE 
 
LM1 has two Learning Outcomes: 
 
LOS 
Outcome 
LOS 1 
Describe the six components of the Code of Ethics and the seven Standards of Professional 
Conduct 
LOS 2 
Explain the ethical responsibilities required of CFA Institute members and candidates by the 
Code and Standards 
 
The Code and Standards are an interwoven tapestry — they must be interpreted as a whole, not as isolated rules. 
The Code contains high-level aspirational principles; the Standards contain the specific practical rules. Together 
they define what CFA Institute expects of members and candidates globally, regardless of job function, cultural 
differences, or local laws. 
 
2023 REVISIONS — THREE CHANGES 
 
In 2023, the CFA Institute Board of Governors approved three changes to the Standards of Professional Conduct. 
These are HIGH-PRIORITY exam items because they are NEW or REVISED. 
 
NEW 
Standard I(E) Competence — NEW STANDARD 
Members and Candidates must act with and maintain the competence necessary to fulfill their 
professional responsibilities. Competence = appropriate knowledge, skills, and abilities for 
one's specific role. No specific CE program required. Role changes require acquiring new 
competencies. Lack of competence ≠ unsuccessful outcome. 
 
REVISED 
Standard V(B) Communication with Clients — REVISED 
Now explicitly requires disclosure of both: (1) the nature of the services provided AND (2) 
the costs to the client associated with those services. Previously only covered disclosure 
of the investment process. 
 
REVISED 
Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — RENAMED & REVISED

heading: Renamed from 'Disclosure of Conflicts' to 'Avoid or Disclose Conflicts.' Now explicitly
body:
Renamed from 'Disclosure of Conflicts' to 'Avoid or Disclose Conflicts.' Now explicitly 
requires members to AVOID conflicts where possible, not only disclose them. Avoiding is 
preferred; when not possible, full disclosure is required. Disclosures must be prominent, 
in plain language, and effectively communicate the information. 
 
! 
All three 2023 changes are prime exam material. Know: I(E) is entirely NEW. V(B) added COST 
disclosure. VI(A) added AVOID — best practice is now to avoid first, disclose when avoidance is 
not reasonable. 
 
WHY ETHICS MATTERS — CONTEXT 
 
Ethics = a set of moral principles that provide guidance for behaviour when it affects others. Fundamental ethical 
principles include: honesty, fairness, diligence, care and respect for others. Ethical conduct balances self-interest 
with the direct and indirect consequences for others. 
 
Concept 
Key Point 
Ethics vs Law 
Legal behaviour = what is required. Ethical behaviour = what is morally correct. 
Ethical principles go beyond legal sufficiency. Compliance with law alone is 
insufficient — members must build a 'culture of integrity.' 
Why ethics matters 
Unethical conduct damages investor trust → reduced capital market participation 
→ less efficient capital allocation → harm to economic growth and society. Each 
participant's actions have systemic effects in an interconnected global market. 
Capital market 
sustainability 
Investment professionals must consider the long-term health of the market in their 
decision-making. Individually innocuous decisions can, in aggregate, precipitate 
market crises. 
Regulatory limits 
Regulations help but cannot cover every ethical situation. Only strong individual 
and firm ethical standards, backed by a genuine culture of integrity, can close the 
gap. 
 
THE SIX COMPONENTS OF THE CODE OF ETHICS 
 
Members of CFA Institute and candidates for the CFA designation MUST: 
 
The Six Code of Ethics Components 
1. Act with integrity, competence, diligence, and respect and in an ethical manner with the public, 
clients, prospective clients, employers, employees, colleagues, and other participants in the global 
capital markets. 
2. Place the integrity of the investment profession and the interests of clients ABOVE their own 
personal interests. 
3. Use reasonable care and exercise independent professional judgment when conducting investment 
analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment actions, and engaging in other 
professional activities. 
4. Practice and encourage others to practice in a professional and ethical manner that will reflect credit 
on themselves and the profession.

heading: 5. Promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets for the ULTIMATE BENE
body:
5. Promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets for the ULTIMATE BENEFIT OF 
SOCIETY. 
6. Maintain and improve their professional competence and strive to maintain and improve the 
competence of other investment professionals. 
 
! 
'For the ultimate benefit of society' — this phrase appears in Code component 5 and in CFA 
Institute's mission statement. It tests whether candidates know the complete wording. 'Integrity 
and viability' NOT just 'integrity and rules.' The correct answer includes BOTH 'viability' AND 'for 
the ultimate benefit of society.' 
 
THE SEVEN STANDARDS OF PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT — 
COMPLETE TEXT 
 
Below are all seven Standards with their full verbatim text as written in the curriculum. The Standards define the 
specific conduct rules that implement the Code of Ethics principles. 
 
Standard I: Professionalism 
 
I(A) 
Knowledge of 
the Law 
Members and Candidates must understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and 
regulations of any government, regulatory organization, licensing agency, or professional 
association governing their professional activities. In the event of conflict, Members and 
Candidates must comply with the MORE STRICT law, rule, or regulation. Members and 
Candidates must not knowingly participate or assist in and must dissociate from any 
violation. 
 
I(B) 
Independence 
and 
Objectivity 
Members and Candidates must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and 
maintain independence and objectivity in their professional activities. Members and 
Candidates must not offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or 
consideration that reasonably could be expected to compromise their own or another's 
independence and objectivity. 
 
I(C) 
Misrepresentation 
Members and Candidates must not knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to 
investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities. 
 
I(D) 
Misconduct 
Members and Candidates must not engage in any professional conduct involving 
dishonesty, fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional 
reputation, integrity, or competence. 
 
I(E) 
Competence 
[NEW 2023] 
Members and Candidates must act with and maintain the competence necessary to fulfill 
their professional responsibilities. 
 
Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

heading: II(A) Material
body:
II(A) Material 
Nonpublic 
Information 
Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the 
value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information. 
 
II(B) Market 
Manipulation 
Members and Candidates must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially 
inflate trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants. 
 
Standard III: Duties to Clients 
 
III(A) Loyalty, 
Prudence, 
and Care 
Members and Candidates have a duty of loyalty to their clients and must act with 
reasonable care and exercise prudent judgment. Members and Candidates must act for the 
benefit of their clients and place their clients' interests before their employer's or their own 
interests. 
 
III(B) Fair 
Dealing 
Members and Candidates must deal FAIRLY and objectively with all clients when providing 
investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or 
engaging in other professional activities. 
 
III(C) 
Suitability 
(1) When in an advisory relationship: (a) Make reasonable inquiry into investment 
experience, risk/return objectives, and financial constraints BEFORE making any 
recommendation. (b) Determine investment is suitable before making recommendation or 
taking action. (c) Judge suitability in context of the client's TOTAL PORTFOLIO. (2) When 
managing to a specific mandate: make only recommendations or take only actions 
consistent with the stated objectives and constraints of the portfolio. 
 
III(D) 
Performance 
Presentation 
When communicating investment performance information, Members and Candidates must 
make reasonable efforts to ensure that it is fair, accurate, and complete. 
 
III(E) 
Preservation 
of 
Confidentiality 
Members and Candidates must keep information about current, former, AND prospective 
clients confidential unless: (1) The information concerns illegal activities on the part of the 
client; (2) Disclosure is required by law; or (3) The client or prospective client permits 
disclosure. 
 
Standard IV: Duties to Employers 
 
IV(A) Loyalty 
In matters related to their employment, Members and Candidates must act for the benefit of 
their employer and not deprive their employer of the advantage of their skills and abilities, 
divulge confidential information, or otherwise cause harm to their employer. 
 
IV(B) 
Additional 
Compensation 
Arrangements 
Members and Candidates must not accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration 
that competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with 
their employer's interest unless they obtain WRITTEN CONSENT from all parties involved.

heading: IV(C)
body:
IV(C) 
Responsibilities 
of Supervisors 
Members and Candidates must make reasonable efforts to ensure that anyone subject to 
their supervision or authority complies with applicable laws, rules, regulations, and the 
Code and Standards. 
 
Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions 
 
V(A) 
Diligence and 
Reasonable 
Basis 
Members and Candidates must: (1) Exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness 
in analyzing investments, making investment recommendations, and taking investment 
actions. (2) Have a reasonable and adequate basis, supported by appropriate research and 
investigation, for any investment analysis, recommendation, or action. 
 
V(B) 
Communication 
with Clients 
[REVISED 2023] 
Members and Candidates must: (1) Disclose the nature of the services provided AND 
costs to the client. (2) Disclose the basic format and general principles of investment 
processes and promptly disclose material changes. (3) Disclose significant limitations 
and risks. (4) Use reasonable judgment in identifying important factors and include them 
in communications. (5) Distinguish between FACT and OPINION. 
 
V(C) Record 
Retention 
Members and Candidates must develop and maintain appropriate records to support their 
investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related 
communications with clients and prospective clients. 
 
Standard VI: Conflicts of Interest 
 
VI(A) Avoid or 
Disclose 
Conflicts 
[REVISED 
2023] 
Members and Candidates must AVOID OR make full and fair disclosure of all matters that 
could reasonably be expected to impair their independence and objectivity or interfere with 
respective duties to their clients, prospective clients, and employer. Disclosures must be 
prominent, in plain language, and communicate the relevant information effectively. 
 
VI(B) Priority 
of 
Transactions 
Investment transactions for clients and employers must have priority over investment 
transactions in which a Member or Candidate is the beneficial owner. 
 
VI(C) Referral 
Fees 
Members and Candidates must disclose to their employer, clients, and prospective clients, 
as appropriate, any compensation, consideration, or benefit received from or paid to others 
for the recommendation of products or services. 
 
Standard VII: Responsibilities as a CFA Institute Member or CFA Candidate 
 
VII(A) 
Conduct as 
Participants 
in CFA 
Institute 
Programs 
Members and Candidates must not engage in any conduct that compromises the 
reputation or integrity of CFA Institute or the CFA designation or the integrity, validity, or 
security of CFA Institute programs.

heading: VII(B)
body:
VII(B) 
Reference to 
CFA Institute, 
the CFA 
Designation, 
and the CFA 
Program 
When referring to CFA Institute, CFA Institute membership, the CFA designation, or 
candidacy in the CFA Program, Members and Candidates must not misrepresent or 
exaggerate the meaning or implications of membership in CFA Institute, holding the CFA 
designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program. 
 
PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT PROGRAM (PCP) 
 
The PCP enforces the Code and Standards. The DRC (Disciplinary Review Committee) — volunteer CFA 
charterholders — reviews conduct and sets policy. 
 
Phase 
Detail 
Sources of inquiry 
(1) Self-disclosure on annual Professional Conduct Statement (civil litigation, criminal 
investigation, written complaint); (2) Written complaints to CFA Institute; (3) 
Media/regulatory notices or public sources; (4) Proctor reports on exam day; (5) Post-
exam analysis; (6) Social media monitoring for exam content disclosure. 
Investigation 
Staff may: request written explanation; interview member/candidate, complaining 
parties, and third parties; collect documents. Outcome: no sanction, cautionary letter, or 
referral to DRC. 
DRC Panel 
If member/candidate does NOT accept charges/proposed sanction → matter referred 
to DRC panel. Panel reviews materials from staff AND from member/candidate. Panel 
determines: (a) was there a violation? (b) what sanction? 
Sanctions 
Public censure; suspension of membership and use of CFA designation; revocation of 
CFA charter. Candidates may be suspended or prohibited from further participation in 
the CFA Program. 
 
! 
PCP Exam traps: (1) Inquiries CAN come from media/regulatory sources — not only self-
disclosure. (2) DRC panels only convene if member REJECTS charges — not for every case. (3) 
Investigations 'may include' requesting written explanation — this is the most frequently tested 
correct answer about the PCP process. 
 
KEY EXAM TRAPS — LM1 
 
! 
'Fairly' not 'equally' under Standard III(B). Members must deal FAIRLY — they are not required to 
treat all clients identically. Different levels of service are permitted if disclosed and available to all. 
 
! 
Standard III(C) — advisory vs. mandate: If managing a POOLED fund to a mandate, the manager 
is NOT responsible for suitability for each individual investor buying fund shares. Suitability 
applies only in an advisory relationship. 
 
! 
Standard III(E) covers CURRENT, FORMER, AND PROSPECTIVE clients — not just current. 
Confidentiality obligation continues after the relationship ends.

! 
Standard IV(A) Loyalty: The standard applies to EMPLOYMENT matters only — not a blanket 
obligation to place employer above personal/family obligations in all areas of life. 
 
! 
Standard VI(A): The 2023 revision added AVOID — best practice is to AVOID conflicts where 
possible. When it is not reasonable to avoid, THEN disclose. The previous standard only required 
disclosure. 
 
! 
Standard VII(A): Candidates MAY express opinions about CFA Institute and the CFA Program. 
What they CANNOT do is disclose specific exam content — topics tested, questions, formulas 
tested or not tested. 
 
! 
Standard VII(B): A candidate may NOT imply partial designation (e.g., 'CFA Level II' on a 
business card is WRONG). Candidates MAY state they have passed Level I or Level II, and MAY 
state they are enrolled as a Level III candidate. They CANNOT say 'CFA Expected 2026.'

heading: Standard I — PROFESSIONALISM
body:
Standard I(A) — Knowledge of the Law

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and
regulations (including the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional
Conduct) of any government, regulatory organization, licensing agency, or professional
association governing their professional activities. In the event of conflict, Members and
Candidates must comply with the more strict law, rule, or regulation. Members and
Candidates must not knowingly participate or assist in and must dissociate from any violation
of such laws, rules, or regulations.

GUIDANCE

The standard requires members and candidates to understand the applicable laws and regulations of all countries
and jurisdictions where they engage in professional activities. Professional activities include — but are not limited
to — trading securities, providing investment advice, conducting research, and performing other investment
services. Members and candidates need not become compliance experts, but they must be sufficiently informed
to identify potential legal and ethical issues and know their firm's procedures for accessing compliance guidance.

During periods of regulatory change, members must remain especially vigilant. New financial products, evolving
communication technologies (social media, mobile apps), and uncovered ethical missteps continuously reshape
the regulatory environment. Members and candidates must update their procedures and practices to remain
compliant.

RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN CODE AND STANDARDS AND APPLICABLE LAW

When applicable law and the Code and Standards require different conduct, members and candidates must follow
the MORE STRICT requirement. The 'applicable law' is determined by the specific facts and circumstances of the
member's activities. The 'more strict' standard imposes greater restrictions or calls for greater action to protect
investors.

Scenario
Applicable Law
Required Action
Why
Member in NS (no securities law),
does business in LS country
LS law applies
Follow Code and
Standards
Applicable law is less
strict than the Code
and Standards

business in MS country
MS law applies
Follow MS country
law

stricter than Code and
Standards

business in NS country

Follow Code and

strict

business in NS; LS law says use
locality law
No locality law

Standards

law, so Code and
Standards apply

business in MS; LS law says use
locality law

stricter than Code and

less strict, so Code
and Standards apply

business in LS with LS citizen; MS
says client home country governs
LS home country law
governs

Standards

law is LS = less strict
Member in MS country, does
business in LS with MS citizen; MS
says client home country governs
MS home country
law governs
Follow MS country
law
Client home country
law is MS = stricter

PARTICIPATION IN / ASSOCIATION WITH VIOLATIONS

Members are responsible for violations in which they KNOWINGLY participate or assist. 'Knowingly' includes what
the member 'knew or should have known.' If a member has reasonable grounds to believe that imminent or
ongoing employer/client activities are illegal or unethical, the member must dissociate from those activities.

DISSOCIATION PROCESS — STEPS WHEN VIOLATIONS OCCUR
Step 1: Attempt to stop the behaviour by bringing it to the attention of the employer — through a
supervisor or the firm's compliance department.
Step 2: If Step 1 is unsuccessful — DISSOCIATE from the activity. This may mean: removing one's
name from written reports, asking for a different assignment, refusing to advise a current client, or
refusing a new client.
Step 3: In extreme cases, dissociation may require resignation from employment.
Whistleblowing: The Code and Standards do NOT compel reporting to governmental or regulatory
organisations unless applicable law requires it. Voluntary whistleblowing may be prudent — consult
legal and compliance advisors.
WARNING: Inaction combined with continuing association with those involved in illegal or unethical
conduct may be construed as participation or assistance in that conduct.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES — MEMBERS AND CANDIDATES

Standard I(A) Recommended Procedures
Stay informed: Establish — or encourage the firm to establish — procedures by which employees are
regularly updated on changes in applicable laws, rules, regulations, and case law. Participate in
continuing education or internal/external training programs.
Review procedures: Periodically review the firm's compliance procedures to ensure they reflect
current law and adequately guide employees on permissible conduct.

Maintain current files: Keep readily accessible copies of applicable statutes, rules, regulations, and
important cases relevant to professional activities.
Understand distribution area laws: Make reasonable efforts to understand applicable laws (national
and regional) for all countries where investment products are developed and distributed.
Seek legal counsel: When in doubt about appropriate action, seek advice from compliance personnel
or legal counsel. Also seek advice if a fellow employee appears to be committing a potential violation.
Document and dissociate: When dissociating from a violation, document the situation and urge the
firm to persuade the perpetrator(s) to cease the conduct.
Firms — develop a code of ethics; provide information on applicable laws; establish procedures for
reporting violations.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example 1 — Appropriate Reporting (Allen)
Michael Allen is a broker responsible for a securities underwriting. A company official reveals that
the financial statements overstate the issuer's earnings. Allen's general counsel says it would be
'difficult to prove wrongdoing.' COMPLIANT ACTION: Allen reports to his supervisor, seeks
independent legal opinion, and determines whether the regulator should be notified. Relying on
counsel's view that wrongdoing is hard to prove does NOT absolve Allen of his responsibility to
act.

✓
Example 2 — Appropriate Dissociation (Collins)
James Collins works in a developing country with minimal securities laws and no prohibition
against insider trading. He is an analyst who accidentally comes into possession of material
nonpublic information. COMPLIANT: Collins follows Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information
— because the Code and Standards impose a higher standard than the applicable local law,
Collins must abide by the Code and Standards.

✓
Example 3 — Reporting Unethical Activity (Blume)
Krista Blume notices a broker receiving 'research' payments despite the portfolio not investing in
the broker's country and no research actually coming from the broker. COMPLIANT: She follows
firm procedures, discusses concerns with her supervisor or compliance department, and
advocates for disclosure of the arrangement between the new broker relationship and the
research payments.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example 1 — Using Promotional Material with Inflated Performance (Washington)
Kamisha Washington discovers her firm's composite omits terminated accounts, inflating the 10-
year performance figure — even though the description claims all accounts are included. Her firm
wants her to use this material when soliciting clients. VIOLATION: Using the inflated performance
number would violate Standard I(A) through association with a violation, even though Washington
did not create the misrepresentation. She must refuse to use the material and report the issue to
compliance.


✗
Example 2 — Ignoring Applicable Local Law (Jameson)
Laura Jameson is a US citizen working in Karramba as a registered investment adviser.
Karrambian law prohibits registered advisers from personally participating in IPOs. Jameson —
believing US law applies because her firm is US-based — ignores this local prohibition.
VIOLATION: As a registered adviser in Karramba, she must comply with the stricter local law,
regardless of her home country's law.

✗
Example 3 — Failure to Maintain Knowledge (White)
Colleen White begins posting investment information and recommendations to Facebook and X.
Prior to this, her local regulator had issued guidance governing online electronic communications.
White's communications conflict with the new guidance. VIOLATION of Standard I(A): She must
be aware of evolving legal requirements governing her professional activities, including new social
media regulations.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Relying on counsel's advice: Legal counsel saying an action is 'hard to prove' does
NOT absolve a member of responsibility to comply with the law. Allen must still report and
potentially sever the connection. Counsel's opinion is informative, not determinative.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: JURISDICTION RULE: Always apply the MORE STRICT of applicable law vs. Code
and Standards. 'More strict' = imposes greater restrictions OR requires greater protections for
investors. This is the fundamental rule tested repeatedly.


Standard I(B) — Independence and Objectivity

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain
independence and objectivity in their professional activities. Members and Candidates must
not offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that reasonably
could be expected to compromise their own or another's independence and objectivity.

GUIDANCE

Standard I(B) requires members and candidates to maintain independence and objectivity so that their clients
benefit from work and opinions unaffected by any conflict of interest. External parties — corporations, investment
banks, buy-side managers, credit rating clients — may attempt to influence the investment process through gifts,
lavish entertainment, IPO allocations, and research fee structures. Even subtle pressures must be resisted.

Members are personally responsible for maintaining independence when preparing research reports, making
recommendations, and taking investment actions. Recommendations must convey the member's TRUE opinion,
free from internal or external pressure, stated in clear and unambiguous language.

Pressure Source
Guidance
Gifts & entertainment
from third parties
Modest gifts and ordinary business entertainment are acceptable. Lavish
gifts/entertainment that could compromise independence must be rejected.

BEST PRACTICE: reject any offer that COULD BE EXPECTED to threaten
independence. A flat value limit (per gift or aggregate annual) should be set by
the firm based on local customs.
Gifts from existing clients
Less problematic than third-party gifts because a compensation arrangement
already exists. STILL requires disclosure to employer BEFORE acceptance if
possible; if not possible before, must disclose promptly after. Disclosure allows
the employer to monitor for favouritism.
IPO allocations to
personal accounts
PROHIBITED. Accepting oversubscribed IPO shares in a personal account from
companies seeking research coverage fundamentally compromises
independence.
Buy-side pressure on sell-
side analysts
Portfolio managers must NOT threaten or engage in retaliation against sell-side
analysts who issue negative ratings. Even implicit pressure — the perception
that a reprisal is possible — is improper and can make it difficult for analysts to
remain objective.
Investment banking
relationships
IB personnel must NOT have authority to approve, disapprove, or change
research reports. Analyst compensation must NOT be linked to specific IB
deals. Firewalls required between IB and research. Separate reporting
structures for both groups.
Issuer-paid research
Analysts must conduct thorough, independent, unbiased analysis.
Compensation that could influence the report includes: (a) fees tied to
conclusions, (b) stock warrants or equity instruments that increase in value with
positive coverage. ONLY a flat fee NOT linked to conclusions or
recommendations is acceptable. Full disclosure of the arrangement is
mandatory.
Travel funding
BEST PRACTICE: always use commercial transport at own/firm expense. May
accept modestly arranged travel ONLY when commercial transport is
unavailable (e.g., remote property tour). Corporate or chartered flights must not
be the norm.
Manager selection / pay-
to-play
Must NOT solicit gifts, contributions, or other compensation when hiring
managers. Must NOT offer gifts when trying to win mandates. Requesting
contributions to a charity or political organisation on behalf of a hiring
representative = violation.
Credit rating agencies
Must develop firewalls between rating operations and advisory/consulting
businesses. Must prevent undue influence from the company sponsoring the
rating. Analysts may need to independently validate ratings when conflicts of
interest exist.
Performance
measurement analysts
Must not allow internal or external influences to affect their independence when
completing performance calculations. Cannot alter composite construction or
exclude accounts at the request of management.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard I(B) Recommended Procedures
Protect integrity of opinions: All research reports must reflect the analyst's unbiased opinion.
Compensation systems must protect research independence — analyst remuneration must not be
directly linked to IB assignments.
Create a restricted list: If the firm is unwilling to allow adverse opinions on a corporate client, remove
the company from the research universe and place it on a restricted list (factual information only, no
recommendations).

Restrict special cost arrangements: Pay commercial transport and hotel at own/firm expense when
visiting issuers. No corporate aircraft reimbursement unless commercial transport is unavailable.
Issuers should not always host the member.
Limit gifts: Strictly limit acceptance to token items. Firm should set a clear value limit per gift or as an
aggregate annual amount. Token gifts for customary business purposes are acceptable.
Restrict investments: Formal policy for employee purchases of equity and equity-related IPOs. Prior
approval required. Strict limits on private placement participation.
Independence policy: Formal written policy stating analysts do not report to, and are not supervised or
controlled by, any department that could compromise their independence (e.g., IB, sales).
Appointed compliance officer: Senior officer with oversight responsibilities for ethics compliance, with
clear procedures for reporting potentially unethical behaviour.
Review procedures: Supervisory and review procedures to ensure analysts and portfolio managers
comply with personal investment policies.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Taylor's Mining Trip (Travel Not a Violation)
Steven Taylor accepts a chartered group flight and modest motel accommodation arranged by
Precision Metals for a multi-site mining tour. The trip is strictly for business, the itinerary required
chartered flights (no alternative), and accommodations were Spartan. Taylor's objectivity was not
compromised. COMPLIANT: The travel arrangements were necessary and modest, and the trip
served a legitimate business purpose.

✓
Example — Thompson's Stress-Testing (Resisting Market Pressure)
Bob Thompson insists on running a worst-case housing scenario (−10%, −5%, 0%) in his
subprime mortgage analysis, against the portfolio manager's preference to limit analysis to
historical norms. His analysis leads to a recommendation NOT to invest. The housing market
subsequently collapsed. COMPLIANT: Thompson maintained his independence against internal
pressure to limit the analysis to scenarios comfortable for the portfolio manager.

✓
Example — Green's Client Gift (Disclosed Bonus)
Theresa Green achieves consistent outperformance for client Ian Knowlden. Knowlden offers her
two Wimbledon tickets and use of his London flat. Green discloses the gift to her supervisor at
Tisbury Investments. COMPLIANT: Gifts from clients in an existing compensation relationship are
less problematic; disclosure to the employer is what makes this compliant. The employer can then
monitor for any undue favouritism.

✓
Example — Analyst Refuses Price Increase (Objectivity Under Sales Pressure)
Lindsey Warner is pressured by her sales desk to push Milton Ltd. bonds because the firm has a
large inventory position after Milton announced an operating problem. Warner must not push the
bonds unless she can justify that the market price has already fully adjusted for the operating
problem. COMPLIANT when: Warner refuses to adjust her opinion unless the analysis
independently justifies doing so.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES


✗
Example — Grant's World Cup Trip (Lavish Gift)
Edward Grant accepts two World Cup tickets, two nights at a resort, several meals, and limousine
transportation from a brokerage house in appreciation for directing commission business to it.
Grant does not disclose this to his supervisor. VIOLATION of I(B): The package is substantial and
could impair Grant's independence in allocating commission business. Even the appearance of a
conflict is sufficient to violate this standard.

✗
Example — Herrero's Issuer-Paid Research (Contingent Compensation)
Javier Herrero is hired to write a research report on an educational software company. The
investor-relations firm offers a flat fee PLUS a bonus if new investors buy stock as a result of the
report. VIOLATION of I(B): The bonus arrangement incentivises a positive recommendation
regardless of the facts, compromising independence and objectivity. Only a flat fee not tied to
conclusions is acceptable.

✗
Example — Wade's Service Fee Arrangement (Ongoing Bias)
Bob Wade accepts a 0.25% service fee from Western Funds without disclosing it to clients, then
instructs his team to exclusively promote Western's funds. Two years later, when Western begins
to underperform, Wade continues to push the funds to meet his profitability targets. VIOLATION:
The ongoing fee arrangement has affected the objectivity of his recommendations to the detriment
of clients.

✗
Example — Mandel's Campaign Contributions (Pay-to-Play)
Adrian Mandel makes significant monetary contributions to a union leader's reelection campaign
to improve ZZYY Capital's chances of winning a pension mandate — and continues contributions
to maintain the position. VIOLATION of I(B): Offering monetary gifts intended to influence the
hiring decision compromises the independence of the hiring representative.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Client gifts vs. third-party gifts: Gifts from clients are LESS problematic than third-
party gifts because a compensation arrangement already exists. However, disclosure to the
employer is STILL required. Lavish gifts from clients that could affect objectivity should still be
rejected.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Issuer-paid research: ANY compensation that could influence the report — stock
warrants, equity instruments, fees contingent on conclusions or recommendations — violates I(B).
ONLY a pre-negotiated flat fee not tied to conclusions complies.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Restricted list: If a firm cannot permit adverse opinions on a client (due to IB
relationships etc.), the correct solution is to REMOVE the company from the research universe
and put it on a restricted list — not to issue an artificially positive report.


Standard I(C) — Misrepresentation

EXACT STANDARD WORDING

Members and Candidates must not knowingly make any misrepresentations relating to
investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities.

GUIDANCE

A misrepresentation is any UNTRUE STATEMENT, any OMISSION OF FACT, or any statement that is otherwise
false or misleading — whether written, oral, or electronic. 'Knowingly' = the member or candidate either KNEW or
SHOULD HAVE KNOWN that the misrepresentation was being made or that the omitted information could alter
investment decisions.

Written materials include research reports, underwriting documents, market letters, newspaper columns, books,
and webpages. Electronic communications include internet posts, mobile apps, and emails. Members who publish
webpages must regularly monitor them to ensure information remains current and accurate.

Type of
Misrepresentation
Core Requirements and Guidance
Qualifications and
credentials
Cannot misrepresent own qualifications, firm's qualifications, or credentials of
any third party. Includes errors in promotional material that are allowed to persist
— if the member uses materials numerous times over extended periods and
'should have known' of the error, it constitutes a violation even without
intentional misrepresentation.
Investment guarantees
Cannot guarantee specific returns or capital preservation on volatile investments
(e.g., 'I guarantee you will earn 8% on equities'). EXCEPTION: Products with
guarantees built into their structure (e.g., FDIC-insured deposits within insured
limits, or where an institution has contractually agreed to cover losses) may use
the word 'guaranteed' accurately.
Performance records
Cherry-picking composites is a violation. Cannot omit terminated accounts
without disclosure. Simulated/backtested results must be clearly identified as
such — they cannot be presented as actual performance.
Benchmarks
Must use the most appropriate available benchmark. Cannot shop for higher
valuations by switching pricing providers. If a non-standard benchmark is used
(e.g., 'cash plus' for a hedge fund), must disclose the reason to avoid misleading
clients.
Social media
Members must follow the same standards on social media as in all other
communications. Perceived anonymity does not excuse misrepresentations.
Online pseudonyms tagged with 'CFA' are a violation of both I(C) and VII(B).
Plagiarism
Copying or using materials prepared by others without acknowledgement;
distributing third-party research as own work; using others' models without
credit; changing a few words and signing the report; citing 'leading analysts'
without naming them; using charts/graphs without stating sources. Must
acknowledge ALL sources of ideas, data, models, charts, and methodologies.
Omissions
Knowingly omitting information that alters investment decisions =
misrepresentation. Model outputs cannot be presented as 'facts' — they are
expected results based on inputs and assumptions. Cherry-picking composite
accounts to omit negative performance = misrepresentation.
Third-party research
Members may use and distribute outsourced third-party research as long as
they do NOT represent themselves as the author. Must disclose that the
research comes from an external source. They add value by identifying and
synthesising the best research.

Work completed for
employer
Research and models developed while employed at a firm are the FIRM'S
property. The firm may continue to use the work and issue future reports without
attributing them to the departed analyst. The departed analyst CANNOT reissue
a previously released report solely under their own name.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard I(C) Recommended Procedures
Factual presentations: Understand the limits of the firm's and individual's capabilities. Be accurate and
complete in all presentations. Firms should provide a written list of available services and descriptions
of qualifications, and designate which employees may speak on behalf of the firm.
Qualification summary: Each member should prepare a personal summary of qualifications,
experience, and services they can competently perform. Firms should periodically review employee
correspondence for misrepresentations.
Verify outside information: Share responsibility for the accuracy of third-party marketing and
distribution materials. Encourage firms to develop procedures to verify third-party information.
Maintain webpages: Regularly monitor posted materials for currency, accuracy, and completeness.
Take reasonable precautions to protect the site's integrity and security.
Plagiarism policy — Maintain copies of all research materials relied upon.
Plagiarism policy — Attribute to sources any direct quotations, projections, tables, statistics, model
ideas, and new methodologies prepared by others.
Plagiarism policy — Attribute to sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by
others.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Yao Corrects Typographical Error
Hijan Yao distributes a performance presentation that states the composite has ¥350 billion in
assets when the actual figure is ¥35 billion — a typographical error. Once discovered, he
immediately ceases distribution and corrects the error. COMPLIANT: Yao did not KNOWINGLY
make the misrepresentation. Prompt correction of unintentional errors does NOT constitute a
violation of Standard I(C).

✓
Example — Smith Declines Complex Securities (Avoiding Misrepresentation)
Trina Smith concludes that she and her team cannot effectively evaluate highly structured
mortgages, as each deal is unique and requires specialised vendor software they do not have. To
avoid misrepresenting their understanding, the team decides to limit exposure to this sector.
COMPLIANT: By not investing in securities she cannot adequately understand and describe to
beneficiaries, Smith avoids potentially misrepresenting the risk and return profile.

✓
Example — Distributing Third-Party Research (Paul Ostrowski Corrected)
A compliant approach: Paul Ostrowski subscribes to third-party research and distributes it to
clients — but DISCLOSES clearly that the research comes from an external firm and that he is
providing value by selecting the best available research from multiple sources. COMPLIANT if: the
member does not imply authorship of the research.


VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Muhammad's Promotional Material (Known Error Not Corrected)
Syed Muhammad's firm distributes promotional material incorrectly claiming he has an advanced
degree. Although he attended the school briefly, he never received a degree. He and others in the
firm distribute this material to clients and consultants over an extended period. VIOLATION:
Muhammad used the material numerous times and 'should have known' of the misrepresentation
— the standard of 'knew or should have known' is met even without deliberate deception.

✗
Example — Marks Describes IOs as 'Guaranteed by US Government'
Ricki Marks sells interest-only mortgage strips (IOs) to pension plan clients, describing them as
'guaranteed by the US government.' Although the underlying mortgages are government-backed,
the interest stream on the IOs is NOT guaranteed. When interest rates drop, prepayments
increase and clients lose a portion of their investment. VIOLATION: Marks materially
misrepresented the character of the investment.

✗
Example — Swanson Plagiarises Prior Firm's Report
Steve Swanson is told to 'take the Davis report, change a few words, sign your name, and get it
out.' VIOLATION if Swanson complies: He may reference Davis's portions with attribution and add
his own analysis and conclusions — but cannot simply change a few words and sign the report as
his own.

✗
Example — Finch Distributes Outdated Fund Fact Sheets
David Finch knows that one fund's fact sheet is outdated — the fund recently approved the use of
significant leverage — but continues to distribute the old sheets because the bank has no control
over the fund firms' marketing materials. VIOLATION of I(C): Finch has an obligation to ensure his
team and clients have current information about the funds being offered.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Unintentional vs. knowing misrepresentation: An unintentional error that is promptly
corrected is NOT a violation. However, 'knowingly' includes what the member 'should have known.'
Distributing erroneous materials over an extended period without correction eventually becomes a
violation even without initial intent to deceive.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Plagiarism applies to oral communications too — group meetings, audio/video
media, and electronic data transfer. It is not limited to written research reports.


Standard I(D) — Misconduct

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must not engage in any professional conduct involving dishonesty,
fraud, or deceit or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation,
integrity, or competence.


GUIDANCE

Standard I(D) addresses all conduct that reflects poorly on professional integrity, reputation, or competence — not
merely illegal acts. The standard applies when conduct: (1) involves lying, cheating, stealing, or other dishonest
conduct related to professional activities; (2) damages trustworthiness or competence; or (3) negatively affects
the member's ability to perform professional responsibilities.

The standard also covers the absence of appropriate conduct — failure to perform necessary due diligence may
damage the trust clients place in members and violate this standard. The investment profession is built on trust;
failing to meet the fundamental duty to understand the nature and risks of investments before recommending
them may violate I(D).

Situation
Violation or Not?
Alcohol abuse during business
hours, clearly intoxicated at work
VIOLATION — negatively affects ability to fulfil professional
responsibilities; reflects on professionalism and competence
Fraudulent expense claims
(altering health insurance bills,
reimbursement fraud)
VIOLATION — intentional conduct involving fraud and deceit in the
workplace, adversely reflecting on integrity
Participation in charity fraud for
personal benefit
VIOLATION — involves dishonesty, fraud, and misrepresentation even if
outside core investment activities
Arrests for nonviolent civil
disobedience in support of
personal beliefs (e.g.,
environmental protests)
NOT a violation — Standard I(D) generally does not cover legal
transgressions from acts of civil disobedience that do not reflect on
professional reputation, integrity, or competence
Personal bankruptcy
NOT automatically a violation — but if the bankruptcy involves fraudulent
or deceitful business conduct, it may constitute a violation
Failure to conduct required due
diligence before making
investment recommendations
VIOLATION — absence of appropriate conduct; loss of client trust; harm
to capital market integrity

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard I(D) Recommended Procedures
Code of ethics: Firms should adopt a code of ethics to which every employee subscribes, making
clear that personal behaviour reflecting poorly on the individual, institution, or investment industry will
not be tolerated.
List of violations: Disseminate to all employees a list of potential violations and associated disciplinary
sanctions, up to and including dismissal.
Employee references: Check references of potential hires to ensure they are of good character and
not ineligible to work in the investment industry due to past infractions.

COMPLIANCE AND VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Sasserman's Intoxication at Work

Simon Sasserman lunches daily at the country club, clearly intoxicated back at work in the
afternoon. Colleagues avoid doing business with him after lunch. VIOLATION: Excessive drinking
and subsequent intoxication raise serious questions about his professionalism and competence,
reflecting poorly on him, his employer, and the investment industry.

✗
Example — Hoffman's Fraudulent Expense Claims
Howard Hoffman submits more than two dozen altered health insurance reimbursement forms
over two years, inflating the amounts claimed. VIOLATION: Intentional conduct involving fraud and
deceit, adversely reflecting on his integrity.

✗
Example — Brink's Charity Fraud
Jody Brink, while volunteering to handle vehicle purchases for a charity, agrees to a price 20%
above market and splits the excess with a friend — effectively defrauding the charity. VIOLATION:
Involves dishonesty, fraud, and misrepresentation, even though the conduct relates to non-
investment charitable activities.

✓
Example — Garcia's Civil Disobedience
Carmen Garcia manages a socially responsible investment fund and has been arrested on several
occasions for nonviolent environmental protests. COMPLIANT: Standard I(D) is generally not
intended to cover legal transgressions resulting from acts of civil disobedience in support of
personal beliefs, as such conduct does not reflect on professional integrity or competence.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(D) covers professional integrity broadly — not just illegal acts. Conduct need not
be illegal to violate this standard. The test is whether the conduct reflects adversely on
professional reputation, integrity, OR competence.


Standard I(E) — Competence [NEW STANDARD — 2023]

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must act with and maintain the competence necessary to fulfill their
professional responsibilities.

GUIDANCE

Standard I(E) requires members and candidates to BOTH act with AND maintain appropriate knowledge, skills,
and diligence. The Code of Ethics already requires 'acting with competence and diligence' — Standard I(E)
directly supports this requirement.

Competence is defined as having sufficient knowledge, skills, and abilities suitable for a professional to work in a
specific role with success:

Component
Definition

Knowledge
The body of information applied directly to the performance of a function and how
effectively it is applied
Skills
Capabilities to perform role-specific acts or functions to complete specific tasks and
achieve professional goals
Abilities
Capabilities and attitudes that support behaviours that result in observable outcomes

KEY POINTS ON COMPETENCE
Competence is ROLE-SPECIFIC: A highly educated consultant may not be competent to undertake
activities in an unfamiliar area. An accounting expert may not be competent in GIPS compliance. A
regulatory attorney may not be competent in sales licensing rules without study.
Failure does NOT equal incompetence: A competent investment professional may make unprofitable
decisions or fail to predict future events accurately. Incompetence is determined by lack of required
knowledge/skills/abilities — NOT by negative outcomes.
Role changes require new competencies: When responsibilities expand or change, members must
acquire the necessary competencies BEFORE undertaking the new role. Simply passing a licensing
exam is insufficient if the material was not actually learned.
No specific CE program mandated: Members can maintain competence through: (a) CE programs; (b)
professional certifications; (c) conferences/seminars/webinars; (d) employer training; (e) self-study
and reading; (f) expert groups/organisations; (g) learning new skills as responsibilities change.
Client urgency does NOT override competence: Members must not invest in unfamiliar assets simply
because clients are eager or a trend is popular, if they lack the competence to evaluate those assets.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard I(E) Recommended Procedures
Regularly engage in a professional development or continuing education programme
Study for or earn professional certifications or designations relevant to current and prospective roles
Attend conferences, seminars, or webinars to stay current with developments
Regularly participate in training offered by the employer
Engage in informal continuing education through outside reading of subject matter articles and
publications
Participate in expert groups or professional organisations
Become proficient with new skills or knowledge when professional responsibilities change

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Lee Stays Current (Competence Maintained)
Michelle Lee runs a geopolitical consulting firm. When a major trade deal is ratified, she
immediately downloads and reads the full agreement, then consults with experts to confirm her
understanding. COMPLIANT: She actively maintained competence by staying current with
relevant developments through direct study and expert consultation.


✓
Example — Choe Extends Competence (ESG)
Chris Choe's clients request ESG-integrated research. Choe hires an experienced ESG analyst
and requires all analysts (including herself) to attend ESG seminars and earn an ESG specialist
certificate BEFORE incorporating ESG factors into reports. COMPLIANT: She extended the
team's competence before undertaking the new analysis responsibility.

✓
Example — Mifune's Insurance Recommendation
Kwame Mifune thoroughly researches Vouchsafe Insurance — high credit rating, strong financials,
decade-long track record — before recommending their products to 40 clients. Vouchsafe later
goes bankrupt due to previously undisclosed CFO fraud that had misled the entire industry.
COMPLIANT: Mifune's failure to uncover a fraud that misled the investment industry as a whole
does not indicate incompetence, given his thorough and appropriate research.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Glusker Passes Exam Without Learning Material
Gerhardt Glusker, a regulatory attorney transitioning to a sales role, is required to pass a licensing
exam covering client fairness rules he was unfamiliar with. He does not study or learn the material,
relying on industry knowledge to pass. VIOLATION: Passing the exam alone does not
demonstrate competency if the required knowledge was not actually acquired. He does not
possess the knowledge needed to serve in his new sales role.

✗
Example — Evans Promoted Without Acquiring Management Skills
Linda Evans is promoted to Director of Research but spends no time learning people
management, team leadership, or compliance requirements for subordinates — only getting up to
speed on existing research projects. VIOLATION: Her new role demands competence in
management and supervisory skills she has not acquired. She must obtain these before
discharging her new supervisory responsibilities.

✗
Example — Halsey Invests in Crypto Without Understanding
Alex Halsey, pressured by clients eager not to miss a 'hot trend', purchases a heavily promoted
cryptocurrency product for client accounts despite being unfamiliar with the asset class, its nature,
or the specific products available. VIOLATION of I(E): Hastily investing in products he cannot
competently evaluate violates the competence standard.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Passing a licensing exam does NOT by itself demonstrate competency if the
material was not genuinely learned. Competence requires actually possessing the required
knowledge and skills — not merely clearing a regulatory hurdle.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: No specific CE programme is required under I(E). The standard is outcome-based:
do you HAVE the required knowledge, skills, and abilities for your role? How you maintain them is
up to you.

section_title: Standard II — INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS
heading: Standard II — INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS
body:
Standard II(A) — Material Nonpublic Information

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the
value of an investment must not act or cause others to act on the information.

GUIDANCE

Trading on or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information (MNPI) erodes confidence in capital
markets by supporting the perception that insiders can take unfair advantage of the public. The prohibition
extends beyond securities to derivatives (swaps, options), mutual funds, and other alternative investments. ANY
trading based on MNPI constitutes a violation.

WHAT IS 'MATERIAL' INFORMATION?

Information is material if: (1) its disclosure would PROBABLY have an impact on the price of a security, OR (2) a
REASONABLE INVESTOR would want to know it before making an investment decision. Key factors: specificity,
extent of difference from public information, nature, and reliability.

EXAMPLES OF MATERIAL INFORMATION
Earnings announcements and earnings guidance
Mergers, acquisitions, tender offers, or joint ventures
Changes in assets or asset quality
Innovative products, new product trials, new licences, patents, or regulatory approvals/rejections
Developments regarding customers or suppliers (acquisition or loss of a major contract)
Changes in management or key personnel
Events regarding securities (defaults, calls for redemption, repurchase plans, stock splits, dividend
changes)
Bankruptcies and significant legal disputes
Large trade orders pending execution
New or changing equity or debt ratings issued by a third party (analyst recommendations, credit
ratings)
Government reports of significant economic trends (employment, housing starts, currency data)

LESS MATERIAL: Information from unreliable sources. Information with ambiguous price effects. Information that
has grown stale. A specialist outside the trials making educated conjectures (vs. personnel directly involved in
trials).


WHAT IS 'NONPUBLIC' INFORMATION?

Information is nonpublic until it has been DISSEMINATED or made available to the MARKETPLACE IN
GENERAL (not just a select group). Key points:

NONPUBLIC — CORE RULES
A briefing to a room full of analysts does NOT constitute public dissemination — it remains nonpublic
until broadly disseminated to investors in general.
Company filings, internet press releases, and reports widely distributed through press releases =
public information.
Social media platforms requiring membership may NOT constitute public dissemination. Members
must verify that information can also be accessed from publicly available sources (company filings,
press releases, websites).
Analysts who receive selective guidance or interpretation of publicly available data from company
insiders must treat this as nonpublic if it is material.
Members may use MNPI provided legitimately for due diligence (mergers, loan underwriting, credit
ratings per a specific business agreement) — but cannot use that same information to trade the
issuer's securities.

MOSAIC THEORY

THE MOSAIC THEORY — CRITICAL CONCEPT
DEFINITION: A financial analyst may act on significant conclusions derived from the analysis of
MATERIAL PUBLIC information PLUS NONMATERIAL NONPUBLIC information — even if those
conclusions would have been material inside information had they been provided directly by a
corporate insider.
The mosaic theory protects analysts who reach material conclusions through appropriate research
methods. The KEY question: HOW did the analyst reach the conclusion?
ESSENTIAL: Members must save and document all research used (public and nonmaterial nonpublic
information) to demonstrate that conclusions were reached through appropriate means.
Expert networks: Members may compensate industry experts for insights. However, members are
ultimately responsible for ensuring they do not REQUEST or ACT ON material nonpublic information
received from experts — regardless of agreements the experts have signed.
Analyst research reports: If a well-known analyst changes a recommendation, the new
recommendation itself may be material. However, the analyst is NOT required to make the report
public — clients who pay for the research are entitled to receive it first.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard II(A) Recommended Procedures
Achieve public dissemination: If a member determines information is material nonpublic, encourage
the issuing company to make it public. If public dissemination is not possible, communicate only to
designated supervisory/compliance personnel and do NOT take investment action.

Adopt compliance procedures: Review employee and proprietary trading; review investment
recommendations; document procedures; supervise interdepartmental communications.
Firewall — Substantive control of interdepartmental communications through a compliance/legal
clearance area.
Firewall — Review employee trading through 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists.
Firewall — Document all procedures limiting information flow between departments.
Firewall — Physically separate investment banking from sales/research; bank commercial lending
from trust/research.
Firewall — No personnel overlap: An analyst who assists IB on a deal must be treated as an IB
employee and CANNOT return to a research function until all information is public.
Firewall — A single compliance officer reviews what can be shared across the wall and approves
communications.
Personal trading limitations: Employees must periodically report personal transactions and family
member transactions. Use watch lists (shown only to compliance) and restricted lists.
Market making: When a firm possesses MNPI, market makers should remain PASSIVE — taking only
the contra side of unsolicited customer trades. Risk arbitrage should be suspended.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Teja Uses Mosaic Analysis (Swan Furniture)
Jagdish Teja analyses Swan Furniture by studying financial reports, visiting operations, and talking
to designers and retailers. He concludes that SFC's new extravagant designs are losing sales and
that next-quarter earnings will drop substantially. He issues a sell recommendation. COMPLIANT:
Teja reached his conclusion from public information plus pieces of nonmaterial nonpublic
information. The fact that his conclusion turned out to be material does not make it a violation.

✓
Example — Clement's Multi-Source Analysis (Turgot Chariots)
Roger Clement interviews salespeople, labour leaders, currency analysts, and banking officials to
analyse Turgot Chariots. He concludes a downgrade from 'outperform' to 'underperform' is
warranted and retains all support materials. COMPLIANT: He pieced together public and
nonmaterial nonpublic information using mosaic theory. He documents his research.

✓
Example — McCoy's Expert Network (Cancer Treatment)
Mary McCoy uses an expert network to speak with specialists in cancer treatment, enhancing her
understanding of therapies and the companies producing related drugs. She has neither asked for
nor received information that may be material and nonpublic. COMPLIANT: She appropriately
used expert networks to enhance her evaluation without seeking or obtaining MNPI.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Barnes Tip Chain (SmartTown)
Frank Barnes decides to sell his company and tells his sister (treasurer), who tells her daughter,
who tells her husband Staple, who tells his broker Alex Halsey. Halsey immediately buys

SmartTown stock. VIOLATION by Staple: communicated MNPI to his broker. VIOLATION by
Halsey: traded on material nonpublic information.

✗
Example — Peter Failed to Prevent MNPI Spread
Samuel Peter conducts a conference call with IB staff and a company CEO revealing that
earnings projections will drop significantly. During the call, several salespeople and portfolio
managers walk in and out of the room. Before the call ends, they trade the company's stock.
VIOLATION: Peter failed to prevent the transfer and misuse of MNPI to others in his firm. Physical
access to the conversation constituted a breach of the information barrier.

✗
Example — Zito Trades Ahead of Analyst's TV Appearance
Before going on air, journalist Mary Zito learns that prominent analyst Clement will announce a
downgrade. She sells her holdings and calls her father with the information. VIOLATION: Zito
received advance notice of material nonpublic information (the imminent change of
recommendation). She traded on it and caused her father to trade — both actions violate II(A).

✗
Example — Watson Acts on Expert's Inside Information
Tom Watson, a hedge fund analyst, calls a scientist leading product tests at a technology
company. The scientist expresses disappointment with the product's performance. Watson relays
this to the fund, which sells its position and buys put options. VIOLATION: Watson passed along
material nonpublic information about ongoing product tests. He cannot rely on the expert's signed
agreement as protection — he must personally determine whether the information meets the
materiality threshold.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: 'Causing others to act': The prohibition covers BOTH acting on MNPI AND
CAUSING others to act on it. Telling a family member about a pending announcement — even if
they are the one who ultimately trades — is a violation by the member who communicated the
information.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Mosaic theory: The analyst's CONCLUSION being material does not violate II(A) if
the conclusion was derived from public information plus nonmaterial nonpublic information. The
test is HOW the conclusion was reached — not whether the conclusion itself is material.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Expert networks: Even if experts sign non-disclosure agreements, if they DO
provide MNPI, the member is prohibited from trading until the information becomes public. The
agreement does not protect the member.


Standard II(B) — Market Manipulation

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must not engage in practices that distort prices or artificially inflate
trading volume with the intent to mislead market participants.


GUIDANCE

Standard II(B) prohibits practices that distort security prices or trading volume WITH THE INTENT TO DECEIVE.
Market manipulation reduces investor confidence, increases risk premiums, reduces market efficiency, and can
cause broader economic harm. INTENT is critical: legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market
inefficiencies are permitted.

Type
Definition and Examples
Information-based
manipulation
Spreading false or misleading information: pump-and-dump schemes (issuing
misleading positive information to inflate price, then selling); online rumour
campaigns; exaggerating earnings projections to force a company response;
sensationalising a negative report during a company's quiet period to benefit
clients' short positions.
Transaction-based
manipulation
Transactions that artificially affect prices or volume: trading the same stock
between two accounts to create artificial volume and price movement; cross-
trading between managed funds at artificial prices; securing a dominant position
in a financial instrument to exploit related derivatives. Must be designed to
mislead — transactions that move prices for legitimate strategic reasons are
NOT manipulation.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Market Making with MNPI (Passive Approach)
A market maker is instructed to remain PASSIVE when the firm possesses material nonpublic
information — taking only the contra side of unsolicited customer trades. This approach protects
liquidity while not actively exploiting MNPI. COMPLIANT with II(B) when the market maker does
not proactively trade to benefit from the MNPI.

✓
Example — ACME Futures Exchange Launch (with Disclosure)
ACME enters agreements with members for minimum trading volume on a new bond futures
contract to demonstrate liquidity. IF the exchange fully discloses this arrangement to the market,
this does NOT violate II(B) — the intent is to improve service, not to deceive. Without disclosure,
however, it would be a violation.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Sekar's Cross-Fund Trading
Rajesh Sekar manages two funds — an equity fund and a balanced fund. To address an
overweight in Digital Design Inc. (low liquidity), he begins trading DD stock back and forth between
his two funds to create the appearance of growing volume and price interest, hoping other
investors will buy in and he can reduce his overweight. VIOLATION: Creates artificial price
movement and volume. Even though the intent is to benefit fund participants, the manipulation
itself violates II(B).

✗
Example — Murphy's Timed Negative Report
Matthew Murphy times the release of a negative research report specifically to maximise
downward price pressure during a company's quiet period, to benefit his hedge fund clients' short

positions. The report is based on speculation, not fact. VIOLATION of II(B): Designed to create
artificial price volatility. Also violates V(A) (no reasonable basis for the speculative report).

✗
Example — Weinberg's Internet Chat Rumours
Steve Weinberg posts false rumours in investor chat rooms claiming Moosehead & Belfast
Railroad is about to expand its network and receive a large lumber-shipping contract. VIOLATION:
Disseminating false information with the intent to mislead market participants, regardless of
personal profit motive.

✗
Example — Mandeville's Structured Product Model Inputs
Bill Mandeville uses only positive scenarios as model inputs when rating structured investment
products, minimising apparent downside risk to achieve better ratings. His compensation is partly
based on the rating level achieved. VIOLATION: Manipulates inputs to achieve desired outputs —
causing significant harm to investors and capital markets when the products subsequently fail.

✗
Example — King Creates Rumours Against Portfolio Manager
Allen King creates fake user profiles on online forums under a portfolio manager's name and starts
rumours about potential mergers for companies in the portfolio. The resulting price increases allow
the manager to sell positions, leading to a regulatory investigation. VIOLATION: King has violated
II(B) even though he personally profited nothing. The intent to manipulate — not profit motive — is
what matters.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: INTENT matters for II(B). The question is whether the conduct was designed to
artificially distort prices or deceive market participants. Legitimate trading strategies that move
prices — based on genuine market analysis — are NOT market manipulation.

heading: Standard III — DUTIES TO CLIENTS
body:
Standard III(A) — Loyalty, Prudence, and Care

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates have a duty of loyalty to their clients and must act with reasonable
care and exercise prudent judgment. Members and Candidates must act for the benefit of
their clients and place their clients' interests before their employer's or their own interests.

GUIDANCE

Client interests are PARAMOUNT. Members must exercise the same level of prudence, judgement, and care they
would apply in the management of their own interests in similar circumstances. Prudence requires caution and
discretion — acting with the care a reasonable person in a like capacity, familiar with such matters, would use.
This standard applies regardless of whether a formal fiduciary duty exists under applicable law.

IDENTIFYING THE 'CLIENT' — CRITICAL CONCEPT
Individual portfolio management: The individual is the client.
Pension plans and trusts: The CLIENT is the BENEFICIARIES — NOT the plan sponsor or trustee
who hires the manager. Decisions must be evaluated on their merits for the beneficiaries.
Pooled funds managed to a mandate (e.g., large-cap income fund): The 'client' is the stated mandate
itself. Individual investor suitability is the responsibility of whoever has the advisory relationship with
those individuals.
Execution-only professional: Duty of loyalty, prudence, and care is centred on executing trades on the
most favourable terms possible within the client's stated parameters — not advising on the investment
merits.

Topic
Key Rules
Soft dollars (soft
commissions)
Using client brokerage to purchase research that benefits clients =
ACCEPTABLE. Using client brokerage for goods or services that benefit the
manager but NOT the client = VIOLATION. Specific example: paying rent,
offices, or personal travel expenses with client brokerage = violation.
Directed brokerage
Client directing brokerage is acceptable — it is the client's asset. BUT: member
must (1) still seek best execution within the client's direction, and (2) disclose to
client that they may not receive best execution from the directed broker.
Proxy voting
Proxies have economic value. Members must vote proxies in an informed and
responsible manner. Voting blindly with management on non-routine
governance issues may violate III(A). A cost-benefit analysis may justify not
voting all proxies in every case. Proxy voting policies must be disclosed to
clients.
Excessive trading
(churning)
Trading beyond what is necessary to achieve client objectives in order to
generate commissions = violation. Using a fee structure that incentivises
excessive trading creates a direct conflict.

Family accounts
Family member accounts that are REGULAR FEE-PAYING client accounts
must be treated the SAME as all other accounts — neither favoured NOR
disadvantaged. This applies in both directions.
Investment decisions
Must be evaluated in the context of the TOTAL PORTFOLIO — not on a
security-by-security basis. A high-risk security may be appropriate if it
represents a small percentage of a diversified portfolio.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard III(A) Recommended Procedures
Regular account information: Provide clients at least quarterly with itemised statements showing all
funds, securities, debits, credits, and transactions in their accounts.
Disclose asset locations: Inform clients where assets are maintained and when/where they are
moved.
Separate assets: Keep client assets separate from any other party's assets, including the member's
own assets.
Client approval: When uncertain about an appropriate course of action, consider what the client would
reasonably expect and, if in doubt, disclose the matter in writing and obtain client approval.
Follow applicable laws; establish investment objectives; consider total portfolio; diversify unless
contrary to plan guidelines; carry out regular reviews.
Deal fairly with all clients; disclose conflicts of interest; disclose all compensation arrangements; vote
proxies in clients' best interests; seek best execution; place client interests first.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Dill's Family Account — Equal Treatment
Adam Dill manages accounts for his father and brother, both fee-paying clients, alongside other
clients. An IPO allocation is insufficient for all requesting clients. Dill allocates shares
proportionately to ALL suitable accounts — including his brother's. COMPLIANT: The brother's
account is a regular fee-paying account and must be treated equally to all other accounts.

✓
Example — Nguyen Corrects Performance Report
Teresa Nguyen, a performance analyst, discovers a missed trade that creates a large loss for a
client on the firm's 'watch' list. She informs the appropriate people and requires the report to be
corrected before it reaches the client — knowing this may lead to the firm's termination by the
client. COMPLIANT: Even though the correction may harm the firm's business, withholding
information on errors would not be in the best interest of the client.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Wiley Buys Overvalued Stock for Pension (Wrong Client Identification)
Julian Wiley, trustee for a pension plan, is persuaded by Miller Company management to buy
Miller stock to resist a hostile takeover bid. Wiley believes the stock is overvalued but buys it
anyway, hoping to win additional business for the bank. VIOLATION: Wiley's duty of loyalty is to

the plan BENEFICIARIES — not to management, not to the employer, and not to himself. He must
evaluate the takeover offer solely on its merits for the beneficiaries.

✗
Example — JNI Uses Client Brokerage for Rent (Soft Dollar Abuse)
JNI Investment Counseling uses a large brokerage firm (Thompson) almost exclusively because
the executives have a close friendship, even though Thompson's commissions are high and
services are average. In exchange, Thompson absorbs JNI's overhead expenses including rent.
VIOLATION of III(A): Using client brokerage for manager overhead that provides no benefit to
clients; failing to seek best execution.

✗
Example — Knauss's Excessive Trading
Vida Knauss manages high-net-worth accounts with fees partly based on trading commissions.
She engages in extensive trading for each client to reach the minimum commission level set by
her firm — even though the amount of trading exceeds what is necessary for the client's
investment objectives. The securities themselves may be appropriate; the EXCESSIVE
FREQUENCY is the violation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Client identification: For pension plans and trusts, the CLIENT is the
BENEFICIARIES — not the plan sponsor or the entity that hired the manager. Decisions must be
based on what is in the beneficiaries' best interest, not what benefits management or the hiring
entity.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Soft dollars vs. directed brokerage: Soft dollars = manager uses client brokerage (at
manager's discretion) to buy research. Directed brokerage = CLIENT directs manager to use a
specific broker. Directed brokerage is permissible because it is the client's asset — but the
manager must still seek best execution and must disclose that best execution may not be obtained
through the directed broker.


Standard III(B) — Fair Dealing

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing
investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or
engaging in other professional activities.

GUIDANCE

The standard uses 'FAIRLY' — not 'equally.' Members cannot reach all clients at exactly the same time. Different
service levels are permitted if: (1) disclosed to all clients, and (2) available to everyone (not selectively offered).
What is NEVER permissible: giving one client advance information that disadvantages other clients.

Area
Key Rules

Dissemination of
recommendations
All clients must have a FAIR OPPORTUNITY to act on every recommendation.
New recommendations must be simultaneously disseminated to all clients. Limit
the time between the investment decision and dissemination.
Changing
recommendations
Material changes must be communicated to all clients who have acted on or
been affected by the earlier advice. If a client places an order contrary to a
current changed recommendation they haven't received, advise them of the
change BEFORE accepting the order.
Different service levels
Permitted if (1) disclosed to all clients and (2) available to everyone. Example:
Calling three largest clients AFTER widely distributing a report to discuss it in
detail = ACCEPTABLE. Calling those three clients BEFORE distributing the
report = VIOLATION.
IPO distributions
Distribute on a PRO-RATA basis to all subscribing clients. Members must forgo
their own/family allocations to free shares for clients if oversubscribed. Hot
issues must have bona fide public distribution — cannot be used as rewards or
incentives for generating business.
Block trades
All participating client accounts receive the SAME execution price and
commission rate. Partial fills = allocated PRO-RATA based on order size.
Allocating profitable trades to favoured clients and losses to others AFTER the
fact = clear violation.
Social media
Recommendations distributed to a limited social media audience before all
clients = violation. Must ensure all clients have fair opportunity to act before
recommendations are posted to selective online groups.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard III(B) Recommended Procedures
Limit the number of people who know a recommendation will be disseminated — to minimise the
potential for improper use of the information before distribution.
