Shorten the time between investment decision and dissemination — same-day release if possible;
consider publishing a brief summary in advance of a detailed report that takes weeks.
Publish guidelines clients will receive regarding how recommendations are disseminated.
Simultaneous dissemination: Use systems that deliver investment information simultaneously to all
clients (fax, email blast, website posting) to ensure fair access.
Allocate block trades: Establish systematic procedures so that partial fills are distributed pro-rata, and
all accounts receive the same execution price.
IPO policy: Document that hot issues are distributed fairly and not used as rewards for business
relationships.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Ames Discusses Pending Recommendation at Luncheon
At a client luncheon, Ames discusses a pending research recommendation with several clients
BEFORE the formal dissemination to all clients. VIOLATION of III(B): Even though the discussion
was at a scheduled meeting, those clients received advance information that gave them a trading
advantage over other clients who had not yet received the recommendation.


✗
Example — Baker Allocates Hot IPO to Husband's Account First
Carol Baker, managing an aggressive growth fund, instructs brokers to buy hot IPO issues for her
husband's account whenever they become available. Her fund clients are frequently unable to
participate because the issues are scarce. VIOLATION of III(B) and VI(B): Baker should acquire
shares for her fund clients first; she may participate in hot issues personally only after clients have
been served.

✗
Example — Burdette Posts Buy Recommendation on X Before Distribution
Mary Burdette posts a buy recommendation to FIM's X followers before the formal research report
is distributed to all FIM clients. VIOLATION: Only some clients — those following FIM on X —
received the information before other clients. This violates the fair opportunity requirement.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: 'Fairly' not 'equally': Different service levels for different fees ARE acceptable.
Wealthier clients paying higher fees may receive more personal service. What is never acceptable:
giving one client advance information that DISADVANTAGES other clients.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Changing recommendations: If a client calls to place an order CONTRARY to a
recommendation that has been changed but not yet communicated to that client, the member must
ADVISE the client of the changed recommendation BEFORE accepting the order.


Standard III(C) — Suitability

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
When Members and Candidates are in an advisory relationship with a client, they must: (1)
Make a reasonable inquiry into a client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk
and return objectives, and financial constraints prior to making any investment
recommendations or taking investment action and must reassess and update this information
regularly. (2) Determine that an investment is suitable to the client's financial situation and
consistent with the client's written objectives, mandates, and constraints before making an
investment recommendation or taking investment action. (3) Judge the suitability of
investments in the context of the client's total portfolio. When Members and Candidates are
responsible for managing a portfolio to a specific mandate, strategy, or style, they must only
make investment recommendations or take investment actions that are consistent with the
stated objectives and constraints of the portfolio.

GUIDANCE

Two distinct contexts: (1) ADVISORY RELATIONSHIP — detailed suitability analysis required for each client. (2)
MANAGING TO A MANDATE — only required to invest consistently with the stated mandate, NOT responsible
for suitability of the investment for each individual investor who chooses to invest in the fund.

IPS COMPONENTS — RRIITLU

INVESTMENT POLICY STATEMENT (IPS) — REQUIRED COMPONENTS

Return Objectives — income requirements, growth targets, maintenance of purchasing power
Risk Tolerance — financial ability to bear risk AND psychological willingness; must reconcile both
dimensions
Investment Constraints: Time Horizon — when funds will be needed; Liquidity — cash flow
requirements; Legal and Regulatory — applicable laws, trust restrictions; Taxes — client's tax
situation; Unique Circumstances — client-specific preferences, ethical screens, concentrated
positions
Must also identify: roles and responsibilities of all parties, and schedule for regular review
Regular updates: At least ANNUALLY and prior to any material changes in the client's circumstances
or objectives
Clients must be encouraged to disclose their complete financial picture to enable effective suitability
analysis

CRITICAL SUITABILITY RULES
Suitability is assessed in the context of the CLIENT'S TOTAL PORTFOLIO — not on a security-by-
security basis. A high-risk security may be suitable if it represents 2% of a broadly diversified portfolio.
Unsolicited unsuitable trades: Discuss concerns with the client. If the trade would materially affect the
portfolio — update the IPS to reflect the change. If the client insists without an IPS update, the
member must evaluate whether to continue the advisory relationship.
Managing to a mandate: NOT responsible for suitability for individual fund investors — only for
consistency with the stated mandate. A large-cap income fund manager cannot invest heavily in
speculative small-caps regardless of any client's request.
Submanager selection: Must conduct full due diligence including risk profile match, compliance, code
of ethics, investment process, and quality of performance information. Selecting solely on lowest fees
= violation of both III(C) and V(A).
Complex products (leverage, derivatives): Pay special attention to leverage inherent in complex
products when assessing suitability.
Locked-up investments: An investment locked up for 3+ years in a portfolio requiring 'highly liquid
investments' violates III(C) even if total equity exposure stays within guideline limits.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard III(C) Recommended Procedures
Investment Policy Statement: Develop and maintain a written IPS for each advisory client before
making any investment recommendation. Review and update at least annually and when client
circumstances change.
Regular updates: Develop effective procedures for gathering client information and for updating the
IPS when material changes occur.
Suitability test policies: Develop systematic procedures for evaluating new investment products and
strategies for suitability for specific client profiles before recommending them.
Document all suitability determinations: Evidence that each investment was evaluated in the context
of the client's total portfolio.


VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Gubler's Illiquid PE Investment in Liquid Portfolio
Gubler invests a significant portion of a portfolio that requires 'highly liquid investments' into a
private equity fund locked up for over three years. VIOLATION of III(C) even if the total equity
allocation stays within the guideline limits — the liquidity constraint is violated by the nature of the
investment.

✗
Example — Selecting Submanager Solely on Fees
A manager selects an external subadviser based solely on its lower fee structure, without
reviewing the adviser's investment process, risk profile, compliance practices, code of ethics, or
quality of performance information. VIOLATION of both III(C) and V(A): Suitability requires
matching the submanager's risk profile and capabilities to the client; V(A) requires diligent due
diligence.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Managing to a mandate vs. advisory: When managing a pooled fund to a stated
mandate, the manager is NOT responsible for whether the fund is appropriate for each individual
underlying investor who buys shares. That suitability obligation belongs to whoever has the
advisory relationship with those investors.


Standard III(D) — Performance Presentation

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
When communicating investment performance information, Members and Candidates must
make reasonable efforts to ensure that it is fair, accurate, and complete.

GUIDANCE

Performance information must not create a misleading impression about a member's track record or investment
results. The standard applies to individual account performance, composite performance, and analysts'
recommendation track records.

Topic
Key Rules
GIPS compliance
Best practice — firms should be encouraged to comply with GIPS. When
claiming GIPS compliance, must meet ALL requirements including asset-
weighted composites. Non-GIPS-compliant presentations must not mislead
clients.
Composite construction
Must include TERMINATED accounts and clearly indicate when they were
terminated. Cannot cherry-pick accounts to inflate composite performance.
Cannot deviate from stated composite construction policies solely to capture
positive performance.
Simulated/backtested
results
Must clearly identify simulated results as simulated. Cannot present backtested
performance as actual historical performance.

Prior firm performance
Permissible to show performance earned at a prior firm IF accompanied by
disclosure of: (1) where the performance was earned, and (2) the member's
specific role in generating that performance.
Attribution methodology
changes
Must notify clients. If changing, must show both old and new methodologies and
explain why the new approach is preferred — to allow meaningful comparisons
over time.
Performance calculation
methodology
Cannot selectively show whichever of TWR or MWR is higher without
disclosure. Must provide sufficient information for clients to judge performance
fairly.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Cupp Adds Account Before Policy Date
Cupp adds a new account to the composite before the firm's stated policy date — to capture the
large initial gain in that account. VIOLATION of III(D): Deviating from stated composite
construction policies to capture positive performance misrepresents the manager's track record.

✗
Example — Washington Inflates Composite with Cherry-Picked Accounts
Washington's employer omits terminated accounts from the composite while describing it as
including 'all firm accounts.' Using this promotional material inflates the 10-year performance
figure. VIOLATION: Both I(A) and III(D) are implicated — the performance presentation is
misleading and Washington must not use the inflated numbers.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Performance presentation: ALWAYS include terminated accounts (with disclosure of
termination) — omitting them inflates apparent skill. Simulated/backtested results MUST be clearly
labelled. Prior firm performance is permissible but must include disclosure of where it was earned
and the member's role.


Standard III(E) — Preservation of Confidentiality

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must keep information about current, former, and prospective
clients confidential unless: (1) the information concerns illegal activities on the part of the
client; (2) disclosure is required by law; or (3) the client or prospective client permits
disclosure of the information.

GUIDANCE

KEY APPLICATIONS AND EXCEPTIONS
The confidentiality obligation applies to CURRENT, FORMER, AND PROSPECTIVE clients — it does
not end when the relationship ends.

EXCEPTION 1: Illegal activities — if a client is engaged in illegal activities, the member MAY have a
duty to disclose to authorities. Must consult legal counsel and employer compliance before acting. It is
not automatic.
EXCEPTION 2: Required by law — applicable law may compel disclosure; always comply with legal
requirements.
EXCEPTION 3: Client permission — if a client expressly authorises disclosure, the member may
follow the terms of the authorisation.
Electronic security: Must follow employer policies on electronic communication and data storage.
Does not need to become an IT security expert but must understand and comply with firm policies.
Regular training for all staff on confidentiality obligations.
CFA Institute PCP investigations: Forwarding confidential client information to the Professional
Conduct Programme in support of an investigation is NOT a violation of III(E). Members are
encouraged to cooperate.
Accidental disclosure by client: If a client accidentally posts personal financial information to a semi-
public forum, the MEMBER is not in violation if they took reasonable precautions (e.g., warned clients
not to post confidential information in semi-public channels).

VIOLATION AND COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — Moody's Group Instructions (Accidental Disclosure)
Moody manages a social media investment discussion group for clients. She instructs clients not
to post personal financial information in the group. A client accidentally posts personal financial
details. Moody has not violated III(E) because she took reasonable precautions (warning clients
not to post confidential information) to protect client data.

✗
Example — Sharing Client Information Without Authorisation
A member discusses specific details of a client's financial situation, holdings, or investment
objectives with a third party — without client permission, without legal requirement, and without
the client being engaged in illegal activities. VIOLATION of III(E): The obligation is absolute absent
one of the three specific exceptions.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: The confidentiality obligation extends to FORMER and PROSPECTIVE clients —
not only current clients. The obligation does NOT end when the advisory relationship ends.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Cooperating with CFA Institute PCP: Providing confidential client information to the
PCP in support of a conduct investigation is explicitly NOT a violation of III(E). Members are
encouraged to cooperate fully with PCP investigations.

heading: Standard IV — DUTIES TO EMPLOYERS
body:
Standard IV(A) — Loyalty

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
In matters related to their employment, Members and Candidates must act for the benefit of
their employer and not deprive their employer of the advantage of their skills and abilities,
divulge confidential information, or otherwise cause harm to their employer.

GUIDANCE

Members must act for their employer's benefit in all matters related to employment. The standard does NOT
require members to put employer interests above all personal and family obligations in ALL areas of life — only 'in
matters related to their employment.'

Scenario
Rule
Independent practice while
employed
Must NOTIFY employer of: (1) types of services to be rendered, (2)
expected duration, (3) compensation. Must receive WRITTEN CONSENT
before rendering services. 'Preparations' alone (incorporating a new
company, registering with regulators on own time) are permissible.
Leaving an employer —
prohibited actions
Must not solicit clients BEFORE resignation is effective; must not take
records or files without written permission from the EMPLOYER (not just a
colleague); must not misappropriate trade secrets.
Leaving an employer —
permissible preparations
Updating CV, forming a new company, registering with regulators — all
permissible on own time before resigning. Responding to unsolicited
inquiries about future employment is permissible.
After departure — what is
NOT confidential
Skills and experience gained during employment. Simple knowledge of
client names. General industry knowledge. These are NOT confidential and
may be used at a new employer.
After departure — what IS
confidential
Client lists and detailed contact information from the former employer.
Research, models, and work products created at the former firm. Cannot
use employer records even if you helped create them.
Contacting former clients post-
departure
Permissible using PUBLIC INFORMATION (e.g., phone numbers from
public records) ABSENT a noncompete agreement. NOT permissible using
employer records.
Social media accounts
Firm-approved professional accounts = FIRM property. Must transfer or
delete as directed by firm policies. Best practice: maintain SEPARATE
personal and professional social media accounts.
Whistleblowing
Permitted — and may be required — when it protects clients or the integrity
of the market. NOT permitted for personal gain. Actions that would normally
violate IV(A) (copying records, contradicting employer instructions) may be
JUSTIFIED when clearly aimed at protecting clients or market integrity.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES


Standard IV(A) Recommended Procedures
Competition policy: Understand any restrictions on offering similar services outside the firm while
employed. Ensure noncompete agreement terms are clearly explained before signing.
Termination policy: Understand the resignation process, how termination will be disclosed to clients
and staff, whether social media updates are allowed, and procedures for transferring ongoing
responsibilities.
Incident-reporting procedures: Know the firm's whistleblowing policies and industry best practices for
reporting unethical or illegal activities.
Employee classification: Understand employment status (full-time, part-time, contractor) and how firm
policies apply to each class.

COMPLIANCE EXAMPLES

✓
Example — McQuillen's Post-Departure Client Contact
Shawna McQuillen, after leaving her employer, obtains phone numbers of former clients from
PUBLIC RECORDS (not from employer records). She clears her personal computer of all
employer records before leaving. She then contacts former clients to solicit their business.
COMPLIANT: She used only public information, left employer records behind, and had no
noncompete agreement preventing client contact.

✓
Example — Allen Registers New Company Before Resigning
Geraldine Allen, while still employed, registers a new competing investment company with
regulatory authorities, on her own time outside the office. She does not solicit clients or actively
operate the business until after she resigns. COMPLIANT: Preparations to compete are
permissible; active solicitation or operation before resignation is not.

✓
Example — Webb Updates LinkedIn After RSI Notifies Clients
Laura Webb left RSI and had a non-solicitation agreement. LinkedIn automatically notified her
network of her employment change when she updated her profile — AFTER RSI had already
notified clients and AFTER her employment ended. COMPLIANT: She placed the employer's
interests ahead of her own by waiting until after RSI's notification before updating.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Magee Solicits Clients Before Resignation
Samuel Magee, frustrated with his employer, asks four big pension accounts to leave and open
accounts with his new employer before he has formally resigned. He also solicits prospective
clients he had previously approached on behalf of his current employer. VIOLATION of IV(A): His
duty of loyalty remains to Trust Assets until his employment ends. Solicitation before resignation is
a violation regardless of the strength of the relationships.

✗
Example — Hightower Takes Employer's Records (Comprehensive List)
James Hightower plans to take: client lists, account statements, marketing presentations with
performance records, recommended securities lists, computer models for asset allocation and

stock selection, and personal spreadsheets for major recommendations. VIOLATION: Employer
records — including everything Hightower helped create — belong to the firm. Taking any
employer records without written permission violates IV(A), regardless of whether records are in
hard copy, electronic, or any other medium.

✗
Example — Acertado Downloads Models with Colleague's Permission
Acertado obtains Boswin's (a colleague's) consent to download the company's proprietary
financial model before leaving. However, Acertado does NOT obtain the FIRM'S written
permission. VIOLATION: Written permission must come from the EMPLOYER — not a colleague.
A colleague cannot authorise use of firm property on the employer's behalf.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Preparations vs. active solicitation: 'Preparations to compete' (incorporating a
company, registering with authorities, updating a CV) are permissible on own time. Actively
SOLICITING clients or OPERATING a competing business before resignation = VIOLATION,
regardless of how large the client or how close the relationship.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Downloading/copying employer records: Written permission must come from the
EMPLOYER (the firm) — not from a supervisor, colleague, or client. Even if a colleague consents,
using firm records without firm-level authorisation violates IV(A).


Standard IV(B) — Additional Compensation Arrangements

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must not accept gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that
competes with or might reasonably be expected to create a conflict of interest with their
employer's interest unless they obtain written consent from all parties involved.

GUIDANCE

Members must obtain WRITTEN CONSENT from their employer before accepting ANY gift, benefit,
compensation, or consideration that competes with — or could create a conflict with — their employer's interests.
Written consent includes any documented form of communication (e.g., email). Compensation includes both
direct and indirect benefits, and all NON-MONETARY benefits (board perks, club memberships, free travel).

IV(B) KEY RULES
Disclosure must be made BEFORE accepting the arrangement. If not possible before, must disclose
promptly after.
Written report to supervisor and compliance officer must include: (1) nature of the compensation, (2)
approximate amount, (3) duration of the agreement.
Non-monetary compensation counts: Board membership perks, club memberships, free travel, etc. —
all require disclosure just as monetary compensation does.
Part-time employees: Potential service limitations should be discussed during the negotiation/hiring
process. IV(B) requirements still apply to limitations identified at that stage.


VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Whitman's Monaco Trip (No Disclosure)
Geoff Whitman manages Carol Cochran's account. Cochran offers Whitman and his wife a trip to
Monaco whenever the portfolio achieves 15%+ return. Whitman does not disclose this to his
employer. The next January, he vacations in Monaco at Cochran's expense. VIOLATION: Failed
to notify his employer of the supplemental compensation arrangement. The arrangement could
create partiality toward Cochran's account to the detriment of other accounts.

✗
Example — Jones's Board Membership (Non-Monetary Benefits)
Terry Jones serves on the board of Exercise Unlimited and receives unlimited family gym
memberships in exchange. He does not disclose this to his employer because there is no
monetary compensation. He then purchases Exercise Unlimited stock for client accounts.
VIOLATION: Non-monetary benefits create conflicts of interest in the same manner as monetary
compensation. Disclosure to employer was required.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: 'Written consent' in IV(B) includes any documented communication — emails
qualify. The FORM of consent can be informal; what matters is that it is documented and obtained
BEFORE accepting the arrangement.


Standard IV(C) — Responsibilities of Supervisors

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must make reasonable efforts to ensure that anyone subject to their
supervision or authority complies with applicable laws, rules, regulations, and the Code and
Standards.

GUIDANCE

ANY investment professional who has employees subject to their 'control or influence' — whether or not those
employees are CFA Institute members, charterholders, or candidates — exercises supervisory responsibility. The
standard applies beyond formal direct-report relationships.

Obligation
Detail
Establishing compliance
systems
Must understand what constitutes an adequate compliance system and ensure
that procedures are established, documented, communicated to covered
personnel, and FOLLOWED. Procedures must be in place PRIOR to violations
occurring — not designed reactively after misconduct is discovered.
Response to discovered
violations
Must PROMPTLY investigate. Simply warning the employee, reporting up the
chain, or relying on employee assurances that it won't recur is NOT enough.
Must: (1) promptly investigate the extent of wrongdoing; (2) place limits on the
employee's activities; (3) increase monitoring — PENDING the outcome of the
investigation.

Inadequate compliance
system
If the member cannot discharge supervisory responsibilities due to an absent or
inadequate compliance system, must bring this to senior management's
attention. May need to DECLINE supervisory responsibility in WRITING until the
firm adopts adequate procedures.
Supervision includes
detection
Must make reasonable efforts to DETECT violations through periodic review of
employee actions and trading. If procedures are in place AND followed but a
violation still occurs, the member may NOT be in violation — but the occurrence
of violations may indicate procedures are inadequate.
Incentive structures
Must not encourage unethical behaviour. Compensation systems rewarding
short-term performance at the expense of client interests create pressure that
constitutes a supervisory failure.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard IV(C) Recommended Procedures — Adequate Compliance Procedures Must:
Be contained in a clearly written, accessible manual tailored to the firm's operations
Designate a compliance officer with clearly defined authority, resources, and responsibility
Describe the hierarchy of supervision and assign duties among supervisors
Implement a system of checks and balances; outline scope; outline procedures for testing compliance
Delineate procedures for reporting violations and associated sanctions
Once in place: disseminate to appropriate personnel; update periodically; continually educate; issue
periodic reminders; incorporate ethics into performance reviews; review employee actions; enforce
promptly when violations occur
Once a violation is discovered: respond promptly; conduct thorough investigation; increase
supervision or limit the wrongdoer's activities; review procedures for changes to prevent future
violations
Establish an incentive structure that rewards ethical conduct, not just short-term returns

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Henry Fails to Set Up Record-Keeping for Tabbing
Charles Henry assigns Samantha Tabbing to manage fund futures trading but establishes NO
written procedures, compliance manual, or review processes for her futures trading. Tabbing
subsequently assigns more favourable executions to the employee profit-sharing plan and worse
executions to client funds. VIOLATION of IV(C): Henry failed to establish record-keeping and
review procedures. He cannot delegate responsibilities without implementing governance
frameworks.

✗
Example — Mattock Fails to Prevent Trading on Changed Recommendation
Jane Mattock orally advises certain executives of a pending recommendation change before the
report is published. One executive — Frampton — sells stock from his own account and client
accounts. Mattock did not establish procedures to prevent such trading. VIOLATION of IV(C): She
failed to prevent or establish reasonable procedures designed to prevent dissemination of or
trading on the pending change.


✗
Example — Chen Wang Skips Review for Late Submission
Chen Wang, head of research, receives Huang Mei's research report very late. Given prior
competent work and time pressure, Chen compiles the report without review — even though
Huang has plagiarised a blogger's sell recommendation without attribution. VIOLATION: The
delayed submission and prior competence do not remove the supervisory requirement to follow
the firm's established review process.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Supervision extends beyond direct reports: Any professional with 'control or
influence' over employees exercises supervisory responsibility — not just formal direct managers.
Delegating supervision to a subordinate does not discharge the supervisor's responsibility unless
the subordinate is also instructed on compliance expectations.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Discovery of a violation: Must do MORE than warn the employee and report up the
chain. Must PROMPTLY investigate AND limit the employee's activities pending the investigation
outcome. Relying on assurances that misconduct will not recur is NOT sufficient.

section_title: Standard V — INVESTMENT ANALYSIS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND
heading: Standard V — INVESTMENT ANALYSIS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND
body:
ACTIONS

Standard V(A) — Diligence and Reasonable Basis

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must: 1. Exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness in
analysing investments, making investment recommendations, and taking investment actions.
2. Have a reasonable and adequate basis, supported by appropriate research and
investigation, for any investment analysis, recommendation, or action.

GUIDANCE

The required level of diligence and thoroughness varies based on: (1) the investment philosophy being followed;
(2) the member's role in the investment decision-making process; (3) the support and resources available.
Members must make reasonable efforts to cover all pertinent issues when arriving at a recommendation.

Context
Requirements
Primary research
Must conduct thorough, independent research. Cannot base recommendations
solely on overheard opinions, conventional wisdom, news articles alone, or
limited information without adequate analysis.
Secondary/third-party
research
May rely on it but must make reasonable and diligent efforts to verify the
research is sound. Evaluate: assumptions used, rigour of analysis,
date/timeliness, objectivity and independence. If reason to suspect unsound
basis — cannot rely on it.
Quantitative models
Must understand parameters, assumptions, and limitations. Not required to be
experts in every technical aspect, but must understand how results are used.
Must test model output BEFORE incorporating into recommendations.
Components of a strategy must be evaluated as an INTEGRATED WHOLE.
Developing quantitative
techniques
Higher standard for those who CREATE or MAINTAIN models: must understand
the technical aspects fully. Thorough testing — including negative/stress
scenarios — before distribution. Cannot substitute limited new data for a full
required data update ('quick fix').
External
adviser/subadviser
selection
Full due diligence required: review code of ethics, compliance and internal
controls, quality of performance information, adherence to stated investment
process. Cannot select solely on lowest fees.
Group research and
decision making
Members identified on a group report need not personally agree with every
conclusion — as long as the process was sound and conclusions have a
reasonable and adequate basis. May ask to have their name removed if they
disagree with the process or basis.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard V(A) Recommended Procedures

Establish a policy requiring that all research reports, credit ratings, and investment recommendations
have a basis that can be substantiated as reasonable and adequate. A supervisory analyst or review
committee should approve prior to external distribution.
Develop detailed written guidance for analysts establishing due diligence procedures for each type of
recommendation or rating.
Develop measurable criteria for assessing research quality, reasonableness of basis, and accuracy of
recommendations over time.
Written guidance establishing minimum levels of scenario testing for all computer-based models —
including stress scenarios with negative market events.
Standardised criteria for reviewing external advisers: code of ethics, compliance procedures, quality of
performance information, investment process adherence.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Witt Bases Report on News Article Alone
Witt writes an investment report based solely on a news article without conducting any
independent analysis or investigation. VIOLATION: A news article, by itself, does not constitute
reasonable and adequate basis for an investment recommendation.

✗
Example — Liakos's Derivative Strategy (Piecemeal Evaluation)
Liakos evaluates individual components of a complex derivative strategy in isolation and
concludes each is suitable, without evaluating the strategy as an integrated whole. The combined
leverage and risk exposures are far greater than the client's objectives. VIOLATION: Components
of a strategy must be evaluated as an integrated whole.

✗
Example — Dupont's 'Quick Fix' Model Update
Dupont substitutes a small amount of new data for a full required data update to save time,
resulting in a model that produces incorrect outputs for portfolios. The error is later discovered
after Smith has rebalanced affected portfolios without notifying clients. VIOLATION: Substituting
limited new data for a full required update is insufficient diligence for a model developer.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Selecting submanagers based solely on lowest fees = violation of BOTH V(A)
(insufficient diligence in selection) AND III(C) (suitability — must ensure the manager's risk profile
matches the client). Due diligence must cover investment process, compliance, ethics, and quality
of performance — not just cost.


Standard V(B) — Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients [REVISED 2023]

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must: 1. Disclose to clients and prospective clients the basic format
and general principles of the investment processes they use to analyse investments, select
securities, and construct portfolios and must promptly disclose any changes that might
materially affect those processes. 2. Disclose to clients and prospective clients significant

risks associated with the investment process. 3. Use reasonable judgment in identifying
which factors are important to their investment analyses, recommendations, or actions and
include those factors in communications with clients and prospective clients. 4. Distinguish
between fact and opinion in the presentation of investment analysis and recommendations. 5.
Disclose to clients and prospective clients the nature of the services provided and the basic
costs the client can expect.

GUIDANCE

Requirement
Key Details
Nature of services AND
costs [expanded 2023]
Must disclose ALL costs the client will bear — including those from affiliates,
related entities, and third parties involved in providing services. Cannot rely on
client sophistication as a reason not to disclose. Disclosures are ONGOING —
must update clients when services or costs change. Need not specify exact
dollar amounts if not determinable in advance.
Investment process
disclosure
Must describe factors with BOTH positive and negative influences. Must
promptly disclose material changes. Examples of material changes: changing
valuation model (e.g., from P/E to DDM), losing key investment professionals,
adding derivatives or changing constraints, shifting from direct to external
management.
Risks and limitations
Must disclose significant risks known at time of disclosure (ex ante basis). Use
of leverage = significant risk that MUST always be disclosed. Capacity
limitations must be disclosed (e.g., small-cap strategy limited to ~$100M AUM).
Fact vs. opinion
Must clearly distinguish. 'Gold reserves = 500,000 oz' based on analyst's own
calculation = OPINION — must be labelled as such. 'The dividend WILL
increase' when it is actually contingent on legislation = presenting opinion as
fact = violation.
Notification of model
errors
Discovering and correcting a model error must be communicated to affected
clients. Rebalancing portfolios affected by a model error without notifying clients
= violation of V(B).

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Scott States 'Dividend WILL Increase' on Walkton Report
Scott writes a research report on Walkton stating the dividend 'will increase' — even though the
increase is contingent on pending legislation. VIOLATION: Presenting an opinion that is
contingent on an external event as a certain fact — must be labelled as an expectation or opinion.

✗
Example — ABC Capital's Undisclosed Fee-Sharing Arrangement
ABC Capital fails to disclose a fee-sharing arrangement with affiliated companies who restructure
client portfolios — even though the arrangement ultimately benefited investors. VIOLATION of
V(B): Must disclose ALL costs arising from affiliates, third parties, and related entities. Cannot
claim investor benefit or investor sophistication as a basis for non-disclosure.

✗
Example — Smith Rebalances Without Notifying Clients of Model Error

Smith discovers a model error and rebalances the affected portfolios without notifying any clients
of the error or the correction. VIOLATION of V(B): Clients must be informed of material model
errors and their implications, even when the correction is beneficial.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Cost disclosure is now explicitly required under V(B) (2023 revision): ALL costs the
client will bear — including those from affiliates and third parties — must be disclosed. Cannot use
client sophistication as a justification for omission.


Standard V(C) — Record Retention

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must develop and maintain appropriate records to support their
investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and other investment-related
communications with clients and prospective clients.

GUIDANCE

Records support the reasonableness and adequacy of analyses, recommendations, and actions. Records may be
in hard copy or electronic form. Firms bear primary responsibility for record retention, but individual members
must also maintain documentation to support their work.

V(C) KEY RULES
Records are FIRM PROPERTY — not employee property. Work completed for the employer
(research, models, client communications) belongs to the firm.
When leaving: CANNOT take records or files without the firm's EXPRESS WRITTEN CONSENT. This
applies to all formats (hard copy, electronic, portable storage devices, home computers, cell phones).
Re-creating records at a new firm: Must use publicly available information and information obtained
directly from companies — NOT from memory or sources from the prior employer.
New media: Email, text messages, blog posts, and social media posts used in professional activities
MUST ALL be retained.
Regulatory requirements: Must comply with local regulatory retention requirements. In ABSENCE of
regulatory guidance or firm policy, CFA Institute recommends retaining records for AT LEAST 7
YEARS.
Applies to decisions that result in NO change of position (e.g., a decision to hold rather than trade) as
well as to actual trades.

heading: Standard VI — CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
body:
Standard VI(A) — Disclosure of Conflicts [REVISED 2023 — 'AVOID OR DISCLOSE']

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must make full and fair disclosure of all matters that could
reasonably be expected to impair their independence and objectivity or interfere with
respective duties to their clients, prospective clients, and employer. Members and Candidates
must ensure that such disclosures are prominent, are delivered in plain language, and
communicate the relevant information effectively.

GUIDANCE

A conflict of interest = any matter that could REASONABLY BE EXPECTED to impair independence and
objectivity or raise questions about whether judgement is free from bias. The 2023 revision added explicit
emphasis on AVOIDING conflicts, not just disclosing them.

BEST PRACTICE HIERARCHY FOR CONFLICTS
FIRST BEST: AVOID the conflict entirely. If the conflict is reasonably avoidable, members should
avoid it rather than merely disclose it.
SECOND BEST: If the conflict cannot reasonably be avoided, DISCLOSE FULLY AND
PROMINENTLY to all affected parties.
Disclosures must be: PROMINENT (not buried in footnotes), in PLAIN LANGUAGE (not boilerplate),
and must COMMUNICATE THE INFORMATION EFFECTIVELY.

Type of Conflict
Key Guidance
Conflicts to disclose to
EMPLOYERS
Ownership of stocks being analysed or recommended; participation on outside
boards; financial or other pressures that could influence decisions;
compensation from third parties for firm work.
Conflicts to disclose to
CLIENTS
Material beneficial ownership in a recommended security; broker/dealer market-
making activities; investment banking or underwriting relationships; rebates or
service fees from fund classes recommended; compensation structures that
create conflicts.
Stock ownership
Best approach: avoid owning stock in companies being analysed. If
unavoidable, must disclose ownership to employer AND in research reports. If
spouse inherits stock in a covered company, disclose at minimum; best practice
= assign a different analyst.
Board membership
Three conflicts: (1) duty to clients vs. duty to company shareholders; (2)
personal compensation via securities/options that could benefit from trading
recommendations; (3) access to material nonpublic information. Investment
personnel on boards should be isolated from investment decisions by firewalls.
Cross-departmental
conflicts
Sell-side analysts pressured by IB to issue favourable reports; marketing
pressuring analysts for recommendations to win business; broker-sponsored
limited partnerships that the firm manages and then covers.

Compensation
arrangements
Performance bonuses that incentivise short-term results conflicting with client
IPSs must be disclosed to clients. Changes in compensation structure that
create new conflicts must be communicated.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Carter's Badger Stock Promoter Arrangement
Kevin Carter accepts additional compensation from a Badger Company stock promoter to sell
Badger stock to clients. He fails to disclose this to clients OR to his employer. DOUBLE
VIOLATION of I(B) and VI(A): Best practice = avoid the arrangement entirely. Disclosing only to
clients (not employer) is still a violation of VI(A). Disclosing only to employer (not clients) still
violates the duty to disclose conflicts to clients.

✗
Example — Snead Changes Strategy Due to New Bonus Structure
Sarah Snead changes her investment strategy for long-term pension accounts — purchasing high-
beta stocks — after her firm introduces a quarterly performance bonus. She does not disclose the
changed compensation structure to clients. VIOLATION of VI(A): The new bonus structure creates
a conflict between her compensation and her clients' long-term investment objectives; this must be
disclosed.

✗
Example — Fargmon's Wife Inherits Kincaid Stock
Karl Fargmon is asked to write a follow-up report on Kincaid Printing. During the prior year, his
wife inherited US$3 million of Kincaid stock. MINIMUM required: Disclose his wife's ownership to
his employer and in the follow-up report. BEST PRACTICE: Ask his employer to assign a different
analyst to write the report.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VI(A) vs VI(B): VI(A) = avoid or DISCLOSE conflicts (focus on independence and
objectivity). VI(B) = PRIORITY of transactions (client transactions first). These are different
standards. Confusing them is a common exam error.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: 'Avoid or disclose' hierarchy: The 2023 revision made AVOID explicit. The duty to
avoid conflicts where reasonably possible now comes BEFORE the fallback to disclose. Simply
disclosing a conflict does not automatically make the action appropriate — avoidance should
always be considered first.


Standard VI(B) — Priority of Transactions

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Investment transactions for clients and employers must have priority over investment
transactions in which a Member or Candidate is the beneficial owner.

GUIDANCE


Client and employer transactions must take priority over personal transactions. A member with beneficial
ownership includes their own accounts, family accounts, trusts, and retirement accounts. The goal is to prevent
personal trading from adversely affecting client interests.

Nothing is inherently unethical about investment professionals making money from personal investments,
provided: (1) the client is not disadvantaged by the trade; (2) the professional does not benefit from trades
undertaken for clients; (3) applicable regulatory requirements are followed.

KEY APPLICATIONS
Cannot 'front-run': Must not delay placing client orders to trade for personal account first. Personal
trades must come AFTER clients have had adequate opportunity to act.
Having the same investment as clients or being co-invested does NOT by itself create a conflict — the
conflict arises only when personal positions adversely affect client interests.
Family accounts that ARE regular fee-paying client accounts must be treated the SAME as all other
accounts — NEITHER favoured NOR disadvantaged. Toffler violated VI(B) by giving her parents
LOWER priority than other clients.
Personal trading contrary to current recommendations may be necessary in some circumstances
(college tuition payment, margin call) — permissible as long as the three criteria above are met.
Standard VI(B) covers knowledge of PENDING client trades. Members cannot use knowledge of
pending client orders to trade personally ahead of those clients.

RECOMMENDED PROCEDURES

Standard VI(B) Recommended Firm Procedures
Limited IPO participation: Prior approval required for personal IPO participation. Members generally
should not participate in IPOs to avoid any appearance of taking client opportunities.
Restrictions on private placements: Strict limits; appropriate review procedures; receiving private
placements as incentives for business = serious conflict.
Blackout/restricted periods: Establish blackout periods before client trades to prevent front-running.
Reporting: Disclose personal holdings on commencement of employment and annually thereafter.
Require duplicate confirmations of all personal transactions sent to firm. Preclearance procedures for
planned personal trades.
Disclosure of policies: Firm's personal investing policies must be disclosed to clients.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Baker Allocates Hot IPO to Husband First
Carol Baker instructs brokers to buy hot IPO issues for her husband's account whenever they
become available, ahead of her fund clients who are often unable to participate. VIOLATION of
VI(B): Personal trades (including through a family member's account) must come AFTER clients
have been adequately served.

✗
Example — Toffler's Reverse Discrimination Against Parents

Erin Toffler manages her parents' retirement account and always allocates IPO shares to ALL
other clients first, only giving remainder to her parents — to avoid appearing to favour them.
VIOLATION of VI(B): Fee-paying family member accounts must be treated the SAME as other
client accounts — they cannot be systematically disadvantaged any more than they can be
favoured.

✗
Example — Riley Trades Ahead of Published Insurance Report
Seven minutes after a negative closed-circuit broadcast about a major insurance company, Riley
(head of the brokerage's trading department) closes a long call position and establishes a put
position — before the report is disseminated to clients. VIOLATION of VI(B): The firm's own
proprietary trading gave it an advantage over clients who had not yet received the
recommendation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Over-correcting for family account bias is ALSO a violation: Toffler violated VI(B) by
systematically giving her parents' fee-paying account LOWER priority than other clients. Family
fee-paying accounts must receive EQUAL treatment — neither better nor worse.


Standard VI(C) — Referral Fees

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must disclose to their employer, clients, and prospective clients, as
appropriate, any compensation, consideration, or benefit received from or paid to others for
the recommendation of products or services.

GUIDANCE

Standard VI(C) requires disclosure of referral fee arrangements so that clients and employers can: (1) evaluate
any partiality shown in service recommendations, and (2) understand the full cost of the services they receive.
The disclosure obligation applies equally to: (a) fees received for referring clients to others, AND (b) fees paid to
others for referring clients to the member.

VI(C) KEY RULES
Disclosure must be made BEFORE entry into any formal agreement for services — not after the client
relationship is established.
Must disclose the NATURE of the consideration: flat fee vs. percentage, one-time vs. continuing,
performance-based, cash vs. soft dollars vs. non-cash, and the estimated dollar value.
Covers BOTH directions: fees received for sending referrals AND fees paid to others for sending
clients your way.
Internal referral arrangements within the same firm must ALSO be disclosed (e.g., a trust department
paying an employee for referrals to the personal financial management department).
Best practice: employers may completely restrict referral fees. If allowed, require quarterly reporting of
amounts and nature of compensation received.


VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — White Accepts Account Without Disclosing Referral Arrangement
A client is referred to Lewis Brothers by a Brady Securities broker under an undisclosed
arrangement where Lewis Brothers pays Brady in research services and directed commissions.
When the new client says 'Harold Hill at Brady recommended you without qualification,' partner
White accepts the account WITHOUT disclosing the referral arrangement. VIOLATION of VI(C):
The disclosure could have caused the client to question whether the recommendation was
impartial and to evaluate the full cost of the services.

✗
Example — Handley's Internal Referral Fee (Central Trust Bank)
James Handley works in Central Trust's trust department and receives compensation for each
referral he makes to the bank's personal financial management department. He makes several
referrals without disclosing the arrangement to his clients. VIOLATION: Standard VI(C) does not
distinguish between external and INTERNAL referral arrangements — all such fees must be
disclosed.

✗
Example — Roberts Directs Business to Classmate's Brokerage
Katherine Roberts directs a large portion of Katama's commission business to Naushon (run by
her classmate) in exchange for Naushon referring Naushon clients to Roberts. This arrangement
is not disclosed to Katama or to referred clients. VIOLATION of VI(C): Roberts is also failing to
seek best execution for clients (potential III(A) violation), and she is not disclosing the
arrangement to her employer.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: TIMING: Referral fee disclosure must happen BEFORE the client enters into a
formal agreement. Disclosure after the relationship is established is not sufficient.

section_title: Standard VII — RESPONSIBILITIES AS A CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER OR
heading: Standard VII — RESPONSIBILITIES AS A CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER OR
body:
CFA CANDIDATE

Standard VII(A) — Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
Members and Candidates must not engage in any conduct that compromises the reputation
or integrity of CFA Institute or the CFA designation or the integrity, validity, or security of CFA
Institute programs.

GUIDANCE

Standard VII(A) prohibits any conduct that undermines public confidence that the CFA charter represents a level
of achievement based on merit and ethical conduct. The standard covers all CFA Institute programs (CFA, CIPM,
Investment Foundations), not just the CFA exam.

Prohibited Conduct
Permissible Conduct
Giving or receiving assistance (cheating) on
any CFA Institute examination
Expressing personal opinions about CFA Institute, its
policies, and procedures
Violating testing policies (calculator policy,
personal belongings policy, Candidate Pledge)
Discussing non-confidential curriculum material in study
groups preparing for the exam
Providing confidential program/exam
information to candidates or the public: specific
questions, broad topical areas, formulas
TESTED OR NOT TESTED
Stating facts about passed levels after results are officially
published
Continuing to write after time is called — even
a few answers constitute a violation
Factual statements about the CFA Program's rigour,
reputation, and educational value
Sharing exam content online after the exam
(tested topics AND untested topics)
Volunteering for CFA Institute programs with integrity and
confidentiality
Using a volunteer CFA Institute position for
personal business advantage
Stating that you served as a grader (but NOT disclosing
specific questions or marking guidelines)
Misrepresenting information on the
Professional Conduct Statement
Discussing the Programme's structure, requirements, or
general nature

CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION SCOPE
Specific details of questions appearing on the exam — CONFIDENTIAL
Broad topical areas tested on the exam — CONFIDENTIAL
Formulas TESTED on the exam — CONFIDENTIAL
Broad topical areas NOT tested on the exam — CONFIDENTIAL
Formulas NOT tested on the exam — CONFIDENTIAL
All aspects of the exam remain confidential until CFA Institute PUBLICLY RELEASES them.


VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Rossi's Post-Exam Blog Post
Annabelle Rossi posts: 'It was really difficult but fair. I especially liked the derivatives questions —
there were 18! I was surprised there were no questions about IPO allocations at all.' VIOLATION:
(1) Disclosing the number and topic of derivatives questions = disclosing tested topics. (2) Stating
there were 'no questions about IPO allocations' = disclosing what was NOT tested. Both are
confidential until officially released. Writing about general difficulty or how well she did = NOT a
violation.

✗
Example — Ramirez Uses Society Position for Personal Clients
Jose Ramirez is programme chair of a local CFA Institute society. He schedules ONLY his own
consulting clients to present to the society and excludes other companies. VIOLATION: Uses his
volunteer position at CFA Institute to benefit himself and his clients, compromising the reputation
and integrity of CFA Institute.

✗
Example — Warrenski Implies GIPS Committee Knowledge Will Benefit Clients
Marguerite Warrenski, a member of the GIPS Executive Committee, tells clients her committee
membership will allow her to better assist them in keeping up with changes and implicitly suggests
she would use confidential committee information for their benefit. VIOLATION: Using the CFA
Institute volunteer position as a competitive business advantage and implying the use of
confidential information to benefit clients compromises CFA Institute's integrity.

✗
Example — Davis Continues Writing After Time Called
John Davis continues filling in ovals on his answer sheet after the proctor announces 'stop writing
now,' even after repeated instructions to stop. VIOLATION of VII(A): Continuing to write after time
was called constitutes an unfair advantage over other candidates and violates testing policies.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Topics TESTED and topics NOT TESTED are BOTH confidential until officially
released by CFA Institute. Rossi's comment about 'no questions about IPO allocations' = violation
even though she was describing what was NOT on the exam.


Standard VII(B) — Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA
Program

EXACT STANDARD WORDING
When referring to CFA Institute, CFA Institute membership, the CFA designation, or
candidacy in the CFA Program, Members and Candidates must not misrepresent or
exaggerate the meaning or implications of membership in CFA Institute, holding the CFA
designation, or candidacy in the CFA Program.

GUIDANCE


Standard VII(B) prevents promotional efforts that make promises or guarantees tied to the CFA designation.
Members must not exaggerate the meaning or implications of holding the designation. Factual statements and
statements of opinion (clearly labelled as opinion) about the CFA Programme's value are permissible.

MAINTAINING CHARTERHOLDER STATUS

To MAINTAIN charterholder status, members must ANNUALLY: (1) submit a completed Professional Conduct
Statement renewing commitment to Code and Standards AND the CFA Institute Professional Conduct
Programme, AND (2) pay applicable CFA Institute membership dues. Failure on EITHER requirement = charter
suspended, right to use CFA designation forfeited until reactivated.

Proper References
Improper References
'I passed Level I of the CFA Program'
'CFA Level II' on a business card — implies a partial
designation
'I am a 2026 Level III candidate in the CFA
Program'
'CFA Expected 2026' — implies an expected/partial
designation
'John Smith passed all three CFA exams in
three consecutive years' (factual)
'John Smith is among the elite, having passed all three
exams consecutively' — implies superior ability
'The CFA designation attests to success in a
rigorous study program'
'CFA charterholders achieve better performance results' —
exaggerates meaning
'I passed all three levels and may be eligible for
the CFA charter upon completion of required
work experience'
'CFA, Charter Pending' — no such partial designation
exists
'Completion of the CFA Program has enhanced
my portfolio management skills'
'As a CFA charterholder, I am the most qualified to
manage client investments' — overstates meaning
'I enrolled in the CFA Program to obtain the
highest credentials in investment management'
Using the CFA designation when membership has lapsed
(dues not paid, PCS not submitted)

PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT STATEMENT (PCS) DISCLOSURE

Members MUST disclose on the annual PCS all matters questioning their professional conduct — including civil
litigation, criminal investigations, regulatory investigations, or being the subject of a written complaint. Failure to
disclose = violation of VII(A) (misrepresenting information on the PCS). A member under investigation for their
OWN conduct must disclose it. A member who knows of another person's investigation is ENCOURAGED but
NOT required to report it.

VIOLATION EXAMPLES

✗
Example — Vasseur Prints 'CFA' on Business Cards After Lapse
Louis Vasseur resigns as an investment analyst, travels abroad for two years, and does not file his
PCS or pay dues. When he returns as an independent contractor, he prints 'CFA' on his business
cards WITHOUT reinstating his membership. VIOLATION: His right to use the CFA designation
was suspended when he failed to file the PCS and pay dues. He may NOT use the designation
until membership is reactivated by filing the PCS and paying dues.


✗
Example — AZ Advisors Links CFA to Superior Performance
AZ Investment Advisors advertises that all principals passed all three exams on the first attempt
and prominently links this to the suggestion that the firm's mutual funds have achieved superior
performance. VIOLATION: May state the factual statement about passing on the first attempt, but
may NOT link this fact to performance claims or imply that passing on the first try makes someone
a superior investor.

✗
Example — 'Expert CFA' Blog Pseudonym
Barry Glass incorporates research from an online blog called 'Expert CFA' into his fund's models.
VIOLATION by the blog author: Using the CFA designation inappropriately in an anonymous
pseudonym. Authorship of online content must include the charterholder's full name along with
any reference to the CFA designation — anonymous use is prohibited.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: A charterholder whose dues have lapsed CANNOT use the CFA designation on
business cards or anywhere else — even if they are actively working in the investment profession.
May say 'I was a CFA charterholder from X to Y.'

⚠
EXAM TRAP: Candidates may NOT imply a partial designation. 'CFA Level II' on a business card
= violation. 'I passed Level II of the CFA Program' = acceptable factual statement.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: PCS disclosure: Must disclose ALL matters questioning professional conduct —
including investigations that are merely pending and not yet resolved. The investigation's outcome
is irrelevant — the existence of the investigation must be disclosed.
