MASTER EXAM TRAPS — ALL STANDARDS

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(A) JURISDICTION: The 'more strict' law or regulation = imposes greater
restrictions OR requires greater protections for investors. Always follow the stricter floor — either
applicable law or Code and Standards.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(A) LEGAL COUNSEL: Relying on counsel's advice that wrongdoing is 'hard to
prove' does NOT absolve the member. Must still report and potentially dissociate.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(B) CLIENT GIFTS vs. THIRD-PARTY GIFTS: Client gifts are less problematic
because a compensation relationship exists. But disclosure to employer is STILL required. Lavish
client gifts must still be rejected if they could affect objectivity.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(C) UNINTENTIONAL vs. KNOWING: Promptly correcting an unintentional error is
NOT a violation. But distributing erroneous materials over extended periods without correction =
violation (should have known).

⚠
EXAM TRAP: I(E) COMPETENCE: Passing a licensing exam WITHOUT learning the material
does NOT demonstrate competency. Competence requires actually possessing the required
knowledge — not merely clearing a regulatory hurdle.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: II(A) 'CAUSING OTHERS TO ACT': Prohibited equally with acting directly on MNPI.
Telling a family member who then trades = violation by the member who communicated.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: II(A) MOSAIC CONCLUSION: The fact that an analyst's conclusion is material does
NOT make it a violation if the conclusion was derived through public + nonmaterial nonpublic
information. The test is HOW it was reached.


⚠
EXAM TRAP: II(A) EXPERT NETWORKS: A signed non-disclosure agreement from the expert
does NOT protect the member if the expert provides MNPI. The member must personally assess
materiality and must not trade if MNPI was received.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(A) CLIENT IDENTIFICATION: For pension plans and trusts, the CLIENT is the
BENEFICIARIES — not the plan sponsor or the company management. Decisions must be based
on beneficiary interests ONLY.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(A) SOFT DOLLARS vs. DIRECTED BROKERAGE: Soft dollars = manager uses
client brokerage (at manager's discretion) for research. Directed brokerage = CLIENT directs
manager to use a specific broker. Directed brokerage is permissible but member must still seek
best execution and disclose to client that best execution may not be obtained.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(B) 'FAIRLY' NOT 'EQUALLY': Different service levels for different fees ARE
acceptable if disclosed and available to all. What is NEVER acceptable: giving one client
ADVANCE information that disadvantages other clients.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(B) CHANGING RECOMMENDATIONS: If a client calls to place an order contrary
to a changed recommendation they haven't received, ADVISE them of the change BEFORE
accepting the order.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(C) MANAGING TO A MANDATE: Not responsible for individual investor suitability
when managing a pooled fund — only for investing consistently with the stated mandate.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(D) PERFORMANCE PRESENTATION: Must ALWAYS include terminated
accounts (with disclosure of termination date). Omitting terminated accounts inflates apparent
performance.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: III(E) CONFIDENTIALITY: The obligation extends to FORMER and PROSPECTIVE
clients — not only current ones. Cooperating with PCP investigations is NOT a violation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: IV(A) DEPARTURE: Written permission must come from the FIRM — not from a
colleague, supervisor, or client. Even if a colleague consents, using firm records without firm-level
authorisation is a violation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: IV(B) WRITTEN CONSENT: Email qualifies as 'written consent' in IV(B). The FORM
can be informal; what matters is documentation and obtaining it BEFORE accepting the
arrangement.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: IV(C) SUPERVISION: Must do MORE than warn the employee and report up the
chain when a violation is discovered. Must INVESTIGATE and LIMIT the employee's activities
PENDING the investigation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: V(A) SUBMANAGER SELECTION: Selecting based solely on lowest fees violates
BOTH V(A) (insufficient diligence) AND III(C) (suitability of the submanager for the client).


⚠
EXAM TRAP: V(B) COST DISCLOSURE: ALL costs the client bears — including those from
affiliates and third parties — must be disclosed. Cannot claim client sophistication as a basis for
omission.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VI(A) BEST PRACTICE HIERARCHY: AVOID conflicts first (2023 revision). If
unavoidable, DISCLOSE prominently in plain language. Disclosure does NOT automatically make
a conflict acceptable.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VI(B) FAMILY ACCOUNT DISCRIMINATION: Deliberately giving family fee-paying
accounts LOWER priority is ALSO a violation. Family accounts must receive EQUAL treatment —
not worse, not better.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VI(C) TIMING: Referral fee disclosure must be made BEFORE the client enters into
a formal agreement.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VII(A) BOTH TESTED AND UNTESTED TOPICS: Topics NOT tested on the exam
are ALSO confidential. Saying 'there were no questions on IPO allocations' is a violation.

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VII(B) LAPSED CHARTER: A charterholder whose dues have lapsed CANNOT use
the CFA designation on business cards, emails, or any other communications. May say 'I was a
CFA charterholder from X to Y.'

⚠
EXAM TRAP: VII(B) PARTIAL DESIGNATION: Candidates may NEVER imply a partial
designation. 'CFA Level II' on a business card = violation. 'I passed Level II of the CFA Program' =
acceptable factual statement.

heading: Standard I — PROFESSIONALISM
body:
I(A) Knowledge of the Law

The core rule: when applicable law and the Code and Standards conflict, follow the MORE STRICT of the two.
'Applicable law' = the law governing the member's conduct based on specific facts and circumstances.

Jurisdiction Scenario
Required Action
No Securities (NS) country —
no local law applies
Follow Code and Standards (they become the floor)
Less Strict (LS) country —
local law less strict than Code
Follow Code and Standards (stricter floor wins)
More Strict (MS) country —
local law stricter than Code
Follow MS local law (stricter floor wins)
Member in LS, does business
in NS; LS law applies
Follow Code and Standards
Member in MS, does business
in LS; MS applies but says use
local law
Follow Code and Standards (local law LS < Code)
Member in MS, does business
in LS; MS applies but says use
client home country law (MS)
Follow MS law (client home country is MS, which is stricter)

I(A) Dissociation Process — Steps When Employer Violates Law or Code
Step 1: Bring violation to attention of supervisor or compliance department.
Step 2: If unsuccessful at Step 1 — step away and DISSOCIATE from the activity. This may mean:
removing name from reports, refusing the assignment, requesting different assignment.
Step 3: In extreme cases, dissociation may require resignation from employment.
Whistleblowing is NOT compelled by the Code unless required by applicable law. It may be prudent;
consult legal counsel.
Inaction + continued association with violators = may be construed as participation or assistance in the
violation.


I(A) Recommended Procedures for Members
Stay informed: Regular updates on regulatory changes; participate in continuing education.
Review procedures: Ensure firm's compliance procedures reflect current law.
Maintain current files: Keep accessible copies of applicable statutes, rules, regulations.
Legal counsel: When in doubt, seek compliance/legal advice — but reliance on legal advice does NOT
absolve member of responsibility to comply.
Distribution area laws: Understand applicable laws for each country/region where investment products
are developed and distributed.

!
Relying on legal counsel's advice does NOT automatically absolve a member of responsibility to
comply with law (Brown prospectus example — counsel said hard to prove but Brown still must
report and may need to sever connection with underwriting).

!
Social media compliance: Members must stay current with evolving regulations governing
electronic/social media communications. Using platforms in a way that conflicts with existing
regulations = violation of I(A), even if the member was unaware of the new rules.

I(B) Independence and Objectivity

Core obligation: maintain independence and objectivity so clients benefit from unbiased work. Resist ALL
attempts by external parties to influence investment recommendations or actions.

Pressure Source
Key Guidance
Gifts and
entertainment
Modest gifts are acceptable. Lavish gifts/entertainment must be rejected. Best
practice: reject any offer that COULD be expected to threaten independence. Gifts
from clients (already in compensation relationship) are less problematic but must still
be disclosed to employer.
IPO allocations to
personal accounts
PROHIBITED. Accepting oversubscribed IPO shares in personal account from
companies seeking research coverage = compromises independence.
Buy-side pressure on
sell-side
Portfolio managers must NOT threaten or engage in retaliation against sell-side
analysts who issue negative ratings. Implicit pressure (fear of retaliation) is also
improper.
Investment banking
relationships
IB personnel must NOT have authority to approve, disapprove, or change research
reports. Analyst compensation must NOT be linked to specific IB assignments.
Firewalls required between IB and research.
Issuer-paid research
Flat fee ONLY — never contingent on conclusions, never stock warrants or equity.
Full disclosure of arrangement required. Research must still be objective, diligent,
and thorough.
Travel funding
Best practice: use commercial transport at own/firm expense. May accept modestly
arranged travel ONLY when commercial transport unavailable (e.g., remote property
tour).
Manager selection
(pay-to-play)
Must NOT solicit gifts/contributions when hiring managers. Must NOT offer
gifts/contributions when trying to win mandates. The infamous US pay-to-play
scandal (pension fund political donations) = clear violation.

Credit rating
agencies
Must prevent undue influence from sponsoring companies. Develop firewalls
between rating and advisory businesses within the same firm.

I(B) Recommended Procedures
Protect integrity of opinions: Every report reflects unbiased analyst opinion. Compensation systems
must protect research independence.
Create a restricted list: If firm unwilling to permit adverse opinions on a corporate client, remove
company from research universe and put on restricted list (factual info only).
Restrict special cost arrangements: Pay own commercial transport/hotel when visiting issuers. No
corporate aircraft unless commercial unavailable.
Limit gifts: Strictly limit acceptance to token items. Set a firm value limit based on local customs (per
gift or aggregate annual limit).
Independence policy: Formal written policy; analysts do NOT report to, and are not supervised by,
departments that could compromise their independence.
Appointed compliance officer: Senior officer with oversight responsibility for ethics compliance and
violation reporting procedures.

!
Travel example: Taylor's chartered flights and modest motel accommodations for a genuine
business site tour were NOT a violation — the trip was strictly for business, accommodations
modest, and Taylor's objectivity was not compromised. Grant's World Cup tickets + resort stay +
limousine = VIOLATION (substantial gift that could impair independence).

!
Issuer-paid research: Compensation contingent on conclusions or based on stock warrants/equity
= VIOLATION. Only a flat fee NOT tied to conclusions complies with I(B).

I(C) Misrepresentation

A misrepresentation is any untrue statement, any omission of a fact, or any statement that is otherwise false or
misleading — whether written, oral, or electronic. 'Knowingly' = knew or should have known.

Type of
Misrepresentation
Key Points
Qualifications/credentials Cannot misrepresent own qualifications, firm's qualifications, or credentials of
third parties. Includes uncorrected errors in promotional material.
Performance records
Cherry-picking composites = misrepresentation. Cannot omit terminated
accounts without disclosure. Cannot present simulated results without clearly
identifying them as simulated.
Guarantees
Cannot guarantee specific return on volatile investments. Exception: products
with guarantees built into the structure (e.g., FDIC-insured deposits), or institution
has agreed to cover losses.
Benchmarks
Misrepresenting performance by using inappropriate benchmarks = violation.
Cannot 'shop' for higher valuations. Must use most appropriate available
benchmark.

Social media
Same standards apply. Perceived anonymity does NOT excuse
misrepresentations. Online pseudonyms tagged with 'CFA' = violation of I(C) and
VII(B).
Plagiarism
Copying without attribution; distributing third-party research as own work; using
others' models without credit; changing a few words on a report and signing it.
Must acknowledge sources of ALL ideas, data, models, charts, charts, and
methodologies.
Omissions
Knowingly omitting information that alters investment decisions =
misrepresentation. Model outputs cannot be presented as 'facts.'
Work done for employer
Research and models developed while employed = firm's property. Firm may
issue future reports without attribution to the analyst. Analyst CANNOT reissue a
previously released report solely under own name after departure.

!
Unintentional errors that are promptly corrected = NOT a violation of I(C). But known errors that
are not corrected = violation. Muhammad's promotional material with incorrect degree = violation
because he distributed it numerous times over an extended period and should have known of the
error.

!
Distributing third-party outsourced research is PERMITTED as long as member does not
represent themselves as the author. Must disclose that research comes from another source.

I(D) Misconduct

Standard I(D) covers ALL conduct reflecting adversely on professional reputation, integrity, or competence — not
just illegal acts. Conduct need not be illegal to violate I(D).

I(D) Key Applications
VIOLATION: Alcohol abuse during business hours — detrimental to ability to fulfill professional
responsibilities.
VIOLATION: Fraudulent expense claims (altering health insurance bills, fraudulent reimbursements).
VIOLATION: Charity fraud — even for non-professional purposes, if it involves dishonesty, fraud,
deceit.
NOT a violation: Civil disobedience for personal beliefs (e.g., environmental protests) — does not
reflect on professional integrity or competence.
VIOLATION: Absence of appropriate conduct — failure to perform necessary due diligence. Not just
acts of commission, but acts of omission that damage trust.
NOTE: Personal bankruptcy is generally NOT a violation UNLESS the circumstances involve
fraudulent or deceitful business conduct.

I(E) Competence [NEW 2023]

Members must ACT with competence AND MAINTAIN competence. Competence = knowledge + skills + abilities
appropriate for one's specific role. No specific CE program is required — competence can be maintained in many
ways.


Key Concept
Guidance
Competence is role-
specific
Different roles require different competencies. An accounting expert may not be
competent to assess GIPS compliance. A regulatory attorney is not competent in
sales licensing rules without study.
Failure ≠
incompetence
A competent professional may not always make profitable decisions or accurately
predict future events. Lack of competence cannot necessarily be determined by an
unsuccessful outcome.
Role changes
When responsibilities expand or change, must acquire new competencies BEFORE
undertaking the new role. Cannot simply rely on passing a licensing exam if the
material was not actually learned.
No specific CE
required
Members can maintain competence via: CE programs, professional certifications,
conferences, employer training, self-study, expert groups, staying current with
relevant developments.
Investing in
unfamiliar assets
Purchasing cryptocurrency products for clients without understanding the asset or its
suitability = violation. Cannot let client urgency override competence requirements.

!
I(E): Passing a licensing exam does NOT by itself demonstrate competency if the material was
not genuinely learned (Glusker example). Competence requires actually possessing the required
knowledge and skills, not merely clearing a regulatory hurdle.

section_title: Standard II — INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS
heading: Standard II — INTEGRITY OF CAPITAL MARKETS
body:
II(A) Material Nonpublic Information

Members in possession of material nonpublic information must NOT act on it AND must NOT cause others to act
on it. The prohibition extends to all investment instruments — securities, derivatives, mutual funds.

Concept
Definition and Key Points
What is 'material'?
Information is material if: (1) its disclosure would PROBABLY impact the price of a
security, OR (2) a REASONABLE INVESTOR would want to know it before making
an investment decision. Examples: earnings, M&A, management changes,
regulatory approvals, large trade orders, analyst rating changes, bankruptcy,
significant legal disputes. Ambiguous price effect → less material. Unreliable source
→ less material.
What is 'nonpublic'?
Information is nonpublic until disseminated or available to the marketplace in
GENERAL (not a select group). A briefing to a room full of analysts does NOT
constitute public dissemination. Company filings, internet press releases
disseminated widely = public. Social media groups with membership requirements
may NOT constitute public dissemination.
Selective disclosure
Company briefings to select analysts = nonpublic until broadly disseminated.
Analysts receiving selective guidance/interpretation of public data must treat it as
nonpublic if material.
Due diligence
exception
Using insider information for legitimate due diligence (mergers, loan underwriting,
credit ratings) per a specific business agreement = permissible. Using that same
insider info to trade = violation.


Mosaic Theory — The KEY Concept
Definition: A financial analyst may use MATERIAL PUBLIC information PLUS NONMATERIAL
NONPUBLIC information to reach conclusions that would be material if communicated directly by a
corporate insider.
The conclusions reached through mosaic analysis are permissible to act on — even if those
conclusions would qualify as material inside information had they been provided directly.
CRITICAL: Must document all research used (public and nonmaterial nonpublic) to demonstrate
conclusions were reached through appropriate means — not through insider information.
Expert networks: Compensation to industry experts is permitted. BUT member is responsible for
ensuring no material nonpublic information is received or acted on. Cannot simply rely on agreements
signed by experts.
Analyst research reports: If a well-known analyst changes recommendation, that change itself may be
material — but the analyst is NOT required to make the report public. Clients who pay for the research
are entitled to it first.

Firewall Elements — Recommended Procedures
Substantial control of interdepartmental communications — through a compliance/legal clearance
area.
Review of employee trading via 'watch,' 'restricted,' and 'rumor' lists.
Documentation of procedures limiting information flow between departments.
Physical separation of IB/corporate finance from sales/research; bank commercial lending from
trust/research.
No personnel overlap between IB and research — analyst who assists IB on a deal must be treated as
an IB employee and CANNOT return to research function until all information is public.
Reporting system: compliance officer decides what can be shared across the wall.
Personal trading limitations and preclearance procedures for employees.

!
Mosaic trap: A conclusion that is material is NOT a violation if it was reached through public +
nonmaterial nonpublic information. The test is HOW the analyst arrived at the conclusion, not
whether the conclusion itself is material.

!
Expert networks: Even when experts sign agreements not to disclose MNPI, if they DO provide
material nonpublic information, the member is PROHIBITED from trading until the information
becomes public. The agreement does not protect the member.

!
Social media as 'public': Membership-restricted groups are NOT necessarily public. Information
must be verifiable as also accessible from a source available to the general public (company
filings, press releases, websites) before it can be treated as public.

II(B) Market Manipulation


Two types: (1) Information-based manipulation — spreading false or misleading information. (2) Transaction-
based manipulation — trades designed to distort price or volume. INTENT is critical. Legitimate strategies based
on perceived market inefficiencies are permitted.

II(B) Key Examples
VIOLATION: Pump-and-dump — spreading positive false rumors to inflate price, then selling.
Weinberg's internet chat room rumors about railroad = violation.
VIOLATION: Creating artificial volume — trading same stock between two accounts to create
appearance of activity and liquidity (Sekar's fund-to-fund trading).
VIOLATION: Manipulating model inputs — Mandeville's structured products: using only positive
scenarios in rating models to achieve better ratings. Adjusting inputs to achieve desired outputs =
manipulation.
VIOLATION: Timed negative report to maximize benefit of clients' short positions — Murphy releasing
sensationalized report during issuer's quiet period specifically to create downward price pressure.
VIOLATION: Exaggerating earnings projection to 'force' a company response (Gordon's Hygene
example).
PERMITTED: Legitimate trading strategies based on perceived market inefficiencies, even if they
move prices, as long as not designed to deceive.
PERMITTED: Market making that continues during MNPI possession — market makers should remain
passive (take only contra side of unsolicited customer trades).

!
Intent matters for II(B). The question is whether the conduct was designed to artificially distort
prices or deceive market participants. King violated II(B) by creating false online rumors even
though he personally profited nothing — intent to manipulate is what matters, not profit motive.

heading: Standard III — DUTIES TO CLIENTS
body:
III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care

Client interests are paramount. Members must exercise the same level of prudence, judgment, and care they
would apply to their OWN interests in similar circumstances. Standard III(A) applies regardless of legally imposed
fiduciary duties.

Key Concept
Guidance
Who is the client?
For individual portfolios: the individual. For pension/trust: the BENEFICIARIES, not
the plan sponsor or trustees. For funds managed to a mandate: the stated mandate
itself. Members managing a fund are NOT responsible for suitability for individual
investors buying fund shares.
Soft dollars
Using client brokerage to purchase research that benefits clients = acceptable. Using
client brokerage for goods/services that benefit the manager but NOT clients =
violation of duty of loyalty.
Directed brokerage
Client directing brokerage is acceptable — it's the client's money. But member must:
(1) still seek best execution, (2) disclose to client they may not be getting best
execution from the directed broker.

Proxy voting
Proxies have economic value — must vote in an informed manner. Voting blindly
with management on non-routine governance issues = potential violation. Cost-
benefit analysis may justify not voting ALL proxies in every case. Must disclose proxy
voting policies to clients.
Excessive trading
Trading beyond what is necessary to achieve client objectives in order to generate
commissions = violation. Churning = clear violation.
Family accounts
Family member accounts that are regular fee-paying client accounts must be treated
the same as all other accounts — neither favoured NOR excluded.

!
Client identification trap: Wiley (pension trustee) violated III(A) by buying overvalued stock to
support management during a takeover bid. The BENEFICIARIES of the plan are the client — not
the company management, not the employer. The trustee must evaluate the takeover offer on its
merits for beneficiaries.

!
Soft dollars trap: Research payments must benefit the CLIENT. Using client brokerage to pay for
manager's rent, offices, or personal travel = violation. Parker's hotel expenses during vacation
days attached to a research trip = violation.

III(B) Fair Dealing

'Fairly' NOT 'equally.' Members cannot reach all clients at exactly the same time. Different service levels are
permitted if disclosed and available to all. The standard covers: (1) investment recommendations, and (2)
investment actions.

Area
Key Rules
Dissemination of
recommendations
Must ensure all clients have a FAIR OPPORTUNITY to act on every
recommendation. New recommendations = simultaneous dissemination to all clients
and firm personnel. Limit time between decision and dissemination.
Changing
recommendations
Material changes must be communicated to all clients who have acted on or been
affected by the earlier advice. If client places order contrary to a changed
recommendation they haven't received, ADVISE them of the change before
accepting the order.
Different service
levels
Permitted if: (1) disclosed to all clients, (2) available to everyone (not selective).
Calling 3 largest clients after widely distributing a report to discuss in detail = NOT a
violation. Calling those 3 clients BEFORE distributing = violation.
IPO oversubscription
Distribute on a PRO-RATA basis to all subscribing clients. Members must forgo their
own/family allocations to free shares for clients. Hot issues = bona fide public
distribution required; cannot use as reward or incentive.
Block trades
All participating accounts receive the SAME execution price and commission. Partial
fills = allocated PRO-RATA based on order size. Assigning profitable trades to
favoured clients and losses to others AFTER the fact = clear violation.
Social media
Investment recommendations distributed to a limited social media audience before
all clients = violation (Burdette sending X post with buy recommendation).

!
Fair dealing vs. equal treatment: Different service levels for different fees ARE acceptable. What
is NOT acceptable is giving one client advance information that disadvantages other clients.

Ames violated III(B) by discussing the pending recommendation with clients at a luncheon
BEFORE the formal dissemination.

III(C) Suitability

Two contexts: (1) Advisory relationship — detailed suitability analysis required. (2) Managing to a mandate —
must only make recommendations consistent with stated mandate, not responsible for suitability for individual
investors.

IPS Components — Must Cover All RRIITLU
Return Objectives — income, growth, maintenance of purchasing power.
Risk Tolerance — appropriate risk given financial situation, time horizon, psychological factors.
Investment Constraints: (1) Time Horizon — when funds needed; (2) Liquidity — cash flow needs; (3)
Legal and Regulatory — applicable laws, trust restrictions; (4) Taxes — tax situation of client; (5)
Unique Circumstances — client-specific preferences, restrictions.
IPS must also identify roles/responsibilities and schedules for review.
Updates: At least ANNUALLY and prior to material changes. Clients must be encouraged to disclose
complete financial picture for effective suitability analysis.

III(C) Critical Rules
Suitability is assessed in context of the CLIENT'S TOTAL PORTFOLIO — not on a security-by-
security basis. A high-risk security may be suitable if it represents 2% of a diversified portfolio.
Unsolicited unsuitable trades: First, discuss concerns with client and educate. If material impact on
portfolio → update IPS to reflect change. If client insists and no IPS update → member must evaluate
whether to continue the advisory arrangement.
Managing to a mandate: NOT responsible for suitability for individual fund investors. Managing a
large-cap income fund → cannot invest heavily in speculative small-cap stocks regardless of client
requests.
Submanager selection: Must conduct full due diligence including risk profile match — selecting solely
on lowest fees = violation of III(C).
Leverage and derivatives: Pay special attention to leverage inherent in complex products when
assessing suitability.
Investment locked up for 3+ years in portfolio that requires 'highly liquid investments' = violation even if
total equity exposure stays within limits (Gubler private equity example).

!
Managing to a mandate vs. advisory: When managing to a pooled fund's stated mandate, the
manager is only responsible for investing consistently with the mandate — NOT for whether the
fund is appropriate for each underlying investor. Individual investor suitability is the responsibility
of whoever has the advisory relationship with that investor.

III(D) Performance Presentation


Must make REASONABLE EFFORTS to ensure performance information is fair, accurate, and complete. Applies
to individual accounts, composites, analysts' recommendation records.

Rule
Detail
GIPS compliance
Best practice. Firms should be encouraged to comply with GIPS. When claiming
GIPS compliance, must meet ALL requirements — including asset-weighted
composites.
Composites
Must include TERMINATED accounts with clear indication of when terminated.
Cannot cherry-pick accounts to inflate performance. Manipulating composite
construction = violation of I(C) as well as III(D).
Simulated results
Must clearly identify simulated/model results as such. Cannot present backtested
results without disclosing they are simulated.
Prior firm performance
Permissible to show performance earned at a prior firm IF accompanied by
disclosure of: (1) where performance was earned, (2) member's specific role.
Attribution methodology
changes
Must notify clients. If changing, must show both old and new methodology and
explain why new is preferred — to allow clients to make meaningful comparisons.
Performance
calculation
methodology
Cannot show whichever of time-weighted or money-weighted returns is higher
without disclosure. Full information = clients have sufficient info to judge
performance.

III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality

Confidentiality obligation covers CURRENT, FORMER, and PROSPECTIVE clients. Three exceptions to the
confidentiality rule: (1) illegal activities by the client, (2) disclosure required by law, (3) client permits disclosure.

III(E) Key Applications
Former client information: Obligation continues after relationship ends. Client may expressly authorise
disclosure, then member may follow terms of the authorisation.
Electronic security: Members must follow employer's policies on electronic communication and data
storage. Do not need to become IT security experts but must understand firm policies. Regular training
for ALL staff (including non-investment staff) on confidentiality.
CFA Institute PCP investigations: Members should consider the PCP as an extension of themselves.
Forwarding confidential client information to the PCP in support of an investigation = NOT a violation
of III(E). Members encouraged to cooperate.
Accidental disclosure BY client: If a client accidentally discloses confidential information (e.g., posting
personal financial details on a semi-public social media group), the MEMBER is not in violation if
reasonable precautions were taken (Moody example — she warned clients not to post confidential
info).
When in doubt about disclosure: Ask (1) In what context was info disclosed? (2) Is it background
material that would improve service?

!
III(E) exception for illegal activity: If client is engaged in illegal activities, member MAY have a
duty to disclose to authorities. However, member should consult legal counsel and employer
compliance before acting. It is NOT automatic — applicable law determines whether disclosure is
required or merely permitted.

heading: Standard IV — DUTIES TO EMPLOYERS
body:
IV(A) Loyalty

Must act for employer's benefit. Must not: deprive employer of skills/abilities, divulge confidential info, or cause
harm. Does NOT mean employer interests above personal/family obligations in all areas of life — only 'in matters
related to employment.'

Scenario
Rule
Independent practice
while employed
Must NOTIFY employer of: (1) types of services to be rendered, (2) expected
duration, (3) compensation. Must receive CONSENT before rendering services.
'Practice' = services employer currently provides for remuneration. 'Preparations'
alone are permissible.
Leaving an employer
Must not: solicit clients BEFORE resignation is effective, take records/files without
written permission, misappropriate trade secrets. Permissible preparations (on
own time) are allowed before resignation.
After departure
Skills and experience are NOT confidential. Simple knowledge of client names is
generally not confidential. May use public information to contact former clients
(absent noncompete agreement). Cannot use client lists/records from former
employer.
Social media accounts
Firm-approved professional accounts = firm property. Must transfer or delete as
directed. Best practice: maintain SEPARATE personal and professional social
media accounts.
Whistleblowing
Permitted — even required — when it protects clients or the integrity of the market.
NOT permitted for personal gain. Actions that would normally violate IV(A)
(contradicting employer, copying records) may be justified when clearly aimed at
protecting clients or market integrity.
Noncompete
agreements
Must review terms carefully when leaving. Must comply with applicable restrictions.

!
Solicitation before resignation = violation. Contact with former clients AFTER departure using only
public information = permissible (if no noncompete). Contact using employer records = violation.
McQuillen: obtained numbers from public records, cleared personal computer of employer
records → NOT a violation.

!
Downloading or copying employer research/models before leaving — even with consent of a
colleague = violation. Written permission must come from the EMPLOYER (firm), not a colleague.
Acertado: had Boswin's consent to download model but NOT Greenfield Financial Corporation's
consent → violation of IV(A).

IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements

Must obtain WRITTEN CONSENT from employer before accepting any gift, benefit, compensation, or
consideration that competes with or could create a conflict with employer's interests. Written consent = any
documented form, including email.


IV(B) Key Rules
Disclosure is required BEFORE accepting the arrangement — if not possible before, must disclose
previously accepted benefits promptly.
Written report to supervisor and compliance officer must include: nature of compensation, approximate
amount, duration of agreement.
Includes NON-MONETARY benefits: board membership perks, club memberships, free travel —
Jones serving on Exercise Unlimited board receiving free memberships = violation when not disclosed.
Part-time status: Discuss potential limitations during negotiation/hiring. Requirements still apply to
limitations identified at that time.

IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors

Supervisors must make reasonable efforts to PREVENT AND DETECT violations — not just detect after the fact.
ANY investment professional with control or influence over employees exercises supervisory responsibility,
regardless of whether those employees are CFA members.

Obligation
Detail
Establishing
compliance systems
Must understand what constitutes an adequate compliance system. Ensure
procedures are established, documented, communicated, and FOLLOWED.
Procedures must be in place BEFORE violations occur.
Response to
discovered violations
PROMPT investigation. Not sufficient to: warn employee, report up the chain, or
rely on employee assurances. Must: place limits on employee's activities, increase
monitoring, pending investigation outcome.
Inadequate system
If cannot discharge supervisory responsibilities due to absent/inadequate
compliance system, must bring to senior management's attention and recommend
corrective action. May need to DECLINE supervisory responsibility in writing until
fixed.
Supervision includes
detection
Must make reasonable efforts to detect violations through periodic review of
employee actions and trading. If procedures are in place and followed but a
violation still occurs, member may NOT be in violation — but fact that violations
DO occur may indicate procedures are inadequate.
Incentive structures
Must not encourage unethical behaviour. Compensation systems that reward
short-term performance over client interests can pressure employees to act
unethically.

!
IV(C) — Supervision extends beyond direct reports. Any investment professional with employees
subject to their 'control or influence' is a supervisor — not just formal direct managers. The
standard explicitly says supervisors cannot avoid responsibility by delegating to subordinates
without also instructing those subordinates on compliance expectations.

!
IV(C) — Discovering a violation: Must promptly investigate AND limit employee's activities.
Simply telling the employee to stop and reporting up the chain is NOT sufficient. Henry's failure to
set up record-keeping and review procedures for Tabbing's futures trading = violation even
though he 'assigned' her the role without any governance framework.

heading: Standard V — INVESTMENT ANALYSIS, RECOMMENDATIONS,
body:
AND ACTIONS

V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis

All investment analyses and recommendations must be based on diligent, independent research with a
reasonable and adequate basis. The required level of diligence varies based on the member's role, the type of
product, and available resources.

Context
Key Requirements
Primary research
Must conduct thorough independent research. Cannot base recommendations on
overheard opinions (Willier's hallway-heard analyst opinion), conventional wisdom,
or news articles alone (Witt's news-article report).
Secondary/third-party
research
May rely on it but must make reasonable and diligent efforts to determine whether
the research is SOUND. Evaluate: assumptions used, rigour of analysis,
timeliness, objectivity and independence. If reason to suspect unsound basis,
cannot rely on it.
Quantitative models
Must understand parameters, assumptions, and limitations. Not required to be
experts in every technical aspect, but must understand how results are used. Must
test output BEFORE incorporating into recommendations. Components of a
strategy must be evaluated as an INTEGRATED whole — not just individually
(Liakos derivative strategy failure).
Model development
Higher standard for those who create/maintain models. Must understand technical
aspects fully. Thorough testing — including negative/stress scenarios — before
distribution. Cannot substitute limited new data for full required data update
(Dupont's 'quick fix' = violation).
External
adviser/subadviser
selection
Full due diligence required: code of ethics, compliance/internal controls, quality of
performance information, investment process adherence. Cannot select solely on
lowest fees.
Group research
May be identified with group report even if personal view differs — as long as
process is sound and conclusions have reasonable basis. Can ask to have name
removed if desired.

!
Selecting submanagers based solely on fees = violation of both V(A) and III(C). Due diligence
must cover investment process, performance history, risk profile match, compliance, code of
ethics — not just cost structure.

V(B) Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients [REVISED 2023]

Five requirements: (1) Disclose nature of services AND costs. (2) Disclose investment process and promptly
disclose material changes. (3) Disclose significant risks and limitations. (4) Use reasonable judgment on which
factors to include. (5) Distinguish fact from opinion.

Requirement
Key Details
Nature of services and
COSTS [NEW]
Must disclose all costs CLIENT will bear — including costs from affiliates, related
entities, and third parties. Cannot rely on client sophistication as reason not to

disclose. Need not specify exact dollar amounts if not determinable in advance
(e.g., fee based on AUM). Disclosures are ONGOING — must update clients when
services or costs change.
Investment process
disclosure
Must describe factors with positive AND negative influences. Promptly disclose
MATERIAL changes. Examples of material changes: changing valuation model
from P/E to DDM, losing key investment professionals, adding derivatives or
changing constraints, shifting from direct to external management.
Risks and limitations
Must disclose significant risks known at time of disclosure (ex ante basis). Use of
leverage = significant risk, must always be disclosed. Capacity limitations must be
disclosed (Yakovlev's small-cap fund strategy limited to ~$100M). Cannot withhold
limitations that will materially affect returns.
Fact vs. opinion
Must clearly distinguish. 'Gold reserves = 500,000 oz' based on analyst's own
calculation = opinion, not fact. Earnings projections stated as 'the dividend WILL
increase' when actually contingent on legislation = violation (Scott's Walkton
report).

!
V(B) cost disclosure: Must cover ALL costs — including those arising from affiliates, third parties,
and related entities used in providing services. Cannot claim client sophistication as a basis for
not disclosing. ABC Capital's undisclosed restructuring fee-sharing arrangement = violation, even
though it ultimately benefited investors.

!
V(B) notification of model errors: Discovering and correcting a model error must be
communicated to affected clients. Smith's rebalancing of portfolios affected by Dupont's bad
model without notifying anyone = violation of V(B).

V(C) Record Retention

Must develop and maintain appropriate records to support investment analyses, recommendations, actions, and
client communications. Records may be in hard copy or electronic form. Firm's responsibility primarily, but
individual members must archive supporting documentation.

V(C) Key Rules
Records are FIRM PROPERTY — not employee property. Work completed for employer, including
research, models, and client communications, belongs to the firm.
When leaving: CANNOT take records or files without the firm's EXPRESS WRITTEN CONSENT.
Cannot use historical recommendations from prior firm if supporting documentation is unavailable.
Re-creating records at new firm: Must use publicly available information and information obtained
directly from companies — not from memory or sources from prior employer.
New media: Email, text messages, blog posts, social media posts used in professional activities must
ALL be retained.
Local regulatory requirements: Must comply. In ABSENCE of regulatory guidance or firm policy: CFA
Institute recommends retaining records for AT LEAST 7 YEARS.
Applies to decisions that result in NO change of position as well as to actual trades.

heading: Standard VI — CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
body:
VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts [REVISED 2023]

A conflict of interest = any matter that could reasonably be expected to impair independence and objectivity or
raise questions about whether judgment is free from bias. BEST PRACTICE: Avoid conflicts first. When not
possible, disclose fully. Disclosures must be: prominent, plain language, and effectively communicate the
information.

Type of Conflict
Key Guidance
Conflicts to disclose to
employers
Ownership of stocks analyzed or recommended. Participation on outside boards.
Financial/other pressures that could influence decisions. Compensation from third
parties for firm work.
Conflicts to disclose to
clients
Material beneficial ownership in a recommended security. Broker/dealer market-
making activities. Investment banking or underwriting relationships. Rebates or
service fees from fund classes. Compensation structures creating conflicts.
Cross-departmental
conflicts
Sell-side analysts pressured by IB to recommend corporate clients. Marketing
pressuring analysts to generate recommendations to obtain business. Broker-
sponsored limited partnerships the firm manages and then covers.
Stock ownership
Best approach: avoid owning stock in companies one analyzes. If unavoidable,
must disclose ownership. Analyst's wife inheriting stock = must disclose at
minimum; best practice = assign different analyst.
Board membership
Creates three potential conflicts: duty to clients vs. duty to shareholders, personal
compensation via options, access to MNPI. Investment personnel on boards
should be isolated from investment decisions by firewalls.

!
VI(A) vs. VI(B): VI(A) = avoid or DISCLOSE conflicts (independence/objectivity focus). VI(B) =
PRIORITY of transactions (client transactions first, personal second). These are different
standards — confusing them is a common error.

!
VI(A) — Pay-to-play: Carter accepted additional compensation from Badger stock promoter to sell
Badger to clients without disclosing to clients OR employer = clear double violation (I(B) and
VI(A)). Disclosure to clients only = still a violation; best practice = avoid the arrangement entirely.

VI(B) Priority of Transactions

Client and employer transactions have priority over member's personal transactions. A member with beneficial
ownership includes: own account, family accounts, trusts, retirement accounts. The goal is preventing personal
trading from adversely affecting client interests.

Rule
Detail
Client first
Investment transactions for clients and employers MUST have priority over
personal transactions. Cannot 'front-run' — must not delay placing client orders to
trade for personal account first.
Aligned positions
Having the same investment as clients or being co-invested with clients does NOT
create a conflict by itself. The conflict arises when personal positions adversely
affect client interests.

Family accounts
Family member accounts that are regular fee-paying accounts must be treated the
same as all other accounts — not given advantage (Baker allocating hot issues to
husband's account first = violation) AND not disadvantaged (Toffler giving parents
lower priority than other clients = violation).
Nonpublic information
Standard VI(B) covers access to nonpublic info during normal preparation of
research. Cannot use knowledge of pending client trades to trade personally
ahead of clients.
Personal trading
contrary to
recommendations
Permitted in certain circumstances (need to sell for tuition, margin call) as long as:
(1) client not disadvantaged by trade, (2) professional does not benefit from client
trades, (3) applicable regulations followed.

VI(B) Recommended Firm Procedures
Limited IPO participation: Prior approval required for personal IPO participation. Should generally not
participate to avoid appearance of taking client opportunities.
Restrictions on private placements: Strict limits; appropriate review procedures. Managers receiving
private placement opportunities as incentives for business = serious conflict.
Blackout/restricted periods: Establish blackout periods before client trades to prevent front-running.
Reporting requirements: Disclosure of personal holdings on commencement and annually. Duplicate
confirmations of all personal transactions sent to firm. Preclearance procedures for planned personal
trades.
Disclosure of policies: Firm's personal investing policies must be disclosed to clients.

VI(C) Referral Fees

Must disclose to employer, clients, and prospective clients any compensation, consideration, or benefit received
from or paid to others for recommendation of products or services.

VI(C) Key Rules
Disclosure must be made BEFORE entry into any formal agreement for services — not after the client
relationship is established.
Must disclose the NATURE of the consideration: flat fee vs. percentage, one-time vs. continuing,
performance-based, cash vs. soft dollars vs. non-cash.
Applies to BOTH receiving fees for referrals AND paying fees to others for referrals.
Internal referral arrangements within the same firm must ALSO be disclosed (Handley's bank trust
department paying him for referrals to the personal financial management department = must disclose
to clients).
Best practice: employers may completely restrict referral fees. If allowed, require quarterly reporting of
amount and nature of compensation received.
Disclosure allows client to evaluate: (1) any partiality shown in service recommendation, (2) the full
cost of the services received.

section_title: Standard VII — RESPONSIBILITIES AS CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER
heading: Standard VII — RESPONSIBILITIES AS CFA INSTITUTE MEMBER
body:
OR CFA CANDIDATE


VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs

Must not engage in any conduct that compromises the reputation or integrity of CFA Institute, the CFA
designation, or the integrity, validity, or security of CFA Institute programs.

Prohibited Conduct
Permissible Conduct
Giving or receiving assistance (cheating) on
any CFA Institute examination.
Expressing personal opinions about CFA Institute, the CFA
Program, its policies and procedures.
Providing confidential program or exam
information to candidates or public —
includes: specific questions, broad topical
areas, formulas tested OR NOT TESTED on
the exam.
Discussing non-confidential curriculum material in study
groups in preparation for the exam.
Continuing to write after time is called —
even a few answers = violation (Davis
example).
Stating facts about passed levels after results are published.
Sharing exam content online (even after the
exam) — Rossi's blog post mentioning
derivative questions tested and IPO
questions NOT tested = violation.
Factual statements about the CFA Program's rigor,
reputation, and education value.
Using volunteer CFA Institute position for
personal business advantage (Ramirez
scheduling only his clients; Warrenski
implying she would use GIPS committee
inside knowledge to benefit clients).
Volunteering for CFA Institute programs with integrity and
confidentiality.
Misrepresenting information on the
Professional Conduct Statement.
Stating that you served as a grader (but NOT disclosing
specific questions or guidelines).

!
VII(A): Topics TESTED and topics NOT TESTED are BOTH confidential until CFA Institute
publicly releases them. Rossi's comment that there were 'no questions about IPO allocations' =
violation of VII(A) because it discloses what was NOT tested.

VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program

Proper References
Improper References
'I passed Level I of the CFA Program'
'CFA Level II' on a business card — implies a partial
designation
'I am a 2026 Level III candidate in the CFA Program'
'CFA Expected 2026' — implies an expected/partial
designation
'John Smith passed all three CFA exams in three
consecutive years' (factual)
'John Smith is among the elite having passed all
three exams consecutively' — implies superior ability
'The CFA designation attests to success in a
rigorous study program'
'CFA charterholders achieve better performance
results' — exaggerates meaning

'I passed all three levels and may be eligible for the
CFA charter upon completion of required work
experience'
Claiming the CFA designation when membership has
lapsed due to non-payment of dues
'Completion of the CFA Program has enhanced my
portfolio management skills'
'As a CFA charterholder, I am the most qualified to
manage client investments'

To MAINTAIN charterholder status, members must ANNUALLY: (1) submit a completed Professional Conduct
Statement renewing commitment to Code and Standards, AND (2) pay CFA Institute membership dues. Failure
on either = charter suspended, right to use CFA designation lost.

PCS disclosure requirement: Members MUST disclose on the annual PCS all matters questioning their
professional conduct — including civil litigation, criminal investigations, or being the subject of a written complaint.
Craw must disclose the investigation INTO HIS OWN CONDUCT. He is encouraged but not required to report
Voser's investigation.

!
VII(B): A charterholder whose dues have lapsed CANNOT use the CFA designation on business
cards or anywhere else. Vasseur printing 'CFA' on cards after lapsing = violation. May say 'I was
a CFA charterholder' or 'my CFA membership was active from X to Y.'

!
VII(B): Candidates may NOT imply a partial designation. 'CFA Level II' on a business card =
violation. 'I passed Level II of the CFA Program' = acceptable factual statement. The distinction:
no implication that 'Level II' is a recognised designation or credential.

EXAM TRAPS — MASTER REFERENCE

!
I(A) Jurisdiction: Always apply the MORE STRICT of (applicable law vs. Code and Standards).
'More strict' = imposes greater restrictions OR requires greater protections for investors.

!
I(B) Gifts from clients: Less problematic than gifts from third parties because client is already in a
compensation relationship. But disclosure to employer is STILL required. Lavish gifts even from
clients must be rejected if they could affect objectivity.

!
I(C) Misrepresentation: Covers OMISSIONS as well as false statements. Must have 'knowingly'
— but 'knew or should have known' is a low bar. Includes models presenting outputs as 'facts'
when they are estimates.

!
I(E) Competence: No specific CE program required. A 'competent' professional may still have
unsuccessful outcomes. Incompetence is determined by lack of required
knowledge/skills/abilities, not by outcomes. Role changes require acquiring new competencies.

!
II(A) 'Causing others to act': Standard II(A) prohibits BOTH acting on MNPI AND causing
OTHERS to act on it. Telling a family member about a pending announcement — even if they are
the one who trades — = violation by the member.


!
II(A) Mosaic: The analyst's CONCLUSION being material does not violate II(A) if it was derived
from public + nonmaterial nonpublic information. The KEY question: what information did the
analyst use to reach the conclusion?

!
III(A) Soft dollars vs. directed brokerage: Soft dollars = manager's discretion to use client
brokerage for research. Directed brokerage = CLIENT directs manager to use specific broker.
Directed brokerage is permissible because it's the client's asset — must still disclose that best
execution may not be obtained.

!
III(B) 'Fairly' not 'equally': Cannot interpret fair dealing to mean all clients receive identical service.
Wealthier clients paying higher fees may receive more personal service — permissible. What is
never permissible: giving one client advance information that DISADVANTAGES other clients.

!
III(C) Suitability vs. mandate: If managing to a stated mandate (e.g., large-cap income fund), you
are NOT responsible for whether the fund is appropriate for each individual investor who buys it.
Suitability applies only in an advisory relationship.

!
III(D) Performance Presentation: Cupp's adding the new account to the composite before the
firm's stated policy date (to capture a large gain) = violation of III(D). Deviating from stated
policies solely to capture positive performance = misrepresentation.

!
IV(A) Departure: 'Preparations' to compete are permissible on own time. Actively soliciting clients
BEFORE resignation is effective = violation regardless of how large the client or how close the
relationship.

!
IV(C) Supervision: Not limited to 'direct reports' under the Standards — covers anyone subject to
'control or influence.' Inadequate system → must decline supervisory responsibility in writing until
fixed, or bring to senior management.

!
V(A) Submanager selection: Selecting solely on lowest fees = violation of BOTH V(A) (insufficient
due diligence) AND III(C) (suitability — manager must match client risk profile). Must review
investment process, compliance, ethics, performance quality.

!
V(B) Fact vs. opinion: Projections, estimates, future expectations = OPINION and must be
labelled as such. 'Dividend WILL increase' when it is contingent on legislation = presenting
opinion as fact = violation of V(B).

!
VI(A) Best practice hierarchy: AVOID conflict first → if cannot reasonably avoid, DISCLOSE fully.
The 2023 revision made 'avoid' explicit in the standard text. Previously only 'disclose' was stated
explicitly.

!
VI(B) Toffler trap: Over-correcting for family relationship bias = also a violation. Treating parents'
fee-paying account less favourably than other clients because of the family relationship =
violation. Family fee-paying accounts must receive equal treatment.


!
VI(C) Timing of disclosure: Disclosure of referral fees must happen BEFORE the client enters into
a formal agreement. Disclosure after is not sufficient per the standard.

!
VII(B) PCS disclosure: Must disclose on annual PCS all matters questioning professional conduct
— civil litigation, criminal investigation, written complaints. Failure to disclose = violation of VII(A)
(misrepresenting info on PCS). Craw must disclose the investigation into HIS OWN conduct even
if it is still pending.

heading: Classic Examples of Early Quality Recognition ..................................................... 5
Types of Accounting Choices and Their Effects .................................................... 9
Common Exam Pitfalls and Tips ........................................................................ 36
body:
Formula Application ............................................................................................... 36
Conceptual Understanding ..................................................................................... 36
Analysis Approach ................................................................................................. 36
Watch for These Traps ........................................................................................... 36




Page 5
1. Introduction: Why Quality Matters
The ability to assess financial reporting quality is a critical analytical skill. Analysts who
can recognize high-quality reporting have greater confidence in their analyses and
investment decisions. Those who can identify poor quality early—before deficiencies
become widely known—can make more profitable investment decisions or avoid losses.

heading: Classic Examples of Early Quality Recognition
body:
Enron Corporation (2000-2001)
•
James Chanos took a short position in November 2000, more than a year before
bankruptcy
•
Quantitative factors: Return on capital < comparable companies AND < cost of
capital
•
Qualitative factors: Aggressive revenue recognition, complex related-party
transactions, one-time earnings gains, insider stock sales, senior executive
resignations
•
Result: Bankruptcy filing in December 2001
Royal Ahold (2001-2003)
•
Analyst Enitan Adebonojo identified problems in June 2001
•
Red flags: Claiming acquired profits as 'organic growth', booking sale-leaseback
gains as operating profit, billions in off-balance sheet debt
•
Outcome: 2003 announcement of significant profit overstatement, CEO/CFO
resignations, regulatory investigations, massive market value decline

heading: Dual Nature of Quality
body:
Financial report quality encompasses two interrelated but distinct dimensions:

faithful representation of

from actual economic
activities

sustainable, reflective of
economic performance


Page 6
KEY INSIGHT: High-quality earnings increase company value more than low-quality
earnings. High reporting quality is necessary to assess results quality, but alone is
insufficient to ensure high-quality results.



Page 7
2. Conceptual Framework for Assessing Quality

heading: The Two Fundamental Questions
body:
The conceptual framework revolves around asking two basic questions about any
company's financial reports:

1. Are the financial reports GAAP-compliant and decision-useful?
2. Are the results (earnings) of high quality—do they provide an adequate level of
return and are they sustainable?

These questions provide a basis for locating a company's financial reports along the
quality spectrum.

heading: Quality Spectrum of Financial Reports
body:
Financial reports exist along a continuum from highest to lowest quality:

Key Definitions

Page 8
Decision-Useful Information: Information embodying relevance and faithful
representation. Relevant information can affect decisions and includes material
information. Faithful representation is complete, neutral, and free from error.

Sustainable Earnings: Earnings from activities a company can likely sustain in the
future, providing returns ≥ cost of capital.

Adequate Returns: Returns equal to or exceeding the cost of capital.

Important Nuance: Low-Quality Earnings
Earnings can be 'low quality' for two distinct reasons:
•
The reported information properly represents genuinely poor economic
performance (high reporting quality, poor results)
•
The reported information misrepresents economic reality (low reporting quality)

Example: A company with only a one-off lawsuit settlement as earnings (otherwise huge
losses) has low-quality earnings. But if properly disclosed, it can still have high reporting
quality.



Page 9
3. Potential Problems Affecting Financial Report Quality
The basic choices giving rise to quality problems involve: (1) Reported amounts, (2)
Timing of recognition, and (3) Classification. Even GAAP-compliant reports can diverge
from economic reality through biased choices.

heading: The Accounting Equation Foundation
body:
All accounting choices must respect the fundamental equation:
Assets = Liabilities + Equity
Equivalently: Assets − Liabilities = Equity = Net Assets

CRITICAL INSIGHT: Income statement choices affect the balance sheet through equity.
If equity changes, other balance sheet elements must also change to maintain
equilibrium.

body:

Other Balance Sheet Manipulations
•

overstated OCI
•

unrealized gains
•
Understatement of Financial Liabilities at Fair Value: ↑ Equity from understated
unrealized losses
Cash Flow Manipulations
Operating cash flow can be temporarily inflated through:
•
Deferring payments on payables
•
Accelerating collections from customers
•
Deferring inventory purchases
•
Deferring operational expenditures (maintenance, R&D)

WARNING: These manipulations are unsustainable and create future cash flow
problems.

Earnings Management and Smoothing
Definition: Using accounting techniques to understate earnings volatility relative to true
economic volatility.

Technique: Understate earnings when operations are strong (creating reserves),
overstate earnings when operations are weak (using reserves), resulting in artificially
stable earnings.

Problem: Impedes investors' ability to assess true business risk and variability of
returns. Violates faithful representation principle.

heading: Classification Issues
body:
Classification problems can obscure the true nature of transactions without violating
recognition or measurement rules:
•
Revenue Classification: Operating vs. non-operating revenue affects quality
perception
•
Expense Classification: Operating vs. non-operating expenses, core vs. non-core
•
Classification Shifting: Moving expenses from continuing operations to special
items to inflate core earnings
•
Cash Flow Classification: Financing activities presented as operating activities

EXAM TIP: Classification shifting is a subtle form of earnings management. Watch for
unusual patterns in 'special items' or 'other income/expenses'.

Page 12



Page 13
4. General Steps for Evaluating Financial Report Quality
A systematic approach to evaluating financial report quality involves seven key steps:
Step 1: Understand the Company and Industry
•
Business model and revenue drivers
•
Competitive dynamics and industry trends
•
Regulatory environment
•
Economic cycle sensitivity
•
Critical success factors
Step 2: Evaluate Management
•
Management integrity and track record
•
Incentive structures and compensation arrangements
•
Corporate governance quality
•
Related-party transactions
•
History of accounting changes or restatements
Step 3: Identify Significant Accounting Areas
•
Areas requiring significant management judgment or estimates
•
Revenue recognition policies
•
Inventory valuation methods
•
Depreciation/amortization approaches
•
Impairment testing assumptions
•
Contingent liabilities
Step 4: Make Comparisons
•
Compare to industry peers (cross-sectional analysis)
•
Compare across time periods (trend analysis)
•
Compare to company's own guidance and budgets
•
Compare accounting policies to industry norms
Step 5: Check for Warning Signs
Exhibit 4 from the reading lists common accounting warning signs:

Page 14
•
Revenue growing faster than peers without clear explanation
•
Receivables or inventory growing faster than revenue
•
Unusual changes in accounting policies or estimates
•
Frequent one-time or non-recurring items
•
Complex organizational structures or related-party transactions
•
Qualified audit opinions or auditor changes
•
Management turnover, especially CFO
•
Inconsistencies between different financial statements
Step 6: Perform Segment Analysis
•
For multi-segment or multinational firms, analyze each segment separately
•
Compare inventory, sales, and expenses across segments
•
Look for unusual inter-segment transfers or allocations
•
Identify segments used to "hide" poor performance
Step 7: Use Quantitative Tools
•
Apply models like Beneish M-Score for manipulation likelihood
•
Calculate and analyze accruals ratios
•
Use bankruptcy prediction models (Altman Z-Score, Merton model)
•
Perform detailed ratio analysis



Page 15
5. Beneish M-Score Model
The Beneish M-Score is a quantitative model designed to assess the likelihood of
earnings manipulation. It uses eight variables to calculate a manipulation score.

heading: The M-Score Formula
body:
The original 8-variable model:
M-Score = −4.84 + 0.92(DSRI) + 0.528(GMI) + 0.404(AQI) + 0.892(SGI)
+ 0.115(DEPI) − 0.172(SGAI) + 4.679(TATA) − 0.327(LVGI)

Interpretation:
•
M-Score > −1.78: Suggests likely manipulation (higher probability)
•
M-Score ≤ −1.78: Suggests unlikely manipulation (lower probability)

A 5-variable model is also used: M = −6.065 + 0.823(DSRI) + 0.906(GMI) + 0.593(AQI)
+ 0.717(SGI) + 0.107(DEPI)

heading: How to Use the M-Score
body:
3. Calculate each of the 8 (or 5) variables using financial statement data
4. Plug variables into the M-Score formula
5. Compare result to cutoff of −1.78
6. If M > −1.78, investigate further for potential manipulation
7. Examine individual variables to identify specific red flags

EXAM TIP: You may be asked to calculate individual variables and interpret them, or to
identify which variables signal manipulation based on their values.



Page 17
6. Earnings Quality Indicators

heading: The Concept of Sustainable (Persistent) Earnings
body:
Sustainable earnings are repeatable, core earnings from the company's ongoing
business activities. They exclude:
•
One-time or non-recurring items
•
Gains/losses from discontinued operations
•
Unusual or extraordinary items
•
Changes in accounting estimates (unless recurring)
•
Non-operating or peripheral activities

High-quality earnings demonstrate:
8. Sustainability: Can be repeated in future periods
9. Adequate returns: Exceed the cost of capital
10. Cash backing: Supported by operating cash flow, not just accruals

heading: Indicators of Low Earnings Quality
body:
Revenue-Related Indicators
•
Revenue growing significantly faster than industry peers without clear operational
reasons
•
Unusual revenue recognition policies compared to industry norms
•
Bill-and-hold arrangements or other unusual revenue structures
•
High proportion of revenue near period end (channel stuffing)
•
Growing backlog of unfilled orders or cancellations
Balance Sheet Indicators
•
Accounts receivable growing faster than revenue (days sales outstanding
increasing)
•
Inventory growing faster than cost of goods sold
•
Declining asset turnover ratios
•
Large amounts of 'other assets' or intangible assets
•
Off-balance sheet financing or special purpose entities
Cash Flow Indicators
•
Operating cash flow significantly less than net income

Page 18
•
Increasing gap between earnings and cash flow over time
•
Negative free cash flow despite positive earnings
•
Unusual classification of cash flows
Other Indicators
•
Frequent changes in accounting policies or estimates
•
High proportion of earnings from non-recurring items
•
Fourth quarter adjustments that significantly impact annual results
•
Complexity and lack of transparency in financial statements

heading: Classification Shifting
body:
Classification shifting involves moving expenses from continuing operations to special
items, discontinued operations, or other below-the-line categories to inflate
core/operating earnings.

Example: A company might classify normal operating expenses as 'restructuring
charges' or 'other expenses' to make operating earnings appear higher.

Detection methods:
•
Compare core earnings to comprehensive income over time
•
Analyze frequency and magnitude of 'special items'
•
Examine whether special items truly are one-time/unusual
•
Check if special items occur regularly (suggesting they're normal operating costs)

heading: Non-GAAP Earnings Issues
body:
Many companies report non-GAAP or 'adjusted' earnings metrics. Quality issues arise
when:
•
Adjustments are not clearly explained or justified
•
Adjustments systematically exclude negative items but include positive items
•
Non-GAAP metrics are emphasized more than GAAP metrics
•
Adjustments change frequently from period to period
•
Non-GAAP earnings consistently exceed GAAP earnings

CRITICAL: Non-GAAP metrics can provide useful information but require careful
scrutiny. Always reconcile to GAAP numbers and understand each adjustment.



Page 19
7. Earnings Persistence and the Role of Accruals
Earnings can be decomposed into cash and accrual components. The accrual
component is generally less persistent than the cash component.
Earnings = Cash Flow from Operations + Accruals

heading: Two Approaches to Measuring Accruals
body:
Balance Sheet Approach
Accruals = NOAt − NOAt-1
Where NOA = Net Operating Assets = (Operating Assets − Operating Liabilities)
= (Total Assets − Cash) − (Total Liabilities − Total Debt)

Cash Flow Approach
Accruals = NI − CFO − CFI
Or more simply:
Accruals = Net Income − Cash Flow from Operations

The accruals ratio measures accruals relative to average net operating assets:
Accruals Ratio = (NOAt − NOAt-1) / ((NOAt + NOAt-1) / 2)

heading: Aggregate vs. Specific Accruals
body:
Aggregate Accruals: Total accruals calculated using balance sheet or cash flow
approach

Specific Accruals: Individual accrual items such as:
•
Change in accounts receivable
•
Change in inventory
•
Change in accounts payable
•
Depreciation and amortization
•
Changes in other current assets/liabilities

Discretionary vs. Non-Discretionary Accruals:

Page 20
•
Non-discretionary: Arise naturally from business changes (e.g., receivables
increase with sales growth)
•
Discretionary: Result from management judgment/choice (e.g., changes in
allowances, depreciation methods)

heading: Implications of High Accruals
body:
•
High accruals relative to peers or historical norms suggest lower earnings quality
•
Accrual-based earnings are less persistent than cash-based earnings
•
Extreme accruals often precede poor future stock returns
•
Accruals tend to mean-revert (high accruals in one period reverse in future
periods)

EXAM TIP: Understand that accruals are not inherently bad—they're necessary under
accrual accounting. The issue is when accruals are abnormally high, growing, or driven
by discretionary management choices.



Page 21
8. Mean Reversion in Earnings
Mean reversion is the tendency of earnings to revert toward the mean (average) over
time. This concept is critical for forecasting and valuation.

heading: The Concept of Mean Reversion
body:
Key principles:
•
Firms with very high ROA/ROE tend to see declining returns over time as
competition erodes advantages
•
Firms with very low returns tend to improve or exit the industry
•
The speed of mean reversion varies by industry and competitive dynamics
•
Sustainable competitive advantages slow the rate of mean reversion

Mean Reversion Model: ROAt+1 = α + βROAt + εt
Where β < 1 indicates mean reversion (the lower β, the faster the reversion)

heading: Accruals and Speed of Mean Reversion
body:
KEY FINDING: Earnings with higher accrual components mean-revert faster than cash-
based earnings.

Why accruals accelerate mean reversion:
11. Accruals reflect management estimates and judgments that may not persist
12. High discretionary accruals often represent aggressive accounting that reverses
13. Accruals can be used to manage earnings toward targets, creating unsustainable
patterns
14. Cash flows are harder to manipulate and more reflective of economic reality

Implications for analysts:
•
Forecast lower persistence for accrual-heavy earnings
•
Place more weight on cash earnings for valuation
•
Adjust growth expectations downward for companies with high accruals

heading: Earnings Thresholds and Targets
body:
Research shows companies avoid missing certain thresholds:
•
Reporting losses (small profits preferred over small losses)

Page 22
•
Earnings decreases (prefer small increases over small decreases)
•
Analyst consensus estimates

Evidence of threshold management:
•
Unusually high frequency of small profits vs. small losses
•
Distribution of earnings changes shows gap around zero
•
Patterns of 'just meeting or beating' analyst estimates

WARNING: Companies that consistently just meet thresholds may be managing
earnings and have lower quality earnings.



Page 23
9. Cash Flow Quality
High-quality cash flows are sustainable, arise from core operations, and support
reported earnings.

heading: Indicators of Cash Flow Quality
body:
Positive Indicators
•
Operating cash flow consistently exceeds net income
•
CFO growth rate similar to or exceeding revenue growth rate
•
Stable or improving cash conversion cycle
•
Positive free cash flow (CFO − Capital expenditures)
•
Low reliance on external financing
Warning Signs
•
Operating cash flow less than net income (large positive accruals)
•
Declining operating cash flow despite growing earnings
•
Negative operating cash flow
•
Aggressive classification of cash flows
•
Frequent working capital management for cash flow purposes

heading: Real vs. Nominal Cash Flow Growth
body:
When evaluating cash flow growth:
•
Adjust for inflation to calculate real growth rates
•
Compare real growth to industry and economic growth
•
Distinguish organic growth from acquisition-driven growth
•
Consider currency effects for multinational firms
Real Growth Rate = (1 + Nominal Growth) / (1 + Inflation) − 1

Page 25
10. Balance Sheet Quality
Balance sheet quality reflects the degree to which reported assets and liabilities
faithfully represent the company's financial position.

heading: Key Indicators of Balance Sheet Quality
body:
Asset Quality Issues
•
High proportion of intangible assets, especially goodwill
•
Increasing deferred tax assets
•
Large 'other assets' category
•
Inventory valuation methods (LIFO liquidation concerns)
•
Aging receivables or high allowances for doubtful accounts
•
Related-party receivables
Liability Quality Issues
•
Off-balance sheet financing arrangements
•
Understated or undisclosed contingent liabilities
•
Pension and post-retirement benefit obligations
•
Operating leases (now capitalized under ASC 842/IFRS 16 but historical
comparison issues)
•
Warranty and other long-term reserves

heading: Goodwill and Intangible Assets
body:
Goodwill represents a quality concern because:
•
It's not amortized, only tested for impairment
•
Impairment testing involves significant management judgment
•
Impairments often lag economic reality
•
High goodwill may indicate overpayment for acquisitions

Analysis approach:
15. Calculate goodwill as % of total assets—compare to peers and historical levels
16. Assess history of impairment charges—frequent charges suggest poor
acquisition strategy
17. Review impairment testing assumptions in footnotes
18. Consider whether separately identified intangibles should have been higher

Page 26

heading: Off-Balance Sheet Financing
body:
Common forms:
•
Operating leases (historical issue—now addressed by ASC 842/IFRS 16)
•
Special purpose entities (SPEs) or variable interest entities (VIEs)
•
Joint ventures and equity method investments
•
Take-or-pay contracts
•
Throughput agreements

Impact on ratios and analysis:
•
Understated leverage
•
Overstated ROA and ROE
•
Distorted liquidity ratios
•
Incomplete picture of company obligations

Analytical adjustments:
19. Capitalize operating leases (using footnote disclosures)
20. Consolidate SPEs/VIEs if substance suggests control
21. Add contingent obligations to liabilities
22. Recalculate ratios with adjusted balance sheet

heading: Working Capital Management Issues
body:
Watch for manipulation via:
•
Fourth-quarter 'cleanup' of receivables or inventory
•
Aggressive payment deferral near period-end
•
Channel stuffing (loading inventory onto customers)
•
Changes in payment terms or credit policies

Detection methods:
•
Calculate and track Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
•
Calculate Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO)
•
Calculate Days Payables Outstanding (DPO)
•
Monitor Cash Conversion Cycle = DSO + DIO − DPO
•
Compare working capital metrics to industry norms

Page 27



Page 28
11. Sources of Information About Risk

heading: Financial Statement Notes
body:
Key areas to examine:
•
Accounting policies: Revenue recognition, inventory methods, depreciation, etc.
•
Significant estimates and judgments
•
Contingencies and commitments
•
Related-party transactions
•
Subsequent events
•
Segment information
•
Debt covenants and restrictions

heading: Management Discussion and Analysis (MD&A)
body:
The MD&A provides insights into:
•
Management's view of business performance and outlook
•
Critical accounting estimates
•
Liquidity and capital resources
•
Known trends and uncertainties
•
Off-balance sheet arrangements

Red flags in MD&A:
•
Vague or boilerplate language
•
Inconsistencies with financial statements
•
Excessive focus on non-GAAP metrics
•
Lack of transparency about challenges

Other important elements:

•
