heading: Regulatory Filings
body:
Additional sources:
•
Form 10-K/20-F: Annual reports with extensive disclosures
•
Form 10-Q: Quarterly reports
•
Form 8-K/6-K: Current reports on material events
•
Proxy statements (DEF 14A): Executive compensation, related parties
•
SEC comment letters and company responses

heading: External Research and Market Data
body:
•
Analyst reports and earnings call transcripts
•
Credit rating agency reports
•
Short seller research reports
•
Industry publications and research
•
News articles and investigative journalism
•
Litigation and regulatory proceedings



Page 30
12. Bankruptcy and Credit Risk Prediction Models
These models help assess financial distress and default probability.

heading: Altman Z-Score Model
body:
The Altman Z-Score predicts bankruptcy probability using five financial ratios:
Z = 1.2(X1) + 1.4(X2) + 3.3(X3) + 0.6(X4) + 1.0(X5)
Where:
•
X1 = Working Capital / Total Assets
•
X2 = Retained Earnings / Total Assets
•
X3 = EBIT / Total Assets
•
X4 = Market Value of Equity / Book Value of Total Liabilities
•
X5 = Sales / Total Assets

Interpretation:
•
Z > 2.99: Safe zone (low bankruptcy probability)
•

heading: < Z < 2.99: Grey zone (caution)
body:
•
Z < 1.81: Distress zone (high bankruptcy probability)

Model variations:
•
Original: For public manufacturing companies
•
Model A: For private manufacturing companies (substitutes book value for
market value in X4)
•
Model B: For private non-manufacturing companies (adjusted coefficients)

heading: Merton Model (Structural Model)
body:
The Merton model views equity as a call option on the firm's assets:
•
Default occurs when asset value falls below debt value
•
Uses option pricing theory to estimate default probability
•
Requires market value of equity and volatility

Distance to Default metric:
DD = (V − D) / (σV × V)

Page 31
Where:
•
V = Market value of assets
•
D = Face value of debt
•
σV = Volatility of asset value

Higher DD indicates lower default probability.

heading: Other Predictive Models
body:
•
Ohlson O-Score: Logistic regression model using nine variables
•
Zmijewski Score: Probit model using ROA, leverage, and liquidity
•
Credit rating agency models: Proprietary models used by S&P, Moody's, Fitch

EXAM TIP: Know the Altman Z-Score formula and interpretation zones. Understand
conceptually how the Merton model works.



Page 32

Cash Flow Approach: Accruals = NI − CFO
Accruals Ratio = (NOAt − NOAt-1) / Avg. NOA
Altman Z-Score
Z = 1.2(X1) + 1.4(X2) + 3.3(X3) + 0.6(X4) + 1.0(X5)
•
X1 = Working Capital / Total Assets
•
X2 = Retained Earnings / Total Assets

Page 33
•
X3 = EBIT / Total Assets
•
X4 = Market Value Equity / Book Value Liabilities
•
X5 = Sales / Total Assets
Z > 2.99 = Safe | 1.81–2.99 = Grey | Z < 1.81 = Distress
Quality Ratios
Ratio
Formula
Interpretation
Quality of Earnings
CFO / Net Income
> 1 is ideal; indicates
earnings backed by cash
Accruals Ratio
Accruals / Avg NOA
Lower is better; high
accruals = lower quality
Cash Flow Margin
CFO / Revenue
Higher is better; measures
cash efficiency
Free Cash Flow
CFO − CapEx
Positive and growing is ideal



Page 34

heading: Key Concepts Summary
body:
Two Dimensions of Quality
•
Financial Reporting Quality: Information disclosed (decision-useful, GAAP-
compliant)
•
Earnings/Results Quality: Economic substance (sustainable, adequate returns)
Quality Spectrum
•
HIGHEST: GAAP-compliant, decision-useful, sustainable, adequate returns
•
LOWEST: Fictitious transactions
Aggressive vs. Conservative Accounting
•
Aggressive: Increase current performance (borrow from future)
•
Conservative: Decrease current performance (cookie jar reserves)
Seven Steps of Evaluation
23. Understand the company and industry
24. Evaluate management
25. Identify significant accounting areas
26. Make comparisons (peers, time, budgets)
27. Check for warning signs
28. Perform segment analysis
29. Use quantitative tools (M-Score, Z-Score)
Earnings Quality Indicators
•
Sustainable vs. one-time earnings
•
Cash-backed vs. accrual-heavy earnings
•
Core vs. peripheral activities
•
Classification shifting concerns
•
Non-GAAP adjustments scrutiny
Accruals Insights
•
Earnings = CFO + Accruals
•
Accrual component less persistent than cash component
•
High accruals → faster mean reversion
•
Discretionary accruals more concerning than non-discretionary
Cash Flow Quality

Page 35
•
CFO should support or exceed net income
•
Examine CFO trends relative to earnings trends
•
Calculate Free Cash Flow (CFO − CapEx)
•
Watch for classification shifting in cash flow statement
Balance Sheet Quality
•
Asset quality: Goodwill, intangibles, receivables, inventory
•
Liability quality: Off-balance sheet items, contingencies
•
Working capital management monitoring
•
Off-balance sheet financing adjustments



Page 36

heading: Common Exam Pitfalls and Tips
body:
Formula Application
•
For M-Score: Remember the cutoff is −1.78 (NEGATIVE value)
•
Individual M-Score variables > 1.0 generally indicate manipulation risk
•
For Z-Score: Know the three zones (Safe > 2.99, Grey 1.81–2.99, Distress <
1.81)
•
Accruals can be calculated from either balance sheet or cash flow statement
Conceptual Understanding
•
High reporting quality doesn't guarantee high earnings quality
•
Conservative accounting can also signal quality problems (cookie jar reserves)
•
Earnings management differs from fraud—it's within GAAP but biased
•
Accruals aren't inherently bad—focus on abnormal or discretionary accruals
Analysis Approach
•
Always compare to industry peers and historical trends
•
Look for patterns over multiple periods, not single-period anomalies
•
Integrate quantitative (ratios, models) with qualitative (MD&A, governance)
analysis
•
Consider incentives—what might motivate management to manipulate?
Watch for These Traps
•
Confusing low earnings quality with low reporting quality
•
Assuming all non-GAAP metrics are suspicious (some are legitimately useful)
•
Overlooking that aggressive accounting is still GAAP-compliant
•
Not adjusting for off-balance sheet items when calculating ratios
•
Focusing only on income statement while ignoring balance sheet/cash flow
warning signs



Page 37
END OF STUDY NOTES
Review these notes regularly and practice with sample problems.
Focus on understanding the conceptual framework and being able to apply the formulas
in various scenarios.
Good luck with your May 2026 CFA Level II exam!

heading: IFRS 9 — Two-Test Classification Framework
body:
IFRS 9 (effective Jan 2018) replaced IAS 39's portfolio approach (held-for-trading, available-for-sale, held-to-
maturity — these terms NO LONGER APPEAR in IFRS 9). Instead, classification depends on TWO TESTS:
● BUSINESS MODEL TEST — is the asset held (a) to COLLECT contractual cash flows, (b) to COLLECT AND
SELL, or (c) for trading/other?
● CASH FLOW CHARACTERISTIC TEST — are contractual cash flows SOLELY PAYMENTS OF PRINCIPAL
AND INTEREST (SPPI) on principal?

IFRS 9 — Three Measurement Categories
AMORTIZED COST — requires BOTH tests pass: (a) hold to collect contractual CFs AND (b) SPPI only. Typical
for simple debt held to maturity.

FAIR VALUE THROUGH OCI (FVOCI) — "hold to collect and sell" + SPPI. Debt default; equity optional
(irrevocable choice at initial recognition).
FAIR VALUE THROUGH PROFIT OR LOSS (FVPL) — default for everything else. Mandatory for equity held-for-
trading. Can also be elected to avoid accounting mismatches.
Initial measurement: fair value on acquisition date (= cost at acquisition for arm's-length transaction).

heading: Debt vs Equity Instrument Rules
body:
Instrument
Eligible classifications
P&L treatment
OCI treatment
Debt

(based on business model)
Interest, impairment
(expected loss), and FX
gains/losses
Unrealized
gains/losses (FVOCI
only) — RECYCLED
to P&L on sale
Equity held-for-
trading

Dividends + all fair-value
changes
None
Equity (other)

Dividends only (if FVOCI);
full fair-value changes (if
FVPL)

NOT RECYCLED
even on sale

heading: Reclassification Rules (IFRS 9)
body:
● DEBT instruments can be reclassified ONLY when business model changes (very infrequent; requires
senior management judgment).
● EQUITY instruments CANNOT be reclassified — the initial FVPL/FVOCI choice is IRREVOCABLE.
● When reclassification occurs: NO restatement of prior periods; fair value at reclassification date becomes
new carrying amount.

heading: Expected-Loss Impairment Model
body:
A major IFRS 9 change: impairment uses an EXPECTED CREDIT LOSS model (forward-looking), replacing IAS 39's
INCURRED LOSS model (only recognize losses after default event). Key mechanics:
● PERFORMING assets: recognize 12-MONTH expected losses upfront.
● NON-PERFORMING / credit-deteriorated assets: recognize LIFETIME expected losses.
● Applies to financial assets, financial guarantees, loan commitments, and lease receivables.
● US GAAP parallel: ASC 326 (Current Expected Credit Loss, CECL) — effective most public companies Jan
2020.

⚠  Trap 2 — IAS 39 terminology does NOT exist under IFRS 9

Held-to-maturity, available-for-sale, and held-for-trading are IAS 39 terms that NO LONGER EXIST in IFRS 9.
On the exam, any question referring to "AFS" or "HTM" under IFRS 9 is using obsolete terminology. The new
categories are: Amortized Cost / FVOCI / FVPL.

⚠  Trap 3 — FVOCI recycling: debt YES, equity NO
When a DEBT instrument classified as FVOCI is sold, the cumulative unrealized gain/loss sitting in OCI is
RECYCLED to P&L. When an EQUITY instrument classified as FVOCI is sold, the cumulative gain/loss STAYS
in equity (retained earnings) — it is NEVER recycled to P&L. Big difference for EPS.

section_title: INVESTMENTS IN ASSOCIATES & JOINT VENTURES: EQUITY METHOD
heading: Equity Method Basics — "One-Line Consolidation"
body:
Mechanics
INITIAL: record investment at COST on investor's balance sheet (non-current asset).
EACH PERIOD: investor's share of investee's NET INCOME → INCREASES investment account AND appears
as "equity income" on investor's income statement.
DIVIDENDS received → treated as RETURN OF CAPITAL; REDUCE the investment account; NOT recognized as
income (avoids double-counting).
Balance sheet: 1 line item "Investment in Associate" (non-current).
Income statement: 1 line item "Equity income" (share of investee's profit/loss).
If investment value falls to ZERO, investor DISCONTINUES equity method; resumes only when cumulative
profits exceed unrecognized losses.

✓  Example 1 — Branch acquires 20% of Williams for €200,000 (2016)
Williams reports 3-year aggregate income €900,000 and dividends €350,000.
Investment account at Dec 31, 2018:  €200,000 + 20% × (€900,000 − €350,000) = €310,000.
Each year: ADD 20% of income, SUBTRACT 20% of dividends received. Dividends do NOT enter investor's
income.
Implicit assumption: purchase price = 20% of Williams' book value of net assets (no excess price, no
goodwill).

section_title: INVESTMENTS IN ASSOCIATES & JOINT VENTURES: EQUITY METHOD
heading: Excess Purchase Price — Allocation to Assets & Goodwill
body:
When cost > investor's share of book value of investee's net assets, the EXCESS is first allocated to specific
identifiable net assets using fair values, then the residual is GOODWILL.

Allocation sequence
Excess purchase price = Cost − (Ownership % × Book value of investee net assets).
Step 1: Allocate to identifiable assets per fair-value differences (inventory, PP&E, intangibles, land).
Step 2: Any remainder = GOODWILL (not separately recognized — INCLUDED in investment carrying amount;
not amortized; tested for impairment).
Amortization treatment mirrors the underlying asset: PP&E → depreciate; inventory → expense when sold; land
and goodwill → indefinite (no amortization).
If cost < investor's share of fair value of net assets: difference is NOT carried on BS — recognized as INCOME
in period of acquisition (bargain purchase).

✓  Example 3 — Parker acquires 30% of Prince for €500,000 (Jan 2018)
Prince net assets: BV €1,200,000 (PP&E BV €1.9M, FV €2.2M — €300k undervalued; 10-yr life).  Prince 2018
NI €100,000, dividends €50,000.
Goodwill calc: Purchase €500k − 30% × BV €1.2M = €140k excess. Attribute €90k to PP&E (30% × €300k FV
step-up). Residual €50k = GOODWILL.
Year-end investment balance:
  + Start: €500,000
  + Equity income (30% × €100k) = €30,000
  − Dividends received (30% × €50k) = €15,000
  − PP&E amortization adjustment (€90k / 10 yr) = €9,000
  = €506,000 at Dec 31, 2018.
Validation: Prince ending net assets €1.25M → 30% × €1.25M = €375,000 + unamortized excess (€140k − €9k)
= €131,000 → €506,000 ✓.

section_title: INVESTMENTS IN ASSOCIATES & JOINT VENTURES: EQUITY METHOD
heading: Intercompany Transactions — Unrealized Profit Elimination
body:
Because the investor can influence transaction terms with the associate, profits on intercompany sales must be
DEFERRED (eliminated to the extent of investor's interest) until the inventory is SOLD to a third party. Both
upstream (associate → investor) and downstream (investor → associate) are treated symmetrically: both IFRS and
US GAAP eliminate unrealized profit to the extent of the investor's interest.

✓  Example 4 — UPSTREAM sale (Wicker 25% of Foxworth)
Wicker purchased 25% of Foxworth Jan 2018 for €1,000,000. Foxworth BV net assets €3,800,000.
Fair-value step-up: building undervalued by €40,000, 20-yr life. Wicker's excess: €50,000 = €10k to building +
€40k goodwill.
Foxworth 2018 NI €20,000, dividends €3,200. Year-end €8,000 unsold inventory from upstream sale.
Equity income 2018:  25% × €20,000 − (€10,000 ÷ 20) − (25% × €8,000) = €5,000 − €500 − €2,000 = €2,500.

Investment balance Dec 31, 2018:  €1,000,000 + €2,500 − 25% × €3,200 = €1,001,700.

✓  Example 5 — DOWNSTREAM sale (Jones 25% of Jason, 2017)
Jones sold €96,000 inventory to Jason for €160,000 (profit €64,000 = 40% margin). Jason resold €120,000
(75%) in 2017; remainder €40,000 (25%) sold in 2018.
Unrealized profit end-2017 = €64,000 × 25% unsold = €16,000. Investor's share = 25% × €16,000 = €4,000.
Equity income 2017: 25% × €800,000 − €8,000 amortization − €4,000 unrealized = €200,000 − €8,000 −
€4,000 = €188,000.
In 2018, the €4,000 reverses as deferred profit is realized (inventory sold to 3rd party).

section_title: INVESTMENTS IN ASSOCIATES & JOINT VENTURES: EQUITY METHOD
heading: Equity Method: Fair Value Option
body:
Both IFRS and US GAAP allow the investor to ELECT fair value option at initial recognition (irrevocable). US GAAP:
available to all entities. IFRS: limited to venture capital orgs, mutual funds, unit trusts, investment-linked insurance
funds. Under FV option:
● Investment carried at FV with changes in P&L.
● No excess purchase price amortization, no goodwill.
● Dividends AND fair-value changes hit P&L.

section_title: INVESTMENTS IN ASSOCIATES & JOINT VENTURES: EQUITY METHOD
heading: Equity Method Impairment
body:
Both IFRS and US GAAP require periodic impairment review of equity-method investments. When fair value falls
below carrying value and decline is OTHER THAN TEMPORARY, impairment loss must be recognized.
● IFRS: needs OBJECTIVE EVIDENCE of impairment (a loss event) after initial recognition that impacts
future cash flows measurably.
● Loss is recognized on the SINGLE investment line — NOT separately tested for goodwill (goodwill is
bundled in the line).

⚠  Trap 4 — Goodwill on equity-method investments is NOT separately impairment tested
Under equity method, goodwill is BUNDLED in the "Investment in Associate" line. It is NOT separately
recognized and NOT separately impairment tested. The ENTIRE investment is tested for impairment together
at each balance sheet date.

⚠  Trap 5 — Analyst issues with equity method
One-line consolidation HIDES balance-sheet detail: associate's debt does NOT appear on investor's balance
sheet → understates leverage. Net margin ratios may be overstated because equity income is in NI but NOT in
revenue. An investor may prefer equity method (instead of consolidation) with 49% ownership to keep
associate debt off-books. Analysts should look through to the associate's full statements.

heading: Three US GAAP Structural Types (IFRS treats all identically)
body:
Type
Structure
Accounting
Merger
Company A + Company B = Company A.
Only acquirer survives; target's net assets
absorbed.
Acquisition method. Acquirer issues
stock/cash for B's net assets; B ceases to
exist.
Acquisition
Company A + Company B = (Company A +
Company B).  Both survive as parent-
subsidiary; < 100% ownership possible.
Acquisition method + consolidation. NCI
reported if < 100% acquired.
Consolidation
Company A + Company B = Company C
(NEW).  Neither A nor B survives.
Acquisition method; new entity C assumes
both.

Both IFRS and US GAAP use the ACQUISITION METHOD for all business combinations. The pooling-of-interests
method is no longer permitted. IFRS makes no legal-structure distinction among business combinations; one party
is identified as the acquirer.

heading: Measurement of Identifiable Assets and Liabilities
body:
Acquisition Method — Recognition Rules
Identifiable assets & liabilities: measured at FAIR VALUE on acquisition date.
Contingent liabilities (IFRS): recognized if fair value can be reliably measured.
Contingent liabilities (US GAAP): recognized if probable AND reasonably estimable.
Indemnification assets: recognized at same time and same basis as the indemnified liability.
In-process R&D: recognized as separate intangible asset at fair value (if reliably measurable). Subsequently
amortized if commercialized, or impaired if not.
Restructuring costs: NOT part of acquisition cost — expensed when incurred.

heading: Goodwill: Full vs Partial (IFRS vs US GAAP)
body:
Goodwill Computation
FULL GOODWILL (US GAAP mandatory; IFRS optional) = FV of entity as a whole − FV of identifiable net assets.
Includes goodwill attributable to NCI.
PARTIAL GOODWILL (IFRS optional) = FV of consideration − Acquirer's share of FV of identifiable net assets.
NCI receives NO goodwill.
IFRS option is chosen on a TRANSACTION-BY-TRANSACTION basis.

Goodwill has INDEFINITE LIFE — not amortized; tested for impairment annually (or more frequently if events
indicate impairment).

✓  Example 6 — Acquirer pays $800,000 for 80% of Acquiree; FV of identifiable net assets $900,000; FV
of entire entity $1,000,000
PARTIAL GOODWILL (IFRS option):
  FV consideration: $800,000
  − 80% × FV of identifiable net assets ($900,000) = $720,000
  = Goodwill $80,000.
FULL GOODWILL (US GAAP + IFRS option):
  FV of entity: $1,000,000
  − FV of identifiable net assets: $900,000
  = Goodwill $100,000.
Full goodwill captures an additional $20,000 attributable to the 20% non-controlling interest.

heading: Non-controlling (Minority) Interest
body:
When a parent acquires LESS than 100% of a subsidiary, the remaining equity held by third parties is a NCI. NCI is
presented as a SEPARATE COMPONENT OF STOCKHOLDERS' EQUITY on the consolidated balance sheet.
NCI Measurement
IFRS: CHOICE — either (a) FAIR VALUE of NCI (full goodwill) or (b) NCI's PROPORTIONATE SHARE of
acquiree's identifiable net assets (partial goodwill).
US GAAP: MUST use fair value of NCI (full goodwill method).
Full goodwill chosen → NCI = NCI% × FV of entire entity.
Partial goodwill chosen → NCI = NCI% × FV of identifiable net assets.

✓  Example 8 — Parent acquires 90% of Subsidiary for shares with FV €180,000; FV of sub = €200,000; FV
of net assets = €160,000
PP&E on consolidated BS: €235,000 (Parent BV) + €155,000 (Sub FV) = €390,000. 100% of Sub's assets at FV
regardless of % acquired.
FULL GOODWILL (US GAAP + IFRS):  €200,000 − €160,000 = €40,000. NCI = 10% × €200,000 FV of entity =
€20,000.
PARTIAL GOODWILL (IFRS only):  €180,000 − 90% × €160,000 = €36,000. NCI = 10% × €160,000 FV of net
assets = €16,000.
Goodwill is NOT amortized under either method — tested for impairment at least annually.

heading: Post-Combination Balance Sheet — Key Rules
body:
● Assets/liabilities of acquirer combined at BOOK VALUE + target at FAIR VALUE.
● Acquirer's retained earnings are carried forward; target's RE is NOT carried to consolidated.
● Stock issued in acquisition recorded at FAIR VALUE, not book value.
● Target's post-acquisition earnings included in consolidated income statement.

✓  Example 7 — Franklin acquires 100% of Jefferson via 1,000,000 €1-par shares (€15 FV each = €15M
total)
Purchase price allocation:
  FV consideration: €15,000,000
  − BV Jefferson net assets: €1,900,000
  = Excess €13,100,000
  Of which: €1.3M to inventory (FV step-up), €2.0M to PP&E, −€0.2M to long-term debt (FV < BV), €9.6M
GOODWILL.
Consolidated BS impact: combined inventory €15M (€12M + €3M FV), PP&E €31.5M, goodwill €9.6M.
Equity: capital stock €6M (€5M Franklin + €1M par issued), APIC €20M (€6M existing + €14M on issuance
above par), RE €14M (Franklin only — Jefferson's RE NOT carried).
Consolidated assets €66.4M; liabilities €26.4M; equity €40M (balances).

⚠  Trap 6 — Target's retained earnings NOT carried to consolidated statements
Only the ACQUIRER's pre-acquisition retained earnings are reported on the consolidated BS at acquisition
date. The target's RE is eliminated as part of the purchase price allocation. Target's POST-acquisition earnings
are consolidated into RE in subsequent periods.

⚠  Trap 7 — Bargain purchase recognized immediately as a gain
If acquisition price < FV of target's identifiable net assets, it is a BARGAIN PURCHASE. Both IFRS and US GAAP
require IMMEDIATE recognition in P&L of the difference as a gain (NOT spread over time). Any contingent
consideration is initially at FV and subsequently remeasured (with changes through P&L).

section_title: SPECIAL PURPOSE AND VARIABLE INTEREST ENTITIES
heading: What is an SPE / VIE?
body:
A SPECIAL PURPOSE ENTITY (SPE, IFRS) or VARIABLE INTEREST ENTITY (VIE, US GAAP) is created by a
sponsoring entity to accomplish specific objectives: isolate assets, raise financing, transfer risks. Common forms:
limited liability companies, trusts, partnerships, unincorporated entities. Often have deliberately restrictive
governance limits.

section_title: SPECIAL PURPOSE AND VARIABLE INTEREST ENTITIES
heading: The Pre-Reform Abuse
body:
Historically, sponsors structured SPEs so they retained financial control but not voting control — allowing them to
AVOID consolidation, keep debt off-balance-sheet, and recognize gains on "sales" of assets to the SPE. Enron is
the canonical cautionary example: SPEs whose debt Enron had guaranteed fell onto Enron's balance sheet during
collapse.

section_title: SPECIAL PURPOSE AND VARIABLE INTEREST ENTITIES
heading: Current Consolidation Rules
body:
IFRS 10 — Control-Based Consolidation
Revised definition of CONTROL encompasses most SPEs. Evaluate purpose, design, and risks.
Control = power over relevant activities + exposure/rights to variable returns + ability to use power to affect
returns.
If sponsor meets this test, SPE is consolidated regardless of voting interest.

US GAAP — VIE Classification (ASC 810)
A VIE is an entity where:
  (1) Total equity at risk is INSUFFICIENT to finance activities without support; OR
  (2) Equity investors lack one of: (a) ability to make decisions, (b) obligation to absorb losses, (c) right to
receive returns.
PRIMARY BENEFICIARY consolidates the VIE — regardless of voting/equity interest.
Primary beneficiary = entity absorbing MAJORITY of expected losses AND/OR receiving MAJORITY of expected
residual returns.
If losses and returns go to DIFFERENT parties, the loss-absorber consolidates.


✓  Example 11 — Odena receivables securitization (€55M capital need)
STARTING BS: Cash €30M, AR €60M, Other €40M = Assets €130M. Current liab €27M + Noncurrent €20M +
Equity €83M.
ALTERNATIVE 1 — Direct borrowing against AR: Cash +€55M (= €85M), debt +€55M (= €75M Noncurrent liab).
Assets €185M.
ALTERNATIVE 2 — Set up SPE: Odena invests €5M equity + SPE borrows €55M → SPE buys €60M AR from
Odena. On Odena's standalone statements: AR falls €60M, cash rises €55M. SPE BS: AR €60M = debt €55M +
equity €5M.
IF ODENA CONSOLIDATES SPE: intercompany AR transfer reversed → Odena consolidated BS IDENTICAL to
Alternative 1. Cash €85M, AR €60M, total assets €185M, Noncurrent debt €75M, equity €83M.
KEY INSIGHT: consolidation REVERSES the off-balance-sheet benefit. If SPE is NOT consolidated, the AR
would be off Odena's books and debt would NOT appear — misleadingly improved leverage/ROA.

⚠  Trap 10 — Primary beneficiary consolidates regardless of equity %
A company owning 0% equity of a VIE but absorbing majority of expected losses OR receiving majority of
expected returns MUST CONSOLIDATE the VIE as its primary beneficiary. Consolidation is based on
ECONOMIC SUBSTANCE (risk/reward exposure), not legal/voting interest.

⚠  Trap 11 — Consolidation removes the "off-balance-sheet" benefit
If the sponsor consolidates the SPE, the SPE's assets and debt flow onto consolidated BS — the balance sheet
looks IDENTICAL to direct borrowing. Sponsors that AVOIDED consolidation pre-reform achieved improved
asset turnover, lower leverage, higher ROA (reduced asset base). Analysts should test whether SPEs are
properly consolidated and adjust ratios if not.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM1

⚠  T1. % Ownership thresholds are presumptions
< 20% presumes no influence; 20-50% presumes significant influence; > 50% presumes control. All can be
rebutted by substance (board seat, policy participation, material transactions, personnel exchange,
technological dependency).

⚠  T2. IFRS 9 removed HTM / AFS / HFT labels
IAS 39's "held-to-maturity," "available-for-sale," "held-for-trading" terms DO NOT EXIST under IFRS 9. New
categories: Amortized Cost / FVOCI / FVPL. Classification based on business model + cash flow tests.


⚠  T3. SPPI test for debt amortized-cost / FVOCI
Solely Payments of Principal and Interest. If CF structure is NOT SPPI (e.g., equity-linked, leveraged structure),
debt instrument MUST be FVPL. Business model test alone is insufficient.

⚠  T4. Amortized cost requires BOTH tests
For amortized cost: business model = hold to collect + cash flow characteristics = SPPI. Fail either → NOT
amortized cost (goes to FVOCI or FVPL).

⚠  T5. Equity FVOCI option is IRREVOCABLE at recognition
Equity classification as FVOCI (for non-trading equities) is IRREVOCABLE — cannot reclassify later. Once
chosen, only dividend income hits P&L; fair-value changes permanently in OCI (not recycled on sale).

⚠  T6. Debt FVOCI recycling vs equity FVOCI no-recycling
When DEBT in FVOCI is sold, cumulative unrealized gain/loss RECYCLES to P&L. When EQUITY in FVOCI is
sold, cumulative gain/loss STAYS in OCI (retained earnings) — NEVER recycled to P&L.

⚠  T7. IFRS 9 uses EXPECTED credit loss; IAS 39 used INCURRED
Forward-looking recognition: 12-month expected loss on performing assets; lifetime expected loss on credit-
deteriorated. This means companies recognize SOME loss on ALL debt, even performing. US GAAP parallel:
CECL (ASC 326).

⚠  T8. Reclassification is RARE and only for DEBT
Debt instruments can be reclassified only when business model changes (infrequent, material). Equity
classifications NEVER reclassified. No restatement on reclassification.

⚠  T9. Equity method = one-line consolidation
Single line "Investment in Associate" on BS (non-current); single line "Equity income" on IS. Investor's share of
associate's net assets and net income — but associate's DEBT is hidden. Leverage understated.

⚠  T10. Dividends REDUCE equity-method investment
Under equity method, dividends received are treated as RETURN OF CAPITAL — they REDUCE the investment
account balance. They are NOT income (income already recognized when associate earned it).


⚠  T11. Purchase price > BV → excess allocated to assets first, goodwill last
Excess = cost − investor's share of BV of net assets. Allocate to identifiable assets per fair-value differences
(amortize accordingly). Remainder = goodwill (indefinite life, bundled in investment account, NOT separately
tested for impairment).

⚠  T12. Excess amortization REDUCES equity income
PP&E fair-value step-up is amortized to REDUCE equity income on investor's IS and REDUCE the investment
account on investor's BS. Land and goodwill are NOT amortized (indefinite lives).

⚠  T13. Equity method impairment — single test on whole line
Equity-method goodwill is NOT separately impairment-tested. The ENTIRE investment line is tested at each BS
date for "other than temporary" decline in fair value.

⚠  T14. Intercompany unrealized profit deferred — both upstream and downstream
IFRS & US GAAP require elimination of unrealized profit to the extent of investor's interest in the associate, for
BOTH upstream (associate → investor) and downstream (investor → associate) sales. Applied to equity income
on investor's IS.

⚠  T15. Fair Value Option — irrevocable election at initial recognition
Both IFRS and US GAAP permit FV option for equity-method investments (IFRS limited to specific fund types;
US GAAP open to all). Once elected, investment at FV; gains/losses and dividends hit P&L; no goodwill; no
excess amortization.

⚠  T16. Joint ventures use equity method (not proportionate consolidation)
IFRS 11 and US GAAP both require EQUITY METHOD for joint ventures. Proportionate consolidation (line-by-
line share of JV assets/liabilities) is ONLY permitted in rare circumstances. Equity method hides JV debt and
expenses but shows the same net income and net assets.

⚠  T17. Acquisition method is universal — pooling-of-interests obsolete
All business combinations use ACQUISITION METHOD under IFRS 3 and ASC 805. Pooling-of-interests is no
longer permitted under either framework.

⚠  T18. Identifiable assets/liabilities at FAIR VALUE on acquisition date

Target's assets and liabilities recognized at FV, not BV, on acquisition date. Applies to 100% of target's
identifiable net assets even when acquirer buys < 100% (NCI takes proportional share).

⚠  T19. Full vs partial goodwill — IFRS option, US GAAP mandatory full
US GAAP: FULL GOODWILL always. IFRS: transaction-by-transaction choice between FULL (FV of entity − FV
of identifiable net assets) and PARTIAL (FV of consideration − acquirer's % × FV of net assets).

⚠  T20. NCI measurement mirrors goodwill choice
Full goodwill → NCI = NCI% × FV of entity. Partial goodwill → NCI = NCI% × FV of identifiable net assets. Full
NCI > Partial NCI.

⚠  T21. Goodwill NOT amortized — indefinite life
Under both frameworks. Tested for impairment at least annually (more often if triggering events). Once
impaired, CANNOT be reversed (even under IFRS).

⚠  T22. Goodwill impairment allocation cascade (IFRS)
One-step test: carrying > recoverable → loss = difference. First write down goodwill to 0, THEN allocate
remaining loss pro rata to other non-cash assets. US GAAP caps loss at goodwill carrying amount.

⚠  T23. Bargain purchase — gain in P&L immediately
If purchase price < FV of identifiable net assets → bargain purchase. Difference recognized as GAIN in P&L in
period of acquisition. Not spread over time. (Rare — typically signals distress.)

⚠  T24. Target's retained earnings NOT consolidated at acquisition
Only acquirer's pre-acquisition RE flows to consolidated BS. Target's RE is eliminated as part of purchase price
allocation. Target's POST-acquisition earnings consolidate going forward.

⚠  T25. Stock-for-stock acquisitions at FV
Stock issued as consideration recorded at FAIR VALUE (market price × shares), NOT par value. Par value flows
to capital stock; excess flows to additional paid-in capital.

⚠  T26. Contingent liabilities: IFRS FV if measurable; US GAAP probable + estimable

IFRS 3 recognizes contingent liabilities at FV if reliably measurable (including non-probable). US GAAP
recognizes only those meeting probable + reasonably estimable criteria.

⚠  T27. Restructuring costs EXPENSED, not capitalized
Both IFRS and US GAAP: restructuring costs associated with a business combination are NOT included in
acquisition cost. Recognized as expense in period incurred. Exception: in-process R&D IS capitalized as
separate intangible.

⚠  T28. In-process R&D: capitalized as separate intangible
IFRS and US GAAP recognize IPR&D from an acquisition as a separate intangible asset at FV. Subsequently
amortized if product succeeds, or impaired if product fails.

⚠  T29. Contingent consideration at FV initially; subsequent changes through P&L
Earn-outs, contingent payments to target shareholders — initially at FV. Liability-classified consideration:
changes in FV through P&L. Equity-classified consideration: NOT remeasured.

⚠  T30. Acquisition method: consolidate 100% even if < 100% acquired
Parent consolidates 100% of subsidiary's assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses regardless of acquisition
%. NCI captures the portion NOT owned by parent (separate component of equity and separate line on IS).

⚠  T31. NCI is in EQUITY on consolidated BS (not liability)
Under both frameworks. Separately labeled line in stockholders' equity section. NCI share of net income
shown separately on consolidated IS.

⚠  T32. VIE: primary beneficiary consolidates regardless of voting %
Economic substance over legal form. Primary beneficiary = absorbs majority of expected losses OR receives
majority of expected residual returns. If split, loss-absorber consolidates.

⚠  T33. IFRS 10 control-based definition broader than voting-share
Power over relevant activities + exposure to variable returns + ability to use power to affect returns.
Encompasses most SPEs even without voting control. Enforces consolidation where economic substance
dictates.

⚠  T34. SPE consolidation eliminates off-BS benefit

Pre-reform: sponsors avoided consolidation → off-BS debt, improved leverage/ROA. Post-reform: consolidated
SPE → BS shows same assets and debt as direct borrowing. Check if SPEs are properly consolidated in peer
comparisons.

⚠  T35. Equity-method investment hides debt
Associate's debt doesn't appear on investor BS under equity method. When analyzing leverage, look through to
associates' full BS. Pro-rata adjustment may be needed for comparability with consolidated peers.

⚠  T36. Equity-method income quality watch
Equity income includes investor's share of associate profit — but associate may not distribute cash (analyst
should check the dividend payout). Operating cash flow may significantly underperform accrued equity
income.

⚠  T37. Acquisition method inflates combined RE by target post-acq earnings only
Pre-acquisition RE of target stripped out in PPA. Only post-combination earnings of target flow into
consolidated RE. Analysts who assume simple RE addition will overstate continuity.

⚠  T38. Full goodwill method yields higher asset base than partial
Because NCI gets goodwill portion. Example 8: full goodwill €40,000 + NCI €20,000 = €60,000. Partial:
€36,000 + €16,000 = €52,000. Higher assets → lower ROA for full method.

⚠  T39. Goodwill allocation to CGU is required BEFORE impairment testing
Under IFRS, goodwill must be allocated to CGUs at acquisition. These become the units for annual impairment
testing. US GAAP uses REPORTING UNITS similarly. Choosing higher-level units can smooth impairment.

⚠  T40. Fair value option prevents equity method complexities
If FV option elected: no goodwill, no excess amortization, no impairment testing, no unrealized profit
elimination on intercompany sales. Trade-off: volatility of fair-value changes hits P&L each period.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM1

Category Decision Tree

By ownership & influence
< 20% & no influence:  Financial asset → IFRS 9 (amortized cost / FVOCI / FVPL).
20-50% & significant influence:  Associate → Equity method (or FV option if eligible).
> 50% control:  Subsidiary → Consolidation (acquisition method).
Joint control:  JV → Equity method (proportionate consolidation only rare exceptions).
SPE/VIE with economic control:  Consolidate per primary-beneficiary test.

IFRS 9 Classification (Financial Assets)
Three categories
AMORTIZED COST: business model = hold to collect AND cash flows = SPPI only.
FVOCI: business model = hold to collect & sell AND SPPI (debt); or irrevocable election (equity).
FVPL: everything else; mandatory for equity held-for-trading; FV option available to avoid accounting
mismatch.
Debt FVOCI gains/losses RECYCLED to P&L on sale. Equity FVOCI NEVER recycled.
Impairment: EXPECTED credit loss model (12-month for performing; lifetime for deteriorated).

Equity Method Balance
Investment account formula
Investment = Cost + Σ (% × Investee Net Income) − Σ (% × Investee Dividends) − Σ Excess-price amortization
Excess purchase price = Cost − (% × BV of investee net assets).
Excess allocated: first to identifiable asset fair-value differences, residual = goodwill (in investment line, not
amortized).
PP&E excess amortization = (% × Δ FV vs BV) / useful life → reduces both investment and equity income each
year.
Equity income = (% × Investee NI) − Amortization − Unrealized intercompany profit share.

Business Combination — Goodwill Formulas
Full vs Partial
FULL GOODWILL = FV of entire entity − FV of identifiable net assets.  (US GAAP; IFRS option)
PARTIAL GOODWILL = FV of consideration − (Acquirer % × FV of identifiable net assets).  (IFRS only)
NCI (full) = NCI% × FV of entity.    NCI (partial) = NCI% × FV of identifiable net assets.
Full > Partial for both goodwill AND NCI by NCI% × (FV of entity − FV of net assets).

Goodwill Impairment

IFRS vs US GAAP
IFRS (one-step):  Impairment = max(0, Carrying − Recoverable Amount). Recoverable = max(FV less costs to
sell, Value in use). Allocate first to goodwill, then pro rata to other non-cash assets.
US GAAP (two-step):  Step 1 — trigger: Carrying > FV of reporting unit. Step 2 — measurement: Loss = Current
Goodwill − Implied Goodwill, where Implied = FV of unit − FV of identifiable net assets.
US GAAP loss CAPPED at goodwill carrying amount. No reversal under either framework.

Financial Statement Impact — Quick Compare
Equity method vs Consolidation (same investee, 80% ownership)
NET INCOME:  equity method = consolidation (same bottom-line profit).
REVENUE:  consolidation >> equity method (full revenue vs 0).
TOTAL ASSETS:  consolidation >> equity method (target's full assets vs single line).
TOTAL LIABILITIES:  consolidation >> equity method (target's debt hidden under equity).
NET MARGIN:  equity method > consolidation (same NI over smaller revenue base).
ROA:  equity method > consolidation (same NI over smaller asset base).
DEBT/EQUITY:  consolidation > equity method (target debt visible).
DEBT/ASSETS:  varies by numerator & denominator scaling.

Key Numerical Anchors
Curriculum examples for recall
Ex 1 — Branch 20% of Williams €200k cost → €310k after 3 yrs (= €200k + 20% × (€900k NI − €350k div)).
Ex 3 — Parker 30% of Prince €500k → goodwill €50k; YE balance €506,000 (NI +€30k, div −€15k, amort −€9k).
Ex 6 — Partial goodwill $80k / Full goodwill $100k (Acquirer $800k for 80%; FV entity $1M; FV net assets
$900k).
Ex 7 — Franklin/Jefferson consolidated: goodwill €9.6M; total assets €66.4M; equity €40M.
Ex 8 — Full method goodwill €40k/NCI €20k vs Partial €36k/€16k (Parent 90% of Sub, FV €200k, FV NA
€160k).
Ex 9 — IFRS impairment €100k (Recoverable €1.3M vs Carrying €1.4M).
Ex 10 — US GAAP impairment $200k (Implied GW $100k vs Current $300k).
Ex 11 — Odena consolidated SPE = same BS as direct €55M borrowing.

— End of Notes — LM1: Intercorporate Investments —

heading: High-Yield Exam Topics .................................................................................... 20
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction: What Makes Financial Institutions Different?
Financial institutions—particularly banks—play a crucial role in the economy by serving
as intermediaries between providers and recipients of capital. Their unique
characteristics require specialized analysis.

heading: Types of Financial Institutions
body:
•
Deposit-taking, loan-making institutions (banks)
•
Investment banks
•
Credit card companies
•
Brokers, dealers, exchanges
•
Investment managers and advisers
•
Insurance companies

This reading focuses primarily on banks and insurance companies.

heading: Systemic Importance of Banks
body:
SYSTEMIC IMPORTANCE: Banks' smooth functioning is essential to the
overall health of an economy. The failure of one bank can negatively affect
other financial and non-financial entities.

Why banks are systemically important:
•
Serve as intermediaries between capital providers and recipients
•
Create financial inter-linkages across all types of entities (households,
businesses, governments)
•
Network effects: Failure of one bank spreads to others
•
Larger banks have greater potential impact on entire financial system


Page 5
SYSTEMIC RISK: The risk of failure of the financial system as a result of the
failure of a major financial institution. FINANCIAL CONTAGION: Situation
where financial shocks spread from their place/sector of origin to other
locales or sectors.

heading: Heavy Regulation of Financial Institutions
body:
Due to systemic importance, financial institutions face extensive regulation:

•
Capital requirements: Minimum amounts that must be maintained
•
Liquidity requirements: Minimum liquidity levels
•
Asset quality restrictions: Limits on risky assets
•
Deposit insurance: Government-backed protection

Regulations aim to:
1. Constrain excessive risk-taking that could cause failure
2. Protect depositors and maintain confidence
3. Prevent bank runs (sudden large withdrawals)
4. Maintain stability of financial system



Page 6
2. The CAMELS Framework for Bank Analysis
CAMELS is a comprehensive framework used by bank regulators and analysts to
evaluate bank health and performance:

Component
Focus Area
C - Capital Adequacy
Sufficient capital to absorb losses
A - Asset Quality
Quality of loan portfolio and investments
M - Management Capabilities
Quality of leadership and risk management
E - Earnings
Profitability and sustainability of earnings
L - Liquidity Position
Ability to meet short-term obligations
S - Sensitivity to Market Risk
Exposure to interest rate, FX, and other
market risks

EXAM TIP: CAMELS is heavily tested. Know what each component
measures and the key ratios for each.



Page 7
3. Capital Adequacy (C)
Capital adequacy assesses whether a bank has sufficient capital to absorb potential
losses and continue operations.

heading: Basel III Capital Framework
body:
Basel III establishes global minimum capital standards with three tiers of capital:

Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) Capital
•
Highest quality capital
•
Common stock
•
Retained earnings
•
Accumulated other comprehensive income
•
Less: Intangible assets and deferred tax assets

Other Tier 1 Capital
•
Perpetual instruments subordinate to deposits
•
No fixed maturity
•
Dividends/interest at issuer's discretion

Tier 2 Capital
•
Subordinated debt
•
Minimum 5-year original maturity
•
Subordinate to depositors and general creditors

heading: Basel III Minimum Capital Ratios
body:
Ratio
Minimum Requirement
Formula

Page 8
CET1 Ratio
4.5%
CET1 Capital / RWA
Tier 1 Ratio
6.0%
(CET1 + Other T1) / RWA
Total Capital Ratio
8.0%
(Tier 1 + Tier 2) / RWA

Where RWA = Risk-Weighted Assets

RISK-WEIGHTED ASSETS: Assets are weighted based on credit risk, with
riskier assets receiving higher weights. Government bonds get low weights
(~0-20%), while unsecured loans get high weights (100%).

heading: Additional Capital Buffers
body:
•
Capital Conservation Buffer: 2.5% of RWA (in addition to minimums)
•
Counter-cyclical Buffer: Up to 2.5% (activated during credit booms)
•
Systemically Important Bank Buffer: Additional for G-SIBs

heading: Key Capital Ratios
body:
Tier 1 Capital Ratio = Tier 1 Capital / RWA
Total Capital Ratio = (Tier 1 + Tier 2) / RWA
Equity/Assets = Total Equity / Total Assets

Higher ratios indicate stronger capital position and greater ability to absorb losses.



Page 9
4. Asset Quality (A)
Asset quality pertains to credit risk associated with a bank's assets, primarily loans and
investment securities.

heading: Key Components
body:
•
Loans: Typically largest portion of bank assets
•
Investments in securities: Often significant portion
•
Credit risk exposure: Quality depends on borrower creditworthiness
•
Diversification: Avoiding concentration risk

Page 11
5. Management Capabilities (M)
Management quality is critical but qualitative. Key indicators:

•
Risk management processes and effectiveness
•
Corporate governance structure
•
Board independence and oversight
•
Internal controls
•
Compliance with laws and regulations
•
Financial reporting quality and transparency
•
Strategic planning and execution
•
Overall performance track record

For banks specifically: Ability to identify, measure, monitor, and control
risks (credit, market, operational, legal, liquidity) is paramount.



Page 12
6. Earnings (E)
Earnings quality and sustainability are crucial for long-term viability.

heading: Components of Bank Earnings
body:
Source
Description
Sustainability
Net Interest Income
Interest earned on loans −
Interest paid on deposits
Most stable and sustainable
Service/Fee Income
Account fees, transaction
fees, advisory fees
Stable recurring revenue
Trading Income
Gains/losses from trading
activities
Most volatile, least
sustainable

Net Interest Income (NII) = Interest Income − Interest Expense
Net Interest Margin (NIM) = NII / Average Earning Assets

heading: Earnings Quality Considerations
body:
•
Loan loss provisions: Require significant estimates (conservative is better)
•
Fair value measurements: Level 1 (observable) best; Level 3 (unobservable)
requires judgment
•
One-time items: Strip out for core earnings
•
Revenue mix: More NII and fees = more sustainable than trading



Page 14
7. Liquidity Position (L)
Adequate liquidity is essential to honor deposit withdrawals and meet obligations.

heading: Basel III Liquidity Standards
body:
Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)
LCR = High-Quality Liquid Assets / Expected Cash Outflows (30 days)
•
Minimum: 100%
•
Ensures bank can survive 30-day stress scenario
•
HQLA = Cash, central bank reserves, high-quality government bonds

Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)
NSFR = Available Stable Funding / Required Stable Funding
•
Minimum: 100%
•
Focuses on long-term structural funding (>1 year)
•
Ensures stable funding for asset base

heading: Other Liquidity Metrics
body:
•
Concentration of Funding: Avoid over-reliance on single source
•
Maturity Mismatch: Comparison of asset vs. liability maturities
•
Liquid Assets / Total Assets: Higher is more liquid
•
Liquid Assets / Short-term Liabilities: Coverage of near-term needs



Page 15
8. Sensitivity to Market Risk (S)
Banks are exposed to multiple market risks through their operations and trading
activities.

heading: Types of Market Risk
body:
Risk Type
Source
Impact
Interest Rate Risk
Mismatch in repricing of
assets vs. liabilities
Changes in net interest
income
Foreign Exchange Risk
Currency exposure on
foreign assets/liabilities
FX gains/losses
Equity Price Risk
Equity holdings and trading
positions
Valuation changes
Commodity Price Risk
Commodity-linked loans or
trading
Valuation changes

heading: Interest Rate Sensitivity
body:
Most important for banks. Key factors:
•
Maturity structure: Longer assets vs. shorter liabilities
•
Repricing frequency: How often rates reset
•
Rate type: Fixed vs. floating

Sensitivity Analysis: Shows impact of rate changes on earnings
•
Example: '+25 basis points' scenario shows NII impact if rates rise
•
Example: '−25 basis points' scenario shows NII impact if rates fall

Page 16

heading: Value at Risk (VaR)
body:
VaR: Statistical measure estimating potential loss on trading positions over
specific time horizon at given confidence level (e.g., 99% confidence, 1-day
holding period).



Page 17
9. Analyzing Insurance Companies
Insurance companies have different business models and risks than banks.

heading: Property & Casualty (P&C) Insurance
body:
P&C insurers provide coverage for property damage, liability, auto, homeowners, etc.

P&C Business Model
•
Collect premiums upfront
•
Invest premiums (float) until claims paid
•
Profit from: (1) Underwriting margin, (2) Investment returns
•
Risks: Catastrophic events, underpricing, poor investment returns

heading: Life & Health Insurance
body:
Life insurers provide mortality coverage and savings/investment products.

L&H Business Model Differences
•
Long-term liabilities: Payouts decades in future
•
Investment matching: Must match long-duration assets to liabilities

Page 19
•
Interest rate sensitivity: Critical due to long duration
•
Actuarial assumptions: Mortality, lapse, expenses, investment returns



Page 20
10. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: CAMELS Framework
body:
•
Capital: Basel III ratios (CET1 ≥4.5%, Tier 1 ≥6%, Total ≥8%)
•
Assets: NPL ratio, loan loss coverage, credit quality distribution
•
Management: Qualitative assessment of governance and risk management
•
Earnings: ROE, ROA, NIM, efficiency ratio, earnings composition
•
Liquidity: LCR ≥100%, NSFR ≥100%, funding concentration
•
Sensitivity: Interest rate risk, VaR, market risk exposure

EXAM STRATEGY: Focus on ratio calculations and interpretations. Know

END OF STUDY NOTES
These institutions are the backbone of the financial system—master
this analysis!

heading: Exam Strategies ................................................................................................ 25
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction and Framework Overview
Financial analysis is a means to an end, not the end itself. Rather than apply every
technique to every situation, analysts must identify the proper type of analysis for a
given context.

KEY PRINCIPLE: Financial analysis helps make economic decisions by
making potential outcomes more visible, improving the probability of
success rather than leaving outcomes to chance.

heading: Six-Phase Framework for Financial Analysis
body:
This reading demonstrates a systematic framework applicable to any financial analysis:

Phase
Description
1. Define Purpose & Context
Establish objectives, identify specific
questions, set scope and timeline,
determine resource needs
2. Collect Input Data
Gather financial statements, obtain industry
data, conduct management discussions,
perform site visits
3. Process Data
Adjust for unusual items, create common-
size statements, calculate ratios, develop
trend analysis

Page 5
4. Analyze/Interpret
Identify relationships, compare to
benchmarks, test hypotheses, draw
preliminary conclusions
5. Communicate Conclusions
Prepare report, make recommendations,
present findings, justify conclusions
6. Follow-Up
Monitor developments, update analysis,
reassess recommendations, track outcomes

Key Sources of Information
•
Financial statements and regulatory filings
•
Management discussions and presentations
•
Industry reports and economic data
•
Peer company comparisons
•
Analyst reports and market research

Key Outputs from Framework
•
Adjusted and common-size financial statements
•
Comprehensive ratio analysis
•
Trend identification and peer comparisons
•
Investment recommendation with supporting rationale
•
Ongoing monitoring plan

CRITICAL INSIGHT: This framework is iterative, not linear. Analysts
frequently move between phases as new information emerges. The
framework provides structure while allowing flexibility.



Page 6
2. Case Study: Nestlé S.A. Overview

body:

heading: Analysis Objectives (Phase 1)
body:
The analyst establishes seven specific objectives:

1. Understand business model and historical financial performance
2. Assess profitability with focus on ROE and its drivers
3. Evaluate capital structure and financial leverage
4. Analyze capital allocation (dividends, capex, acquisitions)
5. Examine segment performance (products and geography)
6. Assess earnings quality and cash flow sustainability
7. Develop investment recommendation with supporting rationale

DATA QUALITY PRINCIPLE: Comprehensive data collection in Phase 2 is
crucial. Analysis quality depends directly on input data quality and
completeness.

9. Investment Recommendation

heading: Strengths
body:
•
Exceptional operating cash flow: CHF 12.8B annually
•
High earnings quality: CFO/NI consistently >1.2
•
Stable EBIT margins: 14.9-15.0%
•
Negative working capital: Efficient with supplier financing
•
Diversified portfolio: Products and geography
•
Strategic positioning: Growth segments (nutrition, health)

heading: Concerns
body:
•
ROE declining: 14.8% → 13.9%
•
Increased leverage: LT debt 7.8% → 12.3% of capital
•
Declining liquidity: Current ratio <1.0
•
High goodwill: 39% of assets (impairment risk)
•
Mature markets: Europe growing only 1.2%

heading: Final Recommendation
body:
RECOMMENDATION: BUY
Target: Long-term hold with 3-5 year horizon
Rationale:
13. Strong fundamentals with market-leading positions
14. Exceptional cash generation supporting growth and dividends
15. Strategic positioning in attractive segments
16. High-quality earnings backed by cash flow
17. Proven management executing strategy

Key Risks to Monitor:

Page 24
•
Leverage trends—ensure stays within prudent limits
•
ROE recovery—target return to 15%+ range
•
Acquisition integration—critical for value creation
•
Mature market stabilization—especially Europe

10. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Framework Mastery
body:
•
Always use the six-phase framework systematically
•
Define clear objectives before starting analysis
•
Collect comprehensive data—quality matters
•
Process and analyze iteratively between phases
•
Support conclusions with specific evidence

heading: Technical Skills
body:
•
Master DuPont decomposition—both 3 and 5 factor
•
Calculate and interpret all cash flow ratios
•
Understand accruals and earnings quality metrics
•
Analyze working capital efficiency metrics
•
Perform segment analysis for diversified companies

heading: Exam Strategies
body:
•
Read cases thoroughly—all facts are relevant
•
Show your analytical process, not just answers
•
Use case data to support conclusions
•
Manage time based on point values
•
Look beyond surface metrics to find insights

INTEGRATION IS KEY: Effective analysis requires combining multiple
techniques. No single ratio or metric tells the complete story.

heading: IFRS vs. US GAAP on VIEs/SPEs ....................................................................... 18
body: 8. Financial Statement Presentation and Analysis ........................................................ 20

heading: IFRS vs. US GAAP Differences ........................................................................... 22
body:
Page 4
1. Introduction
Intercorporate investments (investments in other companies) can significantly impact an
investing company's financial performance and position. Companies invest in debt and
equity securities of other companies to:

•
Diversify their asset base
•
Enter new markets
•
Obtain competitive advantages
•
Deploy excess cash
•
Achieve additional profitability

KEY POINT: The percentage of equity ownership determines the appropriate
accounting treatment based on the level of influence or control the investor
has over the investee.

This reading covers accounting standards that apply to classification, measurement,
and disclosure of intercorporate investments under both IFRS and US GAAP, including
areas where differences remain.



Page 5
2. Basic Corporate Investment Categories
Investments in marketable debt and equity securities are categorized based on the
degree of influence or control:

Category
Degree of
Influence
Typical Ownership
%

Treatment
Financial Assets

influence
Usually < 20%

28 / ASC 323)

(IFRS 11 / ASC
323)
Business

Usually > 50%
Consolidation (IFRS
3, 10 / ASC 805,
810)

CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Categories are based on degree of
influence/control, not purely on percentage ownership. However, ownership
percentages provide useful presumptions.

heading: Summary of Influence Levels
body:
•
No Significant Influence (<20%): Investor is passive; cannot influence investee's
operating/financial policies

Page 6
•
Significant Influence (20-50%): Investor can participate in policy decisions but
doesn't control them
•
Control (>50%): Investor has power to govern financial and operating policies to
obtain benefits
•
Shared Control (Joint Ventures): Two or more parties contractually share control

heading: Evidence of Significant Influence
body:
Indicators of significant influence include:
•
Representation on board of directors
•
Participation in policy-making processes
•
Material transactions between investor and investee
•
Interchange of managerial personnel
•
Provision of essential technical information



Page 7
3. Investments in Financial Assets (IFRS 9)
For investments where the investor has no significant influence (typically <20%
ownership), IFRS 9 provides classification and measurement guidance.

heading: Classification of Financial Assets
body:
IFRS 9 classifies financial assets into three categories based on:
1. The entity's business model for managing the financial assets
2. The contractual cash flow characteristics of the financial asset

Page 8
SPPI TEST: 'Solely Payments of Principal and Interest' test determines if
contractual cash flows represent basic lending arrangement. Principal =
amount advanced; Interest = time value of money + credit risk.

heading: Equity Investments
body:
For equity investments (where no significant influence exists):

•
Default treatment: Fair Value through Profit or Loss (FVPL)
•
Irrevocable election: Can designate as Fair Value through OCI (FVOCI) at initial
recognition
•
If FVOCI elected: Dividends recognized in P&L, but all other gains/losses stay in
OCI (never recycled to P&L)

heading: Comparison: IFRS 9 vs. US GAAP (ASC 320)
body:
US GAAP classifications for debt securities:

US GAAP Category
Intent
Measurement
Held-to-Maturity (HTM)
Hold until maturity
Amortized cost
Trading Securities
Actively trade for profit
Fair value (changes to P&L)
Available-for-Sale (AFS)
Neither HTM nor trading
Fair value (unrealized
gains/losses to OCI)

Key US GAAP differences:
•
Equity securities (no significant influence): Fair value through net income (similar
to FVPL)

Page 9
•
Equity securities without readily determinable fair value: Can elect to measure at
cost minus impairment, plus/minus observable price changes
•
AFS securities: Unrealized gains/losses in OCI can be reclassified (recycled) to
P&L upon sale

heading: Impairment of Financial Assets
body:
IFRS 9 uses an 'expected credit loss' model:
•
Recognize expected credit losses at all times
•
Update at each reporting date
•
Based on probability-weighted estimate

US GAAP (ASC 326) also uses expected credit loss model for financial assets
measured at amortized cost.



Page 10
4. Investments in Associates and Joint Ventures

heading: The Equity Method
body:
When an investor has significant influence (typically 20-50% ownership), the equity
method is used.

EQUITY METHOD: The investment is initially recorded at cost, then adjusted
for the investor's share of the investee's earnings/losses and dividends
received.

Initial Recognition
Investment in Associate (at acquisition) = Purchase Price
Record at cost, including transaction costs.

Subsequent Measurement
Ending Balance = Beginning Balance + Share of Earnings − Share of
Dividends − Impairment

Transaction
Journal Entry Effect
Balance Sheet Impact

income

Cr. Investment Income
↑ Investment account ↑

↓ Investment account ↓
Income

Associate
↓ Investment account (no
P&L impact)

Investment in Associate

Income

Key Points About Equity Method
•
Investor's share of earnings increases investment account
•
Dividends received are NOT income—they reduce the investment account
(return of capital)
•
Investment account can never go negative (floor at zero)
•
Recognize share of associate's OCI in investor's OCI

heading: Fair Value Adjustments and Goodwill
body:
If purchase price exceeds investor's share of fair value of identifiable net assets:

Goodwill = Purchase Price − (Ownership % × Fair Value of Net Assets)

•
Goodwill is included in investment account (not separately reported)
•
Goodwill is NOT amortized
•
Test investment for impairment when indicators exist

heading: Adjustments to Share of Earnings
body:
The investor's share of associate earnings may need adjustment for:

3. Excess depreciation/amortization due to fair value adjustments at acquisition
4. Upstream/downstream profits on intra-company transactions
5. Differing accounting policies (adjust to conform)

Page 12

heading: Joint Ventures
body:
Joint ventures are arrangements where two or more parties have joint control.

Type

(US GAAP)

Note: Under IFRS 11, equity method is mandatory for joint ventures. US GAAP allows
proportionate consolidation in some cases.



Page 13
5. Business Combinations and Consolidation
When an investor obtains control (typically >50% ownership), the investor is the parent
and the investee is the subsidiary. The parent must consolidate the subsidiary.

CONTROL: The power to govern the financial and operating policies of an
entity to obtain benefits from its activities.

heading: Indicators of Control
body:
•
Ownership of majority voting rights (>50%)
•
Power to appoint/remove majority of board of directors
•
Power to cast majority of votes at board meetings
•
Contractual arrangements providing control

heading: The Acquisition Method
body:
IFRS 3 and ASC 805 require the acquisition method for business combinations:

6. Identify the acquirer
7. Determine the acquisition date
8. Recognize and measure identifiable assets, liabilities, and NCI
9. Recognize and measure goodwill or bargain purchase gain

Step 1: Identify the Acquirer
•
Usually the entity transferring cash or other assets
•
Or the entity issuing equity interests
•
Generally the larger entity

Step 2: Acquisition Date

Page 14
•
Date when acquirer obtains control
•
Usually closing date

Step 3: Recognize Assets and Liabilities at Fair Value
At acquisition date, recognize at fair value:
•
Identifiable assets acquired
•
Liabilities assumed
•
Any non-controlling interest (NCI)

Step 4: Goodwill Calculation
Goodwill = Consideration Transferred + NCI − Fair Value of Net Identifiable
Assets

Component
Description
Consideration Transferred
Cash paid + FV of assets given + FV of
liabilities incurred + FV of equity issued
Non-Controlling Interest (NCI)
FV of NCI or proportionate share of net
assets (choice)
Fair Value of Net Identifiable Assets
Fair value of assets acquired − Fair value of
liabilities assumed

•
If result is positive: Recognize goodwill as asset
•
If result is negative: Bargain purchase gain recognized in P&L

heading: Measurement of Non-Controlling Interest
body:
Page 15
Two methods permitted:

10. Fair Value Method (Full Goodwill): NCI measured at fair value → Results in
goodwill attributable to NCI
11. Proportionate Share Method (Partial Goodwill): NCI measured at proportionate
share of fair value of net identifiable assets → No goodwill for NCI

heading: Goodwill Subsequent to Acquisition
body:
•
Goodwill is NOT amortized
•
Test for impairment annually (or more frequently if indicators exist)
•
Impairment loss recognized in P&L
•
Impairment cannot be reversed (IFRS and US GAAP)



Page 16
6. The Consolidation Process
Consolidation combines 100% of parent and subsidiary accounts, eliminating
intercompany transactions.

heading: Basic Consolidation Steps
body:
12. Combine 100% of parent and subsidiary assets, liabilities, revenues, and
expenses
13. Eliminate the parent's investment in subsidiary account
14. Eliminate intercompany transactions and balances
15. Recognize non-controlling interest in equity
16. Recognize non-controlling interest's share of net income

heading: Elimination Entries
body:
Key elimination entries:

A. Eliminate Investment Account
•
Dr. Subsidiary's Equity accounts (CS, APIC, RE)
•
Dr. Goodwill
•
Cr. Investment in Subsidiary
•
Cr. Non-Controlling Interest (if applicable)

B. Eliminate Intercompany Revenues/Expenses
•
Dr. Intercompany Revenue
•
Cr. Intercompany Expense

C. Eliminate Intercompany Receivables/Payables
•
Dr. Intercompany Payable
•
Cr. Intercompany Receivable

Page 17

D. Eliminate Unrealized Profits on Inventory
If parent sold to subsidiary (downstream):
•
Dr. Retained Earnings (beginning) or Sales
•
Cr. Cost of Goods Sold or Inventory

If subsidiary sold to parent (upstream):
•
Allocate elimination between controlling and NCI based on ownership percentage

heading: Non-Controlling Interest (NCI)
body:
NCI represents the equity in subsidiary not attributable to parent.

Income Statement Treatment
•
Start with consolidated net income (100% of subsidiary)
•
Subtract: Net income attributable to NCI
•
Result: Net income attributable to parent

Balance Sheet Treatment
•
NCI presented in equity section (separate from parent's equity)
•
NCI = NCI at acquisition + Share of income − Share of dividends

heading: Acquisition Mid-Year
body:
•
Include subsidiary's revenues/expenses only from acquisition date forward
•
Full year's changes in subsidiary's balance sheet accounts
•
Pre-acquisition earnings remain in subsidiary's equity (not consolidated income)



Page 18
7. Special Topics in Consolidation

heading: Variable Interest Entities (VIEs)
body:
VIEs are entities where voting rights don't determine control. Instead, control comes
through contractual arrangements.

PRIMARY BENEFICIARY: The party that has both (1) power to direct
activities that most significantly impact economic performance, and (2)
obligation to absorb losses or right to receive benefits that could be
significant.

•
Primary beneficiary must consolidate the VIE
•
Common examples: SPEs, SPVs used in securitizations

heading: Special Purpose Entities (SPEs)
body:
SPEs are entities created for specific, limited purpose:
•
Asset securitization
•
Leasing arrangements
•
R&D funding

Key question: Does the reporting entity control the SPE? If yes, consolidate.

heading: Analytical Implications
body:
•
Consolidation increases total assets and liabilities → Higher leverage ratios
•
Equity method shows only net investment → Lower total assets
•
All methods result in same total parent equity
•
Profit margins affected: Consolidation shows gross revenues/expenses vs. net
income under equity method


Page 21

heading: Key Disclosures
body:
Companies must disclose:
•
Significant accounting policies for investments
•
Fair value of investments (if not carried at FV)
•
For associates: Summarized financial information
•
For subsidiaries: NCI amounts, significant restrictions
•
Changes in ownership interests



Page 22
9. Key Exam Takeaways

heading: Classification Framework
body:
•
Know the ownership % presumptions: <20% = financial asset, 20-50% =
associate, >50% = subsidiary
•
Understand that influence/control can exist outside these ranges
•
IFRS 9 classification depends on business model AND cash flow characteristics

heading: Equity Method Mechanics
body:
•
Dividends received are NOT income—they reduce investment account
•
Share of earnings increases investment account AND income
•
Investment account formula: Beginning + Earnings − Dividends − Impairment
•
Goodwill in equity method is included in investment account (not separate)

heading: Consolidation Essentials
body:
•
Goodwill = Consideration + NCI − FV of Net Identifiable Assets
•
NCI can be measured at fair value (full goodwill) or proportionate share (partial
goodwill)
•
Eliminate 100% of intercompany transactions
•
NCI gets share of subsidiary's income and equity

heading: Ratio Analysis Impact
body:
•
Consolidation → Highest total assets → Worst leverage ratios
•
Equity method → Middle ground on total assets
•
Fair value → Lowest total assets → Best leverage ratios
•
All methods result in same parent equity (NCI is separate)

heading: IFRS vs. US GAAP Differences
body:
Page 23
•
IFRS: Equity investments default to FVPL, can elect FVOCI
•
US GAAP: Equity investments at FV through NI (similar to FVPL)
•
Both use equity method for associates (20-50%)
•
Both require consolidation for controlled subsidiaries
•
VIE treatment differs: IFRS uses control model, US GAAP uses primary
beneficiary concept

EXAM STRATEGY: Focus on mechanics of equity method and goodwill
calculation—these are heavily tested. Be able to prepare basic consolidation
eliminations and calculate NCI.

END OF STUDY NOTES
Master these concepts—they're foundational for FSA!

heading: 1. Types of Employee Compensation
body:
1. Types of Employee Compensation
Context: Using AstraZeneca as an example, wages and salaries represent 30% of total
expenses—a material portion that requires careful analysis.
Five Main Categories
Category
Timeline
Key Examples

< 12 months
Wages, salaries, bonuses, paid
leave

On termination

parachutes

Share-Based
Ongoing/vesting

I Key Point: Share-based compensation and post-employment benefits are the most
complex from both accounting and valuation perspectives.

heading: 2. Share-Based Compensation: Types
body:
2. Share-Based Compensation: Types
A. Restricted Stock
Common shares granted to employees with selling restrictions. Typically used for highly
compensated employees to align interests and aid retention.
Type
Voting
Dividends
Tradeable
Restricted Stock



Performance Shares



Restricted Stock Units



Key Distinctions:
• Performance Shares: Restricted stock where vesting depends on achieving specific
performance targets (e.g., EPS > $5.00)
• RSUs: Represent the right to receive shares, not actual shares. No dividends or voting rights
until vesting.
B. Stock Options
Non-tradeable call options on the employer's stock, typically granted at-the-money.
Characteristics:
• Strike price usually equals current share price at grant
• Employee gains (Share Price - Strike) upon exercise
• Must exercise after vesting but before expiry

heading: I EXAMPLE
body:
I EXAMPLE
Grant date: Stock trading at $50, options granted with $50 strike
Vesting: 3 years
Exercise: 5 years after grant, stock now at $75
Employee profit: ($75 - $50) × number of options = $25 per option
C. Other Share-Based Instruments
• Stock Appreciation Rights (SARs): Cash-settled based on share appreciation
• Phantom Shares: Cash payments mimicking stock performance
• Employee Stock Purchase Plans: Allow employees to buy shares at a discount (typically
10-15%)

heading: 3. Accounting for Share-Based Compensation
body:
3. Accounting for Share-Based Compensation
I Core Principle: Compensation is recognized at fair value in the period that the employee
provides services.
Three Key Dates
Date
Definition
Action
Grant Date
Compensation offered and
accepted
Measure fair value
Vesting Date
Employee entitled to
compensation
Recognize expense over
period
Settlement Date
Compensation given to
employee
Transfer shares/cash
A. Restricted Stock & RSUs
Valuation Formula
Fair Value = Number of Shares × Share Price at Grant Date
Important: For RSUs, adjust downward for dividends paid during vesting (since RSU holders
don't receive dividends).
I WORKED EXAMPLE
Grant: 25,000 shares on January 1, 2024
Share price at grant: JPY 10,920
Total fair value: 25,000 × 10,920 = JPY 273,000,000
Vesting period: 3 years (straight-line)
Annual Journal Entry (Years 2024, 2025, 2026):
Dr. Compensation Expense (I/S) ............. 91,000,000
    Cr. Share-Based Compensation Reserve (Equity) .... 91,000,000

heading: At Settlement (December 31, 2026):
body:
At Settlement (December 31, 2026):
Dr. Share-Based Compensation Reserve ....... 273,000,000
    Cr. Common Stock & Additional Paid-in Capital ... 273,000,000
I Critical Points:
• Fair value is measured only at grant date and does not change
• No cash impact during vesting (non-cash expense)
• Total equity unchanged (expense reduces retained earnings, reserve increases equity)
• At settlement, reserve converts to permanent equity

heading: B. Stock Options Accounting
body:
B. Stock Options Accounting
Stock options must be valued using an option pricing model (Black-Scholes or Binomial) that
reflects all substantive characteristics.
Key Valuation Assumptions
Assumption
Effect if Increases
Notes
Volatility
↑ Option Value
Most subjective; use implied
volatility
Expected Life
↑ Option Value
Based on historical exercise
patterns
Risk-Free Rate
↑ Option Value
Use government bond yields
Dividend Yield
↓ Option Value
Higher dividends reduce call
value
I WORKED EXAMPLE
Grant: 25 million options on January 1, 2021
Strike price: JPY 4,360 (at-the-money)
Fair value per option: JPY 1,288 (from Black-Scholes)
Total fair value: 25,000,000 × 1,288 = JPY 32,200 million
Vesting: December 31, 2023 (3 years, straight-line)
Annual Entry (2021, 2022, 2023):
Dr. Compensation Expense ................... 10,733 million
    Cr. Share-Based Compensation Reserve ........ 10,733 million
I Key Insight: The actual share price at exercise does NOT affect the equity amount. Total
equity increase = Cash proceeds from exercise + Previously recognized expense. This is a
fundamental concept for valuation.

I Exam Focus: Understanding which vesting condition affects fair value versus quantity is a
common exam question. Market conditions affect the fair value calculation, while performance
conditions may affect the quantity recognized.

heading: 5. Tax Treatment
body:
5. Tax Treatment
Share-based compensation creates temporary differences because the timing and amount
differ between financial reporting and tax returns.
Aspect
Financial Reporting
Tax Return
Timing
Spread over vesting period
At settlement
(exercise/vesting)
Amount
Fair value at grant date
Intrinsic value at settlement
The 'Tax Windfall'
When the settlement value exceeds the grant value (common in bull markets), the company
receives a larger tax deduction than the expense recognized.
Scenario
IFRS Treatment
US GAAP Treatment
Settlement > Grant
(Windfall)
Tax benefit goes directly to
equity
Tax benefit reduces income
tax expense (I/S)
Settlement < Grant
(Shortfall)
Tax cost charged directly to
equity
Tax cost increases income
tax expense (I/S)
I Practical Implication: In rising markets, US GAAP companies show lower effective tax
rates as windfall benefits flow through the income statement. This creates volatility in the
effective tax rate that analysts must adjust for.

heading: 6. Diluted Shares Calculation
body:
6. Diluted Shares Calculation
Treasury Stock Method (TSM)
The TSM assumes the company uses proceeds from exercise/vesting to repurchase shares
at the average market price.
Formula
Diluted Shares = Basic Shares + Shares Issued - Shares Repurchased
Where Proceeds include:
1. Cash from exercise (strike price × shares)
2. Unrecognized compensation expense
I WORKED EXAMPLE
Given:
• Basic shares outstanding: 176,401,000
• Outstanding RSUs: 3,028,000
• Unrecognized compensation expense (RSUs): JPY 6,601 million
• Average share price for the year: JPY 4,200
Calculation:
Shares issued (RSUs): 3,028,000
Proceeds: JPY 6,601m
Shares repurchased: 6,601m ÷ 4,200 = 1,572,000
Net dilution: 3,028,000 - 1,572,000 = 1,456,000 shares
Diluted shares: 176,401,000 + 1,456,000 = 177,857,000
Anti-Dilutive Securities
I Important Rule: If earnings are negative, ALL potentially dilutive securities are anti-dilutive
and excluded from the calculation. This avoids the paradox of 'improving' EPS when the
company is losing money.

heading: 7. Forecasting Share-Based Compensation
body:
7. Forecasting Share-Based Compensation
The Challenge
SBC is typically embedded within operating expense line items (R&D;, SG&A;, COGS).
Simply forecasting total operating expenses as a percentage of sales will implicitly include
SBC, which is problematic for several reasons:
• SBC is non-cash and must be added back in cash flow statements
• SBC often grows differently than other operating expenses
• Early-stage companies may have disproportionately high SBC
Best Practice Approach
Step 1: Isolate SBC
Extract SBC from each line item based on note disclosures
Step 2: Forecast Components Separately
• Non-SBC expenses: As % of revenue (historical average)
• SBC: Discretely using historical %, management guidance, or industry
benchmarks
Step 3: Combine Forecasts
Add forecasted SBC back to each operating expense line
Forecasting Shares Outstanding
Beginning Basic Shares
  + RSUs vested (historical % of outstanding)
  + Options exercised (based on intrinsic value)
  + Other issuances
  - Share repurchases
= Ending Basic Shares
For diluted: Add 50-100% of unvested securities (analyst judgment)

heading: 8. Valuation Considerations
body:
8. Valuation Considerations
The Central Question
Should share-based compensation be treated as a real economic cost in valuation models?
View
Arguments
Verdict
Ignore SBC
• Not a cash expense
• Companies repurchase shares to
offset
 Incorrect
Include SBC
• Share issuance transfers value from
existing shareholders
• Repurchases ARE cash
• Economic substance matters
 Correct
Proper DCF Adjustments
Two sources of dilution must be addressed:
1. Outstanding but Unvested Awards
    → Use diluted shares in valuation denominator
2. Future Awards Not Yet Granted
    → Treat forecasted SBC as cash expense in free cash flow
Practical Example
Company A
Company B
Market Cap
$10,000
$10,000
Net Income
$120
$120
SBC Expense
$0

Operating CF
$420
$570

Capex

I After Adjustment: Company B's adjusted FCF = $270 - $150 = $120, identical to
Company A. The apparent valuation discount disappears. Always adjust for SBC!
Equity Valuation Formula
Price per Share = PV(Future FCF adjusted for SBC) ÷ Diluted Shares
Outstanding

heading: 9. Post-Employment Benefits
body:
9. Post-Employment Benefits
A. Defined Contribution (DC) Plans
The employer's obligation ends with the contribution. Simple to account for:
Expense = Contribution Amount
• Investment risk borne by employee
• Example: 401(k) plans in the US
• Trend: Most companies have shifted to DC to transfer risk
B. Defined Benefit (DB) Plans
The pension benefit amount is defined (typically an annuity). Much more complex:
Key Characteristics:
• Investment risk borne by employer
• Benefit based on: years of service, final salary, life expectancy
• Assets held in separate legal trust (bankruptcy remote)
• Complex actuarial assumptions required
Flow of Funds:
Employer → Contributions → Plan Trust → Benefits → Retirees
C. Other Post-Employment Benefits (OPEB)
Defined benefits other than pensions (e.g., retiree healthcare, life insurance). Key difference:
Usually unfunded (no trust), creating larger balance sheet liabilities.

heading: 10. DB Plans: Balance Sheet
body:
10. DB Plans: Balance Sheet
Funded Status
Funded Status = Fair Value of Plan Assets - Pension Benefit Obligation

employer

Obligation (PBO)
Present value of expected future
payments
Discount rate = AA corporate
bond yield
↑ Service cost, interest,
actuarial losses
↓ Benefit payments
Classification on Balance Sheet
• Overfunded: Assets > PBO → Net pension asset
• Underfunded: Assets < PBO → Net pension liability
I Valuation Note: Underfunded pensions represent real obligations and should be added to
debt when calculating enterprise value. Overfunded positions are typically ignored (surplus
cannot be easily extracted).
