heading: 14. Valuation Adjustments
body:
14. Valuation Adjustments
Enterprise Value Calculation
EV = Market Cap + Debt + Pension Deficit - Cash + Other Adjustments
Rationale: Underfunded pensions are real obligations that must eventually be funded.
Overfunded positions are typically ignored (surplus cannot be extracted).
DCF Modeling
Critical Adjustments:
1. Future Service Costs → Treat as cash expense (like SBC)
    These are not included in current funded status but represent real future cash needs
2. Net Interest Expense → Do NOT include in cash flows
    Already on present value basis; including it would double-count
Adjusted OCF = OCF - Forecasted Service Cost + Actual Contributions

heading: 15. Common Pitfalls & Exam Tips
body:
15. Common Pitfalls & Exam Tips
Share-Based Compensation Pitfalls
II Forgetting SBC is non-cash → Always add back in CFO calculation
II Using basic shares for valuation → Must use diluted shares
II Ignoring SBC in DCF → Leads to overvaluation; treat as cash
II Confusing vesting conditions → Market affects fair value; performance affects quantity
Pension Pitfalls
II Confusing service cost vs. net interest → Service is operating; net interest is financial
II Using pension expense for cash forecasts → Expense ≠ Cash contribution
II Ignoring funded status in leverage ratios → Underfunded = Add to debt
II Not comparing assumptions to peers → Small discount rate changes = Large PBO impact
Exam Strategy
Calculation-Heavy Topics:
• Diluted shares (TSM method)
• Pension expense components (IFRS vs US GAAP)
• Funded status reconciliation
Conceptual Focus:
• Why SBC matters for valuation (dilution + economic cost)
• IFRS vs US GAAP key differences
• Vesting condition impacts
• Tax treatment windfall effects
Common Exam Tricks:
• Testing if you add back SBC in CFO
• Which vesting condition affects fair value vs. quantity
• Funded status changes with contributions and benefits
• Asking for remeasurement vs. pension expense components

heading: I Final Thought: Master both the mechanical accounting AND the
body:
I Final Thought: Master both the mechanical accounting AND the
valuation implications. The exam tests whether you can connect theory to
practice.
Good luck with your CFA Level II studies!

heading: CFA Level II
body:
CFA Level II
Multinational Operations
Foreign Currency Translation & Analysis
Comprehensive Study Guide
Learning Outcomes Covered:
• Currency terminology: presentation, functional, and local currency
• Foreign currency transaction exposure and accounting
• Translation effects on subsidiary and parent financials
• Current rate method vs temporal method comparison
• Translation calculations and effects on statements
• Impact on financial ratios
• Hyperinflationary economy translation
• Multinational operations and effective tax rates
• Sales growth sustainability analysis
• Currency fluctuation impacts on financial results

heading: Table of Contents
body:
Table of Contents
• 1. Currency Terminology
• 2. Foreign Currency Transactions
• 3. Translation Exposure and Risk
• 4. Translation Methods Overview
• 5. Current Rate Method
• 6. Temporal Method
• 7. Determining the Appropriate Method
• 8. Translation Process Step-by-Step

heading: 1. Currency Terminology
body:
1. Currency Terminology
Three Key Currency Concepts:
Presentation Currency: The currency in which financial statements are presented. Typically
the currency of the country where the company is located.
Functional Currency: The currency of the primary economic environment in which an entity
operates. The currency in which the entity primarily generates and expends cash.
Local Currency: The currency where the company operates. For subsidiaries, this is the
currency of the host country.
I Key Point: In most cases, all three are the same currency. However, for multinationals with
foreign subsidiaries, these currencies differ, requiring translation.

heading: 2. Foreign Currency Transactions
body:
2. Foreign Currency Transactions
Definition: A foreign currency is any currency other than a company's functional currency.
Foreign currency transactions are those denominated in a currency other than the functional
currency.
Types of Foreign Currency Transactions:
1. Import/Export in a foreign currency
2. Borrow/Lend in a foreign currency
Both lead to assets or liabilities denominated in a foreign currency.
Transaction Exposure: The risk that exchange rate changes will affect the value of foreign
currency receivables or payables between the transaction date and settlement date.
IFRS/GAAP Rule: The change in value of foreign currency assets/liabilities is recognized as a
gain or loss on the income statement.
Position
For. Curr. Strengthens
For. Curr. Weakens
AR (Asset)
Gain (+)
Loss (−)
AP (Liability)
Loss (−)
Gain (+)

heading: Worked Example: Foreign Currency Transaction
body:
Worked Example: Foreign Currency Transaction
Scenario: Company A (EUR) exports to Company B (MXN)
• Transaction date: Nov 1, 20X5, Invoice: 100,000 MXN, Payment: 45 days
• Nov 1 rate: MXN.EUR = 0.0684, Dec 15 rate: MXN.EUR = 0.0703
Nov 1 Entry (Record Sale):
Dr. Inventory 6,840 EUR
  Cr. AP 6,840 EUR (100,000 × 0.0684)
Dec 15 Entry (Payment):
Dr. AP 6,840 EUR
Dr. FX Loss 190 EUR
  Cr. Cash 7,030 EUR (100,000 × 0.0703)
Analysis: Company A owes fixed 100,000 MXN but uses variable EUR. EUR
weakened (takes more EUR to buy same MXN), resulting in 190 EUR realized
loss.

heading: 3. Placement of Foreign Currency Gains/Losses
body:
3. Placement of Foreign Currency Gains/Losses
IFRS/GAAP: Gains/losses go to the income statement, but standards don't specify placement.
Companies can choose:
a) Component of Operating Income/Expense (affects operating margin)
b) Component of Non-Operating Income/Expense (doesn't affect operating margin)
I Important: This choice significantly affects operating profit margin. Analysts should adjust
for consistency when comparing companies.
Disclosure Requirements:
• IFRS: Requires disclosure of 'the amount of exchange differences recognized in P/L'
• US GAAP: Requires disclosure of 'the aggregate transaction gain/loss'
• Neither requires disclosure of the line item location
Why Some Companies Have Minimal Transaction Gains/Losses:
• Limited foreign currency transactions
• Currencies are pegged, fixed, or stable
• Offsetting transactions (import and export in same currency)
• Active hedging practices (forwards, options, swaps)

heading: 4. Translation Methods Overview
body:
4. Translation Methods Overview
The Translation Challenge: Foreign subsidiaries keep records in local currency. For
consolidation, the parent must translate these into its presentation currency.
Two Key Questions:
1. What is the appropriate exchange rate for each financial statement account?
2. How should the translation adjustment be reflected?
   → As a gain/loss on Income Statement? OR
   → As an equity adjustment on Balance Sheet?
Balance Sheet Exposure (Accounting Exposure): Assets and liabilities translated at
current spot rate are 'exposed' to translation adjustments.
• Net Asset Exposure: Exposed asset value > Exposed liability value
• Net Liability Exposure: Exposed asset value < Exposed liability value
Exposure
For. Curr. Strengthens
For. Curr. Weakens
Net Asset
Positive Adj.
Negative Adj.
Net Liability
Negative Adj.
Positive Adj.
I Note: The translation adjustment on the balance sheet is a cumulative account reflecting
the sum of all translation adjustments quarter-to-quarter.

heading: 5. Current Rate Method
body:
5. Current Rate Method
Used when: Foreign currency = Functional currency (subsidiary is self-contained)
Translation Rules:
• Balance Sheet:
  x All assets and liabilities at current spot rate at B/S date
  y Equity accounts at historical rates (except R/E)
• Income Statement:
  z Revenues and expenses at rates when transactions occurred (typically average rate)
Translation Adjustment: Goes to Shareholders' Equity on balance sheet (cumulative).
When subsidiary sold, balance transferred to I/S as realized gain/loss.
I Key Characteristic: Since Assets > Liabilities typically, creates net asset exposure. If
foreign currency strengthens → positive adjustment; if weakens → negative adjustment. The
entire investment is exposed.

heading: 6. Temporal Method
body:
6. Temporal Method
Used when: Presentation currency = Functional currency (subsidiary is extension of parent)
Translation Rules:
• Balance Sheet:
  a) Monetary A/L at spot rate at B/S date
  b) Non-monetary A/L at historical value → historical rates
  c) Non-monetary A/L at current value → current rates
• Equity: Historical rates (except R/E)
• Income Statement:
  a) Revenues/Expenses at rates when transactions occurred (average)
  b) Exceptions: Expenses related to non-monetary assets (COGS, Depreciation,
Amortization) use same rates as related assets
Translation Adjustment: Recognized as gain or loss on Income Statement (US GAAP:
'Remeasurement gain/loss')
I Key Characteristic: Only assets/liabilities at current values are exposed. Since functional
= presentation currency, monetary items in foreign currency generate realized gains/losses in
near term.

body:

Item
Current Rate

FC = Func. Curr.
PC = Func. Curr.

I Exam Tip: Remember: CR-E (Current Rate to Equity), T-IS (Temporal to Income
Statement).

heading: 7. Determining Which Method to Use
body:
7. Determining Which Method to Use
If Functional Currency = Foreign (Local) Currency:
→ Use CURRENT RATE METHOD
→ Subsidiary is self-contained and independent
→ Cash flows primarily in local currency
If Functional Currency = Parent's (Presentation) Currency:
→ Use TEMPORAL METHOD
→ Subsidiary is extension of parent operations
→ Cash flows primarily affect parent's currency
Functional Currency Determination Factors:
1. Currency that mainly influences sales prices
2. Currency of country whose regulations determine sales prices
3. Currency that mainly influences labor, material, and other costs
4. Currency in which funds from financing activities are generated
5. Currency in which receipts from operating activities are retained
I Note: If indicators are mixed, management uses judgment. This is critical as it determines
the translation method.

heading: 8. Translation Process Step-by-Step
body:
8. Translation Process Step-by-Step
Step 1: Identify the Functional Currency
Use the five factors to determine subsidiary's functional currency
Step 2: Translate to Functional Currency (if needed)
If local ≠ functional, translate using temporal method
Step 3: Translate to Presentation Currency
Translate functional currency to parent's presentation currency using current
rate method

heading: 9. Comprehensive Translation Example
body:
9. Comprehensive Translation Example
Scenario: Interco (EUR) establishes Canadaco (CAD) on Jan 1, 20X1

• Canadaco borrows $5,000, purchases $12,000 inventory
• March 31, 20X2: No transactions, USD.EUR = 0.80 (current), 1.00 (historical)

heading: 10. Impact on Financial Ratios
body:
10. Impact on Financial Ratios
Key Insight: Ratios differ when different rates are used under each method to calculate
numerator and/or denominator.
Ratio Preservation: Ratios from functional currency to current rate translation will mostly be
preserved because all accounts use current rate.
Ratio Distortion: Temporal method can distort ratios significantly because:
• Different accounts use different rates (historical vs current)
• Inventory turnover, fixed asset turnover affected
• Profitability ratios may be distorted

heading: 11. Hyperinflationary Economies
body:
11. Hyperinflationary Economies
When subsidiary operates in highly inflationary environment, functional currency not
very relevant.
IFRS: Foreign entity's FS first restated for changes in local general price level, then translated
at current rates.
GAAP: Must use temporal method.
Definition of Excessive Inflation: (1 + i)³ − 1 > 1.00 → i ≈ 26% cumulative over 3 years
Key Difference:
• Current Rate: Under hyperinflation, using current rate creates large
cumulative translation losses as foreign currency devalues
• Temporal: Non-monetary assets at historical rates are preserved; no
translation loss on these items
• IFRS Inflation Adjustment: Restate for inflation first, then translate -
captures purchasing power changes

heading: 12. Effective Tax Rates for Multinationals
body:
12. Effective Tax Rates for Multinationals
Multinationals incur taxes in country where profit is earned.
Key Concepts:
• Most countries have tax treaties preventing double-taxation
• Companies can allocate profit to lower tax jurisdictions through transfer pricing
• If home tax rate > foreign tax rate, may owe incremental tax on repatriation
Disclosure: Companies must reconcile statutory tax rate to effective tax rate, showing impact
of foreign jurisdictions.
I Interpretation: If "earnings taxed in foreign jurisdictions" is negative (reduces effective
rate), company earned more profit in lower-tax countries.

heading: 13. Sales Growth Analysis
body:
13. Sales Growth Analysis
Sales Growth = Quantity × Price × Exchange Rate
Sales growth can be decomposed into:
1. Volume growth: Increase in quantity sold
2. Price realization: Increase in prices
3. Foreign currency exchange: Changes in exchange rates
Sustainability Analysis: Volume growth is most sustainable. Price increases have limits. FX
impacts are temporary and can reverse.

heading: 14. Financial Results and Disclosures
body:
14. Financial Results and Disclosures
Parent may have subsidiaries using different methods:
• Some using temporal method (functional = parent currency)
• Others using current rate (functional = local currency)
• Results in both translation g/l on I/S AND translation adjustment on B/S
Required Disclosures (IFRS/GAAP):
• Amount of exchange differences recognized in net income
• Amount of cumulative translation adjustment on balance sheet
• Reconciliation of beginning and ending cumulative balances
• GAAP: Amount transferred from B/S to I/S when subsidiary sold
I Note: No requirement to separate amounts from transactions vs translations (temporal
method).

heading: 15. Key Exam Tips
body:
15. Key Exam Tips
Critical Concepts to Master:
1. Know the difference: Current Rate → Equity, Temporal → Income
Statement
2. Functional currency determination is key - drives which method to use
3. Exposure direction: Net asset exp. + currency strengthens = positive adj.
4. Temporal method exceptions: COGS, Depreciation, Amortization use
historical rates
5. Hyperinflation: IFRS uses inflation adjustment + current rate; GAAP uses
temporal
6. Current rate preserves ratios; Temporal can distort them significantly
7. Translation adjustment is cumulative on balance sheet (not annual)
8. Practice calculations: Be able to translate both I/S and B/S quickly
9. Memorize the 5 factors for functional currency determination
10. Understand tax implications: Effective rate reduced when profit in
low-tax jurisdictions

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: Spot Rates & Discount Factors — the Core Identity
body:
A spot rate zN is the yield-to-maturity of a risk-free zero-coupon bond maturing in N periods. The discount factor
DFN is the price today of a single unit of currency to be received in N periods. The two are mechanically equivalent
— given either, you can derive the other. The spot curve plots zN versus N and represents the term structure of
default-risk-free interest rates.
Discount factor ↔ spot rate
DF_N = 1 / (1 + z_N)^N        (equivalently  z_N = (1/DF_N)^(1/N) – 1)
No reinvestment-rate assumption is required because a zero has a single cash flow at N.

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: Forward Rates & the Forward Rate Model
body:
A forward rate f(A, B–A) is a rate set today for a loan that begins at time A and ends at time B, tenor (B–A) periods.
Forward rates are implied by the spot curve via no-arbitrage. The forward pricing model says the discount factor to
B equals the discount factor to A multiplied by the forward price from A to B.
Forward pricing model and forward rate model
Prices:   DF_B = DF_A × F(A, B–A)           ⇒  F(A, B–A) = DF_B / DF_A
Rates:    (1 + z_B)^B = (1 + z_A)^A × (1 + f(A, B–A))^(B–A)
Successive 1-period rates:   (1 + z_T)^T = (1 + z_1)(1 + f_{1,1})(1 + f_{2,1})…(1 + f_{T–1,1})
⇒  z_T is the geometric mean of the 1-period spot rate and the T–1 one-period forward rates.

✓  Worked example — Example 1 (z₁ = 7%, z₃ = 9%)
DF₁ = 1/1.07 = 0.9346
DF₃ = 1/1.09³ = 0.7722
Forward price F₁,₂ (2-yr bond delivered in 1 yr):  F₁,₂ = DF₃ / DF₁ = 0.7722 / 0.9346 = 0.8262
Interpretation: 0.8262 is the price agreed today, paid one year hence, for a 2-year zero paying 1 unit at maturity
in 3 years.

✓  Worked example — forward rates from z₁=9%, z₂=10%, z₃=11% (Example 2)
f₁,₁:  (1.10)² = (1.09)(1 + f₁,₁)  ⇒  f₁,₁ = 1.10² / 1.09 – 1 = 11.01%
f₂,₁:  (1.11)³ = (1.10)²(1 + f₂,₁)  ⇒  f₂,₁ = 1.11³ / 1.10² – 1 = 13.03%
f₁,₂:  (1.11)³ = (1.09)(1 + f₁,₂)²  ⇒  f₁,₂ = [1.11³ / 1.09]^(1/2) – 1 = 12.01%
Because the spot curve is upward sloping (9 → 10 → 11), the forward curve lies above it and one-period
forwards rise with start date (11.01% < 13.03%).

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: Shape Relationships — Four Results That Always Hold
body:
● Upward-sloping spot curve  ⇒  forward curve lies above spot curve; forward rates rise with initiation date
A.
● Downward-sloping (inverted) spot curve  ⇒  forward curve lies below spot curve; later A gives a forward
curve further below spot.
● Flat spot curve  ⇒  all one-period forward rates equal the spot rate.
● Humped spot curve  ⇒  rare; intermediate rates exceed both short- and long-term rates. Signals typically a
transition regime.

⚠  Trap 1 — Average-vs-Marginal Intuition
The spot rate is an AVERAGE over the whole period; the forward rate is the MARGINAL rate between future
periods. When an average rises, the marginal data point must be ABOVE the average; when an average falls,
the marginal must be below. That is why forward > spot on an upward slope and forward < spot on an inverted
curve.

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: Bootstrapping — Par Curve → Spot Curve
body:
The par curve plots yields on coupon-paying government bonds priced at par across maturities. A coupon bond is a
portfolio of zeros, so zero rates can be extracted maturity-by-maturity by solving forward-substitution equations —
this is BOOTSTRAPPING.
Bootstrapping recipe (annual coupons)
Step 1: z₁ equals the 1-year par rate (bond has a single cash flow).
Step 2: With z₁ known, set bond price = 1 and solve for z₂:  1 = c/(1+z₁) + (1+c)/(1+z₂)².
Step 3: With z₁ and z₂ known, solve for z₃, and so on for each longer maturity.
Each step uses ALL previously bootstrapped spot rates to discount coupons prior to maturity.

✓  Worked example — Bootstrap (par rates 5%, 5.97%, 6.91%, 7.81%)
z₁ = 5% (par = zero since single cash flow).
2-year:   1 = 0.0597/1.05 + 1.0597/(1+z₂)²   ⇒  z₂ = 6%
3-year:   1 = 0.0691/1.05 + 0.0691/1.06² + 1.0691/(1+z₃)³   ⇒  z₃ = 7%
4-year:   1 = 0.0781/1.05 + 0.0781/1.06² + 0.0781/1.07³ + 1.0781/(1+z₄)⁴   ⇒  z₄ = 8%
Bootstrapped spot curve: 5%, 6%, 7%, 8%.

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: YTM in Relation to Spot Rates
body:
For a coupon bond, by no-arbitrage the price equals the sum of cash flows discounted at the CORRESPONDING
spot rates. The YTM is the SINGLE discount rate that equates those same cash flows to the same price — so YTM
is effectively a weighted average of the spot rates the cash flows depend on.

YTM vs Spot (key relationships)
Upward-sloping curve:    z₁ < y_T < z_T   (YTM sits between shortest and longest spot).
Downward-sloping curve:  z_T < y_T < z₁.
Flat curve:              YTM = spot rate = all forward rates.
The longer the maturity, the more the longest spot rate dominates the YTM because principal (the largest CF) is
discounted at z_T.

✓  Worked example — 6% 2-yr bond with z₁=9%, z₂=10% (Example 5)
Price via spot rates:  60/1.09 + 1060/1.10² = $931.08
Solve for YTM:  60/(1+y₂) + 1060/(1+y₂)² = 931.08  ⇒  y₂ = 9.97%
Because the curve is upward sloping,  z₁ = 9%  <  y₂ = 9.97%  <  z₂ = 10%. ✓

section_title: SPOT RATES, FORWARD RATES, AND YTM
heading: YTM as an Expected Return — Four Invalidating Conditions
body:
Investors earn the YTM only under very restrictive assumptions: all coupons and principal received in full, and all
coupons reinvested at the original YTM. The YTM is a poor estimate of expected return if any of the following holds:
● Interest rates are volatile (reinvestment rate won't match YTM).
● The yield curve is sloped (upward or downward) — reinvestment won't match YTM.
● Significant default risk (actual CFs may differ from promised CFs).
● The bond has embedded options (call / put / conversion) — exercise truncates the holding period.

✓  Expected return vs YTM — 5-yr 10% coupon, z = {5,6,7,8,9}%
Implied forwards: f₁,₁=7.0%, f₂,₁=9.0%, f₃,₁=11.1%, f₄,₁=13.1%. Price as % of par = 105.43.
YTM y₅ solves 105.43 = 10/(1+y)+10/(1+y)²+…+110/(1+y)⁵  ⇒  y₅ = 8.62%.
If forwards ARE the future spot rates, terminal wealth = 10(1.07)(1.09)(1.111)(1.131) + 10(1.09)(1.111)(1.131)
+ 10(1.111)(1.131) + 10(1.131) + 110 ≈ 162.22.
Expected annualized return = (162.22/105.43)^(1/5) – 1 = 9.00%,  not the 8.62% YTM.
Takeaway: YTM ≠ expected return even when forwards are realized, except on a FLAT yield curve.

⚠  Trap 2 — "Forwards extend beyond today's longest spot" — FALSE
Forward rates cannot be derived beyond the longest spot maturity observed today. If the spot curve ends at 30
years, a 3-year-forward curve extends only to year 27 (f₃,₂₇), a 4-year forward to f₄,₂₆, etc. A forecast of f₁₀,₃₀
from today's 30-year spot curve is impossible.

section_title: FORWARD CURVE STABILITY & ACTIVE PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
heading: Forward Contract Price Stability
body:
A forward contract price remains UNCHANGED as long as future spot rates evolve exactly as today's forward
curve predicts. A change in the forward price signals that the realized spot curve has deviated from today's implied
forwards. This is the keystone result for active bond PM.
Trader rules based on the forward curve
Expect future spot < today's forward rate  ⇒  forward price will RISE  ⇒  BUY the forward (bond undervalued).
Expect future spot > today's forward rate  ⇒  forward price will FALL  ⇒  SELL the forward (bond overvalued).
Expect spots = today's forwards            ⇒  forward price unchanged; earn the 1-period spot rate on any bond.

✓  Numerical check — flat 4% yield curve
DF₁ = 1/1.04 = 0.9615,  DF₂ = 1/1.04² = 0.9246,  DF₃ = 1/1.04³ = 0.8890.
Forward price F₂,₁ = DF₃/DF₂ = 0.8890/0.9246 = 0.9615.
After 1 year, if the discount function matches today's forward curve, F^new_{1,1} = 0.9246/0.9615 = 0.9615 —
UNCHANGED.
Every bond "rolls down the curve" and earns exactly 4% (current spot); a 3-year bond returns (0.9246 –

section_title: FORWARD CURVE STABILITY & ACTIVE PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
heading: )/0.8890 = 4% in year 1.

section_title: FORWARD CURVE STABILITY & ACTIVE PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
heading: Rate Expectations and Expected Return
body:
For a given bond, compare the MANAGER'S projected spot curve against today's forward curve:
● Projected spot < forward ⇒ bond is cheap; expected return > 1-period spot rate.
● Projected spot > forward ⇒ bond is rich; expected return < 1-period spot rate.
● Projected spot = forward ⇒ expected return = 1-period spot rate (the "neutral" scenario).

✓  3-yr 8% bond at par; flat 8% curve (Example 7 setup)
If forwards are realized ⇒ expected return = 8% (the 1-period risk-free rate).
If trader projects flat 9% curve 1 year out:  (8 + 8/1.09 + 108/1.09²)/100 – 1 = 6.24%  <  8%.
If trader projects flat 7% curve 1 year out:  (8 + 8/1.07 + 108/1.07²)/100 – 1 = 9.81%  >  8%.
Wider gap between projection and forward ⇒ larger deviation from the current YTM.

section_title: FORWARD CURVE STABILITY & ACTIVE PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT
heading: Rolling Down the Yield Curve (Riding the Yield Curve)
body:
On an upward-sloping curve expected to remain static, buying a bond with maturity LONGER than the investment
horizon and selling before maturity generates coupon income + reinvestment + capital gain as the bond "rolls
down" to a lower yield and higher price. The longer the bond's maturity (relative to the horizon), the more sensitive
total return is to the spot-forward spread.

Bond total return decomposition
Bond return = Coupon income   +   Reinvestment of coupons   ±   Capital gain/loss on sale prior to maturity
On an upward-sloping curve held static, the bond is revalued at a lower yield each period, so price rises —
generating the capital gain.

✓  Rolldown — matched maturity (6%, 5-yr) vs longer (7%, 6-yr); 2-year horizon
Spot rates: 1y = 2%, 3y = 4%, 4y = 5%, 5y = 6%, 6y = 7%.
6% 5-yr bond @ par 100:  After 2 years it is a 3-yr bond priced at unchanged 4% yield ⇒ 105.55.  Terminal
wealth = 6(1.02) + 6 + 105.55 = 117.67.  Annualized 2-yr return = 8.476%.
7% 6-yr bond @ par 100:  After 2 years it is a 4-yr bond priced at unchanged 5% yield ⇒ 107.09.  Terminal
wealth = 7(1.02) + 7 + 107.09 = 121.23.  Annualized 2-yr return = 10.10%.
Excess return ≈ 1.6% p.a. — from higher coupon AND larger rolldown capital gain.

⚠  Trap 3 — Rolldown is NOT riskless
Rolldown returns evaporate if the curve rises, flattens, or inverts over the holding period. The "maturity spread
carry trade" (borrow short, lend long in the same currency) is especially vulnerable to an unexpected spike in
short-term rates — e.g., from inflation surprises. On exam questions, rolldown assumes a STATIC yield curve;
if the question says rates rise, rolldown underperforms.

⚠  Trap 4 — Match bond maturity to horizon? Not for rolldown
Rolling down the yield curve requires bonds LONGER than the horizon, then sold early. A bond with maturity =
horizon simply matures at par and captures no rolldown capital gain. "Equal to" is the wrong answer on exam
MC for rolldown strategy.

section_title: THE SWAP RATE CURVE AND SPREADS
heading: The Swap Rate Curve
body:
An interest rate swap exchanges fixed-rate for floating-rate payments. The swap rate is the single fixed rate that, at
origination, sets the PV of fixed-leg payments equal to the PV of floating-leg payments (so swap value at inception
= 0). The swap curve plots swap rates across maturities and is a key benchmark for time value of money in fixed-
income valuation, alongside the government spot curve.
Swap rate equation (par swap, notional = 1)
Σ_{t=1..T}  s_T / (1 + z_t)^t   +   1 / (1 + z_T)^T   =   1

The RHS value of the floating leg at origination is 1. The LHS equates the fixed leg (coupon s_T at spot rates) +
principal PV to 1.
Given spot rates → solve for s_T.  Given swap rates → solve for spot rates.  Swaps, spots, and discount factors
are mathematically linked.

✓  Worked example — Swap curve from DFs (Example 8)
DF₁=0.9524, DF₂=0.8900, DF₃=0.8163, DF₄=0.7350.
Spot rates:  z₁ = (1/0.9524) – 1 = 5.00%,  z₂ = (1/0.8900)^(1/2) – 1 = 6.00%,  z₃ = 7.00%,  z₄ = 8.00%.
s₁: s₁/(1.05) + 1/(1.05) = 1  ⇒  s₁ = 5.00%.
s₂: s₂/1.05 + (s₂+1)/1.06² = 1  ⇒  s₂ = 5.97%.
s₃: s₃ Σ DFs through year 3  + DF₃ = 1  ⇒  s₃ = 6.91%.
s₄: same recipe through year 4  ⇒  s₄ = 7.81%.
Note that these swap rates equal the PAR rates used in the bootstrapping example in §1.4 — par, swap, and
zero rates are connected.

section_title: THE SWAP RATE CURVE AND SPREADS
heading: Why Institutions Choose Swap vs. Government Curve
body:
Which benchmark to use depends on the institution's hedge profile and the relative liquidity of each market.
Institution / Situation
Preferred benchmark
Why
Wholesale bank — hedges B/S
with swaps
Swap curve
Matches hedging instruments
Retail bank — limited swap
exposure
Government spot curve
Matches funding profile
Asset manager — Treasury-heavy
benchmark
Government spot curve
Matches benchmark composition
Issuer pricing new corporate issue
Swap curve (MRR-referenced)
Spread-to-swap is the market
convention
Market in which government debt
is illiquid
Swap curve
Swaps often have more maturity
points

section_title: THE SWAP RATE CURVE AND SPREADS
heading: Swap Spread — Definition & Use
body:
The swap spread is the spread paid by the fixed-rate payer of an interest rate swap over the on-the-run
government bond of the same maturity. It captures the market's perceived credit risk of interbank lending versus
government default-risk-free debt. Swap spreads typically widen COUNTERCYCLICALLY — larger in recessions,
narrower in expansions.
Swap spread & related spreads
Swap Spread_T  =  Swap Rate_T  –  On-the-run Government Yield_T

I-spread (ISPRD / interpolated spread) = Bond YTM – Swap rate (interpolated) at bond's maturity.
Z-spread = constant spread added to the benchmark spot curve that equates discounted CFs to bond price.
Example: 5-yr swap fixed 2.00%, 5-yr UST 1.70%  ⇒  Swap spread = 30 bps.

✓  Worked example — pricing a bond using the swap spread (GECC 1.625% 2/Jul/2024)
Eval date 12-Jul-2021; remaining maturity = 2 + 350/360 = 2.97 years.
2-yr and 3-yr UST yields = 0.525%, 0.588%.
Interpolated 2.97-yr UST = 0.525% + (350/360)(0.588% – 0.525%) = 0.586%.
Swap spread (same maturity) = 0.918%.  Bond YTM = 0.586% + 0.918% = 1.504%  (I-spread assumed zero).
Discounting semiannual coupons and $1m principal at YTM 1.504% gives invoice price US$1,003,954.12.
Accrued interest = 1,000,000 × (0.01625/2)(10/180) = $451.39;  clean price = $1,003,502.73.

⚠  Trap 5 — Swap Spreads CAN Be Negative
Since 2008, US 30-year swap spreads have periodically turned NEGATIVE (hitting –62 bps intramonth in
November 2008). Causes: higher swap-dealer regulatory capital requirements post-crisis, strong duration
demand, and leverage constraints on balance sheets. Don't assume "govt pays less than private" on the exam
— the presumed positive swap spread is a convention, not a mathematical certainty.

section_title: THE SWAP RATE CURVE AND SPREADS
heading: Short-Term Spreads as Credit/Liquidity Barometers
body:
Short-term interest rate spreads are real-time indicators of perceived credit and liquidity risk in the financial
system. These spreads widen sharply during periods of market stress.
Spread
Definition
What it measures
TED spread
3-month MRR (historically
Libor) – 3-month T-bill yield
Interbank credit & liquidity risk relative to default-free
T-bills. "TED" = T-bill + ED Eurodollar futures ticker.
Spiked in COVID-19 (early 2020).
MRR–OIS (formerly Libor–
OIS)
MRR – overnight indexed
swap rate
Counterparty credit risk on term interbank loans vs.
overnight-collateralized swap rate. OIS floats on
geometric avg of daily unsecured overnight rate (fed
funds for USD, Eonia for EUR).
Swap spread
Swap rate – on-the-run
government yield (same
maturity)
Longer-term credit risk premium between
interbank/bank credit and government debt. Has
turned negative since 2008 in USD 30-yr.
Z-spread
Constant spread over
benchmark spot curve
Credit + liquidity + options + convexity effects of a
specific bond.

⚠  Trap 6 — MRR-based benchmarks are replacing Libor

The Libor swap curve historically reflected A1/A+ commercial bank credit. Post-Libor transition has shifted the
world toward SECURED overnight rates: SOFR (US, Treasury repo), ESTR (euro area, secured), CORRA
(Canada). "MRR" (market reference rate) is the CFA curriculum's umbrella term. Because SOFR is
collateralized by Treasuries, it is more sensitive to government-debt supply/demand than Libor was.

heading: Deep Dive — Liquidity Preference
body:
Liquidity preference asserts that forward rates are upwardly biased estimators of expected future spot rates
because of an additive liquidity premium. The premium compensates investors for interest rate risk when lending
long term and should be non-decreasing across successive periods. This explains the empirical regularity of
positively-sloped yield curves without requiring investors to expect rising rates.

heading: Deep Dive — Segmented Markets & Preferred Habitat
body:
Segmented Markets (extreme form) says life insurers buy long, pension funds buy long, money-market funds buy
short — with no crossover — so each maturity sector's yield is determined entirely by that sector's
supply/demand. Preferred Habitat (softer form) says investors have preferences but WILL switch if yield gaps
become sufficiently large. QE is a classic preferred-habitat application: the Fed buying long-term Treasuries and
MBS drives long-term yields down because natural MBS/Treasury investors (e.g., banks, pensions) cannot easily
withdraw and end up bidding remaining supply more aggressively.

✓  QE + Preferred Habitat example
Fed holdings 20-Sep-2007: $780 bn — all US Treasuries (~34% short-term T-bills).
Fed holdings 29-Oct-2014: $4,219 bn — only 58% Treasuries, ZERO T-bills; $1.7 trillion in MBS (41% of total).
Pre-QE: MBS yields typically 5–6%. By end-2012: MBS yields < 2%.
Preferred habitat explains it: MBS-focused institutions (can't easily exit MBS due to specialized expertise) bid
remaining post-Fed-purchase supply more aggressively, driving yields down.

⚠  Trap 7 — Which theory does each empirical fact support?
• "Fallen angel" bonds sell at depressed prices because HY-restricted pension funds must dump them →
SEGMENTED MARKETS.
• Investors switch maturities when yield gaps widen → PREFERRED HABITAT.
• Short-horizon returns on long bonds > short bonds (empirically true) → REFUTES pure Local Expectations,
supports Liquidity Preference.
• Forward rates empirically OVER-predict future spots → supports Liquidity Preference (upward bias).
• "Bonds of any maturity are perfect substitutes" → PURE (UNBIASED) EXPECTATIONS + risk neutrality
assumption.

⚠  Trap 8 — Pure vs Local Expectations
PURE/UNBIASED says expected returns are equal for ALL maturity strategies over ANY horizon. LOCAL only
makes the weaker claim that expected returns over SHORT holding periods (not long ones) equal the risk-free
rate. Local is compatible with long-horizon risk premia; Pure is not. The empirical finding that long-bond short-
horizon returns > risk-free rate refutes Local Expectations too.

section_title: YIELD CURVE FACTORS, VOLATILITY, KEY RATE DURATION
heading: The Three-Factor Yield Curve Model
body:
Litterman and Scheinkman (1991) showed yield curve movements are well described by three statistically
independent factors extracted from the variance-covariance matrix of historical rate movements: LEVEL,
STEEPNESS (slope), and CURVATURE.
Factor
Description
% of variance explained
Typical interpretation
Level
Parallel up/down shift of entire
curve.
> 75% (dominant)
Affected by monetary policy,
inflation regime.
Steepness
(Slope)
Short rates change more than
long rates (or vice versa); non-
parallel.
Secondary
Bear flattening / bull steepening;
policy vs. growth expectations.
Curvature
"Twist" — short AND long rise
while mid falls, or vice versa.
Smallest
Negative on intermediates,
positive on short/long.

section_title: YIELD CURVE FACTORS, VOLATILITY, KEY RATE DURATION
heading: Shaping Risk & Key Rate Duration
body:
Effective duration measures sensitivity to PARALLEL shifts only. Shaping risk — sensitivity to non-parallel curve
changes — is measured by KEY RATE DURATION (KRD): a bond or portfolio's price sensitivity to a small change in
a single "key" rate on the benchmark curve, holding other rates fixed.
Key Rate Duration Decomposition (Equation 15)
%ΔP  ≈  – Σ_k  KeyDur_k × Δz_k       (sum over key maturities)
Sum of key rate durations = Effective Duration  (because a parallel shift moves all key rates by the same
amount).
Level / Slope / Curvature duration form (Eq 16):   %ΔP ≈ – KeyDur_L·Δx_L – KeyDur_S·Δx_S – KeyDur_C·Δx_C.

✓  Worked example — $300 portfolio of 1-yr, 5-yr, 10-yr zeros ($100 each)
Effective Duration = 0.333(1 + 5 + 10) = 5.333.
KeyDur₁ = 1/[(300)(0.01)] = 0.3333;  KeyDur₅ = 5/[(300)(0.01)] = 1.6667;  KeyDur₁₀ = 10/[(300)(0.01)] = 3.3333.
Sum = 5.333 ✓.
Level/Slope/Curvature decomposition:
  Level (parallel +1):    KeyDur_L = (1+5+10)/3 = 5.3333.
  Steepness (short –1, long +1):  $ chg = (1 – 10); KeyDur_S = –(1–10)/[(300)(0.01)] = 3.0.
  Curvature (short +1, long +1, mid 0):  KeyDur_C = (1+10)/[(300)(0.01)] = 3.6667.
If Δx_L = –0.005, Δx_S = +0.002, Δx_C = +0.001  ⇒  %ΔP ≈ +1.7%.
Relations:  KeyDur_L = KD₁ + KD₅ + KD₁₀;  KeyDur_S = –KD₁ + KD₁₀;  KeyDur_C = KD₁ + KD₁₀.


⚠  Trap 9 — Sum of KRDs equals Effective Duration
Because a parallel shift moves every key rate by the same amount, the sum of all key rate durations must equal
the portfolio's effective duration. If your KRDs don't sum to effective duration, you have a computational error.
On the exam: given KD₁=0.50, KD₅=0.70, KD₁₀=0.90 and a 50-bp parallel decline, %ΔP = –(0.50 + 0.70 +

section_title: YIELD CURVE FACTORS, VOLATILITY, KEY RATE DURATION
YIELD CURVE FACTORS, VOLATILITY, KEY RATE DURATION
heading: Maturity Structure of Yield Volatility
body:
The term structure of volatility σ(t,T) is the annualized standard deviation of proportional changes in the spot rate
at maturity T, measured at time t. It is empirically DOWNWARD-sloping: short rates are more volatile than long
rates.
Volatility term structure (lognormal)
σ(t, T)  =  σ[Δr(t,T) / r(t,T)]  /  √Δt
Annualize a monthly σ by multiplying by √12 (equivalently divide monthly σ by √(1/12)).
Example — US Treasuries Aug 2005 – Dec 2007: monthly σ(3m) = 10.15% ⇒ annual σ(3m) = 10.15% × √12 =

The 30-year σ is 11.69% — falling from 35.15% at 3 months to 11.69% at 30 years.
Short-term volatility is driven by MONETARY POLICY uncertainty; long-term volatility is driven by REAL-ECONOMY
and INFLATION uncertainty. Short-long volatility correlation depends on the evolving linkages among these three
drivers.

⚠  Trap 10 — Volatility vs. Price Volatility
Short-rate YIELD volatility > long-rate yield volatility. BUT long-bond PRICE volatility > short-bond price
volatility because of duration. Don't conflate "yields more volatile at short end" with "prices more volatile at
short end" — duration flips the ranking for prices.

section_title: DEVELOPING RATE VIEWS WITH MACRO VARIABLES
heading: The Bond Risk Premium (Term / Duration Premium)
body:
The bond risk premium is the expected excess return of a default-free long-term bond over a short-term bond (or
the 1-period risk-free rate). It is a FORWARD-LOOKING expectation that must be estimated. Excludes
credit/liquidity risk, which are added on for non-government debt.

section_title: DEVELOPING RATE VIEWS WITH MACRO VARIABLES
heading: Fiscal, Supply, and Flow Effects
body:
● Larger deficits ⇒ more bond supply ⇒ higher yields at the relevant maturity segment (segmented-markets
mechanism).
● Longer debt maturity structure ⇒ greater excess bond returns (supply pushing up long yields).
● Stronger domestic demand (pensions, insurers) ⇒ higher prices, lower bond risk premium.
● Non-domestic demand (reserves, FX management) ⇒ inflows raise prices and compress the risk
premium; outflows reverse it.
● QE asset purchases ⇒ compress long yields by raising demand in targeted segments (preferred habitat).

✓  Example 11 — Rate view based on economic recovery
Setup: recession, low yields, ~flat curve. Firm forecasts rapid recovery + Fed reduces long-term asset
purchases.
Q1. Which curve change is consistent with recovery? → INCREASE in term spread (long vs short). Answer: C.
Growth → long rates rise; curve steepens.
Q2. Reduced long-term asset purchases: → AMPLIFIES the effect on term spread (Answer A). Less Fed
demand at long end = higher long yields; adds to growth-driven long-end sell-off.

⚠  Trap 11 — Bullish vs Bearish Steepening / Flattening
"BULL" means LONG-bond PRICES rise (yields fall); "BEAR" means long-bond PRICES fall (yields rise). • BULL
STEEPENING = short yields fall MORE than long (curve steepens while bonds rally). • BEAR FLATTENING =
short yields rise MORE than long (curve flattens while bonds sell off). • BULL FLATTENING = long yields fall
more than short (flight to quality). • BEAR STEEPENING = long yields rise more than short (growth/inflation
surprise + supply). Memorize all four.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM1

⚠  T1. Spot vs forward direction
Upward spot curve ⇒ forward ABOVE spot. Downward spot ⇒ forward BELOW spot. Flat ⇒ equal. Memorize
as "marginal vs average."

⚠  T2. Bootstrapping uses PRIOR spots
When bootstrapping z₃, use z₁ AND z₂ to discount the coupons in years 1 and 2 — not z₃ for all CFs. If you see
z₃ used for every discount factor, that's a YTM calc, not bootstrapping.

⚠  T3. YTM ≠ expected return on a sloped curve
YTM equals expected return ONLY on a FLAT yield curve AND assuming coupons reinvest at YTM. Any slope or
volatility breaks it.


⚠  T4. YTM as weighted avg of spots
On upward slope: z₁ < y_T < z_T. On downward slope: z_T < y_T < z₁. The last cash flow dominates, so YTM
pulls toward z_T as maturity grows.

⚠  T5. Forwards capped at longest spot maturity
With a 30-year spot curve, the longest 1-period forward you can extract is f₂₉,₁. A 3-year-forward curve extends
only to f₃,₂₇.

⚠  T6. Forward contract price unchanged if forwards realized
A forward price changes only when realized spots DIVERGE from today's implied forwards. If future spot <
current forward ⇒ forward price RISES (buy forward).

⚠  T7. Rolldown requires LONGER maturity than horizon
Bond matched to horizon ⇒ matures at par, no rolldown gain. Only bonds longer than horizon, sold before
maturity, capture the rolldown capital gain.

⚠  T8. Rolldown assumes STATIC curve
Rolldown return evaporates if curve rises, flattens, or inverts. Rolldown trades are short vol in yield-curve
shape.

⚠  T9. Carry trade risk
Maturity spread carry trade (short-fund, long-invest) is very exposed to short-rate spikes (e.g., inflation
shocks). Profitable on steep curves, dangerous at curve turning points.

⚠  T10. Swaps, spots, DFs are interchangeable
Given ANY ONE of (swap rates, spot rates, discount factors), you can calculate the other two. Example 8
shows the explicit algebra.

⚠  T11. Swap rate = par rate (for fixed-for-float par swap)
A par swap is priced to zero value at origination. The swap rate solves the same equation as the par coupon
rate on a bond priced at par. In the bootstrap example, swap rates = par rates = 5, 5.97, 6.91, 7.81%.

⚠  T12. Swap spread CAN be negative

Post-2008: negative US 30-yr swap spreads are persistent. Capital requirements on swap dealers + duration
demand drove them below zero. Don't reflexively answer "positive."

⚠  T13. Swap spread = credit risk, not liquidity alone
Primary measure of the market's perceived credit risk of interbank lending vs. default-risk-free governments.
Widens countercyclically (bigger in recessions).

⚠  T14. I-spread vs Swap spread — direction of subtraction
Swap spread = Swap rate – Govt yield (same maturity). I-spread = Bond YTM – Swap rate (same maturity).
Different reference; the two terms are NOT synonyms.

⚠  T15. TED spread definition
TED = 3-month MRR (historically Libor) – 3-month T-bill yield. Rises = credit + liquidity stress. COVID-19 spike
(early 2020). TED acronym = "T"-bill + "ED" Eurodollar ticker.

⚠  T16. MRR-OIS measures counterparty risk
OIS floats on a GEOMETRIC AVERAGE of a DAILY UNSECURED OVERNIGHT rate. MRR-OIS spread widens
when banks don't trust each other to lend term-unsecured.

⚠  T17. SOFR is SECURED; Libor was UNSECURED
SOFR is collateralized by US Treasuries (from repo transactions) — transaction-based, not survey-based. This
means SOFR responds more to Treasury supply/demand than Libor did.

⚠  T18. Pure vs Local Expectations
PURE = equal expected returns across ALL maturities over ANY horizon (risk-neutral world). LOCAL = equal
expected returns over only SHORT horizons (no-arbitrage); allows long-horizon risk premia. Empirically, long-
bond short-run returns > short-bond short-run returns — refutes both pure AND local.

⚠  T19. Liquidity Preference: forwards UPWARD-biased
Liquidity preference ⇒ forward > expected spot (positive, non-decreasing term premium). Does NOT prohibit
downward-sloping curves — just says premia exist.

⚠  T20. Segmented Markets: pure habitat-bound

Yields determined independently across segments; no cross-maturity arbitrage. Fallen-angel sell pressure is a
classic segmented-markets result (HY-restricted funds must sell).

⚠  T21. Preferred Habitat ≠ Segmented Markets
Preferred habitat permits deviation IF yield gaps are large. QE works under preferred habitat (Fed buys long;
habitat investors push remaining supply higher in price).

⚠  T22. Level factor explains > 75% of curve moves
Level (parallel) shifts dominate — usually > 75% of variance. Steepness is secondary; curvature has the
smallest impact ("twist").

⚠  T23. Curvature = "twist" (short + long UP, mid DOWN)
Curvature factor has NEGATIVE impact on intermediate yields and POSITIVE impact on short & long yields (or
vice versa). It is the remaining independent movement after level and slope.

⚠  T24. Key rate duration ≠ effective duration
Effective duration = sensitivity to PARALLEL shift. Key rate duration = sensitivity to movement in a SINGLE key
rate. Sum of all KRDs = effective duration (because equal-sized shifts in each key rate = parallel shift).

⚠  T25. KRD for zero-coupon portfolio
Zero bond's effective duration = maturity. KRD at its own maturity = its weight × maturity / (total portfolio value
× shift size). For equal-weighted 1/5/10-zero portfolio: KDs are 0.333, 1.667, 3.333.

⚠  T26. Shape-factor durations vs key rate durations
Can be derived from each other: KeyDur_L = KD₁ + KD₅ + KD₁₀;  KeyDur_S = –KD₁ + KD₁₀;  KeyDur_C = KD₁ +
KD₁₀.  Shape-factor sensitivities and key rate sensitivities are linear transformations.

⚠  T27. Short-rate volatility > long-rate volatility (YIELDS)
Short rates more volatile in YIELD. BUT long bonds more volatile in PRICE because of duration. Different
metrics — different rankings.

⚠  T28. Volatility annualization
Annual σ = monthly σ × √12 (or monthly σ / √(1/12)). Example: 10.15% monthly → 35.15% annual.


⚠  T29. Short-term vol driver vs long-term vol driver
Short-term rate vol ≈ monetary policy uncertainty. Long-term rate vol ≈ real-economy + inflation uncertainty.
Correlations between short and long vols vary over time.

⚠  T30. Bond risk premium is forward-looking
Not the historical excess return; it's the EXPECTED excess return of long over short bonds. Must be estimated
(via models / surveys / implied forwards).

⚠  T31. Yield-variance drivers differ by maturity
Short-term yields: ~2/3 monetary policy, ~1/3 growth+inflation. Long-term yields: ~2/3 inflation, ~1/3
monetary policy. The driver mix shifts as you move along the curve.

⚠  T32. Bear flattening = expansion tightening
Tightening cycles raise short rates more than long rates. Curve flattens as bond PRICES fall (bear). Bull
steepening is the opposite — easing cycle, long rates fall less.

⚠  T33. Bull flattening = flight to quality
In a flight to quality, duration demand compresses long yields; short rates are anchored by policy expectations.
Long falls more than short ⇒ curve flattens while bonds rally (bull).

⚠  T34. Bullet vs Barbell for curvature
Barbell = short + long at similar duration to bullet. Barbell OUTPERFORMS bullet when curvature increases
(mid-yield rises more than short/long). Bullet wins when curvature decreases.

⚠  T35. Bond risk premium vs other premia & swap curve coverage
Bond risk premium = term (duration) premium — uses DEFAULT-FREE bonds. Credit/liquidity premia are
ADDED for risky bonds. Swap markets quote reliable rates across many maturities (up to 50 yrs USD/EUR) vs.
limited gov't issuance, and are less country-specific.

⚠  T36. QE pushes down long-term yields
Intended effect: increase money supply, lower long-term rates, drive capital to higher-risk assets. Mechanism
works via preferred habitat — Fed buying long reduces supply and pushes down long-end yields.


⚠  T37. Fiscal supply effect
Larger deficits ⇒ more government bond supply ⇒ higher yields. In late-1990s US, anticipated SURPLUSES →
Treasury suspended 30-year issuance for 4 years → long yields fell.

⚠  T38. Non-domestic vs domestic demand
Both domestic (pensions, insurers) and non-domestic (FX management, reserves) flows influence prices.
Domestic demand is generally more stable; non-domestic flows can be volatile and cause larger risk-premium
moves.

⚠  T39. Duration-neutral curve trades
Steepener/flattener curve trades are often duration-neutral — match nominal duration across legs so you
isolate the SLOPE move from level moves.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM1

Spot, Forward & Discount Factors
Core
DF_N = 1 / (1 + z_N)^N            ⇔    z_N = (1/DF_N)^(1/N) – 1
F(A, B–A) = DF_B / DF_A
Forward rate model:   (1 + z_B)^B = (1 + z_A)^A × (1 + f(A,B–A))^(B–A)
Geometric mean:   (1 + z_T)^T = (1 + z_1) Π_{k=1..T–1}(1 + f_{k,1})
Bootstrap:  solve successive price equations  1 = Σ c/(1+z_t)^t + (1+c)/(1+z_T)^T  for z_T given prior spots.

YTM & Expected Return
YTM facts
Price = Σ CF_t / (1+z_t)^t  =  Σ CF_t / (1+y_T)^t   ⇒  y_T = weighted avg of z_t with CFs as weights.
Upward slope: z_1 < y_T < z_T;  Flat: y = z = f;  Downward slope: z_T < y_T < z_1.
YTM = expected return only on a FLAT curve AND reinvestment at YTM.
Expected return assuming forwards are realized = final wealth compounded at forwards, divided by price,
annualized.

Swap Rates

Par swap
Σ s_T × DF_t + DF_T = 1           (swap value zero at origination)
Swap spread_T = s_T – on-the-run govt yield_T (same maturity).
I-spread = bond YTM – interpolated swap rate at bond maturity.
Z-spread = constant spread added to benchmark spot curve that equates discounted CFs to price.

Key Rate & Shape-Factor Duration
KRD / shape
%ΔP ≈ – Σ_k KeyDur_k × Δz_k            Eff Dur = Σ_k KeyDur_k
KeyDur_L = KD_1 + KD_5 + KD_10   (level / parallel)
KeyDur_S = –KD_1 + KD_10         (steepness / slope)
KeyDur_C = KD_1 + KD_10          (curvature)
%ΔP ≈ – KeyDur_L·Δx_L – KeyDur_S·Δx_S – KeyDur_C·Δx_C

Yield Volatility
Vol
σ(t,T) = σ[Δr(t,T)/r(t,T)] / √Δt
Annual σ = monthly σ × √12.         Example: 10.15% monthly ⇒ 35.15% annual.
Empirical pattern: downward-sloping term structure of σ (short > long).
Short-rate vol → monetary policy.  Long-rate vol → real economy + inflation.

Term-Structure Theories — Quick Map
One-liner summaries
Pure (Unbiased) Expectations: forwards = unbiased predictor; risk-neutral; all maturities perfect substitutes.
Local Expectations: short-horizon returns = risk-free rate (no-arbitrage); long-horizon risk premia allowed.
Liquidity Preference: forwards upward-biased; non-decreasing term premium; typically upward-sloping
curves.
Segmented Markets: yields set by segment-specific S/D (fallen-angel effect).   Preferred Habitat: segmented
but flexible; QE mechanism.

Macro → Rate View — Cheat Sheet
Quick mapping

Expansion+hikes → BEAR FLATTEN.  Recession+cuts → BULL STEEPEN.  FTQ → BULL FLATTEN.
Growth+supply → BEAR STEEPEN.
Rates ↓ → extend dur; ↑ → shorten.  Steeper → short long-dur + long short-dur; flatter = reverse.  Bullish flatten →
BULLET→BARBELL.

— End of Notes — LM1: The Term Structure and Interest Rate Dynamics —

heading: The Law of One Price & Arbitrage
body:
The central idea of financial economics: market prices adjust until there are no opportunities for arbitrage — "free
money." The LAW OF ONE PRICE states that two goods which are perfect substitutes must sell for the same
current price (ignoring transaction costs). Otherwise, an investor would sell the overvalued asset and buy the
undervalued one simultaneously, taking riskless profit until prices converge.

Arbitrage-Free Valuation — Principle
Arbitrage opportunity: a transaction requiring NO cash outlay that yields a RISKLESS profit.
Core implication: If you put up no money and take no risk, your expected return must be zero.
A bond is a PORTFOLIO of zero-coupon bonds. Value additivity: the value of the whole equals the sum of the
values of the zero-coupon parts.
Discount each cash flow at the spot rate for its maturity. Discounting all cash flows at a single YTM gives only
an approximation.

heading: Two Types of Arbitrage Opportunity
body:
Type
Mechanism
Example (Exhibit 1)
Value additivity
Value of whole ≠ sum of parts. Arbitrageur
sells the parts and buys the whole (or vice
versa).
Asset A zero pays $1 in 1y priced

buy B for 97 → $3 riskless.
Dominance
A risk-free payoff in the future must have a
POSITIVE price today. If one asset
"dominates" another (same risk, lower
yield), sell the expensive and buy the
cheap.
Asset C pays 105 in 1y priced 100 (5%).
Asset D pays 220 in 1y priced 200 (10%).
Sell 2×C for 200, buy 1×D for 200 → net 0
today, +10 in 1y.

✓  Example 1 — Arbitrage opportunity check
Bond A: 3% annual 10-yr bond, yield 2.5% in NY; same bond sells $104.376/$100 in Chicago.  Check: 3/1.025
+ 3/1.025² + … + 103/1.025¹⁰ = $104.376. Price matches — NO arbitrage.
Bond B: 3% annual 10-yr bond, yield 3.2% in HK; same sells RMB97.220/RMB100 in Shanghai.  Arbitrage-free
price: 3/1.032 + … + 103/1.032¹⁰ = 98.311. Shanghai < HK price → BUY Shanghai at 97.220, SELL HK at 98.311
→ profit RMB1.091 per RMB100 traded.

heading: Stripping & Reconstitution
body:
US Treasury dealers can physically SEPARATE a coupon bond into its constituent zero-coupon securities (each
coupon + final principal becomes a distinct zero) — this is STRIPPING. They can also REPLICATE a coupon bond
by combining zeros — this is RECONSTITUTION. If the arbitrage-free price (sum of PVs of zeros) differs from the
bond's market price, dealers strip/reconstitute until equality is restored. Sovereign dealers worldwide operate
similarly.

heading: Three Steps of Valuation
body:
● Step 1 — Estimate the future cash flows.
● Step 2 — Determine the appropriate discount rate(s) — for arbitrage-free valuation, spot rates for each
CF's maturity.

● Step 3 — Calculate PV of each cash flow using the appropriate discount rate and sum them.

✓  Example 2 — Arbitrage-free value of a 5% 3-yr bond (par rates 2%, 3%, 4%)
Bootstrap spot rates:  z₁ = 2% (= 1-yr par rate).
Solve z₂:  1 = 0.03/1.02 + 1.03/(1+z₂)²  ⇒  z₂ = 3.015%.
Solve z₃:  1 = 0.04/1.02 + 0.04/1.03015² + 1.04/(1+z₃)³  ⇒  z₃ = 4.055%.
Arbitrage-free price:  P₀ = 5/1.02 + 5/1.03015² + 105/1.04055³ = 102.8102.
If the bond is offered at 102.7751 (priced at YTM 4%), it is mispriced by 0.0351/100 of par.
Takeaway: discounting at YTM over-discounts early coupons on upward-sloping curves (and under-discounts
on downward-sloping). Always use spot rates for arbitrage-free pricing.

⚠  Trap 1 — YTM discounting ≠ arbitrage-free valuation
Discounting ALL cash flows at a single YTM produces a value close to but NOT equal to the arbitrage-free value
— except when the yield curve is flat. To be arbitrage free, each cash flow must be discounted at the spot rate
of its own maturity. Do NOT use the bond's YTM on exam arbitrage-free questions.

⚠  Trap 2 — Equal-risk cash flows must use equal discount rates
Two cash flows with identical risks delivered at the same time are discounted with the same rate EVEN IF they
belong to different bonds. This is the mechanical basis of stripping/reconstitution: a 3-year zero from any AAA
issuer should yield the same 3-year spot rate as any other.

section_title: THE BINOMIAL INTEREST RATE TREE FRAMEWORK
heading: The Lognormal Random Walk
body:
CFA candidates model rates using a LOGNORMAL process. Two properties of lognormal that matter for the exam:
● Non-negativity — interest rates CANNOT go negative because as rates approach zero, the absolute
change shrinks (changes are proportional to the level).
● Higher volatility at higher rates — the absolute change Δr grows with r; specifically, stdev of 1-year rate =
i₀·σ. E.g., σ=10%, i₀=2% → stdev = 20 bps.


Lognormal tree spacing
At any pair of adjacent nodes:   i_H = i_L × e^(2σ)
At any Time-2 triple:   i_{HH} = i_{LL} × e^(4σ)   and   i_{HL} = i_{LL} × e^(2σ)
At any Time-3 quadruple:   i_{LLH} = i_{LLL}·e^(2σ);  i_{HHL} = i_{LLL}·e^(4σ);  i_{HHH} = i_{LLL}·e^(6σ)
Nodes are "(nearly) centered" on the implied forward rate; the geometric spread is e^(2σ) between adjacent
rates.
Example (σ = 15%):   i_{1,L} = 1.194% ⇒ i_{1,H} = 1.194% × e^(2×0.15) = 1.612%.
Recombining tree: HL = LH — there are only n+1 nodes at time n, not 2^n.

section_title: THE BINOMIAL INTEREST RATE TREE FRAMEWORK
heading: Backward Induction — Value at a Node
body:
Fill in the tree RIGHT-TO-LEFT. At maturity each bond redeems at par (plus final coupon). Work back one period at
a time. At every interior node, the bond value equals the present value of (coupon + probability-weighted expected
next-period value). Equal probabilities 0.5/0.5 apply because the lognormal model assumes equal up/down
probabilities at each node.
Node Value Formula
Value at node = [C + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L] / (1 + i)
C = coupon paid at end of period,  V_H = bond value at upper child node, V_L = value at lower child node, i =
one-year forward rate AT THE NODE.
Apply iteratively from Time-N (par + final coupon) back to Time-0 (today's price).

✓  Example 3 — Backward induction through a 3-yr tree (5% coupon, par 100)
Tree: Time-0 = 2.0%;  Time-1 = 5.0% / 3.0%;  Time-2 = 8.0% / 6.0% / 4.0%.
Time-2 discounted terminal values (each receives 105 = 100 + 5):
    105/1.08 = 97.2222;  105/1.06 = 99.0566;  105/1.04 = 100.9615.
Time-1 upper (5%):  V = [5 + 0.5(97.2222) + 0.5(99.0566)] / 1.05 = 98.2280.
Time-1 lower (3%):  V = [5 + 0.5(99.0566) + 0.5(100.9615)] / 1.03 = 101.9506.
Time-0 (2%):  V = [5 + 0.5(98.2280) + 0.5(101.9506)] / 1.02 = 103.0287.

⚠  Trap 3 — Coupon is paid at the END of the period, discounted at the NODE rate
The rate AT a node (e.g., 5% at the 5% Time-1 node) is used to discount cash flows from the end of the NEXT
period back to that node. The coupon for the period just ending is ALREADY included in the value propagated
from the child nodes. Always place the coupon directly to the right of the starting node.

heading: Calibration Procedure
body:
● Fix i₀ = the known 1-year spot rate.
● For Time-1: pick a trial i₁,L. Derive i₁,H = i₁,L · e^(2σ). Price the 2-year benchmark bond via backward
induction. Iterate (Solver) until price = 100.
● For Time-2: pick a trial i₂,HL (a good start is the implied 2y-forward from the spot curve). Derive i₂,HH =
i₂,HL · e^(2σ) and i₂,LL = i₂,HL / e^(2σ). Price the 3-year benchmark bond using the ALREADY-
CALIBRATED Time-1 rates. Iterate until price = 100.
● Continue for each additional maturity — each step ONLY varies the middle rate at that level; prior
calibrated rates are held fixed.

✓  Worked calibration — σ = 15%, benchmark curves from §3.1
Time-0: i₀ = 1.00%.
Time-1 calibration (2-yr 1.20% bond at par):
   Final calibrated:  i₁,L = 1.1943%,  i₁,H = 1.6121% (= 1.1943% × e^0.30).
   V_H = 101.20/1.016121 = 99.5944;  V_L = 101.20/1.011943 = 100.0056.
   V₀ = [1.20 + 0.5(99.5944) + 0.5(100.0056)] / 1.01 = 100.0000  ✓.
   Note average of (1.6121, 1.1943) = 1.4032% — slightly ABOVE implied forward 1.4028% due to lognormal
skew.
Time-2 calibration (3-yr 1.25% bond at par):
   Calibrated: i₂,LL = 0.9803%, i₂,HL = 1.3233%, i₂,HH = 1.7863%.
Time-3 calibration (4-yr 1.40% bond at par):

   Calibrated: i₃,LLL = 1.1521%, i₃,LLH = 1.5552%, i₃,HHL = 2.0994%, i₃,HHH = 2.8338%.

   V₀ = [3 + 0.5(98.427 + 99.573)] / 1.02 = 100.000  ✓.

⚠  Trap 4 — Prior calibration is LOCKED when advancing
When calibrating Time-2, you do NOT re-solve for Time-1 rates — those were fixed by the 2-year bond. You
only vary the middle Time-2 rate. Each maturity uses all previously calibrated rates as fixed parameters.
Changing Time-2 rates does not alter the 2-yr bond price because the 2-yr bond only uses Time-0 and Time-1
discount rates.

⚠  Trap 5 — Average of the H and L rates ≠ the implied forward

Because of lognormal skew, (i_H + i_L)/2 is slightly HIGHER than the arithmetic implied forward rate. In the
example, average of {1.6121%, 1.1943%} = 1.4032% vs. implied forward = 1.4028%. This small gap grows as
σ grows. The tree is "centered" on the forward curve in GEOMETRIC (log-mean) sense, not arithmetic.

section_title: VALUING OPTION-FREE BONDS WITH THE BINOMIAL TREE
heading: %/1.3233%/1.7863%, Time-3: 1.1521%/1.5552%/2.0994%/2.8338%) → V₀ = 102.3254.
body:
Match confirms the tree is arbitrage free.

✓  Example 5 — 3-yr 5% option-free bond using calibrated tree from Example 4
Tree (σ=15%): Time-0 = 2%, Time-1 = 3.442%/4.646%, Time-2 = 4.482%/6.050%/8.167%.
Time-2 terminal PVs: 105/1.08167 = 97.0721;  105/1.06050 = 99.0099;  105/1.04482 = 100.4958.
Time-1: [5 + 0.5(97.0721+99.0099)]/1.04646 = 98.4663;  [5 + 0.5(99.0099+100.4958)]/1.03442 = 101.2672.
Time-0: [5 + 0.5(98.4663+101.2672)]/1.02 = 102.8105.
Matches the spot-discounted price from Example 2 (102.8102; small rounding difference). The tree is arbitrage
free.

⚠  Trap 6 — Both methods MUST match for option-free bonds
A calibrated tree that fails to reprice an option-free bond to the spot-rate value has a calibration error (often the
lognormal spacing or a trial rate). For option-free bonds, the tree provides NO additional insight beyond the
spot curve — its value appears only when valuing bonds whose CASH FLOWS DEPEND on future rates
(callable, putable, MBS, etc.).

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Pathwise Valuation
body:
PATHWISE valuation is an alternative to backward induction that produces the SAME bond value. Steps: (1) list all
interest-rate paths through the tree, (2) compute the PV of each path's cash flows, (3) average the PVs. For a

recombining binomial tree with N steps, the number of paths equals 2^N but collapses to N+1 terminal nodes —
PASCAL'S TRIANGLE gives the number of paths arriving at each node (1, N, N(N-1)/2, …).
Pathwise Mechanics
Step 1 — Enumerate paths. For N time steps, 2^N paths ordered by {H/L} sequence.
Step 2 — For each path, discount all cash flows CF_t at the sequence of forward rates along that path:  PV = Σ
CF_t / Π(1 + i_path,s) for s=0..t-1.
Step 3 — Average across all 2^N paths → this is the bond's value.
Pascal's Triangle rows give # paths to each terminal node:  {1}, {1,1}, {1,2,1}, {1,3,3,1}, {1,4,6,4,1} for N =
0,1,2,3,4.

✓  Example 6 — Pathwise valuation of 3-yr 5% bond using the Example 4 tree
4 paths (HH, HL, LH, LL) — all paths start at 2.000%:
Path 1 (HH):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.04646) + 105/(1.02·1.04646·1.08167) = 100.5298.
Path 2 (HL):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.04646) + 105/(1.02·1.04646·1.06050) = 102.3452.
Path 3 (LH):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.03442) + 105/(1.02·1.03442·1.06050) = 103.4794.
Path 4 (LL):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.03442) + 105/(1.02·1.03442·1.04482) = 104.8877.
Average = (100.5298 + 102.3452 + 103.4794 + 104.8877) / 4 = 102.8105.
Matches backward-induction result from Example 5. ✓

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: The Monte Carlo Method
body:
Monte Carlo randomly samples a large number of interest-rate paths rather than enumerating all paths (which is
infeasible for long-horizon securities like 30-year MBS). It is the standard approach for PATH-DEPENDENT
securities — where cash flows depend on the ROUTE rates took to reach a given level, not just the current level.
The classic application is mortgage-backed securities, where borrower prepayments depend on the rate path.

Monte Carlo Five-Step Procedure (30-yr monthly MBS example)
Step 1 — Simulate 500+ paths of 1-month rates under an interest-rate model (lognormal / normal) and a
volatility assumption.
Step 2 — Generate spot rates from simulated 1-month rates.
Step 3 — Determine the path-dependent cash flow along each path (e.g., prepayment-adjusted MBS flows).
Step 4 — Compute PV along each path.
Step 5 — Average the PVs across all paths → bond value.

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Drift Adjustment
body:
Raw simulations will NOT reproduce market prices of benchmark bonds by chance. A DRIFT TERM — a constant
added to every simulated short rate on every path — is chosen such that the average simulated PV of each

benchmark bond equals its market price. A model calibrated this way is said to be DRIFT ADJUSTED and is
ARBITRAGE FREE. Without drift adjustment, the model is not market-consistent.

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Mean Reversion in Monte Carlo
body:
Empirically rates don't wander indefinitely — they tend to mean-revert to economically reasonable levels.
Modelers implement UPPER and LOWER BOUNDS on the random process or build mean reversion explicitly into
the stochastic process (moving generated rates toward implied forwards). Mean reversion improves long-horizon
simulation realism.

✓  Example 7 — Monte Carlo for 3-yr 5% bond using 8 random paths
Each of 8 random paths has Time-0 = 2.000% but random Time-1 and Time-2 rates.
Path PVs: 105.7459, 103.2708, 100.9104, 103.8543, 101.9075, 102.4236, 103.3020, 101.0680.
Average = 102.8103.
Matches the backward-induction and pathwise results (102.8105). Monte Carlo is calibrated correctly.
For option-free bonds, Monte Carlo is redundant. Its value appears for PATH-DEPENDENT bonds (MBS with
prepayments) where backward induction fails.

⚠  Trap 7 — More paths ≠ more ACCURACY about true value
Increasing Monte Carlo paths from 500 to 5,000 reduces SAMPLING error but does not reduce MODEL error. If
the underlying interest-rate model is misspecified or inputs are wrong, more paths just deliver a more precise
answer to the wrong question. Monte Carlo is "only as good as the valuation model and the accuracy of the
inputs."

⚠  Trap 8 — Backward induction FAILS for path-dependent securities
Backward induction requires cash flows at each node to depend ONLY on the current node (and possibly
current level of rates), not on HOW rates arrived there. Prepayment-adjusted MBS violates this: a borrower's
refinance decision depends on whether they previously had lower rates. Use MONTE CARLO, not backward
induction, for MBS and other path-dependent instruments.

heading: The Four Foundational Models
body:
Stochastic Processes — The "Big Four"
CIR (Cox–Ingersoll–Ross, equilibrium):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t) dt + σ√r_t · dZ
Vasicek (equilibrium):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t) dt + σ · dZ
Ho–Lee (arbitrage-free):   dr_t = θ_t · dt + σ · dZ
Kalotay–Williams–Fabozzi / KWF (arbitrage-free):   d ln(r_t) = θ_t · dt + σ · dZ
k = speed of mean reversion; θ = long-run mean rate; σ = volatility; dZ = std normal random.
