heading: Deep Dive on Each Model
body:
CIR — mean reverts to θ. The volatility term σ√r means volatility shrinks as rates approach zero, preventing
negative rates — a nice economic property. Derived in a general equilibrium model of investor
consumption/investment.

Vasicek — also mean reverts. But volatility is CONSTANT (σ, not σ√r), which means rates CAN theoretically go
negative. Not derived from general equilibrium but treated as equilibrium.
Ho-Lee — the first arbitrage-free model. Drift θ_t varies with time (so it can match any initial term structure
exactly). NO mean reversion. Constant vol → rates CAN go negative. Implemented in a binomial lattice.
KWF — same structure as Ho-Lee but models d ln(r), i.e., rates are LOGNORMAL. Prevents negative rates.
Lognormal rate distribution changes option-pricing tails.

heading: Modern & Multi-Factor Models
body:
MODERN interest-rate models extend the one-factor framework in two directions: (1) multi-factor models
incorporating slope/curvature factors, (2) sophisticated approaches combining observed forward curves with
implied volatilities from option markets.

Gauss+ — A multi-factor example
Three rates modeled: short-, medium-, and long-term rates.
Long-term rate: MEAN REVERTING to a long-run level reflecting macroeconomic trends.
Medium-term rate: mean reverts to the LONG-RUN rate.
Short-term rate: has NO random component — central banks control the short end.
Result: hump-shaped volatility term structure, with MEDIUM-TERM rates being the most volatile.

✓  Example 8 — Which model fits the current term structure most accurately?
Answer: Arbitrage-free models (Ho-Lee, KWF, BDT) match market prices by construction. Equilibrium models
(CIR, Vasicek) fit only by chance.
Observations empirically show parallel shifts explain >90% of yield changes, supporting single-factor models
for most applications.
Why equilibrium models persist: they describe many possible future paths from a fundamental framework,
useful for long-horizon scenario/stress analysis.

⚠  Trap 9 — "Arbitrage-free" = matches TODAY'S market prices
Arbitrage-free models are specifically PARAMETERIZED so the simulated / tree-based values of benchmark
bonds equal their observed market prices. Equilibrium models do NOT have this property — their parameters
come from economic theory, and market prices are an OUTPUT not an INPUT. On exam: "model matches
market" or "fits current term structure" → arbitrage-free.

⚠  Trap 10 — Negative-rate allowance per model

CIR and KWF: rates CANNOT go negative (CIR: σ·√r vanishes at r=0; KWF: log-normal).  Vasicek and Ho-Lee:
rates CAN go negative (constant σ with normal distribution).  On exam: "which model prohibits negative
rates?" → CIR or KWF.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM2

⚠  T1. Arbitrage requires no net outlay AND no risk
An arbitrage opportunity is a transaction with ZERO cash outlay that earns a RISKLESS profit. If either the
outlay or risk is positive, it is not arbitrage — it is just investing. "No arbitrage" is enforced by the law of one
price.

⚠  T2. Value additivity vs. dominance
Value additivity: whole ≠ sum of parts (e.g., portfolio B priced at 97 vs 105 zeros priced at 100). Dominance:
risk-free payoff must be positive (e.g., Asset D pays 220 for 200 while C pays 105 for 100). Both are arbitrage
types — know the distinction.

⚠  T3. YTM ≠ arbitrage-free
Discounting all CFs at a single YTM is only an APPROXIMATION. Arbitrage-free valuation uses SPOT rates for
each CF's maturity. Discounting at YTM over-discounts early coupons on upward curves and under-discounts
on downward curves.

⚠  T4. Bond = portfolio of zeros
The most important conceptual frame: a 5-yr semi-annual coupon bond = 11 zero-coupon cash flows (10
semiannual coupons + 1 principal at maturity). Stripping lets dealers physically separate them; reconstitution
reassembles them. Arbitrage forces the price of the bond = sum of strip prices.

⚠  T5. Stripping prevents arbitrage
If a bond trades BELOW its stripped value, dealers buy the bond, strip it, and sell the strips. If it trades ABOVE,
dealers reconstitute. This pressure enforces value additivity in Treasury markets globally.

⚠  T6. Lognormal guarantees two nice properties
A lognormal interest-rate model gives: (1) NEGATIVE rates are impossible (as r → 0, so does Δr in absolute
terms); (2) volatility RISES with rate level (stdev of 1-yr rate = i₀·σ). Model is the default for CFA-style binomial
trees.


⚠  T7. Stdev of one-year rate = i₀·σ
In a lognormal world: if σ = 10% (volatility parameter) and i₀ = 2%, the stdev of the 1-year rate is 2% × 10% = 20
bps. Rate moves are proportional to the LEVEL of rates — higher rates, bigger moves.

⚠  T8. Adjacent tree nodes differ by e^(2σ)
The spacing multiplier between adjacent 1-year forwards at the same time step is e^(2σ), NOT e^σ. For
σ=15% that's e^0.30 = 1.3499, so i_H is about 35% higher than i_L. Memorize the "2σ" factor.

⚠  T9. Recombining tree — only n+1 nodes at time n
Because HL = LH (order doesn't matter at recombining nodes), at Time N there are only N+1 distinct nodes, not
2^N. A 30-year tree has only 31 rates at maturity, not ~10⁹. Critical for computational feasibility.

⚠  T10. At a lognormal tree node, Pr(up) = Pr(down) = 0.5
The lognormal model uses EQUAL probabilities at each node. That is why the node value formula uses 0.5/0.5
weighting. Don't use other probabilities.

⚠  T11. Backward-induction value formula
At any interior node: V = [C + 0.5(V_H + V_L)] / (1 + i). C = coupon paid at END of period; i = the 1-year forward
rate AT THAT NODE; V_H, V_L = bond values at next period up/down children.

⚠  T12. Work backward, right to left
Start at maturity (value = par + final coupon); work back one period at a time. You cannot value an interior node
until BOTH child nodes are valued. This is the essential asymmetry of backward induction.

⚠  T13. Coupon placement at nodes
Coupon C at Time T+1 is placed directly to the right of the Time T node. The value AT Time T includes the PV of
that coupon plus the expected PV of the bond at T+1. Misplacing the coupon by one period is a common error.

⚠  T14. Calibration lock-in
Time-1 rates are fixed by the 2-year bond and remain LOCKED when solving for Time-2. Time-2 solution only
varies the MIDDLE Time-2 rate; upper and lower follow from e^(2σ) spacing. Each successive maturity only
solves for one more parameter.


⚠  T15. Average of H and L ≠ implied forward (lognormal skew)
Lognormal rate distribution is right-skewed, so arithmetic mean of {i_L, i_H} slightly EXCEEDS the implied
forward rate — geometric mean matches implied forward. Example: (1.6121+1.1943)/2 = 1.4032 vs implied

⚠  T16. Low-σ tree collapses to forward curve
At σ = 0 (deterministic world): the tree IS the implied forward curve with zero dispersion. At σ=0.01% example:
tree rates are very close to 1.4028% at Time-1. As σ rises, the tree spreads out around the forwards.

⚠  T17. Arbitrage-free tree MUST reprice option-free bonds to spot-discounted value
If tree value ≠ spot-rate value for an option-free bond, the tree is miscalibrated. Option-free bonds are the
DIAGNOSTIC TEST for tree correctness.

⚠  T18. Calibrated tree = zero-coupon yield curve valuation
For option-free bonds, binomial tree and spot-rate discounting give identical values. The tree adds NO value for
option-free bonds — its purpose is to value option-embedded or path-dependent bonds.

⚠  T19. Pathwise and backward induction give identical values
For option-free bonds, averaging present values across all paths in the tree = backward-induction value.
Example 5 = Example 6 = 102.8105.

⚠  T20. Pascal's Triangle gives # paths to each terminal node
A 4-period tree has terminal path counts {1, 4, 6, 4, 1}. Total paths = 2^N = 16. The middle (HHLL mix) node has
the most paths leading to it; the extreme nodes have only one path each.

⚠  T21. Number of Paths = 2^N
For N time steps, there are exactly 2^N interest-rate paths through a binomial tree — regardless of
recombining. Pathwise valuation becomes computationally prohibitive above ~25 steps, motivating Monte
Carlo.

⚠  T22. Monte Carlo for PATH-DEPENDENT securities
Use Monte Carlo when cash flows depend on HOW rates arrived at the current level — e.g., MBS prepayments
(borrowers refinance when rates drop). Backward induction works only for state-dependent (not path-
dependent) CFs.


⚠  T23. Drift adjustment keeps Monte Carlo arbitrage-free
Raw Monte Carlo simulations will not match benchmark market prices. A DRIFT TERM — a constant added to
every simulated rate on every path — is chosen so average simulated PV of each benchmark = market price.
This makes the model arbitrage-free.

⚠  T24. Drift adjustment ≠ true rate forecast
Drift is a numerical correction to enforce arbitrage-free pricing. It is NOT an economic forecast of how rates
will evolve.

⚠  T25. More paths ≠ more accuracy of true value
Monte Carlo sampling error decreases as 1/√n. But if the underlying MODEL or INPUTS are wrong, more paths
just produce a more precise wrong answer. Don't conflate sampling precision with model accuracy.

⚠  T26. Mean reversion in Monte Carlo
Modelers impose bounds or drift adjustments that pull rates back toward implied forwards or a long-run mean.
Without mean reversion, simulated paths can drift arbitrarily far over long horizons (e.g., 30 years for MBS).

⚠  T27. CIR vs Vasicek: volatility behavior
Both have mean reversion drift k(θ−r). Difference: CIR has σ·√r (volatility shrinks at low rates) vs. Vasicek has
constant σ. Consequence: CIR rates cannot go negative; Vasicek rates can.

⚠  T28. Ho-Lee vs KWF: log vs level
Ho-Lee models dr (the rate CHANGE) — rates normal, can go negative. KWF models dln(r) — rates
LOGNORMAL, cannot go negative. Both are arbitrage-free with time-dependent drift θ_t.

⚠  T29. Equilibrium models don't fit market
CIR and Vasicek have few parameters (k, θ, σ) and cannot be tuned to match many term-structure points
exactly. Arbitrage-free models use time-varying drift θ_t, which gives as many parameters as maturity buckets,
allowing exact fit.

⚠  T30. Arbitrage-free = risk-neutral probabilities
Arbitrage-free models use RISK-NEUTRAL (Q-measure) probabilities — under which discounted prices are
martingales. Equilibrium models use REAL-world probabilities. Same underlying math, different interpretation.


⚠  T31. One-factor vs multi-factor models
One-factor: only the short rate drives term structure (all rates move same direction in any period, different
magnitudes). Multi-factor: slope and curvature added. Empirically ~90% of yield changes are parallel, so one-
factor works well for most apps.

⚠  T32. Gauss+ has central-bank-controlled short end
Gauss+ models the short rate with NO random component — reflecting central bank rate control. Medium rate
mean reverts to long. Long rate mean reverts to macro trend. Produces hump-shaped vol term structure (mid-
tenor most volatile).

⚠  T33. Implied volatility sources
Rate volatility can be estimated two ways: (a) HISTORICAL — stdev of past rate moves; (b) IMPLIED —
extracted from observed market prices of interest-rate derivatives (swaptions, caps, floors). Forward-looking,
derivative-based implied vol is often preferred.

⚠  T34. No-arbitrage calibration order
Always calibrate SHORTEST maturity first, then work outward. Each step uses all previously calibrated rates
as FIXED inputs. Never re-solve earlier rates when extending the tree.

⚠  T35. Tree spacing at Time-2 and Time-3
Three Time-2 rates: i_{LL}, i_{HL} = i_{LL}·e^(2σ), i_{HH} = i_{LL}·e^(4σ). Four Time-3 rates: i_{LLL}, i_{LLH} =
i_{LLL}·e^(2σ), i_{HHL} = i_{LLL}·e^(4σ), i_{HHH} = i_{LLL}·e^(6σ). Spacing geometric across full column.

⚠  T36. Path present value = discount by PATH, not AVERAGE rate
For each path, compound the specific forward rates along that path: PV = CF_t / Π_{s=0..t-1}(1+i_path,s). Do
NOT average the rates along the path and discount once.

⚠  T37. Arbitrage-free for STATIC pricing; equilibrium for DYNAMIC analysis
Arbitrage-free models are best at static snapshot pricing and hedging (derivatives desk use). Equilibrium
models excel at scenario/stress analysis over long horizons because they capture fundamental dynamics, not
just current snapshot.

⚠  T38. Benchmark bonds assumed correctly priced

In building an arbitrage-free tree, we assume benchmark bond prices are CORRECT — the model is built to
reproduce them exactly. If benchmarks are mispriced, the entire calibrated tree inherits those errors.

⚠  T39. Path enumeration uses {H, L} labels
Standard notation for path listing: each position is H (up) or L (down). For 3 steps, 8 paths: HHH, HHL, HLH,
HLL, LHH, LHL, LLH, LLL. Recombining means HHL = HLH = LHH (same terminal node), but pathwise valuation
treats them as distinct paths.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM2

Arbitrage-Free Pricing of an Option-Free Bond
Core
Price = Σ CF_t / (1 + z_t)^t    (discount each CF at its own spot rate)
Bootstrap: 1 = c/(1+z_1) + c/(1+z_2)^2 + ... + (1+c)/(1+z_T)^T  solve for z_T
YTM discounting gives ≠ arbitrage-free value unless yield curve is flat.
Arbitrage types: value additivity (whole ≠ sum of parts); dominance (risk-free payoff must have positive price).

Binomial Tree Mechanics
Spacing & Node Value
Adjacent node spacing:   i_H = i_L × e^(2σ).
Stdev of 1-yr rate (lognormal):   σ_r = i_0 × σ.
Value at a node:   V = [C + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L] / (1 + i).
Equal 0.5/0.5 probabilities up/down in the lognormal model.
Terminal: V = par + final coupon.  Work right-to-left.

Calibration Recipe
Procedure
Fix i_0 to 1-yr spot.
For each next maturity N: pick trial middle rate at Time N-1; derive all other rates at that column using e^(2σ)
spacing; price the N-yr benchmark bond at par via backward induction; iterate middle rate (Solver) until price =
100.
Previously calibrated rates are LOCKED — only the new column's middle rate varies.

At σ = 0, tree collapses to the implied forward curve.
Avg of (i_H, i_L) slightly EXCEEDS implied forward (lognormal skew).

Pathwise & Monte Carlo
Formulas
Pathwise:  Bond value = (1/2^N) × Σ_paths PV_path,   where PV_path = Σ_t CF_t / Π_{s=0..t-1}(1 + i_path,s).
Pascal's Triangle row N gives # paths to each terminal node: {1, C(N,1), C(N,2), …, 1}. Total paths = 2^N.
Monte Carlo: simulate → generate spots → compute path CFs → average PVs across all paths.
Drift adjustment: add constant to every rate on every path so average simulated PV of each benchmark =
market price (makes MC arbitrage-free).
Use Monte Carlo for PATH-DEPENDENT (MBS); backward induction for STATE-DEPENDENT (callable,
putable).

Term Structure Models
The Big Four Short-Rate Models
CIR (equil):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t)dt + σ√r_t · dZ     mean-revert; vol ~ √r; NO negative rates.
Vasicek (equil):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t)dt + σ · dZ     mean-revert; constant vol; CAN be negative.
Ho-Lee (arb-free):   dr_t = θ_t dt + σ dZ           time-dep drift; no mean-revert; CAN be negative.
KWF (arb-free):   d ln(r_t) = θ_t dt + σ dZ         time-dep drift; lognormal rates; NO negative.

Equilibrium vs Arbitrage-Free — Quick Discriminator
Decision rule
Must match current market prices? → ARBITRAGE-FREE (Ho-Lee, KWF).
Long-horizon scenarios with economic fundamentals? → EQUILIBRIUM (CIR, Vasicek).
Prevent negative rates? → CIR (equil) or KWF (arb-free).  Allow negatives? → Vasicek or Ho-Lee.
Real-world probs → equilibrium;  risk-neutral probs → arbitrage-free.
Gauss+ (multi-factor): short (no random, CB-controlled), mid (→long), long (→macro). Hump-shaped vol peaks
at mid.

— End of Notes — LM2: The Arbitrage-Free Valuation Framework —

section_title: EMBEDDED OPTIONS: TAXONOMY AND VALUE RELATIONSHIPS
heading: What Are Embedded Options?
body:
Embedded options are contingency provisions in a bond's indenture that give the issuer, the bondholder, or both
parties the right to change the bond's cash flows contingent on interest rate or other market conditions. They
CANNOT be traded separately — hence "embedded." Every bond with embedded options has an underlying
STRAIGHT BOND (option-free equivalent) with identical issuer, maturity, principal, coupon structure, and
currency.

body:

coupon); Floor is investor option.

section_title: EMBEDDED OPTIONS: TAXONOMY AND VALUE RELATIONSHIPS
heading: Exercise Styles
body:
● European — can exercise only ONCE on a single call/put date.
● American — continuously exercisable from first exercise date. Common for callable but NOT for putable
bonds.
● Bermudan — exercisable only on a predetermined SCHEDULE of specified dates. Most US gov-sponsored
enterprise callables are Bermudan.

section_title: EMBEDDED OPTIONS: TAXONOMY AND VALUE RELATIONSHIPS
heading: Core Value Relationships
body:
The Foundation Equations
Value of callable bond = Value of straight bond − Value of issuer call option.  (investor is SHORT the call)
⇒  Value of issuer call option = Straight bond value − Callable bond value.


Value of putable bond = Value of straight bond + Value of investor put option.  (investor is LONG the put)
⇒  Value of investor put option = Putable bond value − Straight bond value.

Intuition: Callable bonds are WORTH LESS than equivalent straight bonds. Putable bonds are WORTH MORE.

section_title: EMBEDDED OPTIONS: TAXONOMY AND VALUE RELATIONSHIPS
heading: Complex Embedded Options
body:
● Callable + putable — both parties can exercise.
● Sinking fund bond — requires issuer to retire portions over time. May also have acceleration provision and
delivery option (both issuer options).
● Estate put (survivor's option) — heir can put bond at par on bondholder's death.
● Contingent convertible (CoCo) — converts to equity or write-down if regulatory capital ratios breached.
Usually bank-issued.

body:

Call-option value at zero vol
Value of issuer call = 102.114 (straight) − 101.707 (callable) = 0.407.
Callable trades at a DISCOUNT to straight — investor pays for the expected downside exposure.

section_title: VALUATION AT ZERO INTEREST RATE VOLATILITY
heading: Putable Bond at Zero Volatility — Bermudan 3-yr 4.25% Putable at Par in Years 1, 2
body:
Investor puts when value of future flows < put price. At Year 2, value = 99.700 < 100 → INVESTOR PUTS, reset to
100. At Year 1: (4.25 + 100)/1.03518 = 100.707 > 100 — NOT put. Discount (4.25 + 100.707)/1.025 = 102.397. So
putable value = 102.397.
Put-option value at zero vol
Value of investor put = 102.397 (putable) − 102.114 (straight) = 0.283.
Putable trades at a PREMIUM to straight — investor is paid to hold the long-put protection.

⚠  Trap 1 — Exercise price floor on putable bonds (no-arbitrage argument)
A putable bond's market price cannot fall BELOW the put price in a working market. If it did, you could buy the
bond below put price and immediately put it back to the issuer at the put price — riskless arbitrage. For the
exam: bond currently putable at 100, market price 99 → immediate arbitrage.

⚠  Trap 2 — Optimal exercise for risk-neutral option holder
The curriculum assumes option holders are RISK NEUTRAL: they exercise if and only if the benefit from
exercise exceeds the EXPECTED benefit from waiting. In reality, risk-averse investors/issuers may exercise
EARLY even when holding the option is worth more ("alive"). For American/Bermudan options, the "act now vs
wait for better opportunity" trade-off drives the decision.

section_title: EFFECTS OF INTEREST RATE VOLATILITY, LEVEL, AND SHAPE
heading: Why Volatility Matters
body:
ALL EMBEDDED OPTIONS INCREASE IN VALUE WITH INTEREST RATE VOLATILITY — more paths through the
rate tree create more exercise opportunities. For a callable bond, higher option value means LOWER callable bond
value. For a putable bond, higher option value means HIGHER putable bond value.
Volatility impact — 30-yr 4.50% callable at par in 10 yr; flat 4% curve
At σ = 0% volatility:  call option value ≈ 4.60% of par.
At σ = 30% volatility: call option value ≈ 14.78% of par.
Callable bond value FALLS as σ rises (call value rises, subtracted from straight bond).


Volatility impact — 30-yr 3.75% putable at par in 10 yr; flat 4% curve
At σ = 0%:  put option value ≈ 2.30% of par.
At σ = 30%: put option value ≈ 10.54% of par.
Putable bond value RISES as σ rises (put value rises, added to straight bond).

heading: % of par (vs 92.27 straight → call ≈
body:

Interpretation
As rates FALL, call option moves ITM;
callable upside is CAPPED (110.43%
rises only 27% vs straight's 40%).
As rates RISE, put option moves ITM;
putable downside is LIMITED. Put
provides rate-rise hedge.

section_title: EFFECTS OF INTEREST RATE VOLATILITY, LEVEL, AND SHAPE
heading: Effect of Yield Curve Shape
body:
Call option value rises as curve FLATTENS/INVERTS
Upward-sloping 2→4% curve:  call value ≈ 8% of par.
Flat 4% curve:             call value ≈ 10% of par.
Downward-sloping 6→4%:     call value > 12% of par.
Intuition: steeper upward slope → higher forward rates far out → fewer call opportunities. Flattening lowers
future forwards → more call opportunities.

Put option value FALLS as curve flattens/inverts (opposite of call)
Upward-sloping 2→6%: put value is highest.
Flat 4%: put value is lower.
Downward-sloping 6→4%: put value is lowest.
Intuition: upward-sloping curve → higher future forwards → more put opportunities. Inversion kills put
opportunities.

⚠  Trap 3 — At-issuance moneyness of the embedded call
On a NORMAL upward-sloping curve, a callable bond issued AT PAR has a call option that is OUT OF THE
MONEY — it would not be called if arbitrage-free forwards prevailed. However, callable munis issued at a
LARGE PREMIUM (common in US muni market) have calls that are IN THE MONEY at issuance. This is an
exam subtlety on muni structures.

section_title: VALUATION WITH INTEREST RATE VOLATILITY (BINOMIAL TREE)
heading: Valuation Procedure
body:
● Step 1 — Generate binomial interest-rate tree consistent with given par curve and σ.
● Step 2 — At each node, determine whether the embedded option will be exercised (by comparing node
value to exercise price).
● Step 3 — Apply backward induction starting at maturity. At any node where the option is optimally
exercised, RESET the node value to the exercise price.

Binomial tree at σ = 10%, par curve 2.5%/3.0%/3.5%
Year 0 (root):  2.5000%
Year 1:         3.8695% (up)  /  3.1681% (down)
Year 2:         5.5258% (HH) / 4.5242% (HL) / 3.7041% (LL)
Tree spacing:  R_u = R_d · e^(2σ√t)  where σ = 10%, t = 1 yr.
Probabilities: 0.5 up / 0.5 down at each node.

✓  Callable at par Years 1, 2 — 3-yr 4.25% bond, σ = 10%
Year 2 values (= 104.25 / rate):
  HH: 104.25/1.055258 = 98.791 (< 100, NOT called)
  HL: 104.25/1.045242 = 99.738 (< 100, NOT called)
  LL: 104.25/1.037041 = 100.526 → CALL, reset to 100.
Year 1 — upper (3.8695%):  [4.25 + 0.5(98.791 + 99.738)]/1.038695 = 99.658.
Year 1 — lower (3.1681%):  [4.25 + 0.5(99.738 + 100)]/1.031681 = 100.922 → CALL, reset to 100.
Year 0:  [4.25 + 0.5(99.658 + 100)]/1.025 = 101.540.
Value of call option = 102.114 − 101.540 = 0.574.  Larger than 0.407 at σ=0, confirming options rise with vol.

✓  Putable at par Years 1, 2 — 3-yr 4.25% bond, σ = 10%
Year 2 values:
  HH: 98.791 → PUT, reset to 100.
  HL: 99.738 → PUT, reset to 100.
  LL: 100.526 → NOT put.
Year 1 — upper:  [4.25 + 0.5(100 + 100)]/1.038695 = 100.366 (NOT put — value > 100).
Year 1 — lower:  [4.25 + 0.5(100 + 100.526)]/1.031681 = 101.304 (NOT put).

Year 0:  [4.25 + 0.5(100.366 + 101.304)]/1.025 = 102.522.
Value of put option = 102.522 − 102.114 = 0.408.  Larger than 0.283 at σ=0.

⚠  Trap 4 — Putable vs Extendible Equivalence
A 3-yr 3.30% PUTABLE bond (put in Year 2) has identical economic value to a 2-yr 3.30% EXTENDIBLE bond
(extend by 1 yr). If 1-yr fwd rate at end of Yr 2 > 3.30%, put exercised / extension NOT exercised → both end
after Yr 2. If 1-yr fwd rate < 3.30%, put NOT exercised / extension IS exercised → both run to Yr 3. Different
contractual framings, identical cash flows — must trade at same price or arbitrage arises.

heading: From Z-Spread to OAS
body:
For an OPTION-FREE risky bond, the Z-spread is the constant spread added to each forward rate in the benchmark
curve that makes the discounted cash flows equal the bond's market price. When the bond has embedded
options, the tree must be used — the analog is the OAS: the constant spread added to EVERY forward rate ON
THE TREE so that the tree-valued price equals the bond's market price.
OAS — Definition and Interpretation
OAS = constant spread (bps) added to every node rate in the binomial tree so the arbitrage-free value of the
bond matches its observed market price.
Z-spread of an option-free bond = its OAS at zero volatility.
Relative value: compare OAS across bonds with similar credit / characteristics.  LOWER OAS than peers =
RICH (overpriced, avoid).  HIGHER OAS = CHEAP (underpriced, favor).
"Option adjusted" = removes the option value from the spread, isolating the credit + liquidity + model-risk
spread.

✓  OAS example — risky 3-yr 4.25% callable at par Yrs 1, 2; market price 101.000; σ = 10%
Trial at +30 bps: tree price = 100.973 (slightly too low — discount rates too high).
Trial at +28 bps: tree price = 101.010 (slightly too high).
Converged OAS ≈ 28.55 bps.
Tree after adding 28.55 bps: Year 0 = 2.7855%; Year 1 = 4.1550%/3.4536%; Year 2 =
At LL node of Year 2 (rate 3.9896%), value reaches 100.250 → still called (reset 100).

heading: Volatility-OAS Inverse Relationship for Callables
body:
OAS falls as volatility rises (for callable bonds)
Example: 23-yr 5% callable bond, callable in 3 yrs, priced at 95% of par, flat 4% curve.
At σ = 0%:  OAS = 138.2 bps
At σ = 10%: OAS ≈ 70 bps
At σ = 30%: OAS = 1.2 bps
Intuition: higher vol → more call value → a given market price reflects MORE option value and LESS
credit/liquidity premium → lower OAS.
Takeaway: a callable that looks "cheap" at σ=10% may not be cheap at σ=20%. Vol assumption DRIVES the
OAS.

⚠  Trap 5 — OAS direction for putables
For a PUTABLE bond, OAS behavior is reverse. Higher volatility increases put option value, so a given market
price reflects MORE option value and LESS spread — OAS also generally decreases with volatility for putable
bonds. But the sensitivity is smaller than for callables because putable bonds have limited downside from the
investor's view anyway.

⚠  Trap 6 — To add OAS: ADD to tree rates, not subtract from coupon
OAS is a DISCOUNT-RATE adjustment — add 200 bps to every node rate in the tree, NOT subtract 200 bps
from the bond's coupon. Example: 7% callable French corporate; default-free tree 4.6%/{5.9988%,

heading: %}/{7.7515%, 5.7425%, 4.2541%}. At OAS = 200 bps, every rate adds 2% → tree becomes
%/{7.9988%, 6.4400%}/{9.7515%, 7.7425%, 6.2541%}. Revaluing callable at par Yrs 1, 2 → 99.247 (vs
body: default-free price 102.294).

section_title: EFFECTIVE DURATION, ONE-SIDED, AND KEY RATE DURATIONS
heading: Effective Duration — The Only Duration That Works for Option-Embedded Bonds
body:
YIELD duration measures (modified duration, Macaulay) ASSUME cash flows are independent of yield — this
assumption breaks for option-embedded bonds whose cash flows depend on rates. CURVE duration (effective
duration) measures sensitivity to a small parallel shift of the benchmark yield curve, holding OAS constant — valid
for option-embedded bonds and option-free bonds alike.
Effective Duration Formula
EffDur = [PV_minus − PV_plus] / [2 × ΔCurve × PV_0]
PV_0 = current full price; PV_minus = price when curve shifts DOWN by ΔCurve; PV_plus = price when curve
shifts UP.

Procedure: (1) from PV_0, imply OAS; (2) shift curve down, regenerate tree, reprice at same OAS → PV_minus;
(3) shift curve up, reprice at same OAS → PV_plus; (4) apply formula.
Effective duration of an option-free bond ≈ modified duration.  The two can diverge significantly for option-
embedded bonds.

✓  Effective Duration — 3-yr 4.25% callable, price 101.000, σ=10%, OAS = 28.55 bps, ±30 bps shifts
PV_minus (curve down 30 bps, same OAS): 101.599.
PV_plus (curve up 30 bps, same OAS):    100.407.
EffDur = (101.599 − 100.407) / (2 × 0.003 × 101.000) = 1.97.
Interpretation: a 100-bp rise in the curve → bond value falls ~1.97%.

section_title: EFFECTIVE DURATION, ONE-SIDED, AND KEY RATE DURATIONS
heading: Duration Comparison: Callable vs Putable vs Straight
body:

section_title: EFFECTIVE DURATION, ONE-SIDED, AND KEY RATE DURATIONS
heading: One-Sided Durations
body:
When embedded option is NEAR THE MONEY, the bond's price response to up-shifts differs significantly from
down-shifts. Effective duration (symmetric average) is MISLEADING. ONE-SIDED durations measure price change
on each side separately.
Example — 4.5% 5-yr bond immediately callable at par, flat 4% curve, σ=15%
Base price: 99.75 (at 4% curve)
Curve up 30 bps:   price = 99.17
Curve down 30 bps: price = 100.00  (capped at call price!)
One-sided UP duration = (99.75 − 99.17) / (0.003 × 99.75) = 1.94
One-sided DOWN duration = (100.00 − 99.75) / (0.003 × 99.75) = 0.84

Effective (2-sided) = 1.39. Misleadingly symmetric — the bond is MORE sensitive to rate RISES (1.94) than falls
(0.84).

Example — 4.1% 5-yr bond immediately putable at par, flat 4% curve, σ=15%
Base price: 100.45
Curve up 30 bps:   price = 100.00  (put floor!)
Curve down 30 bps: price = 101.81
One-sided UP duration = 1.49
One-sided DOWN duration = 4.51
Effective (2-sided) = 3.00. Putable bond is MORE sensitive to rate DECLINES than rises.

section_title: EFFECTIVE DURATION, ONE-SIDED, AND KEY RATE DURATIONS
heading: Key Rate (Partial) Durations
body:
Key rate durations measure sensitivity to parallel shifts at a SINGLE maturity point on the benchmark curve. Same
mathematical formula as EffDur, but only one key rate shifts. Reveals "shaping risk"
(steepening/flattening/curvature).

Low-coupon (2%) callables unlikely to be called → behave like straight 30-yr; 30-yr key rate dominates. High-
coupon (10%) callables nearly certain to be called → behave like 10-yr; 10-yr key rate dominates. For putables, the
mirror image: low-coupon likely put → behave like short-maturity; high-coupon unlikely put → behave like 30-yr.

⚠  Trap 7 — Negative key rate durations on low-coupon bonds
Zero-coupon or low-coupon 10-yr bonds can have NEGATIVE key rate durations at intermediate maturities
(e.g., KD_5y = −0.93 for 10-yr zero). Mechanism: raising the 5-yr par rate doesn't affect a 10-yr par bond's value
(by definition of par); but the 5-yr zero-coupon rate rises; to keep the 10-yr par bond at par, the 10-yr zero rate
must FALL → a 10-yr zero gains value when 5-yr par rate rises → negative 5-yr KD.

section_title: EFFECTIVE DURATION, ONE-SIDED, AND KEY RATE DURATIONS
heading: Effective Convexity
body:
Effective Convexity Formula
EffCon = [PV_minus + PV_plus − 2 × PV_0] / [(ΔCurve)² × PV_0]
Same shift mechanics as EffDur: shift curve ±ΔCurve, reprice each side at constant OAS.
Option-free bonds: POSITIVE convexity (always).
Callable bond: POSITIVE when call is deep OTM; NEGATIVE when call is near-the-money (upside capped).
Putable bond: POSITIVE convexity always (downside floored but upside unlimited).

✓  Convexity example — 3-yr 4.25% callable, price 100.785, OAS=40 bps, ±30 bps
PV_minus = 101.381,  PV_plus = 100.146,  PV_0 = 100.785.
EffCon = (101.381 + 100.146 − 2·100.785) / (0.003² × 100.785) = −47.41.
Negative convexity confirms: call option is near the money; bond's upside from rate drops is limited.
Convention note: sometimes divided by 100 for reporting ("scaled convexity"). The curriculum uses raw
convention.

heading: % → value = 100.000 exactly. Similarly for a floor below all tree rates → floor value = 0.
Defining Features
body:
A convertible bond = option-free bond + embedded call option on issuer's COMMON STOCK. Investors accept a
LOWER coupon in exchange for equity upside via conversion. Issuers get cheaper debt but their shareholders face
DILUTION risk upon conversion. Convertibles are usually callable (enables "forced conversion" when share price
rises).
Convertible Bond Value Building Blocks
Simple convertible:   Value = Straight bond value + Call option on issuer stock
Callable convertible: Value = Straight bond + Stock call − Issuer bond call
Callable + putable convertible: Value = Straight bond + Stock call − Issuer call + Investor put
Valuation mechanics: binomial tree + backward induction — same framework as callable/putable bonds.

heading: Convertible Bond Metrics
body:
Core Convertible Metrics
Conversion ratio = # shares per bond par value (e.g., 17.5 shares per $1,000 par).
Conversion price = Par value / Conversion ratio (e.g., $1,000/17.5 = $57.14).
Conversion value (parity value) = Underlying share price × Conversion ratio.
Minimum value = max{Straight value, Conversion value}.  Convertible cannot trade below either floor.
Market conversion price = Convertible bond price / Conversion ratio.   (break-even share price)
Market conversion premium per share = Market conversion price − Share price.
Market conversion premium ratio = Premium per share / Share price.
Premium over straight value = (Convertible price / Straight value) − 1.


✓  Worked Twitter 0.25% 2024 example (Exhibit 28)
Conversion rate: 17.5 shares per $1,000 par. Conversion price = $57.14. Share price at issue: $40.10.
Conversion premium at issue: 42.5%.
On 15-Jun-2019: share price $35.14, convertible price $952.25.
Conversion value = $35.14 × 17.5 = $614.95.
Market conversion price = $952.25 / 17.5 = $54.40.
Market conversion premium per share = $54.40 − $35.14 = $19.26.
Market conversion premium ratio = $19.26 / $35.14 = 54.8%.
Premium over straight value = $952.25/$894.86 − 1 = 6.41% (straight value $894.86 assumed).

heading: Risk-Return Characteristics by Moneyness
body:
Regime
Share price vs
conversion price
Convertible bond behavior
Busted convertible
Share price << conversion
price
Acts like straight BOND. Option is far OTM. Driven by
interest rates + credit spreads. As share price → 0, bond
value → recovery rate in bankruptcy.
Mixed
Share price ≈ conversion
price
Price rises with stock but at a slower rate than stock
(convexity). Call option's delta-like response.
Stock-like
Share price > conversion
price (ITM)
Acts like underlying STOCK. Price tracks conversion
value closely. Rate/credit effects diminish.
Past forced conversion
Share >> conversion price
+ bond callable
Issuer forces conversion by calling the bond —
bondholders convert rather than accept redemption
value.

✓  Example 9 — Heavy Element Inc. convertible (5-yr, 3.75% annual, 23.26 conversion ratio)
On 16-Sep-2022: convertible price $1,230, share price $52.
Conversion price = $1,000/23.26 = $43.
Conversion value = $52 × 23.26 = $1,209.
Market conversion price = $1,230/23.26 = $52.88.
Market conversion premium per share = $52.88 − $52 = $0.88.
Share price ($52) > conversion price ($43) → convertible is STOCK-LIKE.

⚠  Trap 9 — When to exercise vs sell vs wait on a convertible
Just because share > conversion price doesn't mean exercise is optimal. For European-style conversion
options, exercise is only possible at a set date. For American/Bermudan, it's usually better to WAIT or SELL the
convertible than exercise — time value of the option still has positive value. Typical exercise trigger is issuer-
forced conversion via a bond call.


⚠  Trap 10 — "Premium over straight value" is a FLAWED downside metric
Premium over straight value treats straight value as a fixed floor. But straight value FLUCTUATES with interest
rates and credit spreads — it is NOT fixed. A falling straight value (rising rates, credit deterioration) means the
floor is shifting down, so the actual downside risk is understated by this metric.

⚠  Trap 11 — Change-of-control convertible bondholder options
On merger/acquisition events, convertible holders typically have a CHOICE: (a) put option (full par redemption)
OR (b) adjusted downward conversion price (so they can convert favorably into the new entity). Make-whole
premium for forced early conversion also common.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM3

⚠  T1. Callable = short call; putable = long put
The investor in a CALLABLE bond is implicitly SHORT the call option to the issuer. Value of callable = straight −
call value. The investor in a PUTABLE bond is LONG the put. Value of putable = straight + put value. Call and
put option values are ALWAYS positive.

⚠  T2. All options rise with volatility
Every embedded option (call, put, cap, floor, conversion) INCREASES in value with interest rate (or share price)
volatility. Consequence: callable bond FALLS with vol; putable bond RISES with vol; capped floater FALLS with
vol; floored floater RISES with vol.

⚠  T3. Callable value rises less than straight when rates fall
As rates decline, the call option moves ITM, capping the callable bond's upside. Curriculum example: at 3%
flat, straight 30-yr 4.5% = 129.54, callable = 110.43. Callable limits investor upside by ~19 points.

⚠  T4. Putable limits downside
As rates rise, the put option moves ITM, flooring the putable bond value. Curriculum example: curve 3% → 5%,
straight falls 30%, putable falls only 22%.

⚠  T5. Call option value rises as curve flattens/inverts

Upward-sloping curve → high future forwards → FEW call opportunities. Flat or inverted curve → many low-
forward nodes → MANY call opportunities. Call value peaks when curve is inverted.

⚠  T6. Put option value falls as curve flattens/inverts
Opposite of call. Upward-sloping → high forwards → MANY put opportunities. Inversion kills put opportunities.

⚠  T7. Putable ≡ Extendible
A 3-yr putable (put in Yr 2) has same value as a 2-yr extendible (+1yr option). Both pay 3.30% for 2 years then
depending on rate at end of Yr 2, either redeem or continue. Identical cash flows → identical values or arbitrage.

⚠  T8. Exercise style — American NOT available for puts
Call options can be European, Bermudan, or American. PUT options can only be European or (rarely)
Bermudan — NO American-style puts in the curriculum examples.

⚠  T9. No-arbitrage price floor on putable
Market price of a putable bond ≥ put price always — if price < put price, buy bond and immediately put at
higher price for riskless profit. The put price acts as a hard floor.

⚠  T10. Tree-node formula with exercise reset
At each interior node: expected value = [coupon + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L]/(1+i). Callable: if expected > call price →
RESET to call price. Putable: if expected < put price → RESET to put price.

⚠  T11. Make-whole call = essentially non-callable
Modern investment-grade corporates have make-whole calls with call price = PV of remaining CFs discounted
at narrow spread to Treasuries. Refinancing gain to issuer is near zero. Investors face little call risk.

⚠  T12. Raising call price deep OTM → callable = straight value
In the Example 3 scenario: 3-yr 4.25% callable at 102 (not 100) → call never triggers because expected PV max
is ~100.9 < 102 → callable value = straight value = 102.114.

⚠  T13. Lowering put price deep OTM → putable = straight value
In the Example 4 scenario: 3-yr 4.25% putable at 95 (not 100) → put never triggers → putable value = straight
value = 102.114.


⚠  T14. Forward-rate valuation > spot-rate valuation for option-embedded bonds
Forward-rate method allows evaluation at each future node. Spot-rate method prices cash flows directly and
loses the node-by-node decision. For option-free bonds, both methods give equivalent values. For option-
embedded, only forward (tree) approach works.

⚠  T15. Z-spread = OAS at zero volatility
For an OPTION-FREE bond, Z-spread is the constant spread on the forward curve equating discounted CFs to
market price. Z-spread = OAS(σ=0). When σ > 0 for an option-bearing bond, OAS < Z-spread (the difference
captures option value).

⚠  T16. OAS interpretation for relative value
Higher OAS than comparables = CHEAP. Lower OAS = RICH. OAS is the "option-adjusted" credit/liquidity
spread — after stripping option effect, compare like-to-like across similar bonds.

⚠  T17. OAS falls as σ rises (for callables)
Higher vol → larger embedded call option value → at same market price, LESS spread is needed to reconcile
model price. OAS drops steeply with vol: 23-yr 5% callable priced 95: σ=0% → 138.2 bps; σ=30% → 1.2 bps.

⚠  T18. OAS mechanics: add to rates, not subtract from coupon
To value a bond at its market price using OAS, ADD the OAS bps to every forward rate on the tree; do NOT
subtract OAS from the coupon. Common MC distractor.

⚠  T19. Effective duration is the ONLY valid duration for option-embedded bonds
Modified/Macaulay duration assume fixed CFs. Effective duration uses curve shifts and considers how option
exercise changes CFs. Use EffDur for all option-embedded bonds.

⚠  T20. EffDur formula: ±30 bps shift, constant OAS
Procedure: (1) from PV_0 imply OAS; (2) shift curve down 30 bps, regen tree at same OAS → PV_minus; (3) shift
up 30 bps → PV_plus; (4) EffDur = (PV_minus − PV_plus)/(2·ΔCurve·PV_0).

⚠  T21. Callable EffDur < straight EffDur
Callable bond's price upside is capped by call → less sensitive to rate declines → shorter effective duration than
straight bond.


⚠  T22. Putable EffDur < straight EffDur
Putable bond's price downside is floored by put → less sensitive to rate rises → shorter effective duration than
straight bond.

⚠  T23. Callable duration shortens when rates FALL
Falling rates push call ITM → price convergence to call price → effective duration shortens.

⚠  T24. Putable duration shortens when rates RISE
Rising rates push put ITM → price convergence to put price → effective duration shortens.

⚠  T25. One-sided duration > effective duration when near-the-money
Example callable: EffDur = 1.39 (2-sided average), but one-sided up = 1.94, one-sided down = 0.84. When near
ATM, symmetric 2-sided duration UNDERSTATES directional sensitivity.

⚠  T26. One-sided UP duration > DOWN duration for callable
Callable more sensitive to rate rises than falls (upside capped). Opposite for putable: one-sided DOWN > UP
(putable more sensitive to rate falls).

⚠  T27. Key rate duration for callable shifts with moneyness
Low-coupon callable (unlikely called) → 30-yr key rate dominates (like straight bond). High-coupon callable
(likely called) → 10-yr key rate dominates (behaves like bond to first call date).

⚠  T28. Sum of key rate durations = total effective duration
Because a parallel shift = all key rates shift by the same amount. Can use this to sanity-check partial durations.

⚠  T29. Negative key rate durations for zeros at intermediate maturities
Raising the 5-yr par rate doesn't change a 10-yr par bond's value (by definition of par), so the 10-yr zero-
coupon rate must fall to offset the higher 5-yr zero-coupon rate → a 10-yr zero GAINS value → negative 5-yr KD.

⚠  T30. Effective convexity formula and sign
EffCon = [PV_minus + PV_plus − 2·PV_0] / [(ΔCurve)²·PV_0]. Straight and putable bonds: always POSITIVE.
Callable near-the-money: NEGATIVE — upside capped creates concavity.


⚠  T31. Putable always positively convex
Put floor limits downside, no cap on upside → convex always positive. Putable bonds more DEFENSIVE than
callables.

⚠  T32. Callable convexity: positive when call is OTM, negative when near ATM
When call is far OTM, callable behaves like straight → positive convexity. As call moves to ATM, call caps the
upside → convexity turns negative.

⚠  T33. Capped floater < 100; floored floater > 100
Plain floater = 100 at each reset. Cap reduces upside if ref rate spikes → capped floater < 100. Floor increases
coupons when ref rate drops → floored floater > 100.

⚠  T34. Cap/floor binds if within tree rate range
Cap value = 0 if cap > all tree rates (never binds). Floor value = 0 if floor < all tree rates. Essential for exam:
check tree rates before calculating.

⚠  T35. Set-in-arrears coupons use reference rate AT END of period
A floater "set in arrears" uses the reference rate observed at the END of the coupon period (same date as
payment). Curriculum convention: reference rate at each tree node determines the coupon paid at the NEXT
period-end.

⚠  T36. Convertible = straight bond + equity call option
Simple model. Callable convertible subtracts issuer call value. Callable+putable convertible subtracts issuer
call and adds investor put. All valued via binomial tree + backward induction.

⚠  T37. Conversion price = par / conversion ratio
Example: $1,000 par, conversion ratio 17.5 → conversion price = $57.14. Stock split doubles conversion ratio
and halves conversion price.

⚠  T38. Convertible behavior by moneyness
Share << conversion price = BUSTED (bond-like).  Share >> conversion price = STOCK-LIKE.  Mixed zone in
between. Interest rates matter most when busted; stock performance matters most when ITM.


⚠  T39. Market conversion premium ratio vs Conversion premium at issue
"Conversion premium at issue" uses the ISSUE share price (42.5% for Twitter). "Market conversion premium
ratio" uses the CURRENT share price (54.8% on 15-Jun-2019). Different denominators.

⚠  T40. Threshold dividend — conversion price adjustment
If issuer pays an annual dividend above a THRESHOLD, the conversion price is usually adjusted DOWN to
protect convertible holders from dividend dilution. Below threshold, no adjustment.

⚠  T41. Forced conversion via issuer call
When share price rises well above conversion price, issuer may call the bond at a price LOWER than the
conversion value, FORCING bondholders to convert rather than accept redemption. Strengthens issuer capital
structure.

⚠  T42. Change-of-control: put or adjusted conversion
If issuer is acquired, convertible holders typically can (a) PUT at par or (b) accept DOWNWARD-ADJUSTED
conversion price allowing favorable conversion into new entity.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM3

Value Relationships — Embedded Options
Core identities
Value of callable bond = Value of straight bond − Value of issuer call option
Value of putable bond = Value of straight bond + Value of investor put option
Value of capped floater = Value of straight floater − Value of cap
Value of floored floater = Value of straight floater + Value of floor
Value of convertible bond = Value of straight bond + Value of call on stock (± bond call, put)

Binomial Tree Valuation — Backward Induction with Exercise
Procedure
At each interior node: expected value = [C + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L] / (1 + i_node).
Tree spacing: R_u = R_d · e^(2σ√t). Probabilities 0.5/0.5.

Callable: if expected value > call price → RESET to call price.  Putable: if expected value < put price → RESET to
put price.
Work right-to-left. Terminal node = par + final coupon.

OAS
Option-Adjusted Spread
OAS = constant bps spread added to all forward rates on the tree → tree-valued price = market price.
Z-spread = OAS at zero volatility (option-free bonds).
OAS is a RATE adjustment (add to tree), not a coupon adjustment.
Higher OAS than comparables = CHEAP; lower = RICH (for same credit/characteristics).
Callable: OAS falls as σ rises (option value up → less credit spread needed to match price).

Duration & Convexity
Effective Duration & Convexity
EffDur = (PV_minus − PV_plus) / (2 × ΔCurve × PV_0).
EffCon = (PV_minus + PV_plus − 2·PV_0) / ((ΔCurve)² × PV_0).
Procedure: imply OAS from PV_0 → shift curve ± → regen tree → reprice at same OAS.
One-sided durations: up/down computed separately (not averaged) — essential when option is near ATM.
Key rate duration: shift ONLY one key maturity point — sensitivity to shaping risk.
Sum of key rate durations = total effective duration.

Duration/Convexity Signs & Hierarchy
Rules
Effective duration: straight ≥ callable, straight ≥ putable.
Convexity: option-free (+), putable (+), callable (+ when OTM, − when near ATM).
Callable EffDur shortens as rates FALL (call ITM).  Putable EffDur shortens as rates RISE (put ITM).
Cash = 0 dur.  Zero-coupon ≈ maturity.  Fixed-rate < maturity.  Floater ≈ time to next reset.

Convertible Bond Metrics
Formulas
Conversion price = Par / Conversion ratio
Conversion value = Share price × Conversion ratio
Market conversion price = Convertible price / Conversion ratio

Market conversion premium per share = Market conversion price − Share price
Market conversion premium ratio = Premium per share / Share price
Minimum value = max{Straight value, Conversion value}
Premium over straight value = (Convertible price / Straight value) − 1

Convertible Regime Map
Moneyness-based behavior
Share << Conversion price  →  BUSTED — bond-like.  Interest rates + credit drive price.
Share ≈ Conversion price    →  MIXED.  Convertible rises less than stock (option convexity).
Share >> Conversion price   →  STOCK-LIKE.  Tracks conversion value. Rate/credit effects muted.
Very high share, callable   →  FORCED CONVERSION likely — issuer calls, investors convert.

— End of Notes — LM3: Valuation and Analysis of Bonds with Embedded Options —

section_title: CREDIT RISK FUNDAMENTALS: EXPOSURE, LGD, POD, CVA
heading: Default Risk vs Credit Risk
body:
DEFAULT RISK is the narrower concept — the probability of an event of default. CREDIT RISK is broader — it
combines the default probability AND the expected loss if default occurs. A collateralized loan can have high
default risk but low credit risk if collateral value is high relative to debt.

CREDIT SPREAD (also G-SPREAD) = corporate YTM − government YTM (same maturity). Compensates investors
for expected default loss AND a default risk premium for timing uncertainty. Assuming equal tax and liquidity (a
simplification), the spread is driven primarily by credit risk.

section_title: CREDIT RISK FUNDAMENTALS: EXPOSURE, LGD, POD, CVA
heading: The Four Credit Risk Parameters
body:
Core Definitions
EXPECTED EXPOSURE = projected amount an investor could lose if default occurs, BEFORE factoring in
recovery. For a simple one-period bond, exposure = coupon + principal at end of period.
RECOVERY RATE = percentage of exposure recovered in default. Varies by industry, seniority, capital
structure, collateral. Baseline assumption: 40%.
LOSS GIVEN DEFAULT (LGD) = Exposure × (1 − Recovery rate). Loss severity = 1 − recovery rate.
PROBABILITY OF DEFAULT (POD) = probability the issuer fails to meet contractual obligations. Two flavors:
ACTUAL (historical) and RISK-NEUTRAL.
CVA (Credit Valuation Adjustment) = Σ over dates of [LGD × POD × Discount factor]. The PV of expected credit
loss.
Fair Value of risky bond = Value assuming no default (VND) − CVA.

section_title: CREDIT RISK FUNDAMENTALS: EXPOSURE, LGD, POD, CVA
heading: Risk-Neutral vs Actual Default Probabilities
body:
✓  Simple one-year 4% corporate bond priced at par (flat 3% govt)
Exposure at Year 1: 104.  Recovery rate: 40% → LGD = 104 × 0.60 = 62.4.  Recovery if default = 41.6.
ACTUAL POD (from history): 1% → expected future value = 104(0.99) + 41.6(0.01) = 103.376.
Discount at risk-free 3%: 103.376/1.03 = 100.365. This OVERSTATES the observed price of 100.
RISK-NEUTRAL P*: solve 100 = [104(1 − P*) + 41.6(P*)]/1.03  →  P* = 1.60%.
P* > actual POD because the risk-neutral probability includes the default risk PREMIUM for timing uncertainty,
plus liquidity + tax effects.

⚠  Trap 1 — Risk-neutral probabilities are GREATER than actual probabilities
Risk-neutral PODs incorporate investors' default risk premium, liquidity differences, and tax effects — not just
the pure historical default frequency. So they LOOK high relative to rating-agency historical experience. In
Exhibit 24 example, AAA risk-neutral annual POD is 1.01% even though no AAA bond has defaulted since
1995. Both numbers are CORRECT; they measure different things.

body:
(cum)

CVA = 3.1549
Exposures are PV at risk-free rate over remaining years: 100/1.03⁴ = 88.8487 etc. PODs are CONDITIONAL on
survival: Date-2 POD = 1.25% × (1 − 1.25%) = 1.2344%. Cumulative POD over 5 years = 6.0957%.
Bond fair value at Date 0 = 83.1060. YTM = 3.77%. Credit spread = 77 bps.

section_title: CREDIT RISK FUNDAMENTALS: EXPOSURE, LGD, POD, CVA
heading: IRRs Given Timing of Default
body:
Realized IRR if default occurs on each date (5-yr zero at 83.1060)
Default Date 1 (recovery 35.5395):  IRR = −57.24%
Default Date 2 (recovery 36.6057):  IRR = −33.63%
Default Date 3 (recovery 37.7038):  IRR = −23.16%
Default Date 4 (recovery 38.8350):  IRR = −17.32%
Default Date 5 (recovery 40.0000):  IRR = −13.61%
No default → IRR = 3.77%.
Key insight: realized return depends CRITICALLY on WHEN default occurs. Early defaults are far worse.

✓  Example 1 — Ranking bonds by expected loss
Bond A: exposure 104, POD 0.75%, recovery 40 → LGD 64 × 0.75% = 0.480
Bond B: exposure 98, POD 0.90%, recovery 35 → LGD 63 × 0.90% = 0.567
Bond C: exposure 92, POD 0.80%, recovery 30 → LGD 62 × 0.80% = 0.496
Credit risk ranking HIGHEST to LOWEST: B > C > A.

✓  Example 2 — Three-year 5% corporate bond, flat 2.50% govt, POD 1.5%, recovery 40%
VND = 5/1.025 + 5/1.025² + 105/1.025³ = 107.1401.
Date-1 exposure (bond value + coupon): 5 + 5/1.025 + 105/1.025² = 109.8186.  LGD = 65.8911.
Date-2 exposure: 5 + 105/1.025 = 107.4390.  Date-3 exposure (terminal): 105.
PODs: 1.5000%, 1.4775%, 1.4553% (cumulative 4.4328%).
CVA = 2.7222.  Fair value = 107.1401 − 2.7222 = 104.4178.
IRRs: default Date 1 = −57.76%, Date 2 = −33.27%, Date 3 = −22.23%, no default = 3.57%.

Market price of 104 vs fair value 104.4178 → essentially fairly priced.

section_title: CREDIT SCORES, CREDIT RATINGS, AND TRANSITION
heading: Credit Scores vs Credit Ratings
body:
CREDIT SCORES are used in RETAIL lending (individuals, small businesses). CREDIT RATINGS are used in
WHOLESALE lending (corporate bonds, government bonds, ABS).

section_title: CREDIT SCORES, CREDIT RATINGS, AND TRANSITION
heading: FICO Score (US)
body:
Range 300–850. Used by ~90% of US retail lenders. Sourced from Experian, Equifax, and TransUnion. Five-factor
proprietary algorithm:
Factor

Includes

credit

Hard inquiries count; soft inquiries do not
NOT included in FICO: race, color, national origin, sex, marital status, age, salary, occupation, employment
history, home address, child/family support. Soft inquiries (self-checks, employment verification) do NOT affect
score.

section_title: CREDIT SCORES, CREDIT RATINGS, AND TRANSITION
heading: Credit Ratings & Notching
body:
The major agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch) rate issuer and specific issues. Letter grades from AAA (highest) to D
(default). Ratings consider PROBABILITY of default AND EXPECTED LGD via "notching" — subordinated debt is
typically notched 1-2 levels below the issuer's senior unsecured rating. Rating agencies assign OUTLOOK
(positive/stable/negative) and can put issuer "under watch."

RadioShack rating history — demonstrates migration over decades
BBB− (May 1969) → BB+ (Oct 1978) → AAA (Jan 1983) → A+ Watch Negative (Nov 1984) → A−/Stable (Feb 1993)
→ BBB+ (Aug 2005) → BBB− (Apr 2006) → BB (Oct 2006) → CCC (2012-2014) → D (Feb 2015)
Illustrates that ratings can remain stable for YEARS (A+ 1984-1991; A− 1993-2005) before migrating — and
that an AAA company can slowly deteriorate to default.

section_title: CREDIT SCORES, CREDIT RATINGS, AND TRANSITION
heading: One-Year Corporate Rating Transition Matrix (Representative)

section_title: CREDIT SCORES, CREDIT RATINGS, AND TRANSITION
heading: Expected Return Given Rating Migration
body:
✓  A-rated 10-yr corporate bond with end-of-year mod dur = 7.2
Expected price change = −ModDur × Δspread.  From A (spread 1.10%):
To AAA: −7.2 × (0.60% − 1.10%) = +3.60%.
To AA:  −7.2 × (0.90% − 1.10%) = +1.44%.
To BBB: −7.2 × (1.50% − 1.10%) = −2.88%.
To BB:  −7.2 × (3.40% − 1.10%) = −16.56%.
To B:   −7.2 × (6.50% − 1.10%) = −38.88%.
To CCC: −7.2 × (9.50% − 1.10%) = −60.48%.
Expected price change = Σ P(migration) × %price change per A row of matrix. Then add coupon income.

heading: The Two Classes
body:
Feature
Structural (Merton) Models
Reduced-Form (Intensity) Models
Treatment of default
ENDOGENOUS — derived from company
balance sheet; default when assets < barrier
EXOGENOUS — default is a random Poisson
event; intensity is the key parameter
Core question
WHY does default occur?
WHEN does default occur?
Inputs
Company value, asset volatility, default
barrier (liabilities)
Observable variables: leverage ratio, net-
income/assets, macro (unemployment,
GDP)
Data requirement
Difficult — company assets may not trade
(unlike stock)
Easy — uses historical regression data
Economic insight
STRONG — explains fundamental drivers
LIMITED — purely statistical

Feature
Structural (Merton) Models
Reduced-Form (Intensity) Models
Implementation
BURDENSOME — requires option-pricing
machinery
FLEXIBLE — can incorporate business cycle
directly
Default "surprise"?
Not a surprise — continuously observable
Can appear as a surprise (in practice rare —
usually preceded by downgrades)

heading: Structural Model — Equity & Debt as Options on Assets
body:
Denote asset value A(T), face value of debt K (default barrier), equity E(T), debt D(T). Time-T balance sheet:
Merton Option Interpretation of the Capital Structure
Balance sheet identity: D(T) + E(T) = A(T).
Equity:  E(T) = max[A(T) − K, 0]  —  equity is a LONG CALL on assets with strike = face of debt.
Debt:    D(T) = A(T) − max[A(T) − K, 0]  —  debt = long assets + short call on assets.
If A(T) > K: equity = A(T) − K, debt = K (no default).
If A(T) < K: equity = 0, debt = A(T) (default; debtholders receive assets).
Default probability = P[A(T) < K] — lower part of the asset-value distribution.
DP rises with: higher asset volatility, longer time to maturity T, higher financial leverage.
Limited liability is inherent: equity cannot be negative (like options).

heading: Reduced-Form Advantages and Disadvantages
body:
● ADVANTAGE — uses observable company and macro data; captures business cycle directly
(recessionary default intensities rise).
● ADVANTAGE — mathematically simpler than structural models; easy to calibrate.
● DISADVANTAGE — purely statistical; does NOT explain WHY default occurs.
● DISADVANTAGE — assumes default is a "surprise" that can hit at any time; empirically defaults are
usually preceded by a cascade of downgrades (RadioShack being a canonical example).

⚠  Trap 2 — The Merton "equity = long call" interpretation is the exam favorite
On the exam: equity holders are in a LONG CALL position on company assets with strike K (face of debt).
Debtholders are in a LONG ASSETS + SHORT CALL position. The call option premium debtholders "receive" is
the compensation for bearing default risk. Limited liability = equity cannot fall below zero, just like a call option.
Equivalent formulations: D(T) + E(T) = A(T); E(T) = max[A(T)−K, 0]; D(T) = min[A(T), K].

⚠  Trap 3 — Structural model data challenges (Enron, Lehman)
The structural model's key input is company ASSET value — but assets usually don't trade like stock.
Companies can hide debt (Enron, Tyco, WorldCom, Parmalat, Lehman) making the default barrier K hard to

estimate EXACTLY when it matters most. This practical limitation makes reduced-form models attractive to
practitioners despite their lack of economic insight.

heading: Volatility Effect on CVA (Lognormal Asymmetry)
body:
✓  Same bond at 20% volatility
VND stays at 103.5450 (govt bond tree is arbitrage-free regardless of vol).
Wider Date-4 range: 2.0948% to 10.3757% (vs 3.2764%-7.2918% at 10%).
CVA becomes 3.5390 — SLIGHTLY LOWER than 10% vol CVA of 3.5394.
Fair value at 20% vol = 100.0060 vs 10% vol 100.0056 — tiny gap.
Why? Lognormal trees spread rates asymmetrically around the forward; top of tree goes up 5.41%, bottom
only down 2.87%. Higher exposures at top (low bond value = less loss) net to lower expected loss.

section_title: VALUING RISKY BONDS IN AN ARBITRAGE-FREE FRAMEWORK
heading: Example 6 — Three-Year 4% Corporate Bond (Boller)
body:
✓  Three-year 4% annual pay bond, priced 104; POD 2.25%, recovery 40%, vol 10%
VND = 107.3586 (computed via backward induction through the tree).
Date-1 exposure: (0.5)(102.6183) + (0.5)(103.5621) + 4 = 107.0902.  Date-2: 104.9120.  Date-3: 104.
PODs: 2.2500%, 2.1994%, 2.1499%.  CVA = 4.0954.
Fair value = 107.3586 − 4.0954 = 103.2632 (approx).
Market price 104 > fair value → bond is slightly OVERvalued. Boller should NOT buy.

heading: Macro and Microeconomic Drivers
body:
● MACRO — Business cycle (spreads widen in recessions), monetary policy tightness, systemic risk-
aversion shocks.
● MICRO — Company-specific credit quality, expected loss due to default, liquidity and tax differences.
● XVA collection — CVA (credit valuation adjustment, most developed), FVA (funding), LVA (liquidity), TVA
(taxation). Credit spread captures all of these in practice; CVA is the only modeled component in this
curriculum.

heading: Spread-POD-Recovery Implied Relationship
body:
Risk-neutral annual PODs by rating (40% recovery, 5-yr 3.50% corp)
AAA: 0.60% spread → annual POD 1.01% → cumulative 5-yr POD 4.95%
AA:  0.90% spread → annual POD 1.49% → cumulative 5-yr POD 7.23%
A:   1.10% spread → annual POD 1.83% → cumulative 5-yr POD 8.82%
BBB: 1.50% spread → annual POD 2.48% → cumulative 5-yr POD 11.80%
BB:  3.40% spread → annual POD 5.64% → cumulative 5-yr POD 25.19%
B:   6.50% spread → annual POD 10.97% → cumulative 5-yr POD 44.07%
CCC: 9.50% spread → annual POD 16.50% → cumulative 5-yr POD 59.41%

heading: Recovery Rate Sensitivity
body:
✓  Same 5-yr 3.50% corp, POD 1.83%, recovery dropped 40% → 30%
At 40% recovery: CVA = 3.5394, fair value 100.0056, YTM 3.50%, spread 110 bps (baseline).
At 30% recovery: LGD rises 25% → CVA = 5.9781, fair value = 97.5670, YTM 4.05%, spread 130 bps.
Takeaway: 10% recovery drop → 20 bp spread widening for this bond.

✓  Example 8 — Kapili: 3-yr 3% corp, vol 20%, POD 1.5%, recovery 40%
VND = 104.4152.  CVA = 2.6984.  Fair value = 101.7168.  YTM = 2.40%.  Spread = 2.40% − 1.50% = 90 bps.
Doubling POD to 3%: CVA = 5.3174, fair value = 99.0978 → spread widens ~150 bps.
Halving recovery to 20%: LGD rises → larger CVA → spread widens.
Result: Kapili's intuition was correct — DOUBLING POD has larger impact on CVA than HALVING recovery in
this setup, because POD directly multiplies the LGD term.

⚠  Trap 5 — POD and recovery relationship for given market price
For a fixed bond price and credit spread, POD and recovery rate are POSITIVELY correlated (you can solve for
either). Higher recovery → higher implied POD needed to produce same spread. Lower recovery → lower POD
needed. The spread pins down LGD × POD together, not either alone.

heading: Determinants of the Credit Spread Term Structure
body:
● CREDIT QUALITY — higher-quality issuers have flatter credit curves; lower-quality issuers can show
steeper or more complex shapes.
● FINANCIAL CONDITIONS — tight conditions or recession widen spreads especially at long end → steeper.

● MARKET SUPPLY/DEMAND & LIQUIDITY — less liquid maturities trade wider. Expected heavy issuance in
a tenor can flatten the curve via tighter bid-offer.
● MACRO & EQUITY VOLATILITY — higher equity vol implies higher default probability (via structural
models) → steeper credit curves.
● COMPANY-VALUE MODEL RESULTS — structural model implied default probability is a key driver. Equity
volatility feeds directly.

heading: Practitioner Methodology
body:
● Use a benchmark = closest-maturity government bond or, more commonly, the swap curve (greater
liquidity at off-the-run maturities).
● Term structure analysis includes only bonds with SIMILAR credit characteristics — typically senior
unsecured general obligations.
● Exclude bonds with embedded options, first/second lien, or unique structural features.
● Cross-default provisions typically mean all senior unsecured debts of an issuer share the same recovery
in bankruptcy.

heading: Distressed Regime — Price Converges to Recovery
body:
✓  Zero-coupon distressed bonds trading at recovery (40%)
5-yr zero: VND = 86.2609, fair value = 40, implied yield = 20.1124%, credit spread = 17.1124%.
10-yr zero: VND = 74.4094, fair value = 40, implied yield = 9.5958%, credit spread = 6.5958%.
Result: STEEPLY INVERTED credit spread curve — 17.1% short spread vs 6.6% long spread.
This is an "optical" artifact: both bonds trade at the same recovery because cross-default ties them together,
so shorter maturities show artificially higher yields due to the near-term realization.

⚠  Trap 6 — Distressed curve inversion is NOT meaningful "steepness"
When an issuer trades on PRICE basis converging to recovery (distressed/bankruptcy-likely), the spread term
structure becomes a mathematical artifact of the near-term recovery realization, not a reflection of term-
specific default risk. Portfolio managers who disagree with imminent-default view: sell short-term protection,
buy long-term protection in CDS market. If the issuer survives, the manager retains short premium and has
optional long-term gain.

heading: Why Securitized Debt Requires a Different Approach
body:
Securitized debt finances a SPECIFIC POOL of assets (mortgages, auto loans, credit card receivables) via a
BANKRUPTCY-REMOTE SPV (special purpose vehicle) — NOT the issuer's general balance sheet. Investors
analyze:

● The underlying pool of assets (credit quality, seasoning, geographic mix).
● The originator and servicer (performance history, financial health).
● The legal structure (tranches, waterfall, bankruptcy remoteness).
● Credit enhancements (over-collateralization, subordination, excess spread, reserve accounts, letters of
credit).
● Prepayment risk (especially in mortgage-backed securities).

heading: Corporate vs Securitized — Key Differences
body:

business model

⚠  Trap 7 — Granularity and homogeneity dictate analysis method
GRANULAR (many small loans like credit cards, autos) + HOMOGENEOUS → BOOK or PORTFOLIO approach
(aggregated statistics).
NON-GRANULAR (few large loans like CMBS, CLOs) + HETEROGENEOUS → LOAN-BY-LOAN analysis.
Mixed pools (SME ABS) → either approach.
RMBS is an exception: granular & homogeneous but often still analyzed loan-by-loan due to mortgage-specific
risks.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM4

⚠  T1. Credit risk vs default risk
DEFAULT risk = probability of default event.  CREDIT risk = default probability × expected loss given default.
Collateralized loans can have high default risk but low credit risk.

⚠  T2. Credit spread = G-spread
Credit spread = corporate YTM − government YTM (same maturity). Same idea as G-spread. Captures
expected default loss + default risk premium + liquidity/tax effects.

⚠  T3. Exposure includes PRINCIPAL + COUPON
Expected exposure at default is the FULL claim: coupon + principal for bullet bonds. For a zero-coupon bond,
exposure is just the accrued value (PV of face at risk-free rate).

⚠  T4. Recovery = % of EXPOSURE (not % of face)
When exposure is 104, recovery 40% → recover 41.6 (40% × 104). NOT 40% of 100 face = 40. Small but critical
distinction, especially with coupons.

⚠  T5. LGD = Exposure × (1 − Recovery)
Loss given default = exposure minus recovery amount. Loss severity = 1 − recovery rate. Don't confuse LGD
(dollar amount) with loss severity (percentage).

⚠  T6. Actual POD < Risk-Neutral POD

Risk-neutral PODs include a DEFAULT RISK PREMIUM for timing uncertainty + liquidity/tax effects. Rating-
agency historical defaults give ACTUAL PODs. Risk-neutral always ≥ actual.

⚠  T7. Use risk-free rate (not YTM) in risk-neutral valuation
Under risk-neutral valuation, expected cash flows are discounted at the RISK-FREE rate, not the bond's YTM.
The bond's YTM already reflects credit risk; you'd be double-counting.

⚠  T8. POD is CONDITIONAL on prior survival
Year-by-year PODs decline even with constant "hazard rate" because they multiply by survival probability.
Cumulative POD = 1 − Π(1 − annual POD). Example: hazard 1.25% → year-by-year

⚠  T9. Fair Value = VND − CVA
Value assuming no default minus credit valuation adjustment. CVA = Σ [LGD × POD × DF]. Separate steps:
compute VND (arbitrage-free, no credit risk), compute CVA (credit losses), subtract.

⚠  T10. IRR given default date matters enormously
5-yr zero at 83.1060: default Date-1 IRR = −57.24%, Date-5 IRR = −13.61% (defaulting at maturity). No default
= 3.77%. Timing of default is a massive swing factor.

⚠  T11. Bootstrap discount factors sequentially
DF₁ from 1-yr bond price; DF₂ uses DF₁ and 2-yr bond; continue forward. Each DF derives from earlier DFs +
current bond equation. Don't try to solve all DFs simultaneously.

⚠  T12. ESG can directly affect POD and LGD
Environmental fines, labor lawsuits, weak governance (fraudulent accounting) all raise POD and LGD. Green
bonds, pandemic bonds, and catastrophe bonds have explicit ESG-linked cash flows.

⚠  T13. FICO score weights — memorize
Payment history 35%, debt burden 30%, credit history length 15%, types of credit 10%, recent searches 10%.
Range 300-850. Does NOT use race, sex, salary, occupation, marital status, etc.

⚠  T14. Hard vs soft credit inquiries

HARD inquiries (loan applications) AFFECT FICO score. SOFT inquiries (self-check, employment verification)
do NOT. Knowing your own score does not lower it.

⚠  T15. Notching reflects LGD differences
Issuer rating is typically for senior unsecured. Subordinated debt is "notched" down 1-2 levels because its LGD
is higher in bankruptcy. Explains why "credit ratings" not "default ratings."

⚠  T16. Transition matrix use: expected price change = −ModDur × Δspread
From A (1.10% spread), going to BB (3.40%) with ModDur 7.2 → expected price change = −7.2 × (3.40% −

heading: %) = −16.56%. Multiply each scenario's price change by transition probability to get expected.
body:
⚠  T17. Rating can remain stable for YEARS before migrating
RadioShack: A+ 1984-1991; A− 1993-2005. Then rapid deterioration 2005-2015 ending in default. Ratings
show "inertia" during stable business conditions, then quick deterioration under stress.

⚠  T18. Structural model — equity = call option on assets
E(T) = max[A(T) − K, 0] where K = face of debt = default barrier. Debtholders: long assets + short call. Default
when A(T) < K. Default probability rises with asset volatility, time T, leverage.

⚠  T19. Structural ENDOGENOUS; reduced-form EXOGENOUS
Structural explains WHY default occurs (asset < barrier). Reduced-form describes WHEN statistically via
Poisson default intensity. Reduced-form treats default as a "surprise" event.

⚠  T20. Structural strengths vs weaknesses
STRENGTH: deep economic insight. WEAKNESS: requires knowing company asset value and default barrier —
data is scarce when it matters most (Enron, Lehman examples).

⚠  T21. Reduced-form strengths vs weaknesses
STRENGTH: uses OBSERVABLE data (leverage ratio, NI/assets, macro variables); captures business cycle;
flexible. WEAKNESS: purely statistical; no economic explanation; assumes default is surprise (historically
defaults preceded by downgrades).

⚠  T22. Arbitrage-free tree must reprice benchmark govt bonds to par

Before using the tree to value a corporate bond, verify the 2.75% 5-yr govt bond prices at 100 via backward
induction through the tree. If not, calibration is wrong.

⚠  T23. Volatility effect on CVA for option-free risky bond
Tiny impact. 10% vol CVA = 3.5394; 20% vol CVA = 3.5390. Lognormal asymmetry means top of tree moves
further than bottom, but top has lower bond values = lower expected loss. Nets to small difference.

⚠  T24. Bond value NOT affected by vol for OPTION-FREE govt bonds
Regardless of the volatility assumption, an arbitrage-free tree prices the benchmark government bond at par.
Volatility affects only option-embedded bonds and risky bonds (via CVA).

⚠  T25. Expected exposure at node uses NODE probability
For Date-4 in a 10% vol tree: E[exposure] = Σᵢ Pᵢ × V_i + coupon, where Pᵢ are Pascal-triangle probs
(0.0625/0.25/0.375/0.25/0.0625 for 4 steps).

⚠  T26. In-arrears coupons — Date-N pays using Date-(N−1) rate
Floater coupon for Date 1 uses the Date-0 rate. For any interior Date-N, use the rate at that node's
predecessor in the tree. Don't use the current node's rate for the current-date coupon.

⚠  T27. Discount margin vs quoted margin
Quoted margin is CONTRACTUAL. Discount margin is the IMPLIED spread over benchmark that equates PV of
cash flows to market price. DM > quoted → floater below par. DM < quoted → floater above par.

⚠  T28. POD and recovery are POSITIVELY correlated for a given spread
If you raise the recovery assumption, the implied POD must rise to keep the same spread. Lower recovery →
lower POD needed. The spread pins down LGD × POD jointly.

⚠  T29. POD has BIGGER spread impact than recovery for given change
In Kapili example: doubling POD (1.5% → 3%) impacts CVA more than halving recovery (40% → 20%) because
POD multiplies the LGD directly in each period's CVA contribution.

⚠  T30. Credit spread term structure determinants

CREDIT QUALITY (better = flatter), FINANCIAL CONDITIONS (tight = wider/steeper), LIQUIDITY, MACRO +
EQUITY VOL (structural model output), COMPANY-VALUE ANALYSIS. Cross-default + bankruptcy provisions
tie multiple maturities together.

⚠  T31. Distressed issuers trade on PRICE basis, not spread basis
When default is imminent, both 5-yr and 10-yr bonds converge to the same recovery rate. Resulting "credit
curve" is an optical artifact — steeply inverted without reflecting term-specific default risk.

⚠  T32. Swap curve often replaces govt curve for spread calcs
Off-the-run govt bonds have illiquid yields; swap rates are more liquid at all tenors. Practitioners often use
swap curve + credit spread instead of govt curve + Z-spread.

⚠  T33. XVA = collection of valuation adjustments
CVA (credit), FVA (funding), LVA (liquidity), TVA (taxation). CVA is most developed. All captured in the
observed corporate-govt spread in practice; curriculum models only CVA.

⚠  T34. Structural model default probability drivers
Default probability RISES with: (1) higher asset volatility, (2) greater time to T, (3) higher financial leverage
(lower A-K gap). Less debt lowers K and reduces default probability.

⚠  T35. Corporate = balance sheet; Securitized = pool
Corporate bond = GENERAL OBLIGATION of entire balance sheet. Securitized = specific ISOLATED pool in
bankruptcy-remote SPV. Different analysis requirements.

⚠  T36. Credit enhancements list
OVER-COLLATERALIZATION (pool value > bonds issued), SUBORDINATION (senior/junior tranches), EXCESS
SPREAD (interest from pool > bond coupons), RESERVE ACCOUNTS, LETTERS OF CREDIT, monoline
guarantees.

⚠  T37. Waterfall priority = protection
Senior tranches paid first from pool cash flows. Junior absorb losses first. Higher senior credit quality
achievable even from mediocre collateral via the waterfall + enhancements.

⚠  T38. Granularity dictates analysis methodology

GRANULAR + HOMOGENEOUS (credit cards, auto ABS) → Book or portfolio approach. NON-GRANULAR +
HETEROGENEOUS (CMBS, CLOs) → Loan-by-loan. Mixed (SME ABS, RMBS) → either.

⚠  T39. Bankruptcy remoteness is essential for securitization
The SPV must be legally isolated from the originator so originator bankruptcy does NOT pull collateral back.
Without true sale treatment, the securitization collapses to an unsecured loan to the originator.

⚠  T40. RadioShack pattern is the archetype
Slow deterioration from AAA to default takes years with multiple downgrades along the way. Reduced-form
models' "default is a surprise" assumption contradicts this typical path — another argument for using
structural or hybrid models.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM4

Credit Risk Parameters
Core definitions
Expected exposure = projected amount claimed if default occurs (before recovery).
LGD = Exposure × (1 − Recovery rate).   Loss severity = 1 − Recovery rate.
POD (Date t) = hazard × Π(1 − hazard)^(t−1), CONDITIONAL on prior survival.
Cumulative POD = 1 − Π(1 − annual POD) = 1 − POS(T).
Risk-neutral POD > Actual POD (includes default risk premium + liquidity/tax).

CVA and Fair Value
Pricing framework
CVA = Σₜ [LGDₜ × PODₜ × DFₜ]
Fair Value = VND − CVA
Where VND = Value assuming No Default = Σₜ CFₜ × DFₜ (or backward induction on arb-free tree).
Risk-neutral P* solves observed price:  Price = [Survival·CF + POD·Recovery] / (1 + r_f).
YTM from fair value + credit spread = YTM − benchmark yield.

Structural Model (Merton)
Equity as call, debt as short call

D(T) + E(T) = A(T)     (balance sheet identity)
E(T) = max[A(T) − K, 0]     (long call on assets; K = face of debt)
D(T) = A(T) − max[A(T) − K, 0] = min[A(T), K]
Default at T if A(T) < K.
PD rises with asset σ, maturity T, leverage.

Reduced-Form Model
Poisson intensity model
Default time ~ Poisson process with intensity λ(t) (default intensity / hazard rate).
λ = f(leverage, NI/assets, cash/assets, unemployment, GDP, equity vol, macro).
Cumulative survival P(T) = exp(−∫λ(t)dt). POD = 1 − exp(−λT) for constant λ.
Flexible regression; uses observable data; does NOT explain why default.

Credit Rating Transition & Expected Return
One-year return with migration
Δ%P per migration = −ModDur × Δspread.
Expected Δ%P = Σ transitions P(migration) × Δ%P(migration).
Total Expected Return = coupon yield + Expected Δ%P.
Notching: subordinated bonds rated 1-2 levels below issuer senior.

Securitized vs Corporate Credit Analysis
Quick comparison
Corporate: GENERAL OBLIGATION — balance sheet, covenants, cash flows, business model.
Securitized: ISOLATED POOL in SPV — pool assets, servicer, enhancements (over-collat, subord, excess
spread), waterfall, prepayment, bankruptcy remoteness.
Granular + Homogeneous (credit cards, autos) → Book or Portfolio approach.
Non-granular + Heterogeneous (CMBS, CLOs) → Loan-by-loan.
RMBS + SME ABS → Loan-by-loan or Portfolio (hybrid).

Key Numerical Anchors
Curriculum examples for recall
5-yr zero, 1.25% POD, 40% recovery, 3% flat: VND 86.26 · CVA 3.15 · Fair 83.11 · YTM 3.77% · Spread 77 bps.

5-yr 3.50% corp, 1.25% POD, 40% rec, 10% vol: VND 103.5450 · CVA 3.5394 · Fair 100.0056 · Spread 74.88
bps.
Same bond at 20% vol: CVA 3.5390 · Fair 100.0060 (tiny vol effect for option-free).
Spreads-to-POD (40% rec, 5-yr): AAA 60→1.01% · BBB 150→2.48% · BB 340→5.64% · CCC 950→16.50%.
Distressed 5-yr zero at 40 recovery: VND 86.26 · yield 20.11% · spread 17.11%.
Distressed 10-yr zero at 40 recovery: VND 74.41 · yield 9.60% · spread 6.60% → steep inverted curve.

— End of Notes — LM4: Credit Analysis Models —

heading: The Law of One Price & Arbitrage
body:
The central idea of financial economics: market prices adjust until there are no opportunities for arbitrage — "free
money." The LAW OF ONE PRICE states that two goods which are perfect substitutes must sell for the same
current price (ignoring transaction costs). Otherwise, an investor would sell the overvalued asset and buy the
undervalued one simultaneously, taking riskless profit until prices converge.

Arbitrage-Free Valuation — Principle
Arbitrage opportunity: a transaction requiring NO cash outlay that yields a RISKLESS profit.
Core implication: If you put up no money and take no risk, your expected return must be zero.
A bond is a PORTFOLIO of zero-coupon bonds. Value additivity: the value of the whole equals the sum of the
values of the zero-coupon parts.
Discount each cash flow at the spot rate for its maturity. Discounting all cash flows at a single YTM gives only
an approximation.
