✓  Example 1 — Arbitrage opportunity check
Bond A: 3% annual 10-yr bond, yield 2.5% in NY; same bond sells $104.376/$100 in Chicago.  Check: 3/1.025
+ 3/1.025² + … + 103/1.025¹⁰ = $104.376. Price matches — NO arbitrage.
Bond B: 3% annual 10-yr bond, yield 3.2% in HK; same sells RMB97.220/RMB100 in Shanghai.  Arbitrage-free
price: 3/1.032 + … + 103/1.032¹⁰ = 98.311. Shanghai < HK price → BUY Shanghai at 97.220, SELL HK at 98.311
→ profit RMB1.091 per RMB100 traded.

heading: Stripping & Reconstitution
body:
US Treasury dealers can physically SEPARATE a coupon bond into its constituent zero-coupon securities (each
coupon + final principal becomes a distinct zero) — this is STRIPPING. They can also REPLICATE a coupon bond
by combining zeros — this is RECONSTITUTION. If the arbitrage-free price (sum of PVs of zeros) differs from the
bond's market price, dealers strip/reconstitute until equality is restored. Sovereign dealers worldwide operate
similarly.

heading: Three Steps of Valuation
body:
● Step 1 — Estimate the future cash flows.
● Step 2 — Determine the appropriate discount rate(s) — for arbitrage-free valuation, spot rates for each
CF's maturity.

● Step 3 — Calculate PV of each cash flow using the appropriate discount rate and sum them.

✓  Example 2 — Arbitrage-free value of a 5% 3-yr bond (par rates 2%, 3%, 4%)
Bootstrap spot rates:  z₁ = 2% (= 1-yr par rate).
Solve z₂:  1 = 0.03/1.02 + 1.03/(1+z₂)²  ⇒  z₂ = 3.015%.
Solve z₃:  1 = 0.04/1.02 + 0.04/1.03015² + 1.04/(1+z₃)³  ⇒  z₃ = 4.055%.
Arbitrage-free price:  P₀ = 5/1.02 + 5/1.03015² + 105/1.04055³ = 102.8102.
If the bond is offered at 102.7751 (priced at YTM 4%), it is mispriced by 0.0351/100 of par.
Takeaway: discounting at YTM over-discounts early coupons on upward-sloping curves (and under-discounts
on downward-sloping). Always use spot rates for arbitrage-free pricing.

⚠  Trap 1 — YTM discounting ≠ arbitrage-free valuation
Discounting ALL cash flows at a single YTM produces a value close to but NOT equal to the arbitrage-free value
— except when the yield curve is flat. To be arbitrage free, each cash flow must be discounted at the spot rate
of its own maturity. Do NOT use the bond's YTM on exam arbitrage-free questions.

⚠  Trap 2 — Equal-risk cash flows must use equal discount rates
Two cash flows with identical risks delivered at the same time are discounted with the same rate EVEN IF they
belong to different bonds. This is the mechanical basis of stripping/reconstitution: a 3-year zero from any AAA
issuer should yield the same 3-year spot rate as any other.

section_title: THE BINOMIAL INTEREST RATE TREE FRAMEWORK
heading: The Lognormal Random Walk
body:
CFA candidates model rates using a LOGNORMAL process. Two properties of lognormal that matter for the exam:
● Non-negativity — interest rates CANNOT go negative because as rates approach zero, the absolute
change shrinks (changes are proportional to the level).
● Higher volatility at higher rates — the absolute change Δr grows with r; specifically, stdev of 1-year rate =
i₀·σ. E.g., σ=10%, i₀=2% → stdev = 20 bps.


Lognormal tree spacing
At any pair of adjacent nodes:   i_H = i_L × e^(2σ)
At any Time-2 triple:   i_{HH} = i_{LL} × e^(4σ)   and   i_{HL} = i_{LL} × e^(2σ)
At any Time-3 quadruple:   i_{LLH} = i_{LLL}·e^(2σ);  i_{HHL} = i_{LLL}·e^(4σ);  i_{HHH} = i_{LLL}·e^(6σ)
Nodes are "(nearly) centered" on the implied forward rate; the geometric spread is e^(2σ) between adjacent
rates.
Example (σ = 15%):   i_{1,L} = 1.194% ⇒ i_{1,H} = 1.194% × e^(2×0.15) = 1.612%.
Recombining tree: HL = LH — there are only n+1 nodes at time n, not 2^n.

section_title: THE BINOMIAL INTEREST RATE TREE FRAMEWORK
heading: Backward Induction — Value at a Node
body:
Fill in the tree RIGHT-TO-LEFT. At maturity each bond redeems at par (plus final coupon). Work back one period at
a time. At every interior node, the bond value equals the present value of (coupon + probability-weighted expected
next-period value). Equal probabilities 0.5/0.5 apply because the lognormal model assumes equal up/down
probabilities at each node.
Node Value Formula
Value at node = [C + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L] / (1 + i)
C = coupon paid at end of period,  V_H = bond value at upper child node, V_L = value at lower child node, i =
one-year forward rate AT THE NODE.
Apply iteratively from Time-N (par + final coupon) back to Time-0 (today's price).

✓  Example 3 — Backward induction through a 3-yr tree (5% coupon, par 100)
Tree: Time-0 = 2.0%;  Time-1 = 5.0% / 3.0%;  Time-2 = 8.0% / 6.0% / 4.0%.
Time-2 discounted terminal values (each receives 105 = 100 + 5):
    105/1.08 = 97.2222;  105/1.06 = 99.0566;  105/1.04 = 100.9615.
Time-1 upper (5%):  V = [5 + 0.5(97.2222) + 0.5(99.0566)] / 1.05 = 98.2280.
Time-1 lower (3%):  V = [5 + 0.5(99.0566) + 0.5(100.9615)] / 1.03 = 101.9506.
Time-0 (2%):  V = [5 + 0.5(98.2280) + 0.5(101.9506)] / 1.02 = 103.0287.

⚠  Trap 3 — Coupon is paid at the END of the period, discounted at the NODE rate
The rate AT a node (e.g., 5% at the 5% Time-1 node) is used to discount cash flows from the end of the NEXT
period back to that node. The coupon for the period just ending is ALREADY included in the value propagated
from the child nodes. Always place the coupon directly to the right of the starting node.

heading: Calibration Procedure
body:
● Fix i₀ = the known 1-year spot rate.
● For Time-1: pick a trial i₁,L. Derive i₁,H = i₁,L · e^(2σ). Price the 2-year benchmark bond via backward
induction. Iterate (Solver) until price = 100.
● For Time-2: pick a trial i₂,HL (a good start is the implied 2y-forward from the spot curve). Derive i₂,HH =
i₂,HL · e^(2σ) and i₂,LL = i₂,HL / e^(2σ). Price the 3-year benchmark bond using the ALREADY-
CALIBRATED Time-1 rates. Iterate until price = 100.
● Continue for each additional maturity — each step ONLY varies the middle rate at that level; prior
calibrated rates are held fixed.

✓  Worked calibration — σ = 15%, benchmark curves from §3.1
Time-0: i₀ = 1.00%.
Time-1 calibration (2-yr 1.20% bond at par):
   Final calibrated:  i₁,L = 1.1943%,  i₁,H = 1.6121% (= 1.1943% × e^0.30).
   V_H = 101.20/1.016121 = 99.5944;  V_L = 101.20/1.011943 = 100.0056.
   V₀ = [1.20 + 0.5(99.5944) + 0.5(100.0056)] / 1.01 = 100.0000  ✓.
   Note average of (1.6121, 1.1943) = 1.4032% — slightly ABOVE implied forward 1.4028% due to lognormal
skew.
Time-2 calibration (3-yr 1.25% bond at par):
   Calibrated: i₂,LL = 0.9803%, i₂,HL = 1.3233%, i₂,HH = 1.7863%.
Time-3 calibration (4-yr 1.40% bond at par):

   Calibrated: i₃,LLL = 1.1521%, i₃,LLH = 1.5552%, i₃,HHL = 2.0994%, i₃,HHH = 2.8338%.

heading: % (= 3.442% × e^0.30).
body:
V_H = 103/1.04646 = 98.427;  V_L = 103/1.03442 = 99.573.
   V₀ = [3 + 0.5(98.427 + 99.573)] / 1.02 = 100.000  ✓.
Time-2 (calibrate to 4% 3-yr bond): initial trial = implied forward 6.166% gives V₀ = 99.898 — slightly low.
Final: i₂,LL = 4.482%, i₂,HL = 6.050%, i₂,HH = 8.167%.
   Time-2 terminal PVs: 104/1.08167 = 96.148; 104/1.06050 = 98.067; 104/1.04482 = 99.539.
   Time-1: [4 + 0.5(96.148+98.067)]/1.04646 = 96.618;  [4 + 0.5(98.067+99.539)]/1.03442 = 99.382.
   Time-0: [4 + 0.5(96.618+99.382)]/1.02 = 100.000  ✓.

⚠  Trap 4 — Prior calibration is LOCKED when advancing
When calibrating Time-2, you do NOT re-solve for Time-1 rates — those were fixed by the 2-year bond. You
only vary the middle Time-2 rate. Each maturity uses all previously calibrated rates as fixed parameters.
Changing Time-2 rates does not alter the 2-yr bond price because the 2-yr bond only uses Time-0 and Time-1
discount rates.

⚠  Trap 5 — Average of the H and L rates ≠ the implied forward

Because of lognormal skew, (i_H + i_L)/2 is slightly HIGHER than the arithmetic implied forward rate. In the
example, average of {1.6121%, 1.1943%} = 1.4032% vs. implied forward = 1.4028%. This small gap grows as
σ grows. The tree is "centered" on the forward curve in GEOMETRIC (log-mean) sense, not arithmetic.

section_title: VALUING OPTION-FREE BONDS WITH THE BINOMIAL TREE
heading: %/1.3233%/1.7863%, Time-3: 1.1521%/1.5552%/2.0994%/2.8338%) → V₀ = 102.3254.
body:
Match confirms the tree is arbitrage free.

✓  Example 5 — 3-yr 5% option-free bond using calibrated tree from Example 4
Tree (σ=15%): Time-0 = 2%, Time-1 = 3.442%/4.646%, Time-2 = 4.482%/6.050%/8.167%.
Time-2 terminal PVs: 105/1.08167 = 97.0721;  105/1.06050 = 99.0099;  105/1.04482 = 100.4958.
Time-1: [5 + 0.5(97.0721+99.0099)]/1.04646 = 98.4663;  [5 + 0.5(99.0099+100.4958)]/1.03442 = 101.2672.
Time-0: [5 + 0.5(98.4663+101.2672)]/1.02 = 102.8105.
Matches the spot-discounted price from Example 2 (102.8102; small rounding difference). The tree is arbitrage
free.

⚠  Trap 6 — Both methods MUST match for option-free bonds
A calibrated tree that fails to reprice an option-free bond to the spot-rate value has a calibration error (often the
lognormal spacing or a trial rate). For option-free bonds, the tree provides NO additional insight beyond the
spot curve — its value appears only when valuing bonds whose CASH FLOWS DEPEND on future rates
(callable, putable, MBS, etc.).

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Pathwise Valuation
body:
PATHWISE valuation is an alternative to backward induction that produces the SAME bond value. Steps: (1) list all
interest-rate paths through the tree, (2) compute the PV of each path's cash flows, (3) average the PVs. For a

recombining binomial tree with N steps, the number of paths equals 2^N but collapses to N+1 terminal nodes —
PASCAL'S TRIANGLE gives the number of paths arriving at each node (1, N, N(N-1)/2, …).
Pathwise Mechanics
Step 1 — Enumerate paths. For N time steps, 2^N paths ordered by {H/L} sequence.
Step 2 — For each path, discount all cash flows CF_t at the sequence of forward rates along that path:  PV = Σ
CF_t / Π(1 + i_path,s) for s=0..t-1.
Step 3 — Average across all 2^N paths → this is the bond's value.
Pascal's Triangle rows give # paths to each terminal node:  {1}, {1,1}, {1,2,1}, {1,3,3,1}, {1,4,6,4,1} for N =
0,1,2,3,4.

✓  Example 6 — Pathwise valuation of 3-yr 5% bond using the Example 4 tree
4 paths (HH, HL, LH, LL) — all paths start at 2.000%:
Path 1 (HH):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.04646) + 105/(1.02·1.04646·1.08167) = 100.5298.
Path 2 (HL):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.04646) + 105/(1.02·1.04646·1.06050) = 102.3452.
Path 3 (LH):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.03442) + 105/(1.02·1.03442·1.06050) = 103.4794.
Path 4 (LL):  5/1.02 + 5/(1.02·1.03442) + 105/(1.02·1.03442·1.04482) = 104.8877.
Average = (100.5298 + 102.3452 + 103.4794 + 104.8877) / 4 = 102.8105.
Matches backward-induction result from Example 5. ✓

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: The Monte Carlo Method
body:
Monte Carlo randomly samples a large number of interest-rate paths rather than enumerating all paths (which is
infeasible for long-horizon securities like 30-year MBS). It is the standard approach for PATH-DEPENDENT
securities — where cash flows depend on the ROUTE rates took to reach a given level, not just the current level.
The classic application is mortgage-backed securities, where borrower prepayments depend on the rate path.

Monte Carlo Five-Step Procedure (30-yr monthly MBS example)
Step 1 — Simulate 500+ paths of 1-month rates under an interest-rate model (lognormal / normal) and a
volatility assumption.
Step 2 — Generate spot rates from simulated 1-month rates.
Step 3 — Determine the path-dependent cash flow along each path (e.g., prepayment-adjusted MBS flows).
Step 4 — Compute PV along each path.
Step 5 — Average the PVs across all paths → bond value.

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Drift Adjustment
body:
Raw simulations will NOT reproduce market prices of benchmark bonds by chance. A DRIFT TERM — a constant
added to every simulated short rate on every path — is chosen such that the average simulated PV of each

benchmark bond equals its market price. A model calibrated this way is said to be DRIFT ADJUSTED and is
ARBITRAGE FREE. Without drift adjustment, the model is not market-consistent.

section_title: PATHWISE VALUATION AND THE MONTE CARLO METHOD
heading: Mean Reversion in Monte Carlo
body:
Empirically rates don't wander indefinitely — they tend to mean-revert to economically reasonable levels.
Modelers implement UPPER and LOWER BOUNDS on the random process or build mean reversion explicitly into
the stochastic process (moving generated rates toward implied forwards). Mean reversion improves long-horizon
simulation realism.

✓  Example 7 — Monte Carlo for 3-yr 5% bond using 8 random paths
Each of 8 random paths has Time-0 = 2.000% but random Time-1 and Time-2 rates.
Path PVs: 105.7459, 103.2708, 100.9104, 103.8543, 101.9075, 102.4236, 103.3020, 101.0680.
Average = 102.8103.
Matches the backward-induction and pathwise results (102.8105). Monte Carlo is calibrated correctly.
For option-free bonds, Monte Carlo is redundant. Its value appears for PATH-DEPENDENT bonds (MBS with
prepayments) where backward induction fails.

⚠  Trap 7 — More paths ≠ more ACCURACY about true value
Increasing Monte Carlo paths from 500 to 5,000 reduces SAMPLING error but does not reduce MODEL error. If
the underlying interest-rate model is misspecified or inputs are wrong, more paths just deliver a more precise
answer to the wrong question. Monte Carlo is "only as good as the valuation model and the accuracy of the
inputs."

⚠  Trap 8 — Backward induction FAILS for path-dependent securities
Backward induction requires cash flows at each node to depend ONLY on the current node (and possibly
current level of rates), not on HOW rates arrived there. Prepayment-adjusted MBS violates this: a borrower's
refinance decision depends on whether they previously had lower rates. Use MONTE CARLO, not backward
induction, for MBS and other path-dependent instruments.

heading: The Four Foundational Models
body:
Stochastic Processes — The "Big Four"
CIR (Cox–Ingersoll–Ross, equilibrium):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t) dt + σ√r_t · dZ
Vasicek (equilibrium):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t) dt + σ · dZ
Ho–Lee (arbitrage-free):   dr_t = θ_t · dt + σ · dZ
Kalotay–Williams–Fabozzi / KWF (arbitrage-free):   d ln(r_t) = θ_t · dt + σ · dZ
k = speed of mean reversion; θ = long-run mean rate; σ = volatility; dZ = std normal random.

heading: Deep Dive on Each Model
body:
CIR — mean reverts to θ. The volatility term σ√r means volatility shrinks as rates approach zero, preventing
negative rates — a nice economic property. Derived in a general equilibrium model of investor
consumption/investment.

Vasicek — also mean reverts. But volatility is CONSTANT (σ, not σ√r), which means rates CAN theoretically go
negative. Not derived from general equilibrium but treated as equilibrium.
Ho-Lee — the first arbitrage-free model. Drift θ_t varies with time (so it can match any initial term structure
exactly). NO mean reversion. Constant vol → rates CAN go negative. Implemented in a binomial lattice.
KWF — same structure as Ho-Lee but models d ln(r), i.e., rates are LOGNORMAL. Prevents negative rates.
Lognormal rate distribution changes option-pricing tails.

heading: Modern & Multi-Factor Models
body:
MODERN interest-rate models extend the one-factor framework in two directions: (1) multi-factor models
incorporating slope/curvature factors, (2) sophisticated approaches combining observed forward curves with
implied volatilities from option markets.

Gauss+ — A multi-factor example
Three rates modeled: short-, medium-, and long-term rates.
Long-term rate: MEAN REVERTING to a long-run level reflecting macroeconomic trends.
Medium-term rate: mean reverts to the LONG-RUN rate.
Short-term rate: has NO random component — central banks control the short end.
Result: hump-shaped volatility term structure, with MEDIUM-TERM rates being the most volatile.

✓  Example 8 — Which model fits the current term structure most accurately?
Answer: Arbitrage-free models (Ho-Lee, KWF, BDT) match market prices by construction. Equilibrium models
(CIR, Vasicek) fit only by chance.
Observations empirically show parallel shifts explain >90% of yield changes, supporting single-factor models
for most applications.
Why equilibrium models persist: they describe many possible future paths from a fundamental framework,
useful for long-horizon scenario/stress analysis.

⚠  Trap 9 — "Arbitrage-free" = matches TODAY'S market prices
Arbitrage-free models are specifically PARAMETERIZED so the simulated / tree-based values of benchmark
bonds equal their observed market prices. Equilibrium models do NOT have this property — their parameters
come from economic theory, and market prices are an OUTPUT not an INPUT. On exam: "model matches
market" or "fits current term structure" → arbitrage-free.

⚠  Trap 10 — Negative-rate allowance per model

CIR and KWF: rates CANNOT go negative (CIR: σ·√r vanishes at r=0; KWF: log-normal).  Vasicek and Ho-Lee:
rates CAN go negative (constant σ with normal distribution).  On exam: "which model prohibits negative
rates?" → CIR or KWF.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM2

⚠  T1. Arbitrage requires no net outlay AND no risk
An arbitrage opportunity is a transaction with ZERO cash outlay that earns a RISKLESS profit. If either the
outlay or risk is positive, it is not arbitrage — it is just investing. "No arbitrage" is enforced by the law of one
price.

⚠  T2. Value additivity vs. dominance
Value additivity: whole ≠ sum of parts (e.g., portfolio B priced at 97 vs 105 zeros priced at 100). Dominance:
risk-free payoff must be positive (e.g., Asset D pays 220 for 200 while C pays 105 for 100). Both are arbitrage
types — know the distinction.

⚠  T3. YTM ≠ arbitrage-free
Discounting all CFs at a single YTM is only an APPROXIMATION. Arbitrage-free valuation uses SPOT rates for
each CF's maturity. Discounting at YTM over-discounts early coupons on upward curves and under-discounts
on downward curves.

⚠  T4. Bond = portfolio of zeros
The most important conceptual frame: a 5-yr semi-annual coupon bond = 11 zero-coupon cash flows (10
semiannual coupons + 1 principal at maturity). Stripping lets dealers physically separate them; reconstitution
reassembles them. Arbitrage forces the price of the bond = sum of strip prices.

⚠  T5. Stripping prevents arbitrage
If a bond trades BELOW its stripped value, dealers buy the bond, strip it, and sell the strips. If it trades ABOVE,
dealers reconstitute. This pressure enforces value additivity in Treasury markets globally.

⚠  T6. Lognormal guarantees two nice properties
A lognormal interest-rate model gives: (1) NEGATIVE rates are impossible (as r → 0, so does Δr in absolute
terms); (2) volatility RISES with rate level (stdev of 1-yr rate = i₀·σ). Model is the default for CFA-style binomial
trees.


⚠  T7. Stdev of one-year rate = i₀·σ
In a lognormal world: if σ = 10% (volatility parameter) and i₀ = 2%, the stdev of the 1-year rate is 2% × 10% = 20
bps. Rate moves are proportional to the LEVEL of rates — higher rates, bigger moves.

⚠  T8. Adjacent tree nodes differ by e^(2σ)
The spacing multiplier between adjacent 1-year forwards at the same time step is e^(2σ), NOT e^σ. For
σ=15% that's e^0.30 = 1.3499, so i_H is about 35% higher than i_L. Memorize the "2σ" factor.

⚠  T9. Recombining tree — only n+1 nodes at time n
Because HL = LH (order doesn't matter at recombining nodes), at Time N there are only N+1 distinct nodes, not
2^N. A 30-year tree has only 31 rates at maturity, not ~10⁹. Critical for computational feasibility.

⚠  T10. At a lognormal tree node, Pr(up) = Pr(down) = 0.5
The lognormal model uses EQUAL probabilities at each node. That is why the node value formula uses 0.5/0.5
weighting. Don't use other probabilities.

⚠  T11. Backward-induction value formula
At any interior node: V = [C + 0.5(V_H + V_L)] / (1 + i). C = coupon paid at END of period; i = the 1-year forward
rate AT THAT NODE; V_H, V_L = bond values at next period up/down children.

⚠  T12. Work backward, right to left
Start at maturity (value = par + final coupon); work back one period at a time. You cannot value an interior node
until BOTH child nodes are valued. This is the essential asymmetry of backward induction.

⚠  T13. Coupon placement at nodes
Coupon C at Time T+1 is placed directly to the right of the Time T node. The value AT Time T includes the PV of
that coupon plus the expected PV of the bond at T+1. Misplacing the coupon by one period is a common error.

⚠  T14. Calibration lock-in
Time-1 rates are fixed by the 2-year bond and remain LOCKED when solving for Time-2. Time-2 solution only
varies the MIDDLE Time-2 rate; upper and lower follow from e^(2σ) spacing. Each successive maturity only
solves for one more parameter.


⚠  T15. Average of H and L ≠ implied forward (lognormal skew)
Lognormal rate distribution is right-skewed, so arithmetic mean of {i_L, i_H} slightly EXCEEDS the implied
forward rate — geometric mean matches implied forward. Example: (1.6121+1.1943)/2 = 1.4032 vs implied

⚠  T16. Low-σ tree collapses to forward curve
At σ = 0 (deterministic world): the tree IS the implied forward curve with zero dispersion. At σ=0.01% example:
tree rates are very close to 1.4028% at Time-1. As σ rises, the tree spreads out around the forwards.

⚠  T17. Arbitrage-free tree MUST reprice option-free bonds to spot-discounted value
If tree value ≠ spot-rate value for an option-free bond, the tree is miscalibrated. Option-free bonds are the
DIAGNOSTIC TEST for tree correctness.

⚠  T18. Calibrated tree = zero-coupon yield curve valuation
For option-free bonds, binomial tree and spot-rate discounting give identical values. The tree adds NO value for
option-free bonds — its purpose is to value option-embedded or path-dependent bonds.

⚠  T19. Pathwise and backward induction give identical values
For option-free bonds, averaging present values across all paths in the tree = backward-induction value.
Example 5 = Example 6 = 102.8105.

⚠  T20. Pascal's Triangle gives # paths to each terminal node
A 4-period tree has terminal path counts {1, 4, 6, 4, 1}. Total paths = 2^N = 16. The middle (HHLL mix) node has
the most paths leading to it; the extreme nodes have only one path each.

⚠  T21. Number of Paths = 2^N
For N time steps, there are exactly 2^N interest-rate paths through a binomial tree — regardless of
recombining. Pathwise valuation becomes computationally prohibitive above ~25 steps, motivating Monte
Carlo.

⚠  T22. Monte Carlo for PATH-DEPENDENT securities
Use Monte Carlo when cash flows depend on HOW rates arrived at the current level — e.g., MBS prepayments
(borrowers refinance when rates drop). Backward induction works only for state-dependent (not path-
dependent) CFs.


⚠  T23. Drift adjustment keeps Monte Carlo arbitrage-free
Raw Monte Carlo simulations will not match benchmark market prices. A DRIFT TERM — a constant added to
every simulated rate on every path — is chosen so average simulated PV of each benchmark = market price.
This makes the model arbitrage-free.

⚠  T24. Drift adjustment ≠ true rate forecast
Drift is a numerical correction to enforce arbitrage-free pricing. It is NOT an economic forecast of how rates
will evolve.

⚠  T25. More paths ≠ more accuracy of true value
Monte Carlo sampling error decreases as 1/√n. But if the underlying MODEL or INPUTS are wrong, more paths
just produce a more precise wrong answer. Don't conflate sampling precision with model accuracy.

⚠  T26. Mean reversion in Monte Carlo
Modelers impose bounds or drift adjustments that pull rates back toward implied forwards or a long-run mean.
Without mean reversion, simulated paths can drift arbitrarily far over long horizons (e.g., 30 years for MBS).

⚠  T27. CIR vs Vasicek: volatility behavior
Both have mean reversion drift k(θ−r). Difference: CIR has σ·√r (volatility shrinks at low rates) vs. Vasicek has
constant σ. Consequence: CIR rates cannot go negative; Vasicek rates can.

⚠  T28. Ho-Lee vs KWF: log vs level
Ho-Lee models dr (the rate CHANGE) — rates normal, can go negative. KWF models dln(r) — rates
LOGNORMAL, cannot go negative. Both are arbitrage-free with time-dependent drift θ_t.

⚠  T29. Equilibrium models don't fit market
CIR and Vasicek have few parameters (k, θ, σ) and cannot be tuned to match many term-structure points
exactly. Arbitrage-free models use time-varying drift θ_t, which gives as many parameters as maturity buckets,
allowing exact fit.

⚠  T30. Arbitrage-free = risk-neutral probabilities
Arbitrage-free models use RISK-NEUTRAL (Q-measure) probabilities — under which discounted prices are
martingales. Equilibrium models use REAL-world probabilities. Same underlying math, different interpretation.


⚠  T31. One-factor vs multi-factor models
One-factor: only the short rate drives term structure (all rates move same direction in any period, different
magnitudes). Multi-factor: slope and curvature added. Empirically ~90% of yield changes are parallel, so one-
factor works well for most apps.

⚠  T32. Gauss+ has central-bank-controlled short end
Gauss+ models the short rate with NO random component — reflecting central bank rate control. Medium rate
mean reverts to long. Long rate mean reverts to macro trend. Produces hump-shaped vol term structure (mid-
tenor most volatile).

⚠  T33. Implied volatility sources
Rate volatility can be estimated two ways: (a) HISTORICAL — stdev of past rate moves; (b) IMPLIED —
extracted from observed market prices of interest-rate derivatives (swaptions, caps, floors). Forward-looking,
derivative-based implied vol is often preferred.

⚠  T34. No-arbitrage calibration order
Always calibrate SHORTEST maturity first, then work outward. Each step uses all previously calibrated rates
as FIXED inputs. Never re-solve earlier rates when extending the tree.

⚠  T35. Tree spacing at Time-2 and Time-3
Three Time-2 rates: i_{LL}, i_{HL} = i_{LL}·e^(2σ), i_{HH} = i_{LL}·e^(4σ). Four Time-3 rates: i_{LLL}, i_{LLH} =
i_{LLL}·e^(2σ), i_{HHL} = i_{LLL}·e^(4σ), i_{HHH} = i_{LLL}·e^(6σ). Spacing geometric across full column.

⚠  T36. Path present value = discount by PATH, not AVERAGE rate
For each path, compound the specific forward rates along that path: PV = CF_t / Π_{s=0..t-1}(1+i_path,s). Do
NOT average the rates along the path and discount once.

⚠  T37. Arbitrage-free for STATIC pricing; equilibrium for DYNAMIC analysis
Arbitrage-free models are best at static snapshot pricing and hedging (derivatives desk use). Equilibrium
models excel at scenario/stress analysis over long horizons because they capture fundamental dynamics, not
just current snapshot.

⚠  T38. Benchmark bonds assumed correctly priced

In building an arbitrage-free tree, we assume benchmark bond prices are CORRECT — the model is built to
reproduce them exactly. If benchmarks are mispriced, the entire calibrated tree inherits those errors.

⚠  T39. Path enumeration uses {H, L} labels
Standard notation for path listing: each position is H (up) or L (down). For 3 steps, 8 paths: HHH, HHL, HLH,
HLL, LHH, LHL, LLH, LLL. Recombining means HHL = HLH = LHH (same terminal node), but pathwise valuation
treats them as distinct paths.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET — LM2

Arbitrage-Free Pricing of an Option-Free Bond
Core
Price = Σ CF_t / (1 + z_t)^t    (discount each CF at its own spot rate)
Bootstrap: 1 = c/(1+z_1) + c/(1+z_2)^2 + ... + (1+c)/(1+z_T)^T  solve for z_T
YTM discounting gives ≠ arbitrage-free value unless yield curve is flat.
Arbitrage types: value additivity (whole ≠ sum of parts); dominance (risk-free payoff must have positive price).

Binomial Tree Mechanics
Spacing & Node Value
Adjacent node spacing:   i_H = i_L × e^(2σ).
Stdev of 1-yr rate (lognormal):   σ_r = i_0 × σ.
Value at a node:   V = [C + 0.5·V_H + 0.5·V_L] / (1 + i).
Equal 0.5/0.5 probabilities up/down in the lognormal model.
Terminal: V = par + final coupon.  Work right-to-left.

Calibration Recipe
Procedure
Fix i_0 to 1-yr spot.
For each next maturity N: pick trial middle rate at Time N-1; derive all other rates at that column using e^(2σ)
spacing; price the N-yr benchmark bond at par via backward induction; iterate middle rate (Solver) until price =
100.
Previously calibrated rates are LOCKED — only the new column's middle rate varies.

At σ = 0, tree collapses to the implied forward curve.
Avg of (i_H, i_L) slightly EXCEEDS implied forward (lognormal skew).

Pathwise & Monte Carlo
Formulas
Pathwise:  Bond value = (1/2^N) × Σ_paths PV_path,   where PV_path = Σ_t CF_t / Π_{s=0..t-1}(1 + i_path,s).
Pascal's Triangle row N gives # paths to each terminal node: {1, C(N,1), C(N,2), …, 1}. Total paths = 2^N.
Monte Carlo: simulate → generate spots → compute path CFs → average PVs across all paths.
Drift adjustment: add constant to every rate on every path so average simulated PV of each benchmark =
market price (makes MC arbitrage-free).
Use Monte Carlo for PATH-DEPENDENT (MBS); backward induction for STATE-DEPENDENT (callable,
putable).

Term Structure Models
The Big Four Short-Rate Models
CIR (equil):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t)dt + σ√r_t · dZ     mean-revert; vol ~ √r; NO negative rates.
Vasicek (equil):   dr_t = k(θ − r_t)dt + σ · dZ     mean-revert; constant vol; CAN be negative.
Ho-Lee (arb-free):   dr_t = θ_t dt + σ dZ           time-dep drift; no mean-revert; CAN be negative.
KWF (arb-free):   d ln(r_t) = θ_t dt + σ dZ         time-dep drift; lognormal rates; NO negative.

Equilibrium vs Arbitrage-Free — Quick Discriminator
Decision rule
Must match current market prices? → ARBITRAGE-FREE (Ho-Lee, KWF).
Long-horizon scenarios with economic fundamentals? → EQUILIBRIUM (CIR, Vasicek).
Prevent negative rates? → CIR (equil) or KWF (arb-free).  Allow negatives? → Vasicek or Ho-Lee.
Real-world probs → equilibrium;  risk-neutral probs → arbitrage-free.
Gauss+ (multi-factor): short (no random, CB-controlled), mid (→long), long (→macro). Hump-shaped vol peaks
at mid.

— End of Notes — LM2: The Arbitrage-Free Valuation Framework —

heading: THE FUNDAMENTAL PRICING EQUATION
body:
FOUNDATIONAL EQUATION (Equation 1)
Pᵢₜ  =  Σₛ₌₁ᴺ   E_t[CFᵢ_{t+s}] / (1 + l_{t,s} + θ_{t,s} + ρⁱ_{t,s})^s     → Price = PV of expected future
cash flows, discounted at (real risk-free rate + expected inflation + risk premium for asset i).

Three Discount-Rate Components
• l_{t,s} — real default-free rate: return on a real, default-free, inflation-linked investment (think
TIPS). Compensation for deferring consumption.
• θ_{t,s} — expected inflation: additional return required on NOMINAL default-free debt to protect
real purchasing power.
• ρⁱ_{t,s} — asset-specific risk premium: additional return for cash-flow uncertainty / default /
liquidity. VARIES by asset class — the "i" superscript.

For ANY asset, economic factors can move price only via (1) cash flows (numerator), (2) rates, expected
inflation, or risk premium (denominator). Every LOS in this reading hangs off one of those three channels.
Expectations Drive Value
Price reflects EXPECTED cash flows conditional on today's information. Only INFORMATION THAT IS A
SURPRISE (true news) moves markets — anticipated info is already priced. This explains "good news,
falling price" outcomes: a strong report can disappoint if expectations were stronger still.
Investor sentiment (enthusiasm, fear) also affects prices — primarily via shifts in the risk premium ρⁱ, and
indirectly via cash flow expectations.

⚠
PRICING EQUATION TRAPS
Economic factors move price through exactly THREE channels: rates, expected inflation, risk
premium, OR cash flows. Memorize. "Good news, price falls" is possible when the actual news
underperformed expectations — the price was already pricing a better outcome.



Page 3  of  24

heading: REAL RATES, GROWTH & VOLATILITY
body:
The real default-free rate, l_{t,s}, emerges from individuals' saving vs consumption decisions. Key
construct: the INTER-TEMPORAL RATE OF SUBSTITUTION m̃ _{t,s} — the ratio of marginal utility of
consumption s periods in future to marginal utility today.
Marginal Utility and Consumption
• Good times → high current consumption → LOW marginal utility of consumption today.
• Bad times → low current consumption → HIGH marginal utility of consumption today.
• Rising wealth → diminishing marginal utility. Investors derive GREATER benefit from assets that pay
off in BAD times.

EQUATION 3 — Pricing a 1-period Real Default-Free Bond
P_{t,1}  =  E_t(m̃ _{t,1})      →  In equilibrium, the bond price equals the expected inter-temporal
rate of substitution.  If price < E(m̃ ), investors buy (more today → less today's consumption →
higher MU today → lower m̃  until balance).

The Real Rate Relationship
Equation 4 (key intuition): l_{t,s} ≈ (1/s) × [s · ḡ − 0.5 · s · σ²] → the real rate is positively related to the trend
growth rate ḡ and positively related to the volatility σ² of growth, BUT as volatility rises beyond some
point, investors demand PRECAUTIONARY SAVING which pushes the safe rate DOWN.

Economy
Expected real rate
Why
High trend growth, stable
HIGHER real rate
Investors need higher return to
defer consumption (future wealth
expected to be plentiful)
Low trend growth, stable
LOWER real rate
Less need to reward saving —
future consumption already looks
modest
High growth volatility
Real rate DROPS (precautionary
saving)
Investors rush into safe assets as a
hedge, bidding prices up and yields
down
Typical developed economies
(US/UK)
~2% real short rate
Curriculum benchmark —
"normal" neutral real rate


Page 4  of  24
✓
Real-rate intuition across two economies
Country A: trend GDP growth 4%, stable → expect real short rate around 2–3%.  Country B:
trend GDP growth 2%, stable → expect real short rate around 1%.  If Country B then enters a
period of high volatility, its real rate falls below 1% as precautionary saving surges and
Treasury/Gilt/TIPS prices rise.

⚠
GROWTH vs VOLATILITY TRAP
Higher trend growth → HIGHER real rate. Higher growth volatility → initially higher risk premium,
but dominated by PRECAUTIONARY SAVING that pushes the SAFE real rate LOWER. The two
effects pull in OPPOSITE directions. Don't mix them up.



Page 5  of  24

heading: NOMINAL RATES, INFLATION & BREAK-EVEN
body:
Nominal default-free rate ≈ real rate + expected inflation + inflation risk premium. Inflation expectations
and the premium for inflation UNCERTAINTY both drive the wedge between nominal and real yields.
Break-Even Inflation Rate (BEI)
BEI DEFINITION
BEI  =  Yield on nominal default-free bond  −  Yield on real (inflation-linked) bond of same
maturity.      BEI = (expected inflation) + (risk premium for inflation uncertainty).

BEI is NOT a pure expected-inflation figure — it also contains an inflation risk premium. When inflation
uncertainty rises, BEI widens even if the central projection for inflation is unchanged.
• US TIPS / UK Gilts: inflation-linked treasuries provide a real yield directly. BEI = nominal Treasury
yield − TIPS yield.
• Why BEI matters to central banks: it's a real-time, market-based gauge of inflation expectations —
independent of surveys.
The Taylor Rule (Equation 12)
TAYLOR RULE
pr_t  =  l_t + ι_t + 0.5·(ι_t − ι*_t) + 0.5·(Y_t − Y*_t)      where  pr_t = policy rate; l_t = neutral real
rate; ι_t = inflation; ι*_t = target inflation; (Y_t − Y*_t) = output gap (log of actual vs potential
real GDP).

Decoding the Taylor Rule
• Neutral policy rate = l_t + ι*_t  (real rate + inflation target). This is the policy rate when inflation is AT
target and output gap is ZERO.
• Inflation coefficient = 1.5 (appears once as ι_t, then again inside the 0.5(ι−ι*) term). Over-weighting
inflation stabilizes prices over time.
• Inflation ABOVE target → policy rate ABOVE neutral (tightening).  Inflation BELOW target → policy
rate below neutral (easing).
• Output gap POSITIVE (economy running hot, Y > Y*) → policy rate ABOVE neutral.  Gap NEGATIVE
(slack) → policy rate below neutral.

✓
Taylor rule worked example (curriculum)

Page 6  of  24
l_t = 2.0%, ι_t = 3.0%, ι*_t = 2.0%, output gap = 2.0%.  pr = 2.0% + 3.0% + 0.5(3.0% − 2.0%) +
0.5(2.0%) = 2.0 + 3.0 + 0.5 + 1.0 = 6.5%.  With inflation 1pp above target and output 2pp above
potential, the appropriate policy rate is 6.5%.

⚠
TAYLOR RULE TRAPS
Neutral rate = real rate + inflation TARGET (not current inflation). Inflation weight is 1.5 total (not
0.5). Output gap coefficient is 0.5. The rule is PRESCRIPTIVE (what policy rate SHOULD be), not
descriptive — central banks don't mechanically follow it.



Page 7  of  24

heading: BUSINESS CYCLE & THE YIELD CURVE
body:
Short-term nominal rates track the central bank's policy rate closely. LONGER maturities add term-
structure risk premiums on top. The SHAPE of the yield curve encodes both rate EXPECTATIONS and
term-maturity risk premiums.
Why Longer Bonds Earn a Term Premium
Even default-free bonds carry PRICE risk prior to maturity — a 10-year bond sold in 2 years has uncertain
interim value. Equation 5/6 shows: P_{t,s} = E_t(P̃ _{t+1,s−1}) / (1+l_{t,1}) + cov_t(P̃ _{t+1,s−1}, m̃ _{t,1}).
• Negative covariance between bond prices and the inter-temporal rate of substitution → bonds are
POOR consumption hedges → require a POSITIVE risk premium.
• Longer-dated bonds have MORE negative covariance → HIGHER term premium. Yield curve
normally slopes UPWARD.
Business Cycle → Yield Curve Shape
Phase
Policy rate
Term structure
Bond performance
Early expansion
Low (still easing)
Steeply upward
Short bonds outperform
(rates still falling)
Mid expansion
Rising to neutral
Upward, flattening
Short maturities decline;
long bonds flat
Late expansion
Above neutral (tight)
Flat or inverted
Long bonds outperform
(rate-cut expectations
form)
Early recession
Cut sharply
Steepening from the front
Short bonds surge as cuts
materialize
Late recession
Near zero / ZLB
Very steep
Long bonds peaked; short
rates rise again soon

✓
Yield curve inversion as a recession signal
An INVERTED yield curve (long yields below short) has preceded most US recessions.
Mechanism: the market is pricing an imminent policy easing, i.e., short rates EXPECTED to fall,
which lowers long yields relative to current short rates. CFA candidates should recognize
inversion as late-cycle.

⚠
YIELD CURVE TRAP

Page 8  of  24
The slope encodes BOTH rate expectations AND a term premium. A flat curve doesn't always
mean "no rate expectations" — it can mean "expected rate cuts offset the normal term
premium." The CLASSIC inversion signals expected policy EASING (not just recession).



Page 9  of  24

heading: CREDIT RISK & BUSINESS CYCLE
body:
Credit Spread — Definition
CREDIT SPREAD
Credit Spread  =  Yield on corporate bond  −  Yield on government bond (same currency &
maturity).    It compensates for DEFAULT risk + LIQUIDITY risk + LOSS GIVEN DEFAULT
expectations, not just default probability.

Credit spreads are COUNTER-CYCLICAL: they WIDEN in recessions (default risk rises, liquidity falls)
and NARROW in expansions.
Business Cycle Effects on Credit
• Expansion: earnings strong, defaults rare, spreads tight, investors reach for yield. HIGH-YIELD
credits outperform.
• Late cycle: spreads tightest just before turning point — CROWDED positioning, weak covenants,
compressed compensation.
• Recession: spreads blow out, defaults spike, investors flee to quality. INVESTMENT-GRADE
outperforms HIGH-YIELD dramatically.
• Early recovery: spreads still wide but narrowing fast — this is when high-yield RETURNS are highest
(carry + compression).
Industry & Company Credit Quality
Not all businesses are equally exposed to the cycle. Product-market characteristics drive credit quality:
• Cyclical industries (autos, capex, luxury): high volatility of cash flows → higher credit risk premium.
Spreads move more during cycles.
• Non-cyclical industries (utilities, staples, healthcare): stable cash flows → lower credit risk
premium, tighter and more stable spreads.
• Competitive structure: commodity producers (price-takers) face more pressure than firms with
pricing power (brands, network effects, scale).
• Operating leverage: high fixed-cost businesses see earnings fall dramatically in downturns — credit
quality deteriorates fast.

✓
Cyclical vs non-cyclical spread behavior
Between 2007 and 2009 US auto and bank high-yield spreads widened by several thousand
basis points. Meanwhile, utility and consumer-staples investment-grade spreads widened 200-
400bp — painful but modest. The difference reflects underlying CASH FLOW volatility tied to
product markets.

Page 10  of  24

⚠
CREDIT CYCLE TRAP
Credit spreads TIGHTEST at late expansion (crowded). WIDEST in early recession. Best HIGH-
YIELD returns occur in EARLY RECOVERY — carry + spread compression.

heading: EQUITIES & EQUITY RISK PREMIUM
body:
Equity claims have uncertain, perpetual cash flows that are HIGHLY correlated with the economy.
Consumption hedging is poor — equities pay off when you need the money LEAST (good times) and
disappoint in recessions. Hence the largest risk premium among major asset classes.
Why Equities Earn a Large Risk Premium
• Cash flows are uncertain AND pro-cyclical: dividends and earnings rise in expansions, fall in
recessions.
• Residual claim: equity holders sit BEHIND bondholders. In bankruptcy, recovery is typically zero.
• Consumption-hedging property is POOR: negative covariance between equity payoffs and
marginal utility → investors demand compensation.
• Historical equity risk premium: ~4-5% annualized in US over 117+ years (equities over government
bonds).

✓
Equity vs credit risk premium sizing
Curriculum data: US equities outperformed US Treasuries by ~4.5% p.a. over 1900-2017.
Equity risk premium > credit risk premium > bond term premium. The ordering reflects
increasing cash-flow uncertainty and weaker consumption-hedging properties as you move up
the capital stack.
Business Cycle → Earnings Growth Expectations
• Expansion: short-term earnings growth rises; long-term expectations extrapolate upward
(sometimes too far — "this time is different").
• Late cycle: short-term growth peaks; analysts start to mark down forward estimates.
• Recession: short-term growth negative; long-term expectations often remain MORE stable (mean
reversion).
• Early recovery: short-term earnings growth surges off low base (high operating leverage) — often
the fastest-earnings-growth phase.
Cyclical Effects on Valuation Multiples
P/E and P/B are PRO-CYCLICAL — they rise in expansions and fall in recessions.

Page 11  of  24
• P/E rises when: real rates fall, equity risk premium falls, earnings growth expectations rise, OR
perceived risk falls.
• P/E falls when: the opposite. During recessions, numerator and denominator can BOTH fall
simultaneously.
• Trailing vs forward P/E: trailing uses LAST year's earnings; forward uses NEXT year's estimate.
When earnings grow, forward P/E < trailing P/E.
• Historical US P/E benchmark: ~13.5 avg 1900-1990. Dot-com peak ~45. Modern norm ~17-20.

⚠
EQUITY / MULTIPLES TRAPS
Equity risk premium > credit premium > term premium — this ORDERING comes directly from
consumption-hedging logic. P/E is PRO-CYCLICAL (rises in expansion). Forward P/E < trailing
P/E when earnings are GROWING. A "high" P/E needs context — it may reflect low rates / low
equity risk premium rather than overvaluation.



Page 12  of  24

heading: COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE
body:
Commercial real estate (CRE) has hybrid characteristics: BOND-like rental income (stable, long-lease) +
EQUITY-like capital values (pro-cyclical). Plus a distinctive LIQUIDITY discount.
The Dual Nature of CRE Returns
Component
Behavior
Similar to
Rental income
Stable (UK CRE averaged 6.5%
nominal / 2.5% real over 30 years)
Investment-grade corporate bonds
Capital values
Highly pro-cyclical — UK CRE fell
30% in early 1990s recession, 55% in
Ireland 2008-09
Equities
Liquidity
Low — transactions are infrequent,
costly, slow
No parallel — unique CRE feature
Risk premium
High and pro-cyclical — closer to
equity premium than bond premium
Equities + liquidity premium

Why CRE Demands a High Premium
• Capital values are pro-cyclical: poor consumption hedge. Prices collapse precisely when investors
most want cash.
• Liquidity premium: CRE can't be sold quickly at fair value — bidders vanish in downturns. Additional
compensation required.
• Operating leverage at property level: vacancies + fixed financing costs amplify downside in
recession.
• Correlation: CRE risk premium moves with equity and corporate-bond risk premiums — not a
diversifier when diversification is most needed.

✓
REIT vs direct CRE
Direct CRE valuation adds a LIQUIDITY DISCOUNT not applied to REITs (which trade publicly).
A given property held through a REIT trades at a higher effective price than the same property
held direct — the liquidity premium on REITs is smaller because shares trade continuously.

⚠
REAL ESTATE TRAPS
CRE rental income is BOND-like (stable); capital values are EQUITY-like (pro-cyclical). Risk
premium is CLOSER TO EQUITY than to bonds, plus an added liquidity premium. REITs trade
publicly → smaller liquidity premium than direct CRE.

body:
The fundamental pricing equation explains all asset classes through the same three channels. This
section consolidates the business-cycle impact across every asset type.

Key Relationships to Remember
• Real rate ↔ trend growth: positive relationship. High-growth economies have higher safe real rates.
• Growth volatility ↔ real rate: negative (precautionary saving). Higher volatility depresses safe rate.
• Inflation ↑ over target → policy rate ↑ over neutral. Taylor rule mechanics.
• Output gap positive → policy rate ↑ over neutral. Economy running hot signals inflation pressure
ahead.
• Term premium ↑ with maturity: longer bonds are poorer consumption hedges. Yield curve normally
slopes up.
• Credit spreads counter-cyclical: widen in recessions, narrow in expansions. Best HY returns in
early recovery.
• P/E pro-cyclical: rises in expansion via falling rates / falling ERP / rising growth expectations.
• CRE dual nature: bond-like income + equity-like capital values + liquidity premium.
Exam Strategy for This Module
Most exam questions on LM1 test ONE of three things: (1) identifying which discount-rate component
changed — real rate, expected inflation, or risk premium; (2) applying the Taylor rule to compute a policy
rate; (3) matching a business-cycle phase to its effect on yield curve shape, spreads, or multiples.

Page 15  of  24
Memorize the ORDERING of risk premiums (equity > credit > term > inflation) — many questions test this
directly.



Page 16  of  24
MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
Three pricing channels
Economic factors move price via exactly THREE levers: (1) real default-free rate, (2) expected
inflation, (3) risk premium — plus cash flows themselves. Every LOS hangs off one of these.

⚠
News vs expectations
Markets price EXPECTED outcomes. Only SURPRISES (deviations from consensus) move
prices. "Good news + falling price" occurs when the actual news underperformed already-
strong expectations.

⚠
Investor sentiment channel
Sentiment mostly affects DISCOUNT RATES (via the risk premium) — not cash flows directly.
Enthusiasm compresses premiums; fear widens them.

⚠
Real rate and trend growth
Real short rates are POSITIVELY related to TREND real growth. Higher expected growth →
higher real rate (need more compensation to defer consumption).

⚠
Volatility pulls real rates DOWN
Higher growth VOLATILITY raises precautionary SAVING, which pushes the safe real rate
DOWN. Growth level and volatility pull in OPPOSITE directions on the real rate.

⚠
Inter-temporal rate of substitution
m̃ _{t,s} = ratio of marginal utility of future consumption to marginal utility today. Today's bond
price equals E(m̃ ). When everyone rushes to save, m̃  rises → bond price rises → yield falls.

⚠
Consumption hedging logic
Assets that pay off in BAD times (when MU of consumption is high) are valuable hedges → LOW
risk premium. Assets that pay off in GOOD times (equities, CRE) are POOR hedges → HIGH risk
premium.

⚠
Asset ordering of risk premiums
Equity premium > credit premium > term premium > inflation risk premium. Follows directly
from consumption-hedging quality and cash-flow uncertainty.

Page 17  of  24




Page 18  of  24
⚠
Break-even inflation
BEI = nominal yield − real yield = expected inflation + inflation risk premium. NOT pure expected
inflation.

⚠
Taylor rule formula
pr_t = l_t + ι_t + 0.5(ι_t − ι*_t) + 0.5(Y_t − Y*_t). Inflation weight is 1.5 overall (not 0.5). Output
gap weight is 0.5.

⚠
Neutral policy rate
Neutral rate = real rate + INFLATION TARGET (not current inflation). When inflation = target
AND output gap = 0, policy rate = neutral.

⚠
Term premium intuition
Long bonds pay off POORLY in recessions if rates rise unexpectedly → poor consumption hedge
→ POSITIVE term premium. Yield curve normally slopes UP even with flat rate expectations.

⚠
Yield curve inversion
Inverted curve = market expects imminent rate CUTS. Long yields < short yields. Historically a
LATE-CYCLE signal preceding recessions.

⚠
Business cycle → bond performance
Early expansion: short bonds win. Late expansion: long bonds win (cut expectations). Early
recession: short bonds surge as cuts materialize.

⚠
Credit spreads — cyclicality
Spreads WIDEN in recessions (counter-cyclical), NARROW in expansions. Tightest at LATE
expansion (crowded). Widest in EARLY recession.

⚠
Credit return timing
Best HIGH-YIELD returns occur in EARLY RECOVERY — carry + spread compression. High-
yield does WORST in early recession (spread widening + defaults).

⚠
Industry cyclicality → credit
Cyclical industries (autos, capex) have volatile cash flows → HIGHER credit risk premium. Non-
cyclical (utilities, staples) → LOWER premium.


Page 19  of  24



Page 20  of  24
⚠
Pricing power and credit
Firms with pricing power (brands, network effects, scale) maintain margins through cycles →
lower credit risk. Commodity producers suffer most.

⚠
Operating leverage
High-fixed-cost businesses see earnings swing dramatically. Operating leverage amplifies both
upside and downside — translates into bigger credit-spread moves.

⚠
Equity risk premium size
Historical US ERP ~4-5% p.a. over Treasuries. Reflects poor consumption-hedging plus
residual-claim risk.

⚠
Earnings growth across cycle
Short-term growth: highly pro-cyclical (fastest in EARLY RECOVERY off low base). Long-term
growth: more stable (mean-reverts).

⚠
P/E is pro-cyclical
P/E RISES in expansions, FALLS in recessions. "High" P/E may reflect falling rates / falling ERP /
rising growth expectations — not necessarily overvaluation.

⚠
Trailing vs forward P/E
Trailing P/E uses last year's EPS. Forward P/E uses next year's estimate. If earnings grow →
forward P/E < trailing P/E.

⚠
Drivers of P/E expansion
Falling real rates; falling equity risk premium; rising earnings growth expectations; falling
operating/financial risk. Usually multiple drivers at once.




Page 21  of  24
⚠
CRE dual nature
Rental income is BOND-like (stable); capital values are EQUITY-like (pro-cyclical). Combined
risk profile closer to equity than bonds.

⚠
CRE liquidity premium
Direct CRE is illiquid — adds a LIQUIDITY premium on top of the pro-cyclical-price premium.
REITs are public → smaller liquidity premium.

⚠
CRE capital values vs recession
Capital values can fall 30-55% in severe recessions (UK early 90s, Ireland 08-09). Rental
income far more stable. Don't confuse the two income streams.

⚠
Diversification disappointment
Credit, equity, and CRE risk premiums are POSITIVELY CORRELATED — all widen together in
recessions. Cross-asset diversification weakens precisely when you most need it.

⚠
Default-free ≠ risk-free
Even default-free government bonds carry TERM risk (price risk before maturity) and
INFLATION risk. Only a 1-period real bond is truly risk-free in real terms.

⚠
Liquidity isn't free
Liquidity risk premium appears in CRE and low-grade credit especially. Illiquid assets looked
great in 2007 — and then vanished bid-side in 2008-09.

⚠
Output gap concept
Output gap = Y_t − Y*_t (log of actual vs potential real GDP). Positive gap → economy running
hot, inflation pressure builds. Negative gap → slack / unemployment.

⚠
Policy rate vs neutral rate
Central banks TIGHTEN above neutral when inflation above target OR output positive. EASE
below neutral when opposite. The policy rate is adjusted BOTH when inflation misses AND
when output gap changes.

⚠
TIPS yield = real yield
US TIPS pay principal + coupon adjusted for CPI. TIPS yield is a direct real rate. Subtract TIPS
yield from nominal Treasury yield to get BEI.

Page 22  of  24

⚠
Sovereign credit risk
Developed-market governments default-free in their own currency (can print). Emerging
markets issuing in foreign currency face real default risk.

⚠
Risk-neutral valuation
If bond investors were risk-neutral, term structure would reflect ONLY expected future short
rates. Observed yield curves reveal that investors ARE risk-averse — positive term premium
exists.

⚠
Stochastic discount factor
The inter-temporal rate m̃  is also called the STOCHASTIC DISCOUNT FACTOR or PRICING
KERNEL. Same quantity, different names.

⚠
Decreasing absolute risk aversion
Wealthier investors have decreasing absolute risk aversion — they take on MORE risk as wealth
rises. In aggregate this lowers safe asset prices and raises the safe-asset yield.




Page 23  of  24
FORMULAS REFERENCE SHEET

Fundamental Pricing Equation (Eq. 1)
Pᵢₜ  =  Σ_{s=1}^N   E_t[CFᵢ_{t+s}]  /  (1 + l_{t,s} + θ_{t,s} + ρⁱ_{t,s})^s
Components: l = real default-free rate | θ = expected inflation | ρⁱ = asset-specific risk premium | CF =
cash flow.
Inter-Temporal Rate of Substitution (Eq. 3)
P_{t,1}  =  E_t(m̃ _{t,1})      →  1-period real default-free bond price equals expected inter-temporal rate of
substitution.  m̃  rises in bad times (high marginal utility of current consumption) and falls in good times.
Taylor Rule (Eq. 12)
pr_t  =  l_t + ι_t + 0.5·(ι_t − ι*_t) + 0.5·(Y_t − Y*_t)
Neutral rate = l_t + ι*_t. Inflation coefficient = 1.5. Output-gap coefficient = 0.5.
Break-Even Inflation
BEI  =  Yield on nominal default-free bond  −  Yield on real default-free bond (TIPS / linker).
BEI = expected inflation + inflation risk premium. Not a pure expected-inflation measure.

Worked Example — BEI Decomposition
Suppose US 10-year Treasury yields 4.5% and US 10-year TIPS yields 2.0%.  BEI = 4.5% − 2.0% = 2.5%.
If consumer surveys suggest market-expected 10-year inflation is ~2.2%, then:
Inflation risk premium ≈ BEI − expected inflation = 2.5% − 2.2% = 0.3% (30 bps). As inflation uncertainty
rises, BEI widens even when the central inflation projection is unchanged.
Credit Spread
Credit Spread  =  Corporate bond yield  −  Government bond yield (same currency, same maturity).
Counter-cyclical: widens in recessions, narrows in expansions.
Two-Period Bond Pricing (Eq. 5 and 6)
P_{t,s}  =  E_t(P̃ _{t+1,s−1} · m̃ _{t,1})  =  E_t(P̃ _{t+1,s−1}) / (1 + l_{t,1})  +  cov_t(P̃ _{t+1,s−1}, m̃ _{t,1})
The covariance term is the TERM RISK PREMIUM. Negative covariance → positive required premium →
upward-sloping yield curve.
Asset Risk Premium Ordering
Premium
Typical size
Driver
Inflation risk premium
~30-50 bps
Uncertainty about future inflation
Term premium (gov bonds)
~100 bps (10y)
Longer-dated bonds are poor consumption
hedges

Page 24  of  24
Premium
Typical size
Driver
Credit premium (IG corp)
~100-200 bps
Default risk + liquidity + loss given default
Credit premium (high yield)
~400-700 bps
Higher default probability; cyclical cash flows
Equity risk premium
~400-500 bps
Residual-claim + poor consumption hedge +
CF uncertainty
CRE total risk premium
~500-700 bps
Equity-like + liquidity premium on top

All premiums tend to WIDEN TOGETHER in recessions and narrow together in expansions — cross-
asset diversification breaks down precisely when most needed.

End of Notes — LM1 Economics and Investment Markets

Decomposition — Asset Allocation vs Security Selection
DECOMPOSITION (Eq 4)
R_A = Σ_j Δw_j · R_{B,j}   +   Σ_j w_{P,j} · R_{A,j}     →  value added from ASSET ALLOCATION
(active weights on asset classes times benchmark class returns) + value added from
SECURITY SELECTION (portfolio weights on each class times within-class active return).

The first term captures TOP-DOWN decisions (overweighting stocks vs bonds). The second term
captures BOTTOM-UP decisions (picking stocks within the stock allocation). Both contribute to total
active return.

⚠
VALUE ADDED TRAPS
Active weights ALWAYS sum to zero. A positive weight elsewhere is funded by a negative
weight. Alpha in theory is BETA-ADJUSTED (R_P − β_P·R_B); in practice it's often used loosely
as "active return" assuming β = 1. Zero-sum property applies when benchmark = FULL market
— narrower benchmarks break zero-sum.



Page 4  of  17

check: IC·√BR = 0.20·√4 = 0.40. ✓


Page 7  of  17
With CONSTRAINTS (long-only, turnover), actual active weights Δw_i differ from Δw_i*. In Example 6 the
constrained weights produce R_A = 2.1% at the same σ_A = 9%, TC = 0.58. Law check: TC·IC·√BR·σ_A =

⚠
FUNDAMENTAL LAW TRAPS
IC is the CORRELATION between forecasts and outcomes — values of 0.05–0.15 are STRONG
in practice. Small IC is NOT a mistake. √BR (square root of breadth) — independent decisions
matter quadratically less. TC < 1 when constraints bind. In unconstrained portfolios, IR is
invariant to σ_A; in constrained portfolios, IR FALLS as σ_A rises (TC drops).



Page 8  of  17

heading: ). Unconstrained IRs:
body:
• Security selection: IR = IC·√BR = 0.05·√100 = 0.50.
• Sector selection: IR = 0.15·√9 = 0.45.
With TC = 0.60 on security selection (long-only + turnover limits), IR drops to 0.60·0.05·√100 = 0.30, with
expected R_A = 0.30·(3%) = 0.90%. Constraints can easily eliminate the breadth advantage of broad
security selection.
Market Timing — Low-Breadth Trap
Market timing (stocks vs cash, or credit on/off) has VERY LOW BREADTH — often just 4 quarterly
decisions per year (BR = 4). Even with IC = 0.10 (55% correct), unconstrained IR = 0.10·√4 = 0.20 —
modest at best.
Curriculum example: credit-risk timing between IG bonds (7% vol) and HY bonds (15% vol). Active vol of
spread trade ≈ 7.6%. With IC = 0.10 and σ_A capped at 2%, quarterly R_A ≈ 0.263·(IC·σ·score) =

heading: ·3.8% = 10 bps. Annualized: 40 bps. IR = 0.20. Far below security-selection IRs.
body:
Going from quarterly to MONTHLY decisions could raise IR to 0.10·√12 = 0.35 — IF the monthly signals
are TRULY INDEPENDENT (not the same signal repeated). Daily independent signals could theoretically
push IR to 1.58 — but realistically, transaction costs and score-persistence make this a mirage.

BETTER DIFFERENTIATED across assets. A forecast that assigns similar scores to correlated

Common Exam Patterns
Question Type
What to Compute / Reason Through
Calculate R_A given weights
Apply Eq 3: R_A = Σ Δw_i · R_{Ai}. Active weights must sum to zero.
Compare two funds' IR
Higher IR = better manager. Combined SR = √(SR_B² + IR²).
Apply basic fundamental law
IR* = IC·√BR. Expected R_A = IC·√BR·σ_A.
Apply full fundamental law
IR = TC·IC·√BR. Expected R_A = TC·IC·√BR·σ_A.
Determine optimal σ_A
σ_A* = TC·(IR*/SR_B)·σ_B. Blend active fund with benchmark to hit
target.
Market-timing breadth problem
BR = # independent decisions. Quarterly → BR = 4. IR = IC·√BR is
small.
Cross-sectional breadth
BR ≈ N / [1 + (N−1)·ρ]. Correlated bets reduce effective BR.
Constraint impact
Long-only / turnover caps lower TC, lower IR, lower optimal σ_A.



Page 13  of  17
MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
Active weights sum to zero
Σ Δw_i = 0 across all securities. A positive weight on one security is funded by a negative weight
elsewhere. This is a cross-section identity.

⚠
Zero-sum property conditions
Active management is zero-sum BEFORE COSTS only when benchmark = full investable
market. Narrow benchmarks (sector ETF, region ETF) break zero-sum.

⚠
Alpha vs active return
Alpha (formally) = R_P − β_P·R_B. Active return = R_P − R_B. They're equal only if β_P = 1.
Market usage blurs this — watch the question wording.

⚠
Value added decomposition
R_A = Σ Δw_j·R_{B,j} + Σ w_{P,j}·R_{A,j}. First term = asset allocation value add. Second =
security selection within asset classes.

⚠
Sharpe uses R_F; IR uses R_B
Sharpe: reward per unit of TOTAL risk, excess over risk-free. IR: reward per unit of ACTIVE risk,
excess over benchmark. Different denominators entirely.

⚠
Sharpe invariance to leverage
SR doesn't change when you add cash or leverage. Two-fund separation: pick highest SR
portfolio, adjust risk via cash blend.

⚠
Closet indexer signatures
Low active share; IR near zero or slightly negative (fees drag). Sharpe close to benchmark.
Active share = ½ Σ |Δw_i|.

⚠
Market-neutral special case
For a β=0 long/short fund benchmarked to risk-free: SR = IR exactly. Excess return = active
return; total risk = active risk.


Page 14  of  17
⚠
Basic fundamental law
IR* = IC·√BR.  E(R_A) = IC·√BR·σ_A. Unconstrained.




Page 15  of  17
⚠
Full fundamental law
IR = TC·IC·√BR.  E(R_A) = TC·IC·√BR·σ_A. Constrained.

⚠
IC magnitudes
Real IC values are SMALL — 0.02 to 0.15 is realistic; 0.20+ is exceptional; 0.30+ is almost
certainly overconfidence. Don't be fooled by a low-looking IC.

⚠
Breadth isn't just N
Correlated bets reduce effective BR. Persistent signals reduce time BR. True BR ≤ N · periods.

⚠
Breadth formula
BR ≈ N / [1 + (N−1)·ρ] where ρ is avg off-diagonal correlation. NEGATIVE ρ → BR > N (arbitrage-
type situations).

⚠
Grinold rule
μ_i = IC · σ_i · S_i. Scales standardized scores into expected active returns.

⚠
Optimal active weight formula
Δw_i* = (μ_i/σ_i²) · σ_A/(IC·√BR). Higher forecast → larger weight; higher vol → smaller weight.

⚠
Transfer coefficient range
TC ∈ [−1, +1]. Typical 0.20–0.90. TC = 1 = no binding constraints. TC = 0 = zero optimal active
risk — invest in benchmark.

⚠
Unconstrained IR invariant to σ_A
In unconstrained portfolios, doubling σ_A doubles R_A but leaves IR unchanged. In
CONSTRAINED portfolios, IR FALLS as σ_A rises (TC drops).

⚠
Optimal σ_A with constraints
σ_A = TC · (IR*/SR_B) · σ_B. Lower TC means lower optimal σ_A.

⚠
Combined Sharpe ratio
SR²_P = SR²_B + TC²·(IR*)² with constraints. SR²_P = SR²_B + IR² without.


Page 16  of  17
⚠
Ex post variance decomposition
TC² share of variance ≈ skill variation; (1 − TC²) share ≈ constraint noise. TC = 0.60 → 36% skill /
64% noise.

⚠
Market timing low-breadth problem
Quarterly timing: BR = 4. Even IC = 0.10 yields IR = 0.10·√4 = 0.20. Fundamentally limited by
how often you can make an independent call.

⚠
Rebalancing frequency trap
More frequent rebalancing only helps IR if successive signals are INDEPENDENT. Repeating the
same view doesn't add breadth.

⚠
Asymmetric scores raise effective IC
Scores that differentiate correlated assets raise the effective IC in the law. Same-sign scores
across correlated assets collapse effectively to a single decision.

⚠
Fixed income breadth subtleties
Negative correlations between short and long maturities can push BR above N. But almost all
bonds share duration risk — true independence is rare.

⚠
Skill uncertainty cuts realized IR
Qian-Hua: realized IR ≈ 45–91% of naïve fundamental-law estimate due to uncertainty in IC
over time.

⚠
Normality assumption
Fundamental law inherits mean-variance assumptions. Fat tails, default jumps, and non-
linearities in fixed income and derivatives violate this.

⚠
Manager selection criterion
Rank managers by EXPECTED IR, not raw returns. Higher IR = more incremental Sharpe when
blended with benchmark.

⚠
Active share vs active risk
Active share = structural difference. Active risk = realized tracking error. High active share
doesn't guarantee high active risk (diversified deviations cancel).


Page 17  of  17
⚠
Transaction costs absent from law
Basic law ignores trading costs. Net IR can be very different from gross IR for high-turnover
strategies.

⚠
TC can be negative
Possible if the portfolio drifted — optimal weights negatively correlated with current holdings.


FORMULAS REFERENCE SHEET

Value Added and Active Weights
R_A = R_P − R_B = Σ Δw_i · R_i = Σ Δw_i · R_{Ai}       where Δw_i = w_{P,i} − w_{B,i}    (active weights sum to
0)
Decomposition:  R_A = Σ_j Δw_j · R_{B,j}  +  Σ_j w_{P,j} · R_{A,j}       (asset allocation + security selection)
Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio
SR = (R_P − R_F) / σ_P             IR = R_A / σ_A = (R_P − R_B) / σ(R_P − R_B)
Combined:  SR²_combined = SR²_B + IR²     (unconstrained)     SR²_combined = SR²_B + TC²·(IR*)²
(constrained)
Optimal Active Risk
Unconstrained:  σ*_A = (IR* / SR_B) · σ_B       Constrained:  σ*_A = TC · (IR* / SR_B) · σ_B
Fundamental Law
Basic:  IR* = IC · √BR       E(R_A) = IC · √BR · σ_A
Full:   IR  = TC · IC · √BR   E(R_A) = TC · IC · √BR · σ_A
Grinold rule: μ_i = IC · σ_i · S_i       Optimal weights: Δw_i* = (μ_i/σ_i²) · σ_A/(IC·√BR)
Breadth Approximation
BR ≈ N / [1 + (N−1)·ρ]       ρ = average off-diagonal correlation of active returns.       ρ < 0 can yield BR > N.
Ex Post / Conditional Performance
E(R_A | IC_R) = TC · IC_R · √BR · σ_A      Realized R_A = E(R_A | IC_R) + Noise
Variance of realized R_A: TC² share from skill variation; (1 − TC²) share from constraint-induced noise.
Active Share
Active Share = ½ · Σ_i |Δw_i|     →  0 for benchmark replica; 1 for completely different portfolio (after abs-
value adjustment).

End of Notes — LM2 Analysis of Active Portfolio Management

heading: CREATION/REDEMPTION MECHANICS
body:
The defining feature of ETFs is their two-market structure. Understanding how shares move between
investors (secondary market) and between authorized participants and the ETF issuer (primary market) is
essential to understanding every other aspect — tracking error, spreads, premiums/discounts, costs,
and risks.
ETF vs Mutual Fund — Structural Difference
Feature
Mutual Fund
ETF
Trading
End-of-day only, at closing NAV
Intraday on exchange, like a stock
Share creation
Cash-for-shares with fund manager
In-kind securities-for-shares between AP
and issuer
Access to issuer
Any retail investor (via broker)
Only AUTHORIZED PARTICIPANTS in
primary market
Transaction cost
bearer
ALL shareholders bear costs of new
flows
Only TRADING investors bear costs (via
bid-ask spread)

The Two Markets
• Primary market: OTC creation/redemption between AUTHORIZED PARTICIPANTS (APs) and the
ETF issuer. In-kind: AP delivers basket of securities to receive new ETF shares, or delivers shares to
receive basket.
• Secondary market: exchange trading between investors and market makers. Only way retail and
most institutional investors transact. ETF issuer is NOT involved in secondary trades — no money
flows to the fund.
Key Components
• Authorized Participants (APs): large broker-dealers authorized by the ETF issuer. Only APs can
create or redeem shares. Typically also market makers.
• Creation basket: list of required in-kind securities published daily by the issuer (usually mirrors fund
holdings). Also used to calculate intraday NAV (iNAV).
• Creation unit: large block traded between AP and issuer — typically 50,000 shares (range 10,000 to
600,000).
• Redemption basket: securities delivered to AP on redemption. May differ from creation basket —
issuer can target LOW-COST-BASIS securities to enhance tax efficiency.
Arbitrage Mechanism — The Price Discipline
ETF ARBITRAGE

Page 3  of  18
ETF trading at PREMIUM (price > iNAV):  AP SELLS ETF shares in market + BUYS creation
basket → creates new shares → profits from spread, pushes ETF price DOWN and basket price
UP until premium closes.     ETF at DISCOUNT (price < iNAV):  AP BUYS ETF shares in market +
SELLS basket → redeems shares → profits, pushes ETF price UP until discount closes.

✓
Worked example — premium arbitrage
ETF trading at $25.10 on exchange. iNAV (based on basket) = $25.00. AP buys the basket at
$25.00 per ETF-equivalent and simultaneously sells ETF shares at $25.10. Captures $0.10 per
share before fees. Does this until spread closes. Net effect: selling pressure on ETF pushes
price down, buying pressure on basket pushes component prices up — they converge.

The arbitrage GAP (the price deviation that justifies AP action) varies with liquidity of underlying securities
and trading costs. For liquid US equity ETFs the gap can be as tight as one tick ($0.01). For illiquid ETFs
(high-yield bonds, exotic commodities) the gap can be 1% or more. Within that gap, the ETF trades
without triggering arbitrage.
Key Advantages of In-Kind Creation/Redemption
• Cost externalization: APs absorb trading costs of basket; these are passed to ETF traders via bid-
ask spread. Buy-and-hold ETF shareholders are SHIELDED from other investors' flows.
• Tax efficiency: in-kind redemptions are NOT taxable sales. Issuer can deliver out LOWEST-COST-
BASIS lots, raising avg cost basis of remaining holdings → minimizes capital gain distributions.
• Price discipline: arbitrage keeps ETF price near fair value — no mutual-fund-style end-of-day NAV
strike required.
• Cash creation/redemption exception: for illiquid holdings (high-yield munis, swaps in
leveraged/inverse/commodity ETFs), issuers may accept CASH instead of in-kind. Trades off lower
AP friction against some loss of tax efficiency.
Trading & Settlement
• US: T+2 settlement via NSCC (National Securities Clearing Corporation, guarantor) and DTC
(Depository Trust Company, book of accounts). Continuous net settlement nets offsetting trades.
Market makers get up to 6 days to settle.
• Europe: fragmented — 29 central securities depositories (CSDs), multiple exchanges, multiple
share classes. Majority of trades negotiated OTC; published trades reported but not live. Cross-
listings common. Higher implicit trading costs.

⚠
CREATION/REDEMPTION TRAPS
Only APs transact in the primary market — retail investors NEVER deal with the issuer directly.
Creation/redemption is IN-KIND (usually), not cash-for-shares. Flows from investor trades DO

Page 4  of  18
NOT affect non-transacting ETF shareholders. Arbitrage gap, not size of creation unit,
determines when APs step in. ETFs trade IN SECONDARY MARKET; primary market only for AP
transactions.



Page 5  of  18

heading: TRACKING ERROR
body:
Most ETFs (98% of US ETF AUM) are index-tracking. Investors care whether the fund delivers its stated
benchmark return. Tracking difference is measured using daily return differences or 12-month rolling
comparisons.
Measurement
• Daily tracking difference: fund NAV return − index return, measured each day.
• Rolling 12-month tracking difference: comparable to expense ratio, shows persistent
underperformance. Include both central tendency (mean/median) AND variability (stdev or range).
• Benchmark expectation: all else equal, annual tracking difference ≈ expense ratio (fund
underperforms by fee amount).

Seven Sources of Tracking Error
Source
Direction
Notes
Fees & expenses
Negative (always)
Index assumes frictionless trading
— fund fees reduce return.
Baseline drag.
Representative sampling /
optimization
Positive OR negative
Holding subset of index
constituents. Widens tracking
range (e.g., GMF 1.95% vs EEM
0.57%). Increases error
magnitude.
Depositary receipts & other ETFs
Positive OR negative
Different exchange hours → intra-
day price divergence from
underlying.
Index changes
Positive OR negative
Fund may trade index rebalances
at different prices/times than index
methodology.
Fund accounting practices
Positive OR negative
FX valuation conventions, fixed-
income fair-value methods differ
from index.
Regulatory & tax requirements
Usually negative
Foreign dividend withholding —
fund may recover less than index
assumes.

Page 6  of  18
Source
Direction
Notes

recapture
POSITIVE
"Negative costs" — lending
income offsets fees and can make
fund OUTPERFORM benchmark.

EEM vs EMI example: median annual tracking difference of 0.79% vs 0.69% expense ratio — only 10 bps
above fee. Range: 0.38% to 0.95%. Low sampling fund. GMF (sampled heavily) vs its index: range
~1.95%, nearly 4× EEM — heavy optimization tradeoff.

✓
EEM vs GMF comparison
EEM (iShares MSCI Emerging Markets) holds 900 of 1,150 index constituents — light sampling,
median tracking -0.79%, range 0.57%. GMF (SPDR Emerging Asia Pacific) holds only 763 of
2,342 constituents — heavy sampling, median cap $2.8B vs $0.7B for index. Wider range of
outcomes. Key lesson: higher optimization = wider tracking range and regime-sensitive
performance.



Page 7  of  18

heading: BID-ASK SPREADS & PREMIUMS/DISCOUNTS
body:
Bid-Ask Spread Drivers
ETF SPREAD (upper bound)
Spread  ≤  ± Creation/redemption fees + direct trading costs     +  Bid-ask spreads of underlying
securities     +  Market maker risk premium (hedging/carry costs until close)     +  Market maker
desired profit (subject to competition)     −  Discount for likelihood of offsetting order flow in
short time

The last term (offsetting flow) is crucial. For SPY or IWM, two-way flow is continuous — market makers
match orders quickly and skip the underlying-basket side entirely. Result: ETF spread can be TIGHTER
than the combined spread of its underlying securities.
What Widens Spreads
• Illiquid or non-overlapping underlying markets: foreign stocks, high-yield bonds, exotic
commodities. Market maker cannot hedge cheaply.
• Different time zones: underlying closed when ETF trades. AP must wait to complete basket side.
• Low ETF volume: market maker has to warehouse positions; compensates via wider spread.
• Volatility spikes or event risk: spreads widen around announcements and market stress.

Typical US ETF Spreads by Category (asset-weighted)
Category
Avg spread
Driver
US Equity
0.03%
Deepest liquidity, continuous 2-way flow
US Fixed Income
0.02%
Treasuries and IG — still liquid despite OTC
underlying
International Equity
0.05%
Time-zone mismatch with underlying
markets
International Fixed Income
0.06%
Dealer markets, FX overlay
Commodities
0.05%
Futures-based; roll costs embedded
Leveraged
0.29%
Daily swap reset; highest cost
Inverse
0.10%
Swap-based; smaller AUM
Asset Allocation / Alternatives
0.18% – 0.21%
Small AUM, thin flow, complex hedging

Premiums & Discounts to NAV

Page 8  of  18
PREMIUM/DISCOUNT FORMULAS
End-of-day:  (ETF price − NAV per share) / NAV per share     Intraday:  (ETF price − iNAV per
share) / iNAV per share     →  positive = PREMIUM (ETF more expensive than underlying);
negative = DISCOUNT.

Sources of Premiums/Discounts
• Timing differences: NAV may use 3pm futures close for commodities valued at 4pm local, or Asia
close when US ETF trades until 4pm ET. Premium/discount reflects POST-NAV news — not
mispricing.
• Stale pricing in fixed income: bonds trade infrequently in dealer markets. NAV relies on pricing
services that extrapolate from similar bonds. ETF price can be MORE accurate — "price discovery"
feature.
• Infrequent ETF trading: if ETF last traded at 1pm and NAV strikes at 4pm, comparing the two
creates artificial premium/discount. Some providers use last midpoint instead of last trade.
• After-hours / closed underlying markets: US ETFs holding European stocks trade after Europe
closes. Premium/discount reflects the market's BEST GUESS of where Europe would open.
• Demand imbalances: sustained net demand (e.g., HYG over 12 months with 0.20% median
premium) means investors consistently paid above fair value. Arbitrage band is wider when
underlying is illiquid.

⚠
SPREAD / PREMIUM TRAPS
An ETF premium doesn't always mean overpricing — it often reflects that the ETF has the
freshest price data while NAV uses stale inputs. In stressed fixed-income markets, liquid ETFs
become PRICE DISCOVERY vehicles. ETF spread can be TIGHTER than underlying spreads
when two-way flow is strong. Large trades (>10% of ADV) may need capital-markets-desk
negotiation, not quoted spread.

heading: TOTAL COST OF OWNERSHIP
body:
Total cost depends on HOLDING PERIOD. Trading costs (commissions, spreads, premium/discount) are
ONE-TIME and fixed regardless of holding period. Management fees and turnover costs ACCUMULATE
over time.
