Round-Trip Trading Cost Formula
ROUND-TRIP COST
Round-trip % = (One-way commission × 2)  +  (½ × bid-ask spread × 2)     Holding period cost %
= Round-trip trading cost  +  Management fee × (months / 12)

✓
Worked example — holding period costs
ETF: annual fee 0.15%, one-way commission 0.05%, bid-ask 0.15%. Round-trip = (0.05 × 2) +
(½ × 0.15 × 2) = 0.10% + 0.15% = 0.25%.     3-month hold: 0.25% + (3/12)(0.15%) = 0.29%.
12-month hold: 0.25% + 0.15% = 0.40%.     3-year hold: 0.25% + (36/12)(0.15%) = 0.70%.
Trading costs dominate for short holds (86% of 3-month cost); management fees dominate
over 3 years (64% of cost).
Implications by Investor Type
• Tactical / short-term traders: optimize on LIQUIDITY — tight bid-ask spread, high volume, small
premium/discount. Management fee is secondary.
• Strategic / buy-and-hold: optimize on MANAGEMENT FEE and tracking error. Spreads amortize
over years.
• Large trades (>10% ADV): use capital markets desk, negotiated pricing. Quoted spread doesn't
apply.



Page 10  of  18

heading: ETF RISKS
body:
Counterparty Risk (Highest in ETNs)
• Exchange-Traded Notes (ETNs): UNSECURED DEBT obligations of issuing bank, NOT funds. No
underlying holdings. Default risk theoretically 100% on issuer bankruptcy. Lehman 2008 — three
ETNs wiped out. Monitor via issuer's CDS spreads.
• Deposit-based currency ETFs: rely on offshore bank deposits for non-USD exposure. Bear bank
default risk.
• Swap-based ETFs: leveraged, inverse, synthetic-replication. Counterparty failure can impair
returns.
• Securities lending risk: LOW. Borrowers post collateral (102% for same-currency, 105%
international). No shareholder losses in ETF history. Income typically credited back to shareholders.
Fund Closure Risk
ETF closures trigger taxable capital gain events and force replacement investment. Causes:
• Regulation: commodity position limits, ETN bans (Israel 2018 banned ETN structure — 700
products forced to convert).
• Competition: low AUM and low trading volume → issuer shuts down unprofitable products.
• Corporate actions: ETF family mergers lead to rationalization.
• Creation/redemption halts (soft closure): ETN issuer stops creations → ARBITRAGE BREAKS
DOWN. MORL in 2018 traded at >5% premium after UBS halted sales.
• Change in investment strategy (soft closure): issuer changes index — sometimes complete
overhaul, shifting exposure by country/sector/asset class.
Investor-Related Risk — Leveraged & Inverse ETFs
Daily-reset mechanics create COMPOUNDING DRIFT over multi-day holds. Investor's cumulative return
≠ (multiplier × index cumulative return) for holds > 1 day.

✓
Curriculum example — 2× inverse over 3 days
Index: 100 → 110 (+10%) → 99 (−10%). Cumulative index return: (99−100)/100 = −1%.     Naive
expectation for −2× ETF: +2%.     Actual: Day 2 NAV = 100 × (1 − 0.20) = 80. Day 3 NAV = 80 × (1
+ 0.20) = 96. Cumulative ETF return: (96−100)/100 = −4%, NOT +2%.     Difference driven by
daily reset + volatility drag. Not suitable for holds > 1 month without active rebalancing.

⚠
ETF RISK TRAPS
ETNs carry FULL credit risk of issuer — they are not funds. Securities lending has never caused
ETF shareholder loss. Leveraged/inverse ETFs are DAILY-return products — cumulative return

Page 11  of  18
diverges from (multiple × index return) over multi-day holds due to volatility drag. Creation halts
on ETNs break arbitrage → wide premiums. Soft closures (index change) can silently transform
your exposure.



Page 12  of  18

heading: PORTFOLIO APPLICATIONS
body:
Three broad application categories: (1) portfolio EFFICIENCY, (2) asset-class EXPOSURE management,
(3) ACTIVE and FACTOR investing.
(1) Portfolio Efficiency Applications
Application
Role
Example / Benchmark
Cash equitization /
liquidity management
Minimize cash drag, invest idle cash fully
Liquid ETF matched to fund
benchmark
Portfolio rebalancing
Single-trade return to target weights when
allocations drift
Broad equity / fixed income ETFs
Portfolio completion
Fill exposure gap created by active
manager tilts
Sector or factor ETFs (e.g., tech,
small-cap)
Manager transition
Maintain benchmark exposure while
replacing managers
ETF on new manager's target
benchmark

Limitations for VERY LARGE asset owners: can negotiate lower fees via SMAs; ETFs may require public
disclosure of large holdings; SMA customization exceeds ETF mandate. Most asset owners use ETFs
during transitions.
(2) Asset Class Exposure Management
• Strategic (core) exposure: long-term allocation to major asset classes. Broad cap-weighted ETFs
(domestic equity, international equity, aggregate bonds, commodities).
• Tactical tilts: short-term overweight/underweight based on market views. Tight spreads and
liquidity matter more than fees. Ratio of avg dollar volume to AUM indicates suitability for tactical
use.
• Thematic ETFs: niche exposures (cybersecurity, robotics, infrastructure). Evaluate like stocks —
comparable volatility. Specialized active views.
• Fixed income growth driver: post-2008 bank capital rules made bond dealing costlier. FI ETFs
provide exchange-quality liquidity for previously OTC exposures.
(3) Active & Factor Investing
• Smart beta / factor ETFs: rules-based exposure to value, momentum, quality, low volatility,
dividend yield. ~20% of ETF AUM. Captures persistent risk premiums documented in academic
literature.
• Multi-factor ETFs: combine several factors with dynamic weighting. Lower return volatility but less
factor-timing upside.

Page 13  of  18
• Risk management ETFs: low-volatility, currency-hedged, duration-hedged. Adjust portfolio beta,
duration, credit, or currency exposure.
• Alternatively weighted ETFs: dividend-weighted, fundamental-weighted, equal-weighted —
overweight stocks by criteria other than market cap.
• Discretionary active ETFs: largest in fixed income (PIMCO BOND). Small share of total ETF AUM
(~2-3% globally).
• Dynamic asset allocation: multi-asset and global macro strategies using ETFs as building blocks.

Choosing an ETF for a Given Use
Investor type
Primary criteria
Secondary criteria
Long-term strategic
Expense ratio, tracking error
Index methodology, tax efficiency
Tactical trader
Liquidity, bid-ask spread, volume
Futures/hedging instruments available
Transition manager
Benchmark match
Trading costs on entry/exit
Factor investor
Factor definition, construction
Weighting methodology

MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
APs only in primary market
Only Authorized Participants transact with the ETF issuer. Retail investors NEVER deal directly
with the fund. Secondary-market trades don't affect the issuer or non-transacting shareholders.

⚠
In-kind creation = tax shield
In-kind redemptions are NOT taxable sales. Issuer delivers out low-cost-basis lots, raising
average basis of remaining holdings. Capital gains distributions much smaller than comparable
mutual funds.

⚠
Cash creation exceptions
Fixed-income (high-yield munis), leveraged/inverse (swap-based), and commodity ETFs often
use CASH creation/redemption. Trades off tax efficiency for easier AP participation.

⚠
Arbitrage keeps price ≈ NAV
Premium → AP sells ETF + buys basket. Discount → AP buys ETF + sells basket. Arbitrage GAP
depends on underlying liquidity — 1 cent for SPY, >1% for high-yield bond ETFs.


Page 14  of  18
⚠
Creation unit size
Typically 50,000 shares; range 10,000 to 600,000. Larger units mean APs need bigger order
imbalances to justify arbitrage → wider band around NAV.

⚠
Creation basket = daily disclosure
Published daily by issuer. Basis for intraday iNAV calculation. Allows AP to replicate throughout
the trading day before the after-close exchange of baskets.

⚠
Redemption basket ≠ creation basket
Issuer can customize redemption basket to deliver LOW-COST-BASIS securities, enhancing tax
efficiency.

⚠
Non-transacting shareholders shielded
ETF structure PROTECTS buy-and-hold investors from costs of others' trades. Mutual fund
structure imposes these costs on ALL shareholders.

⚠
US settlement = T+2
National Securities Clearing Corporation (NSCC) guarantees. Depository Trust Company (DTC)
holds book of accounts. Market makers get 6 days.

⚠
Europe fragmentation
29 CSDs. Multiple exchanges, share classes, currencies. Majority of trades negotiated OTC.
Higher implicit trading costs than US.




Page 15  of  18
⚠
Tracking error is expected ≈ fee
Frictionless index return minus fund fees = expected tracking difference. Deviation beyond fee
indicates other sources.

⚠
Sampling widens tracking range
Representative sampling vs full replication trades cost/feasibility for tracking variability. GMF
763/2342 constituents → 4× wider range than EEM.

⚠
Security lending = NEGATIVE cost
Lending fees generate income. Can offset or even exceed expense ratio. ETF appears to
OUTPERFORM its benchmark.

⚠
Dividend withholding
Fund may recover less than index assumes. Source of negative tracking for international ETFs.

⚠
Bid-ask spread components
Cr/red fees + underlying spreads + market-maker hedging/carry + profit spread − offsetting-flow
discount. For highly-liquid US ETFs, ETF spread can be TIGHTER than underlying.

⚠
Liquid ETFs = price discovery
In stressed fixed-income markets, bond NAVs become stale. ETF price reflects CURRENT
market consensus. ETF appears at discount when NAV inputs haven't updated.

⚠
Premium/discount ≠ mispricing
Timing/stale/after-hours effects can create apparent premiums. US ETF on European stocks
trades 6 hours after Europe closes — premium reflects market's guess of open.

⚠
Round-trip cost formula
Round-trip % = (One-way commission × 2) + (bid-ask spread). Holding-period cost adds
management fee × (months/12).

⚠
Short-term cost dominance
For holds <1 year, TRADING COSTS dominate total cost. For multi-year holds, MANAGEMENT
FEES dominate.


Page 16  of  18
⚠
Tactical traders care about liquidity
Choose highest-volume ETF in category. Management fee is secondary. Avg dollar volume /
AUM is a good sort key.

⚠
ETN ≠ ETF
ETNs are UNSECURED DEBT of issuer bank. 100% counterparty risk on issuer default. Lehman
2008 destroyed three ETNs. Monitor via issuer CDS.

⚠
ETNs can trade at large premium
If issuer HALTS CREATIONS (to avoid adding debt), arbitrage breaks down. MORL traded >5%
premium in 2018 after UBS sales halt.

⚠
Securities lending risk is LOW
Borrowers post 102-105% collateral. No shareholder loss since ETF inception. Income is
typically credited back.

⚠
Fund closure triggers taxable event
Forced liquidation generates cap gains for investors. Watch AUM + trading volume as early
warning. "Soft closures" (index change) can silently shift exposure.

⚠
Leveraged ETF compounding drift
Daily reset means cumulative ETF return ≠ (multiplier × index cumulative return) over multi-day
holds. Volatility DRAGS on returns in both directions.

⚠
Leveraged ETFs for short holds only
Not suitable for buy-and-hold beyond ~1 month without periodic rebalancing. Curriculum
example: 3-day index return −1%, 2× inverse ETF return −4% (not +2%).

⚠
Cash equitization via ETFs
Keeps portfolio fully invested while matching benchmark — minimizes CASH DRAG. Major
institutional use.

⚠
Portfolio completion strategy
ETFs fill gaps from active-manager tilts. Keep target exposure without replacing managers.
Sector/factor ETFs ideal.


Page 17  of  18
⚠
Transition management
Hold ETF in target benchmark while replacement manager builds portfolio. Maintains market
exposure during gap.

⚠
Large-asset-owner limitations
SMAs may be cheaper than ETFs; regulators may require disclosure of large ETF holdings; SMA
can be customized. Still common for transitions.

⚠
Thematic ETFs like single stocks
Evaluate thematic ETFs (cybersecurity, robotics) with stock-level risk assumptions. They have
concentrated exposure and higher volatility.

⚠
Smart beta = rules-based factor
Captures persistent risk premiums (value, momentum, quality, low vol, yield). Academic
support for size, value, momentum. ~20% of ETF AUM.

⚠
Active fixed-income ETFs growing
Largest active ETFs are in fixed income (PIMCO BOND). Dealer-market opacity makes active
bond management well-suited to ETF wrapping.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET

Premium / Discount
End-of-day P/D (%) = (ETF price − NAV per share) / NAV per share
Intraday P/D (%)   = (ETF price − iNAV per share) / iNAV per share
Round-Trip Trading Cost
Round-trip cost (%)  =  (One-way commission %  × 2)   +   (½ × bid-ask spread %  × 2)
Holding period cost (%)  =  Round-trip trading cost  +  Management fee × (months held / 12)
Bid-Ask Spread Upper Bound
Spread  ≤  ± Cr/Red fees + direct costs  +  Underlying spreads  +  MM hedging/carry  +  MM profit margin  −
Offsetting-flow discount
Creation Unit Size
Typically 50,000 ETF shares. Range: 10,000 – 600,000. Smaller units narrow the arbitrage gap; larger
units widen it.

Page 18  of  18
US Settlement
T+2 standard. NSCC = guarantor/clearing. DTC = book of accounts. Market makers have up to T+6.
Leveraged ETF — Daily Reset
Notional swap exposure = Multiplier × NAV, reset daily. Cumulative L× ETF return ≠ L × cumulative index
return over multi-day holds (volatility drag).
Tracking Error Sources — Summary
Fees (−) | Sampling/optimization (±) | Depositary receipts & other ETFs (±) | Index changes (±) | Fund
accounting (±) | Regulatory & tax (often −) | Security lending & dividend recapture (+ negative cost)

heading: VALUE AT RISK (VaR)
body:
VaR emerged in the late 1980s as the dominant market risk measure in global finance. It consolidates
loss potential into a single number tied to a probability threshold and a time horizon.
Formal Definition
VaR
The MINIMUM LOSS that would be expected a certain percentage of the time over a certain
period of time, given assumed market conditions.   Can be expressed in CURRENCY units or as
a PERCENTAGE of portfolio value.

Example statement: "The 5% VaR of a portfolio is €2.2 million over a one-day period." This means:
• €2.2 million is the MINIMUM loss we would expect 5% of the time (≈ once a month if 20 trading
days/month).
• 5% of the time, losses will be AT LEAST €2.2 million.
• 95% of the time, losses would not exceed €2.2 million.

Three Critical Points
• VaR is a MINIMUM loss, not a maximum. The maximum possible loss is the entire portfolio value.
• VaR has a TIME HORIZON. Could be one day, one week, one month — longer horizons → larger VaR.
• VaR has a CONFIDENCE THRESHOLD. 5% VaR = 95% confidence level. Higher confidence →
larger VaR.
Standard-Normal VaR Thresholds
VaR level
Std devs below mean
Confidence
5% VaR (common)
1.65
95%
1% VaR
2.33
99%
16% VaR (1σ move)
1.00
84%

These cutoffs assume NORMAL distribution. There is no formal requirement for any particular threshold
or distribution — these are choices made by the risk manager.

⚠
VaR DEFINITION TRAPS
VaR is NOT "how much I can lose" — the max loss is the whole portfolio. VaR is NOT an
expected loss — it's a threshold. 5% VaR ≠ 5% chance of losing exactly that amount. Losses

Page 3  of  21
EXCEED VaR 5% of the time, often by much more. VaR is not a worst-case scenario. Daily VaR
cannot be annualized by √250 scaling (that assumes zero expected return).



Page 4  of  21

heading: PARAMETRIC (VARIANCE-COVARIANCE) VaR
body:
Also called analytical or variance-covariance method. Assumes return distribution is NORMAL
(typically), uses only expected return and standard deviation. Most common and simplest method.
VaR Calculation Procedure
PARAMETRIC VaR FORMULA
VaR = [−(E(R_p) − z·σ_p)] × Portfolio Value     z = 1.65 for 5% VaR, 2.33 for 1% VaR, 1.00 for
16% VaR     Daily returns: divide E(R) by 250 and σ by √250 for daily VaR.

Two-Asset Portfolio — Expected Return & Volatility
E(R_p) = w_1·E(R_1) + w_2·E(R_2)
σ_p = √[w_1²·σ_1² + w_2²·σ_2² + 2·w_1·w_2·ρ_{1,2}·σ_1·σ_2]
Curriculum Worked Example — 80% SPY / 20% SPLB
Inputs (annualized): E(R_SPY) = 10.5%, σ_SPY = 20%. E(R_SPLB) = 6%, σ_SPLB = 8.5%. ρ = −0.06.
Portfolio value = $150 million.
E(R_p) = 0.8(10.5%) + 0.2(6%) = 9.6%
σ_p = √[0.64·0.04 + 0.04·0.007225 + 2·0.16·(−0.06)·0.017] = √0.0256 = 15.99%

✓
Daily 5% VaR computation
Daily E(R) = 9.6% / 250 = 0.0384%. Daily σ = 15.99% / √250 = 1.0112%.     Step 1: 1.65 ×
0.010112 = 0.016685.     Step 2: 0.000384 − 0.016685 = −0.016301.     Step 3: Flip sign →
0.016301 (1.63% loss).     Step 4: × $150M = $2,445,150.     Daily 5% VaR ≈ $2.4 million.

✓
Daily 1% VaR (same inputs)
2.33 × 0.010112 = 0.023561. 0.000384 − 0.023561 = −0.023177. VaR = 0.023177 × $150M =
$3,476,550 ≈ $3.5 million.
Advantages & Limitations
• Advantage: Simple, fast, analytically elegant with only two parameters (μ, σ) per factor.
• Limitation: Assumes NORMAL distribution — understates fat tails. Poor fit for OPTIONS (nonlinear
payoffs). Many assets require many parameters (large covariance matrix).



Page 5  of  21

heading: HISTORICAL & MONTE CARLO VaR
body:
Historical Simulation Method
HISTORICAL SIMULATION VaR
Apply historical risk-factor return sequence to the CURRENT portfolio. Sort simulated portfolio
returns low → high. The 5% VaR is the VALUE AT THE 5TH PERCENTILE of the historical
distribution.

• No distribution assumption — uses whatever distribution the data produce.
• CAN accommodate options — just re-prices them under each historical move.
• Can weight recent observations more heavily if desired.
• Advantage — "it actually happened," so results cannot be dismissed as impossible.
• Limitation — history may not repeat. Short lookback may include anomalous events (e.g., Black
Monday 1987) that inflate loss estimates.
Monte Carlo Simulation Method
MONTE CARLO VaR
Analyst specifies the DISTRIBUTION and PARAMETERS for each risk factor. Generate (e.g.,
10,000) random return scenarios incorporating specified CORRELATIONS. Re-price portfolio
for each scenario. Sort low → high. 5% VaR = 5th percentile of simulated results.

• Flexible — any distribution, any factor structure.
• Handles complex instruments (options, path-dependent derivatives).
• Converges to parametric VaR when same parameters are used and sample is large.
• Limitation — computationally intensive. Results sensitive to specified distributions.

Curriculum Worked Example — Monte Carlo Results
Same SPY/SPLB portfolio, 10,000 simulations from normal distribution with historical parameters:
• 1% VaR = $3,541,035
• 5% VaR = $2,517,702
• 16% VaR = $1,524,735
These match the parametric method closely because the Monte Carlo used a normal distribution with
the same parameters. Sample noise accounts for small differences.
Comparing the Three Methods

Page 6  of  21
Aspect
Parametric
Historical
Monte Carlo
Distribution
Usually normal
Empirical (from data)
User-specified
Speed
Very fast
Fast
Slow (many sims)
Handles options
Poor
Good
Excellent
Needs history
For inputs only
Required
For inputs only

No (underestimates)

Yes if specified
Complexity
Low

⚠
VaR METHOD TRAPS
5% parametric VaR = 1.65 std devs below mean (normal). 5% HISTORICAL VaR = 5th
percentile of ACTUAL returns — no std-dev calculation. Historical method's distribution IS the
data. Monte Carlo can use ANY distribution, including fat-tailed. The parametric method is poor
for options because delta-only assumes linearity. Monte Carlo with enough draws + same
inputs ≈ parametric VaR.



Page 7  of  21

heading: VaR ADVANTAGES, LIMITATIONS & EXTENSIONS
body:
Advantages of VaR
• Simple, intuitive concept: a single loss number communicable to non-technical decision-makers.
• Comparability across portfolios: single scale lets risk managers identify hot spots across units.
• Supports capital allocation: VaRs from different desks can be compared and capital rebalanced
accordingly.
• Performance evaluation: risk-adjusted returns use VaR as the risk measure.
• Verifiable by backtesting: if daily 5% VaR = $5M, check whether losses exceeded $5M on roughly
5% of past days.
• Regulatory acceptance: SEC permits VaR in derivatives disclosures; global bank regulators
encourage it (but don't prescribe method or maximum).
Limitations of VaR
• Subjectivity: choice of confidence level, time horizon, distribution, lookback period are all judgment
calls.
• Underestimates tail events: normal distribution assumption understates the frequency of extreme
losses. Real markets have fatter left tails than normal.
• Ignores liquidity: large positions in illiquid markets have larger potential losses than VaR suggests.
Liquidity evaporates precisely during tail events.
• Correlation risk: correlations RISE during stress — diversification benefits shrink when you need
them most. VaR based on historical correlations understates stressed losses.
• Trending / regime vulnerability: a portfolio can stay under its VaR limit every day while
accumulating substantial cumulative losses. Low-volatility regimes produce misleadingly low VaRs.
• Misunderstood as worst case: VaR is NOT a maximum loss. Losses exceed VaR by definition 5% of
the time.
• Right-tail neglect: VaR ignores potential gains. Full risk-reward picture requires both tails.
Extensions of VaR
CONDITIONAL VaR (CVaR / Expected Shortfall)
CVaR = AVERAGE loss given that VaR threshold is exceeded. Answers "how much will I lose IF
VaR is breached?" Not technically a VaR. Best estimated via historical or Monte Carlo (simple
averaging of losses beyond cutoff). Parametric CVaR requires integrating the tail.

✓
CVaR worked example

Page 8  of  21
In curriculum example, 5% historical VaR = $1,622,272 (5th percentile of 1,006-day sample =
50 observations). Average of those 50 worst losses = $2,668,389. CVaR > VaR because the tail
distribution has losses EXCEEDING VaR — CVaR captures those.

INCREMENTAL VaR (IVaR)
Change in portfolio VaR from changing a position size (e.g., increasing SPY from 80% to 90%).
IVaR = new VaR − old VaR. Useful BEFORE executing trades to gauge risk impact.

MARGINAL VaR (MVaR)
Derivative-based — change in VaR for a VERY SMALL change in a position. Used to attribute
total VaR across positions. Sum of position-weighted MVaRs = portfolio VaR.

EX ANTE TRACKING ERROR (Relative VaR)
VaR of portfolio returns RELATIVE to benchmark — effectively VaR of (portfolio − benchmark).
Portfolio close to benchmark → small relative VaR. Expressed as one std dev annualized,
typically.



Page 9  of  21

heading: SENSITIVITY RISK MEASURES
body:
Sensitivity measures estimate portfolio response to changes in a risk factor without probability attached.
Complement VaR by showing WHAT drives the risk.
Equity Risk — Beta
EQUITY BETA
β = Cov(R_i, R_m) / σ²_m     Measures sensitivity of an equity's return to a market index return.
β = 1 → moves with market. β > 1 → more volatile. β < 1 → less volatile. β < 0 → inverse (rare).

Fixed-Income — Duration & Convexity
DURATION + CONVEXITY
ΔB/B ≈ −D · Δy/(1+y)  +  ½ · C · [Δy/(1+y)]²     D = duration, first-order sensitivity to yield change
(assumes parallel shift).     C = convexity, second-order correction — matters for LARGE yield
moves and longer holding periods.

Related measures used by fixed-income risk managers:
• Key rate duration (partial duration): sensitivity to a shift at SPECIFIC points on the yield curve.
Captures non-parallel shifts.
• Credit spread duration: price change from a change in credit spread (with risk-free rate held
constant).
• Granularity: broad "buckets" (e.g. "1-5 year sovereigns") assume perfect correlation within the
bucket. Too broad = hidden correlation risk. Too narrow = complexity. Risk manager chooses the
right width.
Options Risk — The Greeks
Greek
Measures sensitivity to
Interpretation
Delta (Δ)
Price of underlying
Call: 0 to +1 (ATM ≈ 0.5). Put: 0 to −1. Deep ITM → |Δ| → 1.
Deep OTM → |Δ| → 0. First-order.
Gamma (Γ)
Delta (2nd-order of
underlying)
Highest for ATM options near expiration. Long options →
long gamma. Captures curvature.
Vega (V)
Implied volatility of
underlying
Positive for long options. Larger for longer-dated options.
Sensitivity to VOLATILITY risk, separate from price risk.
Theta (Θ)
Passage of time
Negative for long options — time decay. Accelerates as
expiration nears.

Page 10  of  21
Greek
Measures sensitivity to
Interpretation
Rho (ρ)
Risk-free rate
Usually small relative to other Greeks for short-dated
options.

✓
Delta approximation for option pricing
Call approx: c + Δc ≈ c + Δ · ΔS. If call = $2.00, Δ = 0.6, and underlying rises by $1.50, new call ≈
$2.00 + 0.6(1.50) = $2.90. Linear approximation — OK for small moves, fails for large moves
(gamma correction required).
Limitations of Sensitivity Measures
• No probability attached: tell you "if rates move 1bp, I lose X" — not "how often will rates move
1bp."
• Not comparable across asset classes: a 1bp IG move is very different from a 1bp HY move.
• Duration assumes parallel shifts: yield curve shifts are rarely parallel.
• Delta assumes linear payoffs: fails for options under large moves — need gamma.



Page 11  of  21

heading: SCENARIO ANALYSIS & STRESS TESTING
body:
Scenario analysis evaluates portfolio performance under specified market conditions. Unlike VaR, it is
not constrained by recent history or by probability distributions.
Historical Scenarios
Apply a SPECIFIC HISTORICAL PERIOD's shocks to the current portfolio. Typical events: Black Monday
1987, 1997-98 currency crisis, LTCM 1998, Dot-com 2001, GFC 2008-09.
• Re-price portfolio under historical risk-factor movements. Often assume the shock is
INSTANTANEOUS before any management action.
• Captures actual correlations and volatilities that occurred. Cannot be dismissed as "impossible."
• Requires re-pricing bonds/derivatives from current terms: historical prices of specific
instruments may not be relevant (e.g., bond now has 1 year left but had 6 years at the historical
date).
Hypothetical Scenarios
Design a "rare but not impossible" event and compute portfolio impact. Useful for:
• Risks never seen in recent history (new geopolitical event, unprecedented rate move).
• Stressing correlations — simulate perfectly-correlated moves across normally-uncorrelated
markets.
• Identifying vulnerabilities in hedges — assets that normally move together decouple under flight-to-
quality.

Reverse Stress Testing
Start with a TARGETED LOSS level (e.g., the loss that would exhaust available capital) and then identify
which market conditions would produce that loss. Useful to identify multiple-exposure crises where
investors "crowd" into the same positions.
Sensitivity vs Scenario vs VaR — Comparison
Dimension
Sensitivity
Scenario
VaR
Probability assigned
No
No (or optional)
Yes
Magnitude of move
Small (1bp, 1%)
Large (stressed)
Statistical percentile
Relies on history
No
Optional
Yes (usually)
Aggregates risk
Limited
Yes, by scenario
Yes — single number
Handles nonlinearity
Only with convexity /
gamma
Yes (full revaluation)
Yes (MC); No
(parametric)


Page 12  of  21
BEST PRACTICE: use all three in combination. VaR gives probability; sensitivities explain drivers;
scenarios stress-test the portfolio in ways VaR cannot.

⚠
SCENARIO ANALYSIS TRAPS
Scenarios have NO probability — can't weight by likelihood. Making a scenario "plausible" may
UNDERSTATE actual tail risks (pre-2008 real estate scenarios were often half as severe as
what actually happened). Portfolio with zero scenario sensitivity earns ~ risk-free rate —
scenarios guide risk tolerance, not elimination. Scenario analyses work best as final screen
after position limits and VaR limits, not primary control.



Page 13  of  21

heading: MARKET RISK CONSTRAINTS
body:
Risk measures alone don't constrain action — the LIMITS placed on them do. Four common constraint
types used in combination.
(1) Risk Budgeting
Allocate total risk appetite (typically in VaR or tracking error terms) across business units, asset classes,
or strategies. Done top-down from entity level.
• Bank example: €10M total VaR tolerance. European unit gets 20% of market risk capital; Asian unit
gets 40%; etc.
• Pension fund: surplus-at-risk budget → asset allocation → tracking-error budget per manager.
• Over-allocation: sum of unit budgets > total because of diversification. Reduces pro-rata if
correlations spike.
(2) Position Limits
Absolute or relative caps on position size. The ORIGINAL risk measure — predates modern portfolio
theory. Still used as the most common risk control for asset managers because it's simple, verifiable,
and easily disclosed.
• Types: country, currency, sector, single-issuer, leverage, concentration.
• Strength: fills gaps in VaR — constrains positions regardless of whether past volatility captured their
risk.
(3) Scenario Limits
Maximum loss permitted under a specific hypothetical or historical scenario. Apply a HIGHER tolerance
for more extreme scenarios — otherwise the portfolio can't take ANY risk.
(4) Stop-Loss Limits
Require reduction or liquidation of positions when cumulative losses exceed a threshold over a specified
period. Addresses VaR "trending" (portfolio stays under daily VaR but bleeds steadily).
• Simple form: "Liquidate if monthly loss > $8M."
• Advanced: drawdown control / portfolio insurance — hedge PURCHASES triggered by loss levels,
with increasing hedge intensity as losses grow.
Capital Allocation
Similar to risk budgeting but focused on CAPITAL at very high confidence levels (99%+). Used where
leverage or tail risk is significant.
• Economic capital: amount needed to absorb severe losses and survive. Combines market, credit,
operational risk.

Page 14  of  21
• Hurdle rate: minimum expected return per unit of capital allocated. Example: Portfolio A needs
€325K, earns €50K/year (15.4%). Portfolio B needs €1M, earns €100K/year (10%). Hurdle 15% →
Portfolio A wins despite smaller absolute return.
• Regulatory capital: banks, insurers face capital requirements imposed by regulators. Often more
binding than economic capital.



Page 15  of  21

heading: RISK MEASURES BY PARTICIPANT
body:
Different market participants use different risk tools based on leverage, time horizon, accounting
treatment, and regulatory regime.
Banks
• VaR (held-for-sale/trading book): fair-value-accounted portfolios.
• Liquidity gap: asset-liability maturity mismatch.
• Leverage ratio: risk-weighted assets / equity, per regulatory or internal rules.
• Sensitivities: duration, key rate duration, credit spread duration, FX exposure, delta/gamma/vega.
• Economic capital: 99%+ confidence blended market + credit + operational risk.
• Stress tests: regulatory (e.g., CCAR in US) and internal. Applied to FULL balance sheet.
Traditional (Long-Only) Asset Managers
• Position limits: most frequent risk control. Country, sector, issuer caps.
• Sensitivities: duration, key rate duration, credit spread duration, beta. Often relative to benchmark.
• Ex ante tracking error: key risk metric — forward-looking estimate of portfolio's potential
underperformance.
• Ex post tracking error: used to assess skill retrospectively. Differs from ex ante because data
history dominates.
• Active share: % of portfolio different from benchmark. High active share → high tracking error
potential.
• Liquidity measures: days to liquidate at typical market volume.
• Redemption risk: for open-end funds, tracks % redeemable at peak times.
• Scenario analysis: verify risks match investor disclosures.
Hedge Funds / Leveraged Players
• Very high confidence VaR (99%+): focus on tail events that could impair capital.
• Single-factor stress tests: pass/fail on whether capital survives specific shocks.
• Drawdown limits: cumulative loss triggers. Margin calls and financing withdrawal escalate quickly.
• Short time horizon (daily / two-week): reflects financing and margin call realities.
Pension Funds
• Surplus at risk: mismatch between assets and liabilities. Starting point for asset allocation.
• Asset/liability models: duration matching, liability-driven investing.
• Tracking error budgets per manager: explicit active-risk allocation.
• Longer horizon (quarterly/annual): reflects obligation structure.
Insurers

Page 16  of  21
• Asset-liability matching: book-value accounting for long-dated liabilities; duration matching against
liabilities.
• Regulatory capital: Solvency II, risk-based capital standards.
• Economic capital: internal measure of capital needed for tail events.
• Cash flow stress tests: verify ability to meet claim payouts under scenarios.
Mark-to-Market vs Book Value Portfolios
Mark-to-market portfolios (mutual funds, UCITS, bank trading books) are well suited to VaR, economic
capital, duration, and beta — all fair-value-based. BOOK-VALUE portfolios (bank loan books, insurance
reserves) use asset/liability gap models and accounting-based measures.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
VaR is a minimum, not maximum
VaR is the MINIMUM loss expected a certain % of the time. Max loss is the entire portfolio.
Losses WILL exceed VaR with the stated frequency.

⚠
VaR needs 3 things
(1) Confidence level (5%, 1%, 16%), (2) Time horizon (daily, weekly, monthly), (3) Loss
estimate method. All three must be specified.

⚠
Standard normal z-values
5% VaR → 1.65 std devs below mean. 1% VaR → 2.33. 16% VaR → 1.00 (one sigma).

⚠
Cannot annualize daily VaR directly
Daily VaR × √250 ignores the expected return. Must re-annualize E(R) (× 250) and σ (× √250)
separately, then compute annual VaR.

⚠
Parametric assumes normal
Standard implementation uses normal distribution with just μ and σ. Other distributions
technically possible but add parameters (skewness, kurtosis).

⚠
Parametric is poor for options
Options have nonlinear payoffs — delta-only is a bad approximation for even moderate moves.
Monte Carlo or historical simulation preferred.


Page 17  of  21
⚠
Historical simulation = empirical percentile
5% historical VaR is the 5th-percentile value in the sorted historical return series — NOT 1.65
std devs. No normality assumption.

⚠
Historical simulation can handle options
Because each scenario fully re-prices the portfolio, options are handled naturally — advantage
over parametric.

⚠
Monte Carlo needs distribution AND correlation
Analyst specifies distributions and correlation structure. If normal + same parameters + large
N, MC ≈ parametric VaR.

⚠
Parametric < MC < Historical for fat tails
Normal parametric understates tails most. Historical captures actual fat tails in data. MC
depends on chosen distribution.

⚠
Correlation rises in stress
Normal-period correlations underestimate stressed correlations. Diversification benefits
collapse in crises. Key VaR limitation.

⚠
Liquidity underestimates VaR
Large positions in illiquid markets face larger effective losses. VaR based on historical returns
ignores this.

⚠
Trending limitation of VaR
Portfolio can stay under daily VaR every day and still accumulate massive losses. Requires
stop-loss overlay.

⚠
Low-volatility regime = misleading VaR
VaR trained on calm periods understates risk when volatility normalizes. Use stress scenarios
to compensate.

⚠
CVaR > VaR always
CVaR (Expected Shortfall) = average loss given VaR breached. Always larger than VaR since it
averages the tail.


Page 18  of  21
⚠
CVaR best from HS/MC
Parametric CVaR needs tail integration. Historical and Monte Carlo compute it by averaging
losses beyond the VaR point — simple.

⚠
Incremental vs Marginal VaR
Incremental = change in VaR from a discrete position change (big trades). Marginal = derivative-
based, infinitesimal change (attribution).

⚠
Ex ante tracking error = relative VaR
VaR applied to (portfolio − benchmark). Zero if portfolio matches benchmark. Typically
annualized at 1σ level.

⚠
Beta measures market sensitivity
β = Cov(R_i, R_m) / σ²_m. β = 1 → market-matching. β > 1 → more volatile than market.

⚠
Duration assumes parallel shift
ΔB/B ≈ −D · Δy/(1+y). Works for small parallel rate moves. Convexity corrects for large moves.
KEY RATE duration handles non-parallel.

⚠
Convexity = second-order
Correction term ½·C·[Δy/(1+y)]². Matters for large rate moves and long holding periods. Positive
convexity = bond outperforms duration estimate.

⚠
Call delta range 0 to +1
Call Δ = 0 (deep OTM) to +1 (deep ITM). ATM ≈ 0.5. Put Δ = 0 to −1.

⚠
Gamma peaks ATM near expiration
Γ = second derivative wrt underlying. Largest for ATM options near expiry — delta changes
fastest there.

⚠
Vega = volatility sensitivity
Sensitivity to IMPLIED volatility. Long options have positive vega. Longer-dated options have
larger vega.

⚠
Options have 4 key Greeks

Page 19  of  21
Delta (price), Gamma (delta sensitivity / curvature), Vega (volatility), Theta (time). Rho (rates)
usually small.

⚠
Sensitivity vs VaR vs Scenario
SENSITIVITY: what if risk factor moves 1 unit (no probability). SCENARIO: what if specific
adverse condition occurs (no probability). VaR: probability threshold tied to distribution.

⚠
Historical scenarios re-price current holdings
Apply historical moves to CURRENT portfolio composition — not the portfolio that existed at
the historical date. Valuation models required for bonds/options.

⚠
Hypothetical scenarios aren't constrained by history
Can stress correlations, simulate never-observed events. Best for identifying vulnerabilities
VaR misses.

⚠
Reverse stress testing
Start with loss level (e.g., capital exhaustion), find conditions that produce it. Useful for
"crowded trade" multi-exposure crises.

⚠
Scenarios have no probability
Hence the "plausibility" problem — avoid making scenarios too mild to be credible (pre-2008
real estate scenarios understated actual moves).

⚠
Risk budgeting top-down
Firm-wide risk appetite allocated to units. Sum of unit budgets > total due to diversification.
Correlations matter.

⚠
Position limits are oldest tool
Pre-date modern portfolio theory. Most common and easily disclosed. Work where VaR fails
(illiquid positions, new issues, concentration).

⚠
Stop-loss addresses trending
VaR doesn't constrain cumulative losses. Stop-loss forces action when cumulative loss
threshold hit. Drawdown control is more sophisticated.


Page 20  of  21
⚠
Capital allocation uses higher confidence than risk budgeting
Risk budgeting often at 1σ (84%). Capital allocation at 99%+ to capture true tail exposure. Used
by leveraged and tail-risky strategies.

⚠
Economic capital vs regulatory capital
Economic = internal measure for severe losses. Regulatory = minimum required by authorities.
The MAX of the two is the binding constraint.

⚠
Hurdle rate for capital decisions
Expected return per unit of capital. Smaller capital deployment with higher % return beats
larger deployment with lower %.

⚠
Banks use all measures
VaR (trading book), liquidity gap, leverage ratio, duration/KRD/CSD, delta/gamma/vega,
economic capital, stress tests. Distinguish held-for-sale (fair value) vs held-to-maturity (book
value).

⚠
Asset managers focus on relative measures
Ex ante tracking error, active share, position limits. Less concerned with absolute VaR than
benchmark-relative risk.

⚠
Hedge funds use short-horizon tail measures
High confidence VaR (99%+), daily/two-week horizons, drawdown limits. Leverage drives
margin-call risk.

⚠
Pension funds use surplus at risk
Asset-liability mismatch drives allocation. Longer horizon (quarterly+) than banks. Tracking
error budgets per manager.

⚠
Insurers use ALM and reg capital
Duration match assets and liabilities. Solvency II capital rules often bind. Book-value
accounting for long-dated liabilities.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET

Page 21  of  21

VaR
Parametric VaR = [−(E(R_p) − z · σ_p)] × Portfolio Value
z = 1.65 (5%), 2.33 (1%), 1.00 (16%).
Daily μ = annual / 250. Daily σ = annual / √250.
Two-asset σ_p = √[w_1²σ_1² + w_2²σ_2² + 2 w_1 w_2 ρ σ_1 σ_2]
VaR Extensions
CVaR = avg loss given VaR exceeded (Expected Shortfall). IVaR = new VaR − old VaR (discrete change).
MVaR = dVaR/dw (infinitesimal). Ex ante TE (relative VaR) = VaR of (port − bench), 1σ annualized.
Fixed-Income Sensitivities
Duration: ΔB/B ≈ −D · Δy / (1 + y)
With convexity: ΔB/B ≈ −D · Δy/(1+y) + ½ · C · [Δy/(1+y)]²
Option Greeks
Delta (Δ) = ΔPrice_opt / ΔPrice_underlying. Call: 0 to +1. Put: 0 to −1. Gamma (Γ) = ΔΔ/ΔS, peaks ATM
near expiry. Vega (V) = ΔPrice/Δ(implied vol). Theta (Θ) = ΔPrice/Δ(time), negative for long options.
Risk Measure Comparison
VaR: probability + single number, but POOR for tails, ignores liquidity. Sensitivity: "what if factor moves
X" — no probability. Scenario: specified extreme conditions — tests correlation assumptions, handles
fat tails. Best used TOGETHER.

End of Notes — LM5 Measuring and Managing Market Risk

heading: BACKTESTING OBJECTIVES & PROCESS
body:
Backtesting approximates the real-life investment process by using historical data to assess whether a
strategy would have produced desirable results. Implicit assumption: the future will at least somewhat
resemble history.
Four Evaluation Techniques
Technique
What it does
Backtesting
Tests strategy performance in historical environments over long periods. "How
would this have performed if implemented in the past?"
Historical scenario
analysis
Examines strategy performance in DISCRETE historical regimes (recessions, high-
inflation, high-volatility periods).
Simulation
Explores strategy performance in HYPOTHETICAL environments specified by the
user — past may not recur.
Sensitivity analysis
Uncovers impact of changing key ASSUMPTIONS (distributional choice, parameter
values).

Backtesting Objectives
• Acceptance/rejection criterion: strategies that don't perform well historically are unlikely to be
accepted by PMs or clients.
• Quantitative AND fundamental use: systematic strategies are the natural fit, but fundamental
managers use backtests before applying stock-selection criteria.
• Diagnostic — not guarantee: good backtest ≠ future success. Bad backtest ≠ future failure. Just a
screen.
The Three-Step Backtesting Process
BACKTESTING FLOWCHART
(1) STRATEGY DESIGN — specify hypothesis, rules, key parameters.     (2) HISTORICAL
INVESTMENT SIMULATION — form portfolios each period per the rules; rebalance at stated
frequency.     (3) ANALYSIS OF OUTPUT — compute performance statistics and key metrics
(turnover, drawdown, etc.).

Step 1 — Strategy Design Parameters
• Investment universe: Russell 3000 (US), S&P/TSX Composite (Canada), MSCI China A, S&P Global
BMI (other) are common. Broader universe → more statistical power but more complexity.

Page 3  of  18
• Return definition: single-currency (home) vs local currency. Choice depends on whether PM
hedges FX.
• Benchmark: should match the investment universe (MSCI China A → MSCI China A benchmark).
• Rebalancing frequency: monthly is most common. Daily → higher transaction costs + bid-ask bias +
asynchronous trading issues.
• Transaction costs: MUST be addressed — many "anomalies" vanish once costs are included.
Always disclose whether included.
• Start and end dates: longer = more statistical power, BUT financial data is non-stationary, so
supplement with regime-specific scenario analysis.



Page 4  of  18

heading: BACKTESTING WORKED EXAMPLES
body:
Step 2 — Historical Investment Simulation
Portfolio construction depends on the investment hypothesis, manager capabilities, and client mandate.
Most commonly done with ROLLING WINDOWS — strategy constituted from in-sample data at period
start, tested on subsequent out-of-sample period, repeated as time moves forward.

✓
Rolling-window procedure
Backtest earnings-yield strategy monthly Dec 2011 – May 2012.     30 Nov 2011: compute
trailing 12M earnings yield for each stock in universe (EPS Dec 2010–Nov 2011 / price 30 Nov
2011).     Long top quintile, short bottom quintile.     Measure next-month (Dec 2011) OUT-OF-
SAMPLE return.     Repeat on 31 Dec 2011, 31 Jan 2012, etc.     Rolling windows replicate live
investing — managers re-estimate as new data arrives.
Long-Short Quintile Hedged Portfolio
Standard structure: LONG top 20% (best factor scores), SHORT bottom 20% (worst). Stocks within each
quantile equally or cap-weighted. Drawback: not all managers can short.

Worked Example — Earnings Yield Strategy (1986-2019)
Metric
US Market
Europe Market
Avg annual return
9.24%
6.65%
Avg annual volatility
12.36%

Sharpe ratio
0.75

Max drawdown

US has higher return and Sharpe; Europe has lower vol. In both, max drawdown is ~3× volatility. US
distribution shows EXCESS KURTOSIS (fat tails) and NEGATIVE SKEWNESS — strategy more likely to
generate extreme, negative surprises. Drawdowns during tech bubble (late 1990s) and GFC (March-May
2009) visible as structural breaks.
Multifactor Strategies — BM and RP Portfolios
Few managers use a single factor. Two objective weighting schemes:
• Benchmark (BM) portfolio: equal weight across all factors.
• Risk Parity (RP) portfolio: weight factors by equal risk CONTRIBUTION — uses covariance matrix of
factor returns. More stable, less volatile.


Page 5  of  18
Eight Fundamental Factors (Curriculum Set)
• Defensive value: trailing earnings yield (EPS/price).
• Cyclical value: book-to-market ratio.
• Growth: consensus FY1/FY0 EPS growth.
• Price momentum: 12M total return excluding most recent month.
• Analyst sentiment: 3M EPS revision.
• Profitability: return on equity (ROE).
• Leverage: debt-to-equity ratio (inverted — low leverage preferred).
• Earnings quality: non-cash earnings / accruals (low accruals preferred).

RP portfolio has DRAMATICALLY lower downside risk than BM or any single-factor portfolio (max
drawdown 3.8% vs 22.6% for BM, vs 59.7% for momentum). Risk-contribution weighting explicitly
dampens concentration in volatile factors.

⚠
BACKTESTING PROCESS TRAPS
Rolling window = deterministic single-path history. Rebalancing frequency drives transaction
costs — monthly common, higher frequency = higher costs + bid-ask bias. Long-short hedged
portfolio requires ability to SHORT — not all managers can. For multi-factor portfolios using
covariance (like RP), rolling window used TWICE: once for factor returns, once for covariance
matrix.



Page 6  of  18

heading: COMMON PROBLEMS IN BACKTESTING
body:
Survivorship Bias
Deriving conclusions from data reflecting only entities that have SURVIVED to the present. Most
common and obvious mistake in backtesting.

✓
Scale of the problem
RUSSELL 3000: of 1985 constituents, fewer than 400 (< 13%) remain in the index as of May
2019.     S&P BMI EUROPE: started 1989 with ~720 stocks, now ~1,200. Only 142 (about 20%)
of original 720 remain in 2019.     The "survivors" are typically biased — multinational, highly
innovative, most successful firms.

✓
Worked example — low-volatility anomaly
POINT-IN-TIME Russell 3000: low-volatility stocks significantly outperform high-volatility
stocks over ~30 years. CONFIRMS the low-vol anomaly.     SURVIVOR-ONLY universe: high-
volatility stocks outperform low-vol by 5.5× over same period — OPPOSITE conclusion.
Lesson: survivorship bias can REVERSE the direction of a documented anomaly. Use point-in-
time data (casualties included).
Look-Ahead Bias
Using data in a backtest that was NOT available at the historical decision point. Two common sources:
• Reporting lag ignored: assuming full-year 2015 earnings available on 31 Dec 2015. In reality, most
firms report 1-3 months later.
• Database back-fills: when a new company is added to a data vendor's history, several years of
historical data are typically added — these were not in the database during the backtest period.

✓
Impact of reporting lag assumption
Curriculum value strategy: NO-lag (full look-ahead) INFLATES US performance by nearly 100%
vs proper point-in-time.     Appropriate lag assumptions by region: US/Canada/Japan = 1-2
months. Europe/UK/ANZ = 2-3 months. Asia ex-Japan/LATAM/EMEA = ~3 months. Reflects
reporting timeliness in each region.
Data Snooping
Subconscious or conscious manipulation of data to produce a statistically significant result — a low p-
value. Arises from over-testing, multiple hypothesis testing without correction, reverse engineering.
• Cause: testing thousands of factor combinations until one "works" in-sample. By chance, some will
appear statistically significant but have NO predictive power out-of-sample.

Page 7  of  18
• Publication bias: only successful strategies get published; failures disappear. Published
"anomalies" often fail to replicate.
• Cross-validation defense: test strategy in DIFFERENT geographic markets, time periods, or asset
classes. Robust strategy → works across contexts.
• Multiple-testing corrections: Bonferroni adjustment, False Discovery Rate. Increases the required
significance threshold when testing many hypotheses.

✓
Cross-validation — RP vs BM globally
RP strategy tested across 10 global markets (1993-2019). RP delivers LOWER VOLATILITY
than BM in ALL 10 markets. RP outperforms BM in Sharpe ratio in 7 of 10 markets. Robust
across geographies → less likely to be data-snooped artifact.

⚠
BACKTESTING BIAS TRAPS
Survivorship bias: use POINT-IN-TIME universe including casualties, not current survivors.
Look-ahead bias: enforce reporting lags (1-3 months depending on region). Database back-fills
add history for newly-added firms — contaminates early periods. Data snooping: too many
tests produce false positives. Defense: cross-validation across markets, time periods, asset
classes. Published anomalies frequently fail to replicate.

heading: HISTORICAL SCENARIO ANALYSIS
body:
Rather than averaging performance over a long period, historical scenario analysis examines
performance in DIFFERENT STRUCTURAL REGIMES. Addresses structural breaks and regime changes
that are averaged out in a single-window backtest.
Common Regime Classifications
• Expansion vs recession: NBER dates for US. Note: inflection points identified only in HINDSIGHT —
NBER didn't declare Dec 2007 recession start until Dec 2008.
• High vs low volatility: VIX above vs below its 5-year moving average.
• Rate regimes: rising vs falling rates; high vs low inflation.
• Currency regimes: strong vs weak dollar environments.
Worked Example — BM vs RP Across Regimes (1993-2019)
Regime
BM Sharpe
RP Sharpe
Recession
Struggles — lower Sharpe
Robust — stable Sharpe
Non-recession (expansion)
Higher Sharpe
Slightly lower Sharpe

Page 8  of  18
Regime
BM Sharpe
RP Sharpe
High-volatility regime
Marginally higher
Equal to low-vol regime
Low-volatility regime
Slightly lower
Equal to high-vol regime

Return Distribution Across Regimes
BM distribution: negative skewness + excess kurtosis in BOTH regimes; lower mean return in recession,
flatter in expansion. RP distribution: tighter, less skew/kurtosis, more stable.
Why Historical Scenario Analysis Matters
• Averages hide regime sensitivity — a strategy with similar long-run Sharpe as the benchmark may
behave very differently in recessions.
• Risk managers care about regime change timing — strategies that perform well in benign markets
but fail in stress are especially dangerous.
• Complements single-window backtesting — don't use instead of, use in addition to.



Page 9  of  18

heading: SIMULATION ANALYSIS
body:
Backtesting assumes the past can be replayed. Simulation relaxes this — past may not repeat, limited
set of historical observations. Two types: HISTORICAL (random resampling from observed data) and
MONTE CARLO (random draws from specified distribution).
Historical Simulation (Bootstrapping)
HISTORICAL SIMULATION
Random sampling from historical data WITH REPLACEMENT (bootstrapping). In contrast to
rolling-window backtesting, which is deterministic, historical simulation is NON-
DETERMINISTIC — each month is equally likely on each draw.

Historical Simulation Procedure (8 Steps)
• (1) Define TARGET variables (e.g., BM and RP portfolio returns).
• (2) Identify KEY DECISION variables (e.g., returns of the 8 underlying factor portfolios).
• (3) Choose N = number of simulation trials (e.g., 1,000).
• (4) Sample WITH REPLACEMENT (bootstrapping) — random draws from historical data.
• (5) For each trial, compute target variables using the sampled decision-variable values.
• (6) Repeat until N trials complete.
• (7) Compute performance metrics (Sharpe, CVaR, max drawdown) from distribution of N outcomes.
• (8) Plot estimated return distributions for visual comparison.

✓
Bootstrap mechanics (curriculum worked example)
Historical data: 374 months (April 1988 – May 2019). Draw random number from uniform
distribution [0,1). Divide span by 374 → each month corresponds to a 0.00267-wide slice.
Random 0.59163 → month 222 (Sep 2006). Random 0.32185 → month 121 (April 1998). Repeat
1,000 times, compute BM and RP returns each trial. First trial: BM = 0.46% (equal weight of 8
factor returns in Sep 2006). RP = 0.17% (using May 2019 RP weights applied to Sep 2006 factor
returns).
Monte Carlo Simulation
MONTE CARLO SIMULATION
Draw random values from a FITTED STATISTICAL DISTRIBUTION (not from historical
observations). User specifies distribution form (normal, Student's t, etc.), parameters
estimated from data.

Page 10  of  18

Monte Carlo Procedure (Same 8 Steps, Steps 4-5 Differ)
Step 4: FIT a statistical distribution to historical data (model CALIBRATION). For asset allocation
strategies, typically multivariate NORMAL as first approximation. K assets require K means, K std devs,
K(K−1)/2 correlations.
Step 5: Draw random returns from the fitted distribution. For multivariate normal with 8 factors: 8
uniform random numbers mapped via inverse CDF to 8 correlated factor returns.

✓
Example: 8-factor Monte Carlo first trial
Calibrate multivariate normal from 374 months of factor returns. First simulated set: Earnings
Yield −3.2%, B/M −3.1%, Growth −0.2%, Momentum 0.7%, Revision 2.3%, ROE −3.3%,
Debt/Equity −1.7%, Quality 1.9%.     BM return = equal-weighted avg = −0.83%.     RP return =
weighted avg using May 2019 RP weights = −0.86%.     Repeat 1,000 times, compute
performance statistics from simulated distribution.



Page 11  of  18

heading: CONTRASTING METHODS & SENSITIVITY
body:
Historical Simulation vs Monte Carlo
Aspect
Historical Simulation
Monte Carlo Simulation

Distribution
assumption

Captures fat tails
Yes — as in data
Only if distribution assumes them

Comparing Results — Backtest vs HS vs MC (BM and RP)
Curriculum finding: Sharpe ratios are RELATIVELY INSENSITIVE to method used. CVaR is SENSITIVE —
Monte Carlo with multivariate normal UNDERSTATES downside risk for BM portfolio because factor
returns exhibit negative skewness and fat tails not captured by normal distribution.
The RP portfolio is ROBUST to distribution assumption because risk-contribution weighting naturally
dampens exposure to high-volatility factors regardless of distributional tails.
Sensitivity Analysis
Sensitivity analysis explores how target variables respond to changes in INPUT ASSUMPTIONS. Critical
complement to simulation — the simulation results are only as good as the distributional choice.

SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS
Re-run the simulation under DIFFERENT distributional assumptions to test robustness of
conclusions. Typical test: replace multivariate normal with multivariate SKEWED STUDENT'S
t-distribution — accounts for negative skewness and excess kurtosis.

Why Skewed Student's t-Distribution?
• Natural extension of normal distribution — nested within a broader family.
• Captures SKEWNESS — factor and asset returns are typically negatively skewed (larger losses
than gains of same probability).

Page 12  of  18
• Captures EXCESS KURTOSIS (fat tails) — normal distribution understates probability of extreme
events.
• Cost: more parameters to estimate — higher estimation error with limited data.
• Trade-off: bias-variance — more complex distributions have lower SPECIFICATION error (fit data
better) but higher ESTIMATION error (parameters uncertain).

✓
Sensitivity result — skewed t vs normal
Re-running MC with skewed t: first simulated set gives BM = 1.21%, RP = 0.75%. CVaR for BM
becomes materially LARGER than under multivariate normal — capturing the fat-tail risk that
normal missed. Confirms BM portfolio sensitivity to distributional assumption; RP results
remain robust.
Implications for Strategy Evaluation
• Simulation results are MODEL-DEPENDENT — different distribution = different conclusions.
Always run sensitivity analysis.
• Strategies robust across distributions: e.g., RP — suggests the result reflects genuine structure,
not distributional artifact.
• Strategies sensitive to distribution: flag for deeper investigation — conclusion may rely on
unrealistic assumptions.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
Four techniques — know them
Backtesting (historical performance), historical scenario analysis (regime-specific), simulation
(Monte Carlo + historical bootstrap), sensitivity analysis (distributional stress).

⚠
Backtesting = historical replay
Assumes future resembles past. Rolling-window approach: in-sample estimate → out-of-
sample test, repeat. DETERMINISTIC — single path through history.

⚠
Three steps to backtest
Strategy design (hypothesis + rules + parameters) → Historical investment simulation (form and
rebalance portfolios) → Output analysis (compute performance stats).

⚠
Key design parameters

Page 13  of  18
Investment universe, return definition (currency), benchmark, rebalancing frequency,
transaction costs, start/end dates. Each is a judgment call.

⚠
Transaction costs kill anomalies
Many documented "anomalies" disappear once transaction costs are modeled. Always
disclose whether included. Higher rebalancing frequency = higher costs.

⚠
Rebalancing trade-offs
Higher frequency: captures signals faster, but more costs + bid-ask bias + timing issues across
time zones. Monthly is industry norm.

⚠
Rolling window for RP uses procedure TWICE
First for factor returns, second for covariance matrix (for risk-parity weights). Single-use rolling
windows don't work for risk-weighted portfolios.

⚠
Earnings yield US vs Europe (1986-2019)
US: 9.24% return, 12.36% vol, Sharpe 0.75. Europe: 6.65% return, 9.90% vol, Sharpe 0.67. Max
drawdown ~3× vol in both.

⚠
Long-short hedged drawback
Requires ability to SHORT. Not all managers can short. Many mandates prohibit it.

⚠
Eight canonical factors
Defensive value (EY), cyclical value (B/M), growth (EPS growth), momentum (12M-1), analyst
sentiment (3M revision), profitability (ROE), leverage (D/E), earnings quality (accruals).

⚠
BM vs RP comparison
BM = equal-weight factors. RP = equal-risk-contribution weights. RP has dramatically lower
downside risk — max drawdown 3.8% vs 22.6% for BM.

⚠
Survivorship bias — magnitude
<13% of 1985 Russell 3000 remain in 2019. About 20% of 1989 S&P BMI Europe stocks
remain. Survivors biased toward success.

⚠
Point-in-time data is the fix

Page 14  of  18
Use historical constituents as they existed at each date. Include casualties. Critical for
accurate backtesting.

⚠
Low-volatility anomaly flip
With point-in-time Russell 3000: low-vol outperforms. With survivor-only universe: high-vol
outperforms 5.5×. Survivorship bias can REVERSE conclusions.

⚠
Look-ahead bias sources
(1) No reporting lag (assuming data available on report date, not actual release date). (2)
Database back-fills: historical data added for newly-added firms.

⚠
Reporting lag by region
US/Canada/Japan: 1-2 months. Europe/UK/ANZ: 2-3 months. Asia ex-Japan/LATAM/EMEA: ~3
months. Reflects regional reporting timeliness.

⚠
Data snooping definition
Subconscious or conscious data manipulation to produce low p-values. Arises from multiple
testing without correction, reverse engineering, publication bias.

⚠
Cross-validation defense
Test strategy in different geographies, time periods, asset classes. Robust strategy → works
across contexts. RP works in 7 of 10 global markets in curriculum example.

⚠
Historical scenario regimes
Expansion/recession (NBER), high/low volatility (VIX vs 5-year MA), rate regimes, inflation
regimes. Don't rely on single long-window averages.

⚠
Recession indicator lag
NBER declares recession starts/ends only in hindsight, often with 6-18 month lag. Dec 2007
start not declared until Dec 2008.

⚠
BM vs RP regimes
BM struggles in recession (lower Sharpe). RP robust across recession and expansion. RP stable
across high/low volatility regimes; BM sensitive.


Page 15  of  18
⚠
Simulation vs backtesting
Backtesting = deterministic single path. Simulation = many random paths. Simulation relaxes
the assumption that past sequence will repeat.

⚠
Two simulation types
Historical simulation (bootstrapping — resample actual data). Monte Carlo (draw from fitted
distribution — normal, t, etc.).

⚠
Bootstrapping = WITH replacement
Sampling with replacement so # of simulations can exceed # of historical observations. Uses
actual data, preserves empirical properties.

⚠
MC calibration
Step 4 = fit statistical distribution to historical data. Multivariate normal: K means, K std devs,
K(K−1)/2 correlations. Step 5 = draw from fitted distribution.

⚠
MC assumes distribution
Historical simulation makes no distributional assumption. MC requires specifying distribution
form. If wrong distribution, MC can MISSTATE risk.

⚠
Sharpe insensitive to method
Curriculum result: Sharpe ratios similar across backtest, HS, MC (all three show RP better).
Indicates Sharpe robust to methodology.

⚠
CVaR sensitive to method
MC with multivariate normal UNDERSTATES downside risk for BM (due to fat tails in true
distribution). HS and backtest agree — MC with normal disagrees.

⚠
RP robust to distribution
Risk-parity structure dampens sensitivity to distributional assumption. Equal-weight BM more
vulnerable — naive weighting doesn't account for factor risk structure.

⚠
Sensitivity analysis tests robustness
Re-run simulation under different distributions (e.g., normal → skewed t). If conclusions change,
results are distribution-dependent.


Page 16  of  18
⚠
Skewed Student's t captures reality
Asset returns: negative skewness + excess kurtosis. Multivariate normal misses both. Skewed t
captures both — natural upgrade.

⚠
Bias-variance trade-off in distributions
Complex distributions: low specification error (fit data) but high estimation error (parameters
uncertain). Simple distributions: opposite. Choice depends on sample size.

⚠
Survivorship + Look-ahead + Data snooping
The three systematic backtesting biases. All three inflate backtest performance vs reality. Must
be addressed explicitly.

⚠
Publication bias relates to data snooping
Successful strategies published, failures disappear. Published anomalies frequently fail to
replicate when tested on new data.

⚠
Metrics and visuals
Sharpe ratio, volatility, max drawdown, VaR, CVaR. Visuals: cumulative wealth, rolling 12M
returns, distribution plots, regime overlays.

⚠
Excess kurtosis = fat tails
More extreme outcomes than normal distribution. Negative skew + excess kurtosis = negative
surprises more likely than positive extreme events.

⚠
Structural breaks
Visible when plotting cumulative returns — flat periods, sudden drawdowns. Examples: tech
bubble late 1990s, GFC March-May 2009. Suggest regime change.

⚠
Quantitative AND fundamental use backtesting
Systematic strategies are the natural fit, but fundamental managers backtest stock-selection
criteria before using them.

⚠
Not all strategies benefit from simulation
Simulation adds most value when past may not recur OR when limited historical data. For well-
understood stable regimes, backtesting may suffice.


Page 17  of  18
⚠
Multivariate needed for multi-asset
Factors are correlated. Must specify MULTIVARIATE distribution (captures correlations), not
independent univariate marginals.

⚠
Model calibration = parameter estimation
Step in MC where you estimate distribution parameters from historical data. "Calibrate to a
multivariate normal" = estimate means, std devs, covariances.


FORMULAS & REFERENCE SHEET

Backtesting Process
(1) Strategy Design: hypothesis, rules, key parameters. (2) Historical Investment Simulation: rolling-
window construction and rebalancing. (3) Output Analysis: performance metrics.
Key Performance Metrics
Sharpe ratio = (R_p − R_f) / σ_p. VaR(α) = α-percentile loss. CVaR(α) = E[loss | loss > VaR]. Max
drawdown = largest peak-to-trough decline.
Long-Short Quintile Construction
Rank stocks by factor. Long top 20%, short bottom 20%. Equal or cap-weighted within quintile. Requires
ability to short.
Bias Sources and Fixes
Survivorship → use point-in-time constituents. Look-ahead → enforce reporting lag (1-3 months). Data
snooping → cross-validate across markets/periods/asset classes.
Historical Simulation (Bootstrapping)
Draw with replacement from historical observations. Non-deterministic version of rolling-window
backtest. No distributional assumption.
Monte Carlo Simulation
Fit statistical distribution (typically multivariate normal or skewed Student's t) to historical data. Draw N
simulations. K assets: K means + K std devs + K(K−1)/2 correlations.
Sensitivity Analysis
Re-run simulation under alternative distributional assumption. Skewed Student's t captures negative
skew + excess kurtosis. Tests robustness of conclusions.
Regime Classifications
Expansion/recession (NBER). High/low volatility (VIX vs 5-yr MA). Rate regimes. Inflation regimes.


Page 18  of  18
End of Notes — LM6 Backtesting and Simulation

values. Square of this = R².

heading: GOODNESS OF FIT
body:
R², Adjusted R², AIC, and BIC

R² (Coefficient of
Determination)
R² = SSR / SST = Σ(Ŷᵢ − Ȳ)² / Σ(Yᵢ − Ȳ)²
SSR = sum of squares regression; SST = sum of squares total. Ranges from 0 to 1.
Problem: always increases (or stays same) as more IVs are added.

Adjusted R² (R̄ ²)
R̄ ² = 1 − [(n−1)/(n−k−1)] × (1 − R²)
k = number of independent variables. Adjusts for degrees of freedom. Can be
negative. Always less than R² when k ≥ 1.

KEY RULE: Adding a new variable to a regression:

Adjusted R² Rule for Adding Variables
If |t-statistic| of new variable's coefficient > 1.0  →  Adjusted R² INCREASES
If |t-statistic| of new variable's coefficient < 1.0  →  Adjusted R² DECREASES
This threshold of |t| = 1.0 is much lower than typical significance levels (5% or 1%), so adjusted R² is
not a substitute for significance testing.
R² ALWAYS increases (or stays the same) regardless of significance of the added variable.
In a multiple regression, adjusted R² cannot be interpreted as 'percentage of variation explained' in the
same clean way as R² in simple regression.


Akaike's Information
Criterion (AIC)
AIC = n·ln(SSE/n) + 2(k+1)
Lower AIC = better fitting model. Penalty = 2(k+1). Use for PREDICTION purposes.

Schwarz's Bayesian
Information Criterion
(BIC)
BIC = n·ln(SSE/n) + ln(n)·(k+1)
Lower BIC = better fitting model. Penalty = ln(n)·(k+1) > 2(k+1), so BIC penalises
complexity MORE than AIC. Use for best GOODNESS OF FIT.

Metric
Direction
When to Use
Penalty
R²
Higher = better
NEVER alone — always increases
with more variables
None
Adjusted R²
Higher = better
Compare models with same
dependent variable

adjustment)

Choosing the best model for
PREDICTION

FIT/parsimony
ln(n)·(k+1) — larger penalty than
AIC

IMPORTANT: The absolute values of AIC and BIC are meaningless in isolation. Only the RELATIVE values
across models with the same dependent variable matter. In the five-factor portfolio example: Factors 1 and 4
together produced the best model — lowest BIC, lowest AIC, and highest adjusted R².

!
BIC vs. AIC for model selection: BIC imposes a LARGER penalty for additional parameters
because ln(n) > 2 for any n > 7. So BIC will almost always favour a MORE PARSIMONIOUS
model than AIC. If BIC and AIC agree on the best model, the evidence is strong. If they disagree,
AIC's choice is better for prediction; BIC's choice is better for fit.

!
R² problem: A model with high R² may be BAD (overfit; coefficients biased). A model with low R²
may be GOOD (e.g., many asset pricing models have low R²). Do not use R² alone to judge
model quality.

Note: SST = SSR + SSE. Also, R² = SSR/SST.


t-Tests for Individual Coefficients

t-Test for Coefficient
bⱼ
t = (b̂ ⱼ − Bⱼ) / sb̂ ⱼ   with (n − k − 1) degrees of freedom
Bⱼ = hypothesized value (usually 0). Default software output tests H₀: bⱼ = 0 vs. Hₐ: bⱼ
≠ 0.

To test against a value other than zero: either substitute the hypothesized value into the formula above, OR check
whether the hypothesized value falls within the confidence interval for the coefficient.

Joint F-Test: Testing a Subset of Coefficients

The joint F-test compares an UNRESTRICTED model (full model) with a RESTRICTED model (fewer variables).
The null hypothesis is that all the coefficients excluded from the restricted model are jointly zero.

Joint F-Test Statistic
F = [(SSE_R − SSE_U)/q] / [SSE_U/(n−k−1)]
SSE_R = SSE of restricted model; SSE_U = SSE of unrestricted model; q =
number of restrictions (variables excluded from restricted model); k = total number
of IVs in unrestricted model

Joint F-Test — Step-by-Step (Factors 4 and 5 Example)
Unrestricted model: Y = b₀ + b₁X₁ + b₂X₂ + b₃X₃ + b₄X₄ + b₅X₅ + ε (all 5 factors)
Restricted model: Y = b₀ + b₁X₁ + b₂X₂ + b₃X₃ + ε (factors 4 and 5 excluded)
H₀: b₄ = b₅ = 0  vs.  Hₐ: At least one of b₄, b₅ ≠ 0
q = 2 (two restrictions); degrees of freedom = q and n − k − 1 = 44
SSE_R = 64.5176 (restricted, 3 factors);  SSE_U = 56.6182 (unrestricted, 5 factors)
F = [(64.5176 − 56.6182)/2] / [56.6182/44] = 3.9497/1.2868 = 3.0694
Critical F-value (1% level, 2 and 44 df) = 5.120
Decision: 3.0694 < 5.120 → FAIL TO REJECT H₀. Factors 4 and 5 together do NOT provide sufficient
explanatory power.

!
Joint F-test vs. individual t-tests: You CANNOT infer joint significance from individual t-tests when
variables are correlated. Two variables may each be insignificant individually yet jointly significant
(or vice versa). Always perform the joint F-test to assess a group of variables.

General Linear F-Test (Full Model Significance)

A special case of the joint F-test where ALL k slope coefficients are jointly tested: H₀: b₁ = b₂ = … = bₖ = 0 vs. Hₐ:
at least one bⱼ ≠ 0.


General Linear F-
Statistic
F = MSR / MSE = [SSR/k] / [SSE/(n−k−1)]   with k and n−k−1 degrees of
freedom
This is the F-statistic reported in the ANOVA table of any regression output. A
significant F-statistic means the overall model explains a statistically significant
portion of Y's variation.

Summary: In the portfolio returns model, F = 18.1247/1.2868 = 14.0853. Critical F (5%, 5 and 44 df) = 2.427.
Since 14.0853 > 2.427, REJECT H₀ — the model is overall significant.
