heading: FORECASTING USING MULTIPLE REGRESSION
body:
Prediction Formula
Ŷf = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁X₁f + b̂ ₂X₂f + … + b̂ ₖXₖf = b̂ ₀ + Σ b̂ ⱼXⱼf
Xⱼf = assumed/forecasted value for each independent variable in the forecast
period.

Forecasting — Critical Rules
Rule 1: If a regression is estimated with k independent variables, ALL k must be included in the
prediction — even the statistically insignificant ones. Their correlations with other variables were used
in coefficient estimation.
Rule 2: Always include the INTERCEPT term in the prediction.
Rule 3: If the independent variables themselves are forecasts (out-of-sample), there is ADDITIONAL
sampling error from forecasting the IVs, which WIDENS the confidence interval around Ŷf beyond the
within-sample error.
Rule 4: The standard error of the forecast > standard error of the regression. Therefore, the prediction
interval around Ŷf is wider than within-sample error bands.
Rule 5: The calculation of exact forecast confidence intervals is software-dependent; the intuition is
that both model error and sampling error in the IVs contribute to total forecast uncertainty.

Forecasting — Five-Factor Model Example
Ŷf = −2.1876 + 1.5992(0.110) + 0.1923(0.040) − 0.7126(0.080) + 3.3376(−0.010) − 2.6832(0.001)

= −2.1876 + 0.1759 + 0.0077 − 0.0570 − 0.0334 − 0.0027 = −2.0971%
Standard error of the forecast = 1.1466 (larger than standard error of the regression)
95% confidence interval: [−4.4098, +0.2119]
The wide confidence interval reflects both model uncertainty and uncertainty in the IV forecasts.

!
Do NOT drop insignificant variables from the prediction equation. If the model was estimated with
those variables, the coefficients on ALL variables reflect the correlation structure among ALL
variables. Dropping a variable and re-predicting gives a different (and arguably worse) result than
using all variables in the original estimated model.

heading: MODEL SPECIFICATION ERRORS
body:
Model specification = the set of variables included and the regression equation's functional form. Correct
specification requires economic reasoning — not data mining alone.

Principle
Explanation
Economic reasoning
Provide an economic rationale behind choice of variables — not just statistical
significance.
Parsimony
Each variable must play an essential role. More variables is not better.
Good out-of-sample
performance
A model that overfits the training sample will fail in prediction. Validate on held-
out data.
Appropriate functional
form
If a nonlinear relationship is expected, model it explicitly (e.g., use logarithms,
quadratic terms).
Satisfies regression
assumptions
If heteroskedasticity, serial correlation, or multicollinearity are detected, revise
the model.

Four Failures in Regression Functional Form

Failure
Explanation
Consequence
Omitted variables
Important variable(s)
excluded from the
regression.
If omitted variable is CORRELATED with an included IV
→ coefficient estimates for the included IVs are BIASED
AND INCONSISTENT. Standard errors are also wrong.
If omitted variable is UNCORRELATED with included
IVs → intercept is biased, but slope coefficients for
included IVs remain unbiased.
Inappropriate form of
variables
Ignoring nonlinearity —
modelling a nonlinear
May lead to heteroskedasticity in residuals. Model
systematically over- or under-predicts for some ranges.

relationship as linear. Fix:
take natural logarithm of
the variable to represent it
as a proportional change.
Inappropriate
variable scaling
Using unscaled data
when scaled data are
more appropriate (e.g.,
raw financial statement
data vs. common-size
ratios for cross-company
comparison).
May cause heteroskedasticity or multicollinearity.
Inappropriate data
pooling
Pooling data from
samples that should not
be combined (e.g.,
spanning structural
breaks — regulatory
changes, regime shifts
from low-volatility to high-
volatility).
May cause heteroskedasticity or serial correlation.
Residuals will show discrete clusters.

!
Omitted variable bias — the asymmetry: If the omitted variable is CORRELATED with included
IVs, the estimated coefficients on ALL included IVs are biased and inconsistent — essentially
garbage for statistical inference. If it is UNCORRELATED with included IVs, only the intercept is
biased, but slope coefficients remain unbiased. Always check: is the residual correlated with
plausible omitted variables?

heading: HETEROSKEDASTICITY
body:
Heteroskedasticity = the variance of regression residuals differs across observations, violating the
homoskedasticity assumption.

Type
Definition
Effect on Inference
Unconditional
heteroskedasticity
Error variance is NOT correlated
with the independent variables.
Violates a regression assumption, but
creates NO MAJOR problems for
statistical inference. Coefficient estimates
remain consistent.
Conditional
heteroskedasticity
Error variance IS correlated with
(conditional on) the values of the
independent variables.
SERIOUS PROBLEM: Standard errors of
coefficients are UNDERESTIMATED → t-
statistics are INFLATED → more Type I
errors (rejecting H₀ when it's true). The F-
test for overall model significance is also
unreliable.

!
Conditional vs. unconditional heteroskedasticity: Only CONDITIONAL heteroskedasticity
damages statistical inference by biasing standard errors downward (inflating t-statistics).
Unconditional heteroskedasticity is merely a technical violation of an assumption — it does not
affect the reliability of t-tests or F-tests.

Detecting Conditional Heteroskedasticity: Breusch–Pagan (BP) Test


Breusch–Pagan Test — Three Steps
Step 1: Run the original regression: Yt = b₀ + b₁X₁t + b₂X₂t + ut
Step 2: Square the fitted residuals from Step 1. Run a new regression: ût² = a₀ + a₁X₁t + a₂X₂t + εt
Step 3: H₀: a₁ = a₂ = 0 (no conditional heteroskedasticity) vs. Hₐ: at least one aⱼ ≠ 0
If heteroskedasticity exists: the IVs explain a significant portion of the variation in the squared
residuals.

BP Test Statistic
χ²_BP,k = n × R²   (chi-square distributed, k degrees of freedom)
R² is from the Step 2 regression. k = number of IVs in the original model. One-tail,
right-side test.

BP Test Example — XYZ Fama–French Three-Factor Model
Original model: XYZ_RETRF = b₀ + b₁MKTRF + b₂SMB + b₃HML + ε
BP test statistic = 13.40264;  P-value = 0.00038
Since P-value < 0.01 → REJECT H₀ at 1% → conditional heteroskedastic residuals detected
Without correction: MKTRF std error = 0.060, SMB std error = 0.104
With robust (White-corrected) standard errors: MKTRF std error = 0.091, SMB std error = 0.111
NOTE: The COEFFICIENTS are unchanged by the correction — only the standard errors change.
Larger standard errors → smaller t-statistics → less likely to find false significance.

Correcting Conditional Heteroskedasticity

The standard correction is to use ROBUST STANDARD ERRORS (also called: heteroskedasticity-consistent
standard errors, White-corrected standard errors). These adjust the coefficient standard errors to account for the
heteroskedasticity. The coefficients themselves do NOT change — only their standard errors are corrected.
Available in all major software packages.

Market efficiency note: If heteroskedasticity (e.g., volatility clustering) is observed in financial data, it may
represent a TRADEABLE OPPORTUNITY — there may be predictable patterns in volatility that can be exploited
to generate alpha. This is the basis for ARCH/GARCH modelling.

heading: SERIAL CORRELATION
body:
Serial correlation (autocorrelation) = regression errors are correlated across observations. Most commonly arises
in TIME-SERIES regressions.

Type
Definition
Effect

Positive serial correlation
A positive residual for observation t
increases the probability of a positive
residual at t+1 (stable pattern).
MOST COMMON type in financial
data.
Standard errors underestimated → t-
statistics inflated → more Type I errors.
F-test (MSE-based) unreliable.
Negative serial correlation A positive residual increases the
probability of a negative residual in
the next period (alternating pattern).
Less common.
Same effects as positive serial
correlation on standard errors.

Serial Correlation Scenario
Effect on Coefficients
Effect on Standard Errors
No lagged dependent variable as
IV
Coefficient estimates
CONSISTENT (valid)
Standard errors BIASED — t-tests
unreliable
Lagged dependent variable IS
one of the IVs
Coefficient estimates
INCONSISTENT (invalid)
Standard errors BIASED — ALL
inference invalid

!
Serial correlation with a lagged dependent variable: If any IV is a lagged value of Y (e.g., Yt−1),
then serial correlation causes ALL coefficient estimates to be INCONSISTENT — not just the
standard errors. This is a much more serious situation than serial correlation without a lagged DV.

Detecting Serial Correlation: Breusch–Godfrey (BG) Test

The Durbin–Watson (DW) test can only detect first-order serial correlation. The Breusch–Godfrey (BG) test is
more robust — it can detect autocorrelation up to a pre-specified lag order p.

Breusch–Godfrey Test — Three Steps
Step 1: Run original regression: Yt = b₀ + b₁X₁t + b₂X₂t + ut
Step 2: Run new regression with lagged residuals as additional IVs: ût = a₀ + a₁X₁t + a₂X₂t + ρ₁ût−₁ +
et  (for order p=1)
Step 3: H₀: ρ₁ = 0 (no serial correlation up to lag p) vs. Hₐ: ρ₁ ≠ 0
Test statistic is F-distributed with (n − p − k − 1) and p degrees of freedom.
Compare F-statistic to critical F-value at chosen significance level.
If F-statistic > critical F-value → REJECT H₀ → serial correlation present.

Correcting Serial Correlation

CORRECTION: Use ROBUST STANDARD ERRORS that are also autocorrelation-consistent — known as:
Newey–West standard errors, serial-correlation consistent standard errors, or HAC (heteroskedasticity and
autocorrelation consistent) standard errors. These also correct for conditional heteroskedasticity simultaneously.

KEY: Robust standard errors CORRECT the standard errors (making them LARGER than OLS standard errors).
The coefficient estimates are UNCHANGED. The resulting smaller t-statistics reflect the true level of uncertainty
in the parameter estimates.


Market efficiency note: Serial correlation in financial data suggests predictability → opportunity to exploit before
other market participants eliminate it. Efficient markets (even weak form) imply serial correlation should not
persist.

heading: MULTICOLLINEARITY
body:
Multicollinearity = two or more independent variables are so highly correlated that they share so much explanatory
power that individual coefficients cannot be estimated precisely. Approximate (not exact) linear relationships are
the concern here.

Effect of
Multicollinearity
Description
Coefficient estimates
Consistent — the estimates are valid! But they are IMPRECISE and may
fluctuate widely with small data changes.
Standard errors
INFLATED — standard errors of coefficient estimates are too large.
t-statistics
REDUCED — because standard errors are inflated, t-statistics are deflated →
less likely to reject H₀ even when a variable IS truly significant.
Overall model fit
Unaffected — R², adjusted R², and F-statistic for the overall model may still be
high and significant.
Classic symptom
HIGH R² and significant F-test for the whole model, BUT insignificant individual
t-statistics for the slope coefficients.

!
Multicollinearity does NOT make coefficient estimates biased — they remain consistent. The
problem is IMPRECISION (inflated standard errors). This is different from serial correlation (which
biases standard errors) and omitted variable bias (which biases the coefficients themselves).
Know the difference.

Detecting Multicollinearity: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)

Variance Inflation
Factor
VIFⱼ = 1 / (1 − Rⱼ²)
Rⱼ² = R² from regressing Xⱼ on all OTHER independent variables. Measures how
much of Xⱼ's variation is explained by the other IVs.

VIF Value
Interpretation
Action
VIFⱼ = 1
Xⱼ has NO correlation with other IVs. No
multicollinearity for this variable.
No action needed.
VIFⱼ < 5
Low to moderate correlation. Generally acceptable.
No action needed.
VIFⱼ > 5
Warrants FURTHER INVESTIGATION of this variable.
Investigate carefully.
VIFⱼ > 10
Indicates SERIOUS multicollinearity requiring
correction.
Must correct.

FSPTX Fund Example — Multicollinearity Illustration

Original model: FSPTX = b₀ + b₁·SGX + b₂·SVX + ε  (2 IVs: growth and value indexes)
VIF for SGX = 3.97;  VIF for SVX = 3.97  →  Both below 5 → no multicollinearity
REVISED model: FSPTX = b₀ + b₁·SGX + b₂·SVX + b₃·SPX + ε  (added S&P 500 total index)
PROBLEM: S&P 500 = weighted blend of SGX + SVX → near-exact linear combination → severe
multicollinearity
VIF for SGX = 3,863;  VIF for SVX = 3,083;  VIF for SPX = 12,904  →  Extreme multicollinearity!
Effect: Standard errors jump from 0.196 to 6.166 (SGX) and 5.503 (SVX) — coefficients become
insignificant despite high R²
Classic symptom: F-test still significant; individual t-tests all insignificant

Correcting Multicollinearity

Three Remedies for Multicollinearity
1. EXCLUDE one or more of the correlated variables (most common). Choose the most economically
meaningful variable and drop the redundant one.
2. USE A DIFFERENT PROXY for one of the correlated variables (e.g., if total assets and equity are
both in the model, replace one with a ratio of them).
3. INCREASE THE SAMPLE SIZE — more data reduces the relative imprecision caused by
collinearity.
NOTE: If the goal is PREDICTION (not understanding individual variable effects), multicollinearity may
NOT be a major issue — the model can still predict well even if individual coefficients are imprecise.

!
Exam distinction between the three violations: (1) Heteroskedasticity and serial correlation both
INFLATE t-statistics (by underestimating standard errors) → Type I errors. (2) Multicollinearity
DEFLATES t-statistics (by overestimating standard errors) → Type II errors. The direction of bias
in t-statistics is opposite!

heading: ), but conclusion unchanged
body:
KEY: Removing Observation 7 does not flip the conclusion → it is potentially influential but the
conclusion is robust

Detecting Outliers: Studentized Deleted Residuals

Studentized Deleted
Residual
tᵢ* = eᵢ / √[MSE(i) × (1 − hᵢᵢ)]
eᵢ = residual from original regression; MSE(i) = MSE when observation i is deleted;
hᵢᵢ = leverage. Follows t-distribution with (n − k − 2) degrees of freedom.

Rule of Thumb
Action
|tᵢ*| > 3
Flag observation as a potential outlier — investigate further
|tᵢ*| > critical t-value (n−k−2 df, chosen
significance level)
Flag outlier as potentially influential — take action

!
Degrees of freedom for studentized residuals: Use (n − k − 2), NOT (n − k − 1). The extra −1 is
because one observation is sequentially deleted in the computation. In the ROA example with
n=26, k=3: df = 26 − 3 − 2 = 21. Critical t at 5% level = ±2.080. Observations 3 and 5 exceed this
threshold.

REMEDIES for influential observations: (1) Data entry errors → correct or discard. (2) Valid extreme values →
investigate whether important variables are omitted or whether regression assumptions are being violated. (3)
WINSORIZATION — replace extreme values with a percentile value (e.g., replace values above the 99th
percentile with the 99th percentile value).

heading: + 0.020 = 0.085% per year
body:
INTERPRETATION: For growth funds, total extra return per year of age = 0.085% vs. 0.065% for
value funds.

Seasonality with Dummy Variables — Monthly Returns Example
To test monthly seasonality with 12 months: include 11 monthly dummies (January through
November); DECEMBER is the base month.
The intercept coefficient represents December's average excess return.
Each monthly dummy coefficient = difference in return for that month vs. December.
In the portfolio example: F-statistic for the model = 0.879 (P-value = 0.563) → NOT significant.
Adjusted R² is NEGATIVE.
Conclusion: No evidence of monthly seasonality in the portfolio.

Calculating and Interpreting Probabilities from Logistic Regression

Marginal Effect Calculation — Share Repurchase Example
Logit model: Repurchase = −0.4738 − 0.9118·CASH − 0.3186·DE + 0.9407·NPM

Initial probability at mean values: P = 1/{1 + exp[−(−0.4738 − 0.9118(0.083) − 0.3186(0.9182) +

Increase CASH by 0.01 (from 0.083 to 0.093), hold DE and NPM at means: New P = 28.87%
Marginal effect of CASH: 28.87% − 29.06% = −0.19% (increasing cash-to-assets by 1% decreases
repurchase probability by 0.19%)
Increase NPM by 0.01 (from −0.0535 to −0.0435): New P = 29.26%. Marginal effect = +0.20%
Increase DE by 0.01 (from 0.9182 to 0.9282): New P = 29.00%. Marginal effect = −0.07%
KEY: Marginal effects change depending on the starting values of all IVs — they are NOT constant.

!
Logistic regression marginal effects: Unlike OLS where the slope coefficient directly gives the
effect on Y, in logistic regression the COEFFICIENT gives the effect on the LOG ODDS, NOT on
the probability. To find the effect on probability, you must calculate the probability at the starting
values, then increment the variable of interest, and subtract. The marginal effect depends on
WHERE you are on the S-curve.

!
Logistic regression model comparison: A higher log-likelihood (closer to zero) indicates a
BETTER-fitting model. Log-likelihood is always negative. Model 2 with log-likelihood −450.40 is
BETTER than Model 1 with −451.66. Do not confuse 'higher' with 'less negative' — they mean the
same thing here.

!
Pseudo-R² limitation: Pseudo-R² can ONLY be used to compare different specifications of the
SAME model (same dataset, same dependent variable). It cannot be compared across different
datasets or different dependent variables.

heading: TREND MODELS
body:
Trend models predict the value of a time series as a linear function of time only. Two types: linear and log-linear.
Estimated using OLS with t = 1, 2, ..., T.

Linear Trend Model
yt = b₀ + b₁t + εt    →    Predicted: ŷt = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁t

b₁ = CONSTANT AMOUNT of change per period. Use when series
grows/declines by a constant dollar amount.

Log-Linear Trend Model
ln yt = b₀ + b₁t + εt    →    Predicted: ŷt = e^(b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁t)
b₁ = constant log change; implied growth rate = e^b₁ − 1 per period. Use when
series grows at a CONSTANT RATE.

Feature
Linear Trend
Log-Linear Trend
What it models
Grows by constant AMOUNT per period
Grows at constant RATE per period
(exponential)
Prediction formula
ŷt = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁t
ŷt = e^(b̂ ₀+b̂ ₁t); estimate ln yt = b₀ + b₁t,
then exponentiate
Growth implied
Constant absolute change per period
Constant rate: e^b₁ − 1 per period
When to use
Series plots as a straight line
Series curves upward; plotting log of
series looks like straight line
Both models' limitation
Residuals may be serially correlated
(DW significantly different from 2) →
model invalid
Same — always test DW after
estimating trend models

Starbucks Sales — Why Linear Fails, Log-Linear Better
Linear model: R²=0.9603, DW=0.40. Residuals persistently positive 2001–2008, persistently negative
2008–2015 → serial correlation → INVALID even with high R²
Log-linear model: R²=0.9771, DW=0.26. Log of sales tracks the trend with no persistent deviations →
better fit
BUT log-linear DW = 0.26 also fails (critical value ~1.60) → serial correlation still present → need AR
model
Out-of-sample prediction (log-linear, t=75): ln ŷ = 6.7617 + 0.0295(75) = 8.9742 → ŷ = e^8.9742 =
$7,896.7M
Linear prediction (t=75): ŷ = 137.4213 + 80.2060(75) = $6,152.87M — dramatically lower than log-
linear

!
High R² does NOT validate a trend model. If the DW statistic is significantly below 2.0, residuals
are serially correlated and the model FAILS the assumptions of OLS, regardless of R². Must also
test out-of-sample performance.

heading: COVARIANCE STATIONARITY
body:
Three Requirements for Covariance Stationarity
Requirement 1: E(yt) = μ, constant and finite for ALL periods t
Requirement 2: var(yt) = σ², constant and finite for ALL periods t
Requirement 3: cov(yt, yt-s) = λs, a constant finite value for all t and all fixed lags s

ALL THREE must hold. Violation of ANY → non-stationary → AR model yields SPURIOUS results
PRACTICAL CHECK: Plot the series. Trend (non-constant mean) or seasonality (non-constant mean)
or volatility clustering (non-constant variance) = likely non-stationary

Time Series
Stationary?
Reason
CPI inflation (monthly)
Approximately
yes
Mean and variance roughly constant over the
sample
Starbucks raw quarterly sales
NO
Mean increases over time → Requirement 1
violated (non-constant mean)
Retail sales (unadjusted monthly)
NO
Seasonal spikes every December + overall upward
trend → non-constant mean
Random walk (xt = xt-₁ + εt)
NO
var(xt) = (t-1)σ² → variance grows without bound →
Requirement 2 violated
First-differenced random walk (yt = xt
− xt-₁)
YES
Mean=0, variance=σ² (constant), autocov=0 for all
non-zero lags

!
Estimating AR model on non-stationary series: ALL coefficient estimates AND standard errors are
INVALID. Results appear significant but are spurious. Testing for stationarity must come
BEFORE estimating any AR model.

heading: AR MODELS, RESIDUAL AUTOCORRELATIONS & MEAN
body:
REVERSION (LOS 4, 5 & 6)

AR Model Structure

AR(1) Model
xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + εt
One lagged value of xt as the independent variable. Note: DW statistic is
INVALID here.

AR(p) Model
xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + b₂xt-₂ + ... + bpxt-p + εt
p lagged values. Each additional lag costs one degree of freedom.

Testing AR Model Fit: Residual Autocorrelations

Residual Autocorrelation
t-Test
t_k = ρ̂_{ε,k} / (1/√T)    where SE = 1/√T
If |t_k| > critical value (≈2.0) at ANY lag k → significant serial correlation in
residuals → model MISSPECIFIED → upgrade to higher-order AR.

Intel Gross Margin — AR(1) Correctly Specified
AR(1): Gross Margint = 0.1513 + 0.7462·Gross Margint-₁ (T=65)

SE of residual autocorrelations = 1/√65 = 0.1240
Lag 1–4 t-stats: 1.055, -1.682, 0.308, 0.490 — none exceed critical value ~2.0
Conclusion: No serial correlation → AR(1) correctly specified → use for forecasting

CPI Inflation — AR(1) Rejected, AR(2) Accepted
AR(1): Inflationt = 1.3346 + 0.3984·Inflationt-₁ (T=287)
SE = 1/√287 = 0.0590. Critical t ≈ 1.97
Lag 2 residual autocorrelation t-stat = -2.80 → |2.80| > 1.97 → SIGNIFICANT → AR(1)
MISSPECIFIED
AR(2): Inflationt = 1.5996 + 0.4759·Inflationt-₁ − 0.1964·Inflationt-₂
AR(2) residual autocorrelation t-stats (lags 1–4): 0.054, 0.071, -0.570, 0.262 — none significant
Conclusion: AR(2) correctly specified for 1995–2018 sample

Mean Reversion

Mean-Reverting Level
(AR(1))
x* = b₀ / (1 − b₁)
If xt > x*: model predicts xt will FALL. If xt < x*: model predicts xt will RISE. If
b₁=1: mean-reverting level is UNDEFINED → non-stationary (random walk).

Intel Mean Reversion
AR(1): b₀=0.1513, b₁=0.7462
Mean-reverting level = 0.1513 / (1 − 0.7462) = 0.1513 / 0.2538 = 59.61%
If current gross margin > 59.61%: model predicts margin will FALL toward 59.61%
If current margin = 63%: ŷt+1 = 0.1513 + 0.7462(0.63) = 0.6214 = 62.14% (falling toward mean)
Chain rule — two-period forecast: ŷt+2 = 0.1513 + 0.7462(0.6214) = 0.6150 = 61.50%

Chain Rule of Forecasting
x̂ t+1 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁xt  (one period; use actual xt)    x̂ t+2 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁x̂ t+1  (two
periods; use FORECAST of t+1)
Uncertainty COMPOUNDS: two-period forecasts are more uncertain than one-
period forecasts.

heading: FORECAST PERFORMANCE & COEFFICIENT
body:
INSTABILITY (LOS 7 & 8)

Concept
Definition
Application
In-sample forecasts
Fitted values (predicted vs. actual)
WITHIN the estimation period. Errors =
residuals.
Use standard error of regression for
comparison within sample. Smaller
SE = better in-sample fit.

Out-of-sample
forecasts
Predictions for periods OUTSIDE the
estimation period.
More important for real-world
evaluation. Compare models using
RMSE on the same out-of-sample
window.
RMSE
Root Mean Squared Error = √[Σ(actual −
forecast)²/n]. LOWER = more accurate.
Choose model with smaller RMSE.
Must compare models on the same
out-of-sample period to be fair.

CPI Inflation: AR(1) vs AR(2) — Out-of-Sample RMSE
Out-of-sample period: January–September 2019 (9 observations)
AR(1) RMSE = 1.9014;   AR(2) RMSE = 1.4157
AR(2) RMSE = 74.46% of AR(1) RMSE → AR(2) is 25.54% more accurate out-of-sample
AR(2) also more accurate in-sample (SE = 2.9208 vs 2.9687 for AR(1))
Consistent evidence in-sample AND out-of-sample → AR(2) is the better model

COEFFICIENT INSTABILITY: Regression coefficients can change substantially across different sample periods.
An AR(1) may be best for 2008–2018 but AR(2) for 1995–2018. The Federal Reserve's response to the 2008
crisis created two distinct regimes — combining them into one model can be inappropriate. Use plots and
economic knowledge to identify structural breaks; estimate separate models for each regime.

!
Out-of-sample RMSE is the gold standard for evaluating forecast models. In-sample fit can be
gamed by adding more lags (overfitting). A model with lower in-sample SE but higher out-of-
sample RMSE is OVERFIT and will perform poorly in practice.

heading: RANDOM WALKS AND UNIT ROOTS
body:
Random Walk Properties

Random Walk (no drift)
xt = xt-₁ + εt    (b₀=0, b₁=1)
Best forecast = xt-₁ (current value). Changes are completely unpredictable.
Common for exchange rates and stock prices.

Random Walk With Drift
xt = b₀ + xt-₁ + εt    (b₀≠0, b₁=1)
Expected change per period = b₀. Still nonstationary. Fix: first-difference → yt =
b₀ + εt

Property
Random Walk
Covariance-Stationary AR(1)
|b₁|<1
b₁
= 1 (unit root)
|b₁| < 1
Mean-reverting level
UNDEFINED (0/0)
Finite: b₀/(1−b₁)
Variance over time
Grows without bound: (t−1)σ²
Constant and finite

Covariance stationary?
NO
YES
Valid for OLS?
NO — results are spurious
YES
Fix to make stationary
First-difference: yt = xt − xt-₁
No transformation needed

Yen/Dollar Exchange Rate — Random Walk Example
AR(1) on raw series: R²=0.9897, b₁=0.9919, intercept insignificant → looks like random walk
CANNOT test b₁=1 using standard t-test because if it is a random walk, standard errors are invalid!
Solution: First-difference → estimate AR(1) on yt = xt − xt-₁
First-differenced model: R²=0.0008, neither coefficient significant, no residual autocorrelations
significant
Conclusion: Exchange rate IS a random walk. Changes are unpredictable. Cannot profit from
forecasting exchange rate changes.
TRAP: Raw AR(1) had R²=0.9897 (seemingly excellent). First-differenced model has R²=0.0008. High
R² in non-stationary series = spurious, not a sign of quality.

Dickey-Fuller Unit Root Test

Dickey-Fuller
Transformation
xt − xt-₁ = b₀ + g₁xt-₁ + εt    where g₁ = b₁ − 1
H₀: g₁=0 (unit root; nonstationary). Hₐ: g₁<0 (no unit root; stationary). Use Dickey-
Fuller critical values (larger |values| than standard t). FAIL to reject H₀ → unit root
→ first-difference and retest.

AstraZeneca Sales — Unit Root, First-Differencing
Log of AstraZeneca quarterly sales: clear upward trend → likely has unit root → fails DF test
Create first-differenced series: yt = ln Salest − ln Salest-₁ (log-differenced sales)
First-differenced series has no strong trend → assume covariance stationary
Estimate AR(1) on first-differenced series: b₀=0.0222 (2.22% quarterly growth if no change last
quarter), b₁=−0.5493
All four residual autocorrelations insignificant → AR(1) on first-differenced series is correctly specified

!
Dickey-Fuller H₀ direction: H₀ is that there IS a unit root. FAILING to reject = unit root present =
first-difference needed. We WANT to reject H₀ to confirm stationarity. The critical values are
LARGER in absolute value than standard t-distribution values, so a test statistic must be MORE
extreme to reject H₀.

heading: MOVING-AVERAGE MODELS
body:
Model
Formula
Key Rule for Identification

n-period simple moving
average
[xt + xt-₁ + ... + xt-(n-1)] / n
Used for SMOOTHING only. Gives equal
weight to all n periods. Lags large movements.
Not optimal for forecasting.
MA(1) — Moving
Average Order 1
xt = εt + θεt-₁
ONLY first autocorrelation is non-zero. All
others are EXACTLY zero. Memory of 1
period.
MA(q) — Moving
Average Order q
xt = εt + θ₁εt-₁ + ... + θqεt-q
First q autocorrelations are non-zero. All
autocorrelations beyond lag q are EXACTLY
zero. Memory of q periods.

DISTINGUISHING AR FROM MA: AR autocorrelations start large and DECLINE GRADUALLY toward zero.
MA(q) autocorrelations are non-zero for the first q lags, then SUDDENLY DROP TO EXACTLY ZERO. This
abrupt cutoff is the identifying feature of MA processes. Most financial time series → AR is more appropriate.

S&P BSE 100 Monthly Returns — MA(0) (Unpredictable)
168 monthly observations; SE = 1/√168 = 0.0772; critical t ≈ 1.98
All 6 autocorrelations tested: none have |t-stat| > 1.43 → none significant
For MA(q): first q autocorrelations should be significant. None are → MA(0) process
MA(0): xt = μ + εt → the series is COMPLETELY UNPREDICTABLE. Best forecast = long-run mean.
Consistent with weak-form market efficiency: short-term returns are not predictable from past returns

heading: SEASONALITY
body:
Seasonality = regular within-year patterns in the time series. Detected by significant residual autocorrelation at the
seasonal lag (4th for quarterly data, 12th for monthly data).

AR(1) with Seasonal Lag
(Quarterly Data)
xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + b₂xt-₄ + εt
Forecast: x̂ t+1 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁xt + b̂ ₂xt-₃  (shift the seasonal lag one period forward
when forecasting)

AR(1) with Seasonal Lag
(Monthly Data)
xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + b₂xt-₁₂ + εt
Forecast: x̂ t+1 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁xt + b̂ ₂xt-₁₁

Starbucks Quarterly Sales — Seasonality Correction
AR(1) on log-differenced quarterly sales: R²=0.2044. Residual autocorrelation lags 1–3 insignificant.
BUT Lag 4 autocorrelation = 0.7630, t-stat = 6.47 → highly significant → seasonal AR(1) is
MISSPECIFIED
Add seasonal lag (4th lag of log-differenced sales) to the model:
AR(1) with seasonal lag: R² jumps from 0.2044 to 0.7032 — over 3× improvement
All residual autocorrelations lags 1–4 now insignificant → correctly specified

Seasonal lag coefficient b₂ = 0.7549: if log-sales grew 2% four quarters ago, this adds 0.7549×2% =
1.51% to the current quarter's forecast
Canadian retail sales (monthly): adding 12th seasonal lag raised R² from 0.0509 to 0.6724

!
Seasonal lag in forecasting: For AR(1) with seasonal lag xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + b₂xt-₄ (quarterly), the
ONE-PERIOD-AHEAD forecast is x̂ t+1 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁xt + b̂ ₂xt-₃, NOT xt-₄. When forecasting t+1, the
'four quarters ago' value relative to t+1 is xt-₃ (not xt-₄, which was four quarters ago relative to t).

heading: ARMA MODELS AND ARCH
body:
ARMA Models — Brief Overview and Limitations

ARMA(p,q)
xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + ... + bpxt-p + εt + θ₁εt-₁ + ... + θqεt-q
Combines p AR lags and q MA error terms. Rarely outperforms a pure AR model
in financial applications.

ARMA Limitations — Why Pure AR is Usually Preferred
1. Parameters are highly UNSTABLE — small data changes or different starting guesses can produce
very different estimates
2. Choosing p and q is as much art as science — no definitive criteria
3. Even with extra complexity, ARMA models rarely forecast better than pure AR models
4. NOT recommended with fewer than 80 observations (quarterly data requires at least 20 years)
5. Do NOT recommend using ARMA for quarterly sales or gross margins even with 15 years of data

ARCH — Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity

ARCH was developed by Robert F. Engle (2003 Nobel Prize in Economics). It models the case where the
VARIANCE of the error term is itself autoregressive — today's large error increases the expected variance
tomorrow (volatility clustering).

ARCH(1) Error Structure
εt ~ N(0, a₀ + a₁εt-₁²)    Variance: σ²t = a₀ + a₁εt-₁²
If a₁=0: constant variance (homoskedastic — no ARCH). If a₁>0: large errors
today → larger variance tomorrow.

ARCH(1) Detection Test
Estimate: ε̂t² = a₀ + a₁ε̂t-₁² + ut    Test H₀: a₁=0
If a₁ is significant (|t-stat| > critical value) → ARCH(1) present → standard errors
of AR coefficients are WRONG → hypothesis tests invalid.

ARCH(1) Variance
Prediction
σ̂²t+1 = â₀ + â₁ε̂²t

If ARCH(1) is identified, next period's variance CAN BE PREDICTED. This
enables volatility forecasting and option pricing.

CPI Inflation ARCH(1) Test
AR(1) residuals (1995–2018): regress ε̂²t on ε̂²t-₁
a₁ = 0.2754, t-stat = 4.83 → highly significant → ARCH(1) present
CONSEQUENCE 1: Standard errors of AR(1) inflation model are incorrect → t-tests on b₀, b₁ are
INVALID
CONSEQUENCE 2: Can forecast next-period error variance: σ̂²t+1 = 6.3626 + 0.2754·ε̂²t
If current error = 1%: σ̂²t+1 = 6.3626 + 0.2754(1)² = 6.638
ARCH(1) also detected for shorter 2008–2018 sample (t-stat=4.02) → robust across sample periods

!
ARCH test uses SQUARED residuals, not residuals directly. The test regresses ε̂²t on ε̂²t-₁ — it is
testing whether the VARIANCE of the errors is itself serially correlated. This is different from
testing whether the residuals themselves are serially correlated.

heading: REGRESSIONS WITH MULTIPLE TIME SERIES &
body:
COINTEGRATION (LOS 13)

Before regressing one time series on another, ALWAYS test each series for unit roots. Failing to do so can
produce spurious regression results that appear highly significant but are meaningless.

Scenario
Dep. Var
Indep. Var
Result
Implication
1
No unit root
No unit root
Error term covariance
stationary
SAFE to use linear regression
2
Unit root
No unit root
Error term NOT
covariance stationary
DO NOT use regression — spurious
results
3
No unit root
Unit root
Error term NOT
covariance stationary
DO NOT use regression — spurious
results
4
Unit root
Unit root
NOT cointegrated
DO NOT use regression — error term
non-stationary → spurious
5
Unit root
Unit root
ARE cointegrated
CAN use regression — estimates
LONG-TERM relationship; consistent
results

Engle-Granger
Cointegration Test
Step 1: Estimate yt = b₀ + b₁xt + εt. Step 2: Apply Dickey-Fuller to
the residuals using ENGLE-GRANGER critical values (NOT standard
DF values). H₀: residuals have a unit root (NOT cointegrated).
Reject H₀ → residuals are stationary → cointegrated → can use regression for
long-term relationship. FAIL to reject H₀ → regression is spurious.


COINTEGRATION DEFINED: Two time series are cointegrated if a long-term financial or economic relationship
prevents them from diverging without bound. They share a common trend. Example: natural log of Intel sales and
natural log of GDP — if the economy and tech revenues share a long-term relationship, these may be
cointegrated.

Applying the Five Scenarios: Transportation Stocks and Oil Price
Company 1: Has unit root + NOT cointegrated with oil → Scenario 4 → DO NOT regress stock on oil
(spurious)
Company 2: Has unit root + IS cointegrated with oil → Scenario 5 → CAN regress stock on oil for long-
term relationship
Company 3: NO unit root → depends on oil's status. Since oil HAS a unit root, Company 3 and oil =
Scenario 3 → DO NOT regress
Company 1 also has ARCH(1) → error variance is predictable. Has exponential trend + serial
correlation → use log-differenced AR model
Company 3: Has exponential trend + serial correlation but NO unit root → first-differenced log AR(1)
appropriate

!
Engle-Granger vs. standard Dickey-Fuller critical values: The cointegration test uses LARGER
critical values (in absolute value) than the standard DF test because residuals from an estimated
regression have added uncertainty. Using wrong critical values → wrong cointegration
conclusions.

!
Cointegration only matters when BOTH series have unit roots. If only one series has a unit root →
cannot use regression, period (Scenarios 2 and 3). Cointegration is the path to salvation only in
Scenario 4/5, where BOTH series have unit roots.

heading: TIME-SERIES MODEL SELECTION GUIDE
body:
Nine-Step Process for Building a Time-Series Model
Step 1: Define the investment question. Choose causal regression (other variables) vs. time-series
model (past values of same variable).
Step 2: Plot the series. Look for: (a) linear trend, (b) exponential trend, (c) seasonality, (d) structural
shifts in mean or variance.
Step 3: Try a trend model (linear or log-linear). Test DW on residuals. If DW close to 2.0 → trend
model is sufficient → stop.
Step 4: If DW indicates serial correlation → need AR model. Transform to achieve stationarity: linear
trend → first-difference; exponential trend → log then first-difference; structural break → separate
models; seasonality → seasonal lags.
Step 5: Estimate AR(1) on the stationary series. Test residual autocorrelations: t_k = ρ̂_k / (1/√T).
Step 6: If any residual autocorrelation significant → upgrade to AR(2). Repeat until all residual
autocorrelations are insignificant.
Step 7: Check for seasonality. If seasonal lag residual autocorrelation significant → add seasonal lag
to the AR model.

Step 8: Test for ARCH(1) by regressing ε̂²t on ε̂²t-₁. If coefficient significant → use GLS or robust
standard errors. Can also predict next-period variance.
Step 9 (optional): Evaluate out-of-sample performance using RMSE. Model with lower RMSE is
preferred.

heading: MASTER EXAM TRAPS — LM5
body:
!
DW is INVALID in AR models: Cannot use Durbin-Watson when lagged dependent variable is an
independent variable. ALWAYS use residual autocorrelations (t = ρ̂k ÷ (1/√T)) to test AR model
fit.

!
Random walk R² trap: Raw AR(1) on a random walk produces R² ≈ 0.99 (spurious). First-
differenced correct model produces R² ≈ 0.00. Do NOT compare these R² values to choose
between models — they measure different things.

!
Mean-reverting level undefined: b₀/(1−b₁) is undefined when b₁=1 (division by zero). An
undefined mean-reverting level SIGNALS non-stationarity (random walk). Covariance-stationary
series ALWAYS have finite mean-reverting levels.

!
Dickey-Fuller direction: H₀ = unit root (non-stationary). FAIL to reject → unit root → must first-
difference. Critical values are LARGER in absolute value than standard t, so the test statistic
must be more negative to reject H₀.

!
Seasonal forecasting — lag shift: For quarterly AR(1) with seasonal lag xt = b₀ + b₁xt-₁ + b₂xt-₄,
the forecast for t+1 is x̂ t+1 = b̂ ₀ + b̂ ₁xt + b̂ ₂xt-₃. The seasonal lag shifts forward: from xt-₄ to xt-₃
when you advance one period.

!
MA(q) zero autocorrelations: Beyond lag q, autocorrelations are EXACTLY zero, not just small.
This sharp cutoff distinguishes MA from AR. AR autocorrelations decline gradually; MA(q)
autocorrelations drop abruptly to zero after q lags.

!
ARCH test uses squared residuals: Test ARCH by regressing ε̂²t on ε̂²t-₁ (squared residuals, not
residuals). Significant a₁ → ARCH(1) → t-statistics on AR coefficients are INVALID. Can also use
this to predict variance.

!
Cointegration requires BOTH series to have unit roots: If only one series has a unit root →
regression is invalid regardless. Cointegration only rescues regression in Scenario 5 (both have
unit roots AND they are cointegrated). Engle-Granger critical values ≠ standard DF critical values.

heading: Penalized Regression & LASSO
body:
OLS overfits with many correlated features. LASSO adds a penalty term to the objective: minimize Σ(Yᵢ –
Ŷᵢ)² + λ · Σ|b̂ₖ|. A feature must contribute enough to fit to exceed the penalty cost. Features must be
standardized (mean 0, var 1) before running.
• Hyperparameter: λ (lambda). λ = 0 → LASSO reduces to OLS. Larger λ = heavier penalty = fewer
features.
• Automatic feature selection: can shrink coefficients to exactly zero, eliminating features.
• Penalty is training-only: applied while fitting; once the model is built, test-set evaluation uses
ordinary sum of squared residuals.
• Applications: default probability models with many collinear features; stable covariance matrix
estimation for mean–variance optimization.

✓
Parsimonious Default Model
Analyst with 100 historical default observations and 200+ candidate predictors uses LASSO to
shrink the feature set to 8 variables that minimize validation-set error. The resulting model is
parsimonious, less subject to overfitting, and generalizes well out-of-sample.

✗
λ = 0 and Calling it LASSO
With λ = 0 the penalty term vanishes — LASSO reduces to OLS. No regularization is actually
occurring. Standard CFA practice-problem trap (Q2 option B).

⚠
LASSO TRAPS

Page 4  of  19
λ = 0 is NOT LASSO (it's OLS). Standardize features first. Penalty is applied only during training;
test-set evaluation uses ordinary sum of squared residuals.

heading: Support Vector Machine (SVM)
body:
Linear classifier that finds the hyperplane separating two classes by the maximum margin. The margin
is defined by the support vectors — the observations closest to the boundary. Adding data away from
the boundary doesn't shift it.
• Hyperplane dimensions: n=1 → point; n=2 → line; n=3 → plane; n≥4 → hyperplane (no visual
intuition).
• Soft margin: adds a penalty for misclassified training points; trades wider margin vs lower error.
• Non-linear SVM: uses non-linear boundaries — reduces training error but prone to overfitting.
• Hyperparameter: cost C. Large C = thin margin (penalize misclassifications heavily); small C = wide
margin (tolerate more misclassifications).
• Best for: small-to-medium high-dimensional binary classification — bankruptcy prediction,
sentiment classification of text. Resilient to outliers and correlated features.

✓
Lee classifies Biotron Corporation's unrated bond
Fixed-income PM Lee trains SVM on characteristics of known low-investment-grade
(Baa3/BBB–) and high-non-investment-grade (Ba1/BB+) bonds, using profitability, cash flow,
leverage, and coverage ratios as features. She then notes which side of the optimal
hyperplane Biotron's new 10-year fixed-coupon bond lies on to determine the implied rating.

⚠
SVM TRAP
Only observations nearest the boundary (support vectors) matter. Adding more labeled data
far from the boundary changes nothing. Non-linear SVM is NOT always better than linear —
added flexibility introduces variance error (overfitting).

heading: K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
body:
To classify a new observation, find its k nearest labeled observations and take a majority vote
(classification) or average (regression). Non-parametric — no distributional assumptions. Can be used
directly for multi-class classification.
• Hyperparameters: k (# neighbors) and distance metric (Euclidean most common).
• k trade-off: too small → outlier-sensitive; too large → dilutes "nearest" by averaging too many
outcomes; even k → ties possible (prefer odd k for binary problems).

Page 5  of  19
• Weakness: inappropriate distance metric kills the model (especially for ordinal/categorical data).
Sensitive to irrelevant/correlated features. Works best with few features.
• Applications: implied credit ratings for unrated bonds (find similar rated bonds by issuer size,
industry, leverage, cash-flow ratios, tenor, fixed/floating coupon, embedded options); custom
diversified bond index construction; bankruptcy and stock-price prediction.

✓
Lee also uses KNN for Biotron
Lee trains KNN on the same labeled bond data as for SVM. The decision rule for classifying
Biotron's new bond is the rating shared by the majority among its k-nearest neighbors. If both
SVM and KNN agree on the rating, Lee has greater confidence that the classification is robust
to algorithm choice.

⚠
KNN TRAPS
Optimal k is never known a priori — cross-validation finds it. An inappropriate distance metric
kills the model (this is a bigger risk than in most other ML methods). Euclidean is not always
right for ordinal/categorical data.

heading: Classification and Regression Tree (CART)
body:
A tree of if-then rules. Binary CART = root node + decision nodes + terminal nodes. Root and decision
nodes each represent a single feature (f) and cutoff value (c). At each node, CART picks the feature +
cutoff that maximize separation of labeled data (minimize classification error, e.g. MSE). The same
feature can appear multiple times at different depths with different cutoffs — this captures non-
linearities and interactions that linear regression cannot.
Curriculum example: Is stock cheap (P/E < 15x)? → if YES: Is leverage high (Debt/TC > 50%)? → if YES:
Are sales growing (>15%)? → if YES: Is profitability high (NPM > 20%)? → if YES: Attractive Equity
Investment; else Value Trap.
• Prediction: classification = majority class at terminal node; regression = mean of labeled values at
terminal node.
• Prevent overfitting: pre-pruning (max depth, min population, max nodes) or post-pruning (grow
fully, cut low-value branches).
• Strengths: handles non-linear relationships and interactions; interpretable (visual tree); no
distributional assumptions; robust to noisy data.
• Applications: fraud detection in financial statements, equity/fixed-income selection,
communicating strategies to clients.


Page 6  of  19
✓
Kim's high-yield default classifier
PM Kim has 19 fundamental + 5 technical factors = 24 features for several thousand labeled
high-yield bonds (default / no default). CART is appropriate because it's a classification
problem, the relationships are non-linear, and the visual tree output helps communicate
recommendations to the investment committee. To avoid overfitting, Kim's team specifies
max tree depth and min population at a node.

⚠
CART TRAP
Unconstrained CART WILL memorize training data — it can "perfectly learn" the training
sample. Always pair CART with regularization (pruning, max depth, min population, max
nodes). CART is supervised — don't confuse with clustering.

heading: Ensemble Learning & Random Forest
body:
Combine predictions from many models; noise cancels, accuracy and stability rise. Ensemble methods
typically produce more accurate and more stable predictions than the best single model — often the
winning solution in ML competitions. Two types:
• Heterogeneous — voting classifier combines different algorithms (e.g. SVM + KNN + CART →
majority vote). Diversity in algorithms and hypotheses drives the improvement.
• Homogeneous (bagging) — same algorithm trained on n bootstrap samples (random sampling
WITH replacement from training set). Aggregate by majority vote (classification) or average
(regression).
Random Forest = many CART trees trained via bagging + an added twist — at each split, each tree
considers only a random subset of m features (m < n total). The feature-subsetting forces diversity
between trees; without it, every tree would pick the same dominant features first.
• Hyperparameters: # trees, max depth, m (feature subset size).
• Strengths: reduces variance (errors cancel across trees); noise-robust; usually beats single CART.
• Weakness: "black box" — loses CART's visual interpretability. No single decision path explains
predictions.
• Precision = correctly predicted positives / all predicted positives ("when I say fraud, how often am I
right?"). Recall = correctly predicted positives / all actual positives ("how many actual fraud cases
did I catch?").

✓
Kaggle credit-card fraud detection
Open-source dataset with 284,807 transactions; only 492 are fraudulent (0.17%) — extreme
class imbalance. A random forest classifier with oversampling (boosting the representation of

Page 7  of  19
fraudulent cases in training) delivers precision 89% and recall 82% — strong performance
despite the lopsided sample.

⚠
ENSEMBLE TRAP
Improving a CART model → use ENSEMBLE learning (combine with other models), NOT switch
to a different single algorithm like SVM. CFA PP Q8. The "next step" after CART is to add trees
and diverse models, not to replace CART entirely.

heading: Principal Components Analysis (PCA)
body:
Collapses many correlated features into a smaller set of uncorrelated composite variables. Uses two
concepts: eigenvectors (new composite variables, defining directions in feature space) and eigenvalues
(proportion of total variance each eigenvector explains). PC1 captures the most variance; PC2 is
perpendicular to PC1 (thus uncorrelated) and captures the next-most; etc. Features are standardized
(mean 0, std 1) first.
Geometrically, PCA picks PC1 to simultaneously minimize projection error (perpendicular distance from
each point to PC1) AND maximize spread along PC1.
• How many PCs to keep: use a scree plot; retain the smallest number explaining 85–95% of
variance.
• Curriculum example: DLC 500 index — first 3 PCs explain ~90% of return variance (43% + 26% +
21%); VLC 30 — first 3 PCs explain ~86% (55% + 20% + 11%). Researchers conclude 3 PCs are
sufficient.
• Strength: avoids curse of dimensionality; faster model training; enables 2D/3D visualization; more
stable covariance-matrix estimation.
• Weakness: components are linear combinations of original features — typically cannot be easily
labeled or interpreted ("black box").

✓
Reducing 2,000+ features before ML
A quant team has 2,000 highly multicollinear features from market and fundamental data.
Running ML algorithms directly on all features produces unstable results. They apply PCA,
retain the top 5–10 principal components (explaining ~90% of variance), then feed those
reduced components into a downstream supervised model. Result: faster training, less

Page 8  of  19
overfitting, and interpretable in-sample performance — though the PCs themselves remain
uninterpretable.

⚠
PCA TRAPS
PCA is UNSUPERVISED — uses NO target Y. Eigenvector = direction (composite variable);
eigenvalue = variance explained — don't swap. Retain the SMALLEST number of PCs
explaining 85–95% of variance. Retaining more defeats the purpose.

heading: K-Means Clustering
body:
Partition observations into k non-overlapping clusters. Each observation joins the cluster whose
centroid is closest. Algorithm: place k random centroids → assign each point to nearest centroid →
recompute centroids as cluster means → reassign → iterate until no point changes cluster. At
convergence: cohesion maximized (tight clusters), separation maximized (distinct clusters), subject to
k.
Two clustering quality properties: COHESION (observations within a cluster are similar) and
SEPARATION (observations in different clusters are dissimilar). Euclidean distance is most common;
correlation is useful for portfolio diversification.
• Hyperparameter: k (# clusters) — must be pre-specified. Distance measure also chosen in
advance.
• Weakness: results depend on initial centroid locations → run multiple times with different starts
and pick the most useful clustering.
• Strength: fast; works on very large datasets (hundreds of millions of observations).
• Applications: grouping the Russell 3000 into 10, 50, or more clusters by financial/operating
characteristics (companies in the same SIC code often have very different profiles); hedge fund
classification alternatives; data exploration.

✓
Peer-group discovery beyond SIC
An asset manager groups the Russell 3000 using k=50 on features like total assets, revenue,
profitability, leverage, employee headcount, and R&D intensity. The resulting clusters reveal
peer groups that cut across conventional industry classifications — useful for relative-value
screening and pair trades.

heading: Hierarchical Clustering
body:
Page 9  of  19
Builds a nested tree of clusters rather than a fixed partition — no need to pre-specify k. Analysts can
examine alternative segmentations of different granularity before choosing.
• Agglomerative (bottom-up): each observation starts as its own cluster → repeatedly merge the
two closest clusters → end with one big cluster. Faster; best at identifying small clusters (local
patterns).
• Divisive (top-down): start with one cluster → recursively split → end with singletons. Slower; best
at large-cluster structure (global patterns).
• Linkage methods: minimum (single), maximum (complete), or average (centroid) distance
between clusters.
• Dendrogram: tree diagram with distance on y-axis. The "arch" connects two vertical "dendrites";
arch height = distance between merged clusters. Shorter dendrites = more similar. Cut horizontally
at any level to reveal the cluster count at that stage.

✓
Portfolio diversification via clustering
Analyst clusters 8 S&P 500 stocks (AAPL, F, FB, GM, GS, GOOG, JPM, UBS) using agglomerative
clustering on daily log-return vectors (501 days). Closest pair: JPM–GS (distance 0.215).
Result: three clusters — banks (JPM/GS/UBS), autos (F/GM), and FB as an outlier from tech
(AAPL/GOOG). To achieve low-correlation diversification, the recommendation is to pick one
from each cluster.

⚠
CLUSTERING TRAPS
K-means needs k in advance; hierarchical does not. K-means depends on initial centroids (run
multiple times). K-means and hierarchical typically produce DIFFERENT clusters even on
identical data. Agglomerative and divisive can also disagree — different mechanics.

heading: Neural Networks
body:
Three layers: input (one node per feature), hidden layers (where learning happens), output
(prediction). Each node has two operators:
• Summation operator: receives values from all incoming links, multiplies each by its link weight
(initially often random), sums the weighted values → total net input.
• Activation function: transforms total net input into the node's final output. Non-linear by design.
Common choices: sigmoidal (S-shape, 0–1) or ReLU, f(x) = max(0, x). Operates like a "light dimmer
switch" — increases or decreases the strength of the input.

Page 10  of  19
ReLU example: y = β₁·max(0, x₁+x₂+x₃) + β₂·max(0, x₂+x₄) + β₃·max(0, x₂+x₃+x₄) + ε. Each z is the
transformed input after passing through the activation function.
Inputs must be scaled / standardized. Hyperparameters: # hidden layers, # nodes per layer, activation
function, connectivity pattern between layers.

heading: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs)
body:
Neural network with many hidden layers — at least 2, often more than 20. Foundation of deep
learning and the backbone of the AI revolution. Final layer produces probabilities for each target
category; DNN assigns the highest-probability class. Training uses large datasets to find weights that
minimize a specified loss function.
• Drivers of DNN success: (1) availability of large machine-readable datasets; (2) advances in
analytical methods for fitting; (3) fast computers — especially GPU chips tailored for DNN
calculations.
• Applications: image/pattern recognition, credit card fraud detection, autonomous vehicles, NLP
(machine translation), derivative pricing.

✓
Culkin & Das (2017) option pricing
Researchers trained a DNN to mimic Black-Scholes-Merton option pricing using the same six
inputs (spot, strike, time to maturity, dividend yield, risk-free rate, volatility), four hidden
layers of 100 neurons each, and one output. The DNN's predicted option prices regressed on
actual prices achieved R² = 99.8% — indicating the network learned the non-linear pricing
relationship very accurately.

heading: Reinforcement Learning
body:
An agent performs actions to maximize rewards in an environment, learning through trial and error. No
labeled data, no instant feedback — the algorithm observes, tests new actions (some initially sub-
optimal), and reuses previous experiences. Learning happens through millions of trial-and-error
iterations.
• AlphaGo framework: agent = virtual gamer, actions = console commands, environment = game
screen, reward = score. DeepMind's AlphaGo beat the reigning world Go champion in 2017.
• Investment applications: virtual trader (agent) following trading rules (actions) in a market
(environment) to maximize profits (reward). Success at scale remains an open question.

⚠

transforms. NNs are for COMPLEX non-linear tasks with feature interactions — NOT simple
single-variable OLS regression. RL is neither supervised nor unsupervised — its own category.

TF-IDF

TF-IDF = TF × IDF. Higher = more discriminative word for classification. Words with low IDF (stop words)
get filtered automatically when TF-IDF weighting is used in the DTM.

Visualization: WORD CLOUDS show relative word frequency via font size. Bar charts also work for TF or
DF.
Text Feature Selection — Remove Noisy Features
Noisy features are both the MOST FREQUENT (stop words → UNDERFITTING) and the MOST SPARSE
(rare tokens → OVERFITTING). Remove both.
• Frequency pruning: drop tokens with very high or very low TF/DF.
• Chi-square test: tests independence between token occurrence and class occurrence. High chi-
square statistic = strong association with a class = good feature.

Page 10  of  23
• Mutual Information (MI): measures how much information a token contributes to a class. MI = 0 if
equally distributed; MI → 1 if concentrated in one class.
Text Feature Engineering
• Numbers by length: 4-digit → /number4/ (likely year); 10-digit → /number10/ (likely ID).
• N-grams: preserve discriminative word pairs. "stock_market" distinguishes finance context from
generic "market".
• Name Entity Recognition (NER): tags tokens with OBJECT CLASSES — PERSON, ORGANIZATION,
LOCATION, DATE, MONEY, TIME, PERCENT. Avoids incorrect lowercasing/stemming of named
entities.
• Parts of Speech (POS): tags tokens with GRAMMATICAL ROLES — noun, verb, adjective, proper
noun. Disambiguates "market" (verb) vs "market" (noun).

✓
Bigrams that predict loan outcomes
LendALot: trigram "have_worked_hard" appears in non-default loan texts (work-ethic signal);
"had_worked_harder" appears in defaulted loan texts (regret/excuse signal). Same root,
opposite sentiment — an n-gram captures what a unigram would miss.

body:
Stock-sentiment context: FP = buying on falsely-positive sentiment (loss). FN = skipping (or shorting) on
falsely-negative sentiment (missed opportunity). Threshold 0.60 balances the two.

✓
Full text-to-forecast pipeline
14,780 sentences → cleansing (annotations, stopword removal) → BOW with TF–IDF
weighting → DTM (2,180 rows × thousands of token columns) → logistic regression with
LASSO → validate on 20% CV set → tune threshold to p = 0.60 → final AUC ~95%, F1 ~0.94.
Output sentiment feeds a larger stock-selection model alongside structured fundamentals.

MASTER EXAM TRAPS

⚠
The 4 Vs
Volume, Variety, Velocity, Veracity. Veracity is the "fourth V" relevant for
inference/prediction.

⚠
Structured vs text pipeline
Steps 1–4 differ; step 5 (model training) is the same. Text pipeline: text problem formulation
→ data curation → text prep & wrangling → text exploration.

⚠
Six cleansing errors
Incompleteness, Invalidity, Inaccuracy, Inconsistency, Non-uniformity, Duplication. Memorize
— each appears in the LendALot PP.

⚠
Preprocessing transformations
Extraction, Aggregation, Filtration, Selection, Conversion.

⚠
Selection ≠ feature selection
Preprocessing SELECTION drops obviously-unneeded columns. FEATURE SELECTION (later)
uses statistics (chi-square, MI, DF) to rank informative features.

⚠
Outlier rules
±3 SD (normal data). IQR: outside Q1−1.5·IQR or Q3+1.5·IQR; use 3·IQR for extreme. Trimming
REMOVES; winsorization CAPS.

⚠
Normalization vs standardization
Normalization = [0,1] range, outlier-sensitive (treat first!), distribution-free. Standardization =
mean 0, SD 1, less outlier-sensitive, assumes normal.

⚠
Stemming vs lemmatization
Stemming = RULE-based (may not be real words, "analyz"). Lemmatization = ALGORITHMIC
(real words, "analyze"). Stemming is simpler and more common.




Page 19  of  23
⚠
BOW / DTM structure
BOW = distinct tokens (no order). DTM rows = documents, columns = tokens, cells = counts
(or TF–IDF).

⚠
N-gram caveats
N-grams preserve order. But stop words "stick" inside n-grams. And negations like "no
market" survive as bigrams.

⚠
Tokenization basics
Usually WORD-level (not character). Part of PREPROCESSING, not cleansing.

⚠
TF / DF / IDF / TF-IDF
TF = token count / total tokens (collection). DF = docs containing token / total docs. IDF =
log(1/DF). TF-IDF = TF × IDF.

⚠
Noisy text features — BOTH ends
HIGH-freq tokens (stop words) → UNDERFIT. LOW-freq (rare) tokens → OVERFIT. Remove
both.

⚠
Chi-square vs MI
Chi-square tests independence between token and class. MI measures information
contributed; MI = 0 if equal across classes, MI → 1 if concentrated.

⚠
NER vs POS
NER tags OBJECT CLASSES (PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION, DATE, MONEY, TIME,
PERCENT). POS tags GRAMMATICAL ROLES (noun, verb, adjective).

⚠
Dim reduction ≠ feature selection
Dim reduction (PCA) creates NEW combinations. Feature selection keeps/drops ORIGINAL
features.




Page 20  of  23
⚠
60/20/20 split
Training 60%, CV 20%, Test 20%. Supervised only. k-fold sampling. CV tunes hyperparameters;
test is final judge.

⚠
Class imbalance fix
Undersample majority OR oversample minority. Advanced: synthesize new minority
observations.

⚠
Confusion matrix layout
Rows = predictions; columns = actuals. FP = Type I; FN = Type II.

⚠
Precision vs recall
P = TP/(TP+FP) — right when I say positive. R = TP/(TP+FN) — did I catch all positives.

⚠
F1 is harmonic mean
F1 = 2·P·R/(P+R). Penalizes imbalance between P and R. Beats accuracy under class imbalance.

⚠
ROC / AUC
TPR (y) vs FPR (x). AUC = 1.0 perfect; 0.5 random guessing. More convex = better.

⚠
RMSE scope
Regression only. √(Σ(pred−actual)²/n). Smaller = better.

⚠
Parameters vs hyperparameters
Parameters LEARNED; hyperparameters SET. λ, # hidden layers, k, p-threshold — all
hyperparameters.




Page 21  of  23
⚠
Bias vs variance
BIAS = underfit (both errors large). VARIANCE = overfit (train small, CV large). Fitting curve
shows trade-off.

⚠
What tool fights what
Feature engineering fights UNDERFIT. Feature selection / regularization fights OVERFIT.

⚠
Grid search
Systematic hyperparameter search. Not a model — a tuning procedure.

⚠
Ceiling analysis
For pipeline models. Identifies which sub-model is the bottleneck so tuning targets the
biggest lever.

⚠
Case study: LASSO fix
Before LASSO: train 96.5% / CV 86.2% → overfit. After LASSO: 95.7% / 94.8% → good fit.

⚠
Case study: threshold tuning
Default p=0.50 → accuracy 0.90, F1 0.93. p=0.60 optimum → accuracy 0.91, F1 0.94. Higher
threshold = higher precision, lower recall; lower threshold = opposite.

→ 1.0 perfect; 0.5 random guess
Regression Metric
RMSE = √( Σᵢ (Predictedᵢ − Actualᵢ)² / n ).  Smaller = better.  Applies to continuous-target regression only.
Scaling Formulas
• Normalization: Xᵢ (normalized) = (Xᵢ − Xmin) / (Xmax − Xmin). Range [0,1]. Outlier-sensitive — treat
outliers FIRST. Distribution-free.
• Standardization: Xᵢ (standardized) = (Xᵢ − μ) / σ. Mean 0, SD 1. Less outlier-sensitive but assumes
normal distribution.
Outlier Detection
• Standard deviation rule: |Xᵢ − μ| > 3·σ  (normal data only).
• IQR rule: outlier = value < Q1 − 1.5·IQR  or  value > Q3 + 1.5·IQR.  Use 3·IQR multiplier for EXTREME
values.  IQR = Q3 − Q1.  Works for any distribution.
Text Frequency Metrics
• Term Frequency (TF) = count of token in all texts / total tokens in dataset.
• Document Frequency (DF) = # documents containing token / total # documents.
• Inverse Document Frequency: IDF = log(1 / DF). Low IDF = common word; high IDF = rare /
distinctive word.
• TF–IDF = TF × IDF. High value = locally frequent AND globally rare = important word for
classification.
Data Split Ratio
Training 60%  |  Cross-Validation 20%  |  Test 20%  (supervised ML only; unsupervised needs no split
since no labels to validate against).
